Readings, each with a pedigree: Hebrew grammarians hear a solemn idiom of self-reference (kings speak of themselves this way in decrees, and the Bible does it elsewhere); some ancient Christian readers, as early as Justin Martyr, heard two divine persons — Jehovah on earth (the visitor of ch. 18) and Jehovah in heaven — and the verse has served in that argument ever since (the whole case, both ways, is on the Is Jesus God? page). This translation prints the doubled text and reports the argument. As for what fell: KJV, Geneva, Douay and ASV all rain "brimstone and fire" — the phrase that gave English its "fire and brimstone" — while NIV modernizes to "burning sulfur"; this translation reads "sulfur and fire," the plain mineral, in the Hebrew's own order. The verb of the event is haphakh, "overthrew" — and in verse 29 the event becomes a noun, "the overthrow" (hahafekhah): from here on, "like the overthrow of Sodom" is the Bible's standing measure of total ruin (Deuteronomy 29:23; Isaiah 13:19; Jeremiah 49:18).

Lot's wife, and Abraham's window. "His wife looked back from behind him — and she became a pillar of salt." One line, no psychology; the command had been flight without a backward tether, and she is caught mid-tether — fixed forever in the direction of what she could not leave. (The southern Dead Sea shore is salt-pillar country to this day — Mount Sodom's spires; the old tradition of Wisdom 10:7 already pointed at "a standing pillar of salt" as the story's monument. "Remember Lot's wife," Luke 17:32, needs only three words.) Then the camera climbs: Abraham, early in the morning, at "the place where he had stood before Jehovah" — yesterday's courtroom (18:22) — looks down over the plain and sees only smoke "like the smoke of a kiln" (kivshan — the word that will describe Sinai's smoke, Exodus 19:18). No word is recorded. And the narrator hands us the hinge quietly: "God remembered Abraham" — zakhar, the remembering-that-acts of "God remembered Noah" (8:1) — "and sent Lot out of the midst of the overthrow." Ten righteous were not found; but the argument of chapter 18 was not wasted — its other mercy, carrying the few out, is exactly what happened.

Verses 30–38 · מוֹאָב … בֶּן-עַמִּי Moav … Ben-Ammi

The cave. Lot — who begged for a town because he feared the hills (v19) — now fears the town too, and ends in the hills after all, in a cave, with nothing: the man who chose the well-watered plain "like the garden of Jehovah" (13:10) has run out of Sodoms. His daughters, watching the smoke of the only world they knew, draw a conclusion the text lets us hear in full: "there is no man on earth to come in to us" — they appear to believe the overthrow was the end of the world, a second flood in fire, and themselves its Noah's household. Their phrase is ark-vocabulary: "to keep offspring alive" (lehachayot zera) is what the animals were carried aboard to do (7:3). It is desperation, not appetite — and it is also the dark answer to verse 8: the father who once offered his daughters' bodies to a mob has his own body taken by his daughters, wine-blind, "and he knew nothing" — yada again, negated twice. (Over "and of her rising" in verse 33 the scribes set a row of dots — one of ten such dotted spots in the whole Torah, an ancient scribal flag; the rabbis read it as a wink that his ignorance was not quite total. Reported as received.)

Moab and Ben-Ammi. The sons are named with the text's own sound-glosses — Moav, heard as me'av, "from father"; Ben-Ammi, "son of my kin" — and they father Moab and Ammon, Israel's two kin-nations across the Jordan, whose long, prickly cousinhood with Israel the rest of the Bible remembers ("do not harass Moab… I have given Ar to the sons of Lot," Deuteronomy 2:9). Genesis tells its neighbors' origin without flattery — and then history hands the story its grace note: from Moab, from this cave's line, comes Ruth — "your people shall be my people" — great-grandmother of David (Ruth 4:17) and named in the genealogy of Jesus (Matthew 1:5). Lot leaves the story here, silent since Zoar, unmourned by the text; the last thing his family line is given is redemption. The closed break {ס} ends the chapter.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation sides with the literal shelf: "that we may know them" (the verb kept, not paraphrased away), "pillar of salt" plain, the doubled "Jehovah… from Jehovah" printed as it stands, and the daughters' ark-phrase "keep offspring alive" kept exact.

Where it goes its own way: "ruin" for shachat again (the flood's verb, audible through the angels' commission), "he seemed like one who was joking" (keeping the tsachaq laugh-root in view), and the scribal apparatus reported — the shalshelet trill on "but he lingered," and the ten-dots tradition at verse 33.

Echoes paid and planted. Paid: "that we may know them" against "I have known him" (18:19); the mob's "playing the judge" against the Judge of all the earth (18:25); the joking-laugh pays the laugh-thread (17:17, 18:12); the kikkar "like the garden of Jehovah" burns as chapter 13 foretold (13:10); Abraham returns to where he "stood before Jehovah" (18:22); and "God remembered Abraham" is Noah's hinge (8:1). Planted: matzot, the bread of the night-rescue (Exodus 12); the laugh-root's last dark turn (21:9); "like the overthrow of Sodom" as the prophets' byword; and Ruth the Moabite, the cave's unlikely grace (Ruth 4; Matthew 1:5).

Next installment: Genesis 20 — Abraham tries the sister-story again, at Gerar — and this time the king gets a dream.

Genesis 20 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיִּסַּע מִשָּׁם אַבְרָהָם אַרְצָה הַנֶּגֶב, וַיֵּשֶׁב בֵּין-קָדֵשׁ וּבֵין שׁוּר; וַיָּגָר, בִּגְרָר.
And Abraham journeyed from there toward the land of the Negev and settled between Kadesh and Shur; and he sojourned in Gerar.note
2
וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם אֶל-שָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ, אֲחֹתִי הִוא; וַיִּשְׁלַח, אֲבִימֶלֶךְ מֶלֶךְ גְּרָר, וַיִּקַּח, אֶת-שָׂרָה.
And Abraham said of Sarah his wife, "She is my sister." And Abimelech king of Gerar sent, and took Sarah.note
3
וַיָּבֹא אֱלֹהִים אֶל-אֲבִימֶלֶךְ, בַּחֲלוֹם הַלָּיְלָה; וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ, הִנְּךָ מֵת עַל-הָאִשָּׁה אֲשֶׁר-לָקַחְתָּ, וְהִוא, בְּעֻלַת בָּעַל.
But God came to Abimelech in a dream by night, and said to him, "Look — you are a dead man, because of the woman you have taken, for she is a married woman."note
4
וַאֲבִימֶלֶךְ, לֹא קָרַב אֵלֶיהָ; וַיֹּאמַר--אֲדֹנָי, הֲגוֹי גַּם-צַדִּיק תַּהֲרֹג.
Now Abimelech had not come near her. And he said, "Lord, will you kill a nation even though righteous?note
5
הֲלֹא הוּא אָמַר-לִי אֲחֹתִי הִוא, וְהִיא-גַם-הִוא אָמְרָה אָחִי הוּא; בְּתָם-לְבָבִי וּבְנִקְיֹן כַּפַּי, עָשִׂיתִי זֹאת.
Did he not himself say to me, 'She is my sister'? And she — she herself — said, 'He is my brother.' In the integrity of my heart and the innocence of my hands I have done this."note
6
וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו הָאֱלֹהִים בַּחֲלֹם, גַּם אָנֹכִי יָדַעְתִּי כִּי בְתָם-לְבָבְךָ עָשִׂיתָ זֹּאת, וָאֶחְשֹׂךְ גַּם-אָנֹכִי אוֹתְךָ, מֵחֲטוֹ-לִי; עַל-כֵּן לֹא-נְתַתִּיךָ, לִנְגֹּעַ אֵלֶיהָ.
And God said to him in the dream, "I too know that in the integrity of your heart you did this — and it was I who held you back from sinning against me; that is why I did not let you touch her.note
7
וְעַתָּה, הָשֵׁב אֵשֶׁת-הָאִישׁ כִּי-נָבִיא הוּא, וְיִתְפַּלֵּל בַּעַדְךָ, וֶחְיֵה; וְאִם-אֵינְךָ מֵשִׁיב--דַּע כִּי-מוֹת תָּמוּת, אַתָּה וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר-לָךְ.
Now return the man's wife — for he is a prophet, and he will pray for you, and you shall live. But if you do not return her, know that you will surely die, you and all who are yours."note
8
וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אֲבִימֶלֶךְ בַּבֹּקֶר, וַיִּקְרָא לְכָל-עֲבָדָיו, וַיְדַבֵּר אֶת-כָּל-הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה, בְּאָזְנֵיהֶם; וַיִּירְאוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים, מְאֹד.
And Abimelech rose early in the morning and called all his servants, and spoke all these words in their hearing; and the men were very afraid.note
9
וַיִּקְרָא אֲבִימֶלֶךְ לְאַבְרָהָם, וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ מֶה-עָשִׂיתָ לָּנוּ וּמֶה-חָטָאתִי לָךְ, כִּי-הֵבֵאתָ עָלַי וְעַל-מַמְלַכְתִּי, חֲטָאָה גְדֹלָה: מַעֲשִׂים אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יֵעָשׂוּ, עָשִׂיתָ עִמָּדִי.
Then Abimelech called Abraham and said to him, "What have you done to us? How have I sinned against you, that you have brought on me and on my kingdom so great a sin? Things that are not done, you have done to me."note
10
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ, אֶל-אַבְרָהָם: מָה רָאִיתָ, כִּי עָשִׂיתָ אֶת-הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה.
And Abimelech said to Abraham, "What did you see, that you did such a thing?"note
11
וַיֹּאמֶר, אַבְרָהָם, כִּי אָמַרְתִּי רַק אֵין-יִרְאַת אֱלֹהִים, בַּמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה; וַהֲרָגוּנִי, עַל-דְּבַר אִשְׁתִּי.
And Abraham said, "Because I said to myself, Surely there is no fear of God in this place, and they will kill me over my wife.note
12
וְגַם-אָמְנָה, אֲחֹתִי בַת-אָבִי הִוא--אַךְ, לֹא בַת-אִמִּי; וַתְּהִי-לִי, לְאִשָּׁה.
And besides, she truly is my sister — my father's daughter, though not my mother's daughter — and she became my wife.note
13
וַיְהִי כַּאֲשֶׁר הִתְעוּ אֹתִי, אֱלֹהִים מִבֵּית אָבִי, וָאֹמַר לָהּ, זֶה חַסְדֵּךְ אֲשֶׁר תַּעֲשִׂי עִמָּדִי: אֶל כָּל-הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר נָבוֹא שָׁמָּה, אִמְרִי-לִי אָחִי הוּא.
And when God caused me to wander from my father's house, I said to her, 'This is the kindness you must do me: at every place we come to, say of me, He is my brother.'"note
14
וַיִּקַּח אֲבִימֶלֶךְ צֹאן וּבָקָר, וַעֲבָדִים וּשְׁפָחֹת, וַיִּתֵּן, לְאַבְרָהָם; וַיָּשֶׁב לוֹ, אֵת שָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ.
Then Abimelech took sheep and cattle, and male and female slaves, and gave them to Abraham; and he returned Sarah his wife to him.note
15
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ, הִנֵּה אַרְצִי לְפָנֶיךָ: בַּטּוֹב בְּעֵינֶיךָ, שֵׁב.
And Abimelech said, "Look — my land is before you; settle wherever is good in your eyes."note
16
וּלְשָׂרָה אָמַר, הִנֵּה נָתַתִּי אֶלֶף כֶּסֶף לְאָחִיךְ--הִנֵּה הוּא-לָךְ כְּסוּת עֵינַיִם, לְכֹל אֲשֶׁר אִתָּךְ; וְאֵת כֹּל, וְנֹכָחַת.
And to Sarah he said, "Look — I have given your brother a thousand pieces of silver. Look, it is for you a covering of the eyes, for all who are with you; and in everything, you are cleared."note
17
וַיִּתְפַּלֵּל אַבְרָהָם, אֶל-הָאֱלֹהִים; וַיִּרְפָּא אֱלֹהִים אֶת-אֲבִימֶלֶךְ וְאֶת-אִשְׁתּוֹ, וְאַמְהֹתָיו--וַיֵּלֵדוּ.
Then Abraham prayed to God, and God healed Abimelech, and his wife, and his slave-women, and they bore children;note
18
כִּי-עָצֹר עָצַר יְהוָה, בְּעַד כָּל-רֶחֶם לְבֵית אֲבִימֶלֶךְ, עַל-דְּבַר שָׂרָה, אֵשֶׁת אַבְרָהָם. {ס}
for Jehovah had shut fast every womb of the house of Abimelech, because of Sarah, Abraham's wife.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–2 · אֲחֹתִי הִוא achoti hi

"She is my sister" — again. Abraham moves south into Beer-lahai-roi country ("between Kadesh and Shur," 16:14's map) and sojourns in Gerar — the verb is gur, the resident-stranger's verb, and the town's own name nearly rhymes with it: the sojourner sojourns in Sojournville. And there he runs the Egypt play again, word for word — "Say, please, that you are my sister" was 12:13; here the narrator needs only three words, achoti hi, because the reader has seen this show before. So has the tradition: chapters 12, 20, and (with Isaac, ahead) 26 are the Bible's most famous doublet — three tellings of the wife-sister ruse. Readings differ on why: source critics hear the same tale told by different hands (this version has all the marks of the strand scholars call E — God in dreams, moral argument, "prophet"); traditional readers see a man repeating a real habit of fear — and the text supports at least the habit, for verse 13 will reveal the ruse was a standing arrangement, "at every place we come to." Shown, not sanded. One thing said honestly: Sarah is about ninety here, and readers ancient and modern have asked how she endangers a royal harem — the rabbis gave her restored youth (a tradition the next chapter's conception invites), the source critics an original setting earlier in her story. The text itself is silent, and it is the silence this translation keeps.

Verses 3–7 · כִּי-נָבִיא הוּא ki navi hu

The pagan king gets the courtroom. God comes to Abimelech "in a dream by night" — no plagues this time, as in Egypt; a hearing. "Look — you are a dead man" (KJV: "thou art but a dead man" — the "but" is the translators'; the Hebrew is blunter), "for she is a married woman" — literally be'ulat ba'al, "owned by an owner": the ancient legal idiom, printed here in its plain modern sense. And Abimelech answers with a sentence the reader has heard before: "Lord, will you kill a nation even though righteous?" — tsaddiq, the word of Abraham's own argument two chapters ago ("will you sweep away the righteous with the wicked?", 18:23-25). The pagan king of Gerar, without knowing it, files Abraham's brief back at heaven — and the Judge of all the earth honors it from a Philistine's lips exactly as from a prophet's. His plea rests on "the integrity of my heart" — tom-levavi, the noun of tamim, the very wholeness God demanded of Abraham at 17:1 ("walk before me and be blameless"; KJV ASV "integrity of my heart," NIV "clear conscience") — and the chapter's sharpest irony is that in this scene the tom belongs to the pagan, and the scheming to the prophet.

"For he is a prophet." The first occurrence of navi in the Bible — and note what defines it: not prediction but intercession — "he will pray for you, and you shall live." A prophet is one who stands between; it is exactly what Abraham did for Sodom (18:23-32), and God describes him by it. God also volunteers something extraordinary: "it was I who held you back from sinning against me" — Abimelech's innocence was itself grace, restraint given before it was known to be needed. The alternative is stated in Eden's own formula: "know that you will surely die" — mot tamut, the doubled death-sentence of 2:17, served here on a king.

Verses 8–13 · מֶה-עָשִׂיתָ לָּנוּ meh-asita lanu

"What have you done to us?" Pharaoh's exact question (12:18), now asked at Gerar — and Abimelech goes further: "things that are not done, you have done to me" — the pagan king lecturing the patriarch on basic decency, and the text lets the lecture stand. Abraham's defense comes in three descending steps. First the assumption: "Surely there is no fear of God in this place" — demonstrably false; Gerar's whole court "was very afraid" at one dream, and its king argued righteousness with God at midnight. Abraham's fear slandered a town. Second, the technicality: "she truly is my sister — my father's daughter, though not my mother's daughter" — after twenty chapters, the text finally discloses Sarah's parentage, and it is a half-truth doing full-time work (later law will close even this loophole, Leviticus 18:9). Third, the habit: "at every place we come to, say of me, He is my brother" — the ruse was the standing travel policy, from the very first wandering; and he calls it chesed — "this is the kindness you must do me" — the great covenant word of 19:19, spent here on a survival scheme. The grammar of that same sentence carries a famous oddity: "when God caused me to wander" uses a plural verb with Elohim — the one word the dictionary entry calls "plural in form, singular in verb" here takes its plural verb after all (Hebrew grammar allows it in a Gentile's hearing, say some; a trace of an older idiom, say others). The versions all hide it in English, which cannot decline "God"; the note is where it survives.

Verses 14–16 · כְּסוּת עֵינַיִם kesut einayim

The generous ending Egypt never had. Pharaoh loaded Abram with gifts before the truth and expelled him after it (12:16, 19-20); Abimelech gives the flocks and slaves after being wronged — and instead of "take her and go," says "my land is before you; settle wherever is good in your eyes." Then he turns to Sarah, and speaks the chapter's most disputed sentence. "I have given your brother" — the word chosen with an edge, her own cover-story handed back to her — "a thousand pieces of silver. Look, it is for you a covering of the eyes…" The idiom (kesut einayim) is found nowhere else, and the shelf scatters like startled birds: KJV "behold, he is to thee a covering of the eyes" (reading the covering as the husband); Geneva adds its margin that the money is "to buy thee a vaile"; ASV "it is for thee a covering of the eyes"; NIV paraphrases the sense — "to cover the offense against you"; TLB flattens it to compensation. Most likely it means the payment publicly "covers the eyes" of onlookers — no one may look at Sarah as a woman dishonored; the final word, venokhachat ("you are cleared," or "set right"), is itself rough enough that the Douay, following the Vulgate, reads a rebuke instead: "remember thou wast taken." This translation keeps the strange idiom and the clearing, and files the dispute here.

Verses 17–18 · כִּי-עָצֹר עָצַר יְהוָה ki atsor atsar YHWH

The prophet prays, and wombs open. The first thing the Bible's first-named prophet does with the title is intercede for the foreigner who wronged-and-was-wronged-by him — "and God healed Abimelech, and his wife, and his slave-women, and they bore children." Then the narrator's closing line lands the verb: Jehovah had "shut fast every womb" — atsor atsar, the doubled form of atsar — the very verb Sarai used of herself: "Jehovah has kept me from bearing" (16:2). The house that took Sarah was given, for a season, Sarah's own condition. And hold the shape of this ending: Abraham prays for other wombs, and they open — and the very next verse in the book (21:1) begins, "And Jehovah visited Sarah." The man who interceded for strangers' children receives his own. The closed break {ס} draws the line, and the twenty-five-year promise is one verse from payment.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation sides with the literal shelf: "integrity of my heart" (with KJV/ASV, against the paraphrase), "covering of the eyes" kept as the strange idiom it is, "you are a dead man" blunt (dropping KJV's softening "but"), and "shut fast" for the doubled atsor atsar.

Where it goes its own way: "a married woman" for the legal idiom be'ulat ba'al ("owned by an owner" — reported in the note, not printed in the verse), "sojourned in Gerar" with the gur-Gerar near-rhyme flagged, and the plural verb of "God caused me to wander" preserved in the notes where English grammar cannot keep it.

Echoes paid and planted. Paid: the ruse replays Egypt beat for beat — "say you are my sister" (12:13) and "What have you done?" (12:18); Abimelech's "will you kill a nation even though righteous?" files back Abraham's own brief (18:25); "integrity" is the tamim asked of Abraham (17:1); "you will surely die" is Eden's formula (2:17); chesed (19:19) is spent on the ruse; and atsar shuts Abimelech's wombs with Sarai's own verb (16:2). Planted: the prophet-as-intercessor (Moses at Sinai will inherit the office); the third telling of the ruse, waiting for Isaac (ch. 26); Abimelech himself, who returns to cut a treaty at Beersheba (21:22-34); and one open womb, next verse.

Next installment: Genesis 21 — "And Jehovah visited Sarah": Isaac is born, laughter is named — and Hagar and Ishmael are sent into the wilderness.

John 1 — The Mister Translation

1
Ἐν ἀρχῇ ἦν ὁ λόγος, καὶ ὁ λόγος ἦν πρὸς τὸν θεόν, καὶ θεὸς ἦν ὁ λόγος.
In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was divine.note
2
οὗτος ἦν ἐν ἀρχῇ πρὸς τὸν θεόν.
He was in the beginning with God.note
3
πάντα διʼ αὐτοῦ ἐγένετο, καὶ χωρὶς αὐτοῦ ἐγένετο οὐδὲ ἕν. ὃ γέγονεν
Through him all things came to be, and apart from him not one thing came to be that has come to be.note
4
ἐν αὐτῷ ζωὴ ἦν, καὶ ἡ ζωὴ ἦν τὸ φῶς τῶν ἀνθρώπων·
In him was life, and the life was the light of humankind.note
5
καὶ τὸ φῶς ἐν τῇ σκοτίᾳ φαίνει, καὶ ἡ σκοτία αὐτὸ οὐ κατέλαβεν.
The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it.note
6
Ἐγένετο ἄνθρωπος ἀπεσταλμένος παρὰ θεοῦ, ὄνομα αὐτῷ Ἰωάννης·
There came a man, sent from God, whose name was John.note
7
οὗτος ἦλθεν εἰς μαρτυρίαν, ἵνα μαρτυρήσῃ περὶ τοῦ φωτός, ἵνα πάντες πιστεύσωσιν διʼ αὐτοῦ.
He came as a witness, to testify about the light, so that through him everyone might believe.note
8
οὐκ ἦν ἐκεῖνος τὸ φῶς, ἀλλʼ ἵνα μαρτυρήσῃ περὶ τοῦ φωτός.
He himself was not the light, but came to testify about the light.note
9
ἦν τὸ φῶς τὸ ἀληθινὸν ὃ φωτίζει πάντα ἄνθρωπον ἐρχόμενον εἰς τὸν κόσμον.
The true light, which gives light to everyone, was coming into the world.note
10
Ἐν τῷ κόσμῳ ἦν, καὶ ὁ κόσμος διʼ αὐτοῦ ἐγένετο, καὶ ὁ κόσμος αὐτὸν οὐκ ἔγνω.
He was in the world, and the world came to be through him, yet the world did not know him.note
11
εἰς τὰ ἴδια ἦλθεν, καὶ οἱ ἴδιοι αὐτὸν οὐ παρέλαβον.
He came to his own, and his own did not receive him.note
12
ὅσοι δὲ ἔλαβον αὐτόν, ἔδωκεν αὐτοῖς ἐξουσίαν τέκνα θεοῦ γενέσθαι, τοῖς πιστεύουσιν εἰς τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ,
But to all who did receive him, to those who believe in his name, he gave the right to become children of God —note
13
οἳ οὐκ ἐξ αἱμάτων οὐδὲ ἐκ θελήματος σαρκὸς οὐδὲ ἐκ θελήματος ἀνδρὸς ἀλλʼ ἐκ θεοῦ ἐγεννήθησαν.
children born not of blood, nor of the will of the flesh, nor of the will of a husband, but of God.note
14
Καὶ ὁ λόγος σὰρξ ἐγένετο καὶ ἐσκήνωσεν ἐν ἡμῖν, καὶ ἐθεασάμεθα τὴν δόξαν αὐτοῦ, δόξαν ὡς μονογενοῦς παρὰ πατρός, πλήρης χάριτος καὶ ἀληθείας·
And the Word became flesh and pitched his tent among us, and we saw his glory — glory as of the only Son from the Father — full of grace and truth.note
15
Ἰωάννης μαρτυρεῖ περὶ αὐτοῦ καὶ κέκραγεν λέγων· Οὗτος ἦν ὃν εἶπον· Ὁ ὀπίσω μου ἐρχόμενος ἔμπροσθέν μου γέγονεν, ὅτι πρῶτός μου ἦν·
(John testifies about him; he cried out, "This was the one I meant when I said, 'The one coming after me ranks ahead of me, because he was before me.'")note
16
ὅτι ἐκ τοῦ πληρώματος αὐτοῦ ἡμεῖς πάντες ἐλάβομεν, καὶ χάριν ἀντὶ χάριτος·
For from his fullness we have all received — grace upon grace.note
17
ὅτι ὁ νόμος διὰ Μωϋσέως ἐδόθη, ἡ χάρις καὶ ἡ ἀλήθεια διὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ ἐγένετο.
For the law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ.note
18
θεὸν οὐδεὶς ἑώρακεν πώποτε· μονογενὴς θεὸς ὁ ὢν εἰς τὸν κόλπον τοῦ πατρὸς ἐκεῖνος ἐξηγήσατο.
No one has ever seen God. The only God, who is at the Father's side — he has made him known.note
19
Καὶ αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ μαρτυρία τοῦ Ἰωάννου ὅτε ἀπέστειλαν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι ἐξ Ἱεροσολύμων ἱερεῖς καὶ Λευίτας ἵνα ἐρωτήσωσιν αὐτόν· Σὺ τίς εἶ;
And this is John's testimony, when the Judeans sent priests and Levites from Jerusalem to ask him, "Who are you?"note
20
καὶ ὡμολόγησεν καὶ οὐκ ἠρνήσατο, καὶ ὡμολόγησεν ὅτι Ἐγὼ οὐκ εἰμὶ ὁ χριστός.
He confessed — he did not deny, but confessed — "I am not the Messiah."note
21
καὶ ἠρώτησαν αὐτόν· Τί οὖν; σὺ Ἠλίας εἶ; καὶ λέγει· Οὐκ εἰμί. Ὁ προφήτης εἶ σύ; καὶ ἀπεκρίθη· Οὔ.
So they asked him, "What then? Are you Elijah?" He says, "I am not." "Are you the Prophet?" He answered, "No."note
22
εἶπαν οὖν αὐτῷ· Τίς εἶ; ἵνα ἀπόκρισιν δῶμεν τοῖς πέμψασιν ἡμᾶς· τί λέγεις περὶ σεαυτοῦ;
So they said to him, "Who are you? — so we can give an answer to those who sent us. What do you say about yourself?"note
23
ἔφη· Ἐγὼ φωνὴ βοῶντος ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ· Εὐθύνατε τὴν ὁδὸν κυρίου, καθὼς εἶπεν Ἠσαΐας ὁ προφήτης.
He said, "I am a voice of one crying out in the wilderness: 'Make straight the way of the Lord' — as Isaiah the prophet said."note
24
Καὶ ἀπεσταλμένοι ἦσαν ἐκ τῶν Φαρισαίων.
Now they had been sent from the Pharisees.note
25
καὶ ἠρώτησαν αὐτὸν καὶ εἶπαν αὐτῷ· Τί οὖν βαπτίζεις εἰ σὺ οὐκ εἶ ὁ χριστὸς οὐδὲ Ἠλίας οὐδὲ ὁ προφήτης;
And they asked him, "Why then do you baptize, if you are not the Messiah, nor Elijah, nor the Prophet?"note
26
ἀπεκρίθη αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰωάννης λέγων· Ἐγὼ βαπτίζω ἐν ὕδατι· μέσος ὑμῶν ἕστηκεν ὃν ὑμεῖς οὐκ οἴδατε,
John answered them, "I baptize with water. Among you stands one you do not know —note
27
ὁ ὀπίσω μου ἐρχόμενος οὗ οὐκ εἰμὶ ἄξιος ἵνα λύσω αὐτοῦ τὸν ἱμάντα τοῦ ὑποδήματος.
the one coming after me, whose sandal-strap I am not worthy to untie."note
28
ταῦτα ἐν Βηθανίᾳ ἐγένετο πέραν τοῦ Ἰορδάνου, ὅπου ἦν ὁ Ἰωάννης βαπτίζων.
This happened in Bethany beyond the Jordan, where John was baptizing.note
29
Τῇ ἐπαύριον βλέπει τὸν Ἰησοῦν ἐρχόμενον πρὸς αὐτόν, καὶ λέγει· Ἴδε ὁ ἀμνὸς τοῦ θεοῦ ὁ αἴρων τὴν ἁμαρτίαν τοῦ κόσμου.
The next day he sees Jesus coming toward him, and he says, "Look — the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!note
30
οὗτός ἐστιν ὑπὲρ οὗ ἐγὼ εἶπον· Ὀπίσω μου ἔρχεται ἀνὴρ ὃς ἔμπροσθέν μου γέγονεν, ὅτι πρῶτός μου ἦν·
This is the one I meant when I said, 'After me comes a man who ranks ahead of me, because he was before me.'note
31
κἀγὼ οὐκ ᾔδειν αὐτόν, ἀλλʼ ἵνα φανερωθῇ τῷ Ἰσραὴλ διὰ τοῦτο ἦλθον ἐγὼ ἐν ὕδατι βαπτίζων.
I myself did not know him; but I came baptizing with water so that he might be revealed to Israel."note
32
καὶ ἐμαρτύρησεν Ἰωάννης λέγων ὅτι Τεθέαμαι τὸ πνεῦμα καταβαῖνον ὡς περιστερὰν ἐξ οὐρανοῦ, καὶ ἔμεινεν ἐπʼ αὐτόν·
And John testified: "I have seen the Spirit descending like a dove out of heaven, and it remained on him.note
33
κἀγὼ οὐκ ᾔδειν αὐτόν, ἀλλʼ ὁ πέμψας με βαπτίζειν ἐν ὕδατι ἐκεῖνός μοι εἶπεν· Ἐφʼ ὃν ἂν ἴδῃς τὸ πνεῦμα καταβαῖνον καὶ μένον ἐπʼ αὐτόν, οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ βαπτίζων ἐν πνεύματι ἁγίῳ·
I myself did not know him, but the one who sent me to baptize with water — he told me, 'The one on whom you see the Spirit descend and remain, this is the one who baptizes with the Holy Spirit.'note
34
κἀγὼ ἑώρακα, καὶ μεμαρτύρηκα ὅτι οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ ἐκλεκτὸς τοῦ θεοῦ.
And I have seen and have testified that this is the Chosen One of God."note
35
Τῇ ἐπαύριον πάλιν εἱστήκει ὁ Ἰωάννης καὶ ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ δύο,
The next day John was standing again, with two of his disciples,note
36
καὶ ἐμβλέψας τῷ Ἰησοῦ περιπατοῦντι λέγει· Ἴδε ὁ ἀμνὸς τοῦ θεοῦ.
and looking at Jesus as he walked by, he says, "Look — the Lamb of God!"note
37
καὶ ἤκουσαν οἱ δύο μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ λαλοῦντος καὶ ἠκολούθησαν τῷ Ἰησοῦ.
The two disciples heard him say it, and they followed Jesus.note
38
στραφεὶς δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ θεασάμενος αὐτοὺς ἀκολουθοῦντας λέγει αὐτοῖς· Τί ζητεῖτε; οἱ δὲ εἶπαν αὐτῷ· Ῥαββί (ὃ λέγεται μεθερμηνευόμενον Διδάσκαλε), ποῦ μένεις;
Jesus turned, and seeing them following, he says to them, "What are you looking for?" They said to him, "Rabbi" (which means Teacher), "where are you staying?"note
39
λέγει αὐτοῖς· Ἔρχεσθε καὶ ὄψεσθε. ἦλθαν οὖν καὶ εἶδαν ποῦ μένει, καὶ παρʼ αὐτῷ ἔμειναν τὴν ἡμέραν ἐκείνην· ὥρα ἦν ὡς δεκάτη.
He says to them, "Come, and you will see." So they came and saw where he was staying, and they stayed with him that day. It was about the tenth hour.note
40
ἦν Ἀνδρέας ὁ ἀδελφὸς Σίμωνος Πέτρου εἷς ἐκ τῶν δύο τῶν ἀκουσάντων παρὰ Ἰωάννου καὶ ἀκολουθησάντων αὐτῷ·
Andrew, Simon Peter's brother, was one of the two who had heard John and followed him.note
41
εὑρίσκει οὗτος πρῶτον τὸν ἀδελφὸν τὸν ἴδιον Σίμωνα καὶ λέγει αὐτῷ· Εὑρήκαμεν τὸν Μεσσίαν (ὅ ἐστιν μεθερμηνευόμενον χριστός).
He first finds his own brother Simon and says to him, "We have found the Messiah" (which means Christ).note
42
ἤγαγεν αὐτὸν πρὸς τὸν Ἰησοῦν. ἐμβλέψας αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἶπεν· Σὺ εἶ Σίμων ὁ υἱὸς Ἰωάννου, σὺ κληθήσῃ Κηφᾶς (ὃ ἑρμηνεύεται Πέτρος).
He brought him to Jesus. Jesus looked at him and said, "You are Simon, son of John; you will be called Cephas" (which is translated Peter).note
43
Τῇ ἐπαύριον ἠθέλησεν ἐξελθεῖν εἰς τὴν Γαλιλαίαν. καὶ εὑρίσκει Φίλιππον καὶ λέγει αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς· Ἀκολούθει μοι.
The next day he wanted to set out for Galilee. He finds Philip, and Jesus says to him, "Follow me."note
44
ἦν δὲ ὁ Φίλιππος ἀπὸ Βηθσαϊδά, ἐκ τῆς πόλεως Ἀνδρέου καὶ Πέτρου.
Now Philip was from Bethsaida, the town of Andrew and Peter.note
45
εὑρίσκει Φίλιππος τὸν Ναθαναὴλ καὶ λέγει αὐτῷ· Ὃν ἔγραψεν Μωϋσῆς ἐν τῷ νόμῳ καὶ οἱ προφῆται εὑρήκαμεν, Ἰησοῦν υἱὸν τοῦ Ἰωσὴφ τὸν ἀπὸ Ναζαρέτ.
Philip finds Nathanael and says to him, "We have found the one Moses wrote about in the Law, and the prophets too — Jesus son of Joseph, from Nazareth."note
46
καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ Ναθαναήλ· Ἐκ Ναζαρὲτ δύναταί τι ἀγαθὸν εἶναι; λέγει αὐτῷ ὁ Φίλιππος· Ἔρχου καὶ ἴδε.
And Nathanael said to him, "Can anything good come out of Nazareth?" Philip says to him, "Come and see."note
47
εἶδεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς τὸν Ναθαναὴλ ἐρχόμενον πρὸς αὐτὸν καὶ λέγει περὶ αὐτοῦ· Ἴδε ἀληθῶς Ἰσραηλίτης ἐν ᾧ δόλος οὐκ ἔστιν.
Jesus saw Nathanael coming toward him, and says of him, "Look — truly an Israelite, in whom there is no deceit!"note
48
λέγει αὐτῷ Ναθαναήλ· Πόθεν με γινώσκεις; ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ· Πρὸ τοῦ σε Φίλιππον φωνῆσαι ὄντα ὑπὸ τὴν συκῆν εἶδόν σε.
Nathanael says to him, "How do you know me?" Jesus answered him, "Before Philip called you, while you were under the fig tree, I saw you."note
49
ἀπεκρίθη αὐτῷ Ναθαναήλ· Ῥαββί, σὺ εἶ ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ, σὺ βασιλεὺς εἶ τοῦ Ἰσραήλ.
Nathanael answered him, "Rabbi, you are the Son of God; you are the King of Israel!"note
50
ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ· Ὅτι εἶπόν σοι ὅτι εἶδόν σε ὑποκάτω τῆς συκῆς πιστεύεις; μείζω τούτων ὄψῃ.
Jesus answered him, "Because I told you I saw you under the fig tree, you believe? You will see greater things than these."note
51
καὶ λέγει αὐτῷ· Ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν, ὄψεσθε τὸν οὐρανὸν ἀνεῳγότα καὶ τοὺς ἀγγέλους τοῦ θεοῦ ἀναβαίνοντας καὶ καταβαίνοντας ἐπὶ τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου.
And he says to him, "Amen, amen, I tell you: you will see heaven opened, and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man."note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. The Greek shown is the SBL Greek New Testament (ed. Michael W. Holmes, Society of Biblical Literature, 2010), a modern critical text in the Alexandrian tradition; quotations from the copyrighted English versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison, and the KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims, and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–2 · Ἐν ἀρχῇ ἦν ὁ λόγος … θεὸς ἦν ὁ λόγος en archē ēn ho logos … theos ēn ho logos

"In the beginning." John opens on the first two words of Genesis — the Septuagint's ἐν ἀρχῇ, the very phrase the Greek Old Testament uses in Genesis 1:1 — and reaches back past the first day to what already was before anything was made. Every version keeps "In the beginning"; the echo is the point, and this translation flags it as the first cross-reference between the testaments.

"the Word" — ho logos. Logos means "word, reason, account," and John is reaching for two streams at once. There is the Hebrew stream: the "word of Jehovah" by which the world is made (Genesis 1 says "and God said" ten times), and Wisdom, who was "with" God at creation in Proverbs 8. And there is the Greek stream: the logos of the philosophers — from Heraclitus to the Stoics to Philo — the rational principle that orders the cosmos. No English word carries all of that, so every version simply keeps "the Word," and so does this one.

"was with God" — pros ton theon. The preposition pros with the accusative suggests being turned toward, face-to-face; the Word is not merely near God but in relationship with him. "With" is the standard rendering across the shelf and here.

"and the Word was God" — or "divine," or "a god"? — kai theos ēn ho logos. This is the most argued clause in the New Testament, and the honest starting point is that the Greek grammar underdetermines it. The predicate noun theos has no article and stands before the verb, while the subject ho logos has the article. Three readings compete: definite ("the Word was God"), qualitative ("the Word was divine / what God was, the Word was" — stressing the Word's nature), and indefinite ("the Word was a god"). A definite predicate noun placed before its verb regularly drops the article in Greek — the observation behind "Colwell's rule" — so the missing article does not by itself force the indefinite "a god"; the closest study of the construction (Harner, 1973) found it usually qualitative, and concluded this clause is neither "the Word was God" nor "a god" but "the Word had the same nature as God." Six of the seven shelf versions read "the Word was God" (NIV KJV GNV DRB ASV TLB); the NWT alone reads "the Word was a god," resting its case on the absent article.

Mister takes the qualitative road — "and the Word was divine" — because it says what the construction most likely says (the Word shares fully in what God is) while keeping the distinction the next clause insists on: he was with God, so he is not simply the person "God." "Divine" is deliberately neither the flat "was God" (which an English reader can hear as "the Word is the Father" — a collapse the verse itself rules out) nor "a god" (which the grammar least supports, and which reads awkwardly against "besides me there is no god," Isaiah 44:6). This is the one place in the Gospel where whole traditions divide — is the Son fully God, a lesser divine being, or the highest of created beings? — so the note refuses to cast the deciding vote. The full argument, on every side (the grammar, "firstborn of all creation," the Angel of Jehovah, the prayer to the Father, and the counter-testimony), is gathered in the Ask Mr. Librarian post on this verse.

Verses 3–4 · πάντα διʼ αὐτοῦ ἐγένετο … ὃ γέγονεν panta di' autou egeneto … ho gegonen

"came to be" — the drumbeat. The verb egeneto ("came to be, came into being") tolls through the whole Prologue, and it is set in deliberate contrast to the Word, who does not "come to be" but simply was (ēn, verses 1–2). Creation "came to be" through him; a man will "come" (verse 6); and finally the Word himself will "become" flesh (verse 14). Rendering it consistently "came to be" keeps the beat audible — the same instinct behind this translation's "and God said… and so it was" in Genesis 1.

The famous punctuation. The words ho gegonen ("that which has come to be") can be read with the end of verse 3 ("…not one thing came to be that has come to be") or with the start of verse 4 ("What has come to be — in him was life…"). The oldest manuscripts carried no punctuation at all, so both were heard in antiquity, and several early church fathers took it with verse 4. Modern critical editions are split; the SBL text shown here prints it at the close of verse 3, and Mister follows suit, as do KJV NIV ASV. It is a genuine fork in the road, and the note is here to say so rather than hide it.

Verse 5 · ἡ σκοτία αὐτὸ οὐ κατέλαβεν hē skotia auto ou katelaben

"has not overcome" / "did not comprehend." The verb katelaben carries two senses at once: to seize, overpower and to grasp, understand. KJV GNV DRB hear the second — "the darkness comprehended it not"; NIV ASV and most moderns hear the first — "has not overcome it." John may well intend both: the darkness neither understood the light nor defeated it. Mister renders "has not overcome it" and flags the double meaning, because a footnote can hold what a single English verb cannot.

Verses 6–8 · Ἐγένετο ἄνθρωπος … εἰς μαρτυρίαν egeneto anthrōpos … eis martyrian

The drumbeat lands on a man. After three verses of the Word who simply was, the verb egeneto — "came to be" — now marks the appearance of a man in history: "there came a man." The hymn drops for a moment into a name and a date.

"witness" / "testify" — martyria, martyreō. This is one of the Gospel's master words, and it enters here. John's whole role is to testify; the text is careful, even insistent, that he "was not the light" but only its witness — a demotion the Gospel keeps making, probably against those in its own day who prized the Baptist too highly. (Note that the Gospel of John never names its own author; every "John" in this book is John the Baptist.)

Verses 9–13 · τέκνα θεοῦ … ἐκ θεοῦ ἐγεννήθησαν tekna theou … ek theou egennēthēsan

What "was coming into the world" (v. 9)? The Greek participle can attach to "the true light" (the light was coming into the world) or to "everyone" (every person coming into the world). KJV takes the second — "every man that cometh into the world"; most moderns take the first, and so does Mister: it is the light that is coming.

"children of God," not "sons" (vv. 12–13). John keeps a careful distinction the whole Gospel through: he reserves "Son" (huios) for Jesus and calls believers "children" (tekna). NIV ASV and this translation keep "children"; KJV GNV blur it with "sons of God." These children are "born not of blood, nor of the will of the flesh, nor of the will of a husband, but of God" — a birth from above. (The Greek is plural, "who were born"; a few Old Latin witnesses read a singular "who was born," turning the line into a statement about the virgin birth — but the plural, about believers, is the text.)

Verse 14 · ὁ λόγος σὰρξ ἐγένετο καὶ ἐσκήνωσεν ho logos sarx egeneto kai eskēnōsen

"became flesh." The drumbeat reaches its shock: the Word who was in the beginning now became — egeneto — flesh. And it is flesh (sarx), the frail human stuff, not "a body" or "a man": the plainest, earthiest word available, chosen on purpose.

"pitched his tent among us." The verb eskēnōsen is built on skēnē, "tent, tabernacle." John says the Word literally "tented" among us — an unmistakable echo of the tabernacle of Exodus, where Jehovah's glory came to dwell in the middle of Israel's camp (the later Hebrew term for that indwelling presence, shekinah, is from the same root as the Hebrew for "tent"). Most versions flatten it — KJV "dwelt among us," NIV "made his dwelling among us." Mister keeps the tent: "pitched his tent among us," so the tabernacle and its glory — the next word — stay in view.

monogenēs — "only," not "only-begotten." The word is mono- ("only") + -genēs (from genos, "kind, kin") — "one of a kind, an only child" — not mono- + gennaō ("to beget"), despite the long "only-begotten" tradition, which entered English through the Latin unigenitus. KJV GNV ASV DRB NWT all read "only-begotten"; NIV reads "the one and only." Mister renders "the only Son" — "only" for monogenēs, "Son" supplied because the verse plainly means the Son — and lets the etymology-driven "begotten," which reads a later doctrine back into the word, go.

"grace and truth." The pair charis kai alētheia is almost certainly the Greek for a famous Hebrew pair — chesed ve-emet, "steadfast love and faithfulness" — the words Jehovah speaks over himself to Moses in Exodus 34:6. Held here, it sets a trap that verse 17 will spring.

Verses 15–17 · χάριν ἀντὶ χάριτος … διὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ charin anti charitos … dia Iēsou Christou

John breaks in (v. 15). The Baptist's voice interrupts the hymn — the parenthesis is his — to say the thing he keeps saying: the one who comes after me (born later) ranks ahead of me, "because he was before me" (existed first). Later in time, first in rank and in being.

"grace upon grace" (v. 16). Charin anti charitos: anti can mean "in place of" (one grace replacing another) or "upon, in return for" (grace heaped on grace). Most render it "grace upon grace" and so does Mister.

Moses and the trap sprung (v. 17). "The law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ." This is not law against grace as enemies — it is the chesed ve-emet of Sinai (verse 14), now arriving in a person. And here, for the first time, the name: Jesus Christ. "Christ" (Christos) is Greek for "anointed," the Messiah — a title, not a surname, and the note flags it as such the first time it lands.

Verse 18 · μονογενὴς θεὸς ὁ ὢν εἰς τὸν κόλπον τοῦ πατρός monogenēs theos ho ōn eis ton kolpon tou patros

"No one has ever seen God" echoes Exodus 33:20 — no one sees God and lives. What follows is one of the sharpest manuscript decisions in the Gospel, and exactly the kind of thing the New Testament introduction and its apparatus exist for.

The variant: "only God" or "only Son"? The earliest witnesses read monogenēs theos, "the only God" (or "God the only Son") — this is the text of the papyri P66 and P75 (c. 200), together with Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus. The later Byzantine majority, and the King James tradition, read ho monogenēs huios, "the only Son." This split is precisely why NIV reads "the one and only Son, who is himself God" while KJV GNV ASV DRB read "the only begotten Son." Mister follows the early papyri: "the only God, who is at the Father's side." The harder, older reading is the one the manuscripts push us toward.

"has made him known" — exēgēsato, the root of "exegesis." The Son "explains, narrates" the God no one has seen. It is a quiet claim about the whole book to follow: the Gospel is that explanation.

Verses 19–23 · οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι … φωνὴ βοῶντος ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ hoi Ioudaioi … phōnē boōntos en tē erēmō

"the Judeans." John's term hoi Ioudaioi can mean "the Jews" broadly or "the Judean authorities" specifically — here, the temple establishment in Jerusalem sending its priests and Levites. The blanket rendering "the Jews," carried by KJV and most versions, has done real historical harm, read across later centuries as a charge against a whole people. Mister renders "the Judeans" where the context is plainly the authorities, and says why — the same plainness this translation brought to the curse-of-Ham verses in Genesis 9.

"I am not…" The delegation offers John three end-time roles and he refuses all three: the Messiah; Elijah, expected to return before the day of Jehovah (Malachi 4:5); and the Prophet like Moses (Deuteronomy 18:15). He claims only to be a voice — quoting Isaiah 40:3, "make straight the way of the Lord." Not the Word; a voice for it.

Verses 24–28 · τὸν ἱμάντα τοῦ ὑποδήματος … ἐν Βηθανίᾳ πέραν τοῦ Ἰορδάνου ton himanta tou hypodēmatos … en Bēthania peran tou Iordanou

"whose sandal-strap I am not worthy to untie." Untying a master's sandal was a slave's job; John ranks himself below a slave to the one coming after him. Every version keeps the image.

"Bethany beyond the Jordan." Not the Bethany near Jerusalem (a different town two miles from the city) — a place east of the Jordan whose exact site was already lost early: Origen, in the third century, said he could not find a "Bethany" there and proposed reading "Bethabara" instead, a variant that entered some manuscripts (and the KJV). Mister keeps "Bethany beyond the Jordan," the reading of the earliest witnesses, and the map marks the site as undetermined rather than guess a pin.

Verses 29–34 · ὁ ἀμνὸς τοῦ θεοῦ … ὁ ἐκλεκτὸς τοῦ θεοῦ ho amnos tou theou … ho eklektos tou theou

"the Lamb of God." The image gathers several at once: the Passover lamb of Exodus 12, whose blood spared Israel; the lamb "led to the slaughter" of Isaiah 53; and, some hear, the ram God provided in place of Isaac at the binding (Genesis 22, where Abraham says "God will provide the lamb"). "Who takes away the sin of the world" widens the lamb's work from one nation to all.

"Look —" The Baptist's word here, and again in verse 36 and verse 47, is ide, "behold, look." This translation renders the Hebrew hineh as "Look —" all the way through Genesis; ide is its natural Greek heir, so Mister keeps "Look —" here, and the pointing gesture of the Old Testament carries straight into the New.

"the Chosen One of God" (v. 34) — another variant. The earliest text read here, "the Chosen One" (ho eklektos, echoing the "chosen servant" of Isaiah 42:1); the majority of later manuscripts read "the Son of God" (ho huios). Mister follows the early reading — "the Chosen One of God" — and notes the familiar "Son of God" of KJV and the majority. As at verse 18, the older reading and the later reading are both shown, with their pedigrees.

Verses 35–42 · Ῥαββί … Μεσσίαν … Κηφᾶς Rhabbi … Messian … Kēphas

The evangelist as translator. Three times in a few verses the Gospel stops to render a word for its Greek readers: "Rabbi (which means Teacher)," "the Messiah (which means Christ)," "Cephas (which is translated Peter)." The writer is doing, inside his own text, exactly what this project does in the margin — catching a word in the source tongue and handing across its meaning.

"the tenth hour" — about four in the afternoon, counting from dawn. It is the kind of detail a man remembers because he was there.

"you will be called Cephas." Aramaic kepha means "rock"; its Greek rendering is Petros. Jesus renames Simon on sight — a God-given name-change for a destiny, in the exact tradition of Abram becoming Abraham and (as Genesis will soon reach) Jacob becoming Israel. This translation surfaced every Hebrew name-pun in Genesis; here the Gospel hands us the pun already translated, and we simply keep both halves — "Cephas … Peter."

Verses 43–46 · Ἐκ Ναζαρὲτ δύναταί τι ἀγαθὸν εἶναι; ek Nazaret dynatai ti agathon einai?

"Follow me." The first direct call in this Gospel — two words, to Philip.

"Can anything good come out of Nazareth?" Nazareth was an obscure Galilean village, never named in the Old Testament, and Nathanael's snobbery is honest and unedited — the Gospel leaves it standing. Philip does not argue back; he answers with an invitation this chapter has already used once (verse 39): "Come and see." Behind it stands the larger claim — that Jesus is "the one Moses wrote about in the Law, and the prophets" — the whole Old Testament pointing forward.

Verses 47–51 · Ἰσραηλίτης ἐν ᾧ δόλος οὐκ ἔστιν … τοὺς ἀγγέλους … ἀναβαίνοντας καὶ καταβαίνοντας Israēlitēs en hō dolos ouk estin … tous angelous … anabainontas kai katabainontas

"an Israelite in whom there is no deceit." The word for deceit is dolos, "guile," and it is chosen with care. The nation "Israel" is named for Jacob — and Jacob earned that name after a lifetime of guile: it is the very word the Greek Genesis puts in Isaac's mouth — "your brother came with deceit and took your blessing" (Genesis 27:35). Jesus sees in Nathanael a true son of Israel without Jacob's guile — a Jacob undeceived. (Genesis 27 is still ahead in this project; when it lands, this becomes a live cross-reference.)

Jacob's ladder, and the whole chapter's key (v. 51). "You will see heaven opened, and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man" quotes Jacob's dream at Bethel, where angels go up and down a stairway between earth and heaven and Jacob wakes to say "this is the gate of heaven" (Genesis 28:12). Jesus puts himself where the ladder stood: the Son of Man — his own favored title, from Daniel 7:13, and its first appearance here — is now the meeting-place of heaven and earth, the true Bethel. That a chapter which opened "In the beginning" should close on Jacob's ladder is the Gospel telling you how to read it: Genesis, all the way down. (Genesis 28 is also still ahead; the cross-reference waits for it.)

"Amen, amen." Jesus' solemn double amēn — his signature formula, unique to this Gospel in its doubled form. KJV renders it "Verily, verily," NIV "Very truly."Mister keeps "Amen, amen," because amen is itself a Hebrew word the Greek simply carried over untranslated — a loanword all the way down, best left as it stands.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation sides with the literal shelf (ASV, and the KJV where it is plain): "children of God" not "sons," keeping the evangelist's own Aramaic glosses ("Rabbi … Teacher," "Messiah … Christ," "Cephas … Peter"), and keeping "Amen, amen" as the loanword it is.

Where it follows the earliest manuscripts over the King James tradition: "the only God" at verse 18 and "the Chosen One of God" at verse 34 — both on the strength of the papyri P66 and P75 and the great fourth-century codices, exactly the witnesses the New Testament introduction sets out.

Where it goes its own way: "the Word was divine" for the article-less theos of 1:1 (the qualitative reading — see the note and the post on this verse), "pitched his tent" for eskēnōsen (holding the tabernacle echo), "the only Son" for monogenēs without "begotten," "the Judeans" where the authorities are meant, and "Look —" for ide, carried straight over from the hineh of Genesis.

Echoes planted. "In the beginning" is paid now against Genesis 1:1; light-in-the-darkness against Genesis 1:3–4; the Spirit descending against Genesis 1:2. Two more are planted and wait for Genesis to reach them: Nathanael as a Jacob "without deceit" (Genesis 27:35), and the angels ascending and descending on the Son of Man — Jacob's ladder (Genesis 28:12).

Next installment: John 2 — the wedding at Cana, water into wine, and the first of the signs.

John 2 — The Mister Translation

1
Καὶ τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ γάμος ἐγένετο ἐν Κανὰ τῆς Γαλιλαίας, καὶ ἦν ἡ μήτηρ τοῦ Ἰησοῦ ἐκεῖ·
And on the third day there was a wedding at Cana of Galilee, and the mother of Jesus was there;note
2
ἐκλήθη δὲ καὶ ὁ Ἰησοῦς καὶ οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ εἰς τὸν γάμον.
and Jesus too was invited to the wedding, with his disciples.note
3
καὶ ὑστερήσαντος οἴνου λέγει ἡ μήτηρ τοῦ Ἰησοῦ πρὸς αὐτόν· Οἶνον οὐκ ἔχουσιν.
And when the wine ran short, the mother of Jesus said to him, "They have no wine."note
4
καὶ λέγει αὐτῇ ὁ Ἰησοῦς· Τί ἐμοὶ καὶ σοί, γύναι; οὔπω ἥκει ἡ ὥρα μου.
And Jesus said to her, "Woman, what is that to me and to you? My hour has not yet come."note
5
λέγει ἡ μήτηρ αὐτοῦ τοῖς διακόνοις· Ὅ τι ἂν λέγῃ ὑμῖν ποιήσατε.
His mother said to the servants, "Whatever he tells you, do it."note
6
ἦσαν δὲ ἐκεῖ λίθιναι ὑδρίαι ἓξ κατὰ τὸν καθαρισμὸν τῶν Ἰουδαίων κείμεναι, χωροῦσαι ἀνὰ μετρητὰς δύο ἢ τρεῖς.
Now six stone water-jars were standing there, for the purification of the Jews, each holding two or three measures.note
7
λέγει αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς· Γεμίσατε τὰς ὑδρίας ὕδατος· καὶ ἐγέμισαν αὐτὰς ἕως ἄνω.
Jesus said to them, "Fill the jars with water." And they filled them up to the brim.note
8
καὶ λέγει αὐτοῖς· Ἀντλήσατε νῦν καὶ φέρετε τῷ ἀρχιτρικλίνῳ· οἱ δὲ ἤνεγκαν.
And he said to them, "Now draw some out, and take it to the master of the feast." And they took it.note
9
ὡς δὲ ἐγεύσατο ὁ ἀρχιτρίκλινος τὸ ὕδωρ οἶνον γεγενημένον, καὶ οὐκ ᾔδει πόθεν ἐστίν, οἱ δὲ διάκονοι ᾔδεισαν οἱ ἠντληκότες τὸ ὕδωρ, φωνεῖ τὸν νυμφίον ὁ ἀρχιτρίκλινος
When the master of the feast tasted the water now become wine — and he did not know where it had come from, though the servants who had drawn the water knew — the master of the feast called the bridegroomnote
10
καὶ λέγει αὐτῷ· Πᾶς ἄνθρωπος πρῶτον τὸν καλὸν οἶνον τίθησιν, καὶ ὅταν μεθυσθῶσιν τὸν ἐλάσσω· σὺ τετήρηκας τὸν καλὸν οἶνον ἕως ἄρτι.
and said to him, "Everyone sets out the good wine first, and the lesser wine once they have drunk freely. You have kept the good wine until now."note
11
ταύτην ἐποίησεν ἀρχὴν τῶν σημείων ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐν Κανὰ τῆς Γαλιλαίας καὶ ἐφανέρωσεν τὴν δόξαν αὐτοῦ, καὶ ἐπίστευσαν εἰς αὐτὸν οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ.
This, the beginning of the signs, Jesus did at Cana of Galilee, and revealed his glory; and his disciples believed in him.note
12
Μετὰ τοῦτο κατέβη εἰς Καφαρναοὺμ αὐτὸς καὶ ἡ μήτηρ αὐτοῦ καὶ οἱ ἀδελφοὶ καὶ οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ, καὶ ἐκεῖ ἔμειναν οὐ πολλὰς ἡμέρας.
After this he went down to Capernaum — he and his mother and his brothers and his disciples — and they stayed there a few days.note
13
Καὶ ἐγγὺς ἦν τὸ πάσχα τῶν Ἰουδαίων, καὶ ἀνέβη εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα ὁ Ἰησοῦς.
And the Passover of the Jews was near, and Jesus went up to Jerusalem.note
14
καὶ εὗρεν ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ τοὺς πωλοῦντας βόας καὶ πρόβατα καὶ περιστερὰς καὶ τοὺς κερματιστὰς καθημένους,
And in the temple courts he found the sellers of cattle and sheep and doves, and the money-changers at their seats;note
15
καὶ ποιήσας φραγέλλιον ἐκ σχοινίων πάντας ἐξέβαλεν ἐκ τοῦ ἱεροῦ τά τε πρόβατα καὶ τοὺς βόας, καὶ τῶν κολλυβιστῶν ἐξέχεεν τὰ κέρματα καὶ τὰς τραπέζας ἀνέστρεψεν,
and he made a whip of cords and drove them all out of the temple courts, the sheep and the cattle too; and he poured out the money-changers' coins and overturned the tables;note
16
καὶ τοῖς τὰς περιστερὰς πωλοῦσιν εἶπεν· Ἄρατε ταῦτα ἐντεῦθεν, μὴ ποιεῖτε τὸν οἶκον τοῦ πατρός μου οἶκον ἐμπορίου.
and to the sellers of the doves he said, "Take these things away from here. Stop making my Father's house a house of trade."note
17
ἐμνήσθησαν οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ ὅτι γεγραμμένον ἐστίν· Ὁ ζῆλος τοῦ οἴκου σου καταφάγεταί με.
His disciples remembered that it is written: "Zeal for your house will consume me."note
18
ἀπεκρίθησαν οὖν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι καὶ εἶπαν αὐτῷ· Τί σημεῖον δεικνύεις ἡμῖν, ὅτι ταῦτα ποιεῖς;
Then the Jews answered him, "What sign do you show us, that you do these things?"note
19
ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς· Λύσατε τὸν ναὸν τοῦτον καὶ ἐν τρισὶν ἡμέραις ἐγερῶ αὐτόν.
Jesus answered them, "Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up."note
20
εἶπαν οὖν οἱ Ἰουδαῖοι· Τεσσεράκοντα καὶ ἓξ ἔτεσιν οἰκοδομήθη ὁ ναὸς οὗτος, καὶ σὺ ἐν τρισὶν ἡμέραις ἐγερεῖς αὐτόν;
Then the Jews said, "Forty-six years this temple has been under building — and you will raise it up in three days?"note
21
ἐκεῖνος δὲ ἔλεγεν περὶ τοῦ ναοῦ τοῦ σώματος αὐτοῦ.
But he was speaking of the temple of his body.note
22
ὅτε οὖν ἠγέρθη ἐκ νεκρῶν, ἐμνήσθησαν οἱ μαθηταὶ αὐτοῦ ὅτι τοῦτο ἔλεγεν, καὶ ἐπίστευσαν τῇ γραφῇ καὶ τῷ λόγῳ ὃν εἶπεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς.
So when he was raised from the dead, his disciples remembered that he had said this; and they believed the scripture, and the word that Jesus had spoken.note
23
Ὡς δὲ ἦν ἐν τοῖς Ἱεροσολύμοις ἐν τῷ πάσχα ἐν τῇ ἑορτῇ, πολλοὶ ἐπίστευσαν εἰς τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ, θεωροῦντες αὐτοῦ τὰ σημεῖα ἃ ἐποίει·
Now while he was in Jerusalem at the Passover, at the festival, many believed in his name, watching the signs he was doing.note
24
αὐτὸς δὲ Ἰησοῦς οὐκ ἐπίστευεν αὑτὸν αὐτοῖς διὰ τὸ αὐτὸν γινώσκειν πάντας,
But Jesus himself did not entrust himself to them, because he knew them all,note
25
καὶ ὅτι οὐ χρείαν εἶχεν ἵνα τις μαρτυρήσῃ περὶ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου, αὐτὸς γὰρ ἐγίνωσκεν τί ἦν ἐν τῷ ἀνθρώπῳ.
and because he had no need for anyone to bear witness about the human; for he himself knew what was in the human.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. The Greek shown is the SBL Greek New Testament (ed. Michael W. Holmes, Society of Biblical Literature, 2010), a modern critical text in the Alexandrian tradition; quotations from the copyrighted English versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison, and the KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims, and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–5 · Τί ἐμοὶ καὶ σοί, γύναι; ti emoi kai soi, gynai?

"On the third day." Count the days from chapter 1 and something appears: the Baptist's testimony, then "the next day" three times (1:29, 35, 43), then "on the third day" — a running week, and at its end, a wedding. Many readers hear a deliberate echo of the creation week: the Gospel that opened "In the beginning" walks its own seven days and ends them, as Genesis ends its second chapter, with a marriage. (A structural reading, not a doctrine — reported because the day-count is really there.) The mother of Jesus is present, and stays unnamed: John's Gospel never once says "Mary" — she is "the mother of Jesus" here and "woman… your mother" at the cross (19:26-27), the two scenes framing the whole story.

"Woman, what is that to me and to you?" The idiom first: ti emoi kai soi is Hebrew wearing Greek clothes — mah-li valakh (Judges 11:12; 2 Samuel 16:10), "what business is this of ours?" — distance, not disrespect. And "Woman" (gynai) was a courteous address in Greek — Jesus uses the same word tenderly from the cross (19:26). But the shelf shows how hard the line is to carry into English: KJV "Woman, what have I to do with thee?" (harsher than the Greek), Douay "what is that to me and to thee?" (closest, and the reading kept here), NIV "why do you involve me?", TLB famously overcorrected to "I can't help you now" — and his mother, who hears no refusal at all, simply turns to the servants: "Whatever he tells you, do it" — her last recorded words in scripture. "My hour has not yet come": the first tick of John's great clock — hōra, the hour of cross-and-glory, will sound through the whole Gospel until "the hour has come" (12:23).

Verses 6–12 · ἀρχὴν τῶν σημείων archēn tōn sēmeiōn

Six stone jars, and the arithmetic of abundance. Stone, because stone vessels do not contract ritual impurity (a detail archaeology confirms everywhere in first-century Galilee) — these are jars "for the purification of the Jews," holding "two or three measures" each (metrētēs, roughly 39 liters; KJV charmingly reads "two or three firkins apiece"). Do the sum: something like five to seven hundred bottles of wine, for one village wedding, after the guests have already "drunk freely" — and not table wine: "you have kept the good wine until now." The purification water becomes celebration wine, in industrial quantity; the sign is not a conjuring trick but a statement about what has arrived. The steward's line to the bridegroom is the Gospel's theology in one sentence, spoken by a man who has no idea what he is saying.

"The beginning of the signs." John never says "miracle": his word is sēmeion, sign — a deed that points past itself — and he numbers Cana "the beginning" of a series that structures the Gospel's whole first half (seven signs, by the usual count). The reader of this site has met the root before, in the other John: "he made it known in signs" (Revelation 1:1) — sign-language is this author's declared method at both ends of the shelf. "And revealed his glory" — the glory the Prologue announced (1:14) — "and his disciples believed in him." Then a quiet travel verse with a loaded phrase: down to Capernaum, "he and his mother and his brothers" — and on those brothers the traditions divide: full siblings, children of Mary (the plain reading, held by most Protestants); sons of Joseph by a former marriage (the Eastern reading, as old as the second century); cousins (the Western reading behind Douay's tradition, argued by Jerome to protect Mary's perpetual virginity). The word says brothers; what the word covers, the church has argued for eighteen centuries. Reported, not settled.

Verses 13–17 · μὴ ποιεῖτε τὸν οἶκον τοῦ πατρός μου οἶκον ἐμπορίου mē poieite ton oikon tou patros mou oikon emporiou

The cleansing — at the beginning? Here is the Gospels' most famous chronology puzzle: Matthew, Mark and Luke set the temple cleansing in the last week of Jesus' life, the act that triggers the end; John sets it at the very start, first Passover, chapter 2. Readings with pedigrees: two cleansings, one early and one late (the traditional harmonization, held from Augustine to many moderns); or one event, placed theologically — John moving it forward as the programmatic opening of a ministry whose whole story is the replacement of the temple (most modern scholarship). This translation reports both and notes what is at stake in each: harmonizers preserve every text's clock; the one-event reading takes John's arrangement as meaning rather than memory. Shown, not sanded.

The scene itself. A whip of cords (KJV "a scourge of small cords") — made, the Greek implies, on the spot, and aimed at driving the animals; the coins poured out, the tables overturned, and the dove-sellers — the poor folk's traders — told, not whipped: "Take these things away." John's version of the word differs from the synoptics' famous "den of robbers": here it is "stop making my Father's house a house of trade" (KJV "house of merchandise," NIV "a market!") — the charge is not extortion but commerce as such, in the courts built for prayer. The disciples remember Psalm 69: "Zeal for your house will consume me" — and the psalm's verb is future tense, and the psalm is the passion-psalm the New Testament quotes more than almost any other: the memory is already pointing at the cross. (A note on "the Jews" in this Gospel, said once and meant throughout: in John the phrase most often means the Judean authorities — the whole cast of this Gospel, its author included, is Jewish, and this translation renders the Greek as it stands while flagging the idiom here.)

Verses 18–22 · λύσατε τὸν ναὸν τοῦτον lysate ton naon touton

Two temple-words, one riddle. Greek has a word for the whole temple complex — hieron, the precinct with its courts, where the traders sat (vv 14-15, "temple courts" here) — and a word for the sanctuary-house itself: naos. When Jesus answers the demand for a sign, he switches words: "Destroy this naos, and in three days I will raise it up." His hearers take the building; John glosses the riddle: "he was speaking of the naos of his body" — the first full example of this Gospel's signature device, the misunderstanding that carries the meaning (Nicodemus and the Samaritan woman are next). The Prologue said it already, in tent-language: the Word "tabernacled among us" (1:14) — the dwelling of God is a person now; Revelation will finish the thought ("I saw no temple in the city," 21:22).

"Forty-six years" — and for once, a number you can put on a calendar: Josephus dates Herod's reconstruction of the temple from the eighteenth year of his reign, 20/19 BC; forty-six years of building lands this Passover at about AD 27/28 — one of the very few countable dates in the Gospels, and a peg for the whole ministry (see the site's Chronology page). The retort itself misunderstands grandly; and John adds the note that makes his whole book's method explicit: "when he was raised from the dead, his disciples remembered… and they believed the scripture, and the word that Jesus had spoken" — this Gospel is written from after, by memory that only made sense in reverse.

Verses 23–25 · αὐτὸς γὰρ ἐγίνωσκεν τί ἦν ἐν τῷ ἀνθρώπῳ autos gar eginōsken ti ēn en tō anthrōpō

The wordplay English can just barely keep. "Many believed in his name… but Jesus did not entrust himself to them" — in Greek it is the same verb twice (episteusan… ouk episteuen): they trusted him; he did not trust them back. Sign-faith is real but thin — the crowd believes what it watches, and Jesus knows exactly how far that goes. Then the chapter's last line, rendered here with a word this project's readers know from the other Testament: "he himself knew what was in the human" (anthrōpos — generic humanity, exactly the register of Genesis's adam; KJV "he knew what was in man"). The claim is quietly enormous: reading what is in the human heart is, in Genesis, God's own work — "Jehovah saw… every inclination of the thoughts of their heart" (6:5). The next verse of the Gospel (3:1) opens on "a human of the Pharisees, named Nicodemus" — the first specimen brought to the one who knows what is in the human.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation sides with the literal shelf: "what is that to me and to you?" (with Douay, against KJV's harsher idiom and the paraphrases), "house of trade" plain, "measures" for metrētēs (the note does the arithmetic; KJV's "firkins" saluted), and the same-verb wordplay of verses 23-24 kept visible ("believed… did not entrust himself").

Where it goes its own way: "the human" for the generic anthrōpos of verse 25 — the register of Genesis's adam, carried across Testaments — "temple courts" vs "temple" to keep the hieron/naos switch audible, and the cleansing-placement question reported in full rather than harmonized silently.

Echoes paid and planted. Paid: "the beginning of the signs" speaks the method Revelation declared — "he made it known in signs" (Rev 1:1); "the naos of his body" completes the Prologue's tent — the Word who "tabernacled among us" (1:14); the third day closes the week of days begun at (1:29–43); and "he knew what was in the human" is the heart-reading that was God's own work before the flood (6:5). Planted: the hour (12:23; 13:1; 17:1); the three Passovers that give the ministry its length; the brothers "who did not believe in him" (7:5); Psalm 69, waiting at the cross; and Capernaum, the ministry's base camp, entered here by a travel-verse.

Next installment in this book: John 3 — Nicodemus comes by night: "born again," or "born from above" — the Greek means both at once — and the Bible's most quoted sentence.

2 John — The Mister Translation

1
Ὁ πρεσβύτερος ἐκλεκτῇ κυρίᾳ καὶ τοῖς τέκνοις αὐτῆς, οὓς ἐγὼ ἀγαπῶ ἐν ἀληθείᾳ, καὶ οὐκ ἐγὼ μόνος ἀλλὰ καὶ πάντες οἱ ἐγνωκότες τὴν ἀλήθειαν,
The elder — to a chosen lady and her children, whom I love in truth; and not I only, but also all who have come to know the truth,note
2
διὰ τὴν ἀλήθειαν τὴν μένουσαν ἐν ἡμῖν, καὶ μεθʼ ἡμῶν ἔσται εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα·
because of the truth that remains in us — and it will be with us forever.note
3
ἔσται μεθʼ ἡμῶν χάρις ἔλεος εἰρήνη παρὰ θεοῦ πατρός, καὶ παρὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ τοῦ υἱοῦ τοῦ πατρός, ἐν ἀληθείᾳ καὶ ἀγάπῃ.
Grace, mercy, peace will be with us from God the Father, and from Jesus Christ the Son of the Father, in truth and love.note
4
Ἐχάρην λίαν ὅτι εὕρηκα ἐκ τῶν τέκνων σου περιπατοῦντας ἐν ἀληθείᾳ, καθὼς ἐντολὴν ἐλάβομεν παρὰ τοῦ πατρός.
I rejoiced greatly that I have found some of your children walking in truth, just as we received commandment from the Father.note
5
καὶ νῦν ἐρωτῶ σε, κυρία, οὐχ ὡς ἐντολὴν καινὴν γράφων σοι ἀλλὰ ἣν εἴχομεν ἀπʼ ἀρχῆς, ἵνα ἀγαπῶμεν ἀλλήλους.
And now I ask you, lady — not as though writing you a new commandment, but the one we have had from the beginning — that we love one another.note
6
καὶ αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ ἀγάπη, ἵνα περιπατῶμεν κατὰ τὰς ἐντολὰς αὐτοῦ· αὕτη ἡ ἐντολή ἐστιν, καθὼς ἠκούσατε ἀπʼ ἀρχῆς, ἵνα ἐν αὐτῇ περιπατῆτε.
And this is love: that we walk according to his commandments. This is the commandment, just as you heard from the beginning: that you walk in it.note
7
ὅτι πολλοὶ πλάνοι ἐξῆλθον εἰς τὸν κόσμον, οἱ μὴ ὁμολογοῦντες Ἰησοῦν Χριστὸν ἐρχόμενον ἐν σαρκί· οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ πλάνος καὶ ὁ ἀντίχριστος.
Because many deceivers have gone out into the world — those not confessing Jesus Christ coming in flesh. This is the deceiver and the antichrist.note
8
βλέπετε ἑαυτούς, ἵνα μὴ ἀπολέσητε ἃ εἰργασάμεθα ἀλλὰ μισθὸν πλήρη ἀπολάβητε.
Watch yourselves, so that you do not lose what we worked for, but may receive a full wage.note
9
πᾶς ὁ προάγων καὶ μὴ μένων ἐν τῇ διδαχῇ τοῦ Χριστοῦ θεὸν οὐκ ἔχει· ὁ μένων ἐν τῇ διδαχῇ, οὗτος καὶ τὸν πατέρα καὶ τὸν υἱὸν ἔχει.
Everyone who runs ahead and does not remain in the teaching of the Christ does not have God. The one who remains in the teaching — this one has both the Father and the Son.note
10
εἴ τις ἔρχεται πρὸς ὑμᾶς καὶ ταύτην τὴν διδαχὴν οὐ φέρει, μὴ λαμβάνετε αὐτὸν εἰς οἰκίαν καὶ χαίρειν αὐτῷ μὴ λέγετε·
If anyone comes to you and does not bring this teaching, do not take him into a house, and do not say "Welcome" to him;note
11
ὁ λέγων γὰρ αὐτῷ χαίρειν κοινωνεῖ τοῖς ἔργοις αὐτοῦ τοῖς πονηροῖς.
for the one who says "Welcome" to him shares in his wicked works.note
12
Πολλὰ ἔχων ὑμῖν γράφειν οὐκ ἐβουλήθην διὰ χάρτου καὶ μέλανος, ἀλλὰ ἐλπίζω γενέσθαι πρὸς ὑμᾶς καὶ στόμα πρὸς στόμα λαλῆσαι, ἵνα ἡ χαρὰ ὑμῶν πεπληρωμένη ᾖ.
Having many things to write to you, I did not want to do so with paper and ink. But I hope to come to you and to speak mouth to mouth, so that your joy may be made full.note
13
Ἀσπάζεταί σε τὰ τέκνα τῆς ἀδελφῆς σου τῆς ἐκλεκτῆς.
The children of your chosen sister greet you.note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–3 · the elder, and a lady nobody can identify

"The elder." Ho presbyteros — the writer names no name, and this is the shortest self-introduction of any letter in the New Testament. ⚠️ Who he is is a genuinely open question, laid out on the book's introduction page: the same hand as the Gospel and 1 John is nearly universally granted, but whether that hand is John the apostle or a distinct figure called "John the elder" — a man Papias mentions early in the second century — has been argued since antiquity. He writes as someone whose authority is assumed rather than asserted.

⚠️ "A chosen lady." Eklektē kyria, and nobody can say with confidence who she is. Three readings, all old and all defensible. (1) A real woman — a householder with children, wealthy enough to host a congregation, addressed with courtesy. (2) A congregation personified — "the lady" as a church and "her children" as its members, with "your chosen sister" in verse 13 as the sister congregation sending greetings; this reads verse 13 most naturally, since a sister who greets through her children but not herself is odd for a person. (3) A proper name — either "the chosen Kyria" or "to Eklektē, lady," since both words are attested as names. The Greek is genuinely ambiguous and the translation is kept ambiguous with it: a chosen lady, without an article and without a decision. KJV "the elect lady"; ASV the same; the NIV "the lady chosen by God," footnoting the church reading; the NWT "the chosen lady." ⚠️ Note the pronouns do shift — singular in verses 4-5 and 13, plural in 6, 8, 10 and 12 — which is exactly what you would expect if a person is addressed as the head of a household, and also what you would expect if a church is addressed as a woman. The evidence does not resolve.

Truth, five times in three verses. Alētheia saturates the opening: loved in truth, known by all who know the truth, because of the truth that remains, and finally grace and peace "in truth and love." ⚠️ The letter's whole argument is welded here at the start — truth and love are not set against each other and never will be. The people this letter warns about are refused hospitality precisely because of love, not in spite of it.

"Grace, mercy, peace will be with us." ⚠️ Every other letter in the New Testament makes this a wish — "grace to you and peace." Here the verb is a plain future indicative, estai: not may it be but it will be. And it is "with us," not "with you." The greeting has quietly become a statement of fact that includes the writer. KJV renders it "Grace be with you," smoothing to the expected form; the ASV keeps "shall be with us," which is right.

Verses 4–6 · an old commandment, walked in

"Some of your children." Ek tōn teknōn sou — literally "from among your children," a partitive. He rejoiced greatly to find some of them walking in truth. ⚠️ Whether that implies the rest were not, or simply means "those of your children I happened to meet," is not stated, and translations quietly take sides: KJV "of thy children"; the ESV "some of your children," which is what the Greek construction says. Kept as the partitive.

"Not a new commandment, but the one we have had from the beginning." The same move as the Gospel's opening instinct — reach back to archē, the beginning — and the same phrase as 1 John 2:7. ⚠️ There is a deliberate paradox behind it, since Jesus called the love-command new (John 13:34). The word is entolē, a specific charge rather than a body of law. Both are true in their own frame: new in the way he commanded it, old in that it was there from the start. This letter is written to people being offered novelty, so it emphasises the old.

Love defined as a way of walking. Verse 6 does something tighter than English easily shows. This is love: that we walk according to his commandments. This is the commandment: that you walk in it. The definitions chase each other in a circle — love is keeping the commandments; the commandment is to love — and the effect is not vagueness but a knot: neither term can be held without the other. Peripateō, "walk," is the ordinary Jewish idiom for how a life is conducted (the Hebrew halakh behind it gives halakhah). Agapē here is not a feeling being described but a road being walked.

Verses 7–9 · "coming in flesh"

The deceivers. Planoi — from the verb behind our word "planet," the wanderers: people who lead others off the road. They have "gone out into the world," which in this letter's vocabulary means they left the community (1 John 2:19 says so plainly: "they went out from us").

⚠️ "Jesus Christ coming in flesh" — the tense is doing something. The parallel confession in 1 John 4:2 uses a perfect participle, elēlythota: "having come in flesh," a completed arrival with continuing effect. Here the participle is present, erchomenon: "coming." Readings differ and all are serious. Some take the present as timeless — the confession stated as a standing truth rather than a past event. Some read it of the incarnation and the future coming together, so that a denial of the flesh at either end is refused. Some hold it is stylistic and means nothing distinct. This translation keeps the present participle as the Greek has it and does not smooth it to "has come" — which is what most English versions do (KJV "is come"; NIV "has come"; the NWT "coming in the flesh," keeping it).

The antichrist. ⚠️ Worth knowing precisely: the word antichristos occurs in the whole Bible only in these letters — 1 John 2:18, 2:22, 4:3 and here — and never in Revelation, where popular usage puts it. And in John's usage it is not primarily a single end-times figure: 1 John 2:18 says "many antichrists have come," and the definition given is doctrinal — whoever denies the Son. Here the same move, singular and generic: this is the deceiver and the antichrist. The prefix anti- carries both "against" and "in place of," and the ambiguity is probably intended.

⚠️ Verse 8 has a real textual variant, and it changes who is at risk. The manuscripts divide between "what we worked for" (eirgasametha) and "what you worked for" (eirgasasthe), and similarly between "you do not lose" and "we do not lose." The SBL text this translation follows reads we worked and you lose — the missionaries' labour, the readers' loss — which is the harder and better-attested combination. KJV has "those things which we have wrought"; the NIV "what we have worked for." The variant is noted rather than hidden.

"Everyone who runs ahead." Ho proagōn — literally to lead forward, to go out in front. ⚠️ It is almost certainly ironic. These teachers evidently presented themselves as advanced — progressives, in the root sense — and the letter takes their own word and turns it: to run out ahead of the teaching is not to arrive somewhere better but to leave. Set against it is menō, to remain or abide, this literature's favourite verb. The whole contest of the letter is going ahead versus staying put.

Verses 10–11 · the hardest two verses in the letter

What is actually being refused. ⚠️ This passage has been used to justify shunning family, refusing ordinary courtesy, and cutting off anyone judged wrong — so it is worth being exact about what it says. The situation is travelling teachers. In the first century a congregation met in a house, and to receive a teacher into a house was to give him a platform, a base of operations and material support; to greet him publicly was to endorse him. The Greek confirms the setting: "does not bring this teaching" — he is carrying a message, not holding a private opinion. What is refused is sponsorship of a mission, not human decency toward a neighbour, and certainly not family relationships, which are not in view at all.

"Do not say 'Welcome.'" The word is chairein — the standard Greek greeting, literally "rejoice," the equivalent of "hello" at the door and the opening word of a letter. ⚠️ Translating it as a plain greeting ("do not greet him") is defensible and common (KJV "neither bid him God speed"; ESV "give him no greeting"), but in this context it is functioning as a public reception of a visiting teacher rather than a passing hello, and "Welcome" carries that better in English. Note also the verbs are plural here — the instruction is to the community about whom it hosts, not to an individual about whom she may speak to.

"Shares in his wicked works." Koinōnei — from koinōnia, fellowship, partnership; the word for having a share in a business. The logic is not that hospitality is contaminating but that funding is participation. ⚠️ It is a hard line and this translation does not soften it. It is also, in this letter, an application of love rather than an exception to it: verse 5 asked for love, verse 6 defined it as walking in the commandments, and verses 10-11 are what that looks like when someone arrives selling a different Christ. Whether and how the principle transfers to situations the letter is not describing is a question for the reader; the text is addressing a house, a mission, and a purse.

Verses 12–13 · paper, ink, and a face

"With paper and ink." Chartou kai melanos — chartēs is a sheet of papyrus, melan is literally "the black," the ordinary word for ink. It is a small, concrete glimpse of how the New Testament was physically made, and it explains the letter's length as much as anything else: this is roughly one sheet's worth. ⚠️ 2 John has fewer verses (13) than any other book in the Bible — though 3 John, at 14 verses, is very slightly shorter in actual Greek words. Both are about the size of a single papyrus sheet, which is very likely not a coincidence.

"Mouth to mouth." Stoma pros stoma. Almost every English version renders it "face to face," which is the natural idiom and loses the picture; the Greek says mouths. It is the same expression used of God speaking with Moses in the Greek Old Testament (Numbers 12:8). Kept literal here — a man who has just spent eleven verses on what may and may not be said ends by saying that the real thing needs two mouths in one room.

⚠️ "So that your joy may be made full" — or "our." The manuscripts divide, and the difference in Greek is a single letter: humōn (your) against hēmōn (our). The two are pronounced almost identically in later Greek, which is why this pair of words is confused all over the New Testament and turns up in the apparatus of these letters repeatedly. This translation prints the reading of the SBL Greek text it follows — your — and flags the alternative rather than choosing the warmer one. KJV has "that our joy may be full"; the ESV "so that our joy may be complete"; the NWT "that your joy may be made full." Either way it is a joy that needs the other party present to be complete, which is the point of the sentence around it.

And it ends without a name. No personal greetings, no companions listed, no benediction — just the children of a sister, greeting a lady, in a letter signed only "the elder." For a document that is fierce about who may be let in the door, it is remarkably warm, and it is over in thirteen verses.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "a chosen lady" left as ambiguous as the Greek, with all three readings in the note and none adopted; "will be with us" for the indicative estai, not smoothed into the usual wish; "coming in flesh" keeping the present participle rather than the "has come" nearly every version prints; "runs ahead" for proagōn, holding the irony against remain; "shares in" for koinōnei, the partnership word; and "mouth to mouth" for stoma pros stoma, not "face to face."

Echoes paid. "In the beginning" — the same instinct to reach back to archē, here for a commandment rather than a Word; the love-command of John 13:34, called old by a writer addressing people who were being sold something new; and 1 John's antichristos and menō, both at full strength in a letter a fraction of its length.

Echoes planted. The antichrist, a word this literature owns and popular usage has moved elsewhere; the travelling teacher and who pays for him, which 3 John takes up from the opposite side — where the problem is a man who refuses to receive the brothers; and "mouth to mouth," a letter's own admission that a letter is not enough.

Its natural companion is 3 John — the same elder, the same length, the same concern with who gets welcomed — but read from the other direction: there the fault is a man named Diotrephes, who loves being first and puts people out of the church for taking travellers in.

3 John — The Mister Translation

1
Ὁ πρεσβύτερος Γαΐῳ τῷ ἀγαπητῷ, ὃν ἐγὼ ἀγαπῶ ἐν ἀληθείᾳ.
The elder — to Gaius the beloved, whom I love in truth.note
2
Ἀγαπητέ, περὶ πάντων εὔχομαί σε εὐοδοῦσθαι καὶ ὑγιαίνειν, καθὼς εὐοδοῦταί σου ἡ ψυχή.
