Beloved, concerning all things I pray that you may prosper and be in health, just as your soul prospers.note
3
ἐχάρην γὰρ λίαν ἐρχομένων ἀδελφῶν καὶ μαρτυρούντων σου τῇ ἀληθείᾳ, καθὼς σὺ ἐν ἀληθείᾳ περιπατεῖς.
For I rejoiced greatly when brothers came and testified to your truth — how you walk in truth.note
4
μειζοτέραν τούτων οὐκ ἔχω χαράν ἵνα ἀκούω τὰ ἐμὰ τέκνα ἐν τῇ ἀληθείᾳ περιπατοῦντα.
I have no greater joy than this: to hear that my children are walking in the truth.note
5
Ἀγαπητέ, πιστὸν ποιεῖς ὃ ἐὰν ἐργάσῃ εἰς τοὺς ἀδελφοὺς καὶ τοῦτο ξένους,
Beloved, you act faithfully in whatever you do for the brothers — and these, strangers —note
6
οἳ ἐμαρτύρησάν σου τῇ ἀγάπῃ ἐνώπιον ἐκκλησίας, οὓς καλῶς ποιήσεις προπέμψας ἀξίως τοῦ θεοῦ·
who have testified to your love before the assembly. You will do well to send them on their way worthily of God;note
7
ὑπὲρ γὰρ τοῦ ὀνόματος ἐξῆλθον μηδὲν λαμβάνοντες ἀπὸ τῶν ἐθνικῶν.
for they went out for the sake of the Name, taking nothing from the outsiders.note
8
ἡμεῖς οὖν ὀφείλομεν ὑπολαμβάνειν τοὺς τοιούτους, ἵνα συνεργοὶ γινώμεθα τῇ ἀληθείᾳ.
We ourselves, then, ought to support such people, so that we may become fellow workers with the truth.note
9
Ἔγραψά τι τῇ ἐκκλησίᾳ· ἀλλʼ ὁ φιλοπρωτεύων αὐτῶν Διοτρέφης οὐκ ἐπιδέχεται ἡμᾶς.
I wrote something to the assembly, but Diotrephes, who loves to be first among them, does not receive us.note
10
διὰ τοῦτο, ἐὰν ἔλθω, ὑπομνήσω αὐτοῦ τὰ ἔργα ἃ ποιεῖ, λόγοις πονηροῖς φλυαρῶν ἡμᾶς, καὶ μὴ ἀρκούμενος ἐπὶ τούτοις οὔτε αὐτὸς ἐπιδέχεται τοὺς ἀδελφοὺς καὶ τοὺς βουλομένους κωλύει καὶ ἐκ τῆς ἐκκλησίας ἐκβάλλει.
For this reason, if I come, I will bring to mind the works he does, prating against us with wicked words. And not content with these, he neither receives the brothers himself, and those who want to he prevents, and throws them out of the assembly.note
11
Ἀγαπητέ, μὴ μιμοῦ τὸ κακὸν ἀλλὰ τὸ ἀγαθόν. ὁ ἀγαθοποιῶν ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ ἐστιν· ὁ κακοποιῶν οὐχ ἑώρακεν τὸν θεόν.
Beloved, do not imitate the bad but the good. The one who does good is from God; the one who does bad has not seen God.note
12
Δημητρίῳ μεμαρτύρηται ὑπὸ πάντων καὶ ὑπὸ αὐτῆς τῆς ἀληθείας· καὶ ἡμεῖς δὲ μαρτυροῦμεν, καὶ οἶδας ὅτι ἡ μαρτυρία ἡμῶν ἀληθής ἐστιν.
Demetrius has been testified to by all, and by the truth itself. And we also testify, and you know that our testimony is true.note
13
Πολλὰ εἶχον γράψαι σοι, ἀλλʼ οὐ θέλω διὰ μέλανος καὶ καλάμου σοι γράφειν·
I had many things to write to you, but I do not want to write to you with ink and reed pen.note
14
ἐλπίζω δὲ εὐθέως σε ἰδεῖν, καὶ στόμα πρὸς στόμα λαλήσομεν.
But I hope to see you soon, and we will speak mouth to mouth.note
15
Εἰρήνη σοι. ἀσπάζονταί σε οἱ φίλοι. ἀσπάζου τοὺς φίλους κατʼ ὄνομα.
Peace to you. The friends greet you. Greet the friends by name.note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–4 · the elder, to a friend

The same signature, and now a name to go with it. "The elder" opens this letter exactly as it opened 2 John — no personal name, authority assumed rather than asserted — but here the recipient is a single, ordinary, nameable man: Gaius. ⚠️ It is one of the commonest names in the Roman world (three or four other people called Gaius appear in the New Testament, and there is no way to prove any of them is this one), which is part of the point: 3 John is the most private document in the Bible, a note from one church leader to one Christian host about a local quarrel, and it survived. See the book's introduction page for the author question, which is the same open question as 2 John's.

⚠️ "That you may prosper and be in health." Verse 2 is the single most abused verse in these letters, and the honest thing is to say what it is. Euodousthai — literally "to have a good road," to go well — was a standard health-wish in ancient letters, the Greek equivalent of "I hope this finds you well." It is a greeting, not a doctrine. A whole strand of modern preaching has read it as a promise that God wills material prosperity for every believer; the sentence is a personal courtesy at the head of a letter to one friend, and the parallel it draws — as your soul prospers — is a compliment to Gaius's spiritual state, not a formula for anyone's bank balance. KJV "that thou mayest prosper and be in health"; the NWT "that you may be prospering and having good health."

Truth, and walking in it. The word that saturated 2 John is here too — alētheia four times in four verses — and again the test is not assent to it but walking in it (v. 3, v. 4). ⚠️ These two letters are held together by vocabulary this tight on purpose: they are almost certainly a matched pair, sent by the same hand into the same situation, one warning a community to shut a door and the other thanking a man for keeping one open.

"My children." The elder calls Gaius one of his tekna, as he called the lady's community her children in 2 John. Whether Gaius was literally converted by him or is "his child" in the looser sense of belonging to his circle is not said; the affection is the same either way, and "I have no greater joy than this" is as warm a sentence as the New Testament's letters contain.

Verses 5–8 · the exact thing 2 John forbade — commanded

Read this against 2 John 10, deliberately. There the instruction was: a teacher who brings a false Christ gets no house and no welcome. Here the instruction is: receive the travelling brothers, feed them, and send them on their way. ⚠️ The two are not in tension; they are the same principle applied to opposite people. The test was never hospitality-in-the-abstract but what the traveller carries — a corrupt teaching (2 John) or "the Name" (here). Gaius is praised for exactly the act the lady was told to refuse, because the men at his door are the opposite men. This is the clearest place in the New Testament to see that its hospitality rules are about the message, not about the mood.

"And these, strangers." Kai touto xenous — a tight, emphatic phrase: he helps the brothers, and these ones he did not even know. The praise is specifically for hospitality to people who could not repay it and were not his own. Xenos is the guest-stranger, the word behind philoxenia, "love of strangers," which is simply the Greek for hospitality — a whole virtue named for treating the unknown traveller well.

"Send them on their way worthily of God." Propempsas — a semi-technical word in the early church for outfitting a traveller for the next leg: food, money, letters, an escort. ⚠️ It is the material engine of the whole early Christian mission, and it is what verse 8 makes into a theology: to support such people is to "become fellow workers with the truth" — synergoi tē alētheia. KJV "fellowhelpers to the truth"; the ESV "fellow workers for the truth"; the NIV "work together for the truth". The one who never leaves home but funds the one who does is not a spectator to the mission; he is a partner in it. It is the exact positive of 2 John's warning, where funding the wrong teacher made you a partner in his work.

"For the sake of the Name, taking nothing from the outsiders." They travelled as missionaries and refused support from non-believers — so that the message could never be mistaken for a paid enterprise. "The Name" stands alone, without "of Jesus" or "of the Lord" attached, the way a family says the name; and ethnikoi, "the outsiders" (literally the ethnic/gentile ones), here means simply those outside the community, whoever they are. The principle is that the church funds its own mission, precisely so that its preachers owe nothing to anyone they are preaching to.

Verses 9–10 · Diotrephes, who loves to be first

The one genuinely unpleasant portrait in John's letters, and it is of a churchman. Ho philoprōteuōn — "the one who loves to be first," a word coined for the occasion (it appears nowhere else in Greek literature before this) and a perfect one. KJV "who loveth to have the preeminence among them"; the NIV "who loves to be first". Diotrephes is not a heretic; nothing is said about his doctrine. His fault is entirely about place: he wants the top seat, and everything he does follows from that. ⚠️ It is worth sitting with how ordinary and how modern this is. The danger 2 John names is false teaching from outside; the danger 3 John names is ambition inside — and the second may be the harder of the two, because it wears the right clothes.

The escalation is precise. Read verse 10 as the ladder it is: he (1) does not receive us — he rejects the elder's own authority; (2) prates against us with wicked words — phluarōn, to talk nonsense, to babble accusations; (3) will not receive the brothers himself; (4) prevents those who want to; and (5) throws them out of the assembly — ekballei ek tēs ekklēsias, the first excommunication in the New Testament (KJV "casteth them out of the church"; NWT "tries to throw them out"), and it is being done by the wrong man, to the right people, for the wrong reason. A leader is expelling members for the crime of showing hospitality.

"If I come, I will bring to mind the works he does." ⚠️ Notice the restraint. The elder, whose authority is being flouted, does not command Gaius to depose Diotrephes, does not pronounce a curse, does not even write to the church again (his first letter, verse 9, was ignored). He says only that he will address it in person. It is the same instinct as the close of 2 John — I would rather not do this with ink — and it is striking in a man who plainly has the standing to do more. He intends to handle a personal defiance face to face, not by letter.

Verses 11–12 · imitate the good — and Demetrius

The one line of general teaching in the whole letter. "Do not imitate the bad but the good." Everything else is particular — Gaius, the brothers, Diotrephes, Demetrius — and then verse 11 lifts, for a sentence, into the plain Johannine test: the one who does good is from God; the one who does bad has not seen God. ⚠️ It is placed exactly between the bad example (Diotrephes) and the good one (Demetrius), and it tells Gaius which of his neighbours to copy without naming either in the sentence. "Has not seen God" is the same standard as 1 John — conduct reveals origin; you can read where a person comes from by what they do. KJV "he that doeth good is of God"; the ASV the same.

"Testified to by all, and by the truth itself." ⚠️ Demetrius appears from nowhere and vanishes; almost certainly he is the carrier of this very letter, a travelling brother arriving at Gaius's door who needs vouching for — which is precisely the situation the whole letter is about. So the elder vouches, in a rising series: by all who know him, by the truth itself (his life matches the message), and by us — "and you know that our testimony is true." He is handing Gaius exactly the recommendation 2 John's false teachers could never earn.

Verses 13–15 · ink, a reed, and friends by name

Almost the same ending as 2 John, with the tools spelled out differently. 2 John said "paper and ink" (chartēs and melan); here it is "ink and reed" — melan kai kalamos, the black and the reed pen, the sharpened kalamos that actually made the marks. Between the two letters you get the whole kit: the sheet, the ink, and the pen. And the same longing closes both: stoma pros stoma, mouth to mouth — kept literal here as there, against the usual "face to face."

⚠️ "Peace to you. Greet the friends by name." The ending is quietly extraordinary. Hoi philoi — "the friends" — is how this little circle refers to itself: not "the saints," not "the brothers," but the friends, the word Jesus used at John 15:15 ("I have called you friends"). And "greet them by name" — kat' onoma — is the smallest, most human instruction in the New Testament's correspondence: not a blanket greeting to the congregation but Gaius going round, one at a time, saying each person's name. For a letter that has just described a man who threw people out of the assembly, it is the perfect last line: name them, one by one, and leave nobody out.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "prosper and be in health" left as the ordinary ancient health-wish it is, with the note refusing the prosperity-gospel reading; "and these, strangers" for the emphatic kai touto xenous; "send them on their way" for propempsas, the missionary outfitting-word; "fellow workers with the truth" for synergoi tē alētheia; "who loves to be first" for the coined philoprōteuōn; "throws them out of the assembly" kept plain; "mouth to mouth" for stoma pros stoma, not "face to face"; and "the friends… by name" left as literal as the Greek.

Echoes paid. 2 John's refusal of hospitality answered by its exact positive — the same principle protecting a different traveller; "mouth to mouth" and the reluctance to write, from the companion letter; and the Johannine test of 1 John — origin shown by conduct — dropped in for one verse.

Echoes planted. Propempō and the whole economy of the travelling mission that the letters of Paul assume; the friends, a congregation naming itself by Jesus' own word for them; and the uncomfortable permanence of Diotrephes — a man remembered in scripture for exactly one thing, that he loved to be first.

With 3 John the pair is complete: two single-sheet letters from the same elder, one telling a church to close a door against a false teacher, the other thanking a man for holding one open to true ones — and, between them, a churchman who shut the door on the wrong people because he loved the head of the table.

Jude 1 — The Mister Translation

1
Ἰούδας Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ δοῦλος, ἀδελφὸς δὲ Ἰακώβου, τοῖς ἐν θεῷ πατρὶ ἠγαπημένοις καὶ Ἰησοῦ Χριστῷ τετηρημένοις κλητοῖς·
Jude, a slave of Jesus Christ and brother of James, to those who are called — beloved in God the Father, and kept for Jesus Christ:note
2
ἔλεος ὑμῖν καὶ εἰρήνη καὶ ἀγάπη πληθυνθείη.
May mercy and peace and love be multiplied to you.note
3
Ἀγαπητοί, πᾶσαν σπουδὴν ποιούμενος γράφειν ὑμῖν περὶ τῆς κοινῆς ἡμῶν σωτηρίας ἀνάγκην ἔσχον γράψαι ὑμῖν παρακαλῶν ἐπαγωνίζεσθαι τῇ ἅπαξ παραδοθείσῃ τοῖς ἁγίοις πίστει.
Beloved, though I was making every effort to write to you about our common salvation, I found it necessary to write appealing to you to contend for the faith once for all delivered to the holy ones.note
4
παρεισέδυσαν γάρ τινες ἄνθρωποι, οἱ πάλαι προγεγραμμένοι εἰς τοῦτο τὸ κρίμα, ἀσεβεῖς, τὴν τοῦ θεοῦ ἡμῶν χάριτα μετατιθέντες εἰς ἀσέλγειαν καὶ τὸν μόνον δεσπότην καὶ κύριον ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦν Χριστὸν ἀρνούμενοι.
For certain men have crept in unnoticed — those long ago marked out for this condemnation — ungodly ones who turn the grace of our God into licentiousness and deny our only Master and Lord, Jesus Christ.note
5
Ὑπομνῆσαι δὲ ὑμᾶς βούλομαι, εἰδότας ὑμᾶς ἅπαξ πάντα ὅτι Ἰησοῦς λαὸν ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου σώσας τὸ δεύτερον τοὺς μὴ πιστεύσαντας ἀπώλεσεν,
Now I want to remind you, though you know all things once for all, that Jesus, having saved a people out of the land of Egypt, afterward destroyed those who did not believe;note
6
ἀγγέλους τε τοὺς μὴ τηρήσαντας τὴν ἑαυτῶν ἀρχὴν ἀλλὰ ἀπολιπόντας τὸ ἴδιον οἰκητήριον εἰς κρίσιν μεγάλης ἡμέρας δεσμοῖς ἀϊδίοις ὑπὸ ζόφον τετήρηκεν·
and the angels who did not keep their own domain but left their proper dwelling, he has kept in eternal chains under gloom for the judgment of the great day;note
7
ὡς Σόδομα καὶ Γόμορρα καὶ αἱ περὶ αὐτὰς πόλεις, τὸν ὅμοιον τρόπον τούτοις ἐκπορνεύσασαι καὶ ἀπελθοῦσαι ὀπίσω σαρκὸς ἑτέρας, πρόκεινται δεῖγμα πυρὸς αἰωνίου δίκην ὑπέχουσαι.
as Sodom and Gomorrah and the cities around them, which in the same way as these gave themselves over to sexual immorality and went after strange flesh, are set forth as an example, undergoing the punishment of eternal fire.note
8
Ὁμοίως μέντοι καὶ οὗτοι ἐνυπνιαζόμενοι σάρκα μὲν μιαίνουσιν, κυριότητα δὲ ἀθετοῦσιν, δόξας δὲ βλασφημοῦσιν.
Yet in the same way these dreamers also defile the flesh, reject authority, and blaspheme glories.note
9
ὁ δὲ Μιχαὴλ ὁ ἀρχάγγελος, ὅτε τῷ διαβόλῳ διακρινόμενος διελέγετο περὶ τοῦ Μωϋσέως σώματος, οὐκ ἐτόλμησεν κρίσιν ἐπενεγκεῖν βλασφημίας, ἀλλὰ εἶπεν· Ἐπιτιμήσαι σοι κύριος.
But Michael the archangel, when he disputed with the devil and argued about the body of Moses, did not dare bring a blasphemous judgment, but said, "May the Lord rebuke you."note
10
οὗτοι δὲ ὅσα μὲν οὐκ οἴδασιν βλασφημοῦσιν, ὅσα δὲ φυσικῶς ὡς τὰ ἄλογα ζῷα ἐπίστανται, ἐν τούτοις φθείρονται.
But these blaspheme all that they do not understand, and all that they do understand by instinct, like the unreasoning animals — by these very things they are destroyed.note
11
οὐαὶ αὐτοῖς, ὅτι τῇ ὁδῷ τοῦ Κάϊν ἐπορεύθησαν, καὶ τῇ πλάνῃ τοῦ Βαλαὰμ μισθοῦ ἐξεχύθησαν, καὶ τῇ ἀντιλογίᾳ τοῦ Κόρε ἀπώλοντο.
Woe to them! For they went the way of Cain, and for pay they abandoned themselves to the error of Balaam, and in the rebellion of Korah they perished.note
12
οὗτοί εἰσιν οἱ ἐν ταῖς ἀγάπαις ὑμῶν σπιλάδες συνευωχούμενοι, ἀφόβως ἑαυτοὺς ποιμαίνοντες, νεφέλαι ἄνυδροι ὑπὸ ἀνέμων παραφερόμεναι, δένδρα φθινοπωρινὰ ἄκαρπα δὶς ἀποθανόντα ἐκριζωθέντα,
These are the hidden reefs in your love-feasts, feasting with you without fear, shepherding only themselves — waterless clouds carried along by winds, autumn trees without fruit, twice dead, uprooted,note
13
κύματα ἄγρια θαλάσσης ἐπαφρίζοντα τὰς ἑαυτῶν αἰσχύνας, ἀστέρες πλανῆται οἷς ὁ ζόφος τοῦ σκότους εἰς αἰῶνα τετήρηται.
wild waves of the sea foaming out their own shame, wandering stars for whom the gloom of darkness has been kept forever.note
14
Προεφήτευσεν δὲ καὶ τούτοις ἕβδομος ἀπὸ Ἀδὰμ Ἑνὼχ λέγων· Ἰδοὺ ἦλθεν κύριος ἐν ἁγίαις μυριάσιν αὐτοῦ,
And Enoch, the seventh from Adam, also prophesied about these, saying, "Look — the Lord came with his holy myriads,note
15
ποιῆσαι κρίσιν κατὰ πάντων καὶ ἐλέγξαι πάντας τοὺς ἀσεβεῖς περὶ πάντων τῶν ἔργων ἀσεβείας αὐτῶν ὧν ἠσέβησαν καὶ περὶ πάντων τῶν σκληρῶν ὧν ἐλάλησαν κατʼ αὐτοῦ ἁμαρτωλοὶ ἀσεβεῖς.
to execute judgment against all, and to convict every soul of all their works of ungodliness that they committed in ungodliness, and of all the harsh things that ungodly sinners spoke against him."note
16
οὗτοί εἰσιν γογγυσταί, μεμψίμοιροι, κατὰ τὰς ἐπιθυμίας αὐτῶν πορευόμενοι, καὶ τὸ στόμα αὐτῶν λαλεῖ ὑπέρογκα, θαυμάζοντες πρόσωπα ὠφελείας χάριν.
These are grumblers, malcontents, following after their own desires; their mouth speaks swollen words, flattering faces for the sake of advantage.note
17
Ὑμεῖς δέ, ἀγαπητοί, μνήσθητε τῶν ῥημάτων τῶν προειρημένων ὑπὸ τῶν ἀποστόλων τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ·
But you, beloved, remember the words spoken beforehand by the apostles of our Lord Jesus Christ,note
18
ὅτι ἔλεγον ὑμῖν Ἐπʼ ἐσχάτου χρόνου ἔσονται ἐμπαῖκται κατὰ τὰς ἑαυτῶν ἐπιθυμίας πορευόμενοι τῶν ἀσεβειῶν.
how they told you, "In the last time there will be mockers, following after their own ungodly desires."note
19
οὗτοί εἰσιν οἱ ἀποδιορίζοντες, ψυχικοί, πνεῦμα μὴ ἔχοντες.
These are the ones who cause divisions, worldly, not having the Spirit.note
20
ὑμεῖς δέ, ἀγαπητοί, ἐποικοδομοῦντες ἑαυτοὺς τῇ ἁγιωτάτῃ ὑμῶν πίστει, ἐν πνεύματι ἁγίῳ προσευχόμενοι,
But you, beloved, building yourselves up in your most holy faith, praying in the Holy Spirit,note
21
ἑαυτοὺς ἐν ἀγάπῃ θεοῦ τηρήσατε προσδεχόμενοι τὸ ἔλεος τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον.
keep yourselves in the love of God, waiting for the mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, unto eternal life.note
22
καὶ οὓς μὲν ἐλεᾶτε διακρινομένους,
And have mercy on some, who are doubting;note
23
οὓς δὲ σῴζετε ἐκ πυρὸς ἁρπάζοντες, οὓς δὲ ἐλεᾶτε ἐν φόβῳ, μισοῦντες καὶ τὸν ἀπὸ τῆς σαρκὸς ἐσπιλωμένον χιτῶνα.
save others, snatching them out of the fire; and on some have mercy with fear, hating even the garment stained by the flesh.note
24
Τῷ δὲ δυναμένῳ φυλάξαι ὑμᾶς ἀπταίστους καὶ στῆσαι κατενώπιον τῆς δόξης αὐτοῦ ἀμώμους ἐν ἀγαλλιάσει
Now to him who is able to keep you from stumbling, and to make you stand before his glory blameless, with great joy —note
25
μόνῳ θεῷ σωτῆρι ἡμῶν διὰ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν δόξα μεγαλωσύνη κράτος καὶ ἐξουσία πρὸ παντὸς τοῦ αἰῶνος καὶ νῦν καὶ εἰς πάντας τοὺς αἰῶνας· ἀμήν.
to the only God our Savior, through Jesus Christ our Lord, be glory, majesty, dominion, and authority, before all time and now and forever. Amen.note

Translator’s Notes

Verses 1–2 · the brother of James

«Jude, a slave of Jesus Christ and brother of James.» The name is Ioudas — the same Greek name as Judah and Judas; English keeps “Jude” to set this one apart. He introduces himself with striking humility: not “brother of Jesus” but “slave (doulos) of Jesus Christ,” and then “brother of James” — which is exactly how the first readers would place him. James the Just, the leader of the Jerusalem church, was a brother of Jesus (Mark 6:3 lists “James and Joses and Judas and Simon” among the Lord’s brothers). So Jude is, on the plainest reading, another brother of the Lord — who calls himself only his slave, and points not to his blood but to his more famous brother. ⚠ Because both names are common, a few take him for a different Jude; but the brother-of-James signature points to the family of Jesus.

«Mercy and peace and love.» Even a letter this fierce opens on love (agape), and will close on it (v21). The salutation asks not that these be given but that they be multiplied — the same verb the Greek Old Testament uses of a people made to increase.

Verses 3–4 · the faith once for all delivered

«I found it necessary.» Jude meant to write a warm letter “about our common salvation” and had to write a battle-dispatch instead. The thesis is verse 3: contend (epagonizesthai — the wrestling-and-athletics word, our “agonize”) for “the faith once for all (hapax) delivered to the holy ones.” Hapax is the hinge of the letter: the faith was handed over ONCE, complete — not a thing to be added to, updated, or traded away.

«Crept in unnoticed.» Pareisedysan — a rare word for slipping in by a side door. The danger is not outside the church but already inside the love-feast (v12). They “turn the grace of our God into licentiousness” (aselgeia, grace twisted into an excuse for unrestraint) and “deny our only Master (despotes) and Lord, Jesus Christ.” Despotes is the owner of a slave (our “despot”); Jude, the slave of Christ in verse 1, names the intruders as slaves who deny the one who owns them.

Verses 5–7 · three ruins remembered

⚠ «Jesus… saved a people out of Egypt.» A famous variant, and this translation prints the harder reading the SBL text adopts. The oldest and best witnesses — the early papyrus P72 and the great codices among them — read that JESUS saved the people out of Egypt and then destroyed the unbelievers: a startling statement that the pre-incarnate Christ led the Exodus. Other manuscripts read “the Lord,” and a few “God.” It is one of the most theologically loaded textual decisions in the New Testament, and the note keeps the reading the manuscripts best support. KJV “the Lord”; the ESV prints “Jesus” with a footnote.

Three warnings, in order. The wilderness generation, saved out of Egypt and then destroyed for unbelief (Numbers 14) — rescue is no guarantee. The angels who “did not keep their own domain (arche)” but left their dwelling, now held in chains for the judgment of the great day — even angels fell (the reading of Genesis 6 in Jewish tradition, and of the Enoch material Jude will quote in v14). And Sodom and Gomorrah, “set forth as an example (deigma) of eternal fire.” Three ruins from the old story, each a picture of a mercy received and thrown away.

Verses 8–10 · Michael would not — but these do

⚠ Michael and the body of Moses. Verse 9 refers to a story found nowhere in the Old Testament: the archangel Michael disputing with the devil over the corpse of Moses. It comes from a Jewish work outside the canon — by ancient testimony, the lost ending of the Assumption (or Testament) of Moses. Jude uses it as an illustration: even Michael, contending with the devil himself, “did not dare bring a blasphemous judgment” but left the rebuke to God — “May the Lord rebuke you,” the very words the angel of Jehovah speaks to Satan in Zechariah 3:2. The intruders, by contrast, “blaspheme glories” they do not understand. ⚠ That Jude draws an illustration from a non-canonical book does not make that book Scripture; ancient and modern readers take it as Jude reaching for a story his audience knew — as a preacher might cite a familiar legend to land a true point. But it is worth naming plainly, and it sets up the larger case at verse 14.

«By instinct, like the unreasoning animals.» Their knowledge is animal, not spiritual; and the one thing they think they grasp is the very thing that destroys them.

Verses 11–13 · the woe, and the images

The woe triad. Three archetypes of rebellion, each with a name: the way of Cain (Genesis 4 — the first murderer, envy turned to blood); the error of Balaam “for pay” (Numbers 22–24; 31:16 — the prophet who sold his gift for a fee); and the rebellion (antilogia, the “speaking-against”) of Korah, swallowed by the earth for defying God’s appointed leaders (Numbers 16). Greed, false teaching, and revolt against authority — the three charges, laid out as three men.

The images. Jude piles up pictures of promise without substance: hidden reefs (spilades) in the love-feasts, wrecking the ship from below; waterless clouds that promise rain and give none; autumn trees “twice dead” and uprooted, barren when the fruit is due; wild waves foaming out their own shame; and “wandering stars” (asteres planetai — the planets, which drift off the fixed courses sailors steer by), “for whom the gloom of darkness has been kept forever.”

Verses 14–16 · Enoch prophesied

⚠ Jude quotes ENOCH — and the book is not in the Bible. This is the single most-discussed feature of the letter. Verses 14–15 are a direct quotation of 1 Enoch (1:9), a Jewish apocalypse composed in the centuries before Christ and NOT part of the Hebrew or of most Christian canons. Jude names the source — “Enoch, the seventh from Adam” — and calls it prophecy. This is a canonical book quoting a non-canonical one as a true word, and readers have taken it several ways: that Jude endorses only this saying, not the whole book; that he cites 1 Enoch the way Paul quotes pagan poets (Acts 17:28), for a truth it happens to carry; or — the view of the Ethiopian Orthodox Church, which alone keeps 1 Enoch in its canon — that the book is genuinely Scripture. The one thing the text will not let us do is pretend the quotation is not there. KJV “ten thousands of his saints”; the NWT “holy myriads.”

«Grumblers, malcontents.» The portrait sharpens to a tone of voice: complainers who follow their appetites, whose mouths speak swollen words (hyperonka, bombast), flattering the powerful for whatever can be got from them.

Verses 17–19 · remember what you were told

«Remember the words of the apostles.» Against the intruders’ claim to novelty Jude sets memory: the false teachers were predicted — “in the last time there will be mockers.” ⚠ Note that Jude speaks of the apostles as an established authority whose warnings are already handed down — a hint that the letter is written to a church that already looks back to the apostolic word. “These cause divisions (apodiorizontes) — worldly (psychikoi, ‘soulish’), not having the Spirit”: the sharpest line in the letter, that the very men splitting the church are the ones without the Spirit they boast of.

Verses 20–23 · but you — build, pray, keep, wait; and rescue

«But you, beloved.» The turn to the faithful comes in a near-trinitarian rhythm: build yourselves up in the faith, pray in the Holy Spirit, keep yourselves in the love of God, wait for the mercy of the Lord Jesus Christ. Contending for the faith (v3) turns out to be less an attack than a community holding itself in love and prayer.

⚠ «Have mercy… snatch from the fire… mercy with fear.» Verses 22–23 are among the most textually tangled in the New Testament — the manuscripts vary between two and three groups and cannot all be reconciled; this translation follows the SBL three-clause reading. The mercy is fierce and careful at once: steady the wavering, snatch some straight out of the fire, and show even the compromised a mercy mixed with fear — “hating even the garment (chiton) stained by the flesh,” an image from Zechariah 3, where filthy garments are stripped from the high priest. Love the person; fear the contagion. ESV and NIV divide the groups differently.

Verses 24–25 · the doxology

One of the great doxologies of Scripture — and a deliberate answer to the whole letter. Jude has spent twenty-three verses on those who fall; he ends on the One “able to keep you from stumbling” (aptaistous, ‘from not-tripping’) and to make you stand before his glory “blameless” (amomous — the sacrificial word, without blemish), “with great joy.” The intruders defiled and denied; the doxology is all glory and majesty and dominion and authority to “the only God our Savior, through Jesus Christ our Lord” — before all time, now, and forever. A book about danger ends in safe hands.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the exact / distinct words: “slave” for doulos and “Master” for despotes, kept in the same family so the intruders’ denial answers Jude’s own title; “once for all” for hapax, the letter’s hinge; “contend” for epagonizesthai, the wrestling word; “licentiousness” for aselgeia; “crept in unnoticed” for pareisedysan; “hidden reefs” for spilades and “wandering stars” for asteres planetai, the images left vivid; and the reading “Jesus” at verse 5, the harder text the manuscripts best support.

Echoes paid. The wilderness generation destroyed after the Exodus (Numbers 14); the fallen angels of Genesis 6 as Jewish tradition read them; Sodom and Gomorrah (Genesis 19); “May the Lord rebuke you” from Zechariah 3:2; the way of Cain (Genesis 4), the error of Balaam (Numbers 22–24), the rebellion of Korah (Numbers 16); the stained garment of Zechariah 3; and the great doxology’s “blameless” (amomos), the sacrificial word for a spotless offering.

The two openly-named extra-canonical sources. Jude is the clearest case in the Bible of a canonical book drawing on works outside the canon: the dispute over the body of Moses (v9), from the lost Assumption of Moses; and the prophecy of Enoch (vv14–15), a direct quotation of 1 Enoch 1:9. The notes lay the facts out with their pedigrees and take no vote — the citations are there, and pretending otherwise is not translation.

Jude is a single-chapter book: with this chapter it is complete. Its nearest kin is 2 Peter 2, which shares much of this material — the fallen angels, Sodom, Balaam, the waterless clouds — and the relationship between the two (which drew on which) is one of the New Testament’s standing literary questions.

Revelation 1 — The Mister Translation

1
Ἀποκάλυψις Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ, ἣν ἔδωκεν αὐτῷ ὁ θεὸς δεῖξαι τοῖς δούλοις αὐτοῦ, ἃ δεῖ γενέσθαι ἐν τάχει, καὶ ἐσήμανεν ἀποστείλας διὰ τοῦ ἀγγέλου αὐτοῦ τῷ δούλῳ αὐτοῦ Ἰωάννῃ,
The revelation of Jesus Christ, which God gave him to show his slaves what must happen soon; and he made it known in signs, sending it through his angel to his slave John,note
2
ὃς ἐμαρτύρησεν τὸν λόγον τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ τὴν μαρτυρίαν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ, ὅσα εἶδεν.
who bore witness to the word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ — all that he saw.note
3
μακάριος ὁ ἀναγινώσκων καὶ οἱ ἀκούοντες τοὺς λόγους τῆς προφητείας καὶ τηροῦντες τὰ ἐν αὐτῇ γεγραμμένα, ὁ γὰρ καιρὸς ἐγγύς.
Blessed is the one who reads aloud, and those who hear, the words of this prophecy, and keep what is written in it — for the time is near.note
4
Ἰωάννης ταῖς ἑπτὰ ἐκκλησίαις ταῖς ἐν τῇ Ἀσίᾳ· χάρις ὑμῖν καὶ εἰρήνη ἀπὸ ὁ ὢν καὶ ὁ ἦν καὶ ὁ ἐρχόμενος, καὶ ἀπὸ τῶν ἑπτὰ πνευμάτων ἃ ἐνώπιον τοῦ θρόνου αὐτοῦ,
John, to the seven congregations in Asia: grace to you, and peace, from THE ONE WHO IS, AND WHO WAS, AND WHO IS COMING, and from the seven spirits before his throne,note
5
καὶ ἀπὸ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ, ὁ μάρτυς ὁ πιστός, ὁ πρωτότοκος τῶν νεκρῶν καὶ ὁ ἄρχων τῶν βασιλέων τῆς γῆς. Τῷ ἀγαπῶντι ἡμᾶς καὶ λύσαντι ἡμᾶς ἐκ τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν ἡμῶν ἐν τῷ αἵματι αὐτοῦ—
and from Jesus Christ — the faithful witness, the firstborn of the dead, and the ruler of the kings of the earth. To the one who loves us, and freed us from our sins by his blood —note
6
καὶ ἐποίησεν ἡμᾶς βασιλείαν, ἱερεῖς τῷ θεῷ καὶ πατρὶ αὐτοῦ— αὐτῷ ἡ δόξα καὶ τὸ κράτος εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας τῶν αἰώνων· ἀμήν.
and he made us a kingdom, priests to his God and Father — to him be the glory and the might forever and ever. Amen.note
7
Ἰδοὺ ἔρχεται μετὰ τῶν νεφελῶν, καὶ ὄψεται αὐτὸν πᾶς ὀφθαλμὸς καὶ οἵτινες αὐτὸν ἐξεκέντησαν, καὶ κόψονται ἐπʼ αὐτὸν πᾶσαι αἱ φυλαὶ τῆς γῆς. ναί, ἀμήν.
Look — he is coming with the clouds, and every eye will see him, even those who pierced him; and all the tribes of the earth will beat their breasts over him. Yes. Amen.note
8
Ἐγώ εἰμι τὸ Ἄλφα καὶ τὸ Ὦ, λέγει κύριος, ὁ θεός, ὁ ὢν καὶ ὁ ἦν καὶ ὁ ἐρχόμενος, ὁ παντοκράτωρ.
"I am the Alpha and the Omega," says the Lord God — the one who is, and who was, and who is coming — the Almighty.note
9
Ἐγὼ Ἰωάννης, ὁ ἀδελφὸς ὑμῶν καὶ συγκοινωνὸς ἐν τῇ θλίψει καὶ βασιλείᾳ καὶ ὑπομονῇ ἐν Ἰησοῦ ἐγενόμην ἐν τῇ νήσῳ τῇ καλουμένῃ Πάτμῳ διὰ τὸν λόγον τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ τὴν μαρτυρίαν Ἰησοῦ.
I, John — your brother, and companion in the affliction and kingdom and endurance in Jesus — came to be on the island called Patmos, because of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus.note
10
ἐγενόμην ἐν πνεύματι ἐν τῇ κυριακῇ ἡμέρᾳ, καὶ ἤκουσα ὀπίσω μου φωνὴν μεγάλην ὡς σάλπιγγος
I came to be in the Spirit on the Lord's day, and I heard behind me a great voice, like a trumpet,note
11
λεγούσης· Ὃ βλέπεις γράψον εἰς βιβλίον καὶ πέμψον ταῖς ἑπτὰ ἐκκλησίαις, εἰς Ἔφεσον καὶ εἰς Σμύρναν καὶ εἰς Πέργαμον καὶ εἰς Θυάτειρα καὶ εἰς Σάρδεις καὶ εἰς Φιλαδέλφειαν καὶ εἰς Λαοδίκειαν.
saying, "What you see, write in a scroll and send to the seven congregations: to Ephesus, and to Smyrna, and to Pergamum, and to Thyatira, and to Sardis, and to Philadelphia, and to Laodicea."note
12
Καὶ ἐπέστρεψα βλέπειν τὴν φωνὴν ἥτις ἐλάλει μετʼ ἐμοῦ· καὶ ἐπιστρέψας εἶδον ἑπτὰ λυχνίας χρυσᾶς,
And I turned to see the voice that was speaking with me; and when I had turned, I saw seven golden lampstands,note
13
καὶ ἐν μέσῳ τῶν λυχνιῶν ὅμοιον υἱὸν ἀνθρώπου, ἐνδεδυμένον ποδήρη καὶ περιεζωσμένον πρὸς τοῖς μαστοῖς ζώνην χρυσᾶν·
and in the midst of the lampstands one like a son of man, clothed in a robe reaching to his feet, and girded across the chest with a golden sash.note
14
ἡ δὲ κεφαλὴ αὐτοῦ καὶ αἱ τρίχες λευκαὶ ὡς ἔριον λευκόν, ὡς χιών, καὶ οἱ ὀφθαλμοὶ αὐτοῦ ὡς φλὸξ πυρός,
His head and his hair were white like wool — white as snow — and his eyes like a flame of fire,note
15
καὶ οἱ πόδες αὐτοῦ ὅμοιοι χαλκολιβάνῳ, ὡς ἐν καμίνῳ πεπυρωμένης, καὶ ἡ φωνὴ αὐτοῦ ὡς φωνὴ ὑδάτων πολλῶν,
and his feet like fine bronze, as though glowing in a furnace, and his voice like the sound of many waters.note
16
καὶ ἔχων ἐν τῇ δεξιᾷ χειρὶ αὐτοῦ ἀστέρας ἑπτά, καὶ ἐκ τοῦ στόματος αὐτοῦ ῥομφαία δίστομος ὀξεῖα ἐκπορευομένη, καὶ ἡ ὄψις αὐτοῦ ὡς ὁ ἥλιος φαίνει ἐν τῇ δυνάμει αὐτοῦ.
And in his right hand he held seven stars; and out of his mouth a sharp two-edged sword was going forth; and his face was like the sun shining in its strength.note
17
Καὶ ὅτε εἶδον αὐτόν, ἔπεσα πρὸς τοὺς πόδας αὐτοῦ ὡς νεκρός· καὶ ἔθηκεν τὴν δεξιὰν αὐτοῦ ἐπʼ ἐμὲ λέγων· Μὴ φοβοῦ· ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ πρῶτος καὶ ὁ ἔσχατος,
And when I saw him, I fell at his feet like a dead man. And he laid his right hand on me and said, "Do not be afraid. I am the first and the last,note
18
καὶ ὁ ζῶν— καὶ ἐγενόμην νεκρὸς καὶ ἰδοὺ ζῶν εἰμι εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας τῶν αἰώνων— καὶ ἔχω τὰς κλεῖς τοῦ θανάτου καὶ τοῦ ᾅδου.
and the living one — and I was dead, and look: I am alive forever and ever — and I hold the keys of Death and of Hades.note
19
γράψον οὖν ἃ εἶδες καὶ ἃ εἰσὶν καὶ ἃ μέλλει γίνεσθαι μετὰ ταῦτα.
Write, then, what you saw — what is, and what is about to happen after these things.note
20
τὸ μυστήριον τῶν ἑπτὰ ἀστέρων οὓς εἶδες ἐπὶ τῆς δεξιᾶς μου, καὶ τὰς ἑπτὰ λυχνίας τὰς χρυσᾶς· οἱ ἑπτὰ ἀστέρες ἄγγελοι τῶν ἑπτὰ ἐκκλησιῶν εἰσίν, καὶ αἱ λυχνίαι αἱ ἑπτὰ ἑπτὰ ἐκκλησίαι εἰσίν.
As for the secret of the seven stars that you saw on my right hand, and the seven golden lampstands: the seven stars are angels of the seven congregations, and the seven lampstands are seven congregations.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. The Greek shown is the SBL Greek New Testament (ed. Michael W. Holmes, Society of Biblical Literature, 2010), a modern critical text in the Alexandrian tradition; quotations from the copyrighted English versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison, and the KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims, and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–3 · Ἀποκάλυψις Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ apokalypsis Iēsou Christou

"The revelation." The book is named from its own first word: apokalypsis — an unveiling, the pulling-back of a curtain ("Apocalypse" is simply this word left untranslated; the modern sense of "catastrophe" is a later accretion — the word means disclosure). The opening verse hands down a chain of custody: God → Jesus Christ → his angel → his slave John → the slaves of God; and it states the book's method in one verb — "he made it known in signs" (esēmanen, from sēmeion, "sign": the shelf's "signified," KJV ASV Douay). A book that tells you on its first line that it will speak in symbols has already given you its reading instructions. On "slaves" (douloi): the shelf softens to "servants" (KJV, NIV; ASV margins "bondservants") while the NWT reads "slaves" plainly — as does this translation, on the same plain-word principle as "slave-girl" for Hagar (Genesis 16).

"What must happen soon… the time is near." Said honestly: this is the book's hardest word, and it stands in the first paragraph. En tachei means "soon, quickly" — not "someday" — and readers have wrestled with the nearness ever since (the main proposals: fulfilled in the first-century crisis; "soon" on God's clock, "one day as a thousand years"; or inaugurated then and still unwinding). This translation prints "soon" and leaves the wrestling in the notes, where it belongs. Then the book's first beatitude (there will be seven): "blessed is the one who reads aloud" — ho anaginōskōn is the public lector, one reader before a listening assembly; a window straight into how this book was first experienced: performed whole, in one sitting, out loud.

Verses 4–6 · ἀπὸ ὁ ὢν καὶ ὁ ἦν καὶ ὁ ἐρχόμενος apo ho ōn kai ho ēn kai ho erchomenos

The Name that refuses to decline. Verse 4 contains the most famous deliberate grammatical error in the Greek Bible. After apo ("from"), Greek requires the genitive case — and John writes the nominative, three times: "from THE ONE WHO IS, AND WHO WAS, AND WHO IS COMING." Every Greek schoolboy would wince; John knows exactly what he is doing. Ho ōn, "the One Who Is," is the Greek Bible's rendering of the divine name's own explanation at the burning bush (Exodus 3:14) — and John treats the title as indeclinable, refusing to bend the Name to fit his sentence, grammar yielding to God rather than God to grammar. This translation sets the phrase in small capitals to mark the standing stone in the syntax. It is the divine name unfolded into three tenses — and where a Hebrew chapter would print Jehovah, this Greek circumlocution is the closest the New Testament comes to spelling it (see the Ask Mr. Librarian page on the Name).

One Greek letter: washed, or freed? Verse 5 carries a textual crux you can hear in English: the manuscripts split between lysanti ("freed/loosed us from our sins") and lousanti ("washed us from our sins") — one letter apart, and pronounced almost identically. The older witnesses read "freed" (so ASV "loosed," NIV "has freed," NWT "loosed"); the later text behind the Reformation Bibles read "washed" (KJV Geneva, and Douay from the Vulgate's lavit). This translation follows the critical text: freed. The same seam runs through verse 6: the older text reads "made us a kingdom, priests" (Exodus 19:6's "a kingdom of priests," echoed); the later text behind KJV reads "kings and priests." And note the titles of verse 5 — "the faithful witness" (martys, the word already migrating toward English "martyr"), "the firstborn of the dead" (on "firstborn" as rank, not manufacture, see the Is Jesus God? page), "the ruler of the kings of the earth" — a threefold claim staked, under a Roman emperor, from a penal island.

Verses 7–8 · τὸ Ἄλφα καὶ τὸ Ὦ … ὁ παντοκράτωρ to Alpha kai to Ō … ho pantokratōr

"Look — he is coming with the clouds." Idou — the Greek heir of the Hebrew hineh, rendered "Look —" throughout this translation. The verse is a fusion of two prophets: the cloud-rider who approaches the Ancient of Days in Daniel 7:13, and Zechariah's "they will look on me, whom they pierced, and mourn" (Zechariah 12:10) — the same Zechariah text John's Gospel quotes at the cross. "Beat their breasts" is the mourning gesture the Greek actually names (kopsontai); the shelf's "wail" and "mourn" soften it to sound alone.

"I am the Alpha and the Omega… the Almighty." The first letter and the last letter of the Greek alphabet — A to Z, with everything between implied. (KJV adds "the beginning and the ending" here — a phrase the later manuscripts imported from 21:6; the older text says it with the letters alone.) Then a word this project has met before, from the other side: pantokratōr, "the Almighty" — the very Greek word the old translators reached for when they guessed at El Shaddai in Genesis (see the note at 17:1, where this translation refused "Almighty" because the Hebrew's meaning is uncertain). Here there is no guess: the Greek itself says pantokratōr, "the all-holding" — the title is native, and this translation prints it gladly. Revelation uses it nine times; the rest of the New Testament, once. One more shelf note, factually: the NWT prints "says Jehovah God" in this verse — restoring the name where the Greek has kyrios ho theos, on its argument that the phrase quotes the Old Testament's naming formulas; the Greek text itself reads "Lord God," and that is what stands in the verse above.

Verses 9–11 · ἐν τῇ νήσῳ τῇ καλουμένῃ Πάτμῳ en tē nēsō tē kaloumenē Patmō

Which John? The book says only "John" — no "apostle," no "son of Zebedee," just "your brother, and companion in the affliction." Tradition from the second century (Justin Martyr, Irenaeus) identifies him with the apostle, author of the Gospel; but the question is not modern — Dionysius of Alexandria, in the third century, already argued from style that the Gospel and the Apocalypse cannot come from the same hand (the Gospel's Greek is smooth; Revelation's is wild, Hebrew-boned, and — as verse 4 just showed — deliberately lawless), and proposed a second John, "John of Patmos." The congregation kept the book either way. This translation reports the question and translates the text, which claims only the name.

Patmos, and the Lord's day. A small rocky island off the Asian coast — Rome used such islands for banishment, and "because of the word of God" reads most naturally as exile for preaching. "I came to be in the Spirit on the Lord's day" (kyriakē hēmera) — the phrase's first appearance in all literature: almost certainly Sunday, the resurrection's weekday, which soon bore this name everywhere Greek was spoken (a quiet counter-coinage, too — "the Emperor's day" was already a phrase in the Roman east). The voice commands a scroll for seven congregations, named in exact geographic order: Ephesus north to Smyrna and Pergamum, then inland and south through Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, Laodicea — a courier's circuit; the book is a circular letter, and the list is its postal route. Their seven letters are the next two chapters.

Verses 12–16 · ὅμοιον υἱὸν ἀνθρώπου homoion huion anthrōpou

"One like a son of man." The vision is built almost entirely from Daniel 7 — but with one staggering transfer. In Daniel, "one like a son of man" comes with the clouds to the Ancient of Days, whose "hair is like pure wool" and whose clothing is "white as snow" (Daniel 7:9, 13). Here the son of man wears the Ancient of Days' own description: "his head and his hair were white like wool, white as snow." The two figures of Daniel's throne-room have become one — the chapter's boldest sentence, spoken entirely in imagery (the deity-of-Christ argument built on such texts is catalogued on the Is Jesus God? page). On the capitalization crux: KJV prints "like unto the Son of man" — a confessing capital — where ASV prints "like unto a son of man," indefinite, as the Greek is; this translation reads "one like a son of man" and lets the vision make the argument.

The rest of the figure is a mosaic of the prophets: the long robe and golden sash are priestly vesture (Exodus 28); eyes "like a flame of fire" and feet "like fine bronze… glowing in a furnace" come from the great visions of Daniel 10 and Ezekiel 1 (chalkolibanon, rendered "fine bronze," is a word found nowhere else in Greek literature — a guess by every translator since, honestly made); the voice "like the sound of many waters" is Ezekiel's for the glory of God (Ezekiel 43:2); and "out of his mouth a sharp two-edged sword" is Isaiah's servant, whose mouth is "like a sharp sword" (Isaiah 49:2) — the figure's only weapon is what he says. Seven stars in his right hand; a face like the sun in its strength. John is shown nothing that a prophet had not seen — and everything at once.

Verses 17–20 · Μὴ φοβοῦ mē phobou

"Do not be afraid." John falls "like a dead man" — and the hand that holds seven stars is laid on him, with the vision-word spoken to every terrified seer: mē phobou. It is the word Abram heard in the Bible's first prophetic vision — "Do not be afraid, Abram" (Genesis 15:1) — spoken now at the last book's first vision; the canon opens and closes its visions with the same reassurance. Then a self-naming that out-claims every title so far: "I am the first and the last" — which is not a new coinage but a quotation: it is Jehovah's own self-description in Isaiah ("I am the first and I am the last; besides me there is no god," Isaiah 44:6) — on the lips of the one who says, in the next breath, "and I was dead." The two claims sit in one sentence, and every reading of who Jesus is must pass through it (both readings, at full strength, on the Is Jesus God? page).

"The keys of Death and of Hades." Hadēs is the Greek Bible's rendering of the Hebrew Sheol — the grave-realm, the place of the dead, not the fiery hell of later imagery; KJV prints "the keys of hell and of death" (its text also reverses the order), where ASV and NIV keep "Hades" — as here. Keys mean custody: the door locks from his side now. Then the chapter closes with a gift rare in apocalyptic — the vision interprets itself: "the seven stars are angels of the seven congregations, and the seven lampstands are seven congregations." The book has shown its method twice now — it speaks in signs (v1), and its signs can be read. The "angels" of the congregations (guardian spirits? human messengers? the congregations' heavenly doubles?) are argued over to this day — the seven letters that follow are addressed to them, whoever they are.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation sides with the literal shelf: "slaves" for douloi (with NWT, against the softening "servants"), "Hades" not "hell" (with ASV/NIV), "a son of man" indefinite (with ASV, against KJV's confessing capital), and the critical text's "freed us" and "a kingdom, priests" (with ASV/NIV, against the later text behind KJV's "washed" and "kings and priests").

Where it goes its own way: "made it known in signs" (keeping esēmanen's sign-language audible as the book's method statement), small capitals for THE ONE WHO IS AND WHO WAS AND WHO IS COMING (marking the deliberate grammar-break that refuses to decline the Name), "beat their breasts" for the mourning gesture the Greek names, and "the Almighty" printed gladly for pantokratōr — the word Genesis 17's note refused for El Shaddai, native at last.

Echoes paid and planted. Paid: pantokratōr answers the "Almighty" question left open at Genesis 17:1; "the one who is" unfolds the Name whose story began at Genesis 2:4 (and on the Jehovah page); "the word of God" carries John 1:1 forward; and "do not be afraid," spoken to a man flat on the ground before God, was first heard by Abram in vision (15:1). Planted: the seven letters to the seven congregations (chapters 2–3, next in this book); the seven beatitudes of Revelation (one down); "I am coming" as the book's drumbeat; and Daniel 7's throne-room, waiting for the day Daniel itself joins this translation.

Next installment in this book: Revelation 2 — the letters begin: Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamum, Thyatira.

Revelation 2 — The Mister Translation

1
Τῷ ἀγγέλῳ τῆς ἐν Ἐφέσῳ ἐκκλησίας γράψον· Τάδε λέγει ὁ κρατῶν τοὺς ἑπτὰ ἀστέρας ἐν τῇ δεξιᾷ αὐτοῦ, ὁ περιπατῶν ἐν μέσῳ τῶν ἑπτὰ λυχνιῶν τῶν χρυσῶν·
"To the angel of the congregation in Ephesus write: These things says the one who holds the seven stars in his right hand, who walks among the seven golden lampstands:note
2
Οἶδα τὰ ἔργα σου, καὶ τὸν κόπον καὶ τὴν ὑπομονήν σου, καὶ ὅτι οὐ δύνῃ βαστάσαι κακούς, καὶ ἐπείρασας τοὺς λέγοντας ἑαυτοὺς ἀποστόλους καὶ οὐκ εἰσίν, καὶ εὗρες αὐτοὺς ψευδεῖς·
I know your works — your toil, and your endurance, and that you cannot bear evil men; and you tested those who call themselves apostles, and are not, and found them false;note
3
καὶ ὑπομονὴν ἔχεις, καὶ ἐβάστασας διὰ τὸ ὄνομά μου, καὶ οὐ κεκοπίακες.
and you have endurance, and you have borne up for the sake of my name, and have not grown weary.note
4
ἀλλὰ ἔχω κατὰ σοῦ ὅτι τὴν ἀγάπην σου τὴν πρώτην ἀφῆκες.
But I have this against you: you have left your first love.note
5
μνημόνευε οὖν πόθεν πέπτωκας καὶ μετανόησον καὶ τὰ πρῶτα ἔργα ποίησον· εἰ δὲ μή, ἔρχομαί σοι καὶ κινήσω τὴν λυχνίαν σου ἐκ τοῦ τόπου αὐτῆς, ἐὰν μὴ μετανοήσῃς.
Remember, then, from where you have fallen; repent, and do the first works. If not, I am coming to you, and I will remove your lampstand from its place — unless you repent.note
6
ἀλλὰ τοῦτο ἔχεις ὅτι μισεῖς τὰ ἔργα τῶν Νικολαϊτῶν, ἃ κἀγὼ μισῶ.
But this you have: you hate the works of the Nicolaitans, which I also hate.note
7
ὁ ἔχων οὖς ἀκουσάτω τί τὸ πνεῦμα λέγει ταῖς ἐκκλησίαις. τῷ νικῶντι δώσω αὐτῷ φαγεῖν ἐκ τοῦ ξύλου τῆς ζωῆς, ὅ ἐστιν ἐν τῷ παραδείσῳ τοῦ θεοῦ.
The one who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the congregations. To the one who overcomes I will give to eat from the tree of life, which is in the paradise of God.note
8
Καὶ τῷ ἀγγέλῳ τῆς ἐν Σμύρνῃ ἐκκλησίας γράψον· Τάδε λέγει ὁ πρῶτος καὶ ὁ ἔσχατος, ὃς ἐγένετο νεκρὸς καὶ ἔζησεν·
"And to the angel of the congregation in Smyrna write: These things says the first and the last, who was dead and came to life:note
9
Οἶδά σου τὴν θλῖψιν καὶ τὴν πτωχείαν, ἀλλὰ πλούσιος εἶ, καὶ τὴν βλασφημίαν ἐκ τῶν λεγόντων Ἰουδαίους εἶναι ἑαυτούς, καὶ οὐκ εἰσίν, ἀλλὰ συναγωγὴ τοῦ Σατανᾶ.
I know your affliction and your poverty — but you are rich — and the slander of those who say they are Jews, and are not, but are a synagogue of Satan.note
10
μηδὲν φοβοῦ ἃ μέλλεις πάσχειν. ἰδοὺ μέλλει βάλλειν ὁ διάβολος ἐξ ὑμῶν εἰς φυλακὴν ἵνα πειρασθῆτε, καὶ ἕξετε θλῖψιν ἡμερῶν δέκα. γίνου πιστὸς ἄχρι θανάτου, καὶ δώσω σοι τὸν στέφανον τῆς ζωῆς.
Do not fear what you are about to suffer. Look — the devil is about to throw some of you into prison, so that you may be tested, and you will have affliction ten days. Be faithful until death, and I will give you the crown of life.note
11
ὁ ἔχων οὖς ἀκουσάτω τί τὸ πνεῦμα λέγει ταῖς ἐκκλησίαις. ὁ νικῶν οὐ μὴ ἀδικηθῇ ἐκ τοῦ θανάτου τοῦ δευτέρου.
The one who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the congregations. The one who overcomes shall by no means be harmed by the second death.note
12
Καὶ τῷ ἀγγέλῳ τῆς ἐν Περγάμῳ ἐκκλησίας γράψον· Τάδε λέγει ὁ ἔχων τὴν ῥομφαίαν τὴν δίστομον τὴν ὀξεῖαν·
"And to the angel of the congregation in Pergamum write: These things says the one who has the sharp two-edged sword:note
13
Οἶδα ποῦ κατοικεῖς, ὅπου ὁ θρόνος τοῦ Σατανᾶ, καὶ κρατεῖς τὸ ὄνομά μου, καὶ οὐκ ἠρνήσω τὴν πίστιν μου καὶ ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις Ἀντιπᾶς, ὁ μάρτυς μου ὁ πιστός μου, ὃς ἀπεκτάνθη παρʼ ὑμῖν, ὅπου ὁ Σατανᾶς κατοικεῖ.
I know where you live — where Satan's throne is. And you hold fast my name, and you did not deny my faith even in the days of Antipas, my witness, my faithful one, who was killed among you, where Satan lives.note
14
ἀλλὰ ἔχω κατὰ σοῦ ὀλίγα, ὅτι ἔχεις ἐκεῖ κρατοῦντας τὴν διδαχὴν Βαλαάμ, ὃς ἐδίδασκεν τῷ Βαλὰκ βαλεῖν σκάνδαλον ἐνώπιον τῶν υἱῶν Ἰσραήλ, φαγεῖν εἰδωλόθυτα καὶ πορνεῦσαι·
But I have a few things against you: you have there some who hold the teaching of Balaam, who taught Balak to put a stumbling block before the sons of Israel — to eat idol-sacrifices, and to commit sexual immorality.note
15
οὕτως ἔχεις καὶ σὺ κρατοῦντας τὴν διδαχὴν Νικολαϊτῶν ὁμοίως.
So you too have some who hold the teaching of the Nicolaitans, likewise.note
16
μετανόησον οὖν· εἰ δὲ μή, ἔρχομαί σοι ταχύ, καὶ πολεμήσω μετʼ αὐτῶν ἐν τῇ ῥομφαίᾳ τοῦ στόματός μου.
Repent, then. If not, I am coming to you quickly, and I will make war on them with the sword of my mouth.note
17
ὁ ἔχων οὖς ἀκουσάτω τί τὸ πνεῦμα λέγει ταῖς ἐκκλησίαις. τῷ νικῶντι δώσω αὐτῷ τοῦ μάννα τοῦ κεκρυμμένου, καὶ δώσω αὐτῷ ψῆφον λευκήν, καὶ ἐπὶ τὴν ψῆφον ὄνομα καινὸν γεγραμμένον ὃ οὐδεὶς οἶδεν εἰ μὴ ὁ λαμβάνων.
The one who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the congregations. To the one who overcomes I will give some of the hidden manna; and I will give him a white stone, and on the stone a new name written, which no one knows except the one who receives it.note
18
Καὶ τῷ ἀγγέλῳ τῆς ἐν Θυατείροις ἐκκλησίας γράψον· Τάδε λέγει ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ, ὁ ἔχων τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς αὐτοῦ ὡς φλόγα πυρός, καὶ οἱ πόδες αὐτοῦ ὅμοιοι χαλκολιβάνῳ·
"And to the angel of the congregation in Thyatira write: These things says the Son of God, whose eyes are like a flame of fire, and whose feet are like fine bronze:note
19
Οἶδά σου τὰ ἔργα, καὶ τὴν ἀγάπην καὶ τὴν πίστιν καὶ τὴν διακονίαν καὶ τὴν ὑπομονήν σου, καὶ τὰ ἔργα σου τὰ ἔσχατα πλείονα τῶν πρώτων.
I know your works — your love, and faith, and service, and your endurance — and your last works are more than the first.note
20
ἀλλὰ ἔχω κατὰ σοῦ ὅτι ἀφεῖς τὴν γυναῖκα Ἰεζάβελ, ἡ λέγουσα ἑαυτὴν προφῆτιν, καὶ διδάσκει καὶ πλανᾷ τοὺς ἐμοὺς δούλους πορνεῦσαι καὶ φαγεῖν εἰδωλόθυτα.
But I have this against you: you tolerate the woman Jezebel, who calls herself a prophetess — and she teaches and deceives my slaves to commit sexual immorality, and to eat idol-sacrifices.note
21
καὶ ἔδωκα αὐτῇ χρόνον ἵνα μετανοήσῃ, καὶ οὐ θέλει μετανοῆσαι ἐκ τῆς πορνείας αὐτῆς.
And I gave her time to repent, and she does not want to repent of her immorality.note
22
ἰδοὺ βάλλω αὐτὴν εἰς κλίνην, καὶ τοὺς μοιχεύοντας μετʼ αὐτῆς εἰς θλῖψιν μεγάλην, ἐὰν μὴ μετανοήσωσιν ἐκ τῶν ἔργων αὐτῆς·
Look — I am throwing her onto a sickbed, and those who commit adultery with her into great affliction, unless they repent of her works;note
23
καὶ τὰ τέκνα αὐτῆς ἀποκτενῶ ἐν θανάτῳ· καὶ γνώσονται πᾶσαι αἱ ἐκκλησίαι ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ἐραυνῶν νεφροὺς καὶ καρδίας, καὶ δώσω ὑμῖν ἑκάστῳ κατὰ τὰ ἔργα ὑμῶν.
and her children I will kill with death. And all the congregations will know that I am the one who searches kidneys and hearts; and I will give to each of you according to your works.note
24
ὑμῖν δὲ λέγω τοῖς λοιποῖς τοῖς ἐν Θυατείροις, ὅσοι οὐκ ἔχουσιν τὴν διδαχὴν ταύτην, οἵτινες οὐκ ἔγνωσαν τὰ βαθέα τοῦ Σατανᾶ, ὡς λέγουσιν, οὐ βάλλω ἐφʼ ὑμᾶς ἄλλο βάρος·
But to you I say — to the rest in Thyatira, as many as do not hold this teaching, who have not known "the deep things of Satan," as they say — I lay on you no other burden.note
25
πλὴν ὃ ἔχετε κρατήσατε ἄχρι οὗ ἂν ἥξω.
Only hold fast what you have, until I come.note
26
καὶ ὁ νικῶν καὶ ὁ τηρῶν ἄχρι τέλους τὰ ἔργα μου, δώσω αὐτῷ ἐξουσίαν ἐπὶ τῶν ἐθνῶν,
And the one who overcomes, and keeps my works until the end — I will give him authority over the nations,note
27
καὶ ποιμανεῖ αὐτοὺς ἐν ῥάβδῳ σιδηρᾷ, ὡς τὰ σκεύη τὰ κεραμικὰ συντρίβεται,
and he will shepherd them with an iron rod, as clay pots are shattered —note
28
ὡς κἀγὼ εἴληφα παρὰ τοῦ πατρός μου, καὶ δώσω αὐτῷ τὸν ἀστέρα τὸν πρωϊνόν.
as I too have received from my Father. And I will give him the morning star.note
29
ὁ ἔχων οὖς ἀκουσάτω τί τὸ πνεῦμα λέγει ταῖς ἐκκλησίαις.
The one who has an ear, let him hear what the Spirit says to the congregations.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. The Greek shown is the SBL Greek New Testament (ed. Michael W. Holmes, Society of Biblical Literature, 2010), a modern critical text in the Alexandrian tradition; quotations from the copyrighted English versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison, and the KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims, and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–7 · Ephesus · τὴν ἀγάπην σου τὴν πρώτην ἀφῆκες tēn agapēn sou tēn prōtēn aphēkes

The anatomy of a letter. All seven letters share one form, worth learning on the first: an address ("to the angel of the congregation in…"); a title of the speaker, drawn from chapter 1's vision (here: the stars and lampstands of 1:13-16 — each city gets the facet of the vision it needs); "I know…" — praise first, wherever praise is possible; the complaint ("but I have this against you"); a call — "the one who has an ear, let him hear"; and a promise "to the one who overcomes" (ho nikōn, the victor), every one of which is paid out in the book's last two chapters. A word about the word: "congregation" here renders ekklēsia — an assembly of people, with no building and no institution in it (Acts 19 uses the same word for the Ephesus riot). It was Tyndale's rendering, and King James's translation rules expressly forbade it — "the word Church not to be translated Congregation"; the dictionary entry tells the whole story. Ephesus — the province's great harbor city, home of the temple of Artemis and, by tradition, of John himself — gets the fullest praise in the set: toil, endurance, doctrinal vigilance (they "tested those who call themselves apostles… and found them false"). And then the sentence that has stung readers for nineteen centuries: "you have left your first love" — orthodoxy intact, the fire gone; and the threat is not persecution but removal: "I will remove your lampstand." (The Nicolaitans, hated here and taught at Pergamum: honestly unknown — Irenaeus guessed followers of Nicolaus of Acts 6:5; the letters themselves pair them with "Balaam's teaching," idol-food and immorality, which is likely all we can say.)

The tree, again. The first overcomer-promise reaches all the way back to this project's first pages: "to eat from the tree of life, which is in the paradise of God." Paradeisos — a Persian loan-word for a walled garden — is the very word the Greek Bible chose for the garden of Eden; and the tree of life is the one whose way home has been under guard since "the cherubim… and a flaming sword… to guard the way to the tree of life" (Genesis 3:24). The Bible's last book begins its promises by unbarring its first book's gate.

Verses 8–11 · Smyrna · γίνου πιστὸς ἄχρι θανάτου ginou pistos achri thanatou

The letter with no complaint. Smyrna — the beautiful harbor city whose ringed acropolis ancient writers praised as "the crown of Smyrna" — receives, with Philadelphia, one of only two letters containing no rebuke at all. Its speaker-title is the one a persecuted congregation needs: "the first and the last, who was dead and came to life" (1:17-18's own words). "I know your… poverty — but you are rich": the city was wealthy; the congregation was poor, likely because confessing Christ cost trade and standing. "Affliction ten days" — a bounded number: short, counted, survivable; the point is the limit, not the calendar. And the charge that follows became one of the most famous in martyrdom's history: "Be faithful until death, and I will give you the crown of life" — the stephanos, the victor's laurel, not a monarch's diadem. Two generations later Smyrna's own bishop, Polycarp — who tradition says knew John — was burned in this city's stadium at 86, refusing to recant ("eighty-six years I have served him, and he has done me no wrong"): the letter, kept to the letter.

A hard verse, handled honestly. "The slander of those who say they are Jews, and are not, but are a synagogue of Satan." Two things are true and must both be said. First: in its own century this is an intra-Jewish polemic — John is Jewish, much of Smyrna's congregation was Jewish, and the charge is that the accusers (likely denouncing Christians to the Roman authorities, as Polycarp's martyrdom-account describes) have forfeited the name they claim; "synagogue" here is simply "assembly," the ordinary word. Second: torn from that setting, the phrase has been used for centuries to license Christian hatred of Jews as such — a use the text does not support and this project names as the abuse it is, exactly as it did with the "curse of Ham" (Genesis 9). The second death of the promise (v11) is defined by the book itself much later — the lake of fire (20:14) — and the promise is an exact mirror: those faithful until death are untouchable by the death that matters.

Verses 12–17 · Pergamum · ὅπου ὁ θρόνος τοῦ Σατανᾶ hopou ho thronos tou Satana

"Where Satan's throne is." Pergamum, the old royal capital of Asia, terraced up its mountain like a crown — and something up there is being called Satan's throne (KJV "Satan's seat" undersells the Greek: thronos is a throne). The candidates, each with a pedigree: the Great Altar of Zeus, whose sculpted frieze of gods battling serpent-legged giants dominated the acropolis (the altar itself was excavated and carried off to Berlin — it stands today in the Pergamon Museum, one of the strangest afterlives any biblical landmark has had); the temple of Rome and Augustus — Pergamum was the first city of Asia licensed for the imperial cult, and emperor-worship is Revelation's great enemy; or the healing shrine of Asclepius with its serpent emblem. Perhaps all of them at once: the whole skyline. Here Antipas was killed — "my witness, my faithful one" (martys, the word already crossing into "martyr," and the exact title of Jesus himself in 1:5) — the book's only named Christian casualty.

Balaam's program, and the white stone. The complaint reaches back to Numbers: Balaam, who could not curse Israel, taught Balak to seduce it instead — "idol-sacrifices and sexual immorality" — the Bible's shorthand for assimilation by dinner-party (in these trade cities, the guild feasts in the gods' honor were where business got done; the pressure was economic, and the "teaching" said go along). The promises answer the menu: instead of idol-meat, hidden manna; and "a white stone, and on the stone a new name written." The psēphos (NWT reads "white pebble," which is exactly what the word means) had many uses — a juror's pebble of acquittal, an admission-token to a feast, an amulet bearing a secret name — and the letter may intend the whole cluster: verdict, invitation, identity. As for the new name: readers of this site have watched God rename people since "your name shall be Abraham" (Genesis 17:5); the last book makes the renaming personal, private, and permanent.

Verses 18–23 · Thyatira · ὁ ἐραυνῶν νεφροὺς καὶ καρδίας ho eraunōn nephrous kai kardias

The longest letter goes to the smallest city. Thyatira — no acropolis, no famous temple, a workshop town of trade guilds on the inland road (its one other New Testament appearance is a businesswoman: Lydia, "a seller of purple from Thyatira," Acts 16:14) — receives the longest of the seven letters, from "the Son of God" (the title's only occurrence in all Revelation), with the furnace-eyes and bronze feet of the vision. The praise is real and rising — "your last works are more than the first," the exact inverse of Ephesus. The complaint is a prophetess the letter code-names Jezebel — as Pergamum's problem was code-named Balaam: Old Testament villains as ciphers for local teachers — whose program is the same guild-feast accommodation ("eat idol-sacrifices"), taught from inside the congregation, with time already given to repent and refused. (A textual note the KJV tradition preserves: some manuscripts read "your wife Jezebel" — one Greek pronoun, and the town's whole drama becomes the angel's own household; the older text reads simply "the woman.")

"The one who searches kidneys and hearts." Kept literal, and worth its strangeness: Hebrew anatomy located the inmost self in the kidneys (kelayot; KJV "reins," from the Latin) — and the sentence is Jehovah's own self-description at Jeremiah 17:10, "I, Jehovah, search the heart and test the kidneys… to give each according to his ways," here spoken by the Son of God in the first person. The same claim closed John 2 a chapter shelf away: "he knew what was in the human." The sickbed sentence is grim, and its idiom is Hebrew to the bone — "I will kill with death" (apoktenō en thanatō), which in the Greek Bible's usage likely means by pestilence; this translation keeps the doubled strangeness and files the reading here.

Verses 24–29 · Thyatira, continued · ποιμανεῖ αὐτοὺς ἐν ῥάβδῳ σιδηρᾷ poimanei autous en rhabdō sidēra

"No other burden." To the rest of Thyatira — those who never learned what the sect grandly called "the deep things of Satan" (the letter quotes their slogan back with visible irony: "as they say") — the demand is almost startlingly light: "I lay on you no other burden; only hold fast what you have, until I come." No new program, no heroics: endurance as the whole assignment.

The iron rod, and the letter of the LXX. The overcomer's promise quotes Psalm 2 — "authority over the nations… he will shepherd them with an iron rod" — and inside that verb sits one of the tidiest text-critical stories on this site. The Hebrew of Psalm 2:9 reads "you will break them" (tero'em); the Greek translators read the same consonants from a different root and wrote "you will shepherd them" (poimaneis) — and Revelation quotes the Greek, then keeps the Hebrew's shattering in the very next clause ("as clay pots are shattered"): both readings of one ancient word, side by side in a single sentence. KJV and Douay flatten to "rule"; ASV margins the truth ("as a shepherd"); this translation prints "shepherd" because that is the word John wrote. Last, the strangest gift in the seven letters: "I will give him the morning star." The book decodes it only on its final page — "I am the root and the offspring of David, the bright morning star" (22:16). The gift is the giver.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation sides with the literal shelf: "Satan's throne" (thronos — against KJV's "seat"), "white stone" with NWT's "pebble" saluted as exactly right, "slaves" for douloi again, and "shepherd them with an iron rod" (the verb John actually wrote, against the shelf's "rule").

Where it goes its own way: "searches kidneys and hearts" kept anatomically literal (KJV's "reins" honored in the note), "I will kill with death" kept as the Hebraism it is (with the pestilence reading filed), and the synagogue-of-Satan sentence translated plainly and then handled in full — first-century meaning and later abuse both named.

Echoes paid and planted. Paid: the tree of life, under guard since Eden's east gate (3:24), promised open in the paradise of God; the new name on the white stone from the God who renamed Abram (17:5); "the first and the last, who was dead" — Smyrna addressed by the Patmos vision's own title (1:17); and the searcher of hearts, who a chapter-shelf away "knew what was in the human" (John 2:25). Planted: the second death, defined at 20:14; the hidden manna (Exodus's jar, waiting for that book); the morning star, decoded at 22:16; and three letters still to deliver — Sardis, Philadelphia, Laodicea, next in this book.

Next installment in this book: Revelation 3 — the letters conclude: Sardis ("you have a name that you live, and you are dead"), Philadelphia's open door, and lukewarm Laodicea.

Daniel 1 — The Mister Translation

1
בִּשְׁנַת שָׁלוֹשׁ, לְמַלְכוּת יְהוֹיָקִים מֶלֶךְ-יְהוּדָה--בָּא נְבוּכַדְנֶאצַּר מֶלֶךְ-בָּבֶל יְרוּשָׁלִַם, וַיָּצַר עָלֶיהָ.
In the third year of the reign of Jehoiakim king of Judah, Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came to Jerusalem and laid siege to it.note
2
וַיִּתֵּן אֲדֹנָי בְּיָדוֹ אֶת-יְהוֹיָקִים מֶלֶךְ-יְהוּדָה, וּמִקְצָת כְּלֵי בֵית-הָאֱלֹהִים, וַיְבִיאֵם אֶרֶץ-שִׁנְעָר, בֵּית אֱלֹהָיו; וְאֶת-הַכֵּלִים הֵבִיא, בֵּית אוֹצַר אֱלֹהָיו.
And the Lord gave Jehoiakim king of Judah into his hand, with some of the vessels of the house of God; and he brought them to the land of Shinar, to the house of his god — and the vessels he brought into the treasure-house of his god.note
3
וַיֹּאמֶר הַמֶּלֶךְ, לְאַשְׁפְּנַז רַב סָרִיסָיו: לְהָבִיא מִבְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, וּמִזֶּרַע הַמְּלוּכָה--וּמִן-הַפַּרְתְּמִים.
And the king told Ashpenaz, the chief of his court officials, to bring some of the sons of Israel — of the royal seed, and of the nobles —note
4
יְלָדִים אֲשֶׁר אֵין-בָּהֶם כָּל-מאוּם וְטוֹבֵי מַרְאֶה וּמַשְׂכִּלִים בְּכָל-חָכְמָה, וְיֹדְעֵי דַעַת וּמְבִינֵי מַדָּע, וַאֲשֶׁר כֹּחַ בָּהֶם, לַעֲמֹד בְּהֵיכַל הַמֶּלֶךְ; וּלְלַמְּדָם סֵפֶר, וּלְשׁוֹן כַּשְׂדִּים.
youths in whom was no blemish — good-looking, skilled in all wisdom, knowing knowledge and understanding learning, with strength in them to stand in the king's palace — to teach them the writing and the tongue of the Chaldeans.note
5
וַיְמַן לָהֶם הַמֶּלֶךְ דְּבַר-יוֹם בְּיוֹמוֹ, מִפַּת-בַּג הַמֶּלֶךְ וּמִיֵּין מִשְׁתָּיו, וּלְגַדְּלָם, שָׁנִים שָׁלוֹשׁ; וּמִקְצָתָם--יַעַמְדוּ, לִפְנֵי הַמֶּלֶךְ.
And the king appointed them a daily portion from the king's food and from the wine he drank — to raise them three years, and at the end of them, they would stand before the king.note
6
וַיְהִי בָהֶם, מִבְּנֵי יְהוּדָה--דָּנִיֵּאל חֲנַנְיָה, מִישָׁאֵל וַעֲזַרְיָה.
Among them, from the sons of Judah, were Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah;note
7
וַיָּשֶׂם לָהֶם שַׂר הַסָּרִיסִים, שֵׁמוֹת; וַיָּשֶׂם לְדָנִיֵּאל בֵּלְטְשַׁאצַּר, וְלַחֲנַנְיָה שַׁדְרַךְ, וּלְמִישָׁאֵל מֵישַׁךְ, וְלַעֲזַרְיָה עֲבֵד נְגוֹ.
and the chief of the officials set names on them: to Daniel he gave Belteshazzar, to Hananiah Shadrach, to Mishael Meshach, and to Azariah Abednego.note
8
וַיָּשֶׂם דָּנִיֵּאל עַל-לִבּוֹ, אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יִתְגָּאַל בְּפַת-בַּג הַמֶּלֶךְ וּבְיֵין מִשְׁתָּיו; וַיְבַקֵּשׁ מִשַּׂר הַסָּרִיסִים, אֲשֶׁר לֹא יִתְגָּאָל.
But Daniel set on his heart that he would not defile himself with the king's food or with the wine he drank; and he asked the chief of the officials that he might not defile himself.note
9
וַיִּתֵּן הָאֱלֹהִים אֶת-דָּנִיֵּאל, לְחֶסֶד וּלְרַחֲמִים, לִפְנֵי, שַׂר הַסָּרִיסִים.
And God gave Daniel kindness and compassion before the chief of the officials.note
10
וַיֹּאמֶר שַׂר הַסָּרִיסִים, לְדָנִיֵּאל--יָרֵא אֲנִי אֶת-אֲדֹנִי הַמֶּלֶךְ, אֲשֶׁר מִנָּה אֶת-מַאֲכַלְכֶם וְאֶת-מִשְׁתֵּיכֶם: אֲשֶׁר לָמָּה יִרְאֶה אֶת-פְּנֵיכֶם זֹעֲפִים, מִן-הַיְלָדִים אֲשֶׁר כְּגִילְכֶם, וְחִיַּבְתֶּם אֶת-רֹאשִׁי, לַמֶּלֶךְ.
And the chief of the officials said to Daniel, "I fear my lord the king, who has appointed your food and your drink. Why should he see your faces more haggard than the youths your own age? You would forfeit my head to the king."note
11
וַיֹּאמֶר דָּנִיֵּאל, אֶל-הַמֶּלְצַר--אֲשֶׁר מִנָּה, שַׂר הַסָּרִיסִים, עַל-דָּנִיֵּאל חֲנַנְיָה, מִישָׁאֵל וַעֲזַרְיָה.
So Daniel said to the steward whom the chief of the officials had appointed over Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah:note
12
נַס-נָא אֶת-עֲבָדֶיךָ, יָמִים עֲשָׂרָה; וְיִתְּנוּ-לָנוּ מִן-הַזֵּרֹעִים, וְנֹאכְלָה--וּמַיִם וְנִשְׁתֶּה.
"Test your servants, please, ten days: let them give us some of the vegetables to eat, and water to drink;note
13
וְיֵרָאוּ לְפָנֶיךָ, מַרְאֵינוּ, וּמַרְאֵה הַיְלָדִים, הָאֹכְלִים אֵת פַּת-בַּג הַמֶּלֶךְ; וְכַאֲשֶׁר תִּרְאֵה, עֲשֵׂה עִם-עֲבָדֶיךָ.
then let our appearance be seen before you, and the appearance of the youths who eat the king's food — and as you see, deal with your servants."note
14
וַיִּשְׁמַע לָהֶם, לַדָּבָר הַזֶּה; וַיְנַסֵּם, יָמִים עֲשָׂרָה.
And he listened to them in this matter, and tested them ten days.note
15
וּמִקְצָת, יָמִים עֲשָׂרָה, נִרְאָה מַרְאֵיהֶם טוֹב, וּבְרִיאֵי בָּשָׂר: מִן-כָּל-הַיְלָדִים--הָאֹכְלִים, אֵת פַּת-בַּג הַמֶּלֶךְ.
And at the end of ten days their appearance was seen to be better, and fuller in flesh, than all the youths who ate the king's food.note
16
וַיְהִי הַמֶּלְצַר, נֹשֵׂא אֶת-פַּת-בָּגָם, וְיֵין, מִשְׁתֵּיהֶם; וְנֹתֵן לָהֶם, זֵרְעֹנִים.
So the steward carried away their food-portion and the wine they were to drink, and kept giving them vegetables.note
17
וְהַיְלָדִים הָאֵלֶּה אַרְבַּעְתָּם, נָתַן לָהֶם הָאֱלֹהִים מַדָּע וְהַשְׂכֵּל בְּכָל-סֵפֶר וְחָכְמָה; וְדָנִיֵּאל הֵבִין, בְּכָל-חָזוֹן וַחֲלֹמוֹת.
And these four youths — God gave them knowledge and skill in all writing and wisdom; and Daniel had understanding in every vision and dream.note
18
וּלְמִקְצָת, הַיָּמִים, אֲשֶׁר-אָמַר הַמֶּלֶךְ, לַהֲבִיאָם; וַיְבִיאֵם שַׂר הַסָּרִיסִים, לִפְנֵי נְבֻכַדְנֶצַּר.
And at the end of the days the king had set for bringing them in, the chief of the officials brought them before Nebuchadnezzar.note
19
וַיְדַבֵּר אִתָּם, הַמֶּלֶךְ, וְלֹא נִמְצָא מִכֻּלָּם, כְּדָנִיֵּאל חֲנַנְיָה מִישָׁאֵל וַעֲזַרְיָה; וַיַּעַמְדוּ, לִפְנֵי הַמֶּלֶךְ.
And the king spoke with them, and among them all none was found like Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah; and they stood before the king.note
20
וְכֹל, דְּבַר חָכְמַת בִּינָה, אֲשֶׁר-בִּקֵּשׁ מֵהֶם, הַמֶּלֶךְ--וַיִּמְצָאֵם עֶשֶׂר יָדוֹת, עַל כָּל-הַחַרְטֻמִּים הָאַשָּׁפִים, אֲשֶׁר, בְּכָל-מַלְכוּתוֹ.
And in every matter of wisdom and understanding that the king sought from them, he found them ten hands better than all the magicians and enchanters in his whole kingdom.note
21
וַיְהִי, דָּנִיֵּאל, עַד-שְׁנַת אַחַת, לְכוֹרֶשׁ הַמֶּלֶךְ. {פ}
And Daniel continued until the first year of King Cyrus.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–2 · וַיְבִיאֵם אֶרֶץ-שִׁנְעָר vayvi'em erets-Shinar

A fourth book opens on this shelf — and it is the strangest one yet: half court-tales (chapters 1–6), half apocalyptic visions (7–12), and written in two languages — Hebrew here, but at 2:4 the book will switch to Aramaic in mid-verse and stay there through chapter 7: the Bible's longest stretch of its second tongue, the diplomatic language of the empires Daniel served. The Hebrew Bible shelves Daniel among the Writings, between Esther and Ezra; the Greek tradition behind the English Bibles moved him to the Prophets, after Ezekiel. And the book carries the sharpest dating debate on this site so far, reported per the house rule: its setting is the sixth-century exile; much modern scholarship dates the finished book to the second century BC (Greek loanwords in chapter 3's orchestra; visions whose detail tracks history precisely down to Antiochus IV and blurs after 164 BC), while the traditional reading holds the sixth-century claim the book itself makes. Both cases have real arguments; this translation renders the text and files the dispute where it belongs — here.

"The third year of Jehoiakim" — and a date you can check. 605 BC: the year Nebuchadnezzar crushed Egypt at Carchemish and swept south — a campaign fixed by the Babylonian Chronicle tablets, which makes this the first verse on this site pinned by a document outside the Bible (the Chronology page's "third clock," engaged at last). Jeremiah 25:1 calls the same year Jehoiakim's fourth — Babylonian counting starts a reign's "year one" at the first New Year, Judean counting at the accession itself; both point at 605. The theology is in the verb: "the Lord GAVE Jehoiakim into his hand" — natan: Babylon did not win, God handed over; and the same verb will structure the chapter (God gave the king, v2; gave kindness, v9; gave knowledge, v17 — every power in this story is on loan). The vessels go "to the land of Shinar" — not the ordinary word for Babylonia but the archaic name of the tower (Genesis 10:10; 11:2; NIV flattens it to "Babylonia," KJV keeps Shinar, as here): the narrator files this empire, from its first verse, under Babel. One more quiet fact: the divine name never appears in this chapter — "the Lord" (Adonai) and "God" (Elohim) only; Jehovah enters Daniel only in the prayer of chapter 9.

Verses 3–7 · וַיָּשֶׂם לָהֶם … שֵׁמוֹת vayasem lahem … shemot

The empire harvests elites. Royal seed, nobles, "youths in whom was no blemish" (KJV "children in whom was no blemish" — the vocabulary of sacrificial flawlessness, applied to boys), handsome, brilliant — and enrolled in a three-year course in "the writing and tongue of the Chaldeans": cuneiform — Akkadian, plus the scholars' dead Sumerian — the hardest scribal curriculum on earth, all tablet-lists, omen-series and mathematics. Babylon absorbed conquered nations by educating their best (and feeding them: the daily pat-bag, "the king's food" — a Persian loanword sitting in the Hebrew, one of this book's tell-tale foreign words; KJV "the king's meat" — meat = food, the archaism flagged since Genesis 1 — ASV, charmingly, "the king's dainties").

The renamings. Each of the four names carries God: Dani-El, "El is my judge"; Chanan-Yah, "Yah has been gracious"; Misha-El, "who is what El is?"; Azar-Yah, "Yah has helped." Each replacement smuggles in a Babylonian god: Belteshazzar (Bel, protect his life!), Abednego — "servant of Nebo," with the god's name apparently garbled to Nego (scribes misspelling idols on purpose is a real and delicious habit), Shadrach and Meshach obscure. Readers of this site have watched renaming since "your name shall be Abraham" (Genesis 17:5) — and this is that thread, inverted: God renames to bless; the empire renames to erase. Watch the narrator's quiet resistance: the book goes right on calling them Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah.

Verses 8–14 · וַיָּשֶׂם דָּנִיֵּאל עַל-לִבּוֹ vayasem Daniel al-libbo

"Daniel set on his heart." (KJV "purposed in his heart.") The book's hinge sentence — and its puzzle: why would the food defile (yitga'al)? Kosher law does not ban wine, and vegetables were not the only clean option on a royal menu. The readings: meat from idol-sacrifices; unclean species; or — perhaps likeliest — the politics of the table: to eat the king's portion is to be the king's man, and Daniel draws the line at dependence, not diet. Whatever the reason, watch the manner: no protest, no bid for martyrdom — a polite request, and "God gave Daniel kindness and compassion" — chesed (the covenant word of Genesis 19:19) and rachamim, womb-feeling, granted this time in a foreign court.

Ten days of vegetables — history's first controlled trial. A test group, a control group ("the youths who eat the king's food"), a fixed duration, an observable endpoint: "as you see, deal with your servants." The design is so clean that medical historians regularly cite it as the earliest recorded comparative dietary experiment. The word for the diet, zero'im — seed-food — occurs only here in the Bible, and the KJV gave English one of its best archaisms for it: "give us pulse to eat."
