καὶ ἰδοὺ ἔσῃ σιωπῶν καὶ μὴ δυνάμενος λαλῆσαι ἄχρι ἧς ἡμέρας γένηται ταῦτα, ἀνθʼ ὧν οὐκ ἐπίστευσας τοῖς λόγοις μου, οἵτινες πληρωθήσονται εἰς τὸν καιρὸν αὐτῶν.
And behold, you will be silent and unable to speak until the day these things happen, because you did not believe my words, which will be fulfilled in their time."note
21
Καὶ ἦν ὁ λαὸς προσδοκῶν τὸν Ζαχαρίαν, καὶ ἐθαύμαζον ἐν τῷ χρονίζειν ἐν τῷ ναῷ αὐτόν.
And the people were waiting for Zechariah, and they wondered at his lingering in the sanctuary.note
22
ἐξελθὼν δὲ οὐκ ἐδύνατο λαλῆσαι αὐτοῖς, καὶ ἐπέγνωσαν ὅτι ὀπτασίαν ἑώρακεν ἐν τῷ ναῷ· καὶ αὐτὸς ἦν διανεύων αὐτοῖς, καὶ διέμενεν κωφός.
But when he came out he could not speak to them, and they realised that he had seen a vision in the sanctuary; and he kept making signs to them and remained mute.note
23
καὶ ἐγένετο ὡς ἐπλήσθησαν αἱ ἡμέραι τῆς λειτουργίας αὐτοῦ, ἀπῆλθεν εἰς τὸν οἶκον αὐτοῦ.
And it happened, when the days of his service were completed, that he went away to his house.note
24
Μετὰ δὲ ταύτας τὰς ἡμέρας συνέλαβεν Ἐλισάβετ ἡ γυνὴ αὐτοῦ· καὶ περιέκρυβεν ἑαυτὴν μῆνας πέντε, λέγουσα
Now after these days Elizabeth his wife conceived, and she kept herself hidden five months, saying,note
25
ὅτι Οὕτως μοι πεποίηκεν κύριος ἐν ἡμέραις αἷς ἐπεῖδεν ἀφελεῖν ὄνειδός μου ἐν ἀνθρώποις.
"Thus the Lord has done for me in the days when he looked on me, to take away my disgrace among people."note
26
Ἐν δὲ τῷ μηνὶ τῷ ἕκτῳ ἀπεστάλη ὁ ἄγγελος Γαβριὴλ ἀπὸ τοῦ θεοῦ εἰς πόλιν τῆς Γαλιλαίας ᾗ ὄνομα Ναζαρὲθ
Now in the sixth month the angel Gabriel was sent from God to a city of Galilee named Nazareth,note
27
πρὸς παρθένον ἐμνηστευμένην ἀνδρὶ ᾧ ὄνομα Ἰωσὴφ ἐξ οἴκου Δαυίδ, καὶ τὸ ὄνομα τῆς παρθένου Μαριάμ.
to a virgin betrothed to a man named Joseph, of the house of David, and the virgin's name was Mary.note
28
καὶ εἰσελθὼν πρὸς αὐτὴν εἶπεν· Χαῖρε, κεχαριτωμένη, ὁ κύριος μετὰ σοῦ
And coming in to her he said, "Rejoice, favoured one — the Lord is with you."note
29
ἡ δὲ ἐπὶ τῷ λόγῳ διεταράχθη καὶ διελογίζετο ποταπὸς εἴη ὁ ἀσπασμὸς οὗτος.
But she was greatly troubled at the word, and she kept wondering what kind of greeting this might be.note
30
καὶ εἶπεν ὁ ἄγγελος αὐτῇ· Μὴ φοβοῦ, Μαριάμ, εὗρες γὰρ χάριν παρὰ τῷ θεῷ·
And the angel said to her, "Do not be afraid, Mary, for you have found favour with God.note
31
καὶ ἰδοὺ συλλήμψῃ ἐν γαστρὶ καὶ τέξῃ υἱόν, καὶ καλέσεις τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ Ἰησοῦν.
And behold, you will conceive in your womb and bear a son, and you shall call his name Jesus.note
32
οὗτος ἔσται μέγας καὶ υἱὸς Ὑψίστου κληθήσεται, καὶ δώσει αὐτῷ κύριος ὁ θεὸς τὸν θρόνον Δαυὶδ τοῦ πατρὸς αὐτοῦ,
He will be great and will be called Son of the Most High, and the Lord God will give him the throne of David his father,note
33
καὶ βασιλεύσει ἐπὶ τὸν οἶκον Ἰακὼβ εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας, καὶ τῆς βασιλείας αὐτοῦ οὐκ ἔσται τέλος.
and he will reign over the house of Jacob forever, and of his kingdom there will be no end."note
34
εἶπεν δὲ Μαριὰμ πρὸς τὸν ἄγγελον· Πῶς ἔσται τοῦτο, ἐπεὶ ἄνδρα οὐ γινώσκω;
And Mary said to the angel, "How will this be, since I do not know a man?"note
35
καὶ ἀποκριθεὶς ὁ ἄγγελος εἶπεν αὐτῇ· Πνεῦμα ἅγιον ἐπελεύσεται ἐπὶ σέ, καὶ δύναμις Ὑψίστου ἐπισκιάσει σοι· διὸ καὶ τὸ γεννώμενον ἅγιον κληθήσεται, υἱὸς θεοῦ·
And the angel answered and said to her, "The Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you; therefore the one to be born will be called holy, the Son of God.note
36
καὶ ἰδοὺ Ἐλισάβετ ἡ συγγενίς σου καὶ αὐτὴ συνείληφεν υἱὸν ἐν γήρει αὐτῆς, καὶ οὗτος μὴν ἕκτος ἐστὶν αὐτῇ τῇ καλουμένῃ στείρᾳ·
And behold, Elizabeth your relative — she too has conceived a son in her old age, and this is the sixth month for her who was called barren;note
37
ὅτι οὐκ ἀδυνατήσει παρὰ τοῦ θεοῦ πᾶν ῥῆμα.
for no word will be impossible with God."note
38
εἶπεν δὲ Μαριάμ· Ἰδοὺ ἡ δούλη κυρίου· γένοιτό μοι κατὰ τὸ ῥῆμά σου. καὶ ἀπῆλθεν ἀπʼ αὐτῆς ὁ ἄγγελος.
And Mary said, "Behold, the bondslave of the Lord; may it happen to me according to your word." And the angel went away from her.note
39
Ἀναστᾶσα δὲ Μαριὰμ ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις ταύταις ἐπορεύθη εἰς τὴν ὀρεινὴν μετὰ σπουδῆς εἰς πόλιν Ἰούδα,
And Mary arose in those days and went into the hill country with haste, to a city of Judah,note
40
καὶ εἰσῆλθεν εἰς τὸν οἶκον Ζαχαρίου καὶ ἠσπάσατο τὴν Ἐλισάβετ.
and she entered the house of Zechariah and greeted Elizabeth.note
41
καὶ ἐγένετο ὡς ἤκουσεν τὸν ἀσπασμὸν τῆς Μαρίας ἡ Ἐλισάβετ, ἐσκίρτησεν τὸ βρέφος ἐν τῇ κοιλίᾳ αὐτῆς, καὶ ἐπλήσθη πνεύματος ἁγίου ἡ Ἐλισάβετ,
And it happened, when Elizabeth heard Mary's greeting, the baby leaped in her womb, and Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Spirit,note
42
καὶ ἀνεφώνησεν κραυγῇ μεγάλῃ καὶ εἶπεν· Εὐλογημένη σὺ ἐν γυναιξίν, καὶ εὐλογημένος ὁ καρπὸς τῆς κοιλίας σου.
and she cried out with a loud cry and said, "Blessed are you among women, and blessed is the fruit of your womb.note
43
καὶ πόθεν μοι τοῦτο ἵνα ἔλθῃ ἡ μήτηρ τοῦ κυρίου μου πρὸς ἐμέ
And how has this come to me, that the mother of my Lord should come to me?note
44
ἰδοὺ γὰρ ὡς ἐγένετο ἡ φωνὴ τοῦ ἀσπασμοῦ σου εἰς τὰ ὦτά μου, ἐσκίρτησεν ἐν ἀγαλλιάσει τὸ βρέφος ἐν τῇ κοιλίᾳ μου.
For behold, as the sound of your greeting came into my ears, the baby leaped for gladness in my womb.note
45
καὶ μακαρία ἡ πιστεύσασα ὅτι ἔσται τελείωσις τοῖς λελαλημένοις αὐτῇ παρὰ κυρίου.
And happy is she who believed that there would be a fulfilment of the things spoken to her from the Lord."note
46
Καὶ εἶπεν Μαριάμ· Μεγαλύνει ἡ ψυχή μου τὸν κύριον,
And Mary said:
My soul magnifies the Lord,note
47
καὶ ἠγαλλίασεν τὸ πνεῦμά μου ἐπὶ τῷ θεῷ τῷ σωτῆρί μου·
and my spirit has rejoiced in God my Saviour,note
48
ὅτι ἐπέβλεψεν ἐπὶ τὴν ταπείνωσιν τῆς δούλης αὐτοῦ, ἰδοὺ γὰρ ἀπὸ τοῦ νῦν μακαριοῦσίν με πᾶσαι αἱ γενεαί·
because he has looked on the low estate of his bondslave.
For behold, from now on all generations will call me happy,note
49
ὅτι ἐποίησέν μοι μεγάλα ὁ δυνατός, καὶ ἅγιον τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ,
because the Mighty One has done great things for me —
and holy is his name,note
50
καὶ τὸ ἔλεος αὐτοῦ εἰς γενεὰς καὶ γενεὰς τοῖς φοβουμένοις αὐτόν.
and his mercy is for generations and generations
on those who fear him.note
51
Ἐποίησεν κράτος ἐν βραχίονι αὐτοῦ, διεσκόρπισεν ὑπερηφάνους διανοίᾳ καρδίας αὐτῶν·
He has done a mighty deed with his arm;
he has scattered the proud in the thoughts of their hearts.note
52
καθεῖλεν δυνάστας ἀπὸ θρόνων καὶ ὕψωσεν ταπεινούς,
He has brought down rulers from thrones
and lifted up the lowly;note
53
πεινῶντας ἐνέπλησεν ἀγαθῶν καὶ πλουτοῦντας ἐξαπέστειλεν κενούς.
the hungry he has filled with good things,
and the rich he has sent away empty.note
54
ἀντελάβετο Ἰσραὴλ παιδὸς αὐτοῦ, μνησθῆναι ἐλέους,
He has taken hold of Israel his servant,
to remember mercy —note
55
καθὼς ἐλάλησεν πρὸς τοὺς πατέρας ἡμῶν, τῷ Ἀβραὰμ καὶ τῷ σπέρματι αὐτοῦ εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα.
just as he spoke to our fathers —
to Abraham and to his seed forever.note
56
Ἔμεινεν δὲ Μαριὰμ σὺν αὐτῇ ὡς μῆνας τρεῖς, καὶ ὑπέστρεψεν εἰς τὸν οἶκον αὐτῆς.
And Mary stayed with her about three months, and returned to her house.note
57
Τῇ δὲ Ἐλισάβετ ἐπλήσθη ὁ χρόνος τοῦ τεκεῖν αὐτήν, καὶ ἐγέννησεν υἱόν.
Now the time was fulfilled for Elizabeth to give birth, and she bore a son.note
58
καὶ ἤκουσαν οἱ περίοικοι καὶ οἱ συγγενεῖς αὐτῆς ὅτι ἐμεγάλυνεν κύριος τὸ ἔλεος αὐτοῦ μετʼ αὐτῆς, καὶ συνέχαιρον αὐτῇ.
And her neighbours and relatives heard that the Lord had magnified his mercy toward her, and they rejoiced with her.note
59
Καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ὀγδόῃ ἦλθον περιτεμεῖν τὸ παιδίον, καὶ ἐκάλουν αὐτὸ ἐπὶ τῷ ὀνόματι τοῦ πατρὸς αὐτοῦ Ζαχαρίαν.
And it happened on the eighth day that they came to circumcise the child, and they were going to call it Zechariah, after the name of its father.note
60
καὶ ἀποκριθεῖσα ἡ μήτηρ αὐτοῦ εἶπεν· Οὐχί, ἀλλὰ κληθήσεται Ἰωάννης.
And its mother answered and said, "No — he shall be called John."note
61
καὶ εἶπαν πρὸς αὐτὴν ὅτι Οὐδείς ἐστιν ἐκ τῆς συγγενείας σου ὃς καλεῖται τῷ ὀνόματι τούτῳ.
And they said to her, "There is no one among your relatives who is called by this name."note
62
ἐνένευον δὲ τῷ πατρὶ αὐτοῦ τὸ τί ἂν θέλοι καλεῖσθαι αὐτό
And they made signs to its father, as to what he wanted it to be called.note
63
καὶ αἰτήσας πινακίδιον ἔγραψεν λέγων· Ἰωάννης ἐστὶν ὄνομα αὐτοῦ. καὶ ἐθαύμασαν πάντες.
And asking for a writing tablet he wrote, saying, "John is his name." And they all marvelled.note
64
ἀνεῴχθη δὲ τὸ στόμα αὐτοῦ παραχρῆμα καὶ ἡ γλῶσσα αὐτοῦ, καὶ ἐλάλει εὐλογῶν τὸν θεόν.
And immediately his mouth was opened and his tongue, and he spoke, blessing God.note
65
καὶ ἐγένετο ἐπὶ πάντας φόβος τοὺς περιοικοῦντας αὐτούς, καὶ ἐν ὅλῃ τῇ ὀρεινῇ τῆς Ἰουδαίας διελαλεῖτο πάντα τὰ ῥήματα ταῦτα,
And fear came upon all who lived around them, and in all the hill country of Judea all these things were being talked about.note
66
καὶ ἔθεντο πάντες οἱ ἀκούσαντες ἐν τῇ καρδίᾳ αὐτῶν, λέγοντες· Τί ἄρα τὸ παιδίον τοῦτο ἔσται; καὶ γὰρ χεὶρ κυρίου ἦν μετʼ αὐτοῦ.
And all who heard laid them up in their hearts, saying, "What then will this child be?" For indeed the hand of the Lord was with him.note
67
Καὶ Ζαχαρίας ὁ πατὴρ αὐτοῦ ἐπλήσθη πνεύματος ἁγίου καὶ ἐπροφήτευσεν λέγων·
And Zechariah his father was filled with the Holy Spirit and prophesied, saying:note
68
Εὐλογητὸς κύριος ὁ θεὸς τοῦ Ἰσραήλ, ὅτι ἐπεσκέψατο καὶ ἐποίησεν λύτρωσιν τῷ λαῷ αὐτοῦ,
Blessed be the Lord, the God of Israel,
because he has visited and worked redemption for his people,note
69
καὶ ἤγειρεν κέρας σωτηρίας ἡμῖν ἐν οἴκῳ Δαυὶδ παιδὸς αὐτοῦ,
and has raised up a horn of salvation for us
in the house of David his servant,note
70
καθὼς ἐλάλησεν διὰ στόματος τῶν ἁγίων ἀπʼ αἰῶνος προφητῶν αὐτοῦ,
just as he spoke through the mouth of his holy prophets from of old,note
71
σωτηρίαν ἐξ ἐχθρῶν ἡμῶν καὶ ἐκ χειρὸς πάντων τῶν μισούντων ἡμᾶς,
salvation from our enemies
and from the hand of all who hate us,note
72
ποιῆσαι ἔλεος μετὰ τῶν πατέρων ἡμῶν καὶ μνησθῆναι διαθήκης ἁγίας αὐτοῦ,
to show mercy to our fathers
and to remember his holy covenant,note
73
ὅρκον ὃν ὤμοσεν πρὸς Ἀβραὰμ τὸν πατέρα ἡμῶν, τοῦ δοῦναι ἡμῖν
the oath that he swore to Abraham our father, to grant usnote
74
ἀφόβως ἐκ χειρὸς ἐχθρῶν ῥυσθέντας λατρεύειν αὐτῷ
that, rescued from the hand of enemies,
we might serve him without fear,note
75
ἐν ὁσιότητι καὶ δικαιοσύνῃ ἐνώπιον αὐτοῦ πάσαις ταῖς ἡμέραις ἡμῶν.
in holiness and righteousness before him
all our days.note
76
καὶ σὺ δέ παιδίον, προφήτης Ὑψίστου κληθήσῃ, προπορεύσῃ γὰρ ἐνώπιον κυρίου ἑτοιμάσαι ὁδοὺς αὐτοῦ,
And you, child, will be called a prophet of the Most High,
for you will go on ahead before the Lord to prepare his ways,note
77
τοῦ δοῦναι γνῶσιν σωτηρίας τῷ λαῷ αὐτοῦ ἐν ἀφέσει ἁμαρτιῶν αὐτῶν,
to give his people knowledge of salvation
in the forgiveness of their sins,note
78
διὰ σπλάγχνα ἐλέους θεοῦ ἡμῶν, ἐν οἷς ἐπισκέψεται ἡμᾶς ἀνατολὴ ἐξ ὕψους,
through the tender mercy of our God,
by which the dawn from on high will visit us,note
79
ἐπιφᾶναι τοῖς ἐν σκότει καὶ σκιᾷ θανάτου καθημένοις, τοῦ κατευθῦναι τοὺς πόδας ἡμῶν εἰς ὁδὸν εἰρήνης.
to shine on those sitting in darkness and the shadow of death,
to guide our feet into the way of peace.note
80
Τὸ δὲ παιδίον ηὔξανε καὶ ἐκραταιοῦτο πνεύματι, καὶ ἦν ἐν ταῖς ἐρήμοις ἕως ἡμέρας ἀναδείξεως αὐτοῦ πρὸς τὸν Ἰσραήλ.
And the child grew and became strong in spirit, and he was in the wilderness until the day of his public showing to Israel.note

Translator’s Notes

Verses 1–4 · a doctor writes a Greek preface

The only Gospel that opens like a book. These four verses are a single, balanced, classical Greek sentence — the most polished Greek in the New Testament — the kind of periodic preface a Greek historian or physician would put at the head of a serious work. Then Luke drops it and shifts, from verse 5 on, into the plain Septuagint-flavoured Greek of the Hebrew scriptures. He can write like Thucydides and chooses to write like the Old Testament: the announcement is deliberate. This is the one place in the four Gospels where the author steps forward and speaks as I.

"Most excellent Theophilus." The name means "friend of God" or "loved by God" — possibly a real patron, possibly every reader who loves God. Kratistos ("most excellent") is a form of address for a person of rank; Luke uses it again for the governors Felix and Festus in Acts. He addresses the same Theophilus at the start of Acts (1:1), which is the seam that makes Luke and Acts one two-volume work by one author.

"That you may know the certainty." Asphaleia — firmness, security, the opposite of stumbling. Luke says outright why he is writing: not to move the emotions but to give solid ground under things the reader has already been catechised in (katecheo, the root of "catechism"). He is a careful witness who "followed all things closely from the start," and early tradition names him a physician and a travelling companion of Paul. KJV "the certainty of those things"; the NWT "the certainty."

Verses 5–7 · a barren couple, on purpose

"In the days of Herod." Luke anchors everything in datable history — Herod the Great, the priestly courses, later a Roman census, a named emperor and governor. The story is placed inside the calendar of the world, not in "once upon a time."

The division of Abijah. David split the priests into twenty-four rotating courses (1 Chronicles 24); Abijah's was the eighth. Zechariah is an ordinary rural priest, married to a daughter of Aaron — priestly on both sides. Both are called "righteous" and "blameless," which makes their childlessness a grief, not a punishment.

⚠ Barren and advanced in years. The words are chosen to make the reader hear the old stories: Sarah and Abraham, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah, the Shunammite. Israel's whole history begins with wombs God opens against the odds. Luke's Gospel opens the same way — and then, one chapter later, will out-do all of them with a womb that needs no man at all.

Verses 8–10 · the lot of a lifetime

The lot fell to him. With so many priests, burning the incense in the sanctuary was a once-in-a-lifetime honour assigned by lot; most priests never drew it. Zechariah is at the single greatest moment of his priestly life — alone in the holy place, at the hour of incense, the smoke standing for the prayers of the people — when the answer to his own long-abandoned prayer walks up beside the altar.

The people praying outside. The whole crowd is at prayer in the courts while the priest is within; the gospel's first scene is a congregation waiting outside a closed door for a man who is taking too long.

Verses 11–17 · a second Elijah

Never wine or strong drink. John is marked out from the womb like a lifelong Nazirite (Numbers 6) — the vow of Samson and Samuel — and "filled with the Holy Spirit from his mother's womb," which verse 41 will show literally when he leaps at Mary's voice.

"In the spirit and power of Elijah." The angel closes by quoting Malachi — the last verse of the last prophet: "he will turn the hearts of fathers to children" (Malachi 4:5–6). Malachi promised Elijah before the great day of the Lord; Gabriel says the promise is now walking toward its fulfilment. John is not Elijah returned in body, but Elijah's role and clothing and fire, sent to "make ready a people prepared." The four-hundred-year silence since Malachi ends with a sentence of Malachi.

Verses 18–20 · the priest who could not believe

⚠ "By what shall I know this?" Watch this line, because Luke means you to hold it against Mary's question in verse 34. Zechariah, the righteous priest who has spent his life at the altar, asks for a proof and is silenced for his unbelief. Mary, an unmarried girl in a nowhere town, asks only how, and is answered and blessed. The learned man of religion doubts; the young woman believes. Luke's Gospel keeps making that reversal, and it starts here, in the temple, with a mute priest.

"I am Gabriel, who stands before God." The same angel who came to Daniel with the timetable of the end (Daniel 8–9) now comes to time the beginning. The silence is both a punishment and a sign: Zechariah asked for a sign, and became one.

Verses 21–25 · the taking away of a disgrace

"To take away my disgrace." Oneidos — reproach, shame. Childlessness was read as a reproach a woman carried in public; Elizabeth's five months of hiding are the quiet of someone scarcely daring to believe. Her words echo Rachel's at the birth of Joseph, "God has taken away my reproach" (Genesis 30:23) — another closed womb opened, another child who would save a family and a nation.

Verses 26–33 · favoured one

⚠ "Rejoice, favoured one" — the words behind the "Hail Mary." Kecharitomene is a perfect passive participle of charitoo, from charis, grace/favour: "one who has been graced, favoured." The old Latin rendered it gratia plena, "full of grace," which the "Hail Mary" carries — but the Greek says she is one who has received favour, not one who is a reservoir of it to dispense. The verse names her the object of God's kindness, and verse 30 spells it plainly: "you have found favour with God," the same thing said of Noah. This translation keeps the plain sense and flags the famous crux rather than smuggling a doctrine into a participle. KJV "highly favoured"; the ESV and NWT "favored one."

The throne of David, and no end. Gabriel folds the covenant to David (2 Samuel 7) into the message: an heir on David's throne over the house of Jacob, a kingdom "with no end." It is the oldest royal promise in Israel, delivered to a girl in Nazareth.

Verses 34–38 · the overshadowing, and the fiat

"Since I do not know a man." A plain statement of virginity — parthenos, virgin, from verse 27. Her question is not Zechariah's demand for proof; it is a real "how," and it is answered.

"Will overshadow you." Episkiazo — the verb the Greek Old Testament uses for the cloud of glory that "overshadowed" the tabernacle so that the presence of God filled it (Exodus 40:35). The same shadow that once filled the tent now falls on a woman; she becomes the place where God dwells.

⚠ "No word will be impossible with God." Almost exactly the sentence the visitor spoke to Sarah at the oaks of Mamre — "Is anything too hard for the LORD?" (Genesis 18:14) — where the same word, rhema, can mean both "word" and "thing." Luke ties the newest birth to the oldest.

"The bondslave of the Lord." Doule — a female slave, the strongest word for it. Mary answers not with resignation but with a soldier's assent: "may it happen to me according to your word." The whole scene turns on it, and where Zechariah was struck dumb, Mary's yes becomes, twelve verses later, a song.

Verses 39–45 · the leap in the womb

The baby leaped. Skirtao — to leap, skip; the Greek Old Testament uses it for the children struggling in Rebekah's womb (Genesis 25:22). Unborn John recognises unborn Jesus and jumps; the forerunner is already going ahead, before either is born. Elizabeth, filled with the Spirit, is the first human being in this Gospel to confess who Mary carries: "the mother of my Lord."

"Happy is she who believed." Makaria — the beatitude word, "happy, blessed." Elizabeth blesses Mary not for her womb but for her faith — and the line lands like a verdict on the whole chapter, because it is exactly what Zechariah failed to do. Belief, not biology, is what she is called happy for.

Verses 46–55 · the Magnificat

Mary's song. Named the Magnificat from its first Latin word, "magnifies." It is built almost entirely out of the Old Testament, and above all out of Hannah's song at the birth of Samuel (1 Samuel 2:1–10) — another poor woman whose God-given child would turn a nation. Mary sings in the past tense, as though the reversal is already done.

"The low estate of his bondslave." Tapeinosis — lowliness, humiliation. God's regard falls on the lowly, and the song then names the reversal in six hammer-strokes: the proud scattered, rulers pulled off thrones, the lowly lifted, the hungry filled, the rich sent away empty. It is the most politically dangerous poem in the Gospels, and it is sung by a teenage girl. "All generations will call me happy" (makarizo) — the same root as verse 45.

"To Abraham and to his seed forever." The song ends where the promises began: mercy remembered, the oath to Abraham kept. What God is doing is not a new departure but the ancient covenant coming due.

Verses 56–58 · a mercy magnified

"The Lord had magnified his mercy toward her." The verb is the same root as the Magnificat's opening — Mary sang that her soul magnifies the Lord, and now the neighbours say the Lord has magnified his mercy for Elizabeth. The joy the angel promised in verse 14 arrives on schedule.

Verses 59–66 · the naming, and the loosened tongue

"No — he shall be called John." On the eighth day, at the circumcision, the neighbours assume the boy takes his father's name; both parents refuse, insisting on the name the angel gave — John, "the Lord is gracious." Zechariah, still mute, writes it on a tablet, and the moment his obedience matches Elizabeth's, his tongue is freed. The sign given for his doubt is lifted the instant he acts on faith; his first freed word is a blessing of God. The whole hill country senses that a hand is on this child.

Verses 67–79 · the Benedictus — the priest sings back

Zechariah's prophecy. Named the Benedictus, "blessed." The priest who was silenced for unbelief is now filled with the Spirit and opens with praise; his song, like Mary's, is woven from covenant promises — the "horn of salvation" in the house of David (a horn is an OT image of strength, Psalm 132:17), the oath sworn to Abraham, deliverance to serve God "without fear."

⚠ "The dawn from on high." Anatole is a deliberate double word: it means both the rising of the sun and, in the Greek Old Testament, the messianic "Branch" — the shoot promised to sprout from David (Jeremiah 23:5; Zechariah 3:8; 6:12). Zechariah's ears would hear both: a light dawning and a Branch springing. It shines "on those sitting in darkness and the shadow of death," Isaiah's own words (Isaiah 9:2). KJV "the dayspring from on high"; the ESV "the sunrise"; the NWT "a daybreak." Each catches half of a word that carries both.

The child addressed. Zechariah turns and speaks to his eight-day-old son (verse 76): "you will go on ahead before the Lord to prepare his ways." The Elijah role Gabriel named in verse 17 is now spoken over the boy by his own father.

Verse 80 · and then, silence again

"He was in the wilderness until the day of his public showing." With one sentence Luke hides John for thirty years. The forerunner grows up in the desert — the same wilderness where prophets were made — and the curtain falls on him until it rises again in chapter 3, at the Jordan, "the word of God came to John." The song is sung; now comes the long waiting.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the exact / distinct words: “most excellent” for kratistos and “certainty” for asphaleia, keeping the historian’s register of the preface; “favoured one” for kecharitomene, one who has received favour, not a container of it; “bondslave” for doule, the strongest word, kept for both Mary’s yes and her song; “overshadow” for episkiazo, the tabernacle-cloud verb; “happy” for makaria/makarizo, the beatitude word, kept the same in Elizabeth’s blessing and Mary’s song; and “the dawn from on high” for anatole, following the rising-light sense while the note keeps the “Branch” that rides underneath it.

Echoes paid. A barren, aged, righteous couple set against Sarah, Hannah, and every opened womb in Israel; Malachi’s last promise of Elijah (Malachi 4:5–6) quoted over John; “no word will be impossible with God” carried straight from Sarah at Mamre (Genesis 18:14); the Magnificat built on Hannah’s song (1 Samuel 2); the Benedictus on the oath to Abraham and the “Branch” of Jeremiah and Zechariah; and Isaiah’s “those sitting in darkness” (Isaiah 9:2) lit at the end.

Echoes planted. The doubting priest and the believing girl, a reversal the whole Gospel will keep making; the Holy Spirit, poured on Elizabeth, Mary, and Zechariah before a single miracle — Luke’s signature; the Magnificat’s reversal (the lowly lifted, the rich sent away empty), waiting to be paid in the Beatitudes and the woes of chapter 6; the leaping forerunner, going ahead of Jesus before birth as he will at the Jordan; and John hidden “in the wilderness,” the curtain that rises again in chapter 3.

Next in this book: Luke 2 — a decree from Caesar Augustus, a census, a birth in Bethlehem with no room in the inn, shepherds and a sky full of angels, the child presented in the temple to old Simeon and the prophetess Anna, and the one glimpse of Jesus as a boy, left behind in his Father’s house.

Jeremiah 1 — The Mister Translation

1
דִּבְרֵי יִרְמְיָהוּ, בֶּן-חִלְקִיָּהוּ, מִן-הַכֹּהֲנִים אֲשֶׁר בַּעֲנָתוֹת, בְּאֶרֶץ בִּנְיָמִן.
The words of Jeremiah son of Hilkiah, of the priests who were in Anathoth, in the land of Benjamin,note
2
אֲשֶׁר הָיָה דְבַר-יְהוָה אֵלָיו, בִּימֵי יֹאשִׁיָּהוּ בֶן-אָמוֹן מֶלֶךְ יְהוּדָה, בִּשְׁלֹשׁ-עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה, לְמָלְכוֹ.
to whom the word of Jehovah came in the days of Josiah son of Amon, king of Judah, in the thirteenth year of his reign,note
3
וַיְהִי, בִּימֵי יְהוֹיָקִים בֶּן-יֹאשִׁיָּהוּ מֶלֶךְ יְהוּדָה, עַד-תֹּם עַשְׁתֵּי עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה, לְצִדְקִיָּהוּ בֶן-יֹאשִׁיָּהוּ מֶלֶךְ יְהוּדָה--עַד-גְּלוֹת יְרוּשָׁלִַם, בַּחֹדֶשׁ הַחֲמִישִׁי. {פ}
and it came in the days of Jehoiakim son of Josiah, king of Judah, until the end of the eleventh year of Zedekiah son of Josiah, king of Judah — until the exile of Jerusalem, in the fifth month.note
4
וַיְהִי דְבַר-יְהוָה, אֵלַי לֵאמֹר.
And the word of Jehovah came to me, saying:note
5
בְּטֶרֶם אצורך אֶצָּרְךָ בַבֶּטֶן יְדַעְתִּיךָ, וּבְטֶרֶם תֵּצֵא מֵרֶחֶם הִקְדַּשְׁתִּיךָ: נָבִיא לַגּוֹיִם, נְתַתִּיךָ.
"Before I formed you in the belly I knew you, and before you came out of the womb I set you apart; a prophet to the nations I appointed you."note
6
וָאֹמַר, אֲהָהּ אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה, הִנֵּה לֹא-יָדַעְתִּי, דַּבֵּר: כִּי-נַעַר, אָנֹכִי. {ס}
And I said, "Ah, Lord Jehovah! Look, I do not know how to speak, for I am only a youth."note
7
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֵלַי, אַל-תֹּאמַר נַעַר אָנֹכִי: כִּי עַל-כָּל-אֲשֶׁר אֶשְׁלָחֲךָ, תֵּלֵךְ, וְאֵת כָּל-אֲשֶׁר אֲצַוְּךָ, תְּדַבֵּר.
But Jehovah said to me, "Do not say 'I am only a youth,' for to everyone I send you, you shall go, and everything I command you, you shall speak.note
8
אַל-תִּירָא, מִפְּנֵיהֶם: כִּי-אִתְּךָ אֲנִי לְהַצִּלֶךָ, נְאֻם-יְהוָה.
Do not be afraid before them, for I am with you to deliver you — the utterance of Jehovah."note
9
וַיִּשְׁלַח יְהוָה אֶת-יָדוֹ, וַיַּגַּע עַל-פִּי; וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֵלַי, הִנֵּה נָתַתִּי דְבָרַי בְּפִיךָ.
Then Jehovah stretched out his hand and touched my mouth; and Jehovah said to me, "Look, I have put my words in your mouth.note
10
רְאֵה הִפְקַדְתִּיךָ הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה, עַל-הַגּוֹיִם וְעַל-הַמַּמְלָכוֹת, לִנְתוֹשׁ וְלִנְתוֹץ, וּלְהַאֲבִיד וְלַהֲרוֹס--לִבְנוֹת, וְלִנְטוֹעַ. {פ}
See, I have appointed you this day over the nations and over the kingdoms, to uproot and to tear down, to destroy and to overthrow, to build and to plant."note
11
וַיְהִי דְבַר-יְהוָה אֵלַי לֵאמֹר, מָה-אַתָּה רֹאֶה יִרְמְיָהוּ; וָאֹמַר, מַקֵּל שָׁקֵד אֲנִי רֹאֶה.
And the word of Jehovah came to me, saying, "What do you see, Jeremiah?" And I said, "I see a branch of an almond tree."note
12
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֵלַי, הֵיטַבְתָּ לִרְאוֹת: כִּי-שֹׁקֵד אֲנִי עַל-דְּבָרִי, לַעֲשֹׂתוֹ. {ס}
And Jehovah said to me, "You have seen well, for I am watching over my word, to do it."note
13
וַיְהִי דְבַר-יְהוָה אֵלַי שֵׁנִית לֵאמֹר, מָה אַתָּה רֹאֶה; וָאֹמַר, סִיר נָפוּחַ אֲנִי רֹאֶה, וּפָנָיו, מִפְּנֵי צָפוֹנָה.
And the word of Jehovah came to me a second time, saying, "What do you see?" And I said, "I see a boiling pot, and its face is turned away from the north."note
14
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה, אֵלָי: מִצָּפוֹן תִּפָּתַח הָרָעָה, עַל כָּל-יֹשְׁבֵי הָאָרֶץ.
And Jehovah said to me, "Out of the north the disaster shall break loose upon all the inhabitants of the land.note
15
כִּי הִנְנִי קֹרֵא, לְכָל-מִשְׁפְּחוֹת מַמְלְכוֹת צָפוֹנָה--נְאֻם-יְהוָה; וּבָאוּ וְנָתְנוּ אִישׁ כִּסְאוֹ פֶּתַח שַׁעֲרֵי יְרוּשָׁלִַם, וְעַל כָּל-חוֹמֹתֶיהָ סָבִיב, וְעַל, כָּל-עָרֵי יְהוּדָה.
For behold, I am calling all the families of the kingdoms of the north — the utterance of Jehovah — and they shall come, and each shall set his throne at the entrance of the gates of Jerusalem, against all its walls around, and against all the cities of Judah.note
16
וְדִבַּרְתִּי מִשְׁפָּטַי אוֹתָם, עַל כָּל-רָעָתָם--אֲשֶׁר עֲזָבוּנִי, וַיְקַטְּרוּ לֵאלֹהִים אֲחֵרִים, וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ, לְמַעֲשֵׂי יְדֵיהֶם.
And I will pronounce my judgments against them for all their evil — in forsaking me, and burning incense to other gods, and bowing down to the works of their own hands.note
17
וְאַתָּה, תֶּאְזֹר מָתְנֶיךָ, וְקַמְתָּ וְדִבַּרְתָּ אֲלֵיהֶם, אֵת כָּל-אֲשֶׁר אָנֹכִי אֲצַוֶּךָּ; אַל-תֵּחַת, מִפְּנֵיהֶם--פֶּן-אֲחִתְּךָ, לִפְנֵיהֶם.
But you — gird up your loins, and rise and speak to them all that I command you. Do not be shattered before them, lest I shatter you before them.note
18
וַאֲנִי הִנֵּה נְתַתִּיךָ הַיּוֹם, לְעִיר מִבְצָר וּלְעַמּוּד בַּרְזֶל וּלְחֹמוֹת נְחֹשֶׁת--עַל-כָּל-הָאָרֶץ: לְמַלְכֵי יְהוּדָה לְשָׂרֶיהָ, לְכֹהֲנֶיהָ וּלְעַם הָאָרֶץ.
And I, behold, I have made you this day a fortified city, and an iron pillar, and bronze walls, against the whole land — against the kings of Judah, its princes, its priests, and the people of the land.note
19
וְנִלְחֲמוּ אֵלֶיךָ, וְלֹא-יוּכְלוּ לָךְ: כִּי-אִתְּךָ אֲנִי נְאֻם-יְהוָה, לְהַצִּילֶךָ. {פ}
They will fight against you, but they will not prevail against you, for I am with you — the utterance of Jehovah — to deliver you."note

Translator’s Notes

Verses 1–3 · a forty-year word

The superscription frames the longest and most turbulent prophetic career in the Bible. Jeremiah’s word comes “in the thirteenth year of Josiah” (627 BC), runs on through the reign of Jehoiakim, and does not stop “until the eleventh year of Zedekiah… until the exile of Jerusalem, in the fifth month” (586 BC). Forty years, five kings, and one direction: down toward the fall. The book’s frame is a nation’s collapse, and the man appointed to say it was coming.

«Of the priests who were in Anathoth.» Jeremiah is a priest by birth. Anathoth — a Levitical town about three miles northeast of Jerusalem in the territory of Benjamin — was very likely the village to which Solomon banished the priest Abiathar (1 Kings 2:26). A son of a sidelined priestly line, from a small town, called to indict the temple and the throne it stood beside.

Verses 4–5 · known before the womb

«Before I formed you… I knew you.» The call reaches back before Jeremiah exists. “Formed” is yatsar — the potter’s verb, the word for God shaping the man from the dust (Genesis 2:7), and the very sign Jeremiah will later act out at the potter’s house (ch 18). “Knew” (yada) is not information but the intimate, covenant knowing that means chosen. And “set you apart” (qadash) — consecrated, devoted to a purpose — all before birth. KJV “I knew thee… I sanctified thee, and I ordained thee a prophet unto the nations.”

«A prophet to the nations.» Not to Judah only — goyim, the nations; Jeremiah’s oracles will reach Egypt, Moab, Ammon, Edom, and Babylon (chs 46–51). The scope is set in the first sentence God speaks to him. Paul heard his own calling in these very words — “he who set me apart before I was born” (Galatians 1:15).

Verses 6–8 · I am only a youth

«Ah, Lord Jehovah!» Jeremiah’s first word back to God is a groan (ahah) and the reluctant prophet’s objection: “I do not know how to speak, for I am only a youth (na’ar).” He joins a line — Moses “I am not eloquent,” Gideon “my clan is the weakest,” Isaiah “I am a man of unclean lips.” The called protest their inadequacy; it is always true, and it is never the point.

God does not argue the weakness — he overrides it with two commands and a promise. “Do not say ‘I am a youth.’” “Do not be afraid before them.” And the promise that will bookend the whole call: “I am with you to deliver you” (repeated word for word at v19). “The utterance of Jehovah” (ne’um YHWH) is the prophet’s signature stamp, sounded all through the book.

Verses 9–10 · the hand on the mouth, the six verbs

«He touched my mouth.» God stretches out his hand and touches Jeremiah’s lips — the commissioning gesture. Where Isaiah’s mouth was cleansed by a burning coal (Isaiah 6:7), Jeremiah’s is filled: “I have put my words in your mouth” — the mark of the true prophet, who speaks not his own message but God’s (Deuteronomy 18:18).

The six verbs. Verse 10 is the mission statement of the whole book: “to uproot and to tear down, to destroy and to overthrow, to build and to plant.” Four verbs of demolition, two of construction — and in that proportion. Jeremiah’s is mostly a ministry of tearing down (a doomed nation, warned to the very end), but it does not end there: “to build and to plant” is the seed of the hope that flowers in the new covenant (ch 31). The wrecking clears the ground for the planting. KJV “to root out, and to pull down, and to destroy, and to throw down, to build, and to plant.”

Verses 11–12 · the almond and the Watcher

A pun you can only half-carry into English. Jeremiah sees “a branch of an almond tree” — maqqel shaqed; and God answers, “You have seen well, for I am watching (shoqed) over my word to do it.” Shaqed (“almond”) and shoqed (“watching, wakeful”) are nearly the same sound. The almond is the first tree to wake and blossom in the Judean spring — the “watcher tree” — and God is awake over his word, watching to perform it. The vision’s whole point is certainty: what God has said, he is wakeful to do. ⚠ The KJV catches the wordplay differently — “I will hasten my word” — reading the root as speed rather than watchfulness; the ESV and most modern versions read “watching.”

Verses 13–16 · the pot from the north

The second vision. “A boiling pot, its face turned away from the north” — a cauldron tilting so its scalding contents pour down toward the south, toward Judah. “Out of the north the disaster (ra’ah) shall break loose.” The enemy from the north is Babylon, whose armies came down the fertile-crescent road from the north though Babylon itself lay to the east; their kings “set each his throne at the entrance of the gates of Jerusalem” — the picture of conquest, the invader holding court where the city once judged.

The reason, stated plainly (v16): “for all their evil — in forsaking me, and burning incense to other gods, and bowing down to the works of their own hands.” Idolatry is the root charge of the whole book: a people who traded the living God for gods they made with their hands. The judgment is not arbitrary; it is a marriage betrayed.

Verses 17–19 · an iron pillar

«Gird up your loins.» The charge to the prophet: tuck up your robe to work or run, stand, and speak everything — and “do not be shattered (chatat) before them, lest I shatter you before them.” The pun is a warning wrapped in a command: fear them, and you will be broken; the only safety is obedience. KJV “be not dismayed at their faces, lest I confound thee before them.”

«A fortified city, an iron pillar, bronze walls.» God makes the reluctant youth a fortress against the whole establishment — kings, princes, priests, and people alike. ⚠ Note carefully what the promise is and is not: “they will fight against you” — and they will (Jeremiah will be beaten, put in the stocks, thrown down a cistern, and left to die) — “but they will not prevail against you, for I am with you… to deliver you.” Not a life without attack, but a life that cannot be destroyed. The call closes on the very promise it opened with (v8): I am with you.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the exact / distinct words: “formed” for yatsar, the potter’s verb that will become a whole sign-act (ch 18); “knew” for yada and “set apart” for qadash, the covenant-and-consecration words of the prenatal call; “a youth” for na’ar, the reluctant prophet’s objection; the six verbs “uproot / tear down / destroy / overthrow / build / plant” kept four-to-two; “watching” for shoqed, held against the “almond” (shaqed) so the pun is at least visible; and “shattered” for chatat, kept the same in the warning “do not be shattered… lest I shatter you.”

Echoes paid. Yatsar forming the man from the dust (Genesis 2:7); the reluctant-prophet line of Moses, Gideon, and Isaiah; the mouth touched, as Isaiah’s was by the coal (Isaiah 6), and filled as the prophet-like-Moses is (Deuteronomy 18:18); the “enemy from the north” that will be named Babylon; and the indictment — forsaking Jehovah for other gods — that the tariff of the last kings turns on (Jer 22:9).

Echoes planted. The six verbs, the program of the whole book, whose two hopeful ones flower in the new covenant (ch 31); the promise “I am with you to deliver you,” which bookends this call (vv8, 19) and is the rope Jeremiah clings to in his darkest confession (20:11); and the iron pillar made of a frightened boy — a man who will be beaten, jailed, and thrown in a cistern, and never destroyed.

This library already carries Jeremiah 20–22 (Pashhur and the stocks, the final siege, the tariff of the last kings). Next in sequence: Jeremiah 2 — the great opening indictment, “my people have committed two evils: they have forsaken me, the fountain of living waters, and hewn out for themselves broken cisterns that can hold no water.”

Jeremiah 20 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיִּשְׁמַע פַּשְׁחוּר בֶּן-אִמֵּר הַכֹּהֵן, וְהוּא-פָקִיד נָגִיד בְּבֵית יְהוָה, אֶת-יִרְמְיָהוּ, נִבָּא אֶת-הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה.
Now Pashhur son of Immer, the priest — and he was chief overseer in the house of Jehovah — heard Jeremiah prophesying these things.note
2
וַיַּכֶּה פַשְׁחוּר, אֵת יִרְמְיָהוּ הַנָּבִיא; וַיִּתֵּן אֹתוֹ עַל-הַמַּהְפֶּכֶת, אֲשֶׁר בְּשַׁעַר בִּנְיָמִן הָעֶלְיוֹן, אֲשֶׁר, בְּבֵית יְהוָה.
And Pashhur struck Jeremiah the prophet, and put him in the stocks at the upper Benjamin Gate, which was by the house of Jehovah.note
3
וַיְהִי, מִמָּחֳרָת, וַיֹּצֵא פַשְׁחוּר אֶת-יִרְמְיָהוּ, מִן-הַמַּהְפָּכֶת; וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו יִרְמְיָהוּ, לֹא פַשְׁחוּר קָרָא יְהוָה שְׁמֶךָ--כִּי, אִם-מָגוֹר מִסָּבִיב. {ס}
And so it was on the next day: Pashhur brought Jeremiah out of the stocks. And Jeremiah said to him: "Not Pashhur has Jehovah called your name — but Magor-Missaviv.note
4
כִּי כֹה אָמַר יְהוָה הִנְנִי נֹתֶנְךָ לְמָגוֹר לְךָ וּלְכָל-אֹהֲבֶיךָ, וְנָפְלוּ בְּחֶרֶב אֹיְבֵיהֶם--וְעֵינֶיךָ רֹאוֹת; וְאֶת-כָּל-יְהוּדָה, אֶתֵּן בְּיַד מֶלֶךְ-בָּבֶל, וְהִגְלָם בָּבֶלָה, וְהִכָּם בֶּחָרֶב.
For thus says Jehovah: Look — I am making you a terror to yourself and to all who love you; and they shall fall by the sword of their enemies, with your own eyes watching. And all Judah I will give into the hand of the king of Babylon, and he will exile them to Babylon, and strike them with the sword.note
5
וְנָתַתִּי, אֶת-כָּל-חֹסֶן הָעִיר הַזֹּאת, וְאֶת-כָּל-יְגִיעָהּ, וְאֶת-כָּל-יְקָרָהּ; וְאֵת כָּל-אוֹצְרוֹת מַלְכֵי יְהוּדָה, אֶתֵּן בְּיַד אֹיְבֵיהֶם, וּבְזָזוּם וּלְקָחוּם, וֶהֱבִיאוּם בָּבֶלָה.
And I will give all the wealth of this city, and all its produce, and all its precious things — and all the treasures of the kings of Judah I will give into the hand of their enemies; and they will plunder them, and take them, and carry them to Babylon.note
6
וְאַתָּה פַשְׁחוּר, וְכֹל יֹשְׁבֵי בֵיתֶךָ, תֵּלְכוּ, בַּשֶּׁבִי; וּבָבֶל תָּבוֹא, וְשָׁם תָּמוּת וְשָׁם תִּקָּבֵר--אַתָּה וְכָל-אֹהֲבֶיךָ, אֲשֶׁר-נִבֵּאתָ לָהֶם בַּשָּׁקֶר. {פ}
And you, Pashhur, and all who live in your house — you shall go into captivity. And to Babylon you shall come, and there you shall die, and there you shall be buried — you, and all who love you, to whom you have prophesied falsehood."note
7
פִּתִּיתַנִי יְהוָה וָאֶפָּת, חֲזַקְתַּנִי וַתּוּכָל; הָיִיתִי לִשְׂחוֹק כָּל-הַיּוֹם, כֻּלֹּה לֹעֵג לִי.
You enticed me, Jehovah, and I was enticed; you overpowered me, and you prevailed. I have become a laughingstock all day long — everyone mocks me.note
8
כִּי-מִדֵּי אֲדַבֵּר אֶזְעָק, חָמָס וָשֹׁד אֶקְרָא: כִּי-הָיָה דְבַר-יְהוָה לִי לְחֶרְפָּה וּלְקֶלֶס, כָּל-הַיּוֹם.
For as often as I speak, I cry out — "Violence and ruin!" I call; for the word of Jehovah has become for me a reproach and a derision all day long.note
9
וְאָמַרְתִּי לֹא-אֶזְכְּרֶנּוּ, וְלֹא-אֲדַבֵּר עוֹד בִּשְׁמוֹ, וְהָיָה בְלִבִּי כְּאֵשׁ בֹּעֶרֶת, עָצֻר בְּעַצְמֹתָי; וְנִלְאֵיתִי כַּלְכֵל, וְלֹא אוּכָל.
And I said: I will not remember him, and I will not speak anymore in his name — but it was in my heart like a burning fire, shut up in my bones; and I wore myself out holding it in — and I could not.note
10
כִּי שָׁמַעְתִּי דִּבַּת רַבִּים, מָגוֹר מִסָּבִיב, הַגִּידוּ וְנַגִּידֶנּוּ, כֹּל אֱנוֹשׁ שְׁלֹמִי שֹׁמְרֵי צַלְעִי; אוּלַי יְפֻתֶּה וְנוּכְלָה לוֹ, וְנִקְחָה נִקְמָתֵנוּ מִמֶּנּוּ.
For I heard the whispering of many — Terror all around! "Report him! Let us report him!" Every man of my peace — watchers of my stumble: "Perhaps he will be enticed, and we shall prevail over him, and take our revenge on him."note
11
וַיהוָה אוֹתִי כְּגִבּוֹר עָרִיץ, עַל-כֵּן רֹדְפַי יִכָּשְׁלוּ וְלֹא יֻכָלוּ; בֹּשׁוּ מְאֹד כִּי-לֹא הִשְׂכִּילוּ, כְּלִמַּת עוֹלָם לֹא תִשָּׁכֵחַ.
But Jehovah is with me like a dread warrior; therefore my pursuers will stumble, and will not prevail. They will be deeply ashamed, for they have not succeeded — a disgrace forever, that will not be forgotten.note
12
וַיהוָה צְבָאוֹת בֹּחֵן צַדִּיק, רֹאֶה כְלָיוֹת וָלֵב; אֶרְאֶה נִקְמָתְךָ מֵהֶם, כִּי אֵלֶיךָ גִּלִּיתִי אֶת-רִיבִי. {ס}
And Jehovah of hosts, who tests the righteous, who sees kidneys and heart — let me see your vengeance on them, for to you I have laid bare my case.note
13
שִׁירוּ, לַיהוָה--הַלְלוּ, אֶת-יְהוָה: כִּי הִצִּיל אֶת-נֶפֶשׁ אֶבְיוֹן, מִיַּד מְרֵעִים. {ס}
Sing to Jehovah! Praise Jehovah! For he has rescued the soul of the needy from the hand of evildoers.note
14
אָרוּר הַיּוֹם, אֲשֶׁר יֻלַּדְתִּי בּוֹ: יוֹם אֲשֶׁר-יְלָדַתְנִי אִמִּי, אַל-יְהִי בָרוּךְ.
Cursed be the day I was born on; the day my mother bore me — let it not be blessed.note
15
אָרוּר הָאִישׁ, אֲשֶׁר בִּשַּׂר אֶת-אָבִי לֵאמֹר, יֻלַּד-לְךָ, בֵּן זָכָר--שַׂמֵּחַ, שִׂמְּחָהוּ.
Cursed be the man who brought my father the news: "A son is born to you — a male!" — making him very glad.note
16
וְהָיָה הָאִישׁ הַהוּא, כֶּעָרִים אֲשֶׁר-הָפַךְ יְהוָה וְלֹא נִחָם; וְשָׁמַע זְעָקָה בַּבֹּקֶר, וּתְרוּעָה בְּעֵת צָהֳרָיִם.
And let that man be like the cities that Jehovah overthrew, and did not relent; and let him hear an outcry in the morning, and a war-shout at noon —note
17
אֲשֶׁר לֹא-מוֹתְתַנִי, מֵרָחֶם; וַתְּהִי-לִי אִמִּי קִבְרִי, וְרַחְמָה הֲרַת עוֹלָם.
because he did not kill me from the womb — so my mother would have been my grave, and her womb pregnant forever.note
18
לָמָּה זֶּה מֵרֶחֶם יָצָאתִי, לִרְאוֹת עָמָל וְיָגוֹן; וַיִּכְלוּ בְּבֹשֶׁת, יָמָי. {פ}
Why is it that I came out of the womb — to see toil and sorrow, and my days consumed in shame?note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–3 · מָגוֹר מִסָּבִיב Magor-Missaviv

You are entering the sixth book of this site mid-story. Jeremiah of Anathoth — a priest's son drafted as a boy ("before I formed you in the womb, I knew you… and I said, Look — I do not know how to speak, for I am only a youth," chapter 1, not yet on these pages) — has by now spent years saying what no one in Jerusalem wants said. "These things" that Pashhur heard are chapter 19, just off this page: a clay flask smashed at the Potsherd Gate, and a sermon preached over the valley of Ben-Hinnom — the very valley whose Greek name this site met three chapters ago as Gehenna (Matthew 5:22). The sermon in Gehenna's valley earned the prophet the first recorded violence of his life: Pashhur son of Immer, paqid nagid — chief overseer in the house of Jehovah, the head of temple order — struck him and locked him overnight in the mahpekhet: "the twister," a frame that bent the prisoner's body (the word is built on haphakh, the overturning-verb — hold that root; the chapter will close with it).

The renaming. A night in the stocks produced not a retraction but a name. "Not Pashhur has Jehovah called your name — but Magor-Missaviv": Terror-All-Around (KJV runs it together as one grim word, "Magormissabib"; Douay translates it away — "fear on every side"). Prophets rename people as verdicts; this one is a forecast, and it is Jeremiah's own signature phrase of dread (6:25; 46:5; 49:29; Psalm 31 borrows it) — verse 10 will show the street throwing it back in his face.

Verses 4–6 · בְּיַד מֶלֶךְ-בָּבֶל beyad melekh-Bavel

Babylon, named at last. Until this oracle, the doom in Jeremiah's preaching comes from an unnamed "north." Here, for the first time in the book's oracles, the destination is said out loud — Babylon, five times in three verses, as if the word itself had been waiting in the stocks overnight. The naming dates the scene: after Carchemish (605 BC — the year this site's Daniel 1 opens), when the identity of the "north" stopped being a riddle. "All Judah I will give into the hand of the king of Babylon" — prophesied here, and recorded as done on Daniel's very first page. Pashhur's own sentence is measured to his office: exile, death, and burial in Babylon, "you, and all who love you, to whom you have prophesied falsehood" — the chief of temple order was also, it turns out, a comfortable false prophet; the man who policed the true one.

A librarian's aside on sealed clay. From the ash of the very destruction Jeremiah foretold, excavations in the City of David (2005, 2008) produced two clay seal-impressions found a few meters apart: "Gedaliah son of Pashhur" and "Jehucal son of Shelemiah" — two officials who appear side by side, as Jeremiah's enemies, in Jeremiah 38:1. Not this chapter's Pashhur (ours is the son of Immer) — but the prophet's enemies list, countersigned in fired clay.

Verses 7–9 · פִּתִּיתַנִי יְהוָה וָאֶפָּת pittitani … va'eppat

The boldest prayer in the prophets. The verb is patah, and its range is the whole scandal: to persuade; to entice (the lying spirit is sent to "entice" Ahab to his death, 1 Kings 22 — this verb); to seduce (the seduction law of Exodus 22:16 — this verb); to deceive. The shelf has spent four centuries deciding how much to let the verse say: KJV "O LORD, thou hast deceived me" — quoted here with its small-caps name set aside — NIV "you deceived me"; NWT "You have fooled me, O Jehovah"; TLB flatly "you deceived me"; the 1917 JPS, whose English runs beside the Hebrew in this site's own source edition, "enticed" — the choice this translation makes, leaving the darker readings in this note at full strength rather than in the verse's mouth. "You overpowered me, and you prevailed" — mark that last verb (yakhol, to be able, to prevail): the chapter is about to decline it through every party.

The fire in the bones. The resignation is tendered in verse 9 — "I will not remember him, and I will not speak anymore in his name" — and rejected by the prophet's own anatomy: "like a burning fire, shut up in my bones" (the KJV's line here — "as a burning fire shut up in my bones" — is nearly perfect and nearly ours); "I wore myself out holding it in — and I could not" (lo ukhal): the prevail-verb again, negated. God can; the prophet cannot. That asymmetry is the entire confession.

Verses 10–13 · כֹּל אֱנוֹשׁ שְׁלֹמִי kol enosh shelomi

His own oracle, thrown back — and the verb declined. The whispering crowd has adopted his nickname-verdict as a taunt: "Terror all around!" — Magor-Missaviv, now street mockery. "Every man of my peace" — the Hebrew idiom for the trusted circle, the table-friends (Psalm 41:9 runs on the same idiom, "the man of my peace, who ate my bread" — a verse with an upper-room future) — are now "watchers of my stumble" (a small shelf-wink: the letters of his "stumble," tsal'i, are the letters this project met in Eden as "side"). And their hope is verbatim: "perhaps he will be enticed, and we shall prevail over him" — the exact two verbs of verse 7. What the prophet said God did to him is precisely what his enemies dream of doing; the difference is the outcome. Verse 11 closes the ledger: "my pursuers… will not prevail… they have not succeeded" — and that last verb (lo hiskilu) is the very root whose participle is Daniel's maskilim, "the wise": his enemies are, by the letter, the un-wise. Four occurrences of one verb of power: God prevailed (v7); the prophet could not (v9); the enemies hope to (v10); they will not (v11).

The assayer, the kidneys, and the pivot. "Jehovah is with me like a dread warrior" (gibbor arits — gibbor, the mighty-man word from Genesis 6's strange page, now God's epithet); "who tests the righteous" — bochen, the assayer's verb, the refining furnace of Daniel 11:35 compressed to a participle; "who sees kidneys and heart" — the kidneys as Hebrew's seat of the hidden self (KJV, wonderfully, "the reins" — Latin renes, kidneys; four centuries of English readers picturing bridles). Revelation quotes this oath back across the canon: Thyatira's Christ — "I am the one who searches kidneys and hearts." And then, with no warning, verse 13: "Sing to Jehovah! Praise Jehovah!" — the lament-psalm's pivot (complaint → confidence → praise) compressed into a single line. Hold the praise carefully; the floor is about to give way.

Verses 14–18 · אָרוּר הַיּוֹם arur hayyom

The birth-curse — kept raw. From "Sing to Jehovah" to "Cursed be the day I was born," with no seam. The book's editors did not smooth the sequence, and this translation will not either: this is the rawest run of verses in the prophets, and its arrangement — praise, then the pit — is the honest shape of a real interior life. Job 3 is the twin ("Let the day perish wherein I was born"); which poet echoes which, scholars split, and this note reports the split. Notice where the curse does not land: not on God, not on himself — on the day, and on the poor messenger who brought the good news ("A son is born to you — a male!"), who is wished the fate of "the cities that Jehovah overthrew" — haphakh, Sodom's own verb from this site's Genesis 19 (ASV "the cities which Jehovah overthrew"), closing the bracket the chapter opened: the man twisted in the mahpekhet ends by wishing the overturning on the world. "And did not relent" — nacham, the comfort-and-regret root this site has tracked since Noah's name.

The womb, twice. "Why is it that I came out of the womb — to see toil and sorrow?" — asked, devastatingly, by the one man in scripture who was told why: "before I formed you in the womb, I knew you" (1:5). The call and the curse are aimed at the same womb. The chapter ends with the question unanswered — no seam, no comfort; the next verse of the book (21:1) is simply the next assignment. The resolution is the one verse 9 already gave: the fire in the bones wins by default, and he keeps speaking. Not an answer — a vocation.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation sides with the literal shelf: "a burning fire shut up in my bones" — the KJV's line, nearly untouchable, nearly kept whole; Magor-Missaviv left standing as the name it is (with KJV, against Douay's "fear on every side").

Where it goes its own way: "You enticed me… and I was enticed" (KJV/NIV "deceived," NWT "fooled" — the darker readings carried in the note at full strength); "kidneys and heart" kept literal (KJV "the reins"); "every man of my peace" kept as the idiom is (KJV "my familiars"); "all who love you" for Pashhur's circle (KJV "thy friends"); "the twister" explained for the stocks.

Echoes paid and planted. Paid: haphakh, Sodom's overthrow-verb (19:25) — bracketing the chapter from the "twister" stocks to the birth-curse; nacham, the relent-root, from Noah's page; gibbor (6:4) turned into God's own epithet; the valley of Ben-Hinnom — the sermon that caused it all, preached in the valley Greek wears as Gehenna (Matthew 5:22); "kidneys and heart," paid forward across the whole canon to Thyatira (Revelation 2:23); and "into the hand of the king of Babylon" — prophesied here, receipted on Daniel's first page (Daniel 1:2). Planted: "every man of my peace," waiting for Psalm 41:9 and an upper room; Babylon, now on this site's map with its own encyclopedia entry — and waiting, much further on, for Revelation to make the name a cipher; and Jeremiah's other confessions (chapters 11–18), of which this chapter is the last and rawest.

Next installment in this book: Jeremiah 21 — the king's own delegation arrives ("inquire of Jehovah for us…") and receives the bleakest answer in the book.

Jeremiah 21 — The Mister Translation

1
הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר-הָיָה אֶל-יִרְמְיָהוּ, מֵאֵת יְהוָה--בִּשְׁלֹחַ אֵלָיו הַמֶּלֶךְ צִדְקִיָּהוּ, אֶת-פַּשְׁחוּר בֶּן-מַלְכִּיָּה, וְאֶת-צְפַנְיָה בֶן-מַעֲשֵׂיָה הַכֹּהֵן, לֵאמֹר.
The word that came to Jeremiah from Jehovah, when King Zedekiah sent to him Pashhur son of Malchiah, and Zephaniah son of Maaseiah, the priest, saying:note
2
דְּרָשׁ-נָא בַעֲדֵנוּ אֶת-יְהוָה, כִּי נְבוּכַדְרֶאצַּר מֶלֶךְ-בָּבֶל נִלְחָם עָלֵינוּ; אוּלַי יַעֲשֶׂה יְהוָה אוֹתָנוּ כְּכָל-נִפְלְאֹתָיו, וְיַעֲלֶה מֵעָלֵינוּ.
"Inquire of Jehovah for us, please — for Nebuchadrezzar king of Babylon is making war against us. Perhaps Jehovah will deal with us according to all his wondrous works, and he will go up from us."note
3
וַיֹּאמֶר יִרְמְיָהוּ, אֲלֵיהֶם: כֹּה תֹאמְרֻן, אֶל-צִדְקִיָּהוּ. {ס}
And Jeremiah said to them: "Thus you shall say to Zedekiah:note
4
כֹּה-אָמַר יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, הִנְנִי מֵסֵב אֶת-כְּלֵי הַמִּלְחָמָה אֲשֶׁר בְּיֶדְכֶם, אֲשֶׁר אַתֶּם נִלְחָמִים בָּם אֶת-מֶלֶךְ בָּבֶל וְאֶת-הַכַּשְׂדִּים, הַצָּרִים עֲלֵיכֶם מִחוּץ לַחוֹמָה; וְאָסַפְתִּי אוֹתָם, אֶל-תּוֹךְ הָעִיר הַזֹּאת.
Thus says Jehovah, the God of Israel: Look — I am turning back the weapons of war that are in your hands, with which you are fighting the king of Babylon and the Chaldeans besieging you outside the wall; and I will gather them into the middle of this city.note
5
וְנִלְחַמְתִּי אֲנִי אִתְּכֶם, בְּיָד נְטוּיָה וּבִזְרוֹעַ חֲזָקָה, וּבְאַף וּבְחֵמָה, וּבְקֶצֶף גָּדוֹל.
And I myself will fight against you — with an outstretched hand and with a strong arm, in anger, and in fury, and in great wrath.note
6
וְהִכֵּיתִי, אֶת-יוֹשְׁבֵי הָעִיר הַזֹּאת, וְאֶת-הָאָדָם, וְאֶת-הַבְּהֵמָה: בְּדֶבֶר גָּדוֹל, יָמֻתוּ.
And I will strike the inhabitants of this city, both human and beast; by a great pestilence they shall die.note
7
וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן נְאֻם-יְהוָה אֶתֵּן אֶת-צִדְקִיָּהוּ מֶלֶךְ-יְהוּדָה וְאֶת-עֲבָדָיו וְאֶת-הָעָם וְאֶת-הַנִּשְׁאָרִים בָּעִיר הַזֹּאת מִן-הַדֶּבֶר מִן-הַחֶרֶב וּמִן-הָרָעָב, בְּיַד נְבוּכַדְרֶאצַּר מֶלֶךְ-בָּבֶל, וּבְיַד אֹיְבֵיהֶם, וּבְיַד מְבַקְשֵׁי נַפְשָׁם; וְהִכָּם לְפִי-חֶרֶב--לֹא-יָחוּס עֲלֵיהֶם, וְלֹא יַחְמֹל וְלֹא יְרַחֵם.
And after that — declares Jehovah — I will give Zedekiah king of Judah, and his servants, and the people, and those left in this city from the pestilence, from the sword, and from the famine, into the hand of Nebuchadrezzar king of Babylon, and into the hand of their enemies, and into the hand of those who seek their life. And he will strike them with the edge of the sword — he will not spare them, and he will not pity, and he will not have compassion.note
8
וְאֶל-הָעָם הַזֶּה תֹּאמַר, כֹּה אָמַר יְהוָה: הִנְנִי נֹתֵן לִפְנֵיכֶם, אֶת-דֶּרֶךְ הַחַיִּים וְאֶת-דֶּרֶךְ הַמָּוֶת.
And to this people you shall say: Thus says Jehovah: Look — I am setting before you the way of life and the way of death.note
9
הַיֹּשֵׁב, בָּעִיר הַזֹּאת, יָמוּת, בַּחֶרֶב וּבָרָעָב וּבַדָּבֶר; וְהַיּוֹצֵא וְנָפַל עַל-הַכַּשְׂדִּים הַצָּרִים עֲלֵיכֶם, יחיה וְחָיָה, וְהָיְתָה-לּוֹ נַפְשׁוֹ, לְשָׁלָל.
Whoever stays in this city shall die — by the sword, and by the famine, and by the pestilence. But whoever goes out and falls away to the Chaldeans besieging you — he shall live; and he shall have his life as spoil.note
10
כִּי שַׂמְתִּי פָנַי בָּעִיר הַזֹּאת לְרָעָה, וְלֹא לְטוֹבָה--נְאֻם-יְהוָה; בְּיַד-מֶלֶךְ בָּבֶל תִּנָּתֵן, וּשְׂרָפָהּ בָּאֵשׁ. {ס}
For I have set my face against this city for evil, and not for good — declares Jehovah. Into the hand of the king of Babylon it shall be given, and he shall burn it with fire.note
11
וּלְבֵית מֶלֶךְ יְהוּדָה, שִׁמְעוּ דְּבַר-יְהוָה.
And to the house of the king of Judah: Hear the word of Jehovah!note
12
בֵּית דָּוִד, כֹּה אָמַר יְהוָה, דִּינוּ לַבֹּקֶר מִשְׁפָּט, וְהַצִּילוּ גָזוּל מִיַּד עוֹשֵׁק: פֶּן-תֵּצֵא כָאֵשׁ חֲמָתִי, וּבָעֲרָה וְאֵין מְכַבֶּה, מִפְּנֵי, רֹעַ מעלליהם מַעַלְלֵיכֶם.
House of David, thus says Jehovah: Render justice in the morning; rescue the robbed from the hand of the oppressor — lest my fury go out like fire, and burn with none to quench it, because of the evil of your doings.note
13
הִנְנִי אֵלַיִךְ יֹשֶׁבֶת הָעֵמֶק, צוּר הַמִּישֹׁר--נְאֻם-יְהוָה; הָאֹמְרִים מִי-יֵחַת עָלֵינוּ, וּמִי יָבוֹא בִּמְעוֹנוֹתֵינוּ.
Look — I am against you, inhabitant of the valley, rock of the plain — declares Jehovah — you who say: "Who shall come down against us? And who shall enter our lairs?"note
14
וּפָקַדְתִּי עֲלֵיכֶם כִּפְרִי מַעַלְלֵיכֶם, נְאֻם-יְהוָה; וְהִצַּתִּי אֵשׁ בְּיַעְרָהּ, וְאָכְלָה כָּל-סְבִיבֶיהָ.
And I will visit upon you according to the fruit of your doings — declares Jehovah — and I will kindle a fire in her forest, and it shall devour everything around her.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–2 · פַּשְׁחוּר בֶּן-מַלְכִּיָּה Pashchur ben-Malkiyyah

The other Pashhur — and the scroll's own filing system. Read carefully: this is not chapter 20's Pashhur (son of Immer, the stocks). This one is Pashhur son of Malchiah — and the book has jumped seventeen years: chapter 20 stood in Jehoiakim's Jerusalem around 605 BC; chapter 21 opens in the final siege under Zedekiah, 588. Jeremiah's chapters are filed by theme and catchword, not by calendar — and the likeliest reason these two scenes sit side by side is the shared name itself: the scroll's ancient editors shelved Pashhur next to Pashhur. This second Pashhur has a future in the book: he stands among the officials who drop Jeremiah into the cistern (38:1) — the same verse where a "Gedaliah son of Pashhur" appears, whose clay seal-impression came out of the City of David's ash (chapter 20's note carries that story). Note also the king's spelling: Nebuchadrezzar, with an r — Jeremiah's usual form, closest to the Akkadian Nabû-kudurri-uṣur (KJV and ASV keep the r here; Douay writes "Nabuchodonosor," a third spelling on one shelf; the familiar "Nebuchadnezzar" is the softened form Kings and Daniel use).

The request: a rerun of the wondrous works. "Perhaps Jehovah will deal with us according to all his wondrous works" — niflaot, the Exodus-wonder word. The delegation is asking, in effect, for the archives: another Red Sea, or another night like 701 BC, when Sennacherib's army melted away from these same walls. It is a real prayer, prayed at the last possible hour, by a court that has ignored the praying prophet for forty years.

Verses 3–7 · בְּיָד נְטוּיָה וּבִזְרוֹעַ חֲזָקָה beyad netuyah uvizroa chazaqah

The Exodus formula, aimed inward. The answer begins with the weapons turned around in the defenders' own hands — and then the most chilling sentence in the book's diplomacy: "I myself will fight against you — with an outstretched hand and with a strong arm." That is the redemption formula: "a mighty hand and an outstretched arm" is how Deuteronomy says brought you out of Egypt, over and over, like a creed. The delegation asked for the Exodus God; the answer is that he is already on the field — on the other side. The triad that will drum through the rest of the book follows — sword, famine, pestilence — and the sentence on the court ends with a triple negation as bleak as Hebrew can build: "he will not spare… and he will not pity… and he will not have compassion." Zedekiah's own end (not told here; 39:5-7 will tell it) matched the oracle's temperature.

Verses 8–10 · דֶּרֶךְ הַחַיִּים וְאֶת-דֶּרֶךְ הַמָּוֶת derekh hachayyim… derekh hammavet

The Two Ways, at their sharpest. "Look — I am setting before you the way of life and the way of death" is Deuteronomy 30:19 quoted back to its own people — "I have set before you life and death… therefore choose life" — but with the choice shrunk to one grim door: staying in the city is the way of death (sword, famine, pestilence, the triad again); the way of life is to walk out and surrender. This site met the Two Ways three chapters ago at the Sermon's narrow gate, and in the Didache's opening line; here the fork is drawn on a wartime map, and the life-road runs straight into the enemy camp. "He shall have his life as spoil" (KJV "his life shall be unto him for a prey"; NIV "will escape with their lives") — a soldier's grim idiom: the only plunder you will carry out of this war is yourself. Jeremiah will hand the phrase, later, to the two men who trusted him (38:2 repeats this oracle nearly verbatim — and it is exactly the preaching that gets him thrown into the cistern as a traitor, 38:4; Ebed-melech and Baruch each receive "your life as spoil" as a personal promise, 39:18; 45:5). A scroll-note: verse 9 carries a ketiv/qere — the written and read forms of "he shall live" standing side by side in the Hebrew column, the manuscript's double track this site first met in Daniel 11.

Verses 11–14 · דִּינוּ לַבֹּקֶר מִשְׁפָּט dinu laboqer mishpat

The throne's one job. The oracle pivots from the people to the dynasty: "House of David… render justice in the morning" (KJV "Execute judgment in the morning") — the king's court sat at the gate in the cool of the morning; daily justice, especially for "the robbed," was the Davidic job description, and this site's shelf holds the original posting: Abraham's household was chosen "to do righteousness and justice" (Genesis 18:19). The alternative is the chapter's fire-chain: wrath "like fire… with none to quench it" (v12), lit for real in verse 14 — "I will kindle a fire in her forest": Jerusalem's "forest" is Solomon's cedar palace, the House of the Forest of Lebanon — the royal complex itself as kindling (2 Kings 25:9 reports the fulfillment: "every great house he burned with fire"). Verse 12 carries the chapter's second ketiv/qere: the written "their doings" beside the read "your doings" — the scribes preserving even the wobble of a pronoun.

The smug topography. "Look — I am against you, inhabitant of the valley, rock of the plain" (KJV "O inhabitant of the valley, and rock of the plain") — Jerusalem, ringed by ravines on three sides, purring over its own geography: "Who shall come down against us? And who shall enter our lairs?" — me'onot, animal dens: the city as a predator confident in its cave. This site has heard a fortified boast answered from above before — at Babel, where the builders reached up and "Jehovah came down." The answer to "who shall come down against us?" was on file.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation sides with the literal shelf: "Nebuchadrezzar" kept with the r, as Jeremiah spells him (with KJV and ASV, against Douay's "Nabuchodonosor" and the harmonized "Nebuchadnezzar" of Kings and Daniel); the sword–famine–pestilence triad in the scroll's own order.

Where it goes its own way: "he shall have his life as spoil" (KJV "for a prey") — the soldier's-booty idiom kept; "wondrous works" for niflaot, the Exodus-wonder word; "our lairs" for the dens of verse 13 (KJV "habitations" — the animal image kept); "render justice in the morning" (KJV "execute judgment").

Echoes paid and planted. Paid: the Two Ways — Deuteronomy's fork, the Sermon's two gates (Matthew 7:13), now drawn on a wartime map; Babylon, named from the stocks (20:4) and now at the walls — the book's two Pashhur scenes filed side by side, seventeen years apart; the house of David measured against Abraham's brief, "to do righteousness and justice" (18:19); the city that asks "who shall come down against us?" answered by Babel's page — "Jehovah came down" (11:5); and the same king on two pages — Daniel's opening (his first visit, 605) and this oracle (his last, 588) (Daniel 1:1). Planted: this oracle as the charge sheet for the cistern (38:1-4 — with this chapter's Pashhur among the accusers); "life as spoil" waiting for Ebed-melech and Baruch; the fire in the cedar "forest," waiting for 586 and 2 Kings 25:9; and Zedekiah's last sight, waiting at Riblah.

Next installment in this book: Jeremiah 22 — the tariff of kings: "weep not for the dead," the donkey's burial, and Coniah the discarded signet ring.

Genesis 21 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיהוָה פָּקַד אֶת-שָׂרָה, כַּאֲשֶׁר אָמָר; וַיַּעַשׂ יְהוָה לְשָׂרָה, כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֵּר.
And Jehovah visited Sarah as he had said, and Jehovah did for Sarah as he had spoken.note
2
וַתַּהַר וַתֵּלֶד שָׂרָה לְאַבְרָהָם בֵּן, לִזְקֻנָיו, לַמּוֹעֵד, אֲשֶׁר-דִּבֶּר אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים.
And Sarah conceived, and bore Abraham a son in his old age — at the appointed time that God had spoken of with him.note
3
וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָהָם אֶת-שֶׁם-בְּנוֹ הַנּוֹלַד-לוֹ, אֲשֶׁר-יָלְדָה-לּוֹ שָׂרָה--יִצְחָק.
And Abraham called the name of his son who was born to him, whom Sarah bore to him — Isaac.note
4
וַיָּמָל אַבְרָהָם אֶת-יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ, בֶּן-שְׁמֹנַת יָמִים, כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֹתוֹ, אֱלֹהִים.
And Abraham circumcised Isaac his son at eight days old, as God had commanded him.note
5
וְאַבְרָהָם, בֶּן-מְאַת שָׁנָה, בְּהִוָּלֶד לוֹ, אֵת יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ.
And Abraham was 100 years old when Isaac his son was born to him.note
6
וַתֹּאמֶר שָׂרָה--צְחֹק, עָשָׂה לִי אֱלֹהִים: כָּל-הַשֹּׁמֵעַ, יִצְחַק-לִי.
And Sarah said: "Laughter God has made for me; everyone who hears will laugh for me."note
7
וַתֹּאמֶר, מִי מִלֵּל לְאַבְרָהָם, הֵינִיקָה בָנִים, שָׂרָה: כִּי-יָלַדְתִּי בֵן, לִזְקֻנָיו.
And she said: "Who would have declared to Abraham: Sarah nurses children! — for I have borne a son in his old age."note
8
וַיִּגְדַּל הַיֶּלֶד, וַיִּגָּמַל; וַיַּעַשׂ אַבְרָהָם מִשְׁתֶּה גָדוֹל, בְּיוֹם הִגָּמֵל אֶת-יִצְחָק.
And the child grew, and was weaned; and Abraham made a great feast on the day Isaac was weaned.note
9
וַתֵּרֶא שָׂרָה אֶת-בֶּן-הָגָר הַמִּצְרִית, אֲשֶׁר-יָלְדָה לְאַבְרָהָם--מְצַחֵק.
And Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, whom she had borne to Abraham — laughing.note
10
וַתֹּאמֶר, לְאַבְרָהָם, גָּרֵשׁ הָאָמָה הַזֹּאת, וְאֶת-בְּנָהּ: כִּי לֹא יִירַשׁ בֶּן-הָאָמָה הַזֹּאת, עִם-בְּנִי עִם-יִצְחָק.
And she said to Abraham: "Drive out this slave-woman and her son — for the son of this slave-woman shall not inherit with my son, with Isaac."note
11
וַיֵּרַע הַדָּבָר מְאֹד, בְּעֵינֵי אַבְרָהָם, עַל, אוֹדֹת בְּנוֹ.
And the thing was very grievous in Abraham's eyes, on account of his son.note
12
וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים אֶל-אַבְרָהָם, אַל-יֵרַע בְּעֵינֶיךָ עַל-הַנַּעַר וְעַל-אֲמָתֶךָ--כֹּל אֲשֶׁר תֹּאמַר אֵלֶיךָ שָׂרָה, שְׁמַע בְּקֹלָהּ: כִּי בְיִצְחָק, יִקָּרֵא לְךָ זָרַע.
And God said to Abraham: "Let it not be grievous in your eyes over the boy and over your slave-woman. All that Sarah says to you — listen to her voice; for in Isaac shall seed be called for you.note
13
וְגַם אֶת-בֶּן-הָאָמָה, לְגוֹי אֲשִׂימֶנּוּ: כִּי זַרְעֲךָ, הוּא.
And also the son of the slave-woman — I will make him into a nation, for he is your seed."note
14
וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אַבְרָהָם בַּבֹּקֶר וַיִּקַּח-לֶחֶם וְחֵמַת מַיִם וַיִּתֵּן אֶל-הָגָר שָׂם עַל-שִׁכְמָהּ, וְאֶת-הַיֶּלֶד--וַיְשַׁלְּחֶהָ; וַתֵּלֶךְ וַתֵּתַע, בְּמִדְבַּר בְּאֵר שָׁבַע.
And Abraham rose early in the morning, and took bread and a skin of water and gave them to Hagar — set them on her shoulder — and the child, and sent her away. And she went, and wandered in the wilderness of Beersheba.note
15
וַיִּכְלוּ הַמַּיִם, מִן-הַחֵמֶת; וַתַּשְׁלֵךְ אֶת-הַיֶּלֶד, תַּחַת אַחַד הַשִּׂיחִם.
And the water from the skin was finished; and she cast the child under one of the bushes.note
16
וַתֵּלֶךְ וַתֵּשֶׁב לָהּ מִנֶּגֶד, הַרְחֵק כִּמְטַחֲוֵי קֶשֶׁת, כִּי אָמְרָה, אַל-אֶרְאֶה בְּמוֹת הַיָּלֶד; וַתֵּשֶׁב מִנֶּגֶד, וַתִּשָּׂא אֶת-קֹלָהּ וַתֵּבְךְּ.
And she went and sat herself down opposite, far off — about a bowshot — for she said: "Let me not see the death of the child." And she sat opposite, and lifted up her voice, and wept.note
17
וַיִּשְׁמַע אֱלֹהִים, אֶת-קוֹל הַנַּעַר, וַיִּקְרָא מַלְאַךְ אֱלֹהִים אֶל-הָגָר מִן-הַשָּׁמַיִם, וַיֹּאמֶר לָהּ מַה-לָּךְ הָגָר; אַל-תִּירְאִי, כִּי-שָׁמַע אֱלֹהִים אֶל-קוֹל הַנַּעַר בַּאֲשֶׁר הוּא-שָׁם.
And God heard the voice of the boy; and the angel of God called to Hagar from the heavens, and said to her: "What is it, Hagar? Do not be afraid — for God has heard the voice of the boy, there where he is.note
18
קוּמִי שְׂאִי אֶת-הַנַּעַר, וְהַחֲזִיקִי אֶת-יָדֵךְ בּוֹ: כִּי-לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל, אֲשִׂימֶנּוּ.
Rise, lift up the boy, and hold him fast with your hand — for I will make him into a great nation."note
19
וַיִּפְקַח אֱלֹהִים אֶת-עֵינֶיהָ, וַתֵּרֶא בְּאֵר מָיִם; וַתֵּלֶךְ וַתְּמַלֵּא אֶת-הַחֵמֶת, מַיִם, וַתַּשְׁקְ, אֶת-הַנָּעַר.
And God opened her eyes, and she saw a well of water; and she went and filled the skin with water, and gave the boy a drink.note
20
וַיְהִי אֱלֹהִים אֶת-הַנַּעַר, וַיִּגְדָּל; וַיֵּשֶׁב, בַּמִּדְבָּר, וַיְהִי, רֹבֶה קַשָּׁת.
And God was with the boy, and he grew; and he lived in the wilderness, and became an archer.note
21
וַיֵּשֶׁב, בְּמִדְבַּר פָּארָן; וַתִּקַּח-לוֹ אִמּוֹ אִשָּׁה, מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם. {פ}
And he lived in the wilderness of Paran; and his mother took a wife for him from the land of Egypt.note
22
וַיְהִי, בָּעֵת הַהִוא, וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ וּפִיכֹל שַׂר-צְבָאוֹ, אֶל-אַבְרָהָם לֵאמֹר: אֱלֹהִים עִמְּךָ, בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה עֹשֶׂה.
And so it was at that time: Abimelech — with Phicol, the commander of his army — said to Abraham: "God is with you in all that you are doing.note
23
וְעַתָּה, הִשָּׁבְעָה לִּי בֵאלֹהִים הֵנָּה, אִם-תִּשְׁקֹר לִי, וּלְנִינִי וּלְנֶכְדִּי; כַּחֶסֶד אֲשֶׁר-עָשִׂיתִי עִמְּךָ, תַּעֲשֶׂה עִמָּדִי, וְעִם-הָאָרֶץ, אֲשֶׁר-גַּרְתָּה בָּהּ.
Now, then — swear to me by God, here, that you will not deal falsely with me, or with my offspring, or with my posterity. Like the kindness I have done with you, you shall do with me, and with the land where you have sojourned."note
24
וַיֹּאמֶר, אַבְרָהָם, אָנֹכִי, אִשָּׁבֵעַ.
And Abraham said: "I will swear."note
25
וְהוֹכִחַ אַבְרָהָם, אֶת-אֲבִימֶלֶךְ, עַל-אֹדוֹת בְּאֵר הַמַּיִם, אֲשֶׁר גָּזְלוּ עַבְדֵי אֲבִימֶלֶךְ.
And Abraham reproved Abimelech on account of the well of water that Abimelech's servants had seized.note
26
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ--לֹא יָדַעְתִּי, מִי עָשָׂה אֶת-הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה; וְגַם-אַתָּה לֹא-הִגַּדְתָּ לִּי, וְגַם אָנֹכִי לֹא שָׁמַעְתִּי--בִּלְתִּי הַיּוֹם.
And Abimelech said: "I do not know who did this thing. You did not tell me either — nor have I heard of it, except today."note
27
וַיִּקַּח אַבְרָהָם צֹאן וּבָקָר, וַיִּתֵּן לַאֲבִימֶלֶךְ; וַיִּכְרְתוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם, בְּרִית.
And Abraham took sheep and cattle and gave them to Abimelech, and the two of them cut a covenant.note
28
וַיַּצֵּב אַבְרָהָם, אֶת-שֶׁבַע כִּבְשֹׂת הַצֹּאן--לְבַדְּהֶן.
And Abraham set seven ewe-lambs of the flock by themselves.note
29
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ, אֶל-אַבְרָהָם: מָה הֵנָּה, שֶׁבַע כְּבָשֹׂת הָאֵלֶּה, אֲשֶׁר הִצַּבְתָּ, לְבַדָּנָה.
And Abimelech said to Abraham: "What are these seven ewe-lambs that you have set by themselves?"note
30
וַיֹּאמֶר--כִּי אֶת-שֶׁבַע כְּבָשֹׂת, תִּקַּח מִיָּדִי: בַּעֲבוּר תִּהְיֶה-לִּי לְעֵדָה, כִּי חָפַרְתִּי אֶת-הַבְּאֵר הַזֹּאת.
And he said: "These seven ewe-lambs you shall take from my hand, so that they may be a witness for me that I dug this well."note
31
עַל-כֵּן, קָרָא לַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא--בְּאֵר שָׁבַע: כִּי שָׁם נִשְׁבְּעוּ, שְׁנֵיהֶם.
Therefore that place is called Beersheba — for there the two of them swore.note
32
וַיִּכְרְתוּ בְרִית, בִּבְאֵר שָׁבַע; וַיָּקָם אֲבִימֶלֶךְ, וּפִיכֹל שַׂר-צְבָאוֹ, וַיָּשֻׁבוּ, אֶל-אֶרֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּים.
And they cut a covenant at Beersheba. Then Abimelech rose — with Phicol, the commander of his army — and they returned to the land of the Philistines.note
33
וַיִּטַּע אֶשֶׁל, בִּבְאֵר שָׁבַע; וַיִּקְרָא-שָׁם--בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה, אֵל עוֹלָם.
And he planted a tamarisk at Beersheba, and called there on the name of Jehovah, El Olam — the Everlasting God.note
34
וַיָּגָר אַבְרָהָם בְּאֶרֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּים, יָמִים רַבִּים. {פ}
And Abraham sojourned in the land of the Philistines many days.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–7 · צְחֹק עָשָׂה לִי אֱלֹהִים tsechoq asah li Elohim

Visited — and the laughter lands. "Jehovah visited Sarah" — paqad, the attending-verb that cuts both ways: it visits a son on a barren woman here, and in Jeremiah it "visits" a city's doings upon her — same verb, opposite freight (KJV "visited" with us; the 1917 JPS softens to "remembered"). The birth comes "at the appointed time" — la-moed, the word hung with the lights on day four. And then the name, banked for three chapters, is finally paid: Abraham laughed face-down (17:17); Sarah laughed inside the tent and denied it (18:12-15); now — "Laughter God has made for me" (the Hebrew fronts the word, and so does this translation; KJV "God hath made me to laugh") — and the boy is Yitschaq, "he laughs." One shadow in the sunlight: "everyone who hears will laugh li" — for me, or at me? The preposition is open, and the woman who once laughed in fear knows both readings. Verse 7's "who would have declared" uses a verb (millel) so rare it is nearly a private word — as if ordinary speech had no form for this sentence. The eighth-day circumcision keeps 17:12 at the first opportunity; Abraham is 100.

Verses 8–13 · מְצַחֵק metsacheq

The boy caught "Isaac-ing." At the weaning feast (two or three years on), Sarah sees the son of Hagar — laughing: metsacheq, the participle of the very verb that names Isaac. Whatever Ishmael was doing, in Hebrew he was doing Isaac — playing the laughter, or playing the heir. The verdicts on the shelf are a spectrum: KJV "mocking"; Douay, from the Vulgate, the gentlest — "playing"; the Septuagint adds "with her son Isaac"; and Paul, reading through his own battles, "persecuted" (Galatians 4:29). This translation prints the neutral surface — laughing — so the pun stays visible, and shelves the verdicts here. Sarah's demand uses the Bible's expulsion-verb: "Drive out" (garesh) — the word that emptied Eden (3:24) and exiled Cain (4:14). Note, too, the vocabulary shift: chapter 16's shifchah (slave-girl) has hardened into amah (slave-woman) — the household's words for Hagar have aged with the trouble.

"Listen to her voice." God's ruling contains a phrase this site has met before, in the garden: shema beqolah — the exact words of 3:17, where "you listened to the voice of your wife" opened the indictment. Here the same phrase is a command: "all that Sarah says to you — listen to her voice." The words that once named a fall now carry the plan forward — "for in Isaac shall seed be called for you" (KJV nearly verbatim) — with the double promise attached: Ishmael too becomes a nation, "for he is your seed."

Verses 14–21 · כִּי-שָׁמַע אֱלֹהִים אֶל-קוֹל הַנַּעַר ki-shama Elohim el-qol hanna'ar

Dawn, a skin of water, and an arithmetic problem. "Abraham rose early in the morning" — remember the habit; it returns on a far worse morning, one chapter from now. Bread, a skin of water (KJV, delightfully, "a bottle"), "and the child" — and here the honest note: by the book's own numbers (16:16; 21:5, plus a weaning), Ishmael is a teenager, yet the scene reads like a small child — carried, cast under a bush, wept over from a distance. Readers who track the Torah's source-seams hear two tellings braided; harmonizers picture an exhausted sixteen-year-old collapsed in the heat. The seam is shown, not sanded. Hagar wanders the wilderness of Beersheba until the water is finished, casts the child under a bush, and sits "about a bowshot" away — the distance measured, with terrible economy, in the weapon her son will one day master (v20).

The name that happens as a verb. Now notice what the narrator has been doing: in this entire chapter the boy is never once called Ishmael — "the son of Hagar," "the child," "the boy." The name is withheld so that it can happen: "And God heard (shama Elohim) the voice of the boy" — Yishma-El, "God hears," paid as an event, exactly as promised at his announcement (16:11). "There where he is" (ba'asher hu sham) — the rabbis loved that clause: judged where he is, not where he will be. "And God opened her eyes, and she saw a well" — for the woman who named God "the One who sees" (16:13), sight returned as rescue: Hagar's two desert scenes close on the same exchange of seeing. "God was with the boy"; he grows, an archer in Paran; and his mother takes him a wife "from the land of Egypt" — Hagar the Egyptian (16:1), full circle.

Verses 22–26 · אֱלֹהִים עִמְּךָ Elohim immekha

Abimelech returns — with a general and a request. The king of Gerar, last seen returning Sarah with a thousand pieces of silver (ch. 20), arrives with Phicol, the commander of his army — a treaty delegation with the muscle standing visibly behind it. His opening line is the chapter's quiet verdict from outside: "God is with you in all that you are doing" — said to the very man whose last appearance in Gerar was a half-truth that nearly wrecked a kingdom. The oath he asks reaches down three generations (nin and nekhed — offspring and posterity, a rare legal pair), invoking the chesed — the covenant-kindness — he showed first. Abraham's answer is two words in Hebrew: "I will swear." Then, business: the seized well. In the Negev, water rights are the real diplomacy, and the narrative gives the dispute more verses than the oath.

Verses 27–34 · בְּאֵר שָׁבַע … אֵל עוֹלָם Be'er Sheva … El Olam

Seven ewes, and a name with two readings that are one. Abraham sets seven ewe-lambs apart; Abimelech's acceptance of them is a receipt — "a witness for me that I dug this well": property law by livestock, the Negev's paperwork. And the place-name banks the whole transaction twice over: Be'er Sheva — "well of the seven" and "well of the oath" — because Hebrew's verb "to swear" (nishba) is literally to seven oneself: the two etymologies the text offers (v30, v31) are one word wearing both faces. Beersheba enters the story here and never leaves it — "from Dan to Beersheba" will become the Bible's own way of saying the whole land.

The tamarisk and the fourth El-name. Abraham plants an eshel — a tamarisk, a desert tree that lives for centuries: planting one at a well is filing a claim on the future in slow motion (KJV, following an older guess, "planted a grove"; ASV "a tamarisk tree" with us). And there he calls on the name of Jehovah, El Olam — the Everlasting God: the fourth El-name this site has collected at a landmark — El Elyon at Salem (ch. 14), El Roi at Hagar's first well (ch. 16), El Shaddai at the covenant (ch. 17), now El Olam at a tree beside a sworn well (NWT, keeping its clock, "the indefinitely lasting God").
