"The land of the Philistines" carries the standing honesty flag: the Philistines of the archaeological record arrive on this coast centuries after the patriarchs — the narrator, as with "Ur of the Chaldeans," speaks the reader's geography, not Abraham's.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation sides with the literal shelf: "Jehovah visited Sarah" with the KJV (against JPS's softer "remembered"); "in Isaac shall seed be called" nearly KJV verbatim; "cut a covenant," as always.

Where it goes its own way: "laughing" for metsacheq (KJV "mocking," Douay "playing" — the verdicts shelved in the note, the pun kept visible in the text); "a skin of water" (KJV "a bottle"); "tamarisk" (KJV "grove"); "slave-woman" for amah, kept distinct from chapter 16's shifchah; and "Laughter God has made for me" — the Hebrew's fronting kept.

Echoes paid and planted. Paid: the laughter thread — Abraham's laugh (17:17), Sarah's hidden laugh (18:12), now a name and a song; "listen to her voice" — the phrase of the garden's indictment (3:17), inverted into a command; garesh, the expulsion-verb of Eden and Cain; "God heard" — Ishmael's name, promised at (16:11), paid as an event; eyes opened for the namer of El Roi (16:13); the eighth day, commanded at (17:12) and kept; and Abimelech's arc from the sister-ruse (20:15) closed with a treaty. Planted: "rose early in the morning" — the habit that returns on the worst morning of all (22:3); Beersheba, the patriarchal address from here on; El Olam joining the El-name series; and the bowshot that became an archer.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 22 — the Aqedah: "Take your son, your only one, whom you love…" — the hardest chapter in the book.

Genesis 22 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיְהִי, אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה, וְהָאֱלֹהִים, נִסָּה אֶת-אַבְרָהָם; וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו, אַבְרָהָם וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֵּנִי.
And so it was, after these things, that God tested Abraham; and he said to him: "Abraham." And he said: "Here I am."note
2
וַיֹּאמֶר קַח-נָא אֶת-בִּנְךָ אֶת-יְחִידְךָ אֲשֶׁר-אָהַבְתָּ, אֶת-יִצְחָק, וְלֶךְ-לְךָ, אֶל-אֶרֶץ הַמֹּרִיָּה; וְהַעֲלֵהוּ שָׁם, לְעֹלָה, עַל אַחַד הֶהָרִים, אֲשֶׁר אֹמַר אֵלֶיךָ.
And he said: "Take now your son, your only one, whom you love — Isaac — and go, you yourself, to the land of Moriah; and bring him up there as a burnt offering, on one of the mountains that I will tell you of."note
3
וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אַבְרָהָם בַּבֹּקֶר, וַיַּחֲבֹשׁ אֶת-חֲמֹרוֹ, וַיִּקַּח אֶת-שְׁנֵי נְעָרָיו אִתּוֹ, וְאֵת יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ; וַיְבַקַּע, עֲצֵי עֹלָה, וַיָּקָם וַיֵּלֶךְ, אֶל-הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר-אָמַר-לוֹ הָאֱלֹהִים.
And Abraham rose early in the morning, and saddled his donkey, and took his two young men with him, and Isaac his son; and he split the wood for the burnt offering, and rose, and went to the place that God had told him.note
4
בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי, וַיִּשָּׂא אַבְרָהָם אֶת-עֵינָיו וַיַּרְא אֶת-הַמָּקוֹם--מֵרָחֹק.
On the third day Abraham lifted up his eyes, and saw the place from afar.note
5
וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם אֶל-נְעָרָיו, שְׁבוּ-לָכֶם פֹּה עִם-הַחֲמוֹר, וַאֲנִי וְהַנַּעַר, נֵלְכָה עַד-כֹּה; וְנִשְׁתַּחֲוֶה, וְנָשׁוּבָה אֲלֵיכֶם.
And Abraham said to his young men: "Stay here, you, with the donkey; and I and the boy will go over there, and we will worship, and we will return to you."note
6
וַיִּקַּח אַבְרָהָם אֶת-עֲצֵי הָעֹלָה, וַיָּשֶׂם עַל-יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ, וַיִּקַּח בְּיָדוֹ, אֶת-הָאֵשׁ וְאֶת-הַמַּאֲכֶלֶת; וַיֵּלְכוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם, יַחְדָּו.
And Abraham took the wood of the burnt offering and laid it on Isaac his son, and took in his hand the fire and the knife; and the two of them went together.note
7
וַיֹּאמֶר יִצְחָק אֶל-אַבְרָהָם אָבִיו, וַיֹּאמֶר אָבִי, וַיֹּאמֶר, הִנֶּנִּי בְנִי; וַיֹּאמֶר, הִנֵּה הָאֵשׁ וְהָעֵצִים, וְאַיֵּה הַשֶּׂה, לְעֹלָה.
And Isaac said to Abraham his father — he said: "My father." And he said: "Here I am, my son." And he said: "Here is the fire and the wood — but where is the lamb for a burnt offering?"note
8
וַיֹּאמֶר, אַבְרָהָם, אֱלֹהִים יִרְאֶה-לּוֹ הַשֶּׂה לְעֹלָה, בְּנִי; וַיֵּלְכוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם, יַחְדָּו.
And Abraham said: "God will see to the lamb for himself, for a burnt offering, my son." And the two of them went together.note
9
וַיָּבֹאוּ, אֶל-הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר אָמַר-לוֹ הָאֱלֹהִים, וַיִּבֶן שָׁם אַבְרָהָם אֶת-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ, וַיַּעֲרֹךְ אֶת-הָעֵצִים; וַיַּעֲקֹד, אֶת-יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ, וַיָּשֶׂם אֹתוֹ עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ, מִמַּעַל לָעֵצִים.
And they came to the place that God had told him; and Abraham built the altar there, and arranged the wood, and bound Isaac his son, and laid him on the altar, on top of the wood.note
10
וַיִּשְׁלַח אַבְרָהָם אֶת-יָדוֹ, וַיִּקַּח אֶת-הַמַּאֲכֶלֶת, לִשְׁחֹט, אֶת-בְּנוֹ.
And Abraham stretched out his hand and took the knife to slaughter his son.note
11
וַיִּקְרָא אֵלָיו מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה, מִן-הַשָּׁמַיִם, וַיֹּאמֶר, אַבְרָהָם אַבְרָהָם; וַיֹּאמֶר, הִנֵּנִי.
And the angel of Jehovah called to him from the heavens, and said: "Abraham! Abraham!" And he said: "Here I am."note
12
וַיֹּאמֶר, אַל-תִּשְׁלַח יָדְךָ אֶל-הַנַּעַר, וְאַל-תַּעַשׂ לוֹ, מְאוּמָה: כִּי עַתָּה יָדַעְתִּי, כִּי-יְרֵא אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה, וְלֹא חָשַׂכְתָּ אֶת-בִּנְךָ אֶת-יְחִידְךָ, מִמֶּנִּי.
And he said: "Do not stretch out your hand against the boy, and do nothing to him at all. For now I know that you are one who fears God — and you have not withheld your son, your only one, from me."note
13
וַיִּשָּׂא אַבְרָהָם אֶת-עֵינָיו, וַיַּרְא וְהִנֵּה-אַיִל, אַחַר, נֶאֱחַז בַּסְּבַךְ בְּקַרְנָיו; וַיֵּלֶךְ אַבְרָהָם וַיִּקַּח אֶת-הָאַיִל, וַיַּעֲלֵהוּ לְעֹלָה תַּחַת בְּנוֹ.
And Abraham lifted up his eyes and looked — and there, behind, a ram caught in the thicket by its horns. And Abraham went and took the ram, and brought it up as a burnt offering in place of his son.note
14
וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָהָם שֵׁם-הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא, יְהוָה יִרְאֶה, אֲשֶׁר יֵאָמֵר הַיּוֹם, בְּהַר יְהוָה יֵרָאֶה.
And Abraham called the name of that place Jehovah-Yireh — of which it is said to this day: "On the mount of Jehovah, it is seen to."note
15
וַיִּקְרָא מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה, אֶל-אַבְרָהָם, שֵׁנִית, מִן-הַשָּׁמָיִם.
And the angel of Jehovah called to Abraham a second time from the heavens,note
16
וַיֹּאמֶר, בִּי נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי נְאֻם-יְהוָה: כִּי, יַעַן אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתָ אֶת-הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה, וְלֹא חָשַׂכְתָּ, אֶת-בִּנְךָ אֶת-יְחִידֶךָ.
and said: "By myself I have sworn — declares Jehovah — because you have done this thing, and have not withheld your son, your only one,note
17
כִּי-בָרֵךְ אֲבָרֶכְךָ, וְהַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה אֶת-זַרְעֲךָ כְּכוֹכְבֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם, וְכַחוֹל, אֲשֶׁר עַל-שְׂפַת הַיָּם; וְיִרַשׁ זַרְעֲךָ, אֵת שַׁעַר אֹיְבָיו.
that I will surely bless you, and surely multiply your seed like the stars of the heavens, and like the sand that is on the shore of the sea; and your seed shall possess the gate of his enemies.note
18
וְהִתְבָּרְכוּ בְזַרְעֲךָ, כֹּל גּוֹיֵי הָאָרֶץ, עֵקֶב, אֲשֶׁר שָׁמַעְתָּ בְּקֹלִי.
And in your seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed — because you listened to my voice."note
19
וַיָּשָׁב אַבְרָהָם אֶל-נְעָרָיו, וַיָּקֻמוּ וַיֵּלְכוּ יַחְדָּו אֶל-בְּאֵר שָׁבַע; וַיֵּשֶׁב אַבְרָהָם, בִּבְאֵר שָׁבַע. {פ}
And Abraham returned to his young men, and they rose and went together to Beersheba; and Abraham lived at Beersheba.note
20
וַיְהִי, אַחֲרֵי הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה, וַיֻּגַּד לְאַבְרָהָם, לֵאמֹר: הִנֵּה יָלְדָה מִלְכָּה גַם-הִוא, בָּנִים--לְנָחוֹר אָחִיךָ.
And so it was, after these things, that it was told to Abraham: "Look — Milcah too has borne sons to your brother Nahor:note
21
אֶת-עוּץ בְּכֹרוֹ, וְאֶת-בּוּז אָחִיו, וְאֶת-קְמוּאֵל, אֲבִי אֲרָם.
Uz his firstborn, and Buz his brother, and Kemuel the father of Aram;note
22
וְאֶת-כֶּשֶׂד וְאֶת-חֲזוֹ, וְאֶת-פִּלְדָּשׁ וְאֶת-יִדְלָף, וְאֵת, בְּתוּאֵל.
and Chesed, and Hazo, and Pildash, and Jidlaph, and Bethuel."note
23
וּבְתוּאֵל, יָלַד אֶת-רִבְקָה; שְׁמֹנָה אֵלֶּה יָלְדָה מִלְכָּה, לְנָחוֹר אֲחִי אַבְרָהָם.
And Bethuel fathered Rebekah. These eight Milcah bore to Nahor, Abraham's brother.note
24
וּפִילַגְשׁוֹ, וּשְׁמָהּ רְאוּמָה; וַתֵּלֶד גַּם-הִוא אֶת-טֶבַח וְאֶת-גַּחַם, וְאֶת-תַּחַשׁ וְאֶת-מַעֲכָה. {פ}
And his concubine, whose name was Reumah — she also bore Tebah, and Gaham, and Tahash, and Maacah.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–5 · קַח-נָא … וְלֶךְ-לְךָ qach-na … velekh-lekha

The hardest chapter in the book, and the word love enters it. Jewish tradition calls this the Aqedah, "the Binding" — named not for the near-killing but for one verb in verse 9. It opens with a warning the reader gets and Abraham does not: God tested him (nissah; KJV "did tempt," a word that has drifted — the old sense was put to the proof, not lure into evil; NIV "tested"). Then the command, built to wound one clause at a time — "your son… your only one… whom you love… Isaac." That word, ahav, is the first appearance of "love" in the whole Bible — and it enters at the exact moment love is asked to be surrendered. The Septuagint softened yechid ("only one") to agapēton, "beloved," the very word the voice at Jesus' baptism will use; the tradition heard the rhyme early.

Go, you yourself — the bookend. "And go, you yourself" — lekh-lekha: the emphatic doubled command that appears exactly twice in the Torah, and both times to Abraham — at (12:1), "go from your land," which began everything, and here, "go to the land of Moriah," which nearly ends it. The first lekh-lekha gave up his past; the second asks for his future. The rest is famous for its restraint: no inner monologue, no protest (this is the man who argued God down to ten righteous over Sodom — and here says nothing). He "rose early in the morning" (wayyashkem, the very habit banked one chapter ago at Hagar's dawn, 21:14 — now on a far darker morning), split the wood with his own hands, and walked three days into silence. And in verse 5 a sentence the reader cannot weigh: "we will worship, and we will return to you" — plural. Resolve, or faith, or a father unable to say otherwise; the Hebrew leaves it (Hebrews 11:19 read it as faith that God could raise the dead).

Verses 6–8 · וַיֵּלְכוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם יַחְדָּו vayelkhu shenehem yachdav

The son carries the wood, and the only conversation. "Abraham took the wood… and laid it on Isaac his son" — the boy carries up the hill the wood he is to die on, a detail the Christian tradition has read as figure since the church fathers; the note records the reading and lets the Hebrew stand. Between them passes the only dialogue of the chapter, and it turns on the word that has been the family's whole life: Isaac says "My father," Abraham answers "Here I am, my son" (hineni again — the second of three; the man is completely available to God, to his son, and, at the knife, to the angel). The child's question is the unbearable one: "where is the lamb?" And the answer is the chapter's theological center: "God will see to the lamb for himself" — Elohim yireh-lo, the seeing-verb that will name the mountain (v14). KJV "God will provide himself a lamb" — and "provide" is exactly right, because in English too, to provide is to see ahead (Latin pro-videre): God's provision is God's seeing. The line bookends with "and the two of them went together" (yachdav) — said in verse 6 and again in verse 8, an envelope around the ascent; when they come down, only one is named as returning (v19), and the rabbis never stopped noticing.

Verses 9–14 · וַיַּעֲקֹד … יְהוָה יִרְאֶה vayya'aqod … Jehovah-Yireh

The binding, and the hand that stops. The whole episode is named from one word here: "he bound Isaac his son" — vayya'aqod, the verb aqad, which occurs only this once in all of scripture, and gave the scene its Hebrew title, the Aqedah. Jewish memory rests on the binding, not the near-death: an old, consenting Isaac (the midrash makes him thirty-seven) laid on the wood. The narrative slows to single motions — stretched out the hand, took the knife — and then the interruption, doubled for urgency: "Abraham, Abraham!" (as "Simon, Simon" and "Saul, Saul" will later mark a life turned mid-motion). "Now I know" (v12) states the test's whole purpose — not that God learned a fact he lacked, but that the fear of God was made visible, driven into the open where it could be seen. "You have not withheld your son" — lo chasakhta: Paul reverses this exact phrase across the canon — God "did not spare his own Son" (Romans 8:32).

The ram, and the mountain of seeing. "Abraham lifted up his eyes and looked" — the same gesture as the woman at the other well, whose "eyes God opened, and she saw" (21:19): the seeing-thread of this book keeps closing on rescue. "A ram caught in the thicket by its horns" — the substitute, offered up "in place of his son" (tachat beno): a life for a life, the logic of every altar to come. And Abraham names the place Jehovah-Yireh, "Jehovah sees / provides" (KJV "Jehovah-jireh"), sealing the pun the chapter has been building — yireh (he sees to it, v8), yera'eh (it is seen to, v14). This site has collected the seeing-God before: Hagar's El Roi, "the God who sees" (16:13); now the mountain bears the name. And there is one more thread the note owes the reader: 2 Chronicles 3:1 says Solomon built the temple "on Mount Moriah" — so the tradition set this altar exactly where the later altar would stand, and "on the mount of Jehovah it is seen to" became a saying about a specific hill in Jerusalem.

Verses 15–19 · בִּי נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי bi nishba'ti

The one oath God swears by himself. "By myself I have sworn" — the only time in the Torah God binds himself with an oath, and the tradition weighed it heavily: there being no one greater to swear by, he swore by himself (Hebrews 6:13 makes the whole argument from this verse). What follows is the seventh and final restatement of the promise first spoken at 12:1-3, and now — after the knife — sealed under oath: seed like the stars of the heavens (the count Abram was shown on a night long ago, 15:5) and now also like the sand on the seashore, a second, earth-bound multitude. And the old universal note returns, its verb upgraded: not just "in you shall all families be blessed" (12:3) but "in your seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed" — the channel named. The reason given is the chapter's last quiet echo: "because you listened to my voice" (shama beqoli) — the phrase that named a fall in the garden (3:17, "you listened to the voice of your wife"), was turned into a command one chapter ago (21:12, "listen to her voice"), and here becomes the ground of the world's blessing: an obedience that undoes a disobedience. Then the descent, and a return "to Beersheba" — exactly where chapter 21 left him, the well of the oath now holding a second, greater one.

Verses 20–24 · וּבְתוּאֵל, יָלַד אֶת-רִבְקָה uVtu'el yalad et-Rivqah

A quiet coda that turns the whole book forward. After the mountain, an ordinary piece of family news arrives from the old country — "your brother Nahor" (left behind in Haran, 11:29) has a houseful of sons. It reads like a genealogical footnote, twelve names of Aramean clans (Uz, where Job will live; Buz; Aram, the people whose language the later scriptures will partly speak). But the list is built to deliver one name, and it lands almost in passing, in verse 23: "and Bethuel fathered Rebekah." The chapter that nearly ended the line of promise closes by naming, without fanfare, the girl who will carry it on — the wife Isaac has not yet met, two chapters early. The son was spared on the mountain; here, quietly, the narrative begins arranging for him to have a future. It is the book's characteristic move: after the unbearable, the ordinary, already turning toward tomorrow.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation sides with the literal shelf: "you have not withheld your son" (KJV) — kept, because Paul reverses this exact verb; "Jehovah-Yireh" left as the name it is (KJV "Jehovah-jireh"); "the two of them went together," the envelope kept in both verses.

Where it goes its own way: "tested" for nissah (KJV "tempt," aged out of its old proving-sense); "God will see to the lamb for himself" for yireh-lo (KJV "provide" — the note keeps both, since to provide is to see-ahead); "bound" for aqad, the hapax that titles the whole scene; "your only one" for yechid (KJV "only son"), the word the Septuagint rhymed toward "beloved."

Echoes paid and planted. Paid: lekh-lekha, "go, you yourself" — the twin command that brackets Abraham's whole story (12:1 and here); "rose early in the morning," the dawn-habit banked at (21:14); the seeing-God — Hagar's El Roi (16:13) and the eyes opened at the well (21:19), now the name of a mountain; the stars Abram counted (15:5), joined by the sand; "all the families/nations blessed" — the 12:3 promise (12:3) sealed under oath; "listened to my voice" — the garden's fatal phrase (3:17), turned to obedience; and Beersheba (21:33), the well of the oath, holding a second. Planted: Rebekah, named in the coda, waiting for chapter 24; Isaac's own unrecorded descent, which the rabbis never stopped reading; and Moriah — the mountain of seeing that 2 Chronicles will make the Temple Mount.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 23 — the death of Sarah, and the cave of Machpelah: Abraham buys a grave, and the promise of the land begins with four hundred shekels and a field.

Proverbs 1 — The Mister Translation

1
מִשְׁלֵי, שְׁלֹמֹה בֶן-דָּוִד-- מֶלֶךְ, יִשְׂרָאֵל.
The proverbs of Solomon son of David, king of Israel:note
2
לָדַעַת חָכְמָה וּמוּסָר; לְהָבִין, אִמְרֵי בִינָה.
to know wisdom and discipline,
to grasp the sayings of understanding;note
3
לָקַחַת, מוּסַר הַשְׂכֵּל; צֶדֶק וּמִשְׁפָּט, וּמֵשָׁרִים.
to receive the discipline of insight —
righteousness, justice, and fairness;note
4
לָתֵת לִפְתָאיִם עָרְמָה; לְנַעַר, דַּעַת וּמְזִמָּה.
to give the simple shrewdness,
to the young, knowledge and discretion —note
5
יִשְׁמַע חָכָם, וְיוֹסֶף לֶקַח; וְנָבוֹן, תַּחְבֻּלוֹת יִקְנֶה.
let the wise hear, and add to their learning,
and the understanding gain the arts of guidance;note
6
לְהָבִין מָשָׁל, וּמְלִיצָה; דִּבְרֵי חֲכָמִים, וְחִידֹתָם.
to understand a proverb and a parable,
the words of the wise and their riddles.note
7
יִרְאַת יְהוָה, רֵאשִׁית דָּעַת; חָכְמָה וּמוּסָר, אֱוִילִים בָּזוּ. {פ}
The fear of Jehovah is the beginning of knowledge;
wisdom and discipline, fools despise.note
8
שְׁמַע בְּנִי, מוּסַר אָבִיךָ; וְאַל-תִּטֹּשׁ, תּוֹרַת אִמֶּךָ.
Hear, my son, your father's discipline,
and do not forsake your mother's teaching;note
9
כִּי, לִוְיַת חֵן הֵם לְרֹאשֶׁךָ; וַעֲנָקִים, לְגַרְגְּרֹתֶךָ.
for they are a wreath of grace for your head,
and pendants for your neck.note
10
בְּנִי-- אִם-יְפַתּוּךָ חַטָּאִים, אַל-תֹּבֵא.
My son, if sinners entice you,
do not consent.note
11
אִם-יֹאמְרוּ, לְכָה אִתָּנוּ: נֶאֶרְבָה לְדָם; נִצְפְּנָה לְנָקִי חִנָּם.
If they say, "Come with us —
let us lie in wait for blood, let us ambush the innocent for no reason;note
12
נִבְלָעֵם, כִּשְׁאוֹל חַיִּים; וּתְמִימִים, כְּיוֹרְדֵי בוֹר.
let us swallow them alive, like Sheol,
whole, like those going down to the pit;note
13
כָּל-הוֹן יָקָר נִמְצָא; נְמַלֵּא בָתֵּינוּ שָׁלָל.
we will find every precious thing,
we will fill our houses with spoil;note
14
גּוֹרָלְךָ, תַּפִּיל בְּתוֹכֵנוּ; כִּיס אֶחָד, יִהְיֶה לְכֻלָּנוּ.
throw in your lot with us,
one purse for us all" —note
15
בְּנִי--אַל-תֵּלֵךְ בְּדֶרֶךְ אִתָּם; מְנַע רַגְלְךָ, מִנְּתִיבָתָם.
my son, do not walk in the way with them,
hold your foot back from their path;note
16
כִּי רַגְלֵיהֶם, לָרַע יָרוּצוּ; וִימַהֲרוּ, לִשְׁפָּךְ-דָּם.
for their feet run to evil,
and they hurry to shed blood.note
17
כִּי-חִנָּם, מְזֹרָה הָרָשֶׁת-- בְּעֵינֵי, כָּל-בַּעַל כָּנָף.
For in vain is the net spread out
in the sight of any winged bird —note
18
וְהֵם, לְדָמָם יֶאֱרֹבוּ; יִצְפְּנוּ, לְנַפְשֹׁתָם.
yet these lie in wait for their own blood,
they ambush their own lives.note
19
כֵּן--אָרְחוֹת, כָּל-בֹּצֵעַ בָּצַע; אֶת-נֶפֶשׁ בְּעָלָיו יִקָּח.
Such are the paths of everyone greedy for gain:
it takes away the life of its owner.note
20
חָכְמוֹת, בַּחוּץ תָּרֹנָּה; בָּרְחֹבוֹת, תִּתֵּן קוֹלָהּ.
Wisdom cries aloud in the street,
in the squares she lifts her voice;note
21
בְּרֹאשׁ הֹמִיּוֹת, תִּקְרָא: בְּפִתְחֵי שְׁעָרִים בָּעִיר--אֲמָרֶיהָ תֹאמֵר.
at the head of the noisy streets she calls,
at the openings of the gates, in the city, she speaks her words:note
22
עַד-מָתַי, פְּתָיִם-- תְּאֵהֲבוּ-פֶתִי: וְלֵצִים--לָצוֹן, חָמְדוּ לָהֶם; וּכְסִילִים, יִשְׂנְאוּ-דָעַת.
"How long, you simple ones, will you love being simple,
and scoffers delight in their scoffing, and fools hate knowledge?note
23
תָּשׁוּבוּ, לְתוֹכַחְתִּי: הִנֵּה אַבִּיעָה לָכֶם רוּחִי; אוֹדִיעָה דְבָרַי אֶתְכֶם.
Turn back at my reproof!
Look — I will pour out my spirit to you, I will make my words known to you.note
24
יַעַן קָרָאתִי, וַתְּמָאֵנוּ; נָטִיתִי יָדִי, וְאֵין מַקְשִׁיב.
Because I called and you refused,
I stretched out my hand and no one paid heed,note
25
וַתִּפְרְעוּ כָל-עֲצָתִי; וְתוֹכַחְתִּי, לֹא אֲבִיתֶם.
and you let go of all my counsel,
and would have none of my reproof —note
26
גַּם-אֲנִי, בְּאֵידְכֶם אֶשְׂחָק; אֶלְעַג, בְּבֹא פַחְדְּכֶם.
I too will laugh at your calamity,
I will mock when your dread comes,note
27
בְּבֹא כשאוה כְשׁוֹאָה, פַּחְדְּכֶם-- וְאֵידְכֶם, כְּסוּפָה יֶאֱתֶה; בְּבֹא עֲלֵיכֶם, צָרָה וְצוּקָה.
when your dread comes like a storm,
and your calamity arrives like a whirlwind, when distress and anguish come upon you.note
28
אָז יִקְרָאֻנְנִי, וְלֹא אֶעֱנֶה; יְשַׁחֲרֻנְנִי, וְלֹא יִמְצָאֻנְנִי.
Then they will call me, but I will not answer;
they will seek me, but they will not find me.note
29
תַּחַת, כִּי-שָׂנְאוּ דָעַת; וְיִרְאַת יְהוָה, לֹא בָחָרוּ.
Because they hated knowledge,
and did not choose the fear of Jehovah;note
30
לֹא-אָבוּ לַעֲצָתִי; נָאֲצוּ, כָּל-תּוֹכַחְתִּי.
they would have none of my counsel,
they spurned all my reproof —note
31
וְיֹאכְלוּ, מִפְּרִי דַרְכָּם; וּמִמֹּעֲצֹתֵיהֶם יִשְׂבָּעוּ.
so they shall eat from the fruit of their way,
and be filled with their own devices.note
32
כִּי מְשׁוּבַת פְּתָיִם תַּהַרְגֵם; וְשַׁלְוַת כְּסִילִים תְּאַבְּדֵם.
For the waywardness of the simple kills them,
and the complacency of fools destroys them.note
33
וְשֹׁמֵעַ לִי, יִשְׁכָּן-בֶּטַח; וְשַׁאֲנַן, מִפַּחַד רָעָה. {פ}
but whoever listens to me will dwell secure,
and be at ease, without dread of harm."note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–7 · יִרְאַת יְהוָה, רֵאשִׁית דָּעַת yirat Jehovah, reshit da'at

A new kind of book — and its whole toolkit in seven verses. This is the site's first wisdom book, and it does not tell a story or deliver an oracle: it teaches. The Hebrew is poetry — every line a couplet, the second half echoing, sharpening, or countering the first (this translation sets the two halves on two lines so the parallel shows). The title-word is the book's own name: mishlei, "the proverbs of Solomon" — a mashal is a comparison, a saying that rules by likeness (the root means "to be like," and also "to rule"). Verses 2–6 are one long sentence of purpose, and they lay out the book's whole vocabulary — and Hebrew keeps these words distinct, so this translation does too (each has its own dictionary entry): chokhmah, wisdom, the master-skill for living; da'at, knowledge, what is known (from yada, "to know"); binah, understanding — from bin, "to discern between," the faculty that tells one thing from another; mezimmah, discretion, shrewd forethought (v4); and haskel, insight, prudence that actually succeeds (v3). They are not synonyms stacked for volume: wisdom is knowledge and understanding put to work. And musar — which this translation renders discipline, with the ASV and NWT, against the KJV's softer "instruction" — is the odd one out: not a kind of knowing at all, but the correction that forms a person, the training with a rod behind it ("do not despise the discipline of Jehovah," 3:11). The two audiences: the simple (peti — the wide-open, gullible young person) and the already-wise, told to keep learning (v5). The prize offered the simple in verse 4 is ormah, "shrewdness" — and here the shelf keeps a secret this project loves: KJV renders it "subtilty," the very word it used for the serpent in (Genesis 3:1), "more subtil than any beast." The serpent's root and Wisdom's are the same (ʿarum / ormah): the craftiness that ruined Eden is exactly what Wisdom gives the naive to survive — shrewdness redeemed.

The motto of the book. Then verse 7, the sentence everything else hangs from: "The fear of Jehovah is the beginning of knowledge" (ASV reads it exactly so — "the fear of Jehovah is the beginning of knowledge" — while KJV prints its small-caps divine name in the same slot). Two things the shelf can't quite carry at once: reshit is "beginning" — and it is the exact word that opens the whole Bible, bereshit, "in the beginning" (Genesis 1:1) — but it also means "the first / chief part," the firstfruits, the best. So the fear of Jehovah is both where knowledge starts and its finest portion. And "fear" (yirah) is not fright but reverent awe — the same posture the angel found in Abraham at the altar, "now I know that you fear God" (22:12). The opposite party is named too: the evilim, the fools who despise wisdom and discipline. That verb (bazu) is a perfect — and the NWT, at its careful most-literal, carries it as "have despised"; this translation reads it as the gnomic perfect that Hebrew wisdom favors — a standing truth stated as already settled, which idiomatic English renders in the present. A considered choice, not a default: the sense is that fools always have and always do. Either way, the evilim are the first entry in the book's long rogues' gallery of the unwise.

Verses 8–19 · אִם-יְפַתּוּךָ חַטָּאִים im-yefattukha chatta'im

The first lecture: a father's voice, a mother's law. "Hear, my son" (shema beni) — the address that will open lecture after lecture; wisdom in Proverbs is handed down at home, across a table, parent to child. And it comes from both parents: "your father's instruction and your mother's teaching" — where "teaching" is torah, the very word for the five books of Moses, here given to a mother's home-lesson (KJV "the law of thy mother"): Torah begins on a lap. The reward is jewelry — "a wreath of grace for your head" (KJV "an ornament of grace"): the disciplined life as something you wear.

"If sinners entice you." The verb is patah — and this site has heard it before, in the rawest place: it is the word Jeremiah threw at God, "you enticed me, Jehovah, and I was enticed" (Jeremiah 20:7). The same seduction the prophet felt from heaven, the son is warned against from the street-gang — whose pitch the father then quotes at length, and it is chillingly modern: easy money, belonging ("one purse for us all"), the thrill of the ambush. The trap-image is precise: even a bird won't fly into a net it can see being spread (v17) — yet these men spread it for themselves, "they lie in wait for their own blood" (v18). The moral is stated as physics, not sermon: "such are the paths of everyone greedy for gain — it takes away the life of its owner" (v19). Greed is a boomerang; the loot ends up costing exactly the thing it was traded for.

Verses 20–33 · חָכְמוֹת בַּחוּץ תָּרֹנָּה chokhmot bachuts taronnah

Lady Wisdom takes the street. Now the book's most startling move: Wisdom stands up and preaches. She is personified — a woman (the noun is feminine), and the plural form chokhmot reads as majestic, "Wisdom" at full stature (KJV "Wisdom" for the plural). And she doesn't sit in a school: she "cries aloud in the street," in the squares, "at the head of the noisy streets," "at the openings of the gates" — the market, the courthouse, the town's loudest corners. This is a prophet's posture, not a professor's; she sounds like Jeremiah at the temple gate. Her first words are the prophets' own weary question: "How long?" — aimed at the whole rogues' gallery at once: the simple (petayim) who love their simplicity, the scoffers (letsim — the cynics who mock everything; KJV "scorners"), and the fools (kesilim — the thick, the dull; a different fool from verse 7's evil) who "hate knowledge." Hebrew has a whole thesaurus for foolishness, and this chapter opens the drawer.

The most frightening promise in the book — and the softest. What follows is hard to hear: the same Wisdom who begs in verse 23 ("turn back… I will pour out my spirit to you") warns that a day is coming when the call reverses — "I too will laugh at your calamity" (v26), and "then they will call me, but I will not answer" (v28). It is the exact inversion of every prophet's promise that God answers those who call; refuse wisdom long enough, the poem says, and you unfit yourself to receive it — not because the door is shut in spite, but because "they hated knowledge and did not choose the fear of Jehovah" (v29), and a life reaps "the fruit of its own way" (v31) the way the greedy men of verse 19 did. The Hebrew here even carries a scribe's fingerprint — verse 27's ketiv/qere, the written and read forms of "storm" standing side by side, the manuscript's honesty this site first met in Daniel. But the chapter does not end in the whirlwind. Its last line drops the volume all the way down to a promise anyone can keep: "whoever listens to me will dwell secure, and be at ease, without dread of harm" (v33). The whole book's offer, in one quiet couplet — safety, not from trouble, but from fear.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation follows the most literal witnesses: "Jehovah" where most of the shelf prints the small-caps substitute for the Name (with the ASV and NWT, the two versions that read this verse most exactly); "Wisdom cries" for the majestic plural; and — the point of the whole prologue — both word-families kept distinct rather than blurred: the fools (peti simple, lets scoffer, kesil and evil, two different fools) and the five wisdom-words (wisdom, knowledge, understanding, discretion, insight), each rendered by its own English word, as Hebrew keeps them.

Where it goes its own way: the couplets set on two lines, to show the Hebrew parallelism the prose shelf hides; "discipline" for musar — with the ASV and NWT, against the softer "instruction" of KJV and NIV — because musar is correction that forms, not mere information (an earlier draft of this page reached for the familiar "instruction"; the sharper word is the right one); "shrewdness" for ormah (KJV "subtilty" — the note keeps the KJV's serpent-echo, since that is the whole point); "your mother's teaching" for torah (KJV "law"), to let a mother's Torah be heard as Torah; and "the beginning / chief part" both kept in the note for reshit.

Echoes paid and planted. Paid: ormah, Wisdom's "shrewdness," the serpent's craft (3:1) turned to a survival skill; reshit, "beginning," the Bible's own first word (Genesis 1:1); the fear of God, found in Abraham at the altar (22:12); and patah, "entice," the verb Jeremiah hurled at heaven (Jeremiah 20:7), now the sinners' pitch. Planted: Lady Wisdom, who will rise to her full height in Proverbs 8 (and whom the New Testament will hear behind the voice of Christ); the fools' gallery, filled in chapter by chapter; and "the fear of Jehovah," the refrain the whole book is tuned to.

Next installment in this book: Proverbs 2 — "if you seek her like silver, and search for her like hidden treasure": wisdom as the thing worth digging for.

Genesis 23 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיִּהְיוּ חַיֵּי שָׂרָה, מֵאָה שָׁנָה וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וְשֶׁבַע שָׁנִים--שְׁנֵי, חַיֵּי שָׂרָה.
And the life of Sarah was 127 years — the years of the life of Sarah.note
2
וַתָּמָת שָׂרָה, בְּקִרְיַת אַרְבַּע הִוא חֶבְרוֹן--בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן; וַיָּבֹא, אַבְרָהָם, לִסְפֹּד לְשָׂרָה, וְלִבְכֹּתָהּ.
And Sarah died at Kiriath-arba — that is, Hebron — in the land of Canaan; and Abraham came to lament for Sarah, and to weep for her.note
3
וַיָּקָם, אַבְרָהָם, מֵעַל, פְּנֵי מֵתוֹ; וַיְדַבֵּר אֶל-בְּנֵי-חֵת, לֵאמֹר.
And Abraham rose up from before his dead, and spoke to the sons of Heth, saying:note
4
גֵּר-וְתוֹשָׁב אָנֹכִי, עִמָּכֶם; תְּנוּ לִי אֲחֻזַּת-קֶבֶר עִמָּכֶם, וְאֶקְבְּרָה מֵתִי מִלְּפָנָי.
"I am a resident alien and a settler among you. Give me a burial holding among you, that I may bury my dead from before me."note
5
וַיַּעֲנוּ בְנֵי-חֵת אֶת-אַבְרָהָם, לֵאמֹר לוֹ.
And the sons of Heth answered Abraham, saying to him:note
6
שְׁמָעֵנוּ אֲדֹנִי, נְשִׂיא אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה בְּתוֹכֵנוּ--בְּמִבְחַר קְבָרֵינוּ, קְבֹר אֶת-מֵתֶךָ; אִישׁ מִמֶּנּוּ, אֶת-קִבְרוֹ לֹא-יִכְלֶה מִמְּךָ מִקְּבֹר מֵתֶךָ.
"Hear us, my lord — a prince of God you are among us; in the choicest of our graves bury your dead. None of us will withhold his grave from you, from burying your dead."note
7
וַיָּקָם אַבְרָהָם וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ לְעַם-הָאָרֶץ, לִבְנֵי-חֵת.
And Abraham rose and bowed down to the people of the land, to the sons of Heth.note
8
וַיְדַבֵּר אִתָּם, לֵאמֹר: אִם-יֵשׁ אֶת-נַפְשְׁכֶם, לִקְבֹּר אֶת-מֵתִי מִלְּפָנַי--שְׁמָעוּנִי, וּפִגְעוּ-לִי בְּעֶפְרוֹן בֶּן-צֹחַר.
And he spoke with them, saying: "If it is your will that I should bury my dead from before me, hear me, and plead for me with Ephron son of Zohar,note
9
וְיִתֶּן-לִי, אֶת-מְעָרַת הַמַּכְפֵּלָה אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ, אֲשֶׁר, בִּקְצֵה שָׂדֵהוּ: בְּכֶסֶף מָלֵא יִתְּנֶנָּה לִּי, בְּתוֹכְכֶם--לַאֲחֻזַּת-קָבֶר.
that he may give me the cave of Machpelah that is his, at the edge of his field. For full silver let him give it to me, among you, for a burial holding."note
10
וְעֶפְרוֹן יֹשֵׁב, בְּתוֹךְ בְּנֵי-חֵת; וַיַּעַן עֶפְרוֹן הַחִתִּי אֶת-אַבְרָהָם בְּאָזְנֵי בְנֵי-חֵת, לְכֹל בָּאֵי שַׁעַר-עִירוֹ לֵאמֹר.
Now Ephron was sitting among the sons of Heth; and Ephron the Hittite answered Abraham in the hearing of the sons of Heth, of all who came in at the gate of his city, saying:note
11
לֹא-אֲדֹנִי שְׁמָעֵנִי--הַשָּׂדֶה נָתַתִּי לָךְ, וְהַמְּעָרָה אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ לְךָ נְתַתִּיהָ; לְעֵינֵי בְנֵי-עַמִּי נְתַתִּיהָ לָּךְ, קְבֹר מֵתֶךָ.
"No, my lord, hear me — the field I give to you, and the cave that is in it, to you I give it; before the eyes of the sons of my people I give it to you. Bury your dead."note
12
וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ, אַבְרָהָם, לִפְנֵי, עַם הָאָרֶץ.
And Abraham bowed down before the people of the land.note
13
וַיְדַבֵּר אֶל-עֶפְרוֹן בְּאָזְנֵי עַם-הָאָרֶץ, לֵאמֹר, אַךְ אִם-אַתָּה לוּ, שְׁמָעֵנִי: נָתַתִּי כֶּסֶף הַשָּׂדֶה, קַח מִמֶּנִּי, וְאֶקְבְּרָה אֶת-מֵתִי, שָׁמָּה.
And he spoke to Ephron in the hearing of the people of the land, saying: "Only — if you would just hear me: I give the silver for the field; take it from me, and I will bury my dead there."note
14
וַיַּעַן עֶפְרוֹן אֶת-אַבְרָהָם, לֵאמֹר לוֹ.
And Ephron answered Abraham, saying to him:note
15
אֲדֹנִי שְׁמָעֵנִי, אֶרֶץ אַרְבַּע מֵאֹת שֶׁקֶל-כֶּסֶף בֵּינִי וּבֵינְךָ מַה-הִוא; וְאֶת-מֵתְךָ, קְבֹר.
"My lord, hear me — land worth four hundred shekels of silver, between me and you, what is that? Bury your dead."note
16
וַיִּשְׁמַע אַבְרָהָם, אֶל-עֶפְרוֹן, וַיִּשְׁקֹל אַבְרָהָם לְעֶפְרֹן, אֶת-הַכֶּסֶף אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר בְּאָזְנֵי בְנֵי-חֵת--אַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שֶׁקֶל כֶּסֶף, עֹבֵר לַסֹּחֵר.
And Abraham listened to Ephron; and Abraham weighed out to Ephron the silver he had named in the hearing of the sons of Heth — four hundred shekels of silver, at the merchant's standard.note
17
וַיָּקָם שְׂדֵה עֶפְרוֹן, אֲשֶׁר בַּמַּכְפֵּלָה, אֲשֶׁר, לִפְנֵי מַמְרֵא: הַשָּׂדֶה, וְהַמְּעָרָה אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ, וְכָל-הָעֵץ אֲשֶׁר בַּשָּׂדֶה, אֲשֶׁר בְּכָל-גְּבֻלוֹ סָבִיב.
So the field of Ephron at Machpelah, which faced Mamre — the field, and the cave that was in it, and every tree in the field, within all its border round about — was made overnote
18
לְאַבְרָהָם לְמִקְנָה, לְעֵינֵי בְנֵי-חֵת, בְּכֹל, בָּאֵי שַׁעַר-עִירוֹ.
to Abraham as a purchase, before the eyes of the sons of Heth, before all who came in at the gate of his city.note
19
וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן קָבַר אַבְרָהָם אֶת-שָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ, אֶל-מְעָרַת שְׂדֵה הַמַּכְפֵּלָה עַל-פְּנֵי מַמְרֵא--הִוא חֶבְרוֹן: בְּאֶרֶץ, כְּנָעַן.
And after this, Abraham buried Sarah his wife in the cave of the field of Machpelah, facing Mamre — that is, Hebron — in the land of Canaan.note
20
וַיָּקָם הַשָּׂדֶה וְהַמְּעָרָה אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ, לְאַבְרָהָם--לַאֲחֻזַּת-קָבֶר: מֵאֵת, בְּנֵי-חֵת. {ס}
So the field, and the cave that was in it, were made over to Abraham as a burial holding, from the sons of Heth.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–6 · גֵּר-וְתוֹשָׁב אָנֹכִי ger-vetoshav anokhi

The only matriarch whose age and death the Torah records. Sarah dies at 127 — scripture gives no other woman's lifespan — and the chapter opens the parashah the tradition calls Chayei Sarah, "the Life of Sarah," a title that names a death. She dies at Kiriath-arba ("town of Arba," a giant of the Anakim, or "town of the four") — the narrator adds, as he does with Ur and the Philistines, the name the reader knows: "that is, Hebron." And Abraham "came to lament for Sarah and to weep for her" — two distinct words this translation keeps apart: sapad, the formal, public mourning-rite (the eulogy, the wailing), and bakah, plain tears. The Masoretes even wrote the kaf of "weep" (velivkotah) as a miniature letter in the scroll — a scribal whisper the sages read as private grief, the tears kept small behind the public lament.

"A resident alien and a settler." Abraham's self-description is two precise legal words, and the shelf mostly blurs them: ger and toshav (KJV "a stranger and a sojourner"; NWT "an alien resident and a settler," keeping them two). A ger is a resident foreigner — living among a people, protected, but with no citizenship and no right to own land; a toshav is a settled outsider, a step more rooted, still landless. That is the whole problem of the chapter stated in a breath: the man to whom God has promised all this land as an everlasting holding (17:8) cannot legally bury his wife in it — he owns not one square foot. And the word he uses for what he needs is loaded: achuzzah, a holding — the permanent, inheritable land-possession (from achaz, "to grasp, hold fast"), the very term 17:8 used for the promised land itself. The sons of Heth answer with Eastern courtesy and real respect: "you are a prince of God among us" — nesi Elohim (NWT "a chieftain of God"; KJV flattens the divine name to a superlative, "a mighty prince"). Both readings live in the phrase — "a prince of God" and "a godlike, mighty prince" — and this translation keeps the literal one, since the whole point is that these Hittites can see whose man Abraham is.

Verses 7–16 · בְּכֶסֶף מָלֵא … עֹבֵר לַסֹּחֵר bekhesef male … over lassocher

A masterclass in Near-Eastern bargaining. Every move here is ritual, and to a modern ear it reads like theater — because it is. Abraham bows (twice), speaks only through intermediaries at first, and insists on paying full silver (kesef male, v9) — no gift, no debt, no lien a future heir could contest. Ephron plays his part with lavish generosity: "the field I give you, and the cave — I give it you!" (v11) — said three times, in public, at the city gate. It is not an offer to donate; it is the opening of a negotiation, the seller's flourish before the number. And the number, when it slips out, is dressed as a trifle between friends: "land worth four hundred shekels of silver — between me and you, what is that?" (v15). Four hundred shekels was a small fortune (Jeremiah later buys a whole field for seventeen, 32:9); Ephron names a steep price with a shrug, and Abraham, who wanted no favors, pays it without a word of haggling. He "weighed out" the silver — coinage was centuries away; you cut and weighed metal — "four hundred shekels, at the merchant's standard" (over lassocher, KJV "current money with the merchant"): full weight, trade-grade, no thumb on the scale. The scene is a legal record disguised as a courtesy.

Verses 17–20 · וַיָּקָם שְׂדֵה עֶפְרוֹן vayyaqom sedeh Efron

The deed, itemized — and the first foothold. The transfer is recorded with a surveyor's care: the field, the cave, "and every tree in the field, within all its border round about" — Hittite and Mesopotamian land-deeds itemized the trees exactly so, because trees were taxable and disputable. The verb for what happened to the property is a legal one: it "rose" — vayyaqom — meaning it stood confirmed, was made over, passed valid title (KJV "were made sure"; the same root that says a covenant is "established"). Witnessed by the whole assembly "at the gate of his city" — the ancient courthouse — it is a transaction no one could ever overturn. And that is the quiet thunder of this chapter: the man promised an entire land dies owning a single field with a cave in it — a grave. The whole inheritance of Israel, in Abraham's lifetime, comes down to Machpelah ("the double" — from kaphal, "to fold, to double") — the first legally-held parcel of the Promised Land, and the down payment on all the rest. It becomes the patriarchal tomb: Abraham himself, Isaac and Rebekah, Jacob and Leah are all laid here (25:9; 49:31; 50:13), and the Herodian enclosure over the cave at Hebron still stands, one of the oldest continuously-venerated sites on earth. Faith buries its dead in the ground of the promise — and holds the receipt.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words distinct: "a resident alien and a settler" for ger and toshav (with the NWT, against KJV's merged "stranger and sojourner") — two different legal statuses, both landless; "lament" and "weep" for sapad and bakah (the public rite and the private tears); and "a prince of God" for nesi Elohim (with the NWT's "chieftain of God," against KJV's "mighty prince"), keeping the divine name the Hittites actually spoke.

Where it goes its own way: "a holding" for achuzzah (KJV "possession"), to let it ring against 17:8's promised holding; "was made over" for the legal vayyaqom (KJV "were made sure"); "at the merchant's standard" for over lassocher (KJV "current money with the merchant"); and numerals for Sarah's 127 years.

Echoes paid and planted. Paid: achuzzah, the "everlasting holding" God promised at (17:8) — and here, in Abraham's lifetime, a burial holding is all of it he owns; Mamre, the oaks where Abraham pitched his tent (13:18), now facing the family tomb; "I will make your name great" (12:2), visible in the Hittites' "prince of God"; and the land promised "to your seed" (12:7), of which this single field is the first legal parcel. Planted: Machpelah, the patriarchal tomb (Isaac and Jacob will come here); Rebekah, named two chapters back, whom the next chapter goes to fetch for the now-motherless Isaac; and Hebron, which will be Israel's first royal capital under David.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 24 — the longest chapter in the book: a servant, ten camels, a prayer at a well, and Rebekah's "I will go" — how Isaac gets a wife.

Exodus 1 — The Mister Translation

1
וְאֵלֶּה, שְׁמוֹת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, הַבָּאִים, מִצְרָיְמָה: אֵת יַעֲקֹב, אִישׁ וּבֵיתוֹ בָּאוּ.
And these are the names of the sons of Israel who came into Egypt with Jacob — each man and his household came:note
2
רְאוּבֵן שִׁמְעוֹן, לֵוִי וִיהוּדָה.
Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah;note
3
יִשָּׂשכָר זְבוּלֻן, וּבִנְיָמִן.
Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin;note
4
דָּן וְנַפְתָּלִי, גָּד וְאָשֵׁר.
Dan and Naphtali, Gad and Asher.note
5
וַיְהִי, כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ יֹצְאֵי יֶרֶךְ-יַעֲקֹב--שִׁבְעִים נָפֶשׁ; וְיוֹסֵף, הָיָה בְמִצְרָיִם.
And all the souls who came from the loins of Jacob were seventy souls; and Joseph was in Egypt already.note
6
וַיָּמָת יוֹסֵף וְכָל-אֶחָיו, וְכֹל הַדּוֹר הַהוּא.
And Joseph died, and all his brothers, and all that generation.note
7
וּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, פָּרוּ וַיִּשְׁרְצוּ וַיִּרְבּוּ וַיַּעַצְמוּ--בִּמְאֹד מְאֹד; וַתִּמָּלֵא הָאָרֶץ, אֹתָם. {פ}
And the sons of Israel were fruitful, and swarmed, and multiplied, and grew mighty — exceedingly, exceedingly; and the land was filled with them.note
8
וַיָּקָם מֶלֶךְ-חָדָשׁ, עַל-מִצְרָיִם, אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יָדַע, אֶת-יוֹסֵף.
And a new king arose over Egypt, who did not know Joseph.note
9
וַיֹּאמֶר, אֶל-עַמּוֹ: הִנֵּה, עַם בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל--רַב וְעָצוּם, מִמֶּנּוּ.
And he said to his people: "Look — the people of the sons of Israel are more numerous and mightier than we.note
10
הָבָה נִתְחַכְּמָה, לוֹ: פֶּן-יִרְבֶּה, וְהָיָה כִּי-תִקְרֶאנָה מִלְחָמָה וְנוֹסַף גַּם-הוּא עַל-שֹׂנְאֵינוּ, וְנִלְחַם-בָּנוּ, וְעָלָה מִן-הָאָרֶץ.
Come, let us outwit them, lest they multiply — and if war should happen, they too may be added to our enemies and fight against us and go up out of the land."note
11
וַיָּשִׂימוּ עָלָיו שָׂרֵי מִסִּים, לְמַעַן עַנֹּתוֹ בְּסִבְלֹתָם; וַיִּבֶן עָרֵי מִסְכְּנוֹת, לְפַרְעֹה--אֶת-פִּתֹם, וְאֶת-רַעַמְסֵס.
So they set over them chiefs of forced labor, to afflict them with their burdens; and they built store-cities for Pharaoh — Pithom and Raamses.note
12
וְכַאֲשֶׁר יְעַנּוּ אֹתוֹ, כֵּן יִרְבֶּה וְכֵן יִפְרֹץ; וַיָּקֻצוּ, מִפְּנֵי בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל.
But the more they afflicted them, so much the more they multiplied and so much the more they spread; and they came to dread the sons of Israel.note
13
וַיַּעֲבִדוּ מִצְרַיִם אֶת-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, בְּפָרֶךְ.
And the Egyptians made the sons of Israel serve with crushing harshness,note
14
וַיְמָרְרוּ אֶת-חַיֵּיהֶם בַּעֲבֹדָה קָשָׁה, בְּחֹמֶר וּבִלְבֵנִים, וּבְכָל-עֲבֹדָה, בַּשָּׂדֶה--אֵת, כָּל-עֲבֹדָתָם, אֲשֶׁר-עָבְדוּ בָהֶם, בְּפָרֶךְ.
and they made their lives bitter with hard service — in mortar and in bricks, and in every service of the field; all their service that they made them serve was with crushing harshness.note
15
וַיֹּאמֶר מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם, לַמְיַלְּדֹת הָעִבְרִיֹּת, אֲשֶׁר שֵׁם הָאַחַת שִׁפְרָה, וְשֵׁם הַשֵּׁנִית פּוּעָה.
And the king of Egypt said to the Hebrew midwives — the name of the one was Shiphrah, and the name of the second, Puah —note
16
וַיֹּאמֶר, בְּיַלֶּדְכֶן אֶת-הָעִבְרִיּוֹת, וּרְאִיתֶן, עַל-הָאָבְנָיִם: אִם-בֵּן הוּא וַהֲמִתֶּן אֹתוֹ, וְאִם-בַּת הִוא וָחָיָה.
and he said: "When you deliver the Hebrew women, look on the two stones: if it is a son, put him to death; but if it is a daughter, she shall live."note
17
וַתִּירֶאןָ הַמְיַלְּדֹת, אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים, וְלֹא עָשׂוּ, כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר אֲלֵיהֶן מֶלֶךְ מִצְרָיִם; וַתְּחַיֶּיןָ, אֶת-הַיְלָדִים.
But the midwives feared God, and did not do as the king of Egypt had spoken to them; and they let the boys live.note
18
וַיִּקְרָא מֶלֶךְ-מִצְרַיִם, לַמְיַלְּדֹת, וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶן, מַדּוּעַ עֲשִׂיתֶן הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה; וַתְּחַיֶּיןָ, אֶת-הַיְלָדִים.
And the king of Egypt called the midwives and said to them: "Why have you done this thing, and let the boys live?"note
19
וַתֹּאמַרְןָ הַמְיַלְּדֹת אֶל-פַּרְעֹה, כִּי לֹא כַנָּשִׁים הַמִּצְרִיֹּת הָעִבְרִיֹּת: כִּי-חָיוֹת הֵנָּה, בְּטֶרֶם תָּבוֹא אֲלֵהֶן הַמְיַלֶּדֶת וְיָלָדוּ.
And the midwives said to Pharaoh: "Because the Hebrew women are not like the Egyptian; for they are vigorous — before the midwife comes to them, they have given birth."note
20
וַיֵּיטֶב אֱלֹהִים, לַמְיַלְּדֹת; וַיִּרֶב הָעָם וַיַּעַצְמוּ, מְאֹד.
And God dealt well with the midwives; and the people multiplied and grew very mighty.note
21
וַיְהִי, כִּי-יָרְאוּ הַמְיַלְּדֹת אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים; וַיַּעַשׂ לָהֶם, בָּתִּים.
And so it was, because the midwives feared God, that he made them households.note
22
וַיְצַו פַּרְעֹה, לְכָל-עַמּוֹ לֵאמֹר: כָּל-הַבֵּן הַיִּלּוֹד, הַיְאֹרָה תַּשְׁלִיכֻהוּ, וְכָל-הַבַּת, תְּחַיּוּן. {פ}
And Pharaoh commanded all his people, saying: "Every son that is born, into the Nile you shall throw him; but every daughter, let live."note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–7 · פָּרוּ וַיִּשְׁרְצוּ וַיִּרְבּוּ paru vayyishretsu vayyirbu

A second book that begins with "and." Exodus opens mid-sentence — ve-elleh, "and these are the names" — because it is not a new story but the next scroll of one story; the Hebrew title is its first real word, Shemot, "Names." It reaches back across the seam to Genesis: the twelve sons, "seventy souls" (nefesh, the whole living person — the count of Jacob's house that went down to Egypt), Joseph already there, and then the hinge that turns a family into a nation: "Joseph died, and all his brothers, and all that generation." One verse buries the whole book of Genesis.

The creation blessing, fulfilled in a foreign land. Then verse 7 stacks up five verbs of increase, and they are not random — they are the language of Eden: the sons of Israel "were fruitful, and swarmed, and multiplied, and grew mighty — exceedingly, exceedingly; and the land was filled with them." "Be fruitful and multiply and fill" was the blessing on all life (Genesis 1:28), and sharats, "to swarm, teem," is the creation-word for the waters teeming with life (Genesis 1:20) — KJV here flattens it to "increased abundantly," but this translation keeps the swarm, because the point is startling: God's first blessing keeps working, at full volume, on a slave people in someone else's country. The blessing is what triggers the terror of the next verse.

Verses 8–14 · אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יָדַע אֶת-יוֹסֵף asher lo-yada et-Yosef

"A new king who did not know Joseph." The most ominous sentence in the book, and the whole pivot of the story: a Pharaoh to whom Israel is not a rescued blessing (Joseph had once saved Egypt) but a demographic threat. On the reading this translation follows — the early date (1 Kings 6:1 sets the Exodus at 1446 BC), argued in our own age most carefully by Joel Kramer of Expedition Bible — this oppressing pharaoh is Thutmose III, Egypt's great empire-builder, whose relentless campaigns into Canaan created exactly the demand for the store-cities the Israelites are set to build (the pharaoh of the Exodus itself, a generation on, would then be his son Amenhotep II; see the encyclopedia entry, with Kramer's film). His scheme is a dark pun on the wisdom-vocabulary: "come, let us outwit them" — nitchakkmah, "let us be wise against them," from chokhmah, wisdom itself, bent to cunning — and it backfires with the chapter's grim refrain: "the more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied and spread" (v12). The blessing outruns the whip.

The store-cities, and an honest anachronism. They build Pithom and Raamses "with crushing harshness" — perekh (KJV "with rigour"; NWT "under tyranny"), a word of pitiless, breaking labor — "in mortar and bricks," the two words that will become the Passover's own bitter memory. About that name Raamses: it is the great argument of those who date the Exodus late (to Ramesses II, 13th century), since Pi-Ramesses was his city. But it is very likely an updated place-name — the region's later, familiar designation stamped onto an older text — exactly the kind of editorial modernizing this site has already met at "Dan" (14:14, a name the town didn't carry until Judges) and "Ur of the Chaldeans." Named for the reader, not the writer; the early date takes it so.

Verses 15–22 · וַתִּירֶאןָ הַמְיַלְּדֹת אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים vattirena hameyalledot et-ha'Elohim

The first civil disobedience in the Bible — and it is two women. Pharaoh, whose name the story never gives, is answered by two women whose names it carefully keeps: Shiphrah ("beauty") and Puah ("splendor," or "one who cries out"). Told to kill the boys "on the two stones" (ovnayim — the birthing-stones a woman crouched on; KJV "upon the stools"), "the midwives feared God" (yare Elohim) — and refused. That phrase is the spine of scripture's ethics, and this site has watched it before: it is what the angel found in Abraham at the altar (22:12), and what Proverbs calls "the beginning of knowledge" (1:7). Here it means a plain thing: when the king and God command opposite, you disobey the king. Their answer to Pharaoh is either a nimble half-truth or dry fact — the Hebrew women are chayot, "vigorous" (the word is close to "animals," lively and quick; KJV "lively"), delivered before the midwife arrives — and the narrator does not pause to scold them for it; "God dealt well with the midwives."

"He made them households." Their reward is a Hebrew idiom worth keeping: God "made them houses" (battim) — that is, households, families, lineages of their own (KJV "he made them houses"; NWT "he gave them families"): the women who protected Israel's children are given children — houses — of their own, the same "house" the promise keeps building. And then Pharaoh, foiled by two midwives, drops all pretense and turns the whole nation into his executioners: "every son that is born, throw into the Nile" (ye'or, Egypt's own word for its river; KJV "the river"). It is a death sentence — and, unwittingly, a stage direction: the river the babies are thrown into is the river a certain basket will float on, and the water Egypt makes a grave will become, at the sea, Egypt's own.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words distinct / exact: the five increase-verbs of v7 each rendered ("fruitful… swarmed… multiplied… grew mighty… filled"), keeping sharats's creation-swarm audible (KJV merges to "increased abundantly"); "crushing harshness" for perekh (KJV "rigour," with the NWT's "tyranny" in view); "seventy souls" for nefesh (the whole living person, with the NWT); "the Nile" for ye'or, Egypt's own river-word (KJV "the river").

Where it goes its own way: "let us outwit them" for nitchakkmah (KJV "let us deal wisely"), to show the wisdom-root turned to cunning; "chiefs of forced labor" for sarei missim (KJV "taskmasters"); "he made them households" for battim (KJV "houses" — the idiom explained); and "Raamses" read as an updated place-name (the early date), flagged like Dan and Ur.

Echoes paid and planted. Paid: the creation blessing — "be fruitful and multiply and fill" (1:28) — fulfilled on a slave people; the affliction foretold, for God had told Abraham "your seed will be sojourners… and they will afflict them" at the covenant of the pieces (15:13); and the fear of God, found in Abraham (22:12) and made the beginning of wisdom (Proverbs 1:7), now the midwives' quiet courage. Planted: the Nile, waiting for a basket (ch. 2) and, at the end, for the sea; Moses, not yet born; and the pharaoh of the Exodus, Amenhotep II, on the reading this translation follows.

Next installment in this book: Exodus 2 — a basket in the reeds, a princess, and a man who kills, and flees, and waits forty years at a well in Midian.

Exodus 2 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיֵּלֶךְ אִישׁ, מִבֵּית לֵוִי; וַיִּקַּח, אֶת-בַּת-לֵוִי.
And a man of the house of Levi went and took a daughter of Levi.note
2
וַתַּהַר הָאִשָּׁה, וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן; וַתֵּרֶא אֹתוֹ כִּי-טוֹב הוּא, וַתִּצְפְּנֵהוּ שְׁלֹשָׁה יְרָחִים.
And the woman conceived and bore a son; and she saw him, that he was good, and she hid him three months.note
3
וְלֹא-יָכְלָה עוֹד, הַצְּפִינוֹ, וַתִּקַּח-לוֹ תֵּבַת גֹּמֶא, וַתַּחְמְרָה בַחֵמָר וּבַזָּפֶת; וַתָּשֶׂם בָּהּ אֶת-הַיֶּלֶד, וַתָּשֶׂם בַּסּוּף עַל-שְׂפַת הַיְאֹר.
And when she could no longer hide him, she took for him an ark of papyrus, and coated it with bitumen and with pitch; and she put the child in it, and set it among the reeds by the bank of the Nile.note
4
וַתֵּתַצַּב אֲחֹתוֹ, מֵרָחֹק, לְדֵעָה, מַה-יֵּעָשֶׂה לוֹ.
And his sister stationed herself at a distance, to know what would be done to him.note
5
וַתֵּרֶד בַּת-פַּרְעֹה לִרְחֹץ עַל-הַיְאֹר, וְנַעֲרֹתֶיהָ הֹלְכֹת עַל-יַד הַיְאֹר; וַתֵּרֶא אֶת-הַתֵּבָה בְּתוֹךְ הַסּוּף, וַתִּשְׁלַח אֶת-אֲמָתָהּ וַתִּקָּחֶהָ.
And the daughter of Pharaoh came down to bathe at the Nile, and her maidens were walking along the bank of the Nile; and she saw the ark among the reeds, and sent her slave-girl, and took it.note
6
וַתִּפְתַּח וַתִּרְאֵהוּ אֶת-הַיֶּלֶד, וְהִנֵּה-נַעַר בֹּכֶה; וַתַּחְמֹל עָלָיו--וַתֹּאמֶר, מִיַּלְדֵי הָעִבְרִים זֶה.
And she opened it and saw the child — and look, a boy weeping; and she had compassion on him, and said: "This is one of the Hebrews' children."note
7
וַתֹּאמֶר אֲחֹתוֹ, אֶל-בַּת-פַּרְעֹה, הַאֵלֵךְ וְקָרָאתִי לָךְ אִשָּׁה מֵינֶקֶת, מִן הָעִבְרִיֹּת; וְתֵינִק לָךְ, אֶת-הַיָּלֶד.
And his sister said to Pharaoh's daughter: "Shall I go and call for you a nursing woman from the Hebrews, that she may nurse the child for you?"note
8
וַתֹּאמֶר-לָהּ בַּת-פַּרְעֹה, לֵכִי; וַתֵּלֶךְ, הָעַלְמָה, וַתִּקְרָא, אֶת-אֵם הַיָּלֶד.
And Pharaoh's daughter said to her: "Go." And the young woman went and called the child's mother.note
9
וַתֹּאמֶר לָהּ בַּת-פַּרְעֹה, הֵילִיכִי אֶת-הַיֶּלֶד הַזֶּה וְהֵינִקִהוּ לִי, וַאֲנִי, אֶתֵּן אֶת-שְׂכָרֵךְ; וַתִּקַּח הָאִשָּׁה הַיֶּלֶד, וַתְּנִיקֵהוּ.
And Pharaoh's daughter said to her: "Take this child and nurse him for me, and I myself will give your wages." And the woman took the child and nursed him.note
10
וַיִּגְדַּל הַיֶּלֶד, וַתְּבִאֵהוּ לְבַת-פַּרְעֹה, וַיְהִי-לָהּ, לְבֵן; וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ, מֹשֶׁה, וַתֹּאמֶר, כִּי מִן-הַמַּיִם מְשִׁיתִהוּ.
And the child grew, and she brought him to Pharaoh's daughter, and he became her son; and she called his name Moses, and said: "Because I drew him out of the water."note
11
וַיְהִי בַּיָּמִים הָהֵם, וַיִּגְדַּל מֹשֶׁה וַיֵּצֵא אֶל-אֶחָיו, וַיַּרְא, בְּסִבְלֹתָם; וַיַּרְא אִישׁ מִצְרִי, מַכֶּה אִישׁ-עִבְרִי מֵאֶחָיו.
And in those days, when Moses had grown, he went out to his brothers, and looked on their burdens; and he saw an Egyptian man striking a Hebrew man, one of his brothers.note
12
וַיִּפֶן כֹּה וָכֹה, וַיַּרְא כִּי אֵין אִישׁ; וַיַּךְ, אֶת-הַמִּצְרִי, וַיִּטְמְנֵהוּ, בַּחוֹל.
And he turned this way and that, and saw that there was no man; and he struck down the Egyptian, and hid him in the sand.note
13
וַיֵּצֵא בַּיּוֹם הַשֵּׁנִי, וְהִנֵּה שְׁנֵי-אֲנָשִׁים עִבְרִים נִצִּים; וַיֹּאמֶר, לָרָשָׁע, לָמָּה תַכֶּה, רֵעֶךָ.
And he went out the second day, and look — two Hebrew men struggling; and he said to the one in the wrong: "Why do you strike your fellow?"note
14
וַיֹּאמֶר מִי שָׂמְךָ לְאִישׁ שַׂר וְשֹׁפֵט, עָלֵינוּ--הַלְהָרְגֵנִי אַתָּה אֹמֵר, כַּאֲשֶׁר הָרַגְתָּ אֶת-הַמִּצְרִי; וַיִּירָא מֹשֶׁה וַיֹּאמַר, אָכֵן נוֹדַע הַדָּבָר.
And he said: "Who set you as a man, a prince and a judge over us? Do you mean to kill me as you killed the Egyptian?" And Moses was afraid, and said: "Surely the thing is known."note
15
וַיִּשְׁמַע פַּרְעֹה אֶת-הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה, וַיְבַקֵּשׁ לַהֲרֹג אֶת-מֹשֶׁה; וַיִּבְרַח מֹשֶׁה מִפְּנֵי פַרְעֹה, וַיֵּשֶׁב בְּאֶרֶץ-מִדְיָן וַיֵּשֶׁב עַל-הַבְּאֵר.
And Pharaoh heard this thing, and sought to kill Moses; but Moses fled from before Pharaoh, and settled in the land of Midian, and sat down by the well.note
16
וּלְכֹהֵן מִדְיָן, שֶׁבַע בָּנוֹת; וַתָּבֹאנָה וַתִּדְלֶנָה, וַתְּמַלֶּאנָה אֶת-הָרְהָטִים, לְהַשְׁקוֹת, צֹאן אֲבִיהֶן.
Now the priest of Midian had seven daughters; and they came and drew water, and filled the troughs to water their father's flock.note
17
וַיָּבֹאוּ הָרֹעִים, וַיְגָרְשׁוּם; וַיָּקָם מֹשֶׁה וַיּוֹשִׁעָן, וַיַּשְׁקְ אֶת-צֹאנָם.
And the shepherds came and drove them off; but Moses stood up and saved them, and watered their flock.note
18
וַתָּבֹאנָה, אֶל-רְעוּאֵל אֲבִיהֶן; וַיֹּאמֶר, מַדּוּעַ מִהַרְתֶּן בֹּא הַיּוֹם.
And they came to Reuel their father, and he said: "Why have you come so quickly today?"note
19
וַתֹּאמַרְןָ--אִישׁ מִצְרִי, הִצִּילָנוּ מִיַּד הָרֹעִים; וְגַם-דָּלֹה דָלָה לָנוּ, וַיַּשְׁקְ אֶת-הַצֹּאן.
And they said: "An Egyptian man delivered us from the hand of the shepherds, and even drew water, drew it for us, and watered the flock."note
20
וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל-בְּנֹתָיו, וְאַיּוֹ; לָמָּה זֶּה עֲזַבְתֶּן אֶת-הָאִישׁ, קִרְאֶן לוֹ וְיֹאכַל לָחֶם.
And he said to his daughters: "And where is he? Why did you leave the man? Call him, that he may eat bread."note
21
וַיּוֹאֶל מֹשֶׁה, לָשֶׁבֶת אֶת-הָאִישׁ; וַיִּתֵּן אֶת-צִפֹּרָה בִתּוֹ, לְמֹשֶׁה.
And Moses was willing to dwell with the man; and he gave Zipporah his daughter to Moses.note
22
וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן, וַיִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמוֹ גֵּרְשֹׁם: כִּי אָמַר--גֵּר הָיִיתִי, בְּאֶרֶץ נָכְרִיָּה. {פ}
And she bore a son, and he called his name Gershom, for he said: "I have been a resident alien in a foreign land."note
23
וַיְהִי בַיָּמִים הָרַבִּים הָהֵם, וַיָּמָת מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם, וַיֵּאָנְחוּ בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל מִן-הָעֲבֹדָה, וַיִּזְעָקוּ; וַתַּעַל שַׁוְעָתָם אֶל-הָאֱלֹהִים, מִן-הָעֲבֹדָה.
And in the course of those many days, the king of Egypt died; and the sons of Israel groaned from the bondage, and cried out; and their cry for help went up to God from the bondage.note
24
וַיִּשְׁמַע אֱלֹהִים, אֶת-נַאֲקָתָם; וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים אֶת-בְּרִיתוֹ, אֶת-אַבְרָהָם אֶת-יִצְחָק וְאֶת-יַעֲקֹב.
And God heard their groaning, and God remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob.note
25
וַיַּרְא אֱלֹהִים, אֶת-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל; וַיֵּדַע, אֱלֹהִים. {ס}
And God saw the sons of Israel; and God knew.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–10 · תֵּבַת גֹּמֶא … מֹשֶׁה tevat gome … Moshe

Saved through the water, in an ark — twice in the Bible, and this is the second. The basket the mother waterproofs and floats her baby in is a tevah — and that word occurs in only two stories in all of scripture: Noah's ark (Genesis 6–9) and this. This translation keeps it "ark" in both, with the NWT and against the KJV, which reads "ark" for Noah but "ark of bulrushes" here and so half-hides the link. The echo is the whole point: humanity is carried through the flood in a tevah of gopher wood sealed with pitch (6:14); Israel's deliverer is carried through the death-waters of the Nile in a tevah of papyrus sealed with pitch — a tiny ark on the very river Pharaoh made a grave. And she sets it "among the reeds" — suph — the same word that will name the sea Israel walks through dry: the Yam Suph, the Sea of Reeds. The baby in the reeds, the nation at the reed-sea: the book plants its ending in its opening. One more quiet seam: the mother "saw him, that he was good" (ki tov) — the exact phrase God speaks over the light on the first day (1:4).

The name, and the Egyptian under it. Pharaoh's daughter names him Moshe and gives a Hebrew reason: "because I drew him out (meshitihu)" — from mashah, "to draw out." But the wordplay sits on top of something the story does not hide: the name is Egyptian. Mose / mes means "born of, son" — it is the very element in Thut-mose ("born of Thoth"), Ah-mose, and Ra-messes ("born of Ra") — so an Egyptian princess names her adopted son with a perfectly Egyptian name (likely with a god's name once attached and dropped). Even the Hebrew pun points at the seam: Mosheh is an active form, "the one who draws out," not the passive "drawn out" (mashuy) the story's reason would require — the mark of a Hebrew wordplay laid over a genuinely Egyptian name. Far from an embarrassment, that Egyptian name is a fingerprint of authenticity: a later writer inventing a national hero would not hand him a foreign name betraying an Egyptian upbringing. The encyclopedia's Moses entry gathers this, and, following Joel Kramer of Expedition Bible, sets the prince in the 18th-dynasty court of the early date. Buried in the middle of it: the princess pays the baby's own mother to nurse him — Israel's rescue funded from Pharaoh's household, one of scripture's driest ironies.

Verses 11–15 · מִי שָׂמְךָ לְאִישׁ שַׂר וְשֹׁפֵט mi samkha le'ish sar veshofet

The deliverer's false start. Moses, grown and Egyptian-raised, "goes out to his brothers" and chooses them — Hebrews 11:24-25 will read this as the great renunciation, refusing "to be called the son of Pharaoh's daughter." But his first act of deliverance is a murder: he kills the Egyptian and hides him "in the sand," and looks "this way and that" first — a man who wants no witness, not yet a man who acts before God. The rebuke that unmasks him is sharp: "Who set you as a prince and a judge over us?" — and the terrible joke is that God will, forty years on; the question is right and wrong at once. Stephen quotes it in his last sermon (Acts 7:27-35): the man Israel rejected as ruler is the very man God sends as ruler and redeemer. "The thing is known" — and Moses, the prince, becomes a fugitive with a price on his head, the second time this book sends its savior fleeing Pharaoh (the first was the babies in the river).

Verses 16–22 · גֵּר הָיִיתִי בְּאֶרֶץ נָכְרִיָּה ger hayiti be'erets nokhriyyah

A well, seven daughters, and the third betrothal at a well. Moses flees to Midian and "sits down by the well" — and any Israelite reader knows what happens next, because it has happened twice already: a stranger meets his future wife at a well (Abraham's servant finds Rebekah, Genesis 24; Jacob finds Rachel, Genesis 29). Here the future deliverer waters a flock and is given Zipporah, daughter of the priest of Midian — called Reuel here ("friend of God") and Jethro elsewhere (a name, and a title, "his excellency" — the shelf and the tradition have long noticed the two names). The daughters report that "an Egyptian delivered us" — Moses now passes as an Egyptian to Midianites, a Hebrew to Egyptians, at home nowhere. And he says so in his son's name: Gershom — "for I have been a resident alien (ger) in a foreign land." That is Abraham's own word for himself, buying a grave in the land of promise (23:4) — the patriarch's statelessness handed down to the man who will end it. Note, too, the verb the narrator uses for Moses at the well: he "saved them" (yasha) — the deliverer already delivering, on a small scale, long before the sea.

Verses 23–25 · וַיֵּדַע אֱלֹהִים vayyeda Elohim

Four verbs, and the whole book turns. The chapter ends not with Moses but with God, and with four verbs that are the hinge of the entire Exodus. The king of Egypt dies — on the early date this translation follows, this is Thutmose III, freeing Moses to return (the very words God will use in 4:19, "all the men who sought your life are dead"). The people cry out (their groan from the bondage) — and then, in a rising drumbeat: "God heard… God remembered his covenant… God saw… and God knew." The remembering is not that God had forgotten; zakhar here means to act on a covenant now come due — the covenant cut with Abraham between the pieces, which had foretold this very slavery and this very deliverance (17:7). And the last verb is left almost bare — "and God knew" (vayyeda Elohim); KJV fills it out to "God took cognizance," but the Hebrew simply stops there, the sentence's object swallowed: God knew. The forty silent years of Midian are over; the God who heard is about to speak, at a bush that burns.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words distinct / exact: "an ark of papyrus" for tevah (with the NWT, keeping it the same word as Noah's ark — KJV's "ark of bulrushes" hides the link); "among the reeds" for suph, the Yam-Suph word; "a resident alien" for ger (as at Genesis 23, kept consistent); "God knew" for the bare vayyeda Elohim (KJV "took cognizance," which fills the silence the Hebrew leaves).

Where it goes its own way: "Moses" with its Egyptian sense flagged (mose, "born of," under the Hebrew pun on mashah); "he was good" for ki tov, to let the creation-phrase be heard; "the one in the wrong" for rasha (KJV "him that did the wrong"); Reuel and Jethro both kept as the text and tradition give them.

Echoes paid and planted. Paid: the tevah / ark — saved through the water, as Noah was (6:14); "good," the creation-word (1:4); Gershom, the ger of Abraham (23:4); and the covenant remembered — the one cut with Abraham (17:7), now come due. Planted: the reeds (suph), waiting for the sea; Midian and the priest's flock, waiting for a bush on the mountain (ch. 3); Moses the fugitive "prince and judge," whom God will make exactly that; and "God knew," waiting for a Name.

Next installment in this book: Exodus 3 — the bush that burns and is not consumed, holy ground, and the Name itself: "I am that I am."

Jeremiah 22 — The Mister Translation

1
כֹּה אָמַר יְהוָה, רֵד בֵּית-מֶלֶךְ יְהוּדָה; וְדִבַּרְתָּ שָׁם, אֶת-הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה.
Thus said Jehovah: Go down to the house of the king of Judah, and speak there this word,note
2
וְאָמַרְתָּ, שְׁמַע דְּבַר-יְהוָה, מֶלֶךְ יְהוּדָה, הַיֹּשֵׁב עַל-כִּסֵּא דָוִד: אַתָּה וַעֲבָדֶיךָ וְעַמְּךָ, הַבָּאִים בַּשְּׁעָרִים הָאֵלֶּה.
and say: Hear the word of Jehovah, O king of Judah who sits on the throne of David — you, and your servants, and your people who enter by these gates.note
3
כֹּה אָמַר יְהוָה, עֲשׂוּ מִשְׁפָּט וּצְדָקָה, וְהַצִּילוּ גָזוּל, מִיַּד עָשׁוֹק; וְגֵר יָתוֹם וְאַלְמָנָה אַל-תֹּנוּ, אַל-תַּחְמֹסוּ, וְדָם נָקִי, אַל-תִּשְׁפְּכוּ בַּמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה.
Thus said Jehovah: Do justice and righteousness, and rescue the robbed from the hand of the oppressor; and do no wrong, do no violence, to the resident alien, the fatherless, and the widow, and shed no innocent blood in this place.note
4
כִּי אִם-עָשׂוֹ תַּעֲשׂוּ, אֶת-הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה--וּבָאוּ בְשַׁעֲרֵי הַבַּיִת הַזֶּה מְלָכִים יֹשְׁבִים לְדָוִד עַל-כִּסְאוֹ, רֹכְבִים בָּרֶכֶב וּבַסּוּסִים, הוּא וַעֲבָדָו, וְעַמּוֹ.
For if you truly do this thing, then through the gates of this house there will enter kings sitting for David on his throne, riding in chariots and on horses — he, and his servants, and his people.note
5
וְאִם לֹא תִשְׁמְעוּ, אֶת-הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה--בִּי נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי נְאֻם-יְהוָה, כִּי-לְחָרְבָּה יִהְיֶה הַבַּיִת הַזֶּה. {פ}
But if you do not obey these words — by myself I have sworn, declares Jehovah — this house will become a ruin.note
6
כִּי-כֹה אָמַר יְהוָה, עַל-בֵּית מֶלֶךְ יְהוּדָה, גִּלְעָד אַתָּה לִי, רֹאשׁ הַלְּבָנוֹן; אִם-לֹא אֲשִׁיתְךָ מִדְבָּר, עָרִים לֹא נושבה נוֹשָׁבוּ.
For thus said Jehovah concerning the house of the king of Judah: Gilead you are to me, the head of Lebanon — yet surely I will make you a wilderness, cities uninhabited.note
7
וְקִדַּשְׁתִּי עָלֶיךָ מַשְׁחִתִים, אִישׁ וְכֵלָיו; וְכָרְתוּ מִבְחַר אֲרָזֶיךָ, וְהִפִּילוּ עַל-הָאֵשׁ.
I will consecrate destroyers against you, each with his weapons; and they will cut down your choicest cedars and cast them on the fire.note
8
וְעָבְרוּ גּוֹיִם רַבִּים, עַל הָעִיר הַזֹּאת; וְאָמְרוּ, אִישׁ אֶל-רֵעֵהוּ, עַל-מֶה עָשָׂה יְהוָה כָּכָה, לָעִיר הַגְּדוֹלָה הַזֹּאת.
And many nations will pass by this city, and each will say to his neighbor: "Why has Jehovah done this to this great city?"note
9
וְאָמְרוּ--עַל אֲשֶׁר עָזְבוּ, אֶת-בְּרִית יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיהֶם; וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ לֵאלֹהִים אֲחֵרִים, וַיַּעַבְדוּם. {ס}
And they will answer: "Because they forsook the covenant of Jehovah their God, and bowed down to other gods and served them."note
10
אַל-תִּבְכּוּ לְמֵת, וְאַל-תָּנֻדוּ לוֹ; בְּכוּ בָכוֹ, לַהֹלֵךְ--כִּי לֹא יָשׁוּב עוֹד, וְרָאָה אֶת-אֶרֶץ מוֹלַדְתּוֹ.
Weep not for the dead, and do not mourn him; weep bitterly for the one who goes away, for he will return no more, nor see the land of his birth.note
11
כִּי כֹה אָמַר-יְהוָה אֶל-שַׁלֻּם בֶּן-יֹאשִׁיָּהוּ מֶלֶךְ יְהוּדָה, הַמֹּלֵךְ תַּחַת יֹאשִׁיָּהוּ אָבִיו, אֲשֶׁר יָצָא, מִן-הַמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה: לֹא-יָשׁוּב שָׁם, עוֹד.
For thus said Jehovah concerning Shallum son of Josiah, king of Judah, who reigned in place of Josiah his father, and who went out from this place: He will not return here again;note
12
כִּי, בִּמְקוֹם אֲשֶׁר-הִגְלוּ אֹתוֹ--שָׁם יָמוּת; וְאֶת-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת, לֹא-יִרְאֶה עוֹד. {ס}
for in the place where they exiled him, there he will die, and this land he will not see again.note
13
הוֹי בֹּנֶה בֵיתוֹ בְּלֹא-צֶדֶק, וַעֲלִיּוֹתָיו בְּלֹא מִשְׁפָּט; בְּרֵעֵהוּ יַעֲבֹד חִנָּם, וּפֹעֲלוֹ לֹא יִתֶּן-לוֹ.
Woe to him who builds his house without righteousness, and his upper chambers without justice; who makes his neighbor work for nothing and does not give him his wages —note
14
הָאֹמֵר, אֶבְנֶה-לִּי בֵּית מִדּוֹת, וַעֲלִיּוֹת, מְרֻוָּחִים; וְקָרַע לוֹ, חַלּוֹנָי, וְסָפוּן בָּאָרֶז, וּמָשׁוֹחַ בַּשָּׁשַׁר.
who says, "I will build myself a spacious house, with roomy upper chambers," and cuts out windows for himself, and panels it in cedar, and paints it in vermilion.note
15
הֲתִמְלֹךְ, כִּי אַתָּה מְתַחֲרֶה בָאָרֶז; אָבִיךָ הֲלוֹא אָכַל וְשָׁתָה, וְעָשָׂה מִשְׁפָּט וּצְדָקָה--אָז, טוֹב לוֹ.
Do you reign because you vie in cedar? Your father — did he not eat and drink, and do justice and righteousness? Then it went well with him.note
16
דָּן דִּין-עָנִי וְאֶבְיוֹן, אָז טוֹב; הֲלוֹא-הִיא הַדַּעַת אֹתִי, נְאֻם-יְהוָה.
He judged the cause of the poor and needy; then it was well. Is that not what it means to know me? declares Jehovah.note
17
כִּי אֵין עֵינֶיךָ וְלִבְּךָ, כִּי אִם-עַל-בִּצְעֶךָ; וְעַל דַּם-הַנָּקִי לִשְׁפּוֹךְ, וְעַל-הָעֹשֶׁק וְעַל-הַמְּרוּצָה לַעֲשׂוֹת. {ס}
But your eyes and your heart are on nothing but your dishonest gain, and on shedding innocent blood, and on oppression and extortion — that is all you do.note
18
לָכֵן כֹּה-אָמַר יְהוָה, אֶל-יְהוֹיָקִים בֶּן-יֹאשִׁיָּהוּ מֶלֶךְ יְהוּדָה, לֹא-יִסְפְּדוּ לוֹ, הוֹי אָחִי וְהוֹי אָחוֹת; לֹא-יִסְפְּדוּ לוֹ, הוֹי אָדוֹן וְהוֹי הֹדֹה.
Therefore thus said Jehovah concerning Jehoiakim son of Josiah, king of Judah: They will not lament him — "Alas, my brother!" or "Alas, sister!" They will not lament him — "Alas, lord!" or "Alas, his majesty!"note
19
קְבוּרַת חֲמוֹר, יִקָּבֵר; סָחוֹב וְהַשְׁלֵךְ, מֵהָלְאָה לְשַׁעֲרֵי יְרוּשָׁלִָם. {ס}
With the burial of a donkey he will be buried — dragged off and flung out beyond the gates of Jerusalem.note
20
עֲלִי הַלְּבָנוֹן וּצְעָקִי, וּבַבָּשָׁן תְּנִי קוֹלֵךְ; וְצַעֲקִי, מֵעֲבָרִים, כִּי נִשְׁבְּרוּ, כָּל-מְאַהֲבָיִךְ.
Go up to Lebanon and cry out; in Bashan lift your voice; cry out from Abarim, for all your lovers are broken.note
21
דִּבַּרְתִּי אֵלַיִךְ בְּשַׁלְוֺתַיִךְ, אָמַרְתְּ לֹא אֶשְׁמָע; זֶה דַרְכֵּךְ מִנְּעוּרַיִךְ, כִּי לֹא-שָׁמַעַתְּ בְּקוֹלִי.
I spoke to you in your times of ease, but you said, "I will not listen." This has been your way from your youth — you have not obeyed my voice.note
22
כָּל-רֹעַיִךְ תִּרְעֶה-רוּחַ, וּמְאַהֲבַיִךְ בַּשְּׁבִי יֵלֵכוּ: כִּי אָז תֵּבֹשִׁי וְנִכְלַמְתְּ, מִכֹּל רָעָתֵךְ.
The wind will shepherd away all your shepherds, and your lovers will go into captivity; then you will be ashamed and humiliated for all your evil.note
23
ישבתי יֹשַׁבְתְּ בַּלְּבָנוֹן, מקננתי מְקֻנַּנְתְּ בָּאֲרָזִים: מַה-נֵּחַנְתְּ בְּבֹא-לָךְ חֲבָלִים, חִיל כַּיֹּלֵדָה.
O you who dwell in Lebanon, nested in the cedars — how you will groan when pangs come upon you, the writhing of a woman in labor!note
24
חַי-אָנִי, נְאֻם-יְהוָה, כִּי אִם-יִהְיֶה כָּנְיָהוּ בֶן-יְהוֹיָקִים מֶלֶךְ יְהוּדָה, חוֹתָם עַל-יַד יְמִינִי: כִּי מִשָּׁם, אֶתְּקֶנְךָּ.
As I live, declares Jehovah, though Coniah son of Jehoiakim king of Judah were a signet on my right hand, yet from there I would tear you off.note
25
וּנְתַתִּיךָ, בְּיַד מְבַקְשֵׁי נַפְשֶׁךָ, וּבְיַד אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה יָגוֹר, מִפְּנֵיהֶם--וּבְיַד נְבוּכַדְרֶאצַּר מֶלֶךְ-בָּבֶל, וּבְיַד הַכַּשְׂדִּים.
And I will give you into the hand of those who seek your life, into the hand of those you dread, into the hand of Nebuchadrezzar king of Babylon, and into the hand of the Chaldeans.note
26
וְהֵטַלְתִּי אֹתְךָ, וְאֶת-אִמְּךָ אֲשֶׁר יְלָדַתְךָ, עַל הָאָרֶץ אַחֶרֶת, אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יֻלַּדְתֶּם שָׁם; וְשָׁם, תָּמוּתוּ.
And I will hurl you, and your mother who bore you, into another land where you were not born — and there you both will die.note
27
וְעַל-הָאָרֶץ, אֲשֶׁר-הֵם מְנַשְּׂאִים אֶת-נַפְשָׁם--לָשׁוּב שָׁם: שָׁמָּה, לֹא יָשׁוּבוּ. {פ}
But to the land they long with all their soul to return to, there they will not return.note
28
הַעֶצֶב נִבְזֶה נָפוּץ, הָאִישׁ הַזֶּה כָּנְיָהוּ, אִם-כְּלִי, אֵין חֵפֶץ בּוֹ; מַדּוּעַ הוּטְלוּ, הוּא וְזַרְעוֹ, וְהֻשְׁלְכוּ, עַל-הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יָדָעוּ.
Is this man Coniah a despised, shattered pot? A vessel no one wants? Why are they hurled out — he and his seed — cast into a land they do not know?note
29
אֶרֶץ אֶרֶץ, אָרֶץ--שִׁמְעִי, דְּבַר-יְהוָה.
Land! Land! Land! Hear the word of Jehovah!note
30
כֹּה אָמַר יְהוָה, כִּתְבוּ אֶת-הָאִישׁ הַזֶּה עֲרִירִי--גֶּבֶר, לֹא-יִצְלַח בְּיָמָיו: כִּי לֹא יִצְלַח מִזַּרְעוֹ, אִישׁ יֹשֵׁב עַל-כִּסֵּא דָוִד, וּמֹשֵׁל עוֹד, בִּיהוּדָה. {פ}
Thus said Jehovah: Write this man down as childless, a man who will not prosper in his days; for none of his seed will prosper, sitting on the throne of David and ruling again in Judah.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–9 · בִּי נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי bi nishbati — "by myself I have sworn"

A charge to the whole royal house. Jeremiah is sent "down" from the temple to the palace to lay one condition before the throne of David: do justice and righteousness (mishpat u-tsedaqah — the same paired demand of 21:12, and the very thing the coming righteous Branch will do, 23:5), rescue the robbed, and protect the three who have no protector — the resident alien, the fatherless, and the widow (ger, yatom, almanah, the Torah's standing triad; ger is kept "resident alien" as at Genesis 23:4 and Exodus 2:22). Obey, and kings keep riding through these gates; refuse, and the house becomes "a ruin."

The oath that cuts both ways. God seals the threat with bi nishbati, "by myself I have sworn" — and that self-oath has one unforgettable precedent: it is word-for-word the oath that sealed the blessing to Abraham on Moriah, "by myself I have sworn, declares Jehovah" (Genesis 22:16). When God swears "by himself," there is no higher court to appeal to — and here the same unbreakable oath that once guaranteed the promise now guarantees the palace's fall if it will not do justice. The chapter's Gilead-and-Lebanon imagery follows: the royal house is God's own "head of Lebanon," its cedar halls (see v14) his cedar forest — which is exactly why "they will cut down your choicest cedars and cast them on the fire," the fire promised for the palace's "forest" back at 21:14. And the passers-by who ask "why has Jehovah done this?" are answered in the old covenant-lawsuit formula (Deuteronomy 29): "because they forsook the covenant."

Verses 10–12 · שַׁלֻּם Shallum — the first son to fall

Do not weep for the dead — weep for the exile. The "dead" is good king Josiah, killed at Megiddo (609 BC) and mourned across Judah; but Jeremiah says the tears belong elsewhere — to "the one who goes away," who "will return no more, nor see the land of his birth" (erets moladto — the very word, moledet, of Abram's call, "go from your land and your birthplace," Genesis 12:1: Abraham left his homeland by promise, this king loses his by force). The exile named is Shallum — the personal name of the king better known by his throne-name Jehoahaz, Josiah's son, who reigned three months before Pharaoh Necho hauled him to Egypt in chains (2 Kings 23:31-34). "In the place where they exiled him, there he will die" — and he did, in Egypt, the first of Josiah's sons to be carried off, never to see Judah again.
