Josiah's death was the mourned one; his son's living exile is the one to weep over.

Verses 13–19 · הֲלוֹא-הִיא הַדַּעַת אֹתִי ha-lo hi ha-da'at oti — "is that not to know me?"

Woe to the palace-builder. Now the woe (hoy) falls on Jehoiakim, Josiah's other son, whom Necho set on the throne after taking Jehoahaz. His crime is written in his architecture: a "spacious house" with cedar panelling and vermilion paint (shashar, a costly red ochre) — built with unpaid labor, "who makes his neighbor work for nothing and does not give him his wages." The taunt is withering: "Do you reign because you vie in cedar?" (the KJV's famously opaque "because thou closest thyself in cedar" is trying to render the same rare verb metachareh, "compete, contend to excel"). Kingship is not measured in lumber.

The verse at the chapter's heart. Against the son, Jeremiah holds up the father: Josiah "ate and drank" — lived, and lived well — and "did justice and righteousness; then it went well with him. He judged the cause of the poor and needy; then it was well. Is that not what it means to know me? declares Jehovah" (v16). This is one of the most important sentences in the prophets: to know God (ha-da'at oti — the noun da'at, "knowledge," that Proverbs 1:7 calls the thing the fear of Jehovah begins) is not a doctrine held in the head but justice done for the poor. Jehoiakim's eyes and heart, by contrast, are on nothing but dishonest gain (betsa), innocent blood, and extortion. So his end is the anti-funeral: no one will cry the customary laments over him — "Alas, my brother! … Alas, lord!" — the very cry hoy that opened the woe returns as the mourning-cry he will not receive. Instead, "the burial of a donkey" (KJV "ass") — that is, no burial at all: "dragged off and flung out beyond the gates." (Kings and Chronicles are quieter about Jehoiakim's actual death; Jeremiah's oracle stands as the sentence pronounced, and the Hebrew leaves the grim picture undimmed.)

Verses 20–30 · חוֹתָם … עֲרִירִי chotam … ariri — the signet, torn off; "childless"

Wail from the heights. The address turns to Jerusalem as a woman: climb Lebanon, Bashan, and Abarim — the northern and eastern heights — and cry, "for all your lovers are broken" (me'ahavayikh, the allied nations, Egypt chief among them, she trusted instead of God). "From your youth you have not obeyed my voice" — lo shama'at be-qoli, the exact inverse of the reason Abraham was blessed, "because you obeyed my voice" (Genesis 22:18): the royal house is the anti-Abraham. There is a bitter pun in v22 — "the wind (ruach) will shepherd (tir'eh) all your shepherds (ro'ayikh)": the leaders who were to pasture the flock are themselves pastured — swept off — by the wind. Verse 23 carries two ketiv/qere pairs (the archaic feminine ending -ti written, the standard -t read) — Mechon-Mamre prints both forms, and this translation keeps the received reading, "you who dwell … nested in the cedars."

The signet torn off. The last king in the roll is Coniah — a clipped, almost contemptuous form of Jehoiachin (also Jeconiah), Jehoiakim's eighteen-year-old son, three months on the throne before Nebuchadnezzar carried him to Babylon in 597. God says: even if Coniah "were a signet (chotam) on my right hand" — the king's own seal-ring, the emblem of delegated authority pressed into every royal decree — "yet from there I would tear you off." Then the threefold cry, unforgettable in the Hebrew: erets, erets, arets — "Land! Land! Land! Hear the word of Jehovah!" And the sentence: "Write this man down as childless" (ariri).

"Childless" — and the line that would not die. The word is a puzzle the verse itself unlocks, and one of the Bible's quietest hinges. Jehoiachin was not literally childless — he fathered seven sons in Babylon (1 Chronicles 3:17-18). The curse is defined in the next breath: "childless" as to the throne — "for none of his seed will prosper, sitting on the throne of David and ruling again in Judah." And so it held: no descendant of Jehoiachin ever again reigned over the old kingdom. Yet two things bend back against the curse. First, the signet is expressly restored to his own grandson: "I will make you like a signet, for I have chosen you," God tells Zerubbabel (Haggai 2:23) — the very image torn off Coniah pressed back onto his line. Second, when the Gospels trace the Messiah's descent, Matthew's royal, legal line runs straight through Jeconiah (Matthew 1:11-12), while Luke's runs by another of David's sons — the two genealogies long read together as the throne-right passing through the cursed king by adoption while the bloodline slips the curse. The king "written childless" as to David's old throne becomes an ancestor of the one who inherits a throne that outlasts it. The Hebrew does not say any of that; it says ariri, and lets the centuries answer.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "the resident alien, the fatherless, and the widow" for ger-yatom-almanah (with the NWT's "alien resident," kept consistent with Genesis 23 and Exodus 2, vs the KJV's vaguer "stranger"); "vie in cedar" for the rare metachareh (the KJV's "closest thyself in cedar" is guessing at a verb that means to compete); "the burial of a donkey" (KJV "ass"); and "Coniah" kept as the text's own clipped form (as KJV/ASV), with Jehoiachin/Jeconiah explained rather than silently swapped in.

Echoes paid. "By myself I have sworn" (v5) — the Aqedah's oath (22:16), the blessing's seal turned into judgment's; the land of your birth (moledet, v10) — Abram's forsaken homeland (12:1) lost the other way, by exile; "you have not obeyed my voice" (v21) — the exact reverse of Abraham's obedience (22:18); "is that not to know me?" (v16) — justice for the poor as the true da'at, the knowledge Proverbs 1:7 prizes; and the cedars felled and burned (v7) — the fire set to the palace "forest" of 21:14.

Next installment in this book: Jeremiah 23 — the shepherds who scatter the flock, and the promise against them: "I will raise up for David a righteous Branch."

Exodus 3 — The Mister Translation

1
וּמֹשֶׁה, הָיָה רֹעֶה אֶת-צֹאן יִתְרוֹ חֹתְנוֹ--כֹּהֵן מִדְיָן; וַיִּנְהַג אֶת-הַצֹּאן אַחַר הַמִּדְבָּר, וַיָּבֹא אֶל-הַר הָאֱלֹהִים חֹרֵבָה.
Now Moses was shepherding the flock of Jethro his father-in-law, the priest of Midian; and he led the flock to the far side of the wilderness, and came to the mountain of God, to Horeb.note
2
וַיֵּרָא מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה אֵלָיו, בְּלַבַּת-אֵשׁ--מִתּוֹךְ הַסְּנֶה; וַיַּרְא, וְהִנֵּה הַסְּנֶה בֹּעֵר בָּאֵשׁ, וְהַסְּנֶה, אֵינֶנּוּ אֻכָּל.
And the angel of Jehovah appeared to him in a flame of fire from within the bush; and he looked, and there was the bush burning with fire — yet the bush was not consumed.note
3
וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה--אָסֻרָה-נָּא וְאֶרְאֶה, אֶת-הַמַּרְאֶה הַגָּדֹל הַזֶּה: מַדּוּעַ, לֹא-יִבְעַר הַסְּנֶה.
And Moses said, "Let me turn aside now and see this great sight — why the bush does not burn up."note
4
וַיַּרְא יְהוָה, כִּי סָר לִרְאוֹת; וַיִּקְרָא אֵלָיו אֱלֹהִים מִתּוֹךְ הַסְּנֶה, וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה מֹשֶׁה--וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֵּנִי.
When Jehovah saw that he turned aside to look, God called to him from within the bush and said, "Moses, Moses!" And he said, "Here I am."note
5
וַיֹּאמֶר, אַל-תִּקְרַב הֲלֹם; שַׁל-נְעָלֶיךָ, מֵעַל רַגְלֶיךָ--כִּי הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר אַתָּה עוֹמֵד עָלָיו, אַדְמַת-קֹדֶשׁ הוּא.
And he said, "Do not come near here; take off your sandals from your feet, for the place on which you are standing is holy ground."note
6
וַיֹּאמֶר, אָנֹכִי אֱלֹהֵי אָבִיךָ, אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם אֱלֹהֵי יִצְחָק, וֵאלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב; וַיַּסְתֵּר מֹשֶׁה, פָּנָיו, כִּי יָרֵא, מֵהַבִּיט אֶל-הָאֱלֹהִים.
And he said, "I am the God of your father — the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob." And Moses hid his face, for he was afraid to look at God.note
7
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה, רָאֹה רָאִיתִי אֶת-עֳנִי עַמִּי אֲשֶׁר בְּמִצְרָיִם; וְאֶת-צַעֲקָתָם שָׁמַעְתִּי מִפְּנֵי נֹגְשָׂיו, כִּי יָדַעְתִּי אֶת-מַכְאֹבָיו.
And Jehovah said, "I have surely seen the affliction of my people who are in Egypt, and I have heard their outcry because of their taskmasters; indeed, I know their pains.note
8
וָאֵרֵד לְהַצִּילוֹ מִיַּד מִצְרַיִם, וּלְהַעֲלֹתוֹ מִן-הָאָרֶץ הַהִוא, אֶל-אֶרֶץ טוֹבָה וּרְחָבָה, אֶל-אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבָשׁ--אֶל-מְקוֹם הַכְּנַעֲנִי, וְהַחִתִּי, וְהָאֱמֹרִי וְהַפְּרִזִּי, וְהַחִוִּי וְהַיְבוּסִי.
So I have come down to rescue them from the hand of Egypt, and to bring them up out of that land to a good and spacious land, to a land flowing with milk and honey — to the place of the Canaanite and the Hittite and the Amorite and the Perizzite and the Hivite and the Jebusite.note
9
וְעַתָּה, הִנֵּה צַעֲקַת בְּנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל בָּאָה אֵלָי; וְגַם-רָאִיתִי, אֶת-הַלַּחַץ, אֲשֶׁר מִצְרַיִם, לֹחֲצִים אֹתָם.
And now, look — the outcry of the sons of Israel has come to me, and I have also seen the oppression with which Egypt oppresses them.note
10
וְעַתָּה לְכָה, וְאֶשְׁלָחֲךָ אֶל-פַּרְעֹה; וְהוֹצֵא אֶת-עַמִּי בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל, מִמִּצְרָיִם.
Come now, and I will send you to Pharaoh, that you may bring my people, the sons of Israel, out of Egypt."note
11
וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה, אֶל-הָאֱלֹהִים, מִי אָנֹכִי, כִּי אֵלֵךְ אֶל-פַּרְעֹה; וְכִי אוֹצִיא אֶת-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, מִמִּצְרָיִם.
But Moses said to God, "Who am I, that I should go to Pharaoh, and that I should bring the sons of Israel out of Egypt?"note
12
וַיֹּאמֶר, כִּי-אֶהְיֶה עִמָּךְ, וְזֶה-לְּךָ הָאוֹת, כִּי אָנֹכִי שְׁלַחְתִּיךָ: בְּהוֹצִיאֲךָ אֶת-הָעָם, מִמִּצְרַיִם, תַּעַבְדוּן אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים, עַל הָהָר הַזֶּה.
And he said, "Because I will be with you. And this is the sign for you that I myself have sent you: when you bring the people out of Egypt, you will serve God on this mountain."note
13
וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל-הָאֱלֹהִים, הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי בָא אֶל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, וְאָמַרְתִּי לָהֶם, אֱלֹהֵי אֲבוֹתֵיכֶם שְׁלָחַנִי אֲלֵיכֶם; וְאָמְרוּ-לִי מַה-שְּׁמוֹ, מָה אֹמַר אֲלֵהֶם.
And Moses said to God, "Look, when I come to the sons of Israel and say to them, 'The God of your fathers has sent me to you,' and they say to me, 'What is his name?' — what shall I say to them?"note
14
וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים אֶל-מֹשֶׁה, אֶהְיֶה אֲשֶׁר אֶהְיֶה; וַיֹּאמֶר, כֹּה תֹאמַר לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, אֶהְיֶה, שְׁלָחַנִי אֲלֵיכֶם.
And God said to Moses, "I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE." And he said, "Thus you shall say to the sons of Israel: 'I WILL BE' has sent me to you."note
15
וַיֹּאמֶר עוֹד אֱלֹהִים אֶל-מֹשֶׁה, כֹּה-תֹאמַר אֶל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲבֹתֵיכֶם אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם אֱלֹהֵי יִצְחָק וֵאלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב, שְׁלָחַנִי אֲלֵיכֶם; זֶה-שְּׁמִי לְעֹלָם, וְזֶה זִכְרִי לְדֹר דֹּר.
And God said further to Moses, "Thus you shall say to the sons of Israel: 'Jehovah, the God of your fathers — the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob — has sent me to you.' This is my name forever, and this is my memorial from generation to generation.note
16
לֵךְ וְאָסַפְתָּ אֶת-זִקְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, וְאָמַרְתָּ אֲלֵהֶם יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲבֹתֵיכֶם נִרְאָה אֵלַי, אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם יִצְחָק וְיַעֲקֹב, לֵאמֹר: פָּקֹד פָּקַדְתִּי אֶתְכֶם, וְאֶת-הֶעָשׂוּי לָכֶם בְּמִצְרָיִם.
Go and gather the elders of Israel, and say to them, 'Jehovah, the God of your fathers, has appeared to me — the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — saying: I have surely attended to you, and to what is being done to you in Egypt.note
17
וָאֹמַר, אַעֲלֶה אֶתְכֶם מֵעֳנִי מִצְרַיִם, אֶל-אֶרֶץ הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְהַחִתִּי, וְהָאֱמֹרִי וְהַפְּרִזִּי וְהַחִוִּי וְהַיְבוּסִי--אֶל-אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב, וּדְבָשׁ.
And I have said: I will bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt to the land of the Canaanite and the Hittite and the Amorite and the Perizzite and the Hivite and the Jebusite — to a land flowing with milk and honey.'note
18
וְשָׁמְעוּ, לְקֹלֶךָ; וּבָאתָ אַתָּה וְזִקְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל-מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם, וַאֲמַרְתֶּם אֵלָיו יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הָעִבְרִיִּים נִקְרָה עָלֵינוּ, וְעַתָּה נֵלְכָה-נָּא דֶּרֶךְ שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים בַּמִּדְבָּר, וְנִזְבְּחָה לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ.
And they will listen to your voice; and you and the elders of Israel will go to the king of Egypt and say to him, 'Jehovah, the God of the Hebrews, has met with us; now please let us go a three days' journey into the wilderness, that we may sacrifice to Jehovah our God.'note
19
וַאֲנִי יָדַעְתִּי--כִּי לֹא-יִתֵּן אֶתְכֶם מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם, לַהֲלֹךְ: וְלֹא, בְּיָד חֲזָקָה.
But I myself know that the king of Egypt will not let you go — no, not except by a mighty hand.note
20
וְשָׁלַחְתִּי אֶת-יָדִי, וְהִכֵּיתִי אֶת-מִצְרַיִם, בְּכֹל נִפְלְאֹתַי, אֲשֶׁר אֶעֱשֶׂה בְּקִרְבּוֹ; וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן, יְשַׁלַּח אֶתְכֶם.
So I will stretch out my hand and strike Egypt with all my wonders that I will do in its midst; and after that he will let you go.note
21
וְנָתַתִּי אֶת-חֵן הָעָם-הַזֶּה, בְּעֵינֵי מִצְרָיִם; וְהָיָה כִּי תֵלֵכוּן, לֹא תֵלְכוּ רֵיקָם.
And I will give this people favor in the eyes of Egypt; and when you go, you will not go out empty-handed.note
22
וְשָׁאֲלָה אִשָּׁה מִשְּׁכֶנְתָּהּ וּמִגָּרַת בֵּיתָהּ, כְּלֵי-כֶסֶף וּכְלֵי זָהָב וּשְׂמָלֹת; וְשַׂמְתֶּם, עַל-בְּנֵיכֶם וְעַל-בְּנֹתֵיכֶם, וְנִצַּלְתֶּם, אֶת-מִצְרָיִם.
Each woman will ask of her neighbor, and of the woman staying in her house, vessels of silver and vessels of gold and clothing; and you will put them on your sons and on your daughters — and so you will strip Egypt bare."note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Each note explains this translation's choice and compares the seven versions on the shelf. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–6 · אַדְמַת-קֹדֶשׁ admat-qodesh — holy ground

The bush that burns and is not consumed. Moses — now the son-in-law shepherding Jethro's flock (the priest of Midian is Reuel in chapter 2, Jethro from here on; see his entry) — leads the sheep to Horeb, "the mountain of God," the height the book also calls Sinai. There the angel of Jehovah (mal'akh YHWH) appears in a flame from within a thornbush (seneh — a word that sounds like Sinai itself). The marvel is not the fire but its restraint: the bush "was burning with fire, yet was not consumed." And the account slides, as such scenes do, straight from the angel (v2) to Jehovah and God speaking in the first person (vv4-6): the same seam this translation met at the Bible's first "angel of Jehovah," Hagar's well (16:7) — the messenger who so carries the Sender that the text stops distinguishing them.

"Moses, Moses" — "Here I am." God calls the doubled name — as he called "Abraham, Abraham!" with the knife raised (22:11) — and Moses answers with Abraham's own word, hineni, "Here I am." Then: "take off your sandals … for the place on which you are standing is holy ground" (admat-qodesh). This is the first patch of earth in the Bible called qodesh, "holy" — and it is admah, the "ground" Adam was formed from (2:7): ordinary soil made holy only by the Presence standing on it. Then the self-introduction that fixes everything to come — "I am the God of your father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob" — the very words Jesus would later press to prove the resurrection: God "is not the God of the dead but of the living" (Matthew 22:32). Moses hides his face.

Verses 7–12 · כִּי-אֶהְיֶה עִמָּךְ ki ehyeh immakh — "because I will be with you"

Seen, heard, known. The four verbs that closed chapter 2 — God heard, remembered, saw, knew (2:24-25) — now come back in the first person, out of the fire: "I have surely seen the affliction … I have heard their outcry … I know their pains." Then the verb that answers them: "I have come down (va-ered) to rescue them" — the same "coming down" as at Babel (11:5), but this time to save, not to scatter — and to bring them to "a land flowing with milk and honey," the phrase's first appearance in scripture. The six peoples of the land are named; the commission is given: "I will send you to Pharaoh."

The answer is not Moses. Moses' first objection — "Who am I?" — is met not with a résumé but with a presence: "Because I will be with you" (ki ehyeh immakh). It does not matter who Moses is; it matters Who goes with him. And that little word ehyeh, "I will be," is planted here on purpose: two verses on, it becomes the Name (v14). The sign God gives is a strange one — a promise that can only be verified after obedience: "when you bring the people out, you will serve God on this mountain." The verb is avad — the very word for the slavery of 1:14 (avodah): Israel serves Pharaoh, and is freed not into idleness but to serve God. The exodus is a change of masters.

Verses 13–15 · אֶהְיֶה אֲשֶׁר אֶהְיֶה ehyeh asher ehyeh — the Name

The sentence the whole Bible turns on. Moses asks the question everything has been building toward: "What is his name?" And God answers with three of the most examined words in any language: ehyeh asher ehyeh. The verb is hayah, "to be," in the Hebrew imperfect — a form that reaches across our tenses at once: I am, I will be, I will prove to be. So the shelf splits, honestly, by which edge of that verb each translator heard. The KJV, Geneva, and Douay give the timeless "I AM THAT I AM"; the ASV prints the same but footnotes the grammar — "Or, I WILL BE THAT I WILL BE"; the NWT, leaning into the imperfect's forward motion, renders it "I Will Become What I Choose to Become" — God's freedom to be whatever the deliverance will require. The ancient Greek translators heard it as pure being — egō eimi ho ōn, "I am THE ONE WHO IS" — the very title John's Revelation hands back to Christ, "him who is and who was and who is coming" (Rev 1:8).

Why this translation reads "I will be." The deciding clue is two verses back: God has just said ehyeh immakh, "I will be with you" (v12). To render the same word "I AM" here would sever the very thread the Hebrew ties — the presence promised to Moses is the Name given to Israel. So this translation keeps them one: "I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE," and "I WILL BE has sent me to you" — the traditional "I AM" kept in view, but the living, forward-tilting verb kept audible.

"I will be" — or "I will become what I choose to become"? The NWT's bolder rendering deserves weighing on its own, because it is not careless — it is the strongest case for hearing the Name as a declaration of sovereign freedom, and the Hebrew genuinely supports it. The construction is what grammarians call idem per idem — the same verb repeated across a relative, "I-will-be that I-will-be" — and its nearest cousin is one book later: "I will be gracious to whom I will be gracious, and show mercy to whom I show mercy" (Exodus 33:19), plainly a God who reserves the right to be who he will be, on his own terms. So "I will become what I choose to become" gains real force: it makes that self-determination explicit and lands like a thunderclap.

Why this translation still keeps the barer form. Three things are spent to gain that thunderclap. First, "choose" is added — there is no verb for choosing in the Hebrew; both halves are simply ehyeh, "I will be." Second, it breaks the mirror: the whole power of ehyeh … ehyeh is that the two words are identical — swap in "will be … choose to become" and the echo is gone. Third, and most costly, it blurs the bridge to the Name: ehyeh ("I will be") is the first person of the very verb YHWH is the third person of (v15), and keeping it one consistent word is what lets a reader watch Presence (v12, "I will be with you") become Name become Jehovah. And there is a quieter paradox worth naming: the barer "I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE" is actually the freer of the two. By naming no mechanism it holds all three ancient readings open at once — self-existence (the "I AM" of pure being, the Greek Bible's "the One who is"), faithful constancy ("I will be what I have always been"), and dynamic becoming ("I will be whatever the rescue requires"). To spell out "choose to become" is to make one of those three vivid and let the other two fade — more force for that one reading, a little less openness across all of them. So this translation keeps the tautology whole and lets the note, not the text, carry the sovereign-freedom weight the NWT hears. (A middle path, "I WILL BECOME WHAT I WILL BECOME," would keep both the mirror and the motion — but "become" will not travel back to v12's "I will be with you," so "be" wins for consistency.)

And then the Name itself. God says it again, in the third person this time (v15): Jehovah (YHWH) — for the four-letter Name is, by the oldest and most widely held reading, the third-person of that very verb: "He is / He will be" (or "He causes to be"). God names himself in the first person, Ehyeh, "I will be"; his people name him in the third, YHWH, "He will be." And God stakes everything on it: "this is my name forever, and this is my memorial from generation to generation" — the verse that authorizes reading the Name as a name and not a title. This is why this whole translation prints Jehovah where most Bibles hide it behind "the LORD" (see the YHWH and Jehovah entries): the Name he asked to be remembered by, said aloud.

Verses 16–22 · פָּקֹד פָּקַדְתִּי … וְנִצַּלְתֶּם paqod paqadti … venitsaltem

The password of the redemption. Moses is to gather the elders with a phrase they already know as the promise: paqod paqadti etkhem, "I have surely attended to you" (the emphatic doubling of paqad, the verb of God "visiting" Sarah with a son, 21:1). It is the very word dying Joseph left as Israel's password out of Egypt — "God will surely attend to you, and carry my bones up from here" (Genesis 50:24-25). The elders "will listen to your voice" — obedience, shama be-qol, the thing the last kings of Judah refused (Jeremiah 22:21). The first ask to Pharaoh is modest — a three days' journey to sacrifice — and God tells Moses plainly it will fail: "the king will not let you go — no, not except by a mighty hand" (yad chazaqah, the opening half of the great redemption-formula, "a mighty hand and an outstretched arm," which Jeremiah will one day turn against Judah).

Wonders — and the stripping of Egypt. "I will strike Egypt with all my wonders" (niflaotai) — the very "wondrous works" Zedekiah's officials would one day beg Jeremiah to see repeated, only to learn the Wonder-worker had changed sides (21:2); here they are first promised. And the exodus ends in a reversal with teeth: the slaves will leave with silver, gold, and clothing, "and you will strip Egypt bare." The Hebrew hides a hard pun — the verb natsal: God "comes down to rescue (le-hatsil) them" in v8, and the people "strip / plunder (nitsaltem) Egypt" in v22 — the same root for the deliverance and for the spoil. It is the quiet fulfillment of a word spoken to Abram in the dark four centuries before: "afterward they will come out with great possessions" (15:14).

Patterns worth carrying forward

The Name, kept as a name. The whole translation's rendering of the divine Name stands on this chapter. Ehyeh (v14) is kept "I will be," matching "I will be with you" (v12) so the thread from Presence to Name is unbroken — the traditional KJV "I AM" noted, the NWT's "I will become" weighed, the Greek "the One who is" carried forward to Revelation. And YHWH (v15), the third-person of the same verb, is printed Jehovah — "my name forever, my memorial to all generations" — rather than the shelf's "the LORD."

Echoes paid. The angel of Jehovah in the bush (v2) — the seam first met at Hagar's well (16:7); "Moses, Moses … Here I am" (v4) — the doubled call and hineni of the Aqedah (22:11); "I have seen … heard … know" (v7) — the four verbs of 2:24-25 spoken aloud; "come down" (v8) — Babel's verb (11:5) turned to rescue; holy ground on the admah (v5) — Adam's soil (2:7) made holy; paqod paqadti (v16) — the visiting of Sarah, and Joseph's password; and the stripping of Egypt (v22) — Abram's "great possessions" (15:14) come due.

Next installment in this book: Exodus 4 — the three signs (the staff, the leprous hand, the Nile-blood), "who made man's mouth?", and the road back to Egypt.

Exodus 4 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיַּעַן מֹשֶׁה, וַיֹּאמֶר, וְהֵן לֹא-יַאֲמִינוּ לִי, וְלֹא יִשְׁמְעוּ בְּקֹלִי: כִּי יֹאמְרוּ, לֹא-נִרְאָה אֵלֶיךָ יְהוָה.
And Moses answered and said, "But look, they will not believe me or listen to my voice, for they will say, 'Jehovah did not appear to you.'"note
2
וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו יְהוָה, מזה מַה-זֶּה בְיָדֶךָ; וַיֹּאמֶר, מַטֶּה.
And Jehovah said to him, "What is that in your hand?" And he said, "A staff."note
3
וַיֹּאמֶר הַשְׁלִיכֵהוּ אַרְצָה, וַיַּשְׁלִכֵהוּ אַרְצָה וַיְהִי לְנָחָשׁ; וַיָּנָס מֹשֶׁה, מִפָּנָיו.
And he said, "Throw it on the ground." And he threw it on the ground, and it became a serpent; and Moses fled from it.note
4
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה, אֶל-מֹשֶׁה, שְׁלַח יָדְךָ, וֶאֱחֹז בִּזְנָבוֹ; וַיִּשְׁלַח יָדוֹ וַיַּחֲזֶק בּוֹ, וַיְהִי לְמַטֶּה בְּכַפּוֹ.
And Jehovah said to Moses, "Reach out your hand and take it by the tail" — and he reached out his hand and seized it, and it became a staff in his palm —note
5
לְמַעַן יַאֲמִינוּ, כִּי-נִרְאָה אֵלֶיךָ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲבֹתָם: אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם אֱלֹהֵי יִצְחָק, וֵאלֹהֵי יַעֲקֹב.
"so that they may believe that Jehovah, the God of their fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, has appeared to you."note
6
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה לוֹ עוֹד, הָבֵא-נָא יָדְךָ בְּחֵיקֶךָ, וַיָּבֵא יָדוֹ, בְּחֵיקוֹ; וַיּוֹצִאָהּ, וְהִנֵּה יָדוֹ מְצֹרַעַת כַּשָּׁלֶג.
And Jehovah said to him further, "Put your hand into your cloak." And he put his hand into his cloak, and drew it out, and behold, his hand was leprous, white as snow.note
7
וַיֹּאמֶר, הָשֵׁב יָדְךָ אֶל-חֵיקֶךָ, וַיָּשֶׁב יָדוֹ, אֶל-חֵיקוֹ; וַיּוֹצִאָהּ, מֵחֵיקוֹ, וְהִנֵּה-שָׁבָה, כִּבְשָׂרוֹ.
And he said, "Put your hand back into your cloak" — and he put his hand back into his cloak, and drew it out from his cloak, and behold, it was restored like the rest of his flesh —note
8
וְהָיָה, אִם-לֹא יַאֲמִינוּ לָךְ, וְלֹא יִשְׁמְעוּ, לְקֹל הָאֹת הָרִאשׁוֹן--וְהֶאֱמִינוּ, לְקֹל הָאֹת הָאַחֲרוֹן.
"and if they will not believe you or heed the voice of the first sign, then they will believe the voice of the latter sign.note
9
וְהָיָה אִם-לֹא יַאֲמִינוּ גַּם לִשְׁנֵי הָאֹתוֹת הָאֵלֶּה, וְלֹא יִשְׁמְעוּן לְקֹלֶךָ--וְלָקַחְתָּ מִמֵּימֵי הַיְאֹר, וְשָׁפַכְתָּ הַיַּבָּשָׁה; וְהָיוּ הַמַּיִם אֲשֶׁר תִּקַּח מִן-הַיְאֹר, וְהָיוּ לְדָם בַּיַּבָּשֶׁת.
And if they will not believe even these two signs or listen to your voice, then take some of the water of the Nile and pour it out on the dry ground; and the water that you take from the Nile will become blood on the dry ground."note
10
וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל-יְהוָה, בִּי אֲדֹנָי, לֹא אִישׁ דְּבָרִים אָנֹכִי גַּם מִתְּמוֹל גַּם מִשִּׁלְשֹׁם, גַּם מֵאָז דַּבֶּרְךָ אֶל-עַבְדֶּךָ: כִּי כְבַד-פֶּה וּכְבַד לָשׁוֹן, אָנֹכִי.
And Moses said to Jehovah, "Please, my Lord, I am not a man of words, neither yesterday nor the day before, nor since you have spoken to your servant, for I am heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue."note
11
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֵלָיו, מִי שָׂם פֶּה לָאָדָם, אוֹ מִי-יָשׂוּם אִלֵּם, אוֹ חֵרֵשׁ אוֹ פִקֵּחַ אוֹ עִוֵּר--הֲלֹא אָנֹכִי, יְהוָה.
And Jehovah said to him, "Who made man's mouth? Or who makes him mute, or deaf, or seeing, or blind? Is it not I, Jehovah?note
12
וְעַתָּה, לֵךְ; וְאָנֹכִי אֶהְיֶה עִם-פִּיךָ, וְהוֹרֵיתִיךָ אֲשֶׁר תְּדַבֵּר.
Now go, and I will be with your mouth and teach you what you shall speak."note
13
וַיֹּאמֶר, בִּי אֲדֹנָי; שְׁלַח-נָא, בְּיַד-תִּשְׁלָח.
But he said, "Please, my Lord, send by the hand of whomever you will send."note
14
וַיִּחַר-אַף יְהוָה בְּמֹשֶׁה, וַיֹּאמֶר הֲלֹא אַהֲרֹן אָחִיךָ הַלֵּוִי--יָדַעְתִּי, כִּי-דַבֵּר יְדַבֵּר הוּא; וְגַם הִנֵּה-הוּא יֹצֵא לִקְרָאתֶךָ, וְרָאֲךָ וְשָׂמַח בְּלִבּוֹ.
And Jehovah's anger burned against Moses, and he said, "Is there not Aaron your brother, the Levite? I know that he can surely speak; and behold, he is even coming out to meet you, and when he sees you he will rejoice in his heart.note
15
וְדִבַּרְתָּ אֵלָיו, וְשַׂמְתָּ אֶת-הַדְּבָרִים בְּפִיו; וְאָנֹכִי, אֶהְיֶה עִם-פִּיךָ וְעִם-פִּיהוּ, וְהוֹרֵיתִי אֶתְכֶם, אֵת אֲשֶׁר תַּעֲשׂוּן.
And you shall speak to him and put the words in his mouth; and I will be with your mouth and with his mouth, and I will teach you both what you shall do.note
16
וְדִבֶּר-הוּא לְךָ, אֶל-הָעָם; וְהָיָה הוּא יִהְיֶה-לְּךָ לְפֶה, וְאַתָּה תִּהְיֶה-לּוֹ לֵאלֹהִים.
And he shall speak for you to the people; and he shall be your mouth, and you shall be to him as God.note
17
וְאֶת-הַמַּטֶּה הַזֶּה, תִּקַּח בְּיָדֶךָ, אֲשֶׁר תַּעֲשֶׂה-בּוֹ, אֶת-הָאֹתֹת. {פ}
And take this staff in your hand, with which you shall do the signs."note
18
וַיֵּלֶךְ מֹשֶׁה וַיָּשָׁב אֶל-יֶתֶר חֹתְנוֹ, וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ אֵלְכָה נָּא וְאָשׁוּבָה אֶל-אַחַי אֲשֶׁר-בְּמִצְרַיִם, וְאֶרְאֶה, הַעוֹדָם חַיִּים; וַיֹּאמֶר יִתְרוֹ לְמֹשֶׁה, לֵךְ לְשָׁלוֹם.
And Moses went and returned to Jethro his father-in-law and said to him, "Let me go, please, and return to my brothers who are in Egypt, and see whether they are still alive." And Jethro said to Moses, "Go in peace."note
19
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה בְּמִדְיָן, לֵךְ שֻׁב מִצְרָיִם: כִּי-מֵתוּ, כָּל-הָאֲנָשִׁים, הַמְבַקְשִׁים, אֶת-נַפְשֶׁךָ.
And Jehovah said to Moses in Midian, "Go, return to Egypt, for all the men who sought your life are dead."note
20
וַיִּקַּח מֹשֶׁה אֶת-אִשְׁתּוֹ וְאֶת-בָּנָיו, וַיַּרְכִּבֵם עַל-הַחֲמֹר, וַיָּשָׁב, אַרְצָה מִצְרָיִם; וַיִּקַּח מֹשֶׁה אֶת-מַטֵּה הָאֱלֹהִים, בְּיָדוֹ.
And Moses took his wife and his sons and set them on the donkey, and returned to the land of Egypt; and Moses took the staff of God in his hand.note
21
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה, אֶל-מֹשֶׁה, בְּלֶכְתְּךָ לָשׁוּב מִצְרַיְמָה, רְאֵה כָּל-הַמֹּפְתִים אֲשֶׁר-שַׂמְתִּי בְיָדֶךָ וַעֲשִׂיתָם לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה; וַאֲנִי אֲחַזֵּק אֶת-לִבּוֹ, וְלֹא יְשַׁלַּח אֶת-הָעָם.
And Jehovah said to Moses, "When you go back to Egypt, see that you do before Pharaoh all the wonders that I have put in your hand; but I will harden his heart, and he will not let the people go.note
22
וְאָמַרְתָּ, אֶל-פַּרְעֹה: כֹּה אָמַר יְהוָה, בְּנִי בְכֹרִי יִשְׂרָאֵל.
And you shall say to Pharaoh, 'Thus says Jehovah: Israel is my firstborn son.note
23
וָאֹמַר אֵלֶיךָ, שַׁלַּח אֶת-בְּנִי וְיַעַבְדֵנִי, וַתְּמָאֵן, לְשַׁלְּחוֹ--הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי הֹרֵג, אֶת-בִּנְךָ בְּכֹרֶךָ.
And I said to you, Let my son go, that he may serve me; but you refused to let him go. Behold, I will kill your firstborn son.'"note
24
וַיְהִי בַדֶּרֶךְ, בַּמָּלוֹן; וַיִּפְגְּשֵׁהוּ יְהוָה, וַיְבַקֵּשׁ הֲמִיתוֹ.
And it happened on the way, at the lodging place, that Jehovah met him and sought to put him to death.note
25
וַתִּקַּח צִפֹּרָה צֹר, וַתִּכְרֹת אֶת-עָרְלַת בְּנָהּ, וַתַּגַּע, לְרַגְלָיו; וַתֹּאמֶר, כִּי חֲתַן-דָּמִים אַתָּה לִי.
Then Zipporah took a flint and cut off her son's foreskin, and touched it to his feet, and said, "Surely you are a bridegroom of blood to me."note
26
וַיִּרֶף, מִמֶּנּוּ; אָז, אָמְרָה, חֲתַן דָּמִים, לַמּוּלֹת. {פ}
So he let him alone. It was then she said "a bridegroom of blood," because of the circumcision.note
27
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-אַהֲרֹן, לֵךְ לִקְרַאת מֹשֶׁה הַמִּדְבָּרָה; וַיֵּלֶךְ, וַיִּפְגְּשֵׁהוּ בְּהַר הָאֱלֹהִים--וַיִּשַּׁק-לוֹ.
And Jehovah said to Aaron, "Go into the wilderness to meet Moses." And he went and met him at the mountain of God, and kissed him.note
28
וַיַּגֵּד מֹשֶׁה לְאַהֲרֹן, אֵת כָּל-דִּבְרֵי יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר שְׁלָחוֹ, וְאֵת כָּל-הָאֹתֹת, אֲשֶׁר צִוָּהוּ.
And Moses told Aaron all the words of Jehovah with which he had sent him, and all the signs that he had commanded him.note
29
וַיֵּלֶךְ מֹשֶׁה, וְאַהֲרֹן; וַיַּאַסְפוּ, אֶת-כָּל-זִקְנֵי בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל.
And Moses and Aaron went and gathered all the elders of the sons of Israel.note
30
וַיְדַבֵּר אַהֲרֹן--אֵת כָּל-הַדְּבָרִים, אֲשֶׁר-דִּבֶּר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה; וַיַּעַשׂ הָאֹתֹת, לְעֵינֵי הָעָם.
And Aaron spoke all the words that Jehovah had spoken to Moses, and did the signs before the eyes of the people.note
31
וַיַּאֲמֵן, הָעָם; וַיִּשְׁמְעוּ כִּי-פָקַד יְהוָה אֶת-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, וְכִי רָאָה אֶת-עָנְיָם, וַיִּקְּדוּ, וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ.
And the people believed; and when they heard that Jehovah had visited the sons of Israel and that he had seen their affliction, they bowed their heads and worshiped.note

Translator’s Notes

Verses 1–9 · three signs for an unbelieving people

Moses’ third objection. After “who am I?” (3:11) and “what is your name?” (3:13), Moses now says no one will believe him. God answers not with argument but with three signs (otot) — proofs to authenticate the messenger. The staff that becomes a serpent and back; the hand made leprous, white as snow, and healed; and water from the Nile turned to blood on the ground. Each will return: the staff-serpent will swallow the magicians’ snakes (ch 7), and the Nile-to-blood is the first plague in miniature. God gives Moses the plagues’ opening act to carry in his own hand.

Note the mercy in the order (v8): if they will not believe the first sign, then the second; if not the second, then the third. God stacks the evidence, patient with unbelief before it has even happened.

Verses 10–17 · heavy of mouth

Moses’ fourth objection is his speech: “I am not a man of words… heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue” (kevad peh) — a stammer, a slowness, or simply the excuse of a reluctant man. God’s answer is one of the great sentences on providence and human limitation: “Who made man’s mouth? Or who makes him mute, or deaf, or seeing, or blind? Is it not I, Jehovah?” The God who made the mouth can use even a heavy one. KJV “I am slow of speech, and of a slow tongue.”

The fifth and last objection is no longer an excuse but a plain refusal: “send by the hand of whomever you will send” — send someone else. And “Jehovah’s anger burned against Moses.” Yet even the anger is a concession: Aaron, already on his way, will be the mouth, and Moses will be “as God” to Aaron — the arrangement that becomes, at 7:1, “I have made you as God to Pharaoh, and Aaron your prophet.” The reluctant man is not replaced; he is given help. (Moses joins the line of the called who protest their inadequacy — and, like Jeremiah after him, “I am only a youth,” he is overruled by presence, not by argument.)

Verses 18–23 · return to Egypt — and “my firstborn son”

«All the men who sought your life are dead» (v19). The pharaoh Moses fled (2:15) has died. ⚠ On the early-date chronology this project foregrounds (the Exodus in 1446 BC, following Associates for Biblical Research / Joel Kramer), the pharaoh of the oppression was Thutmose III — Egypt’s great warrior-king — whose death (~1425 BC) is “the king of Egypt died” of 2:23; Moses now returns to his successor, Amenhotep II, the pharaoh of the Exodus. (The mainstream academic view dates the Exodus later, ~1250 BC under Ramesses II; the early date rests on 1 Kings 6:1’s “480 years” and is given here as the reading the project leads with, not a settled fact.)

Two things are announced before Moses reaches Egypt. First, “I will harden his heart” (v21) — the first of many statements that God will harden Pharaoh; the book will also say Pharaoh hardened his own heart, and the interplay of the two is one of Scripture’s hardest knots, left standing. Second, and staggering: “Israel is my firstborn son… let my son go, that he may serve me; if you refuse, I will kill your firstborn son” (vv22-23). Here, at the very outset, the tenth plague is already spoken. Israel is God’s bekhor — a claim of covenant sonship that reframes the whole conflict as a father reclaiming his son. And on the early date, the firstborn struck at Passover (12:29) is read alongside the Dream Stele of Thutmose IV, Amenhotep II’s successor, which reads as propaganda covering that he was NOT the expected heir — the crown prince, the firstborn, having died. KJV “Israel is my son, even my firstborn”; the ASV “Israel is my son, my first-born.”

Verses 24–26 · a bridegroom of blood

⚠ One of the strangest and most obscure passages in the Torah — and honesty requires saying so plainly. On the way to Egypt, “Jehovah met him and sought to put him to death” — whom is not certain (Moses, most likely; possibly his son). Zipporah acts at once: she takes a flint, circumcises their son, touches the foreskin to “his feet,” and says, “Surely you are a bridegroom of blood to me.” And the danger passes.

Nearly every word is disputed. The most common reading: Moses, about to demand that Pharaoh release God’s “son” and to warn of the firstborn, had left his OWN son uncircumcised — outside the covenant sign (Genesis 17) — and God will not send a covenant-breaker on a covenant errand; Zipporah’s emergency circumcision, and the blood she smears, sets it right, and foreshadows the blood on the doorposts that will save Israel’s firstborn (ch 12). ⚠ But the pronouns are slippery, “his feet” may be a euphemism, and “bridegroom of blood” (chatan damim) has no certain sense; the passage resists a confident solution, and this translation renders it closely and lets it stand strange. KJV “a bloody husband art thou to me.”

Verses 27–31 · the brothers, and a people who believe

Aaron, sent by God, meets Moses “at the mountain of God” — the same Horeb where the bush burned — and kisses him; the two brothers, reunited after Moses’ forty years in Midian, go together to the elders. Aaron speaks the words and Moses does the signs, and the response is the very one the third objection feared would never come: “the people believed.”

And what they believe is not the wonders but what the wonders mean — “that Jehovah had VISITED the sons of Israel and had seen their affliction.” Paqad, the visitation verb, is the word Joseph swore by at the very end of Genesis: “God will surely visit you… and carry up my bones” (50:24). The promise made over Joseph’s coffin, four hundred years earlier, is now being kept. The people bow their heads and worship. The exodus has its messenger, its spokesman, and, at last, its people.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the exact / distinct words: “signs” for otot, the three authenticating wonders; “heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue” for kevad peh, kept literal rather than smoothed to “slow of speech”; “as God” for the arrangement that makes Moses Elohim to Aaron; “harden” for chazak, the hardening-of-the-heart verb left in its full difficulty; “firstborn son” for bekhor; and “bridegroom of blood” for chatan damim, rendered closely and left strange.

Echoes paid. The reluctant-prophet line — Moses’ “heavy of mouth” answered, as Jeremiah’s “I am only a youth” will be, by presence not argument (Jeremiah 1:6); “I will be with your mouth” (ehyeh), the same verb as the Name at the bush (Exodus 3:14); and paqad — Jehovah “visited” Israel, the very word Joseph swore by over his coffin (Genesis 50:24).

Echoes planted. The staff that becomes a serpent and the Nile-to-blood, the plagues’ opening act carried in Moses’ hand (ch 7-8); the hardening of Pharaoh’s heart, a thread through ten plagues; “Israel is my firstborn son” and the threat to Pharaoh’s own, the tenth plague announced at the outset (ch 12); and the blood Zipporah smears, foreshadowing the blood on the doorposts that will save Israel’s firstborn.

Next in this book: Exodus 5 — Moses and Aaron before Pharaoh at last: “Who is Jehovah, that I should obey his voice?” — and Pharaoh answers by ordering the Israelites to make bricks without straw, so that the first result of the deliverance is that it gets worse.

Exodus 5 — The Mister Translation

1
וְאַחַר, בָּאוּ מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן, וַיֹּאמְרוּ, אֶל-פַּרְעֹה: כֹּה-אָמַר יְהוָה, אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, שַׁלַּח אֶת-עַמִּי, וְיָחֹגּוּ לִי בַּמִּדְבָּר.
And afterward Moses and Aaron went in and said to Pharaoh, "Thus says Jehovah, the God of Israel: Let my people go, that they may hold a feast to me in the wilderness."note
2
וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה--מִי יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר אֶשְׁמַע בְּקֹלוֹ, לְשַׁלַּח אֶת-יִשְׂרָאֵל: לֹא יָדַעְתִּי אֶת-יְהוָה, וְגַם אֶת-יִשְׂרָאֵל לֹא אֲשַׁלֵּחַ.
But Pharaoh said, "Who is Jehovah, that I should obey his voice and let Israel go? I do not know Jehovah, and moreover I will not let Israel go."note
3
וַיֹּאמְרוּ, אֱלֹהֵי הָעִבְרִים נִקְרָא עָלֵינוּ; נֵלְכָה נָּא דֶּרֶךְ שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים בַּמִּדְבָּר, וְנִזְבְּחָה לַיהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ--פֶּן-יִפְגָּעֵנוּ, בַּדֶּבֶר אוֹ בֶחָרֶב.
And they said, "The God of the Hebrews has met with us. Let us go, please, a three days' journey into the wilderness and sacrifice to Jehovah our God, lest he strike us with pestilence or with the sword."note
4
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם, מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם, לָמָּה מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן, תַּפְרִיעוּ אֶת-הָעָם מִמַּעֲשָׂיו; לְכוּ, לְסִבְלֹתֵיכֶם.
And the king of Egypt said to them, "Moses and Aaron, why do you take the people away from their work? Get to your burdens!"note
5
וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה, הֵן-רַבִּים עַתָּה עַם הָאָרֶץ; וְהִשְׁבַּתֶּם אֹתָם, מִסִּבְלֹתָם.
And Pharaoh said, "Look, the people of the land are now many, and you would make them rest from their burdens."note
6
וַיְצַו פַּרְעֹה, בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא, אֶת-הַנֹּגְשִׂים בָּעָם, וְאֶת-שֹׁטְרָיו לֵאמֹר.
And that same day Pharaoh commanded the taskmasters of the people and their foremen, saying,note
7
לֹא תֹאסִפוּן לָתֵת תֶּבֶן לָעָם, לִלְבֹּן הַלְּבֵנִים--כִּתְמוֹל שִׁלְשֹׁם: הֵם, יֵלְכוּ, וְקֹשְׁשׁוּ לָהֶם, תֶּבֶן.
"You shall no longer give the people straw to make the bricks, as yesterday and the day before; let them go and gather straw for themselves.note
8
וְאֶת-מַתְכֹּנֶת הַלְּבֵנִים אֲשֶׁר הֵם עֹשִׂים תְּמוֹל שִׁלְשֹׁם, תָּשִׂימוּ עֲלֵיהֶם--לֹא תִגְרְעוּ, מִמֶּנּוּ: כִּי-נִרְפִּים הֵם--עַל-כֵּן הֵם צֹעֲקִים לֵאמֹר, נֵלְכָה נִזְבְּחָה לֵאלֹהֵינוּ.
But the quota of bricks that they made yesterday and the day before you shall lay on them; you shall not reduce it. For they are idle; that is why they cry out, saying, 'Let us go and sacrifice to our God.'note
9
תִּכְבַּד הָעֲבֹדָה עַל-הָאֲנָשִׁים, וְיַעֲשׂוּ-בָהּ; וְאַל-יִשְׁעוּ, בְּדִבְרֵי-שָׁקֶר.
Let the work be heavier on the men, and let them labor at it, and let them pay no regard to lying words."note
10
וַיֵּצְאוּ נֹגְשֵׂי הָעָם, וְשֹׁטְרָיו, וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֶל-הָעָם, לֵאמֹר: כֹּה אָמַר פַּרְעֹה, אֵינֶנִּי נֹתֵן לָכֶם תֶּבֶן.
So the taskmasters of the people and their foremen went out and said to the people, "Thus says Pharaoh: I am giving you no straw.note
11
אַתֶּם, לְכוּ קְחוּ לָכֶם תֶּבֶן, מֵאֲשֶׁר, תִּמְצָאוּ: כִּי אֵין נִגְרָע מֵעֲבֹדַתְכֶם, דָּבָר.
Go yourselves, get straw wherever you can find it; but nothing of your work will be reduced."note
12
וַיָּפֶץ הָעָם, בְּכָל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם, לְקֹשֵׁשׁ קַשׁ, לַתֶּבֶן.
So the people scattered throughout all the land of Egypt to gather stubble for straw.note
13
וְהַנֹּגְשִׂים, אָצִים לֵאמֹר: כַּלּוּ מַעֲשֵׂיכֶם דְּבַר-יוֹם בְּיוֹמוֹ, כַּאֲשֶׁר בִּהְיוֹת הַתֶּבֶן.
And the taskmasters pressed them, saying, "Finish your work, your daily quota, as when there was straw."note
14
וַיֻּכּוּ, שֹׁטְרֵי בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, אֲשֶׁר-שָׂמוּ עֲלֵהֶם, נֹגְשֵׂי פַרְעֹה לֵאמֹר: מַדּוּעַ לֹא כִלִּיתֶם חָקְכֶם לִלְבֹּן, כִּתְמוֹל שִׁלְשֹׁם--גַּם-תְּמוֹל, גַּם-הַיּוֹם.
And the foremen of the sons of Israel, whom Pharaoh's taskmasters had set over them, were beaten, and it was said, "Why have you not finished your appointed task of bricks, yesterday and today, as before?"note
15
וַיָּבֹאוּ, שֹׁטְרֵי בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, וַיִּצְעֲקוּ אֶל-פַּרְעֹה, לֵאמֹר: לָמָּה תַעֲשֶׂה כֹה, לַעֲבָדֶיךָ.
Then the foremen of the sons of Israel came and cried out to Pharaoh, saying, "Why do you deal this way with your servants?note
16
תֶּבֶן, אֵין נִתָּן לַעֲבָדֶיךָ, וּלְבֵנִים אֹמְרִים לָנוּ, עֲשׂוּ; וְהִנֵּה עֲבָדֶיךָ מֻכִּים, וְחָטָאת עַמֶּךָ.
No straw is given to your servants, and they say to us, 'Make bricks!' And look, your servants are beaten — but the fault is your own people's."note
17
וַיֹּאמֶר נִרְפִּים אַתֶּם, נִרְפִּים; עַל-כֵּן אַתֶּם אֹמְרִים, נֵלְכָה נִזְבְּחָה לַיהוָה.
But he said, "Idle, you are idle! That is why you say, 'Let us go and sacrifice to Jehovah.'note
18
וְעַתָּה לְכוּ עִבְדוּ, וְתֶבֶן לֹא-יִנָּתֵן לָכֶם; וְתֹכֶן לְבֵנִים, תִּתֵּנוּ.
Now go, work! No straw will be given you, yet you shall deliver the full quota of bricks."note
19
וַיִּרְאוּ שֹׁטְרֵי בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל, אֹתָם--בְּרָע לֵאמֹר: לֹא-תִגְרְעוּ מִלִּבְנֵיכֶם, דְּבַר-יוֹם בְּיוֹמוֹ.
And the foremen of the sons of Israel saw that they were in trouble when it was said, "You shall not reduce your daily quota of bricks."note
20
וַיִּפְגְּעוּ אֶת-מֹשֶׁה וְאֶת-אַהֲרֹן, נִצָּבִים לִקְרָאתָם, בְּצֵאתָם, מֵאֵת פַּרְעֹה.
And they met Moses and Aaron, who were standing there to meet them as they came out from Pharaoh,note
21
וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֲלֵהֶם, יֵרֶא יְהוָה עֲלֵיכֶם וְיִשְׁפֹּט: אֲשֶׁר הִבְאַשְׁתֶּם אֶת-רֵיחֵנוּ, בְּעֵינֵי פַרְעֹה וּבְעֵינֵי עֲבָדָיו, לָתֶת-חֶרֶב בְּיָדָם, לְהָרְגֵנוּ.
and they said to them, "May Jehovah look upon you and judge, because you have made us a stench in the eyes of Pharaoh and in the eyes of his servants, putting a sword in their hand to kill us."note
22
וַיָּשָׁב מֹשֶׁה אֶל-יְהוָה, וַיֹּאמַר: אֲדֹנָי, לָמָה הֲרֵעֹתָה לָעָם הַזֶּה--לָמָּה זֶּה, שְׁלַחְתָּנִי.
Then Moses returned to Jehovah and said, "My Lord, why have you brought evil on this people? Why did you ever send me?note
23
וּמֵאָז בָּאתִי אֶל-פַּרְעֹה, לְדַבֵּר בִּשְׁמֶךָ, הֵרַע, לָעָם הַזֶּה; וְהַצֵּל לֹא-הִצַּלְתָּ, אֶת-עַמֶּךָ.
For since I came to Pharaoh to speak in your name, he has done evil to this people — and you have not delivered your people at all."note

Translator’s Notes

Verses 1–5 · Who is Jehovah?

The first confrontation. “Thus says Jehovah” — the messenger-formula of the prophets, spoken here to a king. And Pharaoh’s answer is the question the entire book exists to answer: “WHO IS JEHOVAH, that I should obey his voice? I do not know Jehovah.” ⚠ It is not merely defiance; from an Egyptian throne it is a statement of fact — this god is not on his lists. Everything that follows is aimed at it: the plagues come again and again “so that you may KNOW that I am Jehovah” (7:5, 17; 8:22; 14:4). The book is the answer to Pharaoh’s sneer. KJV “Who is the LORD, that I should obey his voice?”

⚠ The modest request. What Moses asks for is a three days’ journey to hold a feast and sacrifice — not, on its face, a permanent departure. Readers have long asked whether this is less than the whole truth. The likeliest reading is that it is a genuine first demand, a test of Pharaoh’s willingness in a small thing, which he refuses outright — so that the total demand (“let my people go,” with no return) follows his own refusal. The text offers no comment, and the note will not manufacture one.

Pharaoh’s counter is pure administration: “why do you take the people away from their work? Get to your burdens!” He hears a request for worship and files it as a labor problem. It is the whole conflict in miniature: to Pharaoh these are units of production; to Jehovah they are his firstborn son (4:22).

Verses 6–14 · bricks without straw

The retaliation is precise and cruel. Straw (teben) was chopped and mixed into Nile mud to bind the bricks — the practice is well attested in Egyptian brickmaking, as are the daily brick quotas recorded by Egyptian scribes. Pharaoh does not lower the quota; he removes the supply and keeps the number. The people must now scatter across Egypt gathering stubble and still deliver as before.

Two ranks stand between Pharaoh and the people: the NOGSIM, Egyptian taskmasters, and the SHOTRIM, Israelite foremen appointed over their own kinsmen. And it is the Israelite foremen who are beaten when the quota fails — the cruelty of every such system, that it makes the oppressed enforce their own oppression and then punishes them when it breaks.

«They are idle» (nirpim). Pharaoh’s theory of worship: a people with time to think about God is a people with too little work. It is the oldest slander against the sabbath, and he will repeat it, doubled, in verse 17.

Verses 15–19 · the fault is your own people’s

The foremen appeal over the taskmasters’ heads to Pharaoh himself — and get his word thrown back at them doubled: “Idle, you are idle!” The appeal to the top, in a system like this, only confirms that the cruelty comes from the top.

⚠ Their protest ends in a genuinely difficult phrase (v16): chatat ammekha — either “the fault is your own people’s” (your Egyptians are to blame) or “you sin against your own people” (a far bolder accusation, aimed at Pharaoh himself). The Hebrew genuinely supports both, and the versions divide: KJV “the fault is in thine own people”; the ESV “the fault is in your own people.” This translation keeps the ambiguity rather than choosing a side for the reader.

And so the foremen “saw that they were in trouble.” The first casualties of the deliverance are the men who tried to work within the system.

Verses 20–23 · you have not delivered your people at all

The chapter ends in a double collapse. The foremen, coming out from Pharaoh, meet Moses and Aaron waiting and turn on them: “May Jehovah look upon you and judge — you have made us a stench in Pharaoh’s eyes and put a sword in his hand to kill us.” The people God sent Moses to save now count him their disaster.

And Moses turns on God, with a bluntness Scripture does not censor: “My Lord, why have you brought evil on this people? Why did you ever send me?… and you have not delivered your people at all.” KJV “neither hast thou delivered thy people at all.”

⚠ This is the honest shape of the story, and it is worth saying plainly: the FIRST result of God’s word of deliverance is that things get WORSE. The oppression intensifies, the people despair, and the deliverer accuses God to his face. The Bible neither hides this nor rebukes Moses for it — chapter 6 opens with the answer, “NOW you shall see what I will do to Pharaoh.” The dawn is real; but it comes after the darkest hour, and Exodus makes the reader sit in that hour for a whole chapter before it breaks. (Jeremiah will one day accuse God in the same register, 20:7.)

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the exact / distinct words: “Let my people go” for shalach, the exodus refrain, kept identical to “let my SON go” of 4:23; “I do not know Jehovah” for yada, the verb the plagues exist to reverse; “straw” and “quota” kept concrete, as the Egyptian brick-records have them; “taskmasters” (nogsim) held distinct from “foremen” (shotrim), the Egyptians over the Israelites over their own; “idle” for nirpim; and the ambiguity of “the fault is your own people’s” (v16) left standing.

Echoes paid. “Let my people go” answering “Israel is my firstborn son… let my son go” (4:22-23); Pharaoh’s “I do not know Jehovah” set against the Name revealed at the bush (3:14-15); and the “burdens” (sivlot) that were the whole condition Jehovah said he had come down to see (3:7).

Echoes planted. The question “Who is Jehovah?”, which the ten plagues will answer one by one (“that you may know that I am Jehovah”); the hardening already promised at 4:21, now visible in Pharaoh’s flat refusal; and Moses’ complaint, which chapter 6 answers with the great “I am Jehovah” speech and the promise, “NOW you shall see what I will do to Pharaoh.”

Next in this book: Exodus 6 — God’s answer to Moses’ accusation: “I am Jehovah… I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as God Almighty, but by my name Jehovah I did not make myself known to them” — the seven “I will” promises of redemption, spoken to a people so crushed by their labor that they cannot hear them.

Exodus 6 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה, אֶל-מֹשֶׁה, עַתָּה תִרְאֶה, אֲשֶׁר אֶעֱשֶׂה לְפַרְעֹה: כִּי בְיָד חֲזָקָה, יְשַׁלְּחֵם, וּבְיָד חֲזָקָה, יְגָרְשֵׁם מֵאַרְצוֹ. {ס}
And Jehovah said to Moses, "Now you shall see what I will do to Pharaoh; for with a strong hand he will let them go, and with a strong hand he will drive them out of his land."note
2
וַיְדַבֵּר אֱלֹהִים, אֶל-מֹשֶׁה; וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו, אֲנִי יְהוָה.
And God spoke to Moses and said to him, "I am Jehovah.note
3
וָאֵרָא, אֶל-אַבְרָהָם אֶל-יִצְחָק וְאֶל-יַעֲקֹב--בְּאֵל שַׁדָּי; וּשְׁמִי יְהוָה, לֹא נוֹדַעְתִּי לָהֶם.
And I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as God Almighty; but by my name Jehovah I did not make myself known to them.note
4
וְגַם הֲקִמֹתִי אֶת-בְּרִיתִי אִתָּם, לָתֵת לָהֶם אֶת-אֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן--אֵת אֶרֶץ מְגֻרֵיהֶם, אֲשֶׁר-גָּרוּ בָהּ.
And I also established my covenant with them, to give them the land of Canaan, the land of their sojournings, in which they sojourned.note
5
וְגַם אֲנִי שָׁמַעְתִּי, אֶת-נַאֲקַת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, אֲשֶׁר מִצְרַיִם, מַעֲבִדִים אֹתָם; וָאֶזְכֹּר, אֶת-בְּרִיתִי.
And I also have heard the groaning of the sons of Israel, whom the Egyptians hold in bondage, and I have remembered my covenant.note
6
לָכֵן אֱמֹר לִבְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל, אֲנִי יְהוָה, וְהוֹצֵאתִי אֶתְכֶם מִתַּחַת סִבְלֹת מִצְרַיִם, וְהִצַּלְתִּי אֶתְכֶם מֵעֲבֹדָתָם; וְגָאַלְתִּי אֶתְכֶם בִּזְרוֹעַ נְטוּיָה, וּבִשְׁפָטִים גְּדֹלִים.
Therefore say to the sons of Israel: I am Jehovah, and I will bring you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians, and I will deliver you from their bondage, and I will redeem you with an outstretched arm and with great judgments.note
7
וְלָקַחְתִּי אֶתְכֶם לִי לְעָם, וְהָיִיתִי לָכֶם לֵאלֹהִים; וִידַעְתֶּם, כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם, הַמּוֹצִיא אֶתְכֶם, מִתַּחַת סִבְלוֹת מִצְרָיִם.
And I will take you to myself for a people, and I will be to you a God; and you shall know that I am Jehovah your God, who brings you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians.note
8
וְהֵבֵאתִי אֶתְכֶם, אֶל-הָאָרֶץ, אֲשֶׁר נָשָׂאתִי אֶת-יָדִי, לָתֵת אֹתָהּ לְאַבְרָהָם לְיִצְחָק וּלְיַעֲקֹב; וְנָתַתִּי אֹתָהּ לָכֶם מוֹרָשָׁה, אֲנִי יְהוָה.
And I will bring you into the land that I lifted my hand to give to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob; and I will give it to you as a possession. I am Jehovah."note
9
וַיְדַבֵּר מֹשֶׁה כֵּן, אֶל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל; וְלֹא שָׁמְעוּ, אֶל-מֹשֶׁה, מִקֹּצֶר רוּחַ, וּמֵעֲבֹדָה קָשָׁה. {פ}
And Moses spoke thus to the sons of Israel; but they did not listen to Moses, from shortness of spirit and from cruel bondage.note
10
וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה, אֶל-מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר.
And Jehovah spoke to Moses, saying,note
11
בֹּא דַבֵּר, אֶל-פַּרְעֹה מֶלֶךְ מִצְרָיִם; וִישַׁלַּח אֶת-בְּנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל, מֵאַרְצוֹ.
"Go in, speak to Pharaoh king of Egypt, that he let the sons of Israel go out of his land."note
12
וַיְדַבֵּר מֹשֶׁה, לִפְנֵי יְהוָה לֵאמֹר: הֵן בְּנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל, לֹא-שָׁמְעוּ אֵלַי, וְאֵיךְ יִשְׁמָעֵנִי פַרְעֹה, וַאֲנִי עֲרַל שְׂפָתָיִם. {פ}
And Moses spoke before Jehovah, saying, "Look, the sons of Israel have not listened to me; how then shall Pharaoh hear me, and I of uncircumcised lips?"note
13
וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה, אֶל-מֹשֶׁה וְאֶל-אַהֲרֹן, וַיְצַוֵּם אֶל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, וְאֶל-פַּרְעֹה מֶלֶךְ מִצְרָיִם--לְהוֹצִיא אֶת-בְּנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל, מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם. {ס}
And Jehovah spoke to Moses and to Aaron and gave them a charge to the sons of Israel and to Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring the sons of Israel out of the land of Egypt.note
14
אֵלֶּה, רָאשֵׁי בֵית-אֲבֹתָם: בְּנֵי רְאוּבֵן בְּכֹר יִשְׂרָאֵל, חֲנוֹךְ וּפַלּוּא חֶצְרֹן וְכַרְמִי--אֵלֶּה, מִשְׁפְּחֹת רְאוּבֵן.
These are the heads of their fathers' houses. The sons of Reuben, Israel's firstborn: Hanoch and Pallu, Hezron and Carmi. These are the families of Reuben.note
15
וּבְנֵי שִׁמְעוֹן, יְמוּאֵל וְיָמִין וְאֹהַד וְיָכִין וְצֹחַר, וְשָׁאוּל, בֶּן-הַכְּנַעֲנִית; אֵלֶּה, מִשְׁפְּחֹת שִׁמְעוֹן.
And the sons of Simeon: Jemuel and Jamin and Ohad and Jachin and Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman. These are the families of Simeon.note
16
וְאֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹת בְּנֵי-לֵוִי, לְתֹלְדֹתָם--גֵּרְשׁוֹן, וּקְהָת וּמְרָרִי; וּשְׁנֵי חַיֵּי לֵוִי, שֶׁבַע וּשְׁלֹשִׁים וּמְאַת שָׁנָה.
And these are the names of the sons of Levi according to their generations: Gershon and Kohath and Merari. And the years of the life of Levi were a hundred and thirty-seven years.note
17
בְּנֵי גֵרְשׁוֹן לִבְנִי וְשִׁמְעִי, לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם.
The sons of Gershon: Libni and Shimei, according to their families.note
18
וּבְנֵי קְהָת--עַמְרָם וְיִצְהָר, וְחֶבְרוֹן וְעֻזִּיאֵל; וּשְׁנֵי חַיֵּי קְהָת, שָׁלֹשׁ וּשְׁלֹשִׁים וּמְאַת שָׁנָה.
And the sons of Kohath: Amram and Izhar and Hebron and Uzziel. And the years of the life of Kohath were a hundred and thirty-three years.note
19
וּבְנֵי מְרָרִי, מַחְלִי וּמוּשִׁי; אֵלֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹת הַלֵּוִי, לְתֹלְדֹתָם.
And the sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. These are the families of Levi according to their generations.note
20
וַיִּקַּח עַמְרָם אֶת-יוֹכֶבֶד דֹּדָתוֹ, לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה, וַתֵּלֶד לוֹ, אֶת-אַהֲרֹן וְאֶת-מֹשֶׁה; וּשְׁנֵי חַיֵּי עַמְרָם, שֶׁבַע וּשְׁלֹשִׁים וּמְאַת שָׁנָה.
And Amram took Jochebed his father's sister as his wife, and she bore him Aaron and Moses. And the years of the life of Amram were a hundred and thirty-seven years.note
21
וּבְנֵי, יִצְהָר--קֹרַח וָנֶפֶג, וְזִכְרִי.
And the sons of Izhar: Korah and Nepheg and Zichri.note
22
וּבְנֵי, עֻזִּיאֵל--מִישָׁאֵל וְאֶלְצָפָן, וְסִתְרִי.
And the sons of Uzziel: Mishael and Elzaphan and Sithri.note
23
וַיִּקַּח אַהֲרֹן אֶת-אֱלִישֶׁבַע בַּת-עַמִּינָדָב, אֲחוֹת נַחְשׁוֹן--לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה; וַתֵּלֶד לוֹ, אֶת-נָדָב וְאֶת-אֲבִיהוּא, אֶת-אֶלְעָזָר, וְאֶת-אִיתָמָר.
And Aaron took Elisheba, the daughter of Amminadab, the sister of Nahshon, as his wife; and she bore him Nadab and Abihu, Eleazar and Ithamar.note
24
וּבְנֵי קֹרַח, אַסִּיר וְאֶלְקָנָה וַאֲבִיאָסָף; אֵלֶּה, מִשְׁפְּחֹת הַקָּרְחִי.
And the sons of Korah: Assir and Elkanah and Abiasaph. These are the families of the Korahites.note
25
וְאֶלְעָזָר בֶּן-אַהֲרֹן לָקַח-לוֹ מִבְּנוֹת פּוּטִיאֵל, לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה, וַתֵּלֶד לוֹ, אֶת-פִּינְחָס; אֵלֶּה, רָאשֵׁי אֲבוֹת הַלְוִיִּם--לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם.
And Eleazar, Aaron's son, took for himself one of the daughters of Putiel as his wife; and she bore him Phinehas. These are the heads of the fathers' houses of the Levites according to their families.note
26
הוּא אַהֲרֹן, וּמֹשֶׁה--אֲשֶׁר אָמַר יְהוָה, לָהֶם, הוֹצִיאוּ אֶת-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, עַל-צִבְאֹתָם.
These are that Aaron and Moses to whom Jehovah said, "Bring out the sons of Israel from the land of Egypt by their hosts."note
27
הֵם, הַמְדַבְּרִים אֶל-פַּרְעֹה מֶלֶךְ-מִצְרַיִם, לְהוֹצִיא אֶת-בְּנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל, מִמִּצְרָיִם; הוּא מֹשֶׁה, וְאַהֲרֹן.
These are the ones who spoke to Pharaoh king of Egypt, to bring out the sons of Israel from Egypt — that Moses and Aaron.note
28
וַיְהִי, בְּיוֹם דִּבֶּר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה--בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם. {ס}
And it happened on the day when Jehovah spoke to Moses in the land of Egypt,note
29
וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר, אֲנִי יְהוָה; דַּבֵּר, אֶל-פַּרְעֹה מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם, אֵת כָּל-אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי, דֹּבֵר אֵלֶיךָ.
that Jehovah spoke to Moses, saying, "I am Jehovah; speak to Pharaoh king of Egypt all that I say to you."note
30
וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה, לִפְנֵי יְהוָה: הֵן אֲנִי, עֲרַל שְׂפָתַיִם, וְאֵיךְ, יִשְׁמַע אֵלַי פַּרְעֹה. {פ}
And Moses said before Jehovah, "Look, I am of uncircumcised lips; how then shall Pharaoh listen to me?"note

Translator’s Notes

Verses 1–8 · “I am Jehovah” — and the hardest verse about the Name

God answers Moses’ accusation not with a rebuke but with his Name. To “you have not delivered your people at all” (5:23) the reply is “NOW you shall see what I will do to Pharaoh” — twice “with a strong hand,” and the second time it is Pharaoh who will drive them out. The complaint is not scolded; it is answered.

⚠ The crux (v3): “I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob as God Almighty (El Shaddai); but by my name Jehovah I did not make myself known to them.” Yet Genesis has the patriarchs using the name freely — Jehovah tells Abram “I am Jehovah who brought you out of Ur” (15:7), and as early as Genesis 4:26 “men began to call on the name of Jehovah.” This is one of the most discussed verses in the Torah. Three readings, each with a real pedigree:

• The KNOWING reading — “made myself known” means known by EXPERIENCE, not merely pronounced. The patriarchs had the syllables; they never saw the covenant-keeping power the Name stands for. In Hebrew, to know a name regularly means to know the character it carries; what is new here is not the word but the acts about to prove it.

• The EDITORIAL reading — Genesis uses the Name proleptically: the narrator’s own word for God, written later, set in earlier mouths. (The same phenomenon as “Dan” in Genesis 14:14, centuries before the tribe was named, which this library flags in its own place.)

• The SOURCE reading — the standard critical account assigns this verse to the Priestly source, which holds the Name was first revealed to Moses, while another source uses it from creation onward. It is one of the pillars on which the Documentary Hypothesis was built.

The library prints the text and lays the readings out with their pedigrees; it does not cast a vote. KJV “but by my name JEHOVAH was I not known to them”; the ASV keeps “Jehovah” likewise.

The SEVEN “I WILL”s (vv6-8). The speech is framed “I am Jehovah” at both ends, and between them stand seven first-person promises: I will BRING OUT, I will DELIVER, I will REDEEM (with an outstretched arm), I will TAKE you to myself for a people, I will BE to you a God, I will BRING you into the land, I will GIVE it to you as a possession. It is the fullest statement of redemption in the Torah, and verse 7 is the covenant formula in one line: a people for me, a God for you. ⚠ The first four verbs are the traditional basis of the FOUR CUPS of the Passover seder — a Jewish reading of this verse that has shaped the Passover table for two millennia, and, through it, the cup Jesus lifted.

Verses 9–13 · they could not hear it

⚠ The most poignant verse in the chapter. Moses delivers the greatest promise in the book — and “they did not listen to Moses, from SHORTNESS OF SPIRIT (kotzer ruach) and from cruel bondage.” It is not quite unbelief; it is breathlessness. The crushed cannot take in good news. The oppression has done its deepest work: it has made them unable to hope, so that the word of deliverance arrives and finds no room. KJV “for anguish of spirit, and for cruel bondage.”

And Moses’ old objection returns in a new form: “I am of UNCIRCUMCISED LIPS” (aral sefatayim) — lips as if unfit, unconsecrated, blocked; the same image the Bible uses for an uncircumcised ear or heart. His logic is bleak and reasonable: if his own people will not hear him, why would Pharaoh? God’s answer is not an argument but a commission (v13).

Verses 14–27 · why a genealogy here?

The narrative stops dead for a family tree — and the shape of it tells you why. It begins dutifully with Reuben and Simeon, Jacob’s first two sons, and then arrives at LEVI and simply stays there: Kohath → Amram → Aaron and Moses, then Aaron’s sons and his grandson Phinehas. It never returns to the other nine tribes. This is not a census; it is a set of CREDENTIALS, inserted at the exact moment two men are about to stand before Pharaoh and, afterward, before Israel as prophet and priest. It establishes the line that will hold the high priesthood, and answers the question the story has raised: who are these men?

⚠ Two details the text records without embarrassment. Amram married “Jochebed his father’s sister” (v20) — an aunt-nephew marriage the later law will forbid outright (Leviticus 18:12). The narrator states it plainly, as he states Jacob’s marriage to two sisters, because the law had not yet been given. And Korah appears among the Kohathites (v21) — the same Korah who will lead the great rebellion of Numbers 16 — listed here, without comment, in the priestly family tree. The genealogy that certifies Moses and Aaron also quietly names the man who will challenge them.

Verses 28–30 · picking the thread back up

The last three verses repeat verses 10-12 almost word for word: Jehovah speaks, Moses objects with his uncircumcised lips. ⚠ This is not a doublet or a copyist’s error but a recognized Hebrew narrative device — a resumptive repetition, which picks the story back up at the point where the long genealogy interrupted it, the way a speaker restates his last sentence after an aside. The story now stands exactly where verse 13 left it, with the credentials established and the objection still on the table — and chapter 7 can open with God’s answer to it: “See, I have made you as God to Pharaoh.”

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the exact / distinct words: “I am Jehovah” kept as the frame at both ends of the speech (vv2, 8); the seven “I will” verbs kept separate and in order (bring out / deliver / redeem / take / be / bring in / give); “God Almighty” for El Shaddai, held against the Name in the same sentence; “shortness of spirit” for kotzer ruach, kept literal rather than smoothed to “discouragement”; “uncircumcised lips” for aral sefatayim; and “possession” for morashah, an inheritance held by grant.

Echoes paid. Moses’ accusation of 5:22-23 answered head-on with “NOW you shall see”; El Shaddai, the name given to Abram at Genesis 17:1; “I have remembered my covenant,” the same remembering that opened the book (2:24); and the promise of the land sworn to the three patriarchs, carried since Genesis 12.

Echoes planted. The seven “I will”s, whose first four became the four cups of the Passover seder; “I will take you to myself for a people, and I will be to you a God” — the covenant formula that will echo through the prophets to Revelation 21:3; Korah, listed here in the priestly line and waiting for Numbers 16; and Phinehas, the grandson whose zeal will win a covenant of peace.

Next in this book: Exodus 7 — “See, I have made you as God to Pharaoh, and Aaron your brother shall be your prophet”; the staff that becomes a serpent and swallows the magicians’; and the first plague, the Nile turned to blood.

Exodus 7 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה, רְאֵה נְתַתִּיךָ אֱלֹהִים לְפַרְעֹה; וְאַהֲרֹן אָחִיךָ, יִהְיֶה נְבִיאֶךָ.
And Jehovah said to Moses, "See, I have made you God to Pharaoh, and Aaron your brother shall be your prophet.note
2
אַתָּה תְדַבֵּר, אֵת כָּל-אֲשֶׁר אֲצַוֶּךָּ; וְאַהֲרֹן אָחִיךָ יְדַבֵּר אֶל-פַּרְעֹה, וְשִׁלַּח אֶת-בְּנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵאַרְצוֹ.
You shall speak all that I command you, and Aaron your brother shall speak to Pharaoh, that he send the sons of Israel out of his land.note
3
וַאֲנִי אַקְשֶׁה, אֶת-לֵב פַּרְעֹה; וְהִרְבֵּיתִי אֶת-אֹתֹתַי וְאֶת-מוֹפְתַי, בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם.
But I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and I will multiply my signs and my wonders in the land of Egypt.note
4
וְלֹא-יִשְׁמַע אֲלֵכֶם פַּרְעֹה, וְנָתַתִּי אֶת-יָדִי בְּמִצְרָיִם; וְהוֹצֵאתִי אֶת-צִבְאֹתַי אֶת-עַמִּי בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל, מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, בִּשְׁפָטִים, גְּדֹלִים.
And Pharaoh will not listen to you, and I will lay my hand on Egypt, and bring out my hosts, my people the sons of Israel, from the land of Egypt with great judgments.note
5
וְיָדְעוּ מִצְרַיִם כִּי-אֲנִי יְהוָה, בִּנְטֹתִי אֶת-יָדִי עַל-מִצְרָיִם; וְהוֹצֵאתִי אֶת-בְּנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל, מִתּוֹכָם.
And the Egyptians shall know that I am Jehovah, when I stretch out my hand over Egypt and bring out the sons of Israel from among them."note
6
וַיַּעַשׂ מֹשֶׁה, וְאַהֲרֹן--כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֹתָם, כֵּן עָשׂוּ.
And Moses and Aaron did as Jehovah commanded them; so they did.note
7
וּמֹשֶׁה, בֶּן-שְׁמֹנִים שָׁנָה, וְאַהֲרֹן, בֶּן-שָׁלֹשׁ וּשְׁמֹנִים שָׁנָה--בְּדַבְּרָם, אֶל-פַּרְעֹה. {פ}
And Moses was eighty years old and Aaron eighty-three years old when they spoke to Pharaoh.note
8
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה, אֶל-מֹשֶׁה וְאֶל-אַהֲרֹן לֵאמֹר.
And Jehovah said to Moses and to Aaron, saying,note
9
כִּי יְדַבֵּר אֲלֵכֶם פַּרְעֹה לֵאמֹר, תְּנוּ לָכֶם מוֹפֵת; וְאָמַרְתָּ אֶל-אַהֲרֹן, קַח אֶת-מַטְּךָ וְהַשְׁלֵךְ לִפְנֵי-פַרְעֹה--יְהִי לְתַנִּין.
"When Pharaoh speaks to you, saying, 'Give a wonder for yourselves,' then you shall say to Aaron, 'Take your staff and throw it down before Pharaoh' — let it become a serpent."note
10
וַיָּבֹא מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן, אֶל-פַּרְעֹה, וַיַּעֲשׂוּ כֵן, כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה; וַיַּשְׁלֵךְ אַהֲרֹן אֶת-מַטֵּהוּ, לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה וְלִפְנֵי עֲבָדָיו--וַיְהִי לְתַנִּין.
And Moses and Aaron went in to Pharaoh and did just as Jehovah had commanded; and Aaron threw down his staff before Pharaoh and before his servants, and it became a serpent.note
11
וַיִּקְרָא, גַּם-פַּרְעֹה, לַחֲכָמִים, וְלַמְכַשְּׁפִים; וַיַּעֲשׂוּ גַם-הֵם חַרְטֻמֵּי מִצְרַיִם, בְּלַהֲטֵיהֶם--כֵּן.
Then Pharaoh also called the wise men and the sorcerers; and they too, the magicians of Egypt, did the same with their secret arts.note
12
וַיַּשְׁלִיכוּ אִישׁ מַטֵּהוּ, וַיִּהְיוּ לְתַנִּינִם; וַיִּבְלַע מַטֵּה-אַהֲרֹן, אֶת-מַטֹּתָם.
And each threw down his staff, and they became serpents; but Aaron's staff swallowed up their staffs.note
13
וַיֶּחֱזַק לֵב פַּרְעֹה, וְלֹא שָׁמַע אֲלֵהֶם: כַּאֲשֶׁר, דִּבֶּר יְהוָה. {ס}
And Pharaoh's heart was hardened, and he did not listen to them, as Jehovah had said.note
14
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה, כָּבֵד לֵב פַּרְעֹה; מֵאֵן, לְשַׁלַּח הָעָם.
And Jehovah said to Moses, "Pharaoh's heart is heavy; he refuses to let the people go.note
15
לֵךְ אֶל-פַּרְעֹה בַּבֹּקֶר, הִנֵּה יֹצֵא הַמַּיְמָה, וְנִצַּבְתָּ לִקְרָאתוֹ, עַל-שְׂפַת הַיְאֹר; וְהַמַּטֶּה אֲשֶׁר-נֶהְפַּךְ לְנָחָשׁ, תִּקַּח בְּיָדֶךָ.
Go to Pharaoh in the morning — look, he is going out to the water — and stand to meet him on the bank of the Nile, and take in your hand the staff that was turned into a serpent.note
16
וְאָמַרְתָּ אֵלָיו, יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הָעִבְרִים שְׁלָחַנִי אֵלֶיךָ לֵאמֹר, שַׁלַּח אֶת-עַמִּי, וְיַעַבְדֻנִי בַּמִּדְבָּר; וְהִנֵּה לֹא-שָׁמַעְתָּ, עַד-כֹּה.
And you shall say to him, 'Jehovah, the God of the Hebrews, sent me to you, saying: Let my people go, that they may serve me in the wilderness. But look, you have not listened until now.note
17
כֹּה, אָמַר יְהוָה, בְּזֹאת תֵּדַע, כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה: הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי מַכֶּה בַּמַּטֶּה אֲשֶׁר-בְּיָדִי, עַל-הַמַּיִם אֲשֶׁר בַּיְאֹר--וְנֶהֶפְכוּ לְדָם.
Thus says Jehovah: By this you shall know that I am Jehovah. Look, I am striking with the staff that is in my hand upon the water that is in the Nile, and it shall be turned to blood.note
18
וְהַדָּגָה אֲשֶׁר-בַּיְאֹר תָּמוּת, וּבָאַשׁ הַיְאֹר; וְנִלְאוּ מִצְרַיִם, לִשְׁתּוֹת מַיִם מִן-הַיְאֹר. {ס}
And the fish that are in the Nile shall die, and the Nile shall stink, and the Egyptians shall be unable to drink water from the Nile.'"note
19
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה, אֱמֹר אֶל-אַהֲרֹן קַח מַטְּךָ וּנְטֵה-יָדְךָ עַל-מֵימֵי מִצְרַיִם עַל-נַהֲרֹתָם עַל-יְאֹרֵיהֶם וְעַל-אַגְמֵיהֶם וְעַל כָּל-מִקְוֵה מֵימֵיהֶם--וְיִהְיוּ-דָם; וְהָיָה דָם בְּכָל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, וּבָעֵצִים וּבָאֲבָנִים.
And Jehovah said to Moses, "Say to Aaron: Take your staff and stretch out your hand over the waters of Egypt — over their rivers, over their canals, over their pools, and over all their reservoirs of water — that they may become blood; and there shall be blood throughout all the land of Egypt, both in vessels of wood and in vessels of stone."note
20
וַיַּעֲשׂוּ-כֵן מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה, וַיָּרֶם בַּמַּטֶּה וַיַּךְ אֶת-הַמַּיִם אֲשֶׁר בַּיְאֹר, לְעֵינֵי פַרְעֹה, וּלְעֵינֵי עֲבָדָיו; וַיֵּהָפְכוּ כָּל-הַמַּיִם אֲשֶׁר-בַּיְאֹר, לְדָם.
And Moses and Aaron did so, just as Jehovah commanded; and he lifted the staff and struck the water that was in the Nile before the eyes of Pharaoh and before the eyes of his servants, and all the water that was in the Nile was turned to blood.note
21
וְהַדָּגָה אֲשֶׁר-בַּיְאֹר מֵתָה, וַיִּבְאַשׁ הַיְאֹר, וְלֹא-יָכְלוּ מִצְרַיִם, לִשְׁתּוֹת מַיִם מִן-הַיְאֹר; וַיְהִי הַדָּם, בְּכָל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם.
And the fish that were in the Nile died, and the Nile stank, and the Egyptians could not drink water from the Nile; and the blood was throughout all the land of Egypt.note
22
וַיַּעֲשׂוּ-כֵן חַרְטֻמֵּי מִצְרַיִם, בְּלָטֵיהֶם; וַיֶּחֱזַק לֵב-פַּרְעֹה וְלֹא-שָׁמַע אֲלֵהֶם, כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה.
But the magicians of Egypt did the same with their secret arts; and Pharaoh's heart was hardened, and he did not listen to them, as Jehovah had said.note
23
וַיִּפֶן פַּרְעֹה, וַיָּבֹא אֶל-בֵּיתוֹ; וְלֹא-שָׁת לִבּוֹ, גַּם-לָזֹאת.
And Pharaoh turned and went into his house, and he did not set his heart to this either.note
24
וַיַּחְפְּרוּ כָל-מִצְרַיִם סְבִיבֹת הַיְאֹר, מַיִם לִשְׁתּוֹת: כִּי לֹא יָכְלוּ לִשְׁתֹּת, מִמֵּימֵי הַיְאֹר.
And all the Egyptians dug around the Nile for water to drink, for they could not drink from the water of the Nile.note
25
וַיִּמָּלֵא, שִׁבְעַת יָמִים, אַחֲרֵי הַכּוֹת-יְהוָה, אֶת-הַיְאֹר. {פ}
And seven days were completed after Jehovah struck the Nile.note
26
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה, בֹּא אֶל-פַּרְעֹה; וְאָמַרְתָּ אֵלָיו, כֹּה אָמַר יְהוָה, שַׁלַּח אֶת-עַמִּי, וְיַעַבְדֻנִי.
And Jehovah said to Moses, "Go in to Pharaoh and say to him, 'Thus says Jehovah: Let my people go, that they may serve me.note
27
וְאִם-מָאֵן אַתָּה, לְשַׁלֵּחַ: הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי, נֹגֵף אֶת-כָּל-גְּבוּלְךָ--בַּצְפַרְדְּעִים.
But if you refuse to let them go, look, I am plaguing all your territory with frogs.note
28
וְשָׁרַץ הַיְאֹר, צְפַרְדְּעִים, וְעָלוּ וּבָאוּ בְּבֵיתֶךָ, וּבַחֲדַר מִשְׁכָּבְךָ וְעַל-מִטָּתֶךָ; וּבְבֵית עֲבָדֶיךָ וּבְעַמֶּךָ, וּבְתַנּוּרֶיךָ וּבְמִשְׁאֲרוֹתֶיךָ.
And the Nile shall swarm with frogs, and they shall come up and go into your house, and into your bedroom, and onto your bed, and into the house of your servants, and among your people, and into your ovens and into your kneading bowls.note
29
וּבְכָה וּבְעַמְּךָ, וּבְכָל-עֲבָדֶיךָ--יַעֲלוּ, הַצְפַרְדְּעִים.
And upon you and upon your people and upon all your servants the frogs shall come up.'"note

Translator’s Notes

Verses 1–7 · as God to Pharaoh

«I have made you God to Pharaoh, and Aaron your brother shall be your prophet.» The arrangement of 4:16 — Moses “as God” to Aaron — is now aimed at the throne. And in doing so the verse hands us the Bible’s clearest working definition of a prophet (navi): not primarily a predictor but a MOUTH — the one who says another’s words. Aaron will be to Moses exactly what a prophet is to God. KJV “I have made thee a god to Pharaoh.”

⚠ The hardening, and its purpose. “I will harden Pharaoh’s heart… and the Egyptians shall KNOW that I am Jehovah” (vv3, 5). That second clause is the direct answer to Pharaoh’s sneer two chapters ago — “I do not know Jehovah” (5:2). Egypt is going to learn the Name, and learn it the hard way; the plagues are not merely punishments but a revelation aimed at a man who said the God of the Hebrews was not on his lists.

Two old men. Moses is eighty and Aaron eighty-three when they walk into that court (v7). The deliverance of a nation begins with a pair of octogenarians — and Moses’ life divides into three forties: forty years in Egypt, forty in Midian, forty in the wilderness ahead.

Verses 8–13 · the staff that swallowed

⚠ Watch the word change. In chapter 4 the staff became a NACHASH, a snake. Here, before Pharaoh, it becomes a TANNIN — a bigger word: serpent, dragon, sea-monster, the same term as the great sea creatures of Genesis 1:21. The escalation is deliberate, and it is pointedly Egyptian: the cobra (the uraeus) reared on Pharaoh’s crown as the very emblem of his power, and Ezekiel will one day call Pharaoh himself “the great TANNIN lying in the midst of his rivers” (29:3). Aaron’s staff swallowing theirs is not a conjuring trick; it is the whole book in one image.

The magicians did the same. The chartummim matched the sign “with their secret arts,” and ⚠ the text does not explain how. The honest reading resists both easy exits: Scripture neither exposes them as frauds nor credits them with power of their own. It simply reports the outcome — whatever they managed, it was swallowed. KJV “with their enchantments.”

Verses 14–25 · the Nile turned to blood

The first plague strikes the Nile — and in Egypt that is not merely a river. The Nile WAS Egypt: its flood laid down the soil, its water was the life of the whole kingdom, and it was itself divine — HAPI, god of the inundation, was worshiped as the giver of all abundance, and the river was bound up with Osiris besides. ⚠ The plagues are consistently aimed at Egypt’s gods — Exodus says so outright at 12:12, “against all the gods of Egypt I will execute judgments” — and this is the opening shot: the god of the Nile turned to blood and made to stink.

⚠ And a grimmer justice runs underneath it. This is the river into which Pharaoh commanded that every Hebrew boy be thrown (1:22). The water that swallowed Israel’s sons now runs with blood, in front of the king who gave the order. The text never says it; it simply strikes that river first.

⚠ Two different heart-verbs. Verse 14 says Pharaoh’s heart is kaved — HEAVY, weighed down — while verses 13 and 22 use chazaq, STRONG, unyielding. (A third, qashah, “stubborn,” joins them later.) Most English versions flatten all of them to “hardened,” which loses the fact that Exodus is describing the same refusal from several angles — a heart heavy as a stone, a heart braced like a wall. KJV “Pharaoh’s heart is hardened” for both; the ESV distinguishes them, reading “Pharaoh’s heart is hardened” here but “heavy” in its notes.

«By this you shall KNOW that I am Jehovah» (v17) — the refrain begins here and will toll through all ten plagues.

The magicians match it again (v22), which — as readers have long noticed — only makes matters worse: their skill can ADD blood; it cannot take any away. And Pharaoh “did not set his heart even to this” but turned and went home, while all Egypt dug holes beside the river looking for water. Seven days.

Verses 26–29 · and the frogs are announced

⚠ A versification note. In the Hebrew Masoretic text, chapter 7 runs to 29 verses and closes with the announcement of the second plague. Most English Bibles end chapter 7 at verse 25 and print these four verses as 8:1-4. This translation follows the Masoretic numbering throughout, so Hebrew 7:26-29 = English 8:1-4. Nothing is missing and nothing is added; the chapter division simply falls in a different place. The KJV, like nearly every English Bible, prints these four verses as 8:1-4. (The same kind of offset appears elsewhere — Genesis 32, for instance, where the Hebrew runs a verse ahead of the English.)

The threat itself is domestic, comic, and horrifying at once: frogs in the house, in the bedroom, ON THE BED, in the servants’ quarters, in the ovens and the kneading bowls. Nowhere to step, nothing to eat that has not been crawled over. And the target is a god again: Heqet, the frog-headed goddess of birth and fertility, whose sacred creature becomes unbearable by sheer multiplication. The second plague does not import a foreign horror into Egypt; it takes something Egypt called holy and gives them far too much of it.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the exact / distinct words: “God to Pharaoh” and “your prophet” kept literal, since together they define what a navi is; “serpent” for tannin held DISTINCT from the nachash of chapter 4 (a deliberate escalation, and Pharaoh’s own emblem); “secret arts” for the magicians’ craft, left unexplained as the text leaves it; “heavy” and “hardened” kept as the different heart-verbs they are; and the Masoretic verse numbering kept, so this chapter runs to 29.

Echoes paid. “The Egyptians shall know that I am Jehovah” answering Pharaoh’s “I do not know Jehovah” (5:2); the staff-sign first given at the bush (4:2-5); the hardening promised at 4:21; and — unstated but unmistakable — the Nile of 1:22, into which Hebrew sons were thrown, struck first and turned to blood.

Echoes planted. The refrain “that you may know that I am Jehovah,” which will toll through all ten plagues; the plagues as judgment on the gods of Egypt, which Exodus will state outright at 12:12; the magicians, who match two signs, fail at the third, and are themselves struck by the sixth; and Aaron’s swallowing staff, the first picture of what the sea will do to Pharaoh’s army.
