«That soul shall be CUT OFF from Israel.» The penalty (karet) is severe and its exact force is debated in the tradition itself — expulsion from the community, childlessness, or a divine rather than judicial punishment. What is clear is the logic: a member of a redeemed people who will not keep the sign of the redemption removes himself from it. KJV “that soul shall be cut off from Israel”; the NIV “must be cut off from Israel.”

⚠ And already, in verse 19, the door is open: the rule binds “the SOJOURNER and the native of the land” alike. The founding festival of Israel is legislated from the first day with the resident foreigner inside its frame — a thread this chapter picks up again at its very end (vv48-49). KJV “whether he be a stranger, or born in the land”; the ESV “whether he is a sojourner or a native.”

Verses 21–28 · hyssop, the destroyer, and a child’s question

Moses passes the instructions to the elders, and adds the practical detail the divine speech did not: a bunch of hyssop as the brush, the blood held in a basin — though the word saf can equally mean the THRESHOLD, so that some have read the blood as caught at the doorway itself rather than in a bowl (KJV “the bason”; the NWT the same, with the alternative noted) — and an order that sounds small and is not — nobody goes outside until morning. The households are shut in with the mark on the door and the night outside. Hyssop reappears wherever blood or water is applied for cleansing (Leviticus 14; Numbers 19; Psalm 51:7, “purge me with hyssop”), and at the cross a sponge is lifted on a hyssop stalk (John 19:29).

⚠ «He will not allow THE DESTROYER to come into your houses.» Verse 23 introduces a destroyer (ha-mashchit) — and the chapter does not harmonize this with verse 12, where Jehovah says “I will pass through… I will strike.” Both are left standing: God himself strikes, and a destroyer is restrained at the door. Ancient and modern readers have taken it as a distinct agent (compare the destroying angel of 2 Samuel 24:16), as a personification of the plague, or as a way of speaking about God’s own act at one remove. The library reports the double statement and does not resolve it. KJV and the ESV both read “the destroyer”; the NWT renders it “the ruination.”

⚠ «And when your sons say to you, what does this service mean to you?» There it is again — and this is the second time in three chapters. At 10:2 the plagues were said to exist so that a father could RECOUNT them to a son and a grandson; here the rite is built around the child’s question, with the answer scripted into the law itself. This verse is the direct ancestor of the Four Questions of the Passover seder, and the reason that meal is conducted as an interrogation rather than a lecture. The instruction to explain is older than the event being explained.

“And the people bowed low and prostrated themselves” — the first act of worship in the book by the whole people, and it comes before anything has been delivered. Then, quietly: “so they did.”

Verses 29–36 · midnight, and a king asking for a blessing

It happens exactly as announced, at the hour named in 11:4 — and the roll of the dead repeats the merism of 11:5 with one change: there the bottom of the range was the slave girl at the millstones, here it is “the firstborn of the CAPTIVE who was in the dungeon.” Even the prisoners of Egypt are counted as Egypt.

⚠ And there is a tomb. On the early-date chronology this translation follows, the pharaoh of the exodus is Amenhotep II — who, Scripture being careful that the ARMY drowned and not the king (14:28), was buried in the Valley of the Kings. Buried with him in KV35 is a son, WEBENSENU, whose organs were found in canopic jars inscribed with his name: a son who demonstrably died before his father did. It is suggestive rather than probative — nothing establishes that he was the crown prince or how he died — and Joel Kramer, who makes the case, is careful to say he does not want to make too much of it, “but I also don’t want to make too little of it either.” The encyclopedia entry carries his film on the tomb.

«There was no house where there was not someone dead.» The narration is flat and short; the book spends no words on the scene. And the sound that fills it is the great OUTCRY promised in 11:6 — the same word this book opened with when Israel cried out under the bondage (2:23).

⚠ Pharaoh’s surrender is total, and its last three words are the most surprising in the chapter. He summons them at night, concedes everything he has spent five chapters withholding — the people, the children, the flocks and the herds — and then says: “AND BLESS ME ALSO.” The man who opened this contest with “Who is Jehovah, that I should obey his voice? I do not know Jehovah” (5:2) ends it asking the God he did not know for a blessing.

“We are all dying!” The Egyptians press Israel out — the same verb-root that has described Pharaoh’s hardened HOLDING ON now describes Egypt pressing them to GO. The expulsion promised in 11:1 arrives.

⚠ And the asking is fulfilled (vv35-36): they ask, the Egyptians give, “and they STRIPPED Egypt.” The verb (natzal) is the plunder-word, and it is the same root used all through the book for God DELIVERING his people — the rescue and the spoil named by one verb. KJV “they spoiled the Egyptians”; the ESV and NIV “plundered.” What Abraham was promised in Genesis 15:14 is now in their hands, and what it will become is a golden calf and a tabernacle.

Verses 37–42 · the numbers, the mixed multitude, and a night of watching

⚠ «About six hundred thousand on foot — the men — besides the little ones.» Read plainly this implies a nation of roughly two million people, and it is one of the most discussed figures in the Bible, because the logistics in the Sinai are formidable and the same books elsewhere describe Israel as small (Deuteronomy 7:7). The readings, each with a real pedigree: (1) the plain sense — the figure is what it says, and the wilderness provision is precisely the point the narrative keeps making; (2) the elef reading — the Hebrew word for “thousand” (elef) also means a CLAN or a military unit, so “600 elef” could be 600 fighting units of unstated size; the same ambiguity turns up in Judges and 1 Samuel; (3) a later census figure carried back into the exodus account. The library lays them out and takes no vote — but the elef reading is a real feature of the language, not a modern escape hatch.

⚠ «And a great MIXED MULTITUDE also went up with them.» This is one of the quietest and most important sentences in Exodus. Erev rav means a mingled crowd — non-Israelites who walked out of Egypt with Israel. Who were they? The book has already told us: at the hail, “the one who FEARED the word of Jehovah among the servants of Pharaoh” brought his household indoors (9:20). Egypt was never a solid block; some of it believed, and some of it left. KJV “a mixed multitude”; the ESV “a mixed multitude”; the NIV flattens it to “many other people,” losing the mingling the Hebrew names. Israel, from its first hour as a free people, is not ethnically pure — which is exactly why the chapter ends by legislating for the foreigner who wants in.

⚠ «Four hundred and thirty years.» Another old crux, because Genesis 15:13 told Abraham four hundred, and Paul, at Galatians 3:17, counts the 430 from the PROMISE to the law — which would place the patriarchs inside the span. The Greek Old Testament and the Samaritan Pentateuch resolve it in the text itself, reading “in Egypt AND IN CANAAN”; the Hebrew here says simply “in Egypt.” Both totals are standing witnesses and both are old; this translation follows the Hebrew and notes the variant rather than importing it. KJV “the sojourning of the children of Israel, who dwelt in Egypt, was four hundred and thirty years”; the NWT reads the same and footnotes the Greek and Samaritan expansion.

“On that very day” — twice (vv41, 51), and once more at v17. The exodus is dated as precisely as a legal instrument.

⚠ «A NIGHT OF WATCHING for Jehovah.» The Hebrew (leil shimmurim) works in both directions and the verse says both: it is a night Jehovah KEPT WATCH to bring them out, and a night to BE KEPT by all Israel through their generations. God’s vigil becomes the people’s vigil — and Jewish tradition has kept it as a night of staying up ever since. KJV “a night to be much observed”; the ESV “a night of watching.”

Verses 43–51 · one law

The chapter closes by drawing the boundary of the meal — and the surprise is where it falls. Not around a bloodline. The foreigner’s son may not eat; but a slave bought with money MAY, once circumcised, and so may any sojourner who takes the covenant sign on himself and his household. ⚠ The line is drawn around the COVENANT, not around ancestry — which is why the mixed multitude of verse 38 is not a problem the text has to explain away. It was legislated for on the same night.

«ONE LAW shall there be for the native and for the sojourner who sojourns among you.» This is stated at the founding moment of the nation, before Sinai, before any of the rest of the legislation — and it is repeated through the Torah (Leviticus 24:22; Numbers 15:15-16). Whatever else Israel is being made into, it is not being made into a closed people.

⚠ «You shall not break a bone of it.» A rule about butchering — the lamb is kept whole, eaten in one house, nothing carried out. It becomes one of the New Testament’s most exact citations: at the crucifixion the soldiers break the legs of the two men beside Jesus and find him already dead, and John writes, “these things happened so that the Scripture might be fulfilled: NOT A BONE OF HIM SHALL BE BROKEN” (19:33-36), quoting this verse (with Psalm 34:20 behind it). KJV “neither shall ye break a bone thereof”; the ESV “you shall not break any of its bones.” Paul makes the identification directly: “Christ our PASSOVER has been sacrificed” (1 Corinthians 5:7).

And the book’s longest chapter ends with a sentence it has been trying to write since chapter 1: “on that very day Jehovah brought the sons of Israel out from the land of Egypt by their armies.” Not “he did not send them” — which is how every chapter since the fifth has ended — but OUT.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the exact / distinct words: “Passover” for pesach with its three old senses flagged rather than decided; “a lamb” for seh, noting the Hebrew covers a kid as well; “between the two evenings” left as the idiom it is; “the destroyer” kept alongside “I will strike,” unharmonized; “a great mixed multitude” for erev rav, not softened to “many other people”; “six hundred thousand” printed as the Hebrew reads, with the elef ambiguity noted; and “a night of watching” for leil shimmurim, which the verse itself uses in both directions.

Echoes paid. The sentence of 4:22-23, executed at midnight; the outcry of 2:23, now Egypt’s (11:6; 12:30); the silver and gold promised to Abraham (Genesis 15:14), asked at 11:2 and handed over here; the Egyptians who feared the word at the hail (9:20), walking out in the mixed multitude; the telling to a child commanded at 10:2, now built into the rite; the month the ripening barley of 9:31 was counting toward; and Pharaoh’s “I do not know Jehovah” (5:2), ending in “bless me also.”

Echoes planted. The lamb without blemish and the unbroken bone, which John will quote at a crucifixion (19:36) and Paul will name outright (1 Corinthians 5:7); the unleavened bread of a Passover table (Luke 22:19); the leaven that spreads through a whole lump, for good and ill; the firstborn, who from this night belong to Jehovah (13:2); and “one law for the native and the sojourner,” stated before Sinai and repeated through the Torah.

Next in this book: Exodus 13 — the firstborn claimed, the unleavened bread explained to a son a second time, the bones of Joseph carried out on an oath four hundred years old, and a pillar of cloud and fire that goes before them by day and by night.

Exodus 13 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה, אֶל-מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר.
And Jehovah spoke to Moses, saying,note
2
קַדֶּשׁ-לִי כָל-בְּכוֹר פֶּטֶר כָּל-רֶחֶם, בִּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל--בָּאָדָם, וּבַבְּהֵמָה: לִי, הוּא.
“Consecrate to me every firstborn, whatever opens the womb among the sons of Israel, among man and among beast; it is mine.”note
3
וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל-הָעָם, זָכוֹר אֶת-הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר יְצָאתֶם מִמִּצְרַיִם מִבֵּית עֲבָדִים, כִּי בְּחֹזֶק יָד, הוֹצִיא יְהוָה אֶתְכֶם מִזֶּה; וְלֹא יֵאָכֵל, חָמֵץ.
And Moses said to the people, “Remember this day, on which you went out from Egypt, from the house of slaves, for by strength of hand Jehovah brought you out from here; and nothing leavened shall be eaten.note
4
הַיּוֹם, אַתֶּם יֹצְאִים, בְּחֹדֶשׁ, הָאָבִיב.
Today you are going out, in the month of Abib.note
5
וְהָיָה כִי-יְבִיאֲךָ יְהוָה אֶל-אֶרֶץ הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְהַחִתִּי וְהָאֱמֹרִי וְהַחִוִּי וְהַיְבוּסִי, אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּע לַאֲבֹתֶיךָ לָתֶת לָךְ, אֶרֶץ זָבַת חָלָב, וּדְבָשׁ; וְעָבַדְתָּ אֶת-הָעֲבֹדָה הַזֹּאת, בַּחֹדֶשׁ הַזֶּה.
And when Jehovah brings you into the land of the Canaanite and the Hittite and the Amorite and the Hivite and the Jebusite, which he swore to your fathers to give you, a land flowing with milk and honey, then you shall perform this service in this month.note
6
שִׁבְעַת יָמִים, תֹּאכַל מַצֹּת; וּבַיּוֹם, הַשְּׁבִיעִי, חַג, לַיהוָה.
Seven days you shall eat unleavened bread, and on the seventh day there shall be a festival to Jehovah.note
7
מַצּוֹת, יֵאָכֵל, אֵת, שִׁבְעַת הַיָּמִים; וְלֹא-יֵרָאֶה לְךָ חָמֵץ, וְלֹא-יֵרָאֶה לְךָ שְׂאֹר--בְּכָל-גְּבֻלֶךָ.
Unleavened bread shall be eaten the seven days; and nothing leavened shall be seen with you, and no leaven shall be seen with you, within all your borders.note
8
וְהִגַּדְתָּ לְבִנְךָ, בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא לֵאמֹר: בַּעֲבוּר זֶה, עָשָׂה יְהוָה לִי, בְּצֵאתִי, מִמִּצְרָיִם.
And you shall tell your son on that day, saying, ‘It is because of what Jehovah did for me when I came out of Egypt.’note
9
וְהָיָה לְךָ לְאוֹת עַל-יָדְךָ, וּלְזִכָּרוֹן בֵּין עֵינֶיךָ, לְמַעַן תִּהְיֶה תּוֹרַת יְהוָה, בְּפִיךָ: כִּי בְּיָד חֲזָקָה, הוֹצִאֲךָ יְהוָה מִמִּצְרָיִם.
And it shall be for you a sign on your hand and a memorial between your eyes, so that the law of Jehovah may be in your mouth; for with a strong hand Jehovah brought you out of Egypt.note
10
וְשָׁמַרְתָּ אֶת-הַחֻקָּה הַזֹּאת, לְמוֹעֲדָהּ, מִיָּמִים, יָמִימָה. {פ}
And you shall keep this statute at its appointed time, from year to year.note
11
וְהָיָה כִּי-יְבִאֲךָ יְהוָה, אֶל-אֶרֶץ הַכְּנַעֲנִי, כַּאֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּע לְךָ, וְלַאֲבֹתֶיךָ; וּנְתָנָהּ, לָךְ.
And when Jehovah brings you into the land of the Canaanite, as he swore to you and to your fathers, and gives it to you,note
12
וְהַעֲבַרְתָּ כָל-פֶּטֶר-רֶחֶם, לַיהוָה; וְכָל-פֶּטֶר שֶׁגֶר בְּהֵמָה, אֲשֶׁר יִהְיֶה לְךָ הַזְּכָרִים--לַיהוָה.
then you shall set apart to Jehovah everything that opens the womb; and every firstling that is dropped of an animal that belongs to you — the males shall be Jehovah’s.note
13
וְכָל-פֶּטֶר חֲמֹר תִּפְדֶּה בְשֶׂה, וְאִם-לֹא תִפְדֶּה וַעֲרַפְתּוֹ; וְכֹל בְּכוֹר אָדָם בְּבָנֶיךָ, תִּפְדֶּה.
And every firstling of a donkey you shall redeem with a lamb, and if you will not redeem it, then you shall break its neck; and every firstborn of man among your sons you shall redeem.note
14
וְהָיָה כִּי-יִשְׁאָלְךָ בִנְךָ, מָחָר--לֵאמֹר מַה-זֹּאת: וְאָמַרְתָּ אֵלָיו--בְּחֹזֶק יָד הוֹצִיאָנוּ יְהוָה מִמִּצְרַיִם, מִבֵּית עֲבָדִים.
And when your son asks you in time to come, saying, ‘What is this?’ then you shall say to him, ‘By strength of hand Jehovah brought us out of Egypt, from the house of slaves.note
15
וַיְהִי, כִּי-הִקְשָׁה פַרְעֹה לְשַׁלְּחֵנוּ, וַיַּהֲרֹג יְהוָה כָּל-בְּכוֹר בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, מִבְּכֹר אָדָם וְעַד-בְּכוֹר בְּהֵמָה; עַל-כֵּן אֲנִי זֹבֵחַ לַיהוָה, כָּל-פֶּטֶר רֶחֶם הַזְּכָרִים, וְכָל-בְּכוֹר בָּנַי, אֶפְדֶּה.
And it came about, when Pharaoh stubbornly refused to send us away, that Jehovah killed every firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of man to the firstborn of beast. That is why I sacrifice to Jehovah every male that opens the womb, and every firstborn of my sons I redeem.’note
16
וְהָיָה לְאוֹת עַל-יָדְכָה, וּלְטוֹטָפֹת בֵּין עֵינֶיךָ: כִּי בְּחֹזֶק יָד, הוֹצִיאָנוּ יְהוָה מִמִּצְרָיִם. {ס}
And it shall be for a sign on your hand and for frontlets between your eyes, for by strength of hand Jehovah brought us out of Egypt.”note
17
וַיְהִי, בְּשַׁלַּח פַּרְעֹה אֶת-הָעָם, וְלֹא-נָחָם אֱלֹהִים דֶּרֶךְ אֶרֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּים, כִּי קָרוֹב הוּא: כִּי אָמַר אֱלֹהִים, פֶּן-יִנָּחֵם הָעָם בִּרְאֹתָם מִלְחָמָה--וְשָׁבוּ מִצְרָיְמָה.
And it came about, when Pharaoh sent the people away, that God did not lead them by way of the land of the Philistines, though it was near; for God said, “Lest the people change their minds when they see war, and return to Egypt.”note
18
וַיַּסֵּב אֱלֹהִים אֶת-הָעָם דֶּרֶךְ הַמִּדְבָּר, יַם-סוּף; וַחֲמֻשִׁים עָלוּ בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל, מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם.
So God led the people around by the way of the wilderness of the Sea of Reeds; and the sons of Israel went up in battle order out of the land of Egypt.note
19
וַיִּקַּח מֹשֶׁה אֶת-עַצְמוֹת יוֹסֵף, עִמּוֹ: כִּי הַשְׁבֵּעַ הִשְׁבִּיעַ אֶת-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, לֵאמֹר, פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד אֱלֹהִים אֶתְכֶם, וְהַעֲלִיתֶם אֶת-עַצְמֹתַי מִזֶּה אִתְּכֶם.
And Moses took the bones of Joseph with him, for he had solemnly made the sons of Israel swear, saying, “God will surely visit you, and you shall carry up my bones from here with you.”note
20
וַיִּסְעוּ, מִסֻּכֹּת; וַיַּחֲנוּ בְאֵתָם, בִּקְצֵה הַמִּדְבָּר.
And they journeyed from Succoth and camped at Etham, at the edge of the wilderness.note
21
וַיהוָה הֹלֵךְ לִפְנֵיהֶם יוֹמָם בְּעַמּוּד עָנָן, לַנְחֹתָם הַדֶּרֶךְ, וְלַיְלָה בְּעַמּוּד אֵשׁ, לְהָאִיר לָהֶם--לָלֶכֶת, יוֹמָם וָלָיְלָה.
And Jehovah went before them by day in a pillar of cloud to lead them on the way, and by night in a pillar of fire to give them light, so that they might travel by day and by night.note
22
לֹא-יָמִישׁ עַמּוּד הֶעָנָן, יוֹמָם, וְעַמּוּד הָאֵשׁ, לָיְלָה--לִפְנֵי, הָעָם. {פ}
The pillar of cloud did not depart by day, nor the pillar of fire by night, from before the people.note

Translator’s Notes

Verses 1–10 · the firstborn claimed, and a son told

⚠ The claim follows directly from the night before. Every firstborn in Egypt died; Israel’s did not — therefore Israel’s firstborn are not simply their parents’. “Consecrate to me every firstborn… IT IS MINE.” The logic is a purchase: what was spared belongs to the one who spared it. That claim will later be transferred wholesale onto the tribe of Levi, taken “instead of every firstborn” (Numbers 3:12-13), which is how a priesthood ends up being founded on this night.

“From the house of slaves.” The phrase (beit avadim) becomes one of the Bible’s standing names for Egypt, and it is worth hearing what it does: it names the place not by its power — the greatest kingdom on earth — but by what it did to them. KJV “the house of bondage.”

The month is named: ABIB, the month of the ripening ear — the same word the hail chapter used of the barley it destroyed (9:31). The agricultural detail dropped there quietly set this calendar.

⚠ «And you shall TELL YOUR SON on that day.» This is the third time in four chapters. At 10:2 the plagues existed so a father could recount them to a son and a grandson; at 12:26 the rite was built around the child’s question; here the telling is commanded outright, and it will come a fourth time at verse 14. Four tellings in four chapters — the drumbeat is deliberate. A people is being made, and the mechanism is a conversation across generations.

⚠ «A sign on your hand and a memorial between your eyes.» Read literally, this and verse 16 are the basis of TEFILLIN — the small boxes of scripture bound to arm and forehead in Jewish practice to this day (the “phylacteries” of Matthew 23:5). Read figuratively, it is an idiom for keeping something before you in what you do and what you think, like Proverbs’ “bind them on your fingers, write them on the tablet of your heart” (7:3). Both readings are ancient and the text supports both; note that the purpose clause here is verbal — “so that the law of Jehovah may be IN YOUR MOUTH.” KJV “for a memorial between thine eyes”; the NIV “a reminder on your forehead.”

Verses 11–16 · a donkey, a lamb, and a broken neck

“You shall SET APART to Jehovah everything that opens the womb.” The verb is ha’avarta, literally to cause to PASS OVER — the same root as the passing over of the destroying night (12:12). What was passed over is now passed across, into God’s possession.

⚠ Then a strange and revealing law. A firstborn ox or sheep is sacrificed; but the DONKEY is unclean and cannot be. So it must be REDEEMED — bought back by substituting a lamb — and if its owner will not pay that price, its neck is broken. The animal does not simply revert to ordinary use. It belongs to God, and it is either bought back or it dies; the one thing not on offer is keeping it for nothing. ⚠ The donkey is singled out because it was the ordinary Israelite’s working animal — the pack-beast, the plow-beast, the most valuable thing a poor family owned. The law puts its most expensive unclean possession under the same claim as everything else. KJV “thou shalt break his neck”; the ESV the same.

And then the sentence the whole law exists to produce: “every firstborn of MAN among your sons you shall redeem.” Human firstborn are never sacrificed — they are bought back, always. In a world that practiced child sacrifice, the law claims Israel’s sons absolutely and then, in the same breath, forbids the obvious way of collecting.

⚠ «And when your son asks you in time to come, ‘What is this?’» The fourth telling — and note its trigger: not a festival date but a child noticing something odd. A boy sees a lamb swapped for a donkey and asks why, and the answer he is given is not about livestock. It is the exodus. The rituals are built to be strange enough to prompt the question. (Jewish tradition reads the four tellings — here and 12:26; 13:8; Deuteronomy 6:20 — as the four sons of the Passover seder: the wise, the wicked, the simple, and the one who does not know how to ask.)

“Frontlets” (totafot) is a rare word of uncertain origin — possibly a band or ornament worn on the head. KJV “frontlets between thine eyes”; the NIV “a symbol on your forehead”; the NWT “a band between your eyes.”

Verses 17–22 · the long way round, a coffin, and a pillar

⚠ The first thing God does with a free people is take them the LONG way. The direct road to Canaan was the coastal highway — Egypt’s military road, garrisoned and fortified — and it is rejected for a stated reason: “LEST THE PEOPLE CHANGE THEIR MINDS WHEN THEY SEE WAR, AND RETURN TO EGYPT.” The route is chosen around their fragility. They have been slaves for four hundred years; they are not ready, and God is said to know it and to route accordingly rather than to demand they be otherwise. It is one of the most humane sentences in the book. KJV “lest peradventure the people repent”; the ESV “lest the people change their minds.”

⚠ «The land of the PHILISTINES» is an anachronism, and a well-known one: the Philistines as history knows them are an Aegean people who settled that coast around 1200 BC, centuries after any date proposed for the exodus. It is the same editorial updating of a place-name this library has already flagged at “Dan” (Genesis 14:14), “Ur of the Chaldeans,” and “Raamses” (Exodus 1:11) — the reader’s name for a region, not the name it bore at the time.

“And the sons of Israel went up in BATTLE ORDER.” Chamushim is uncertain and the versions scatter: armed, equipped for battle, in ranks — or, from the resemblance to the word for FIVE, in five divisions or by fifties. KJV “harnessed”; the ESV “equipped for battle”; the NWT “in battle formation.” Whatever the detail, it cuts against the picture of a fleeing rabble — and it sits pointedly next to the previous verse, where they are judged unready for war.

⚠ «And Moses took the BONES OF JOSEPH with him.» In the middle of a national evacuation, one man carries a coffin. Joseph had made his brothers swear it four hundred years earlier — “God will surely VISIT you, and you shall carry up my bones from here” (Genesis 50:25) — and the doubled Hebrew verb he used, paqod yifqod, is the same one Moses was told to repeat at the burning bush (3:16). Joseph died certain of a rescue he would not see, and left instructions for his own participation in it. The oath is finally discharged in Joshua 24:32, where the bones are buried at Shechem, in ground Jacob had bought — so the arc runs from Genesis to Joshua, and this verse is its hinge.

⚠ And then the pillar. Not a map and not a route given in advance, but a presence that moves and is followed — a pillar of cloud by day and of fire by night, so that they could travel at either hour. Verse 22 states it as a negative, which is stronger: it did NOT depart. This is the beginning of a presence that will fill the tabernacle at the end of the book (40:34-38), where the same cloud settles and Israel moves only when it lifts.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the exact / distinct words: “whatever opens the womb” kept literal for peter rechem; “set apart” for ha’avarta, the causing-to-PASS-OVER that echoes 12:12; “redeem” for padah, the buying-back verb, held distinct from ga’al; “frontlets” for the rare totafot, with both the literal and figurative readings given; “in battle order” for the uncertain chamushim, alternatives noted; and “the house of slaves” rather than the softer “house of bondage.”

Echoes paid. The firstborn spared on Passover night, now claimed outright (12:29; 4:22); the month of Abib, set by the ripening barley of 9:31; the telling of a son, commanded at 10:2 and built into the rite at 12:26, here twice more; and Joseph’s bones, carried out on the oath of Genesis 50:25 with the same doubled verb, “God will surely VISIT you,” that Moses was given at the bush (3:16).

Echoes planted. The firstborn claim, later transferred onto the tribe of Levi (Numbers 3:12-13) — a priesthood founded on this night; the pillar, which will stand between Israel and Egypt at the sea (14:19-20), descend at the tent of meeting (33:9-11), and finally fill the tabernacle so that Israel moves only when it lifts (40:34-38); and Joseph’s coffin, not buried until Joshua 24:32, at Shechem.

Next in this book: Exodus 14 — the sea. Pharaoh changes his mind and comes after them with six hundred chariots; Israel, trapped against the water, discovers it would rather have been a slave than dead — and is told to be still and watch.

Genesis 24 — The Mister Translation

1
וְאַבְרָהָם זָקֵן, בָּא בַּיָּמִים; וַיהוָה בֵּרַךְ אֶת-אַבְרָהָם, בַּכֹּל.
Now Abraham was old, well advanced in years, and Jehovah had blessed Abraham in everything.note
2
וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם, אֶל-עַבְדּוֹ זְקַן בֵּיתוֹ, הַמֹּשֵׁל, בְּכָל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ: שִׂים-נָא יָדְךָ, תַּחַת יְרֵכִי.
And Abraham said to his servant, the eldest of his house, who ruled over all he had, "Put your hand, please, under my thigh,note
3
וְאַשְׁבִּיעֲךָ--בַּיהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם, וֵאלֹהֵי הָאָרֶץ: אֲשֶׁר לֹא-תִקַּח אִשָּׁה, לִבְנִי, מִבְּנוֹת הַכְּנַעֲנִי, אֲשֶׁר אָנֹכִי יוֹשֵׁב בְּקִרְבּוֹ.
and I will make you swear by Jehovah, the God of heaven and the God of the earth, that you will not take a wife for my son from the daughters of the Canaanites, among whom I dwell;note
4
כִּי אֶל-אַרְצִי וְאֶל-מוֹלַדְתִּי, תֵּלֵךְ; וְלָקַחְתָּ אִשָּׁה, לִבְנִי לְיִצְחָק.
but you will go to my land and to my kindred, and take a wife for my son, for Isaac."note
5
וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו, הָעֶבֶד, אוּלַי לֹא-תֹאבֶה הָאִשָּׁה, לָלֶכֶת אַחֲרַי אֶל-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת; הֶהָשֵׁב אָשִׁיב אֶת-בִּנְךָ, אֶל-הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר-יָצָאתָ מִשָּׁם.
And the servant said to him, "Suppose the woman is not willing to follow me to this land — must I then bring your son back to the land you came out of?"note
6
וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו, אַבְרָהָם: הִשָּׁמֶר לְךָ, פֶּן-תָּשִׁיב אֶת-בְּנִי שָׁמָּה.
And Abraham said to him, "See that you do not bring my son back there.note
7
יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם, אֲשֶׁר לְקָחַנִי מִבֵּית אָבִי וּמֵאֶרֶץ מוֹלַדְתִּי, וַאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר-לִי וַאֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּע-לִי לֵאמֹר, לְזַרְעֲךָ אֶתֵּן אֶת-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת--הוּא, יִשְׁלַח מַלְאָכוֹ לְפָנֶיךָ, וְלָקַחְתָּ אִשָּׁה לִבְנִי, מִשָּׁם.
Jehovah, the God of heaven, who took me from my father's house and from the land of my birth, and who spoke to me and swore to me, saying, 'To your seed I will give this land' — he will send his angel before you, and you will take a wife for my son from there.note
8
וְאִם-לֹא תֹאבֶה הָאִשָּׁה, לָלֶכֶת אַחֲרֶיךָ--וְנִקִּיתָ, מִשְּׁבֻעָתִי זֹאת; רַק אֶת-בְּנִי, לֹא תָשֵׁב שָׁמָּה.
And if the woman is not willing to follow you, then you are clear from this oath of mine; only do not bring my son back there."note
9
וַיָּשֶׂם הָעֶבֶד אֶת-יָדוֹ, תַּחַת יֶרֶךְ אַבְרָהָם אֲדֹנָיו; וַיִּשָּׁבַע לוֹ, עַל-הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה.
So the servant put his hand under the thigh of Abraham his master, and swore to him concerning this matter.note
10
וַיִּקַּח הָעֶבֶד עֲשָׂרָה גְמַלִּים מִגְּמַלֵּי אֲדֹנָיו, וַיֵּלֶךְ, וְכָל-טוּב אֲדֹנָיו, בְּיָדוֹ; וַיָּקָם, וַיֵּלֶךְ אֶל-אֲרַם נַהֲרַיִם--אֶל-עִיר נָחוֹר.
And the servant took ten camels from his master's camels and departed, with all his master's good things in his hand; and he arose and went to Aram-naharaim, to the city of Nahor.note
11
וַיַּבְרֵךְ הַגְּמַלִּים מִחוּץ לָעִיר, אֶל-בְּאֵר הַמָּיִם, לְעֵת עֶרֶב, לְעֵת צֵאת הַשֹּׁאֲבֹת.
And he made the camels kneel down outside the city by the well of water, at evening, the time when the women come out to draw water.note
12
וַיֹּאמַר--יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם, הַקְרֵה-נָא לְפָנַי הַיּוֹם; וַעֲשֵׂה-חֶסֶד, עִם אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם.
And he said, "Jehovah, God of my master Abraham, grant me success today, please, and show kindness to my master Abraham.note
13
הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי נִצָּב, עַל-עֵין הַמָּיִם; וּבְנוֹת אַנְשֵׁי הָעִיר, יֹצְאֹת לִשְׁאֹב מָיִם.
Here I am, standing by the spring of water, and the daughters of the men of the city are coming out to draw water.note
14
וְהָיָה הַנַּעֲרָ, אֲשֶׁר אֹמַר אֵלֶיהָ הַטִּי-נָא כַדֵּךְ וְאֶשְׁתֶּה, וְאָמְרָה שְׁתֵה, וְגַם-גְּמַלֶּיךָ אַשְׁקֶה--אֹתָהּ הֹכַחְתָּ, לְעַבְדְּךָ לְיִצְחָק, וּבָהּ אֵדַע, כִּי-עָשִׂיתָ חֶסֶד עִם-אֲדֹנִי.
Let it be that the girl to whom I say, 'Please lower your jar that I may drink,' and who says, 'Drink, and I will water your camels too' — let her be the one you have appointed for your servant, for Isaac; and by this I will know that you have shown kindness to my master."note
15
וַיְהִי-הוּא, טֶרֶם כִּלָּה לְדַבֵּר, וְהִנֵּה רִבְקָה יֹצֵאת אֲשֶׁר יֻלְּדָה לִבְתוּאֵל בֶּן-מִלְכָּה, אֵשֶׁת נָחוֹר אֲחִי אַבְרָהָם; וְכַדָּהּ, עַל-שִׁכְמָהּ.
And it happened, before he had finished speaking, that there was Rebekah coming out — she who was born to Bethuel son of Milcah, the wife of Nahor, Abraham's brother — with her jar on her shoulder.note
16
וְהַנַּעֲרָ, טֹבַת מַרְאֶה מְאֹד--בְּתוּלָה, וְאִישׁ לֹא יְדָעָהּ; וַתֵּרֶד הָעַיְנָה, וַתְּמַלֵּא כַדָּהּ וַתָּעַל.
And the girl was very beautiful to look at, a virgin whom no man had known; and she went down to the spring, filled her jar, and came up.note
17
וַיָּרָץ הָעֶבֶד, לִקְרָאתָהּ; וַיֹּאמֶר, הַגְמִיאִינִי נָא מְעַט-מַיִם מִכַּדֵּךְ.
And the servant ran to meet her and said, "Please give me a little water to drink from your jar."note
18
וַתֹּאמֶר, שְׁתֵה אֲדֹנִי; וַתְּמַהֵר, וַתֹּרֶד כַּדָּהּ עַל-יָדָהּ--וַתַּשְׁקֵהוּ.
And she said, "Drink, my lord," and she quickly lowered her jar onto her hand and gave him drink.note
19
וַתְּכַל, לְהַשְׁקֹתוֹ; וַתֹּאמֶר, גַּם לִגְמַלֶּיךָ אֶשְׁאָב, עַד אִם-כִּלּוּ, לִשְׁתֹּת.
And when she had finished giving him drink, she said, "I will draw for your camels too, until they have finished drinking."note
20
וַתְּמַהֵר, וַתְּעַר כַּדָּהּ אֶל-הַשֹּׁקֶת, וַתָּרָץ עוֹד אֶל-הַבְּאֵר, לִשְׁאֹב; וַתִּשְׁאַב, לְכָל-גְּמַלָּיו.
And she quickly emptied her jar into the trough and ran again to the well to draw, and drew for all his camels.note
21
וְהָאִישׁ מִשְׁתָּאֵה, לָהּ; מַחֲרִישׁ--לָדַעַת הַהִצְלִיחַ יְהוָה דַּרְכּוֹ, אִם-לֹא.
And the man, gazing at her, kept silent, to know whether Jehovah had made his journey succeed or not.note
22
וַיְהִי, כַּאֲשֶׁר כִּלּוּ הַגְּמַלִּים לִשְׁתּוֹת, וַיִּקַּח הָאִישׁ נֶזֶם זָהָב, בֶּקַע מִשְׁקָלוֹ--וּשְׁנֵי צְמִידִים עַל-יָדֶיהָ, עֲשָׂרָה זָהָב מִשְׁקָלָם.
And when the camels had finished drinking, the man took a gold nose-ring weighing a half-shekel, and two bracelets for her wrists weighing ten shekels of gold,note
23
וַיֹּאמֶר בַּת-מִי אַתְּ, הַגִּידִי נָא לִי; הֲיֵשׁ בֵּית-אָבִיךְ מָקוֹם לָנוּ, לָלִין.
and said, "Whose daughter are you? Tell me, please. Is there room in your father's house for us to spend the night?"note
24
וַתֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו, בַּת-בְּתוּאֵל אָנֹכִי--בֶּן-מִלְכָּה, אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה לְנָחוֹר.
And she said to him, "I am the daughter of Bethuel, the son of Milcah, whom she bore to Nahor."note
25
וַתֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו, גַּם-תֶּבֶן גַּם-מִסְפּוֹא רַב עִמָּנוּ--גַּם-מָקוֹם, לָלוּן.
And she said to him, "We have both straw and plenty of fodder, and room to spend the night."note
26
וַיִּקֹּד הָאִישׁ, וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ לַיהוָה.
And the man bowed low and prostrated himself before Jehovah,note
27
וַיֹּאמֶר, בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם, אֲשֶׁר לֹא-עָזַב חַסְדּוֹ וַאֲמִתּוֹ, מֵעִם אֲדֹנִי; אָנֹכִי, בַּדֶּרֶךְ נָחַנִי יְהוָה, בֵּית, אֲחֵי אֲדֹנִי.
and said, "Blessed be Jehovah, the God of my master Abraham, who has not forsaken his kindness and his faithfulness toward my master; as for me, Jehovah has led me on the way to the house of my master's kinsmen."note
28
וַתָּרָץ, הַנַּעֲרָ, וַתַּגֵּד, לְבֵית אִמָּהּ--כַּדְּבָרִים, הָאֵלֶּה.
And the girl ran and told her mother's household these things.note
29
וּלְרִבְקָה אָח, וּשְׁמוֹ לָבָן; וַיָּרָץ לָבָן אֶל-הָאִישׁ הַחוּצָה, אֶל-הָעָיִן.
Now Rebekah had a brother whose name was Laban; and Laban ran out to the man, to the spring.note
30
וַיְהִי כִּרְאֹת אֶת-הַנֶּזֶם, וְאֶת-הַצְּמִדִים עַל-יְדֵי אֲחֹתוֹ, וּכְשָׁמְעוֹ אֶת-דִּבְרֵי רִבְקָה אֲחֹתוֹ לֵאמֹר, כֹּה-דִבֶּר אֵלַי הָאִישׁ; וַיָּבֹא, אֶל-הָאִישׁ, וְהִנֵּה עֹמֵד עַל-הַגְּמַלִּים, עַל-הָעָיִן.
And it happened, when he saw the nose-ring and the bracelets on his sister's wrists, and when he heard the words of Rebekah his sister, "Thus the man spoke to me," that he went to the man; and there he was, standing by the camels at the spring.note
31
וַיֹּאמֶר, בּוֹא בְּרוּךְ יְהוָה; לָמָּה תַעֲמֹד, בַּחוּץ, וְאָנֹכִי פִּנִּיתִי הַבַּיִת, וּמָקוֹם לַגְּמַלִּים.
And he said, "Come, blessed of Jehovah; why do you stand outside? For I have cleared the house, and a place for the camels."note
32
וַיָּבֹא הָאִישׁ הַבַּיְתָה, וַיְפַתַּח הַגְּמַלִּים; וַיִּתֵּן תֶּבֶן וּמִסְפּוֹא, לַגְּמַלִּים, וּמַיִם לִרְחֹץ רַגְלָיו, וְרַגְלֵי הָאֲנָשִׁים אֲשֶׁר אִתּוֹ.
So the man came into the house, and he unloaded the camels and gave straw and fodder to the camels, and water to wash his feet and the feet of the men with him.note
33
ויישם וַיּוּשַׂם לְפָנָיו, לֶאֱכֹל, וַיֹּאמֶר לֹא אֹכַל, עַד אִם-דִּבַּרְתִּי דְּבָרָי; וַיֹּאמֶר, דַּבֵּר.
And food was set before him to eat, but he said, "I will not eat until I have told my errand." And he said, "Speak on."note
34
וַיֹּאמַר: עֶבֶד אַבְרָהָם, אָנֹכִי.
And he said, "I am Abraham's servant.note
35
וַיהוָה בֵּרַךְ אֶת-אֲדֹנִי, מְאֹד--וַיִּגְדָּל; וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ צֹאן וּבָקָר, וְכֶסֶף וְזָהָב, וַעֲבָדִם וּשְׁפָחֹת, וּגְמַלִּים וַחֲמֹרִים.
Jehovah has blessed my master greatly, and he has become great; and he has given him flocks and herds, silver and gold, male and female servants, and camels and donkeys.note
36
וַתֵּלֶד שָׂרָה אֵשֶׁת אֲדֹנִי בֵן, לַאדֹנִי, אַחֲרֵי, זִקְנָתָהּ; וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ, אֶת-כָּל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ.
And Sarah my master's wife bore my master a son after she was old, and to him he has given all he has.note
37
וַיַּשְׁבִּעֵנִי אֲדֹנִי, לֵאמֹר: לֹא-תִקַּח אִשָּׁה, לִבְנִי, מִבְּנוֹת הַכְּנַעֲנִי, אֲשֶׁר אָנֹכִי יֹשֵׁב בְּאַרְצוֹ.
And my master made me swear, saying, 'You shall not take a wife for my son from the daughters of the Canaanites, in whose land I dwell;note
38
אִם-לֹא אֶל-בֵּית-אָבִי תֵּלֵךְ, וְאֶל-מִשְׁפַּחְתִּי; וְלָקַחְתָּ אִשָּׁה, לִבְנִי.
but you shall go to my father's house and to my family, and take a wife for my son.'note
39
וָאֹמַר, אֶל-אֲדֹנִי: אֻלַי לֹא-תֵלֵךְ הָאִשָּׁה, אַחֲרָי.
And I said to my master, 'Suppose the woman will not follow me.'note
40
וַיֹּאמֶר, אֵלָי: יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר-הִתְהַלַּכְתִּי לְפָנָיו, יִשְׁלַח מַלְאָכוֹ אִתָּךְ וְהִצְלִיחַ דַּרְכֶּךָ, וְלָקַחְתָּ אִשָּׁה לִבְנִי, מִמִּשְׁפַּחְתִּי וּמִבֵּית אָבִי.
And he said to me, 'Jehovah, before whom I have walked, will send his angel with you and make your journey succeed, and you will take a wife for my son from my family and from my father's house.note
41
אָז תִּנָּקֶה מֵאָלָתִי, כִּי תָבוֹא אֶל-מִשְׁפַּחְתִּי; וְאִם-לֹא יִתְּנוּ לָךְ, וְהָיִיתָ נָקִי מֵאָלָתִי.
Then you will be released from my oath, when you come to my family; and if they do not give her to you, you will be free from my oath.'note
42
וָאָבֹא הַיּוֹם, אֶל-הָעָיִן; וָאֹמַר, יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם, אִם-יֶשְׁךָ-נָּא מַצְלִיחַ דַּרְכִּי, אֲשֶׁר אָנֹכִי הֹלֵךְ עָלֶיהָ.
So I came today to the spring and said, 'Jehovah, God of my master Abraham, if you would only make the journey succeed on which I am going —note
43
הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי נִצָּב, עַל-עֵין הַמָּיִם; וְהָיָה הָעַלְמָה, הַיֹּצֵאת לִשְׁאֹב, וְאָמַרְתִּי אֵלֶיהָ, הַשְׁקִינִי-נָא מְעַט-מַיִם מִכַּדֵּךְ.
here I am, standing by the spring of water; let it be that the young woman who comes out to draw, to whom I say, "Please give me a little water to drink from your jar,"note
44
וְאָמְרָה אֵלַי גַּם-אַתָּה שְׁתֵה, וְגַם לִגְמַלֶּיךָ אֶשְׁאָב--הִוא הָאִשָּׁה, אֲשֶׁר-הֹכִיחַ יְהוָה לְבֶן-אֲדֹנִי.
and who says to me, "Drink, and I will draw for your camels too" — let her be the woman Jehovah has appointed for my master's son.'note
45
אֲנִי טֶרֶם אֲכַלֶּה לְדַבֵּר אֶל-לִבִּי, וְהִנֵּה רִבְקָה יֹצֵאת וְכַדָּהּ עַל-שִׁכְמָהּ, וַתֵּרֶד הָעַיְנָה, וַתִּשְׁאָב; וָאֹמַר אֵלֶיהָ, הַשְׁקִינִי נָא.
Before I had finished speaking in my heart, there was Rebekah coming out with her jar on her shoulder, and she went down to the spring and drew; and I said to her, 'Please give me a drink.'note
46
וַתְּמַהֵר, וַתּוֹרֶד כַּדָּהּ מֵעָלֶיהָ, וַתֹּאמֶר שְׁתֵה, וְגַם-גְּמַלֶּיךָ אַשְׁקֶה; וָאֵשְׁתְּ, וְגַם הַגְּמַלִּים הִשְׁקָתָה.
And she quickly lowered her jar from her shoulder and said, 'Drink, and I will water your camels too'; so I drank, and she watered the camels also.note
47
וָאֶשְׁאַל אֹתָהּ, וָאֹמַר בַּת-מִי אַתְּ, וַתֹּאמֶר בַּת-בְּתוּאֵל בֶּן-נָחוֹר, אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה-לּוֹ מִלְכָּה; וָאָשִׂם הַנֶּזֶם עַל-אַפָּהּ, וְהַצְּמִידִים עַל-יָדֶיהָ.
And I asked her and said, 'Whose daughter are you?' And she said, 'The daughter of Bethuel, son of Nahor, whom Milcah bore to him.' And I put the ring on her nose and the bracelets on her wrists,note
48
וָאֶקֹּד וָאֶשְׁתַּחֲוֶה, לַיהוָה; וָאֲבָרֵךְ, אֶת-יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם, אֲשֶׁר הִנְחַנִי בְּדֶרֶךְ אֱמֶת, לָקַחַת אֶת-בַּת-אֲחִי אֲדֹנִי לִבְנוֹ.
and I bowed low and prostrated myself before Jehovah, and blessed Jehovah, the God of my master Abraham, who led me by the true way to take the daughter of my master's brother for his son.note
49
וְעַתָּה אִם-יֶשְׁכֶם עֹשִׂים חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת, אֶת-אֲדֹנִי--הַגִּידוּ לִי; וְאִם-לֹא--הַגִּידוּ לִי, וְאֶפְנֶה עַל-יָמִין אוֹ עַל-שְׂמֹאל.
And now, if you will deal with kindness and faithfulness toward my master, tell me; and if not, tell me, that I may turn to the right hand or to the left."note
50
וַיַּעַן לָבָן וּבְתוּאֵל וַיֹּאמְרוּ, מֵיְהוָה יָצָא הַדָּבָר; לֹא נוּכַל דַּבֵּר אֵלֶיךָ, רַע אוֹ-טוֹב.
Then Laban and Bethuel answered and said, "The thing has come from Jehovah; we cannot speak to you bad or good.note
51
הִנֵּה-רִבְקָה לְפָנֶיךָ, קַח וָלֵךְ; וּתְהִי אִשָּׁה לְבֶן-אֲדֹנֶיךָ, כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה.
Here is Rebekah before you; take her and go, and let her become the wife of your master's son, as Jehovah has spoken."note
52
וַיְהִי, כַּאֲשֶׁר שָׁמַע עֶבֶד אַבְרָהָם אֶת-דִּבְרֵיהֶם; וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ אַרְצָה, לַיהוָה.
And when Abraham's servant heard their words, he prostrated himself to the ground before Jehovah.note
53
וַיּוֹצֵא הָעֶבֶד כְּלֵי-כֶסֶף וּכְלֵי זָהָב, וּבְגָדִים, וַיִּתֵּן, לְרִבְקָה; וּמִגְדָּנֹת--נָתַן לְאָחִיהָ, וּלְאִמָּהּ.
And the servant brought out vessels of silver and vessels of gold, and garments, and gave them to Rebekah; and to her brother and her mother he gave precious things.note
54
וַיֹּאכְלוּ וַיִּשְׁתּוּ, הוּא וְהָאֲנָשִׁים אֲשֶׁר-עִמּוֹ--וַיָּלִינוּ; וַיָּקוּמוּ בַבֹּקֶר, וַיֹּאמֶר שַׁלְּחֻנִי לַאדֹנִי.
And they ate and drank, he and the men with him, and spent the night. And they rose in the morning, and he said, "Send me off to my master."note
55
וַיֹּאמֶר אָחִיהָ וְאִמָּהּ, תֵּשֵׁב הַנַּעֲרָ אִתָּנוּ יָמִים אוֹ עָשׂוֹר; אַחַר, תֵּלֵךְ.
But her brother and her mother said, "Let the girl stay with us some days, at least ten; after that she may go."note
56
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם אַל-תְּאַחֲרוּ אֹתִי, וַיהוָה הִצְלִיחַ דַּרְכִּי; שַׁלְּחוּנִי, וְאֵלְכָה לַאדֹנִי.
And he said to them, "Do not delay me, since Jehovah has made my journey succeed; send me off, that I may go to my master."note
57
וַיֹּאמְרוּ, נִקְרָא לַנַּעֲרָ, וְנִשְׁאֲלָה, אֶת-פִּיהָ.
And they said, "Let us call the girl and ask her own mouth."note
58
וַיִּקְרְאוּ לְרִבְקָה וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלֶיהָ, הֲתֵלְכִי עִם-הָאִישׁ הַזֶּה; וַתֹּאמֶר, אֵלֵךְ.
And they called Rebekah and said to her, "Will you go with this man?" And she said, "I will go."note
59
וַיְשַׁלְּחוּ אֶת-רִבְקָה אֲחֹתָם, וְאֶת-מֵנִקְתָּהּ, וְאֶת-עֶבֶד אַבְרָהָם, וְאֶת-אֲנָשָׁיו.
So they sent off Rebekah their sister, and her nurse, and Abraham's servant, and his men.note
60
וַיְבָרְכוּ אֶת-רִבְקָה, וַיֹּאמְרוּ לָהּ--אֲחֹתֵנוּ, אַתְּ הֲיִי לְאַלְפֵי רְבָבָה; וְיִירַשׁ זַרְעֵךְ, אֵת שַׁעַר שֹׂנְאָיו.
And they blessed Rebekah and said to her, "Our sister, may you become thousands of ten thousands, and may your seed possess the gate of those who hate them."note
61
וַתָּקָם רִבְקָה וְנַעֲרֹתֶיהָ, וַתִּרְכַּבְנָה עַל-הַגְּמַלִּים, וַתֵּלַכְנָה, אַחֲרֵי הָאִישׁ; וַיִּקַּח הָעֶבֶד אֶת-רִבְקָה, וַיֵּלַךְ.
And Rebekah arose, and her maids, and they rode on the camels and followed the man; and the servant took Rebekah and went.note
62
וְיִצְחָק בָּא מִבּוֹא, בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי; וְהוּא יוֹשֵׁב, בְּאֶרֶץ הַנֶּגֶב.
Now Isaac had come from the direction of Beer-lahai-roi, for he was dwelling in the land of the Negev.note
63
וַיֵּצֵא יִצְחָק לָשׂוּחַ בַּשָּׂדֶה, לִפְנוֹת עָרֶב; וַיִּשָּׂא עֵינָיו וַיַּרְא, וְהִנֵּה גְמַלִּים בָּאִים.
And Isaac went out to meditate in the field toward evening; and he lifted up his eyes and looked, and there were camels coming.note
64
וַתִּשָּׂא רִבְקָה אֶת-עֵינֶיהָ, וַתֵּרֶא אֶת-יִצְחָק; וַתִּפֹּל, מֵעַל הַגָּמָל.
And Rebekah lifted up her eyes, and when she saw Isaac, she got down from the camel.note
65
וַתֹּאמֶר אֶל-הָעֶבֶד, מִי-הָאִישׁ הַלָּזֶה הַהֹלֵךְ בַּשָּׂדֶה לִקְרָאתֵנוּ, וַיֹּאמֶר הָעֶבֶד, הוּא אֲדֹנִי; וַתִּקַּח הַצָּעִיף, וַתִּתְכָּס.
And she said to the servant, "Who is that man walking in the field to meet us?" And the servant said, "He is my master." And she took her veil and covered herself.note
66
וַיְסַפֵּר הָעֶבֶד, לְיִצְחָק, אֵת כָּל-הַדְּבָרִים, אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה.
And the servant told Isaac all the things he had done.note
67
וַיְבִאֶהָ יִצְחָק, הָאֹהֱלָה שָׂרָה אִמּוֹ, וַיִּקַּח אֶת-רִבְקָה וַתְּהִי-לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה, וַיֶּאֱהָבֶהָ; וַיִּנָּחֵם יִצְחָק, אַחֲרֵי אִמּוֹ. {פ}
And Isaac brought her into the tent of Sarah his mother, and he took Rebekah, and she became his wife, and he loved her; and Isaac was comforted after his mother.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Genesis 24 is the longest chapter in the book — a single, patient story told twice (once as it happens, once as the servant retells it). Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–9 · תַּחַת יְרֵכִי tachat yerekhi — "under my thigh"

An oath by the seat of the promise. Abraham, old, binds his senior servant with the strangest gesture in Genesis: "put your hand under my thigh" (yarekh). It is not idle — the thigh is the region of the covenant-of-circumcision sign (17:11) and the Hebrew idiom for a man's own offspring ("those who came from his yarekh"). To swear by it is to swear by the seed the whole promise runs through — exactly fitting for an oath about finding the wife of the son who carries it (the Bible knows this gesture only here and at 47:29, Jacob and Joseph, both about the promised line). Two things are forbidden: no wife from the Canaanites, and — twice, emphatically — the servant must never take Isaac back east. The promise runs forward; the son must not retrace the road out of Mesopotamia that God called Abraham down. The servant is left unnamed all chapter long — his whole character is his faithfulness — though Jewish tradition has always recognized him as Eliezer of Damascus, the steward who once stood to inherit everything (15:2), now sent to win the heir's bride and hand the inheritance on.

Verses 10–14 · וַעֲשֵׂה-חֶסֶד va'aseh-chesed — "and show kindness"

A sign designed to test character, not luck. At the well of the city of Nahor, at evening — "the time the women come out to draw water" — the servant prays. And what he asks for is the hinge of the whole chapter: not the most beautiful girl, or the richest, but the one who, asked for a sip, offers unbidden to water ten thirsty camels. A camel back from a journey can drink twenty-five gallons; ten of them is a staggering, back-breaking kindness to a stranger. The word for it is chesed — loyal, covenant love (the KJV's "shew kindness," the NWT's "loyal love") — and it rings through the chapter (vv 12, 14, 27, 49). The servant's theology is quietly radical: the mark of the woman fit to carry the promise is not her looks but her kindness. This is the third of the Bible's well-betrothal scenes — Rebekah here, Rachel for Jacob (ch. 29), Zipporah for Moses (Exodus 2) — the type-scene where the covenant future is met at a well.

Verses 15–27 · נַעֲרָה · בְּתוּלָה · עַלְמָה na'arah · betulah · almah

"Before he had finished speaking." The answer outruns the prayer — Rebekah is already coming out before the servant's words are done (a line the narrator will repeat, v45, as the signature of a providence that runs ahead of us). And she does not merely pass the test; she exceeds it, running back and forth to the well until every camel is watered. Three Hebrew words describe her in these verses, and the overlap is worth marking (the KJV flattens the first two to "damsel"): she is a na'arah ("girl," v14), a betulah ("virgin," v16, "whom no man had known"), and — in the servant's retelling — an almah (v43). That last is the very word at the heart of the Isaiah 7:14 debate ("a almah shall conceive"): here the same Rebekah is all three at once, a young woman presumed chaste — which is exactly what almah means. The servant crowns her with a gold nose-ring and bracelets, asks whose daughter she is, and — hearing she is of Abraham's own family — bows and blesses "Jehovah, who has not forsaken his kindness and his faithfulness" (chesed ve-emet, the covenant word-pair for love that keeps its word).

Verses 28–49 · לָבָן Lavan — and the story told again

Laban, introduced by his eyes. Rebekah's brother Laban "ran out to the man" — and the very next verse tells us when: "when he saw the nose-ring and the bracelets" (v30). In one stroke the narrator draws the whole man — the one who will later swap Leah for Rachel and cheat Jacob's wages ten times over (chs. 29–31) — and his hospitality already has an eye on the gold. Then the story is told a second time (vv 34–49). This is deliberate Hebrew art: the servant retells the entire day, and the small changes are the point — he calls Rebekah almah where the narrator said na'arah; he stresses that God led him "by the true way"; he trims his private doubts and foregrounds the family tie — a speech tuned to persuade Laban's house. He will not even eat until it is told (v33): the errand comes before the meal. And he ends where he began, on chesed ve-emet — "if you will deal in kindness and faithfulness with my master, tell me" (v49).

Verses 50–61 · אֵלֵךְ elekh — "I will go"

The bride who repeats Abraham's journey. Laban and Bethuel concede at once — "the thing has come from Jehovah; we cannot speak to you bad or good." But when the family stalls for ten more days, they do the remarkable thing of asking the woman herself: "Will you go with this man?" And Rebekah answers in a single word — elekh, "I will go" (v58). It is the whole faith of Abraham compressed into one syllable: she will leave her land and her kindred for a land she has never seen and a man she has never met — doing of her own free will exactly what the call once commanded Abraham, "go, you yourself, from your land and your birthplace" (12:1). And the blessing her family speaks over her seals the parallel: "may your seed possess the gate of those who hate them" — almost the exact words of the oath sworn to Abraham at the Aqedah (22:17). The promise passes to the second generation not by force but by a young woman's willing "I will go."

Verses 62–67 · לָשׂוּחַ בַּשָּׂדֶה la-suach ba-sadeh — "to meditate in the field"

The field at dusk, and the tent restored. Isaac has come from Beer-lahai-roi — "the well of the Living One who sees me," where the angel once found the cast-out Hagar (16:14): the heir dwells where the seeing-God met the outcast. At evening he goes out "to meditate" in the field — la-suach, a word so rare no one is certain of it (to meditate, to pray, to walk, even to grieve — the rabbis made it the origin of the evening prayer); this translation keeps "meditate," the reading that best fits a solitary man at dusk who lifts his eyes and sees camels — and a bride — on the horizon. Rebekah sees him, gets down, and veils herself. And the chapter closes on the tenderest line in Genesis: "he brought her into the tent of Sarah his mother … and he loved her; and Isaac was comforted after his mother." The love comes after the marriage, not before; and the tent left empty by Sarah's death one chapter ago (23:19) is filled again. The grief of chapter 23 is answered by the wedding of chapter 24.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "under my thigh" for yerekh (the oath by the seed, not softened away); "kindness and faithfulness" for the pair chesed ve-emet, kept as one covenant-idea (vv 27, 49); the three words for Rebekah — na'arah / betulah / almah — each rendered distinctly ("girl" / "virgin" / "young woman") so the NWT-and-shelf-wide almah question at v43 stays visible; and "to meditate" for the rare suach (v63), with the KJV's reading kept over the "stroll" of some moderns.

Echoes paid. "I will go" (elekh, v58) — Rebekah repeating Abraham's call of her own will; "possess the gate of those who hate them" (v60) — the Aqedah oath spoken over the bride; "to your seed I will give this land" (v7) — the covenant of the pieces; Beer-lahai-roi (v62) — Hagar's seeing-God well; and "comforted after his mother" (v67) — Sarah's tent filled again.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 25 — Abraham's death and burial beside Sarah, the sons of Keturah, and the twins who wrestle in the womb: Esau and Jacob.

Genesis 25 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיֹּסֶף אַבְרָהָם וַיִּקַּח אִשָּׁה, וּשְׁמָהּ קְטוּרָה.
And Abraham took another wife, and her name was Keturah.note
2
וַתֵּלֶד לוֹ, אֶת-זִמְרָן וְאֶת-יָקְשָׁן, וְאֶת-מְדָן, וְאֶת-מִדְיָן--וְאֶת-יִשְׁבָּק, וְאֶת-שׁוּחַ.
And she bore him Zimran, and Jokshan, and Medan, and Midian, and Ishbak, and Shuah.note
3
וְיָקְשָׁן יָלַד, אֶת-שְׁבָא וְאֶת-דְּדָן; וּבְנֵי דְדָן, הָיוּ אַשּׁוּרִם וּלְטוּשִׁם וּלְאֻמִּים.
And Jokshan fathered Sheba and Dedan; and the sons of Dedan were the Asshurim, the Letushim, and the Leummim.note
4
וּבְנֵי מִדְיָן, עֵיפָה וָעֵפֶר וַחֲנֹךְ, וַאֲבִידָע, וְאֶלְדָּעָה; כָּל-אֵלֶּה, בְּנֵי קְטוּרָה.
And the sons of Midian: Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah. All these were the sons of Keturah.note
5
וַיִּתֵּן אַבְרָהָם אֶת-כָּל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ, לְיִצְחָק.
And Abraham gave all he had to Isaac.note
6
וְלִבְנֵי הַפִּילַגְשִׁים אֲשֶׁר לְאַבְרָהָם, נָתַן אַבְרָהָם מַתָּנֹת; וַיְשַׁלְּחֵם מֵעַל יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ, בְּעוֹדֶנּוּ חַי, קֵדְמָה, אֶל-אֶרֶץ קֶדֶם.
But to the sons of the concubines that Abraham had, Abraham gave gifts, and while he was still alive he sent them away from Isaac his son, eastward, to the land of the east.note
7
וְאֵלֶּה, יְמֵי שְׁנֵי-חַיֵּי אַבְרָהָם--אֲשֶׁר-חָי: מְאַת שָׁנָה וְשִׁבְעִים שָׁנָה, וְחָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים.
And these are the days of the years of Abraham's life that he lived: a hundred and seventy-five years.note
8
וַיִּגְוַע וַיָּמָת אַבְרָהָם בְּשֵׂיבָה טוֹבָה, זָקֵן וְשָׂבֵעַ; וַיֵּאָסֶף, אֶל-עַמָּיו.
And Abraham breathed his last and died in a good old age, an old man and full of years, and was gathered to his people.note
9
וַיִּקְבְּרוּ אֹתוֹ יִצְחָק וְיִשְׁמָעֵאל, בָּנָיו, אֶל-מְעָרַת, הַמַּכְפֵּלָה: אֶל-שְׂדֵה עֶפְרֹן בֶּן-צֹחַר, הַחִתִּי, אֲשֶׁר, עַל-פְּנֵי מַמְרֵא.
And Isaac and Ishmael his sons buried him in the cave of Machpelah, in the field of Ephron son of Zohar the Hittite, which faces Mamre —note
10
הַשָּׂדֶה אֲשֶׁר-קָנָה אַבְרָהָם, מֵאֵת בְּנֵי-חֵת--שָׁמָּה קֻבַּר אַבְרָהָם, וְשָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ.
the field Abraham bought from the sons of Heth; there Abraham was buried, and Sarah his wife.note
11
וַיְהִי, אַחֲרֵי מוֹת אַבְרָהָם, וַיְבָרֶךְ אֱלֹהִים, אֶת-יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ; וַיֵּשֶׁב יִצְחָק, עִם-בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי. {פ}
And after the death of Abraham, God blessed Isaac his son; and Isaac dwelt by Beer-lahai-roi.note
12
וְאֵלֶּה תֹּלְדֹת יִשְׁמָעֵאל, בֶּן-אַבְרָהָם: אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה הָגָר הַמִּצְרִית, שִׁפְחַת שָׂרָה--לְאַבְרָהָם.
Now these are the generations of Ishmael, Abraham's son, whom Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah's slave-girl, bore to Abraham.note
13
וְאֵלֶּה, שְׁמוֹת בְּנֵי יִשְׁמָעֵאל, בִּשְׁמֹתָם, לְתוֹלְדֹתָם: בְּכֹר יִשְׁמָעֵאל נְבָיֹת, וְקֵדָר וְאַדְבְּאֵל וּמִבְשָׂם.
And these are the names of the sons of Ishmael, by their names, by their generations: Ishmael's firstborn Nebaioth, then Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam,note
14
וּמִשְׁמָע וְדוּמָה, וּמַשָּׂא.
Mishma, Dumah, Massa,note
15
חֲדַד וְתֵימָא, יְטוּר נָפִישׁ וָקֵדְמָה.
Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah.note
16
אֵלֶּה הֵם בְּנֵי יִשְׁמָעֵאל, וְאֵלֶּה שְׁמֹתָם, בְּחַצְרֵיהֶם, וּבְטִירֹתָם--שְׁנֵים-עָשָׂר נְשִׂיאִם, לְאֻמֹּתָם.
These are the sons of Ishmael, and these are their names, by their villages and by their encampments — twelve chieftains according to their tribes.note
17
וְאֵלֶּה, שְׁנֵי חַיֵּי יִשְׁמָעֵאל--מְאַת שָׁנָה וּשְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה, וְשֶׁבַע שָׁנִים; וַיִּגְוַע וַיָּמָת, וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל-עַמָּיו.
And these are the years of Ishmael's life: a hundred and thirty-seven years; and he breathed his last and died, and was gathered to his people.note
18
וַיִּשְׁכְּנוּ מֵחֲוִילָה עַד-שׁוּר, אֲשֶׁר עַל-פְּנֵי מִצְרַיִם, בֹּאֲכָה, אַשּׁוּרָה; עַל-פְּנֵי כָל-אֶחָיו, נָפָל. {פ}
And they settled from Havilah to Shur, which faces Egypt, in the direction of Asshur; over against all his brothers he settled.note
19
וְאֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת יִצְחָק, בֶּן-אַבְרָהָם: אַבְרָהָם, הוֹלִיד אֶת-יִצְחָק.
And these are the generations of Isaac, Abraham's son: Abraham fathered Isaac.note
20
וַיְהִי יִצְחָק, בֶּן-אַרְבָּעִים שָׁנָה, בְּקַחְתּוֹ אֶת-רִבְקָה בַּת-בְּתוּאֵל הָאֲרַמִּי, מִפַּדַּן אֲרָם--אֲחוֹת לָבָן הָאֲרַמִּי, לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה.
And Isaac was forty years old when he took Rebekah, daughter of Bethuel the Aramean of Paddan-aram, sister of Laban the Aramean, to be his wife.note
21
וַיֶּעְתַּר יִצְחָק לַיהוָה לְנֹכַח אִשְׁתּוֹ, כִּי עֲקָרָה הִוא; וַיֵּעָתֶר לוֹ יְהוָה, וַתַּהַר רִבְקָה אִשְׁתּוֹ.
And Isaac entreated Jehovah on behalf of his wife, because she was barren; and Jehovah let himself be entreated by him, and Rebekah his wife conceived.note
22
וַיִּתְרֹצְצוּ הַבָּנִים, בְּקִרְבָּהּ, וַתֹּאמֶר אִם-כֵּן, לָמָּה זֶּה אָנֹכִי; וַתֵּלֶךְ, לִדְרֹשׁ אֶת-יְהוָה.
And the children struggled together within her, and she said, "If it is so, why do I live?" And she went to inquire of Jehovah.note
23
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה לָהּ, שְׁנֵי גֹיִים בְּבִטְנֵךְ, וּשְׁנֵי לְאֻמִּים, מִמֵּעַיִךְ יִפָּרֵדוּ; וּלְאֹם מִלְאֹם יֶאֱמָץ, וְרַב יַעֲבֹד צָעִיר.
And Jehovah said to her:
"Two nations are in your womb,
and two peoples from within you will be divided;
one people will be stronger than the other,
and the elder will serve the younger."note
24
וַיִּמְלְאוּ יָמֶיהָ, לָלֶדֶת; וְהִנֵּה תוֹמִם, בְּבִטְנָהּ.
And when her days to give birth were fulfilled, look — there were twins in her womb.note
25
וַיֵּצֵא הָרִאשׁוֹן אַדְמוֹנִי, כֻּלּוֹ כְּאַדֶּרֶת שֵׂעָר; וַיִּקְרְאוּ שְׁמוֹ, עֵשָׂו.
And the first came out ruddy, all of him like a hairy cloak; and they called his name Esau.note
26
וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן יָצָא אָחִיו, וְיָדוֹ אֹחֶזֶת בַּעֲקֵב עֵשָׂו, וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ, יַעֲקֹב; וְיִצְחָק בֶּן-שִׁשִּׁים שָׁנָה, בְּלֶדֶת אֹתָם.
And after that his brother came out, his hand grasping Esau's heel, so his name was called Jacob; and Isaac was sixty years old when she bore them.note
27
וַיִּגְדְּלוּ, הַנְּעָרִים, וַיְהִי עֵשָׂו אִישׁ יֹדֵעַ צַיִד, אִישׁ שָׂדֶה; וְיַעֲקֹב אִישׁ תָּם, יֹשֵׁב אֹהָלִים.
And the boys grew up; and Esau was a man skilled in hunting, a man of the field, but Jacob was a blameless man, dwelling in tents.note
28
וַיֶּאֱהַב יִצְחָק אֶת-עֵשָׂו, כִּי-צַיִד בְּפִיו; וְרִבְקָה, אֹהֶבֶת אֶת-יַעֲקֹב.
And Isaac loved Esau, because game was in his mouth; but Rebekah loved Jacob.note
29
וַיָּזֶד יַעֲקֹב, נָזִיד; וַיָּבֹא עֵשָׂו מִן-הַשָּׂדֶה, וְהוּא עָיֵף.
And Jacob was boiling a stew, and Esau came in from the field, and he was famished.note
30
וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו אֶל-יַעֲקֹב, הַלְעִיטֵנִי נָא מִן-הָאָדֹם הָאָדֹם הַזֶּה--כִּי עָיֵף, אָנֹכִי; עַל-כֵּן קָרָא-שְׁמוֹ, אֱדוֹם.
And Esau said to Jacob, "Let me gulp down, please, some of that red — that red stuff — for I am famished." (Therefore his name was called Edom.)note
31
וַיֹּאמֶר, יַעֲקֹב: מִכְרָה כַיּוֹם אֶת-בְּכֹרָתְךָ, לִי.
And Jacob said, "Sell me, here and now, your birthright."note
32
וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו, הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי הוֹלֵךְ לָמוּת; וְלָמָּה-זֶּה לִי, בְּכֹרָה.
And Esau said, "Look, I am about to die; what good is a birthright to me?"note
33
וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב, הִשָּׁבְעָה לִּי כַּיּוֹם, וַיִּשָּׁבַע, לוֹ; וַיִּמְכֹּר אֶת-בְּכֹרָתוֹ, לְיַעֲקֹב.
And Jacob said, "Swear to me, here and now." So he swore to him, and sold his birthright to Jacob.note
34
וְיַעֲקֹב נָתַן לְעֵשָׂו, לֶחֶם וּנְזִיד עֲדָשִׁים, וַיֹּאכַל וַיֵּשְׁתְּ, וַיָּקָם וַיֵּלַךְ; וַיִּבֶז עֵשָׂו, אֶת-הַבְּכֹרָה. {פ}
And Jacob gave Esau bread and lentil stew; and he ate and drank and rose and went his way. So Esau despised his birthright.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Genesis 25 is a hinge — it closes the first generation of the promise and opens the third. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–11 · וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל-עַמָּיו vayye'asef el-ammav — "and he was gathered to his people"

The last acts of a long life. Abraham marries again — Keturah — and fathers six more sons who fan out east as the peoples of Arabia and the caravan routes; chief among them is Midian, so the Midianites Moses will one day live among descend from Abraham himself. But the inheritance is not divided: "Abraham gave all he had to Isaac," and sent the others away with gifts, "eastward, to the land of the east." Then, at 175, "he breathed his last and died … and was gathered to his people." The phrase is worth stopping on — it is not the same as burial (he is "gathered" in v8, then buried in v9). Ne'esaf el-ammav reaches past the grave to a joining of the ancestors: a quiet, undogmatic hint of a life continued with one's own, long before the Hebrew Bible speaks plainly of resurrection. And the burial is its own quiet wonder: Isaac and Ishmael — the heir and the son sent into the desert — stand together to lay their father in the cave he had bought for Sarah (23:19). Then Isaac settles at Beer-lahai-roi, Hagar's well: the son of the promise makes his home where the seeing-God once met the cast-out.

Verses 12–18 · שְׁנֵים-עָשָׂר נְשִׂיאִם shneim-asar nesi'im — "twelve chieftains"

Ishmael's promise, kept. Before the story narrows to Isaac's line, it pauses to honor the other son with a toldot ("generations") of his own — and to record that God kept his word to him exactly. Ishmael fathers twelve chieftains (nesi'im, the same "prince" word the Hittites used of Abraham, 23:6) — precisely the "twelve chieftains" promised at 17:20. He too dies at a full age and is "gathered to his people" — the same dignity given him as his father. And his people settle "over against all his brothers" — the very words the angel spoke to Hagar in the desert, "he will dwell in the face of all his brothers" (16:12), now written down as history. The sent-away son is not forgotten by the God who first named him "God hears."

Verses 19–26 · וְרַב יַעֲבֹד צָעִיר ve-rav ya'avod tsa'ir — "the elder will serve the younger"

Two nations at war in a womb. Rebekah is barren twenty years, until Isaac entreats Jehovah — and the Hebrew turns the same verb around for the answer: "Isaac entreated … and Jehovah was entreated of him" (v21), one word for the asking and the yes. The pregnancy is violent — the twins "struggle" inside her — and Rebekah does the remarkable thing of going straight to God: she is the first woman in the Bible to "inquire of Jehovah," and it is she, not Isaac, who receives the oracle. And what an oracle: "the elder will serve the younger." (The Hebrew rav ya'avod tsa'ir is, strictly, ambiguous — with no object-marker it could even read "the elder, the younger will serve" — but the whole of Genesis resolves it: the promise runs through the chosen son, not the firstborn, the pattern Isaac himself embodied over Ishmael, 21:12.) The names are minted at birth: the first out is ruddy and hairy — Esau — and the second grips his twin's heel (aqev), so he is Jacob, "heel-holder" — the very word of the first promise, "you will strike his heel" (3:15). The whole struggle of the seed is folded into a newborn's fist.

Verses 27–34 · אִישׁ תָּם ish tam — "a blameless man"

The word that surprises. Esau grows into a hunter, "a man of the field"; Jacob is an ish tam — and here this translation makes its most surprising choice, following the NWT toward the literal: "a blameless man." The word tam is the one used of Noah and of Job — "blameless, complete, whole" — and the shelf scatters, uneasy, exactly along that tension: the KJV reaches for "a plain man," the ASV for "a quiet man," softening the moral weight to fit a man who is about to drive a hard bargain. Kept literal, it carries the root sense — whole, sound, all-of-a-piece, settled — the domestic man of the tents set against the roving hunter; and it lets the narrator's irony stand undimmed, that the "blameless" one is the one who buys his brother's hunger.

Sold for a bowl of red. Esau comes in famished and cannot even name the food: "let me gulp down some of that red — that red stuff" — and the crude cry earns him his second name, Edom ("Red"). Jacob's price is his brother's whole future: the birthright, the firstborn's double portion and the covenant line itself. Esau takes the trade with a shrug — "I am about to die; what good is a birthright to me?" — and the narrator nails him to it with five flat verbs: "he ate and drank and rose and went his way. So Esau despised his birthright." No lightning, no rebuke — just the terrible casualness of a man who sells the eternal for one meal, which is why the New Testament makes him the very type of the "profane" person (Hebrews 12:16).

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "gathered to his people" for ne'esaf el-ammav (kept distinct from burial, so its reach past the grave stays visible); "a blameless man" for the surprising tam (v27), with the NWT and against the KJV's "plain" and the ASV's "quiet," the note carrying the irony; "he despised his birthright" for bazah (the same verb the fool aims at wisdom, Proverbs 1); and the entreated / was entreated echo of atar kept audible (v21).

Echoes paid. Ishmael's twelve chieftains (v16) — the promise of 17:20 kept; "over against all his brothers" (v18) — the angel's word to Hagar; "the elder will serve the younger" (v23) — the reversal of 21:12; Jacob's grip on the heel (v26) — the aqev of the first promise; and Isaac and Ishmael together at Machpelah (v9), the grave Abraham bought for Sarah.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 26 — Isaac in Gerar, the wife-sister ruse a third time, the re-dug wells, and the covenant with Abimelech.

Genesis 26 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיְהִי רָעָב, בָּאָרֶץ, מִלְּבַד הָרָעָב הָרִאשׁוֹן, אֲשֶׁר הָיָה בִּימֵי אַבְרָהָם; וַיֵּלֶךְ יִצְחָק אֶל-אֲבִימֶלֶךְ מֶלֶךְ-פְּלִשְׁתִּים, גְּרָרָה.
And there was a famine in the land, besides the first famine that was in the days of Abraham; and Isaac went to Abimelech king of the Philistines, to Gerar.note
2
וַיֵּרָא אֵלָיו יְהוָה, וַיֹּאמֶר אַל-תֵּרֵד מִצְרָיְמָה: שְׁכֹן בָּאָרֶץ, אֲשֶׁר אֹמַר אֵלֶיךָ.
And Jehovah appeared to him and said, "Do not go down to Egypt; settle in the land I tell you of.note
3
גּוּר בָּאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת, וְאֶהְיֶה עִמְּךָ וַאֲבָרְכֶךָּ: כִּי-לְךָ וּלְזַרְעֲךָ, אֶתֵּן אֶת-כָּל-הָאֲרָצֹת הָאֵל, וַהֲקִמֹתִי אֶת-הַשְּׁבֻעָה, אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי לְאַבְרָהָם אָבִיךָ.
Sojourn in this land, and I will be with you and bless you; for to you and to your seed I will give all these lands, and I will keep the oath I swore to Abraham your father.note
4
וְהִרְבֵּיתִי אֶת-זַרְעֲךָ, כְּכוֹכְבֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם, וְנָתַתִּי לְזַרְעֲךָ, אֵת כָּל-הָאֲרָצֹת הָאֵל; וְהִתְבָּרְכוּ בְזַרְעֲךָ, כֹּל גּוֹיֵי הָאָרֶץ.
And I will multiply your seed like the stars of the heavens, and I will give your seed all these lands; and in your seed all the nations of the earth will bless themselves —note
5
עֵקֶב, אֲשֶׁר-שָׁמַע אַבְרָהָם בְּקֹלִי; וַיִּשְׁמֹר, מִשְׁמַרְתִּי, מִצְוֺתַי, חֻקּוֹתַי וְתוֹרֹתָי.
because Abraham obeyed my voice and kept my charge — my commandments, my statutes, and my laws."note
6
וַיֵּשֶׁב יִצְחָק, בִּגְרָר.
So Isaac dwelt in Gerar.note
7
וַיִּשְׁאֲלוּ אַנְשֵׁי הַמָּקוֹם, לְאִשְׁתּוֹ, וַיֹּאמֶר, אֲחֹתִי הִוא: כִּי יָרֵא, לֵאמֹר אִשְׁתִּי, פֶּן-יַהַרְגֻנִי אַנְשֵׁי הַמָּקוֹם עַל-רִבְקָה, כִּי-טוֹבַת מַרְאֶה הִוא.
And the men of the place asked about his wife, and he said, "She is my sister," for he was afraid to say "my wife" — thinking, "the men of the place might kill me over Rebekah, because she is beautiful to look at."note
8
וַיְהִי, כִּי אָרְכוּ-לוֹ שָׁם הַיָּמִים, וַיַּשְׁקֵף אֲבִימֶלֶךְ מֶלֶךְ פְּלִשְׁתִּים, בְּעַד הַחַלּוֹן; וַיַּרְא, וְהִנֵּה יִצְחָק מְצַחֵק, אֵת, רִבְקָה אִשְׁתּוֹ.
And it happened, when he had been there a long while, that Abimelech king of the Philistines looked out through the window — and there was Isaac caressing Rebekah his wife.note
9
וַיִּקְרָא אֲבִימֶלֶךְ לְיִצְחָק, וַיֹּאמֶר אַךְ הִנֵּה אִשְׁתְּךָ הִוא, וְאֵיךְ אָמַרְתָּ, אֲחֹתִי הִוא; וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו, יִצְחָק, כִּי אָמַרְתִּי, פֶּן-אָמוּת עָלֶיהָ.
And Abimelech called Isaac and said, "So — she is your wife! How could you say, 'She is my sister'?" And Isaac said to him, "Because I thought I might die on account of her."note
10
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ, מַה-זֹּאת עָשִׂיתָ לָּנוּ; כִּמְעַט שָׁכַב אַחַד הָעָם, אֶת-אִשְׁתֶּךָ, וְהֵבֵאתָ עָלֵינוּ, אָשָׁם.
And Abimelech said, "What is this you have done to us? One of the people might easily have lain with your wife, and you would have brought guilt upon us."note
11
וַיְצַו אֲבִימֶלֶךְ, אֶת-כָּל-הָעָם לֵאמֹר: הַנֹּגֵעַ בָּאִישׁ הַזֶּה, וּבְאִשְׁתּוֹ--מוֹת יוּמָת.
And Abimelech charged all the people, saying, "Whoever touches this man or his wife will surely be put to death."note
12
וַיִּזְרַע יִצְחָק בָּאָרֶץ הַהִוא, וַיִּמְצָא בַּשָּׁנָה הַהִוא מֵאָה שְׁעָרִים; וַיְבָרְכֵהוּ, יְהוָה.
And Isaac sowed in that land and reaped that same year a hundredfold, and Jehovah blessed him.note
13
וַיִּגְדַּל, הָאִישׁ; וַיֵּלֶךְ הָלוֹךְ וְגָדֵל, עַד כִּי-גָדַל מְאֹד.
And the man grew great, and went on growing greater and greater, until he was very great.note
14
וַיְהִי-לוֹ מִקְנֵה-צֹאן וּמִקְנֵה בָקָר, וַעֲבֻדָּה רַבָּה; וַיְקַנְאוּ אֹתוֹ, פְּלִשְׁתִּים.
And he had flocks and herds and a large household, and the Philistines envied him.note
15
וְכָל-הַבְּאֵרֹת, אֲשֶׁר חָפְרוּ עַבְדֵי אָבִיו, בִּימֵי, אַבְרָהָם אָבִיו--סִתְּמוּם פְּלִשְׁתִּים, וַיְמַלְאוּם עָפָר.
And all the wells his father's servants had dug in the days of Abraham his father, the Philistines stopped up and filled with earth.note
16
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ, אֶל-יִצְחָק: לֵךְ, מֵעִמָּנוּ, כִּי-עָצַמְתָּ מִמֶּנּוּ, מְאֹד.
And Abimelech said to Isaac, "Go away from us, for you have grown far mightier than we."note
17
וַיֵּלֶךְ מִשָּׁם, יִצְחָק; וַיִּחַן בְּנַחַל-גְּרָר, וַיֵּשֶׁב שָׁם.
So Isaac went from there and encamped in the valley of Gerar and dwelt there.note
18
וַיָּשָׁב יִצְחָק וַיַּחְפֹּר אֶת-בְּאֵרֹת הַמַּיִם, אֲשֶׁר חָפְרוּ בִּימֵי אַבְרָהָם אָבִיו, וַיְסַתְּמוּם פְּלִשְׁתִּים, אַחֲרֵי מוֹת אַבְרָהָם; וַיִּקְרָא לָהֶן, שֵׁמוֹת, כַּשֵּׁמֹת, אֲשֶׁר-קָרָא לָהֶן אָבִיו.
And Isaac dug again the wells of water they had dug in the days of Abraham his father, which the Philistines had stopped up after Abraham's death; and he gave them the same names his father had given them.note
19
וַיַּחְפְּרוּ עַבְדֵי-יִצְחָק, בַּנָּחַל; וַיִּמְצְאוּ-שָׁם--בְּאֵר, מַיִם חַיִּים.
And Isaac's servants dug in the valley and found there a well of living water.note
20
וַיָּרִיבוּ רֹעֵי גְרָר, עִם-רֹעֵי יִצְחָק לֵאמֹר--לָנוּ הַמָּיִם; וַיִּקְרָא שֵׁם-הַבְּאֵר עֵשֶׂק, כִּי הִתְעַשְּׂקוּ עִמּוֹ.
And the herdsmen of Gerar quarreled with Isaac's herdsmen, saying, "The water is ours." So he named the well Esek, because they contended with him.note
21
וַיַּחְפְּרוּ בְּאֵר אַחֶרֶת, וַיָּרִיבוּ גַּם-עָלֶיהָ; וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמָהּ, שִׂטְנָה.
And they dug another well, and quarreled over that one too; so he named it Sitnah.note
22
וַיַּעְתֵּק מִשָּׁם, וַיַּחְפֹּר בְּאֵר אַחֶרֶת, וְלֹא רָבוּ, עָלֶיהָ; וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמָהּ, רְחֹבוֹת, וַיֹּאמֶר כִּי-עַתָּה הִרְחִיב יְהוָה לָנוּ, וּפָרִינוּ בָאָרֶץ.
And he moved on from there and dug another well, and over that one they did not quarrel; so he named it Rehoboth, and said, "For now Jehovah has made room for us, and we will be fruitful in the land."note
23
וַיַּעַל מִשָּׁם, בְּאֵר שָׁבַע.
And he went up from there to Beersheba.note
24
וַיֵּרָא אֵלָיו יְהוָה, בַּלַּיְלָה הַהוּא, וַיֹּאמֶר, אָנֹכִי אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם אָבִיךָ; אַל-תִּירָא, כִּי-אִתְּךָ אָנֹכִי, וּבֵרַכְתִּיךָ וְהִרְבֵּיתִי אֶת-זַרְעֲךָ, בַּעֲבוּר אַבְרָהָם עַבְדִּי.
And Jehovah appeared to him that same night and said, "I am the God of Abraham your father. Do not fear, for I am with you; and I will bless you and multiply your seed for the sake of Abraham my servant."note
25
וַיִּבֶן שָׁם מִזְבֵּחַ, וַיִּקְרָא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה, וַיֶּט-שָׁם, אָהֳלוֹ; וַיִּכְרוּ-שָׁם עַבְדֵי-יִצְחָק, בְּאֵר.
And he built an altar there and called on the name of Jehovah, and pitched his tent there; and there Isaac's servants dug a well.note
26
וַאֲבִימֶלֶךְ, הָלַךְ אֵלָיו מִגְּרָר; וַאֲחֻזַּת, מֵרֵעֵהוּ, וּפִיכֹל, שַׂר-צְבָאוֹ.
Then Abimelech came to him from Gerar, with Ahuzzath his friend and Phicol the captain of his army.note
27
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יִצְחָק, מַדּוּעַ בָּאתֶם אֵלָי; וְאַתֶּם שְׂנֵאתֶם אֹתִי, וַתְּשַׁלְּחוּנִי מֵאִתְּכֶם.
And Isaac said to them, "Why have you come to me, seeing you hate me and sent me away from you?"note
28
וַיֹּאמְרוּ, רָאוֹ רָאִינוּ כִּי-הָיָה יְהוָה עִמָּךְ, וַנֹּאמֶר תְּהִי נָא אָלָה בֵּינוֹתֵינוּ, בֵּינֵינוּ וּבֵינֶךָ; וְנִכְרְתָה בְרִית, עִמָּךְ.
And they said, "We have seen plainly that Jehovah is with you; so we said, Let there be an oath between us — between us and you — and let us make a covenant with you,note
29
אִם-תַּעֲשֵׂה עִמָּנוּ רָעָה, כַּאֲשֶׁר לֹא נְגַעֲנוּךָ, וְכַאֲשֶׁר עָשִׂינוּ עִמְּךָ רַק-טוֹב, וַנְּשַׁלֵּחֲךָ בְּשָׁלוֹם; אַתָּה עַתָּה, בְּרוּךְ יְהוָה.
that you will do us no harm, just as we have not touched you, and have done you nothing but good, and sent you away in peace. You are now the blessed of Jehovah."note
30
וַיַּעַשׂ לָהֶם מִשְׁתֶּה, וַיֹּאכְלוּ וַיִּשְׁתּוּ.
And he made them a feast, and they ate and drank.note
31
וַיַּשְׁכִּימוּ בַבֹּקֶר, וַיִּשָּׁבְעוּ אִישׁ לְאָחִיו; וַיְשַׁלְּחֵם יִצְחָק, וַיֵּלְכוּ מֵאִתּוֹ בְּשָׁלוֹם.
And they rose early in the morning and swore to one another; and Isaac sent them off, and they departed from him in peace.note
32
וַיְהִי בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא, וַיָּבֹאוּ עַבְדֵי יִצְחָק, וַיַּגִּדוּ לוֹ, עַל-אֹדוֹת הַבְּאֵר אֲשֶׁר חָפָרוּ; וַיֹּאמְרוּ לוֹ, מָצָאנוּ מָיִם.
And it happened that same day that Isaac's servants came and told him about the well they had dug, and said to him, "We have found water!"note
33
וַיִּקְרָא אֹתָהּ, שִׁבְעָה; עַל-כֵּן שֵׁם-הָעִיר בְּאֵר שֶׁבַע, עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה. {ס}
And he named it Shibah; therefore the name of the city is Beersheba to this day.note
34
וַיְהִי עֵשָׂו, בֶּן-אַרְבָּעִים שָׁנָה, וַיִּקַּח אִשָּׁה אֶת-יְהוּדִית, בַּת-בְּאֵרִי הַחִתִּי--וְאֶת-בָּשְׂמַת, בַּת-אֵילֹן הַחִתִּי.
And when Esau was forty years old, he took as wives Judith daughter of Beeri the Hittite, and Basemath daughter of Elon the Hittite;note
35
וַתִּהְיֶיןָ, מֹרַת רוּחַ, לְיִצְחָק, וּלְרִבְקָה. {ס}
and they were a bitterness of spirit to Isaac and to Rebekah.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

The one chapter given wholly to Isaac — the quiet patriarch who re-walks and re-digs his father's ground. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–6 · עֵקֶב אֲשֶׁר-שָׁמַע אַבְרָהָם בְּקֹלִי eqev asher-shama Avraham be-qoli

The promise passes to the son. A famine sends Isaac south, just as one sent his father — but where Abram went down to Egypt (12:10), Isaac is told plainly, "Do not go down to Egypt." He is the patriarch of staying. And there God re-grants him the entire oath sworn to Abraham — seed like the stars, all the nations blessed in his seed (22:17) — the whole Aqedah promise handed to the next generation. Its ground is striking: "because Abraham obeyed my voice" (the obedience of the binding of Isaac, 22:18), and "kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws." That last line is remarkable — the vocabulary of the Torah (mitzvot, chuqqim, torot) laid on Abraham centuries before Sinai. Some read it as later covenant language reaching back; others as the narrator's way of saying the man of faith already kept, by trust, the heart of what the Law would later spell out. Either way, Isaac inherits the covenant not on his own record but on his father's.

Verses 7–16 · מְצַחֵק metsacheq — "Isaac-ing"

The sister-ruse, a third time. For the third time in Genesis a patriarch passes his wife off as his sister for fear of a foreign king — Abraham did it in Egypt (ch. 12) and at Gerar (ch. 20), and now Isaac repeats it in the very same town. The threefold telling is one of the book's most-discussed features: some read three real episodes (the ruse being a known survival tactic), others one old tradition told three ways. This translation lets all three stand as the text gives them and marks the pattern rather than smoothing it away.

Caught by a caress. What gives Isaac away is a single verb: Abimelech looks out a window and sees Isaac metsacheq with Rebekah — KJV "sporting," here "caressing," the affectionate play of a husband, not a brother. And the verb is his own name: metsacheq is from tsachaq, "to laugh, play" — the root of Yitschaq, "Isaac." He is literally caught Isaac-ing. (It is the same participle used of Ishmael "laughing/mocking" that got Hagar sent away, 21:9 — the word cuts warm here, cold there.) Then the blessing shows: Isaac sows and reaps a hundredfold, grows "greater and greater until he was very great," and the Philistines' envy stops up his father's wells.

Verses 17–25 · עֵשֶׂק · שִׂטְנָה · רְחֹבוֹת Esek · Sitnah · Rehoboth

Three wells, and a man who keeps yielding. Isaac's whole character is in this quiet sequence. He does not fight for the wells the Philistines filled in; he re-digs his father's wells and gives them back their father's names — a life of restoration, not conquest. When his men strike fresh water, the herdsmen of Gerar seize it, and he names the well Esek, "Contention." He digs again; they seize that one too, and he names it Sitnah, "Enmity" — the same root, satan, that will one day name the accuser himself. He does not draw a sword; he simply moves on and digs a third, and this one no one fights over — Rehoboth, "Room": "for now Jehovah has made room for us, and we will be fruitful in the land." Contention, enmity, room — a small parable of the patience that outlasts a fight. At Beersheba God meets him in the night with the word given his father before him: "Do not fear, for I am with you" (15:1), and Isaac builds an altar and calls on the Name.

Verses 26–35 · בְּאֵר שֶׁבַע … מֹרַת רוּחַ Be'er Sheva … morat ruach

The treaty, twice. Abimelech comes with Phicol, captain of his army, to cut a covenant — and the reader has watched this exact scene before: Abraham made a pact with an Abimelech and a Phicol at this same Beersheba (21:22-32), some eighty years earlier. Same king's name, same commander's name, same place, same sworn oath. Most take "Abimelech" and "Phicol" as dynastic titles (like "Pharaoh") worn by father and son; a few read one remembered tradition told of both patriarchs. What the pagan king says is the point either way: "We have seen plainly that Jehovah is with you" — the blessing is visible from outside. And the well dug that very day gives the city its name a second time: Isaac calls it Shibah — "oath" (and "seven," the same pun, since "to swear" in Hebrew is "to seven oneself") — re-founding the Beersheba his father had named for the same sworn word (21:31).

A bitterness that sets the next scene. The chapter ends on a sour domestic note: Esau, at forty, marries two Hittite women, and they are "a bitterness of spirit" (morat ruach; KJV "grief of mind") to Isaac and Rebekah. It is a small line with a long fuse — Rebekah's disgust at Hittite daughters-in-law is exactly the pretext she will use, one chapter on, to send Jacob away to the old country (27:46), the journey on which the whole rest of Genesis turns.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: the three well-names carried with their meanings audible — Esek "contention," Sitnah "enmity" (the satan root), Rehoboth "room"; "caressing" for metsacheq (the KJV's "sporting"), so the pun on Isaac's own name stays visible; and "a bitterness of spirit" for morat ruach (KJV "grief of mind").

Echoes paid. The famine that echoes Abraham's (12:10) but keeps Isaac in the land; the Aqedah oath re-granted (22:17) on the ground of Abraham's obedience; metsacheq paying the Ishmael word; "do not fear," the word to Abram; and Beersheba named for an oath a second time (21:31).

Next installment in this book: Genesis 27 — the stolen blessing: Rebekah and Jacob deceive the blind, dying Isaac, and Esau's cry, "Bless me too, my father!"

Genesis 27 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיְהִי כִּי-זָקֵן יִצְחָק, וַתִּכְהֶיןָ עֵינָיו מֵרְאֹת; וַיִּקְרָא אֶת-עֵשָׂו בְּנוֹ הַגָּדֹל, וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו בְּנִי, וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו, הִנֵּנִי.
