And it happened, when Isaac was old and his eyes were too dim to see, that he called Esau his elder son and said to him, "My son." And he said to him, "Here I am."note
2
וַיֹּאמֶר, הִנֵּה-נָא זָקַנְתִּי; לֹא יָדַעְתִּי, יוֹם מוֹתִי.
And he said, "See now, I am old; I do not know the day of my death.note
3
וְעַתָּה שָׂא-נָא כֵלֶיךָ, תֶּלְיְךָ וְקַשְׁתֶּךָ; וְצֵא, הַשָּׂדֶה, וְצוּדָה לִּי, צידה צָיִד.
So now take your gear, your quiver and your bow, and go out to the field and hunt me some game.note
4
וַעֲשֵׂה-לִי מַטְעַמִּים כַּאֲשֶׁר אָהַבְתִּי, וְהָבִיאָה לִּי--וְאֹכֵלָה: בַּעֲבוּר תְּבָרֶכְךָ נַפְשִׁי, בְּטֶרֶם אָמוּת.
And make me savory food, the kind I love, and bring it to me to eat, so that my soul may bless you before I die."note
5
וְרִבְקָה שֹׁמַעַת--בְּדַבֵּר יִצְחָק, אֶל-עֵשָׂו בְּנוֹ; וַיֵּלֶךְ עֵשָׂו הַשָּׂדֶה, לָצוּד צַיִד לְהָבִיא.
Now Rebekah was listening as Isaac spoke to Esau his son. And Esau went to the field to hunt game and bring it.note
6
וְרִבְקָה, אָמְרָה, אֶל-יַעֲקֹב בְּנָהּ, לֵאמֹר: הִנֵּה שָׁמַעְתִּי אֶת-אָבִיךָ, מְדַבֵּר אֶל-עֵשָׂו אָחִיךָ לֵאמֹר.
And Rebekah said to Jacob her son, "Look, I heard your father speaking to Esau your brother, saying,note
7
הָבִיאָה לִּי צַיִד וַעֲשֵׂה-לִי מַטְעַמִּים, וְאֹכֵלָה; וַאֲבָרֶכְכָה לִפְנֵי יְהוָה, לִפְנֵי מוֹתִי.
'Bring me game and make me savory food to eat, that I may bless you before Jehovah before my death.'note
8
וְעַתָּה בְנִי, שְׁמַע בְּקֹלִי--לַאֲשֶׁר אֲנִי, מְצַוָּה אֹתָךְ.
Now, my son, obey my voice in what I command you.note
9
לֶךְ-נָא, אֶל-הַצֹּאן, וְקַח-לִי מִשָּׁם שְׁנֵי גְּדָיֵי עִזִּים, טֹבִים; וְאֶעֱשֶׂה אֹתָם מַטְעַמִּים לְאָבִיךָ, כַּאֲשֶׁר אָהֵב.
Go to the flock and get me two good young goats from there, and I will make them into savory food for your father, the kind he loves.note
10
וְהֵבֵאתָ לְאָבִיךָ, וְאָכָל, בַּעֲבֻר אֲשֶׁר יְבָרֶכְךָ, לִפְנֵי מוֹתוֹ.
And you will bring it to your father, and he will eat, so that he may bless you before his death."note
11
וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב, אֶל-רִבְקָה אִמּוֹ: הֵן עֵשָׂו אָחִי אִישׁ שָׂעִר, וְאָנֹכִי אִישׁ חָלָק.
And Jacob said to Rebekah his mother, "But Esau my brother is a hairy man, and I am a smooth man.note
12
אוּלַי יְמֻשֵּׁנִי אָבִי, וְהָיִיתִי בְעֵינָיו כִּמְתַעְתֵּעַ; וְהֵבֵאתִי עָלַי קְלָלָה, וְלֹא בְרָכָה.
Suppose my father feels me — then I would seem in his eyes a mocker, and bring on myself a curse and not a blessing."note
13
וַתֹּאמֶר לוֹ אִמּוֹ, עָלַי קִלְלָתְךָ בְּנִי; אַךְ שְׁמַע בְּקֹלִי, וְלֵךְ קַח-לִי.
And his mother said to him, "Your curse be on me, my son; only obey my voice, and go, get them for me."note
14
וַיֵּלֶךְ, וַיִּקַּח, וַיָּבֵא, לְאִמּוֹ; וַתַּעַשׂ אִמּוֹ מַטְעַמִּים, כַּאֲשֶׁר אָהֵב אָבִיו.
So he went and got them and brought them to his mother, and his mother made savory food, the kind his father loved.note
15
וַתִּקַּח רִבְקָה אֶת-בִּגְדֵי עֵשָׂו בְּנָהּ הַגָּדֹל, הַחֲמֻדֹת, אֲשֶׁר אִתָּהּ, בַּבָּיִת; וַתַּלְבֵּשׁ אֶת-יַעֲקֹב, בְּנָהּ הַקָּטָן.
And Rebekah took the finest garments of Esau her elder son, which she had with her in the house, and put them on Jacob her younger son;note
16
וְאֵת, עֹרֹת גְּדָיֵי הָעִזִּים, הִלְבִּישָׁה, עַל-יָדָיו--וְעַל, חֶלְקַת צַוָּארָיו.
and she put the skins of the young goats on his hands and on the smooth of his neck.note
17
וַתִּתֵּן אֶת-הַמַּטְעַמִּים וְאֶת-הַלֶּחֶם, אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂתָה, בְּיַד, יַעֲקֹב בְּנָהּ.
And she put the savory food and the bread she had made into the hand of her son Jacob.note
18
וַיָּבֹא אֶל-אָבִיו, וַיֹּאמֶר אָבִי; וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֶּנִּי, מִי אַתָּה בְּנִי.
And he came to his father and said, "My father." And he said, "Here I am; who are you, my son?"note
19
וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֶל-אָבִיו, אָנֹכִי עֵשָׂו בְּכֹרֶךָ--עָשִׂיתִי, כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתָּ אֵלָי; קוּם-נָא שְׁבָה, וְאָכְלָה מִצֵּידִי--בַּעֲבוּר, תְּבָרְכַנִּי נַפְשֶׁךָ.
And Jacob said to his father, "I am Esau your firstborn; I have done as you told me. Please rise, sit, and eat of my game, so that your soul may bless me."note
20
וַיֹּאמֶר יִצְחָק אֶל-בְּנוֹ, מַה-זֶּה מִהַרְתָּ לִמְצֹא בְּנִי; וַיֹּאמֶר, כִּי הִקְרָה יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ לְפָנָי.
And Isaac said to his son, "How is it you found it so quickly, my son?" And he said, "Because Jehovah your God brought it my way."note
21
וַיֹּאמֶר יִצְחָק אֶל-יַעֲקֹב, גְּשָׁה-נָּא וַאֲמֻשְׁךָ בְּנִי: הַאַתָּה זֶה בְּנִי עֵשָׂו, אִם-לֹא.
And Isaac said to Jacob, "Come near, please, that I may feel you, my son — whether you really are my son Esau or not."note
22
וַיִּגַּשׁ יַעֲקֹב אֶל-יִצְחָק אָבִיו, וַיְמֻשֵּׁהוּ; וַיֹּאמֶר, הַקֹּל קוֹל יַעֲקֹב, וְהַיָּדַיִם, יְדֵי עֵשָׂו.
And Jacob came near to Isaac his father, and he felt him and said, "The voice is the voice of Jacob, but the hands are the hands of Esau."note
23
וְלֹא הִכִּירוֹ--כִּי-הָיוּ יָדָיו כִּידֵי עֵשָׂו אָחִיו, שְׂעִרֹת; וַיְבָרְכֵהוּ.
And he did not recognize him, because his hands were hairy like the hands of his brother Esau; so he blessed him.note
24
וַיֹּאמֶר, אַתָּה זֶה בְּנִי עֵשָׂו; וַיֹּאמֶר, אָנִי.
And he said, "Are you really my son Esau?" And he said, "I am."note
25
וַיֹּאמֶר, הַגִּשָׁה לִּי וְאֹכְלָה מִצֵּיד בְּנִי--לְמַעַן תְּבָרֶכְךָ, נַפְשִׁי; וַיַּגֶּשׁ-לוֹ, וַיֹּאכַל, וַיָּבֵא לוֹ יַיִן, וַיֵּשְׁתְּ.
And he said, "Bring it near to me, that I may eat of my son's game, so that my soul may bless you." And he brought it near, and he ate; and he brought him wine, and he drank.note
26
וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו, יִצְחָק אָבִיו: גְּשָׁה-נָּא וּשְׁקָה-לִּי, בְּנִי.
And Isaac his father said to him, "Come near now and kiss me, my son."note
27
וַיִּגַּשׁ, וַיִּשַּׁק-לוֹ, וַיָּרַח אֶת-רֵיחַ בְּגָדָיו, וַיְבָרְכֵהוּ; וַיֹּאמֶר, רְאֵה רֵיחַ בְּנִי, כְּרֵיחַ שָׂדֶה, אֲשֶׁר בֵּרְכוֹ יְהוָה.
And he came near and kissed him, and he smelled the smell of his garments, and blessed him, and said,
"See, the smell of my son
is like the smell of a field that Jehovah has blessed.note
28
וְיִתֶּן-לְךָ, הָאֱלֹהִים, מִטַּל הַשָּׁמַיִם, וּמִשְׁמַנֵּי הָאָרֶץ--וְרֹב דָּגָן, וְתִירֹשׁ.
And may God give you
of the dew of the heavens
and of the fat places of the earth,
and plenty of grain and new wine.note
29
יַעַבְדוּךָ עַמִּים, וישתחו וְיִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לְךָ לְאֻמִּים--הֱוֵה גְבִיר לְאַחֶיךָ, וְיִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לְךָ בְּנֵי אִמֶּךָ; אֹרְרֶיךָ אָרוּר, וּמְבָרְכֶיךָ בָּרוּךְ.
Let peoples serve you,
and nations bow down to you;
be lord over your brothers,
and let your mother's sons bow down to you.
Cursed be those who curse you,
and blessed be those who bless you."note
30
וַיְהִי, כַּאֲשֶׁר כִּלָּה יִצְחָק לְבָרֵךְ אֶת-יַעֲקֹב, וַיְהִי אַךְ יָצֹא יָצָא יַעֲקֹב, מֵאֵת פְּנֵי יִצְחָק אָבִיו; וְעֵשָׂו אָחִיו, בָּא מִצֵּידוֹ.
And it happened, just as Isaac finished blessing Jacob — Jacob had barely gone out from the presence of Isaac his father — that Esau his brother came in from his hunting.note
31
וַיַּעַשׂ גַּם-הוּא מַטְעַמִּים, וַיָּבֵא לְאָבִיו; וַיֹּאמֶר לְאָבִיו, יָקֻם אָבִי וְיֹאכַל מִצֵּיד בְּנוֹ--בַּעֲבֻר, תְּבָרְכַנִּי נַפְשֶׁךָ.
And he too made savory food and brought it to his father, and said to his father, "Let my father rise and eat of his son's game, so that your soul may bless me."note
32
וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ יִצְחָק אָבִיו, מִי-אָתָּה; וַיֹּאמֶר, אֲנִי בִּנְךָ בְכֹרְךָ עֵשָׂו.
And Isaac his father said to him, "Who are you?" And he said, "I am your son, your firstborn, Esau."note
33
וַיֶּחֱרַד יִצְחָק חֲרָדָה, גְּדֹלָה עַד-מְאֹד, וַיֹּאמֶר מִי-אֵפוֹא הוּא הַצָּד-צַיִד וַיָּבֵא לִי וָאֹכַל מִכֹּל בְּטֶרֶם תָּבוֹא, וָאֲבָרְכֵהוּ; גַּם-בָּרוּךְ, יִהְיֶה.
And Isaac trembled with a very great trembling and said, "Who was it, then, who hunted game and brought it to me? I ate it all before you came, and I blessed him — and blessed he shall be!"note
34
כִּשְׁמֹעַ עֵשָׂו, אֶת-דִּבְרֵי אָבִיו, וַיִּצְעַק צְעָקָה, גְּדֹלָה וּמָרָה עַד-מְאֹד; וַיֹּאמֶר לְאָבִיו, בָּרְכֵנִי גַם-אָנִי אָבִי.
When Esau heard the words of his father, he cried out a great and exceedingly bitter cry, and said to his father, "Bless me too, my father!"note
35
וַיֹּאמֶר, בָּא אָחִיךָ בְּמִרְמָה; וַיִּקַּח, בִּרְכָתֶךָ.
And he said, "Your brother came with deceit, and took your blessing."note
36
וַיֹּאמֶר הֲכִי קָרָא שְׁמוֹ יַעֲקֹב, וַיַּעְקְבֵנִי זֶה פַעֲמַיִם--אֶת-בְּכֹרָתִי לָקָח, וְהִנֵּה עַתָּה לָקַח בִּרְכָתִי; וַיֹּאמַר, הֲלֹא-אָצַלְתָּ לִּי בְּרָכָה.
And he said, "Is he not rightly named Jacob? For he has supplanted me these two times: he took my birthright, and now, look, he has taken my blessing." And he said, "Have you not reserved a blessing for me?"note
37
וַיַּעַן יִצְחָק וַיֹּאמֶר לְעֵשָׂו, הֵן גְּבִיר שַׂמְתִּיו לָךְ וְאֶת-כָּל-אֶחָיו נָתַתִּי לוֹ לַעֲבָדִים, וְדָגָן וְתִירֹשׁ, סְמַכְתִּיו; וּלְכָה אֵפוֹא, מָה אֶעֱשֶׂה בְּנִי.
And Isaac answered and said to Esau, "Look, I have made him lord over you, and all his brothers I have given him as servants, and with grain and new wine I have sustained him. So for you — what then can I do, my son?"note
38
וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו אֶל-אָבִיו, הַבְרָכָה אַחַת הִוא-לְךָ אָבִי--בָּרְכֵנִי גַם-אָנִי, אָבִי; וַיִּשָּׂא עֵשָׂו קֹלוֹ, וַיֵּבְךְּ.
And Esau said to his father, "Have you but one blessing, my father? Bless me too, my father!" And Esau lifted up his voice and wept.note
39
וַיַּעַן יִצְחָק אָבִיו, וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו: הִנֵּה מִשְׁמַנֵּי הָאָרֶץ, יִהְיֶה מוֹשָׁבֶךָ, וּמִטַּל הַשָּׁמַיִם, מֵעָל.
And Isaac his father answered and said to him,
"Behold, away from the fat places of the earth will be your dwelling,
and away from the dew of the heavens above.note
40
וְעַל-חַרְבְּךָ תִחְיֶה, וְאֶת-אָחִיךָ תַּעֲבֹד; וְהָיָה כַּאֲשֶׁר תָּרִיד, וּפָרַקְתָּ עֻלּוֹ מֵעַל צַוָּארֶךָ.
By your sword you will live,
and you will serve your brother;
but it will happen, when you break loose,
that you will tear his yoke from your neck."note
41
וַיִּשְׂטֹם עֵשָׂו, אֶת-יַעֲקֹב, עַל-הַבְּרָכָה, אֲשֶׁר בֵּרְכוֹ אָבִיו; וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו בְּלִבּוֹ, יִקְרְבוּ יְמֵי אֵבֶל אָבִי, וְאַהַרְגָה, אֶת-יַעֲקֹב אָחִי.
And Esau harbored enmity against Jacob because of the blessing his father had given him; and Esau said in his heart, "The days of mourning for my father are near; then I will kill my brother Jacob."note
42
וַיֻּגַּד לְרִבְקָה, אֶת-דִּבְרֵי עֵשָׂו בְּנָהּ הַגָּדֹל; וַתִּשְׁלַח וַתִּקְרָא לְיַעֲקֹב, בְּנָהּ הַקָּטָן, וַתֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו, הִנֵּה עֵשָׂו אָחִיךָ מִתְנַחֵם לְךָ לְהָרְגֶךָ.
And the words of Esau her elder son were told to Rebekah; and she sent and called Jacob her younger son and said to him, "Look, your brother Esau is consoling himself with the thought of killing you.note
43
וְעַתָּה בְנִי, שְׁמַע בְּקֹלִי; וְקוּם בְּרַח-לְךָ אֶל-לָבָן אָחִי, חָרָנָה.
So now, my son, obey my voice: rise, flee to Laban my brother, to Haran,note
44
וְיָשַׁבְתָּ עִמּוֹ, יָמִים אֲחָדִים--עַד אֲשֶׁר-תָּשׁוּב, חֲמַת אָחִיךָ.
and stay with him a while, until your brother's fury turns away —note
45
עַד-שׁוּב אַף-אָחִיךָ מִמְּךָ, וְשָׁכַח אֵת אֲשֶׁר-עָשִׂיתָ לּוֹ, וְשָׁלַחְתִּי, וּלְקַחְתִּיךָ מִשָּׁם; לָמָה אֶשְׁכַּל גַּם-שְׁנֵיכֶם, יוֹם אֶחָד.
until your brother's anger turns from you and he forgets what you did to him; then I will send and bring you back from there. Why should I be bereaved of you both in one day?"note
46
וַתֹּאמֶר רִבְקָה, אֶל-יִצְחָק, קַצְתִּי בְחַיַּי, מִפְּנֵי בְּנוֹת חֵת; אִם-לֹקֵחַ יַעֲקֹב אִשָּׁה מִבְּנוֹת-חֵת כָּאֵלֶּה, מִבְּנוֹת הָאָרֶץ--לָמָּה לִּי, חַיִּים.
And Rebekah said to Isaac, "I loathe my life because of the daughters of Heth. If Jacob takes a wife from the daughters of Heth like these, from the daughters of the land — what good is my life to me?"note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

The stolen blessing — a blind old man deceived by his wife and son, and a rejected brother's cry. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–17 · מַטְעַמִּים … אִישׁ שָׂעִר וְאִישׁ חָלָק mat'ammim … ish sa'ir ve-ish chalaq

The plot. Isaac is old and blind, and thinks himself near death (he will in fact live some forty years more). He wants to bless Esau, and the trigger is small and human: the savory food (mat'ammim, a favorite dish) he loves. Rebekah, who has never forgotten the oracle that "the elder will serve the younger" (25:23), overhears — and moves. She will cook the disguise from two young goats and clothe Jacob in Esau's garments and their skins. Jacob's objection is not moral but practical: "Esau my brother is a hairy man (sa'ir), and I am a smooth man (chalaq) — if my father feels me, I would seem in his eyes a mocker" (metateia, one caught making a fool of him) and earn a curse. Rebekah takes even that on herself: "Your curse be on me, my son." Nowhere does the narrator pause to approve; it simply shows a mother and a son conspiring to deceive a blind father — and lets the cost come.

Verses 18–29 · הַקֹּל קוֹל יַעֲקֹב, וְהַיָּדַיִם יְדֵי עֵשָׂו ha-qol qol Ya'aqov, ve-ha-yadayim yedei Esav

Four senses, and the one that told the truth. Robbed of sight, Isaac tests his son by every other sense — and the deception survives each. Touch: the goatskins make Jacob's hands hairy like Esau's. Taste: the savory food and wine. Smell: Esau's field-scented garments — "the smell of my son is like the smell of a field Jehovah has blessed." Only hearing catches it: "the voice is the voice of Jacob, but the hands are the hands of Esau" — the one sense that told the truth, and the one Isaac overrode. And so the blessing is spoken over the wrong son: the covenant plenty (dew, fatness, grain and new wine), then dominion — "be lord over your brothers, let your mother's sons bow" — which is the very oracle of 25:23 enacted; and finally Abraham's own words, "cursed be those who curse you, and blessed be those who bless you" (12:3). The whole promise, stolen intact.

Verses 30–40 · בְּמִרְמָה … צְעָקָה גְּדֹלָה וּמָרָה be-mirmah … tse'aqah gedolah u-marah

The word that cannot be recalled. Esau walks in moments later, and Isaac trembles with a very great trembling — a violent, uncontrollable shudder — as he grasps what has happened. And then the line that makes the whole scene turn: "I blessed him — and blessed he shall be!" In Genesis a father's blessing is not a wish but a released, irrevocable word; once spoken, it stands. Esau answers with the most anguished cry in the book — a "great and exceedingly bitter cry" — "Bless me too, my father!" (the New Testament remembers him as the man who "found no place for repentance, though he sought it with tears," Hebrews 12:17). Isaac names the theft plainly: "your brother came with deceit" (mirmah; KJV "subtilty"). And Esau turns the name into an accusation: "Is he not rightly named Jacob (Ya'aqov)? He has supplanted me (ya'aqveni) these two times — my birthright, and now my blessing." What is left for Esau is a near-anti-blessing, and a real crux: "away from the fat places of the earth will be your dwelling" — the same Hebrew preposition (min) that gave Jacob his portion "of" the fatness now, in context, most likely means "away from" (Edom's land is dry); the KJV reads it in parallel, "of the fatness," but the sword-and-servitude that follows fits the harsher sense. His one hope: "when you break loose, you will tear his yoke from your neck" — Edom's later revolts against Judah in a single line.

Verses 41–46 · קַצְתִּי בְחַיַּי qatsti be-chayyai — "I loathe my life"

The cost begins. Esau's grief curdles into murder: "when my father is mourned, I will kill my brother Jacob" — the oldest sin in the family, brother rising against brother as Cain rose against Abel. Rebekah, who engineered the theft, now has to save her favorite from it: flee to my brother Laban "for a few days," she says — and the few days will be twenty years, and she will die without seeing him again. The deceiver is about to begin his own long schooling in being deceived, at Laban's hands. And Rebekah's last word is a masterstroke of misdirection: to Isaac she says nothing of murder, only "I loathe my life because of these Hittite daughters-in-law" — turning Esau's real Hittite marriages (26:34-35) into the perfect, respectable pretext to send Jacob safely east to find a wife. The next chapter follows him out the door.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "deceit" for mirmah (the KJV's "subtilty"), the word that will haunt Jacob's whole life; "savory food" for mat'ammim; the Ya'aqov / ya'aqveni supplant-pun kept audible; and the hard "away from the fat places" of v39 chosen over the KJV's parallel "of the fatness," with the ambiguity flagged.

Echoes paid. The blessing formula "cursed / blessed" of 12:3; the oracle "the elder will serve the younger" (25:23) enacted in "be lord over your brothers"; the two thefts, birthright (25:33) and blessing; Esau's murder-plan echoing Cain; and the Hittite wives turned into Rebekah's pretext.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 28 — Jacob's flight, and the ladder at Bethel: "Surely Jehovah is in this place, and I did not know it."

Genesis 28 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיִּקְרָא יִצְחָק אֶל-יַעֲקֹב, וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתוֹ; וַיְצַוֵּהוּ וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ, לֹא-תִקַּח אִשָּׁה מִבְּנוֹת כְּנָעַן.
And Isaac called Jacob and blessed him, and charged him, saying to him, "You shall not take a wife from the daughters of Canaan.note
2
קוּם לֵךְ פַּדֶּנָה אֲרָם, בֵּיתָה בְתוּאֵל אֲבִי אִמֶּךָ; וְקַח-לְךָ מִשָּׁם אִשָּׁה, מִבְּנוֹת לָבָן אֲחִי אִמֶּךָ.
Arise, go to Paddan-aram, to the house of Bethuel your mother's father, and take a wife for yourself from there, from the daughters of Laban your mother's brother.note
3
וְאֵל שַׁדַּי יְבָרֵךְ אֹתְךָ, וְיַפְרְךָ וְיַרְבֶּךָ; וְהָיִיתָ, לִקְהַל עַמִּים.
And may El Shaddai bless you and make you fruitful and multiply you, so that you become a company of peoples.note
4
וְיִתֶּן-לְךָ אֶת-בִּרְכַּת אַבְרָהָם, לְךָ וּלְזַרְעֲךָ אִתָּךְ--לְרִשְׁתְּךָ אֶת-אֶרֶץ מְגֻרֶיךָ, אֲשֶׁר-נָתַן אֱלֹהִים לְאַבְרָהָם.
And may he give you the blessing of Abraham, to you and to your seed with you, that you may take possession of the land of your sojournings, which God gave to Abraham."note
5
וַיִּשְׁלַח יִצְחָק אֶת-יַעֲקֹב, וַיֵּלֶךְ פַּדֶּנָה אֲרָם--אֶל-לָבָן בֶּן-בְּתוּאֵל, הָאֲרַמִּי, אֲחִי רִבְקָה, אֵם יַעֲקֹב וְעֵשָׂו.
So Isaac sent Jacob away, and he went to Paddan-aram, to Laban son of Bethuel the Aramean, the brother of Rebekah, mother of Jacob and Esau.note
6
וַיַּרְא עֵשָׂו, כִּי-בֵרַךְ יִצְחָק אֶת-יַעֲקֹב, וְשִׁלַּח אֹתוֹ פַּדֶּנָה אֲרָם, לָקַחַת-לוֹ מִשָּׁם אִשָּׁה: בְּבָרְכוֹ אֹתוֹ--וַיְצַו עָלָיו לֵאמֹר, לֹא-תִקַּח אִשָּׁה מִבְּנוֹת כְּנָעַן.
Now Esau saw that Isaac had blessed Jacob and sent him to Paddan-aram to take a wife from there, and that as he blessed him he charged him, "You shall not take a wife from the daughters of Canaan,"note
7
וַיִּשְׁמַע יַעֲקֹב, אֶל-אָבִיו וְאֶל-אִמּוֹ; וַיֵּלֶךְ, פַּדֶּנָה אֲרָם.
and that Jacob had obeyed his father and his mother and gone to Paddan-aram.note
8
וַיַּרְא עֵשָׂו, כִּי רָעוֹת בְּנוֹת כְּנָעַן, בְּעֵינֵי, יִצְחָק אָבִיו.
And Esau saw that the daughters of Canaan were displeasing in the eyes of Isaac his father;note
9
וַיֵּלֶךְ עֵשָׂו, אֶל-יִשְׁמָעֵאל; וַיִּקַּח אֶת-מָחֲלַת בַּת-יִשְׁמָעֵאל בֶּן-אַבְרָהָם אֲחוֹת נְבָיוֹת, עַל-נָשָׁיו--לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה. {ס}
so Esau went to Ishmael and took Mahalath daughter of Ishmael son of Abraham, sister of Nebaioth, in addition to his wives, as a wife.note
10
וַיֵּצֵא יַעֲקֹב, מִבְּאֵר שָׁבַע; וַיֵּלֶךְ, חָרָנָה.
And Jacob went out from Beersheba and went toward Haran.note
11
וַיִּפְגַּע בַּמָּקוֹם וַיָּלֶן שָׁם, כִּי-בָא הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ, וַיִּקַּח מֵאַבְנֵי הַמָּקוֹם, וַיָּשֶׂם מְרַאֲשֹׁתָיו; וַיִּשְׁכַּב, בַּמָּקוֹם הַהוּא.
And he came upon a certain place and stayed the night there, for the sun had set; and he took one of the stones of the place and put it under his head, and lay down in that place.note
12
וַיַּחֲלֹם, וְהִנֵּה סֻלָּם מֻצָּב אַרְצָה, וְרֹאשׁוֹ, מַגִּיעַ הַשָּׁמָיְמָה; וְהִנֵּה מַלְאֲכֵי אֱלֹהִים, עֹלִים וְיֹרְדִים בּוֹ.
And he dreamed — and there was a stairway set on the earth, its top reaching the heavens; and there were the angels of God ascending and descending on it.note
13
וְהִנֵּה יְהוָה נִצָּב עָלָיו, וַיֹּאמַר, אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם אָבִיךָ, וֵאלֹהֵי יִצְחָק; הָאָרֶץ, אֲשֶׁר אַתָּה שֹׁכֵב עָלֶיהָ--לְךָ אֶתְּנֶנָּה, וּלְזַרְעֶךָ.
And there was Jehovah, standing over it, and he said, "I am Jehovah, the God of Abraham your father and the God of Isaac. The land on which you are lying — to you I will give it, and to your seed.note
14
וְהָיָה זַרְעֲךָ כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ, וּפָרַצְתָּ יָמָּה וָקֵדְמָה וְצָפֹנָה וָנֶגְבָּה; וְנִבְרְכוּ בְךָ כָּל-מִשְׁפְּחֹת הָאֲדָמָה, וּבְזַרְעֶךָ.
And your seed will be like the dust of the earth, and you will spread out to the west and the east and the north and the south; and in you and in your seed all the families of the earth will be blessed.note
15
וְהִנֵּה אָנֹכִי עִמָּךְ, וּשְׁמַרְתִּיךָ בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר-תֵּלֵךְ, וַהֲשִׁבֹתִיךָ, אֶל-הָאֲדָמָה הַזֹּאת: כִּי, לֹא אֶעֱזָבְךָ, עַד אֲשֶׁר אִם-עָשִׂיתִי, אֵת אֲשֶׁר-דִּבַּרְתִּי לָךְ.
And look, I am with you, and I will keep you wherever you go, and I will bring you back to this land; for I will not leave you until I have done what I have promised you."note
16
וַיִּיקַץ יַעֲקֹב, מִשְּׁנָתוֹ, וַיֹּאמֶר, אָכֵן יֵשׁ יְהוָה בַּמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה; וְאָנֹכִי, לֹא יָדָעְתִּי.
And Jacob awoke from his sleep and said, "Surely Jehovah is in this place, and I did not know it."note
17
וַיִּירָא, וַיֹּאמַר, מַה-נּוֹרָא, הַמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה: אֵין זֶה, כִּי אִם-בֵּית אֱלֹהִים, וְזֶה, שַׁעַר הַשָּׁמָיִם.
And he was afraid, and said, "How awesome is this place! This is none other than the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven."note
18
וַיַּשְׁכֵּם יַעֲקֹב בַּבֹּקֶר, וַיִּקַּח אֶת-הָאֶבֶן אֲשֶׁר-שָׂם מְרַאֲשֹׁתָיו, וַיָּשֶׂם אֹתָהּ, מַצֵּבָה; וַיִּצֹק שֶׁמֶן, עַל-רֹאשָׁהּ.
And Jacob rose early in the morning and took the stone he had put under his head and set it up as a pillar, and poured oil on its top.note
19
וַיִּקְרָא אֶת-שֵׁם-הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא, בֵּית-אֵל; וְאוּלָם לוּז שֵׁם-הָעִיר, לָרִאשֹׁנָה.
And he named that place Bethel; but Luz was the name of the city before.note
20
וַיִּדַּר יַעֲקֹב, נֶדֶר לֵאמֹר: אִם-יִהְיֶה אֱלֹהִים עִמָּדִי, וּשְׁמָרַנִי בַּדֶּרֶךְ הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר אָנֹכִי הוֹלֵךְ, וְנָתַן-לִי לֶחֶם לֶאֱכֹל, וּבֶגֶד לִלְבֹּשׁ.
And Jacob vowed a vow, saying, "If God will be with me and keep me on this way that I am going, and give me bread to eat and clothing to wear,note
21
וְשַׁבְתִּי בְשָׁלוֹם, אֶל-בֵּית אָבִי; וְהָיָה יְהוָה לִי, לֵאלֹהִים.
and I return in peace to my father's house — then Jehovah will be my God,note
22
וְהָאֶבֶן הַזֹּאת, אֲשֶׁר-שַׂמְתִּי מַצֵּבָה--יִהְיֶה, בֵּית אֱלֹהִים; וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר תִּתֶּן-לִי, עַשֵּׂר אֲעַשְּׂרֶנּוּ לָךְ.
and this stone, which I have set up as a pillar, will be the house of God; and of all that you give me, I will surely give a tenth to you."note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

The fugitive meets God — a stone for a pillow, a stairway to heaven, and the covenant handed on. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–9 · בִּרְכַּת אַבְרָהָם birkat Avraham — "the blessing of Abraham"

The blessing, this time with open eyes. Isaac blesses Jacob again — but now knowing exactly whom he is blessing, and for the first time naming what he passes on: not just dew and dominion (that was the stolen blessing of ch. 27) but "the blessing of Abraham," the covenant itself, "to you and to your seed … to take possession of the land." He blesses in the name Abraham knew God by, El Shaddai (17:1; KJV "God Almighty," kept here untranslated as the covenant-name it is). And the charge that carries the plot is the same one that has run the family since Abraham sent his servant east: no wife from the daughters of Canaan.

Esau, still just missing it. Watching all this, Esau finally grasps that his Canaanite marriages had grieved his parents (26:34-35) — and tries to fix it. But he fixes it the way he does everything, half-right: he goes to Ishmael's line and marries Mahalath, a granddaughter of Abraham — inside the family, yes, but the rejected branch, the son who was sent away. Even his repentance lands just outside the promise.

Verses 10–15 · סֻלָּם sullam — the stairway

Jacob's "ladder" — or stairway. The word sullam occurs only once in the whole Bible, so its exact shape is uncertain. The KJV's "ladder" gave English "Jacob's ladder," but the likely root is salal, "to cast up (a ramp or highway)," which points to a stairway — the monumental temple-stair of a Mesopotamian ziggurat, built precisely as a stepped bridge between earth and heaven for the gods to come down. NWT and most modern scholars read "stairway," and the broad steps fit the traffic the dream shows — angels "ascending and descending" both at once. (Two small ambiguities ride along: the angels move "on it," or possibly "on him," Jacob; and Jehovah stands "over it," the stairway, or "beside him" — the Hebrew allows either.)

Grace to a man on the run. What God says is the whole Abrahamic covenant, spoken now to the grandson — the land he is fleeing (13:15), seed like the dust, "in you and your seed all the families of the earth will be blessed" (12:3) — and then the personal word Jacob most needs on his first night as a fugitive: "I am with you, and I will keep you wherever you go, and I will bring you back." There is not a syllable of condition or rebuke. The deceiver has just cheated his brother and lied to his blind father, and God meets him on the road with pure promise. It is one of the clearest pictures of unearned grace in the book.

Verses 16–19 · בֵּית אֱלֹהִים … שַׁעַר הַשָּׁמָיִם Beit Elohim … sha'ar ha-shamayim

"And I did not know it." Jacob wakes stunned: "Surely Jehovah is in this place, and I did not know it." He had thought he was leaving God behind with his father, in the land he was fleeing — and finds him waiting at a nameless roadside stop. "How awesome (nora; KJV "dreadful," in its old sense of awe-filled) is this place! This is none other than the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven." So he takes his stone pillow, stands it up as a pillar (matsevah), and pours oil on it — the Bible's first anointing of a sacred stone — and names the place Bethel, "house of God" (the town had been called Luz). The stone that cushioned a fugitive's head becomes the marker of the gate of heaven. Expedition Bible has walked the ruins of the site; their film is on the Bethel entry.

Verses 20–22 · וַיִּדַּר יַעֲקֹב נֶדֶר vayyiddar Ya'aqov neder — "Jacob vowed a vow"

Even here, he bargains. God has just given Jacob everything, free and unconditional — and Jacob answers with the Bible's first recorded vow, and it is pure Jacob: an if / then. "If God will be with me and keep me and feed and clothe me and bring me home safe — then Jehovah will be my God." The very things God has already promised outright, Jacob turns into the terms of a deal; the man who bought a birthright and stole a blessing negotiates even with heaven. And yet it is a real beginning: he pledges the stone as God's house and vows a tenth of all he receives — the first vowed tithe, echoing the tenth Abraham gave Melchizedek (14:20). The faith is transactional and immature, but it is faith, and God will spend the next twenty years growing it up.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "stairway" for the once-only sullam (with the NWT and the ziggurat image, keeping the KJV's "ladder" in view as the traditional name); El Shaddai kept untranslated as a covenant-name (vs the shelf's "God Almighty"); "pillar" for matsevah, the sacred stone the Law will later forbid; and the alternate readings of v12–13 ("on it / on him," "over it / beside him") flagged rather than hidden.

Echoes paid — and one still ahead. The land-grant of 13:15; "all the families of the earth blessed" (12:3); El Shaddai from 17:1; "I am with you," the word to Isaac now to Jacob; and the tithe of Melchizedek. And the great one ahead: Jesus tells Nathanael he will see "the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man" (John 1:51) — he is the stairway, the true Bethel where heaven and earth meet.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 29 — Jacob at the well of Haran, his love for Rachel, and Laban's morning trick: "and behold, it was Leah!"

Genesis 29 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב, רַגְלָיו; וַיֵּלֶךְ, אַרְצָה בְנֵי-קֶדֶם.
Then Jacob lifted his feet and went to the land of the people of the east.note
2
וַיַּרְא וְהִנֵּה בְאֵר בַּשָּׂדֶה, וְהִנֵּה-שָׁם שְׁלֹשָׁה עֶדְרֵי-צֹאן רֹבְצִים עָלֶיהָ--כִּי מִן-הַבְּאֵר הַהִוא, יַשְׁקוּ הָעֲדָרִים; וְהָאֶבֶן גְּדֹלָה, עַל-פִּי הַבְּאֵר.
And he looked, and there was a well in the field, and there, three flocks of sheep lying beside it — for from that well they watered the flocks; and the stone on the well's mouth was large.note
3
וְנֶאֶסְפוּ-שָׁמָּה כָל-הָעֲדָרִים, וְגָלְלוּ אֶת-הָאֶבֶן מֵעַל פִּי הַבְּאֵר, וְהִשְׁקוּ, אֶת-הַצֹּאן; וְהֵשִׁיבוּ אֶת-הָאֶבֶן עַל-פִּי הַבְּאֵר, לִמְקֹמָהּ.
When all the flocks were gathered there, they would roll the stone from the well's mouth and water the sheep, and put the stone back on the well's mouth in its place.note
4
וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם יַעֲקֹב, אַחַי מֵאַיִן אַתֶּם; וַיֹּאמְרוּ, מֵחָרָן אֲנָחְנוּ.
And Jacob said to them, "My brothers, where are you from?" And they said, "We are from Haran."note
5
וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם, הַיְדַעְתֶּם אֶת-לָבָן בֶּן-נָחוֹר; וַיֹּאמְרוּ, יָדָעְנוּ.
And he said to them, "Do you know Laban son of Nahor?" And they said, "We know him."note
6
וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם, הֲשָׁלוֹם לוֹ; וַיֹּאמְרוּ שָׁלוֹם--וְהִנֵּה רָחֵל בִּתּוֹ, בָּאָה עִם-הַצֹּאן.
And he said to them, "Is he well?" And they said, "He is well; and here is Rachel his daughter coming with the sheep."note
7
וַיֹּאמֶר, הֵן עוֹד הַיּוֹם גָּדוֹל--לֹא-עֵת, הֵאָסֵף הַמִּקְנֶה; הַשְׁקוּ הַצֹּאן, וּלְכוּ רְעוּ.
And he said, "Look, it is still broad day; it is not time to gather the livestock. Water the sheep, and go, graze them."note
8
וַיֹּאמְרוּ, לֹא נוּכַל, עַד אֲשֶׁר יֵאָסְפוּ כָּל-הָעֲדָרִים, וְגָלְלוּ אֶת-הָאֶבֶן מֵעַל פִּי הַבְּאֵר; וְהִשְׁקִינוּ, הַצֹּאן.
And they said, "We cannot, until all the flocks are gathered and they roll the stone from the well's mouth; then we water the sheep."note
9
עוֹדֶנּוּ, מְדַבֵּר עִמָּם; וְרָחֵל בָּאָה, עִם-הַצֹּאן אֲשֶׁר לְאָבִיהָ--כִּי רֹעָה, הִוא.
While he was still speaking with them, Rachel came with her father's sheep, for she was a shepherdess.note
10
וַיְהִי כַּאֲשֶׁר רָאָה יַעֲקֹב אֶת-רָחֵל, בַּת-לָבָן אֲחִי אִמּוֹ, וְאֶת-צֹאן לָבָן, אֲחִי אִמּוֹ; וַיִּגַּשׁ יַעֲקֹב, וַיָּגֶל אֶת-הָאֶבֶן מֵעַל פִּי הַבְּאֵר, וַיַּשְׁקְ, אֶת-צֹאן לָבָן אֲחִי אִמּוֹ.
And when Jacob saw Rachel daughter of Laban his mother's brother, and the sheep of Laban his mother's brother, Jacob went near and rolled the stone from the well's mouth and watered the flock of Laban his mother's brother.note
11
וַיִּשַּׁק יַעֲקֹב, לְרָחֵל; וַיִּשָּׂא אֶת-קֹלוֹ, וַיֵּבְךְּ.
And Jacob kissed Rachel, and lifted up his voice and wept.note
12
וַיַּגֵּד יַעֲקֹב לְרָחֵל, כִּי אֲחִי אָבִיהָ הוּא, וְכִי בֶן-רִבְקָה, הוּא; וַתָּרָץ, וַתַּגֵּד לְאָבִיהָ.
And Jacob told Rachel that he was her father's kinsman and that he was Rebekah's son; and she ran and told her father.note
13
וַיְהִי כִשְׁמֹעַ לָבָן אֶת-שֵׁמַע יַעֲקֹב בֶּן-אֲחֹתוֹ, וַיָּרָץ לִקְרָאתוֹ וַיְחַבֶּק-לוֹ וַיְנַשֶּׁק-לוֹ, וַיְבִיאֵהוּ, אֶל-בֵּיתוֹ; וַיְסַפֵּר לְלָבָן, אֵת כָּל-הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה.
And when Laban heard the news of Jacob his sister's son, he ran to meet him and embraced him and kissed him and brought him to his house; and Jacob told Laban all these things.note
14
וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ לָבָן, אַךְ עַצְמִי וּבְשָׂרִי אָתָּה; וַיֵּשֶׁב עִמּוֹ, חֹדֶשׁ יָמִים.
And Laban said to him, "Surely you are my bone and my flesh." And he stayed with him a month.note
15
וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן, לְיַעֲקֹב, הֲכִי-אָחִי אַתָּה, וַעֲבַדְתַּנִי חִנָּם; הַגִּידָה לִּי, מַה-מַּשְׂכֻּרְתֶּךָ.
And Laban said to Jacob, "Just because you are my kinsman, should you serve me for nothing? Tell me, what shall your wages be?"note
16
וּלְלָבָן, שְׁתֵּי בָנוֹת: שֵׁם הַגְּדֹלָה לֵאָה, וְשֵׁם הַקְּטַנָּה רָחֵל.
Now Laban had two daughters: the name of the elder was Leah, and the name of the younger was Rachel.note
17
וְעֵינֵי לֵאָה, רַכּוֹת; וְרָחֵל, הָיְתָה, יְפַת-תֹּאַר, וִיפַת מַרְאֶה.
And Leah's eyes were weak, but Rachel was beautiful in form and lovely to look at.note
18
וַיֶּאֱהַב יַעֲקֹב, אֶת-רָחֵל; וַיֹּאמֶר, אֶעֱבָדְךָ שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים, בְּרָחֵל בִּתְּךָ, הַקְּטַנָּה.
And Jacob loved Rachel, and he said, "I will serve you seven years for Rachel your younger daughter."note
19
וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן, טוֹב תִּתִּי אֹתָהּ לָךְ, מִתִּתִּי אֹתָהּ, לְאִישׁ אַחֵר; שְׁבָה, עִמָּדִי.
And Laban said, "Better that I give her to you than give her to another man; stay with me."note
20
וַיַּעֲבֹד יַעֲקֹב בְּרָחֵל, שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים; וַיִּהְיוּ בְעֵינָיו כְּיָמִים אֲחָדִים, בְּאַהֲבָתוֹ אֹתָהּ.
So Jacob served seven years for Rachel, and they seemed to him but a few days, because of his love for her.note
21
וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֶל-לָבָן הָבָה אֶת-אִשְׁתִּי, כִּי מָלְאוּ יָמָי; וְאָבוֹאָה, אֵלֶיהָ.
And Jacob said to Laban, "Give me my wife, for my days are fulfilled, that I may go in to her."note
22
וַיֶּאֱסֹף לָבָן אֶת-כָּל-אַנְשֵׁי הַמָּקוֹם, וַיַּעַשׂ מִשְׁתֶּה.
And Laban gathered all the men of the place and made a feast.note
23
וַיְהִי בָעֶרֶב--וַיִּקַּח אֶת-לֵאָה בִתּוֹ, וַיָּבֵא אֹתָהּ אֵלָיו; וַיָּבֹא, אֵלֶיהָ.
And in the evening he took Leah his daughter and brought her to him, and he went in to her.note
24
וַיִּתֵּן לָבָן לָהּ, אֶת-זִלְפָּה שִׁפְחָתוֹ--לְלֵאָה בִתּוֹ, שִׁפְחָה.
And Laban gave Zilpah his slave-girl to his daughter Leah as her slave-girl.note
25
וַיְהִי בַבֹּקֶר, וְהִנֵּה-הִוא לֵאָה; וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל-לָבָן, מַה-זֹּאת עָשִׂיתָ לִּי--הֲלֹא בְרָחֵל עָבַדְתִּי עִמָּךְ, וְלָמָּה רִמִּיתָנִי.
And in the morning — behold, it was Leah! And he said to Laban, "What is this you have done to me? Did I not serve you for Rachel? Why then have you deceived me?"note
26
וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן, לֹא-יֵעָשֶׂה כֵן בִּמְקוֹמֵנוּ--לָתֵת הַצְּעִירָה, לִפְנֵי הַבְּכִירָה.
And Laban said, "It is not done so in our place, to give the younger before the firstborn.note
27
מַלֵּא, שְׁבֻעַ זֹאת; וְנִתְּנָה לְךָ גַּם-אֶת-זֹאת, בַּעֲבֹדָה אֲשֶׁר תַּעֲבֹד עִמָּדִי, עוֹד, שֶׁבַע-שָׁנִים אֲחֵרוֹת.
Finish this one's bridal week, and we will give you the other one too, for the service you will serve me another seven years."note
28
וַיַּעַשׂ יַעֲקֹב כֵּן, וַיְמַלֵּא שְׁבֻעַ זֹאת; וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ אֶת-רָחֵל בִּתּוֹ, לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה.
And Jacob did so and finished her week; and Laban gave him Rachel his daughter as a wife.note
29
וַיִּתֵּן לָבָן לְרָחֵל בִּתּוֹ, אֶת-בִּלְהָה שִׁפְחָתוֹ--לָהּ, לְשִׁפְחָה.
And Laban gave Bilhah his slave-girl to his daughter Rachel as her slave-girl.note
30
וַיָּבֹא גַּם אֶל-רָחֵל, וַיֶּאֱהַב גַּם-אֶת-רָחֵל מִלֵּאָה; וַיַּעֲבֹד עִמּוֹ, עוֹד שֶׁבַע-שָׁנִים אֲחֵרוֹת.
And he went in to Rachel also, and he loved Rachel more than Leah; and he served him yet another seven years.note
31
וַיַּרְא יְהוָה כִּי-שְׂנוּאָה לֵאָה, וַיִּפְתַּח אֶת-רַחְמָהּ; וְרָחֵל, עֲקָרָה.
And Jehovah saw that Leah was unloved, and he opened her womb; but Rachel was barren.note
32
וַתַּהַר לֵאָה וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן, וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ רְאוּבֵן: כִּי אָמְרָה, כִּי-רָאָה יְהוָה בְּעָנְיִי--כִּי עַתָּה, יֶאֱהָבַנִי אִישִׁי.
And Leah conceived and bore a son, and she named him Reuben, for she said, "Because Jehovah has looked on my affliction — for now my husband will love me."note
33
וַתַּהַר עוֹד, וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן, וַתֹּאמֶר כִּי-שָׁמַע יְהוָה כִּי-שְׂנוּאָה אָנֹכִי, וַיִּתֶּן-לִי גַּם-אֶת-זֶה; וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ, שִׁמְעוֹן.
And she conceived again and bore a son, and said, "Because Jehovah has heard that I am unloved, he has given me this one too"; and she named him Simeon.note
34
וַתַּהַר עוֹד, וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן, וַתֹּאמֶר עַתָּה הַפַּעַם יִלָּוֶה אִישִׁי אֵלַי, כִּי-יָלַדְתִּי לוֹ שְׁלֹשָׁה בָנִים; עַל-כֵּן קָרָא-שְׁמוֹ, לֵוִי.
And she conceived again and bore a son, and said, "Now this time my husband will be joined to me, for I have borne him three sons"; therefore he was named Levi.note
35
וַתַּהַר עוֹד וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן, וַתֹּאמֶר הַפַּעַם אוֹדֶה אֶת-יְהוָה--עַל-כֵּן קָרְאָה שְׁמוֹ, יְהוּדָה; וַתַּעֲמֹד, מִלֶּדֶת.
And she conceived again and bore a son, and said, "This time I will praise Jehovah"; therefore she named him Judah. And she stopped bearing.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

The deceiver deceived — a wedding-night switch, and the unloved wife God chooses. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–14 · עַצְמִי וּבְשָׂרִי atsmi u-vsari — "my bone and my flesh"

The third meeting at a well. Genesis loves this scene: a traveler comes to a well in a strange land and finds there his future wife. Abraham's servant met Rebekah drawing water for Isaac (ch. 24); Moses will meet Zipporah at a Midianite well (Exodus 2); and here Jacob meets Rachel. His entrance is pure emotion: he single-handedly rolls away the great stone the local shepherds needed a crowd to move, waters her flock, kisses her, and "lifted up his voice and wept" — the fugitive, alone and far from home, undone at finding his mother's family. Laban runs out to greet his nephew (last time it was the gold that made him run, 24:30) and welcomes him with the old kinship-cry, "you are my bone and my flesh" — the very words Adam first spoke over Eve, "bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh" (2:23). A warm welcome; but this is the same Laban, and the warmth has terms.

Verses 15–20 · וְעֵינֵי לֵאָה רַכּוֹת ve-einei Leah rakkot

Two sisters, and a love worth fourteen years. Laban asks what wages Jacob wants — the negotiation that will run, and cheat, for twenty years. The two daughters are set side by side: Leah, the elder, whose eyes were rakkot, and Rachel, the younger, "beautiful in form and lovely to look at." The word for Leah's eyes is genuinely uncertain: the KJV's "tender eyed" can be read as a flaw — weak, dull, watery eyes — set against Rachel's full beauty (the reading the contrast supports, and this translation's), or kindly, as "soft, lovely eyes," her one charm. The Hebrew won't say; the sister who is not chosen is left a little in shadow from her first line. And then one of scripture's tenderest sentences about love: Jacob serves seven years for Rachel, "and they seemed to him but a few days, because of his love for her."

Verses 21–30 · וְלָמָּה רִמִּיתָנִי ve-lammah rimmitani — "why have you deceived me?"

The deceiver deceived. This is the great irony of Jacob's life, and Genesis lets it land without a word of comment. Jacob, who once put on his elder brother's clothes to fool a father who could not see, is now fooled in the dark by an elder daughter brought veiled to his tent — and "in the morning, behold, it was Leah!" His outraged question — "why have you deceived me?" (rimmitani; KJV "beguiled") — is the very root of the word Isaac used of him: "your brother came with deceit" (27:35). And Laban's defense is a blade turned exactly on the wound: "it is not done in our place to give the younger before the firstborn" — a quiet lecture on the rights of the elder to the man who sold his brother's birthright and stole his blessing (27:36). The measure Jacob gave is measured back to him. He serves another seven years for Rachel, loves her more than Leah — and the rivalry that will fill the next chapter is already seeded.

Verses 31–35 · שְׂנוּאָה senuah — the unloved

God on the side of the unloved. The chapter's quietest line is its deepest: "Jehovah saw that Leah was unloved (senuah; KJV "hated," in the Hebrew idiom for the less-loved of two), and he opened her womb." The world's verdict and God's run opposite: Rachel is adored and barren; Leah is unloved and fruitful. And each son's name is a small heartbreak — a woman naming her babies after her longing to be loved. Reuben ("see, a son"): "Jehovah has looked on my affliction — now my husband will love me." Simeon ("hearing"): "Jehovah heard that I am unloved." Levi ("joined"): "now my husband will be joined to me." And then something turns. Judah ("praise"): "this time I will praise Jehovah" — no longer reaching for her husband's love, just thanking God. And it is these two least-expected sons of the least-loved wife who carry everything: from Levi comes the priesthood, and from Judah come David, the kings, and the Messiah — the line the word "Jew" itself is named from. The wife nobody chose is the mother God chose.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "weak" for the contested rakkot of Leah's eyes (with the ambiguity — soft/lovely or weak/dull — flagged); "deceived" for rimmitani (the KJV's "beguiled"), kept the same root as the mirmah Jacob himself trafficked in; and "unloved" for senuah (literally "hated," but the Hebrew idiom for the less-loved), so God's siding with her reads as the mercy it is.

Echoes paid. The well-betrothal type-scene, third time; "bone and flesh," Adam's cry (2:23); "why have you deceived me," turning 27:35 back on Jacob; "the younger before the firstborn," the rebuke to 27:36; and God opening the barren womb, as for Rebekah.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 30 — the war of the wives, the maidservants' sons, the mandrakes, and Jacob's speckled flocks that outwit Laban.

Leviticus 1 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיִּקְרָא, אֶל-מֹשֶׁה; וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֵלָיו, מֵאֹהֶל מוֹעֵד לֵאמֹר.
And Jehovah called to Moses, and spoke to him from the tent of meeting, saying:note
2
דַּבֵּר אֶל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, וְאָמַרְתָּ אֲלֵהֶם, אָדָם כִּי-יַקְרִיב מִכֶּם קָרְבָּן, לַיהוָה--מִן-הַבְּהֵמָה, מִן-הַבָּקָר וּמִן-הַצֹּאן, תַּקְרִיבוּ, אֶת-קָרְבַּנְכֶם.
Speak to the children of Israel, and say to them: When anyone of you brings an offering to Jehovah, you shall bring your offering from the livestock — from the herd or from the flock.note
3
אִם-עֹלָה קָרְבָּנוֹ מִן-הַבָּקָר, זָכָר תָּמִים יַקְרִיבֶנּוּ; אֶל-פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד, יַקְרִיב אֹתוֹ, לִרְצֹנוֹ, לִפְנֵי יְהוָה.
If his offering is a burnt-offering from the herd, he shall bring a male without blemish; he shall bring it to the door of the tent of meeting, for his acceptance before Jehovah.note
4
וְסָמַךְ יָדוֹ, עַל רֹאשׁ הָעֹלָה; וְנִרְצָה לוֹ, לְכַפֵּר עָלָיו.
And he shall lay his hand on the head of the burnt-offering, and it shall be accepted for him, to make atonement for him.note
5
וְשָׁחַט אֶת-בֶּן הַבָּקָר, לִפְנֵי יְהוָה; וְהִקְרִיבוּ בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֲנִים, אֶת-הַדָּם, וְזָרְקוּ אֶת-הַדָּם עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ סָבִיב, אֲשֶׁר-פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד.
And he shall slaughter the young bull before Jehovah; and Aaron's sons, the priests, shall present the blood and dash the blood against the altar all around, at the door of the tent of meeting.note
6
וְהִפְשִׁיט, אֶת-הָעֹלָה; וְנִתַּח אֹתָהּ, לִנְתָחֶיהָ.
And he shall flay the burnt-offering and cut it into its pieces.note
7
וְנָתְנוּ בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֵן, אֵשׁ--עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ; וְעָרְכוּ עֵצִים, עַל-הָאֵשׁ.
And the sons of Aaron the priest shall put fire on the altar and lay wood in order on the fire.note
8
וְעָרְכוּ, בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֲנִים, אֵת הַנְּתָחִים, אֶת-הָרֹאשׁ וְאֶת-הַפָּדֶר--עַל-הָעֵצִים אֲשֶׁר עַל-הָאֵשׁ, אֲשֶׁר עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ.
And Aaron's sons, the priests, shall lay the pieces, the head, and the fat in order on the wood that is on the fire that is on the altar.note
9
וְקִרְבּוֹ וּכְרָעָיו, יִרְחַץ בַּמָּיִם; וְהִקְטִיר הַכֹּהֵן אֶת-הַכֹּל הַמִּזְבֵּחָה, עֹלָה אִשֵּׁה רֵיחַ-נִיחוֹחַ לַיהוָה. {ס}
But its inner parts and its legs he shall wash with water; and the priest shall turn all of it into smoke on the altar — a burnt-offering, an offering by fire, a soothing aroma to Jehovah.note
10
וְאִם-מִן-הַצֹּאן קָרְבָּנוֹ מִן-הַכְּשָׂבִים אוֹ מִן-הָעִזִּים, לְעֹלָה--זָכָר תָּמִים, יַקְרִיבֶנּוּ.
And if his offering is from the flock — from the sheep or from the goats — for a burnt-offering, he shall bring a male without blemish.note
11
וְשָׁחַט אֹתוֹ עַל יֶרֶךְ הַמִּזְבֵּחַ, צָפֹנָה--לִפְנֵי יְהוָה; וְזָרְקוּ בְּנֵי אַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֲנִים אֶת-דָּמוֹ, עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ--סָבִיב.
And he shall slaughter it on the north side of the altar before Jehovah; and Aaron's sons, the priests, shall dash its blood against the altar all around.note
12
וְנִתַּח אֹתוֹ לִנְתָחָיו, וְאֶת-רֹאשׁוֹ וְאֶת-פִּדְרוֹ; וְעָרַךְ הַכֹּהֵן, אֹתָם, עַל-הָעֵצִים אֲשֶׁר עַל-הָאֵשׁ, אֲשֶׁר עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ.
And he shall cut it into its pieces, with its head and its fat; and the priest shall lay them in order on the wood that is on the fire that is on the altar.note
13
וְהַקֶּרֶב וְהַכְּרָעַיִם, יִרְחַץ בַּמָּיִם; וְהִקְרִיב הַכֹּהֵן אֶת-הַכֹּל, וְהִקְטִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחָה--עֹלָה הוּא אִשֵּׁה רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ, לַיהוָה. {פ}
But the inner parts and the legs he shall wash with water; and the priest shall present all of it and turn it into smoke on the altar — it is a burnt-offering, an offering by fire, a soothing aroma to Jehovah.note
14
וְאִם מִן-הָעוֹף עֹלָה קָרְבָּנוֹ, לַיהוָה: וְהִקְרִיב מִן-הַתֹּרִים, אוֹ מִן-בְּנֵי הַיּוֹנָה--אֶת-קָרְבָּנוֹ.
And if his offering to Jehovah is a burnt-offering of birds, then he shall bring his offering from the turtledoves or from the young pigeons.note
15
וְהִקְרִיבוֹ הַכֹּהֵן, אֶל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ, וּמָלַק אֶת-רֹאשׁוֹ, וְהִקְטִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחָה; וְנִמְצָה דָמוֹ, עַל קִיר הַמִּזְבֵּחַ.
And the priest shall bring it to the altar, and pinch off its head, and turn it into smoke on the altar; and its blood shall be drained out against the side of the altar.note
16
וְהֵסִיר אֶת-מֻרְאָתוֹ, בְּנֹצָתָהּ; וְהִשְׁלִיךְ אֹתָהּ אֵצֶל הַמִּזְבֵּחַ, קֵדְמָה--אֶל-מְקוֹם, הַדָּשֶׁן.
And he shall remove its crop with its feathers, and cast it beside the altar on the east side, to the place of the ashes.note
17
וְשִׁסַּע אֹתוֹ בִכְנָפָיו, לֹא יַבְדִּיל, וְהִקְטִיר אֹתוֹ הַכֹּהֵן הַמִּזְבֵּחָה, עַל-הָעֵצִים אֲשֶׁר עַל-הָאֵשׁ: עֹלָה הוּא, אִשֵּׁה רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ--לַיהוָה. {ס}
And he shall tear it open by its wings but shall not divide it completely; and the priest shall turn it into smoke on the altar, on the wood that is on the fire — it is a burnt-offering, an offering by fire, a soothing aroma to Jehovah.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Leviticus opens the manual of worship: how a redeemed people may draw near to the holy God now dwelling in their midst. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verse 1 · וַיִּקְרָא vayiqra — "and he called"

The book named for its first word. As with every book of the Hebrew Bible, the Jewish tradition calls Leviticus by its opening word: Vayiqra, "And he called." It is a gentler verb than the "spoke" that follows — God does not merely issue commands into the tent, he calls Moses in, by name, the way you call someone you know. It is the very verb of the burning bush, where "God called to him from within the bush, Moses, Moses!" (Exodus 3:4) — the same voice, now speaking from the tent that voice had told him to build. Where most of the shelf prints the small-caps substitute for the divine name, this translation keeps the Name itself: Jehovah called (ASV agrees, printing "Jehovah" here natively).

The small aleph. In the Torah scroll, the last letter of that first word — the א (aleph) of vayiqra — is written smaller than the letters around it, a scribal tradition our oldest manuscripts already carry (you can see it above). The rabbis read meaning into it: without the aleph the word would be vayiqar, "and he chanced upon" — the cooler verb used of God's meeting the pagan seer Balaam (Numbers 23:4). Moses, the tradition says, in his humility wanted to write only vayiqar, "God happened to meet me," and God insisted on the aleph of intimate calling — so Moses wrote it, but small. Whether or not that is the reason, the small letter is really there in the text, and it opens the book on exactly its theme: not a God stumbled upon, but a God who calls.

Verses 2–9 · קָרְבָּן · עֹלָה · רֵיחַ נִיחוֹחַ — the offering that draws near, ascends, and rests

Worship as drawing near. The word for "offering" here is qorban, and it is built on the root qarav, "to draw near, to approach." An offering is not first of all something destroyed; it is that which is brought near — the way a person, who cannot simply walk into the presence of holiness, sends a gift near in his place. The whole sacrificial system is framed at its first mention not as appeasement but as approach. (The word survives untranslated on Jesus' own lips: "Corban," Mark 7:11.)

The offering that goes up. The first sacrifice given a law is the olah, the burnt-offering — literally the "ascending" offering (from alah, "to go up"), because, uniquely, the whole animal goes up in smoke and none of it is eaten. Douay-Rheims, following the Latin, calls it a holocaust — the Greek holokauston, "wholly burnt." It is the offering of total self-gift, and the very first burnt-offering in the Bible was Noah's, on the altar he built when the flood receded (Genesis 8:20).

Without blemish, hand laid on. The animal must be tamim, "without blemish" — the same word God asked of Abraham's walk: "walk before me and be blameless" (17:1). The whole, unflawed victim mirrors the whole, unflawed life. Then the worshipper "lays his hand on the head" of it — samakh, to lean, to press down firmly, not merely touch: the gesture identifies him with the animal, this dies in my place. And so it "makes atonement for him" — kipper, from the root "to cover." Its plainest use in the Bible so far was the pitch that covered Noah's ark against the judging waters (kopher, 6:14): the ark that covered Noah and the offering that covers the sinner share one root.

A soothing aroma. The rite closes on a phrase that will become the refrain of the whole book: the priest turns it all to smoke, "a burnt-offering, an offering by fire, a soothing aroma to Jehovah" (reyach nichoach; KJV "a sweet savour"). The word nichoach shares its root with nuach, "to rest" — the root behind Noah's name — and its first appearance in scripture is exactly there: when Noah's burnt-offering rose, "Jehovah smelled the soothing aroma" and swore in his heart never again to curse the ground (8:21). What Noah did once, at the end of the world, Leviticus now makes Israel's daily worship.

Verses 10–13 · from the flock — the descending scale begins

The same rite, a smaller animal. The law now steps down from the herd (an ox or bull) to the flock — a sheep or a goat. The ritual is deliberately repeated almost word for word — Hebrew law, like Hebrew narrative, says things fully rather than cross-referencing — with one new detail: this one is slaughtered "on the north side of the altar." The repetition is the point: the pattern that governs the wealthy man's bull governs the herdsman's goat exactly the same. What is beginning here is a descending scale of cost, and it is a mercy — the door of the tent is not only for those who can bring the most.

Verses 14–17 · מִן-הָעוֹף min ha-of — the offering of the poor

And if you have almost nothing. The scale reaches its bottom rung: a person who owns no livestock at all may bring birds — "turtledoves or young pigeons" (KJV "turtledoves, or of young pigeons," the birds anyone could catch or buy for a copper). The priest does the whole rite by hand — he "pinches off" the tiny head (malaq, a bird needs no knife), drains the blood against the altar wall, removes the crop, and tears it open by the wings without dividing it in two. And the verdict on it is word-for-word the verdict on the bull: "a burnt-offering, an offering by fire, a soothing aroma to Jehovah." The poorest person's two birds smell exactly as sweet to God as the rich man's ox.

The offering Mary brought. This bottom rung is not a footnote of history. When Mary and Joseph came to the Temple to present the infant Jesus, they brought — Luke is careful to tell us — "a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons" (Luke 2:24), naming this very verse: the law's provision for those who could not afford a lamb (Leviticus 12:8). The family of the Messiah came with the offering of the poor. This translation will mark such echoes as they come; it does not force them.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "offering" for qorban (the "drawing near" root kept audible in the note, not flattened to "sacrifice"); "burnt-offering" for the ascending olah (with Douay's holocaust on the shelf); "without blemish" for the sacrificial sense of tamim — Abraham's own word; "soothing aroma" for reyach nichoach (the KJV's lovely "sweet savour" noted); and "slaughter" for shachat (שָׁחַט, the altar verb) — kept carefully distinct from its near-twin shachat (שָׁחַת, "to ruin"), the flood's boomerang word that only sounds the same.

Echoes paid. The soothing aroma of Noah's first altar (Genesis 8:21); the tamim demanded of the offering as it was of Abraham's walk (17:1); kipper, "to cover," reaching back to the pitch on Noah's ark (6:14); and vayiqra, "and he called," the very verb of the burning bush (Exodus 3:4). Echo planted: the offering of the poor (the two birds, v. 14) — paid forward to the family of Jesus at the Temple (Luke 2:24), and to the letter to the Hebrews, which reads the whole apparatus as a shadow of a single, final offering.

Next installment in this book: Leviticus 2 — the grain offering (minchah), the one bloodless sacrifice: fine flour, oil, and frankincense, with no leaven and no honey, but never without salt — "the salt of the covenant of your God."

Numbers 1 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה בְּמִדְבַּר סִינַי, בְּאֹהֶל מוֹעֵד: בְּאֶחָד לַחֹדֶשׁ הַשֵּׁנִי בַּשָּׁנָה הַשֵּׁנִית, לְצֵאתָם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם--לֵאמֹר.
And Jehovah spoke to Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the tent of meeting, on the first day of the second month, in the second year after they came out of the land of Egypt, saying:note
2
שְׂאוּ, אֶת-רֹאשׁ כָּל-עֲדַת בְּנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל, לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם, לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם--בְּמִסְפַּר שֵׁמוֹת, כָּל-זָכָר לְגֻלְגְּלֹתָם.
Lift the head — take the sum — of the whole congregation of the children of Israel, by their clans, by their fathers' houses, according to the number of names, every male, head by head:note
3
מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וָמַעְלָה, כָּל-יֹצֵא צָבָא בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל--תִּפְקְדוּ אֹתָם לְצִבְאֹתָם, אַתָּה וְאַהֲרֹן.
from twenty years old and upward, everyone in Israel who goes out to the army — you shall muster them by their divisions, you and Aaron.note
4
וְאִתְּכֶם יִהְיוּ, אִישׁ אִישׁ לַמַּטֶּה--אִישׁ רֹאשׁ לְבֵית-אֲבֹתָיו, הוּא.
And with you there shall be a man from each tribe, each one the head of his fathers' house.note
5
וְאֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹת הָאֲנָשִׁים, אֲשֶׁר יַעַמְדוּ אִתְּכֶם: לִרְאוּבֵן, אֱלִיצוּר בֶּן-שְׁדֵיאוּר.
And these are the names of the men who shall stand with you: For Reuben, Elizur son of Shedeur.note
6
לְשִׁמְעוֹן, שְׁלֻמִיאֵל בֶּן-צוּרִישַׁדָּי.
For Simeon, Shelumiel son of Zurishaddai.note
7
לִיהוּדָה, נַחְשׁוֹן בֶּן-עַמִּינָדָב.
For Judah, Nahshon son of Amminadab.note
8
לְיִשָּׂשכָר, נְתַנְאֵל בֶּן-צוּעָר.
For Issachar, Nethanel son of Zuar.note
9
לִזְבוּלֻן, אֱלִיאָב בֶּן-חֵלֹן.
For Zebulun, Eliab son of Helon.note
10
לִבְנֵי יוֹסֵף--לְאֶפְרַיִם, אֱלִישָׁמָע בֶּן-עַמִּיהוּד; לִמְנַשֶּׁה, גַּמְלִיאֵל בֶּן-פְּדָהצוּר.
For the sons of Joseph: for Ephraim, Elishama son of Ammihud; for Manasseh, Gamaliel son of Pedahzur.note
11
לְבִנְיָמִן, אֲבִידָן בֶּן-גִּדְעֹנִי.
For Benjamin, Abidan son of Gideoni.note
12
לְדָן, אֲחִיעֶזֶר בֶּן-עַמִּישַׁדָּי.
For Dan, Ahiezer son of Ammishaddai.note
13
לְאָשֵׁר, פַּגְעִיאֵל בֶּן-עָכְרָן.
For Asher, Pagiel son of Ochran.note
14
לְגָד, אֶלְיָסָף בֶּן-דְּעוּאֵל.
For Gad, Eliasaph son of Deuel.note
15
לְנַפְתָּלִי, אֲחִירַע בֶּן-עֵינָן.
For Naphtali, Ahira son of Enan.note
16
אֵלֶּה קְרוּאֵי הָעֵדָה, נְשִׂיאֵי מַטּוֹת אֲבוֹתָם: רָאשֵׁי אַלְפֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, הֵם.
These were the ones summoned of the congregation, the chieftains of their fathers' tribes; they were the heads of the clans of Israel.note
17
וַיִּקַּח מֹשֶׁה, וְאַהֲרֹן, אֵת הָאֲנָשִׁים הָאֵלֶּה, אֲשֶׁר נִקְּבוּ בְּשֵׁמֹת.
And Moses and Aaron took these men who were designated by name,note
18
וְאֵת כָּל-הָעֵדָה הִקְהִילוּ, בְּאֶחָד לַחֹדֶשׁ הַשֵּׁנִי, וַיִּתְיַלְדוּ עַל-מִשְׁפְּחֹתָם, לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם: בְּמִסְפַּר שֵׁמוֹת, מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וָמַעְלָה--לְגֻלְגְּלֹתָם.
and they assembled the whole congregation on the first day of the second month; and they registered their descent by their clans, by their fathers' houses, according to the number of names, from twenty years old and upward, head by head.note
19
כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה, אֶת-מֹשֶׁה; וַיִּפְקְדֵם, בְּמִדְבַּר סִינָי. {ס}
As Jehovah commanded Moses, so he mustered them in the wilderness of Sinai.note
20
וַיִּהְיוּ בְנֵי-רְאוּבֵן בְּכֹר יִשְׂרָאֵל, תּוֹלְדֹתָם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם: בְּמִסְפַּר שֵׁמוֹת, לְגֻלְגְּלֹתָם--כָּל-זָכָר מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וָמַעְלָה, כֹּל יֹצֵא צָבָא.
And the sons of Reuben, Israel's firstborn — their generations, by their clans, by their fathers' houses, according to the number of names, head by head, every male from twenty years old and upward, all who go out to the army:note
21
פְּקֻדֵיהֶם, לְמַטֵּה רְאוּבֵן--שִׁשָּׁה וְאַרְבָּעִים אֶלֶף, וַחֲמֵשׁ מֵאוֹת. {פ}
those mustered of them, of the tribe of Reuben, were 46,500.note
22
לִבְנֵי שִׁמְעוֹן, תּוֹלְדֹתָם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם: פְּקֻדָיו, בְּמִסְפַּר שֵׁמוֹת לְגֻלְגְּלֹתָם--כָּל-זָכָר מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וָמַעְלָה, כֹּל יֹצֵא צָבָא.
Of the sons of Simeon — their generations, by their clans, by their fathers' houses, those mustered of it, according to the number of names, head by head, every male from twenty years old and upward, all who go out to the army:note
23
פְּקֻדֵיהֶם, לְמַטֵּה שִׁמְעוֹן--תִּשְׁעָה וַחֲמִשִּׁים אֶלֶף, וּשְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת. {פ}
those mustered of them, of the tribe of Simeon, were 59,300.note
24
לִבְנֵי גָד, תּוֹלְדֹתָם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם: בְּמִסְפַּר שֵׁמוֹת, מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וָמַעְלָה--כֹּל, יֹצֵא צָבָא.
Of the sons of Gad — their generations, by their clans, by their fathers' houses, according to the number of names, from twenty years old and upward, all who go out to the army:note
25
פְּקֻדֵיהֶם, לְמַטֵּה גָד--חֲמִשָּׁה וְאַרְבָּעִים אֶלֶף, וְשֵׁשׁ מֵאוֹת וַחֲמִשִּׁים. {פ}
those mustered of them, of the tribe of Gad, were 45,650.note
26
לִבְנֵי יְהוּדָה, תּוֹלְדֹתָם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם: בְּמִסְפַּר שֵׁמֹת, מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וָמַעְלָה--כֹּל, יֹצֵא צָבָא.
Of the sons of Judah — their generations, by their clans, by their fathers' houses, according to the number of names, from twenty years old and upward, all who go out to the army:note
27
פְּקֻדֵיהֶם, לְמַטֵּה יְהוּדָה--אַרְבָּעָה וְשִׁבְעִים אֶלֶף, וְשֵׁשׁ מֵאוֹת. {פ}
those mustered of them, of the tribe of Judah, were 74,600.note
28
לִבְנֵי יִשָּׂשכָר, תּוֹלְדֹתָם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם: בְּמִסְפַּר שֵׁמֹת, מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וָמַעְלָה--כֹּל, יֹצֵא צָבָא.
Of the sons of Issachar — their generations, by their clans, by their fathers' houses, according to the number of names, from twenty years old and upward, all who go out to the army:note
29
פְּקֻדֵיהֶם, לְמַטֵּה יִשָּׂשכָר--אַרְבָּעָה וַחֲמִשִּׁים אֶלֶף, וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת. {פ}
those mustered of them, of the tribe of Issachar, were 54,400.note
30
לִבְנֵי זְבוּלֻן, תּוֹלְדֹתָם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם: בְּמִסְפַּר שֵׁמֹת, מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וָמַעְלָה--כֹּל, יֹצֵא צָבָא.
Of the sons of Zebulun — their generations, by their clans, by their fathers' houses, according to the number of names, from twenty years old and upward, all who go out to the army:note
31
פְּקֻדֵיהֶם, לְמַטֵּה זְבוּלֻן--שִׁבְעָה וַחֲמִשִּׁים אֶלֶף, וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת. {פ}
those mustered of them, of the tribe of Zebulun, were 57,400.note
32
לִבְנֵי יוֹסֵף לִבְנֵי אֶפְרַיִם, תּוֹלְדֹתָם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם: בְּמִסְפַּר שֵׁמֹת, מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וָמַעְלָה--כֹּל, יֹצֵא צָבָא.
Of the sons of Joseph, namely the sons of Ephraim — their generations, by their clans, by their fathers' houses, according to the number of names, from twenty years old and upward, all who go out to the army:note
33
פְּקֻדֵיהֶם, לְמַטֵּה אֶפְרָיִם--אַרְבָּעִים אֶלֶף, וַחֲמֵשׁ מֵאוֹת. {פ}
those mustered of them, of the tribe of Ephraim, were 40,500.note
34
לִבְנֵי מְנַשֶּׁה, תּוֹלְדֹתָם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם: בְּמִסְפַּר שֵׁמוֹת, מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וָמַעְלָה--כֹּל, יֹצֵא צָבָא.
Of the sons of Manasseh — their generations, by their clans, by their fathers' houses, according to the number of names, from twenty years old and upward, all who go out to the army:note
35
פְּקֻדֵיהֶם, לְמַטֵּה מְנַשֶּׁה--שְׁנַיִם וּשְׁלֹשִׁים אֶלֶף, וּמָאתָיִם. {פ}
those mustered of them, of the tribe of Manasseh, were 32,200.note
36
לִבְנֵי בִנְיָמִן, תּוֹלְדֹתָם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם: בְּמִסְפַּר שֵׁמֹת, מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וָמַעְלָה--כֹּל, יֹצֵא צָבָא.
Of the sons of Benjamin — their generations, by their clans, by their fathers' houses, according to the number of names, from twenty years old and upward, all who go out to the army:note
37
פְּקֻדֵיהֶם, לְמַטֵּה בִנְיָמִן--חֲמִשָּׁה וּשְׁלֹשִׁים אֶלֶף, וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת. {פ}
those mustered of them, of the tribe of Benjamin, were 35,400.note
38
לִבְנֵי דָן, תּוֹלְדֹתָם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם: בְּמִסְפַּר שֵׁמֹת, מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וָמַעְלָה--כֹּל, יֹצֵא צָבָא.
Of the sons of Dan — their generations, by their clans, by their fathers' houses, according to the number of names, from twenty years old and upward, all who go out to the army:note
39
פְּקֻדֵיהֶם, לְמַטֵּה דָן--שְׁנַיִם וְשִׁשִּׁים אֶלֶף, וּשְׁבַע מֵאוֹת. {פ}
those mustered of them, of the tribe of Dan, were 62,700.note
40
לִבְנֵי אָשֵׁר, תּוֹלְדֹתָם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם: בְּמִסְפַּר שֵׁמֹת, מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וָמַעְלָה--כֹּל, יֹצֵא צָבָא.
Of the sons of Asher — their generations, by their clans, by their fathers' houses, according to the number of names, from twenty years old and upward, all who go out to the army:note
41
פְּקֻדֵיהֶם, לְמַטֵּה אָשֵׁר--אֶחָד וְאַרְבָּעִים אֶלֶף, וַחֲמֵשׁ מֵאוֹת. {פ}
those mustered of them, of the tribe of Asher, were 41,500.note
42
בְּנֵי נַפְתָּלִי, תּוֹלְדֹתָם לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם: בְּמִסְפַּר שֵׁמֹת, מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וָמַעְלָה--כֹּל, יֹצֵא צָבָא.
The sons of Naphtali — their generations, by their clans, by their fathers' houses, according to the number of names, from twenty years old and upward, all who go out to the army:note
43
פְּקֻדֵיהֶם, לְמַטֵּה נַפְתָּלִי--שְׁלֹשָׁה וַחֲמִשִּׁים אֶלֶף, וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת. {פ}
those mustered of them, of the tribe of Naphtali, were 53,400.note
44
אֵלֶּה הַפְּקֻדִים אֲשֶׁר פָּקַד מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן, וּנְשִׂיאֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל--שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר, אִישׁ: אִישׁ-אֶחָד לְבֵית-אֲבֹתָיו, הָיוּ.
These are those mustered, whom Moses and Aaron mustered, with the chieftains of Israel — twelve men, each one for his fathers' house.note
45
וַיִּהְיוּ כָּל-פְּקוּדֵי בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל, לְבֵית אֲבֹתָם, מִבֶּן עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וָמַעְלָה, כָּל-יֹצֵא צָבָא בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל.
And all those mustered of the children of Israel, by their fathers' houses, from twenty years old and upward, all in Israel who go out to the army —note
46
וַיִּהְיוּ, כָּל-הַפְּקֻדִים--שֵׁשׁ-מֵאוֹת אֶלֶף, וּשְׁלֹשֶׁת אֲלָפִים; וַחֲמֵשׁ מֵאוֹת, וַחֲמִשִּׁים.
all those mustered were 603,550.note
47
וְהַלְוִיִּם, לְמַטֵּה אֲבֹתָם--לֹא הָתְפָּקְדוּ, בְּתוֹכָם. {פ}
But the Levites, by their fathers' tribe, were not mustered among them.note
48
וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה, אֶל-מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר.
And Jehovah spoke to Moses, saying:note
49
אַךְ אֶת-מַטֵּה לֵוִי לֹא תִפְקֹד, וְאֶת-רֹאשָׁם לֹא תִשָּׂא, בְּתוֹךְ, בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל.
Only the tribe of Levi you shall not muster, and their head you shall not take in the count among the children of Israel.note
50
וְאַתָּה הַפְקֵד אֶת-הַלְוִיִּם עַל-מִשְׁכַּן הָעֵדֻת וְעַל כָּל-כֵּלָיו, וְעַל כָּל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ--הֵמָּה יִשְׂאוּ אֶת-הַמִּשְׁכָּן וְאֶת-כָּל-כֵּלָיו, וְהֵם יְשָׁרְתֻהוּ; וְסָבִיב לַמִּשְׁכָּן, יַחֲנוּ.
But you, appoint the Levites over the tabernacle of the testimony and over all its furnishings and over all that belongs to it; they shall carry the tabernacle and all its furnishings, and they shall minister to it, and they shall camp around the tabernacle.note
51
וּבִנְסֹעַ הַמִּשְׁכָּן, יוֹרִידוּ אֹתוֹ הַלְוִיִּם, וּבַחֲנֹת הַמִּשְׁכָּן, יָקִימוּ אֹתוֹ הַלְוִיִּם; וְהַזָּר הַקָּרֵב, יוּמָת.
And when the tabernacle sets out, the Levites shall take it down; and when the tabernacle camps, the Levites shall set it up; and the outsider who draws near shall be put to death.note
52
וְחָנוּ, בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, אִישׁ עַל-מַחֲנֵהוּ וְאִישׁ עַל-דִּגְלוֹ, לְצִבְאֹתָם.
And the children of Israel shall camp, each by his own camp and each by his own standard, according to their divisions.note
53
וְהַלְוִיִּם יַחֲנוּ סָבִיב, לְמִשְׁכַּן הָעֵדֻת, וְלֹא-יִהְיֶה קֶצֶף, עַל-עֲדַת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל; וְשָׁמְרוּ, הַלְוִיִּם, אֶת-מִשְׁמֶרֶת, מִשְׁכַּן הָעֵדוּת.
But the Levites shall camp around the tabernacle of the testimony, that there be no wrath upon the congregation of the children of Israel; and the Levites shall keep the charge of the tabernacle of the testimony.note
54
וַיַּעֲשׂוּ, בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל: כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה, אֶת-מֹשֶׁה--כֵּן עָשׂוּ. {פ}
And the children of Israel did so; according to all that Jehovah commanded Moses, so they did.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Numbers opens with a muster: the redeemed people counted and arrayed as an army for the march to the promised land. A census chapter, so the notes zoom out — the repetition is the point. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–4 · בְּמִדְבַּר bemidbar — "in the wilderness"

The book named "In the wilderness." English calls this book Numbers (Greek Arithmoi, Latin Numeri) for the two great censuses it contains, in chapters 1 and 26. But the Hebrew names it, as always, from its opening words: Bemidbar, "in the wilderness" — and that is the truer title, for this is the book of the forty wilderness years, the generation that left Egypt and, but for two men, died in the desert without seeing the land. It opens with careful bookkeeping and ends in a graveyard.

One month after Leviticus. The date is exact: "the first day of the second month, in the second year." The tabernacle was raised on the first day of the first month of that second year (Exodus 40:17), and all of Leviticus was spoken there; now, one month later, from the same tent of meeting, the voice turns from worship to war. In about three weeks the cloud will lift and Israel will march (Numbers 10:11).

"Lift the head." The idiom for taking a census is se'u et rosh, literally "lift the head" — to count is to raise each face and reckon it. But this is no ordinary head-count: it is a muster. Everyone counted is a male "from twenty years old and upward, all who go out to the tsava" — the host, the army. To paqad, "muster/number," is the same verb by which "Jehovah visited Sarah" with a son (21:1): God's turning of his attention, here turned toward an army. This census is Israel becoming, for the first time, a nation under arms.

Verses 5–16 · נְשִׂיאֵי מַטּוֹת nesi'ei mattot — the chieftains of the tribes

Twelve names, twelve tribes. One nasi — a "chieftain" (KJV "prince," NWT "chieftain"), a lifted-up one — is named from each tribe to stand with Moses. The list quietly solves an arithmetic problem: Levi will be set apart (below), which would leave only eleven fighting tribes — so Joseph is split into his two sons, Ephraim and Manasseh (v. 10), and the twelve hold. Their names are a little sermon in themselves, most of them built on the names of God: Eli-zur "my God is a rock," Zuri-shaddai "my rock is Shaddai," Gamli-el "God is my reward" (the name the Pharisee teacher of Acts 5 would carry), Nethan-el "God has given."

Nahshon of Judah. One name repays watching: Nahshon son of Amminadab, chieftain of Judah (v. 7) — brother-in-law of Aaron, and, the genealogies will show, the great-great-great-grandfather of King David (Ruth 4:20–22) and an ancestor of the Messiah (Matthew 1:4). It is fitting that Judah, Leah's "this time I will praise Jehovah" son, heads the muster and will camp first, on the east; the praise-tribe leads the host. (Jewish tradition remembers Nahshon as the first to step into the Red Sea before it parted.)

The called ones. The chieftains are qeru'ei ha-eda, "the summoned of the congregation" (v. 16; here the scroll's written form qeri'ei is read as qeru'ei, a ketiv/qere pair, both from qara, "to call"). They are "the heads of the alfei Israel" — the clans, or, on the older reading, the "thousands" (see the numbers note); the two senses of eleph sit side by side in one word.

Verses 17–46 · the muster, and the great number — 603,550

The drumbeat is the point. Twelve times the same clause turns over, tribe by tribe: "their generations (toldot), by their clans, by their fathers' houses, according to the number of names, head by head, every male from twenty years old and upward, all who go out to the army." A modern ear wants it abbreviated; the Hebrew refuses. The repetition is the theology — no tribe is summarized, none ranked above another, each counted with the identical dignity. And it is counted "head by head," le-gulgelotam (KJV "by their polls"), from gulgolet, "skull" — the word that, in Aramaic dress, gives us Golgotha, "the place of the skull." Every skull is numbered and known.

Six hundred thousand — and the promise made countable. The total of fighting men comes to 603,550 (v. 46; the KJV counts it out longhand, "six hundred thousand and three thousand and five hundred and fifty," and writes Judah's 74,600 as "threescore and fourteen thousand and six hundred") — implying a whole people of perhaps two million. To Abram, on a night long before, God had said, "Look toward the heavens and count the stars — if you are able to count them… so shall your seed be" (15:5): a seed as uncountable as the stars. Now, at the foot of Sinai, the uncountable has become an army you can number to the fifty. How large, exactly? The figures are famously debated. Taken plainly they give a nation of two million crossing a desert — a wonder sustained by daily manna. Some scholars, noting that the word eleph means both "thousand" and "clan/troop/military-unit," read the totals as (say) so-many units of so-many men, yielding a smaller host; others hold the plain numbers. This translation prints the numbers as the text gives them and flags the honest uncertainty in the word itself — the same eleph that named the chieftains "heads of the clans/thousands" a few verses back.

Verses 47–54 · וְהַלְוִיִּם — the tribe that is not counted

One tribe left off the roll. When the muster is done, a thirteenth tribe stands uncounted: "But the Levites, by their fathers' tribe, were not mustered among them" (v. 47). It is not a slight but a setting-apart. The Levites are appointed — the same verb paqad, now not "muster for war" but "put in charge" (v. 50) — over the tabernacle: they alone carry it, strike it when the camp moves, raise it when the camp halts, minister at it, and camp around it as a living wall. The one tribe exempted from the army is the tribe whose whole warfare is the presence of God.

A wall against wrath. The reason is given starkly: the Levites ring the tent "that there be no qetsef, no wrath, upon the congregation" (v. 53), and "the outsider who draws near shall be put to death" (v. 51; KJV "the stranger that cometh nigh"). Holiness is not safe to approach carelessly; the tabernacle that is Israel's greatest gift is also its greatest danger, and the Levites are the trained guard who keep the people at a saving distance. Note the geography the chapter is quietly drawing: the twelve tribes in their armies on the outside, the Levites in a ring within, and at the dead center the tent where the glory dwells — the whole camp is built around the presence. And the book that will record so much rebellion opens on a perfect note of it: "And the children of Israel did so; according to all that Jehovah commanded Moses, so they did."

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "muster" for paqad when it counts an army (kept the same verb as God's "visiting" of Sarah, and as the Levites' "appointing" — one root, three turns); "army / host" for tsava; "tribe" for matteh (literally a "staff"); "congregation" for eda, the appointed assembly; "head by head" for le-gulgelotam (the skull-word behind Golgotha); and the numbers printed as the text gives them, with the eleph "thousand/clan" ambiguity flagged rather than silently resolved.

Echoes paid. The tent of meeting still speaking, one month on (Leviticus 1:1); paqad, the verb that "visited" Sarah (21:1), now mustering her descendants; the star-promise to Abram (15:5) made countable in an army of 600,000; Judah the praise-son (29:35) heading the host; and Reuben the firstborn (29:32) counted first though the leadership has passed on. Echo planted: the gulgolet, "skull," that will surface again on a hill called the Place of the Skull.

Next installment in this book: Numbers 2 — the camp arranged around the tent, three tribes to a side under four great standards (Judah east, Reuben south, Ephraim west, Dan north), the order in which they will march.

Deuteronomy 1 — The Mister Translation

1
אֵלֶּה הַדְּבָרִים, אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר מֹשֶׁה אֶל-כָּל-יִשְׂרָאֵל, בְּעֵבֶר, הַיַּרְדֵּן: בַּמִּדְבָּר בָּעֲרָבָה מוֹל סוּף בֵּין-פָּארָן וּבֵין-תֹּפֶל, וְלָבָן וַחֲצֵרֹת--וְדִי זָהָב.
These are the words that Moses spoke to all Israel across the Jordan — in the wilderness, in the Arabah, opposite Suph, between Paran and Tophel and Laban and Hazeroth and Di-zahab.note
2
אַחַד עָשָׂר יוֹם מֵחֹרֵב, דֶּרֶךְ הַר-שֵׂעִיר, עַד, קָדֵשׁ בַּרְנֵעַ.
Eleven days from Horeb by the way of Mount Seir to Kadesh-barnea.note
3
וַיְהִי בְּאַרְבָּעִים שָׁנָה, בְּעַשְׁתֵּי-עָשָׂר חֹדֶשׁ בְּאֶחָד לַחֹדֶשׁ; דִּבֶּר מֹשֶׁה, אֶל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֹתוֹ, אֲלֵהֶם.
And it happened in the fortieth year, in the eleventh month, on the first of the month, that Moses spoke to the sons of Israel according to all that Jehovah had commanded him for them,note
4
אַחֲרֵי הַכֹּתוֹ, אֵת סִיחֹן מֶלֶךְ הָאֱמֹרִי, אֲשֶׁר יוֹשֵׁב, בְּחֶשְׁבּוֹן--וְאֵת, עוֹג מֶלֶךְ הַבָּשָׁן, אֲשֶׁר-יוֹשֵׁב בְּעַשְׁתָּרֹת, בְּאֶדְרֶעִי.
after he had struck down Sihon king of the Amorites, who dwelt in Heshbon, and Og king of Bashan, who dwelt in Ashtaroth, at Edrei.note
5
בְּעֵבֶר הַיַּרְדֵּן, בְּאֶרֶץ מוֹאָב, הוֹאִיל מֹשֶׁה, בֵּאֵר אֶת-הַתּוֹרָה הַזֹּאת לֵאמֹר.
Across the Jordan, in the land of Moab, Moses undertook to make this instruction plain, saying:note
6
יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ דִּבֶּר אֵלֵינוּ, בְּחֹרֵב לֵאמֹר: רַב-לָכֶם שֶׁבֶת, בָּהָר הַזֶּה.
"Jehovah our God spoke to us at Horeb, saying: You have stayed long enough at this mountain.note
7
פְּנוּ וּסְעוּ לָכֶם, וּבֹאוּ הַר הָאֱמֹרִי וְאֶל-כָּל-שְׁכֵנָיו, בָּעֲרָבָה בָהָר וּבַשְּׁפֵלָה וּבַנֶּגֶב, וּבְחוֹף הַיָּם--אֶרֶץ הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְהַלְּבָנוֹן, עַד-הַנָּהָר הַגָּדֹל נְהַר-פְּרָת.
Turn and set out, and go to the hill country of the Amorites and to all its neighbours — in the Arabah, in the hill country and in the lowland and in the Negev and by the seashore — the land of the Canaanite, and the Lebanon, as far as the great river, the river Euphrates.note
8
רְאֵה נָתַתִּי לִפְנֵיכֶם, אֶת-הָאָרֶץ; בֹּאוּ, וּרְשׁוּ אֶת-הָאָרֶץ, אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּע יְהוָה לַאֲבֹתֵיכֶם לְאַבְרָהָם לְיִצְחָק וּלְיַעֲקֹב לָתֵת לָהֶם, וּלְזַרְעָם אַחֲרֵיהֶם.
See — I have set the land before you. Go in and take possession of the land that Jehovah swore to your fathers, to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, to give to them and to their offspring after them.note
9
וָאֹמַר אֲלֵכֶם, בָּעֵת הַהִוא לֵאמֹר: לֹא-אוּכַל לְבַדִּי, שְׂאֵת אֶתְכֶם.
And I said to you at that time: I am not able to carry you by myself.note
10
יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם, הִרְבָּה אֶתְכֶם; וְהִנְּכֶם הַיּוֹם, כְּכוֹכְבֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם לָרֹב.
Jehovah your God has multiplied you, and look — today you are as many as the stars of the heavens.note
11
יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲבוֹתֵכֶם, יֹסֵף עֲלֵיכֶם כָּכֶם--אֶלֶף פְּעָמִים; וִיבָרֵךְ אֶתְכֶם, כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר לָכֶם.
May Jehovah, the God of your fathers, add to you a thousand times as many as you are, and bless you as he has spoken to you!note
12
אֵיכָה אֶשָּׂא, לְבַדִּי, טָרְחֲכֶם וּמַשַּׂאֲכֶם, וְרִיבְכֶם.
How can I carry by myself your bother and your burden and your quarrelling?note
13
הָבוּ לָכֶם אֲנָשִׁים חֲכָמִים וּנְבֹנִים, וִידֻעִים--לְשִׁבְטֵיכֶם; וַאֲשִׂימֵם, בְּרָאשֵׁיכֶם.
Provide for yourselves men who are wise and discerning and known, by your tribes, and I will set them as your heads.note
14
וַתַּעֲנוּ, אֹתִי; וַתֹּאמְרוּ, טוֹב-הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר-דִּבַּרְתָּ לַעֲשׂוֹת.
And you answered me and said, "The thing you have spoken is good to do."note
15
וָאֶקַּח אֶת-רָאשֵׁי שִׁבְטֵיכֶם, אֲנָשִׁים חֲכָמִים וִידֻעִים, וָאֶתֵּן אוֹתָם רָאשִׁים, עֲלֵיכֶם: שָׂרֵי אֲלָפִים וְשָׂרֵי מֵאוֹת, וְשָׂרֵי חֲמִשִּׁים וְשָׂרֵי עֲשָׂרֹת, וְשֹׁטְרִים, לְשִׁבְטֵיכֶם.
So I took the heads of your tribes, men wise and known, and gave them as heads over you — commanders of thousands and commanders of hundreds and commanders of fifties and commanders of tens, and officers for your tribes.note
16
וָאֲצַוֶּה, אֶת-שֹׁפְטֵיכֶם, בָּעֵת הַהִוא, לֵאמֹר: שָׁמֹעַ בֵּין-אֲחֵיכֶם וּשְׁפַטְתֶּם צֶדֶק, בֵּין-אִישׁ וּבֵין-אָחִיו וּבֵין גֵּרוֹ.
And I commanded your judges at that time, saying: Hear the cases between your brothers, and judge righteously between a man and his brother and the stranger who is with him.note
17
לֹא-תַכִּירוּ פָנִים בַּמִּשְׁפָּט, כַּקָּטֹן כַּגָּדֹל תִּשְׁמָעוּן--לֹא תָגוּרוּ מִפְּנֵי-אִישׁ, כִּי הַמִּשְׁפָּט לֵאלֹהִים הוּא; וְהַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר יִקְשֶׁה מִכֶּם, תַּקְרִבוּן אֵלַי וּשְׁמַעְתִּיו.
You shall not recognise faces in judgement; you shall hear the small as the great; you shall not shrink before any man, for the judgement is God's. And the case that is too hard for you, bring to me, and I will hear it.note
18
וָאֲצַוֶּה אֶתְכֶם, בָּעֵת הַהִוא, אֵת כָּל-הַדְּבָרִים, אֲשֶׁר תַּעֲשׂוּן.
And I commanded you at that time all the things that you should do.note
19
וַנִּסַּע מֵחֹרֵב, וַנֵּלֶךְ אֵת כָּל-הַמִּדְבָּר הַגָּדוֹל וְהַנּוֹרָא הַהוּא אֲשֶׁר רְאִיתֶם דֶּרֶךְ הַר הָאֱמֹרִי, כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ, אֹתָנוּ; וַנָּבֹא, עַד קָדֵשׁ בַּרְנֵעַ.
And we set out from Horeb and went through all that great and fearful wilderness that you saw, by the way of the hill country of the Amorites, as Jehovah our God had commanded us; and we came to Kadesh-barnea.note
20
וָאֹמַר, אֲלֵכֶם: בָּאתֶם עַד-הַר הָאֱמֹרִי, אֲשֶׁר-יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ נֹתֵן לָנוּ.
And I said to you: You have come to the hill country of the Amorites, which Jehovah our God is giving to us.note
21
רְאֵה נָתַן יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ, לְפָנֶיךָ--אֶת-הָאָרֶץ: עֲלֵה רֵשׁ, כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲבֹתֶיךָ לָךְ--אַל-תִּירָא, וְאַל-תֵּחָת.
See — Jehovah your God has set the land before you. Go up, take possession, as Jehovah the God of your fathers has spoken to you. Do not be afraid and do not be dismayed.note
22
וַתִּקְרְבוּן אֵלַי, כֻּלְּכֶם, וַתֹּאמְרוּ נִשְׁלְחָה אֲנָשִׁים לְפָנֵינוּ, וְיַחְפְּרוּ-לָנוּ אֶת-הָאָרֶץ; וְיָשִׁבוּ אֹתָנוּ, דָּבָר--אֶת-הַדֶּרֶךְ אֲשֶׁר נַעֲלֶה-בָּהּ, וְאֵת הֶעָרִים אֲשֶׁר נָבֹא אֲלֵיהֶן.
And all of you came near to me and said, "Let us send men ahead of us, and let them dig out the land for us and bring us back word about the way by which we should go up and the cities we shall come to."note
23
