וַיִּיטַב בְּעֵינַי, הַדָּבָר; וָאֶקַּח מִכֶּם שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר אֲנָשִׁים, אִישׁ אֶחָד לַשָּׁבֶט.
And the thing was good in my eyes, and I took twelve men from you, one man for each tribe.note
24
וַיִּפְנוּ וַיַּעֲלוּ הָהָרָה, וַיָּבֹאוּ עַד-נַחַל אֶשְׁכֹּל; וַיְרַגְּלוּ, אֹתָהּ.
And they turned and went up into the hill country and came to the Valley of Eshcol, and spied it out.note
25
וַיִּקְחוּ בְיָדָם מִפְּרִי הָאָרֶץ, וַיּוֹרִדוּ אֵלֵינוּ; וַיָּשִׁבוּ אֹתָנוּ דָבָר, וַיֹּאמְרוּ, טוֹבָה הָאָרֶץ, אֲשֶׁר-יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ נֹתֵן לָנוּ.
And they took some of the fruit of the land in their hand and brought it down to us, and brought us back word and said, "The land that Jehovah our God is giving us is good."note
26
וְלֹא אֲבִיתֶם, לַעֲלֹת; וַתַּמְרוּ, אֶת-פִּי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם.
But you were not willing to go up, and you rebelled against the mouth of Jehovah your God.note
27
וַתֵּרָגְנוּ בְאָהֳלֵיכֶם, וַתֹּאמְרוּ, בְּשִׂנְאַת יְהוָה אֹתָנוּ, הוֹצִיאָנוּ מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם--לָתֵת אֹתָנוּ בְּיַד הָאֱמֹרִי, לְהַשְׁמִידֵנוּ.
And you grumbled in your tents and said, "It is because Jehovah hated us that he brought us out of the land of Egypt — to give us into the hand of the Amorite, to destroy us.note
28
אָנָה אֲנַחְנוּ עֹלִים, אַחֵינוּ הֵמַסּוּ אֶת-לְבָבֵנוּ לֵאמֹר עַם גָּדוֹל וָרָם מִמֶּנּוּ, עָרִים גְּדֹלֹת וּבְצוּרֹת, בַּשָּׁמָיִם; וְגַם-בְּנֵי עֲנָקִים, רָאִינוּ שָׁם.
Where are we going up? Our brothers have melted our heart, saying: A people greater and taller than we, cities great and fortified to the heavens — and we saw the sons of the Anakim there too."note
29
וָאֹמַר, אֲלֵכֶם: לֹא-תַעַרְצוּן וְלֹא-תִירְאוּן, מֵהֶם.
And I said to you: Do not be terrified and do not be afraid of them.note
30
יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם הַהֹלֵךְ לִפְנֵיכֶם, הוּא יִלָּחֵם לָכֶם: כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה אִתְּכֶם, בְּמִצְרַיִם--לְעֵינֵיכֶם.
Jehovah your God, who goes before you — he himself will fight for you, according to all that he did with you in Egypt before your eyes,note
31
וּבַמִּדְבָּר, אֲשֶׁר רָאִיתָ, אֲשֶׁר נְשָׂאֲךָ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ, כַּאֲשֶׁר יִשָּׂא-אִישׁ אֶת-בְּנוֹ--בְּכָל-הַדֶּרֶךְ אֲשֶׁר הֲלַכְתֶּם, עַד-בֹּאֲכֶם עַד-הַמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה.
and in the wilderness, where you saw how Jehovah your God carried you as a man carries his son, all the way that you went until you came to this place.note
32
וּבַדָּבָר, הַזֶּה--אֵינְכֶם, מַאֲמִינִם, בַּיהוָה, אֱלֹהֵיכֶם.
And in this thing you were not trusting Jehovah your God,note
33
הַהֹלֵךְ לִפְנֵיכֶם בַּדֶּרֶךְ, לָתוּר לָכֶם מָקוֹם--לַחֲנֹתְכֶם: בָּאֵשׁ לַיְלָה, לַרְאֹתְכֶם בַּדֶּרֶךְ אֲשֶׁר תֵּלְכוּ-בָהּ, וּבֶעָנָן, יוֹמָם.
who goes before you on the way to seek out a place for you to camp — in fire by night, to show you the way you should go, and in the cloud by day.note
34
וַיִּשְׁמַע יְהוָה, אֶת-קוֹל דִּבְרֵיכֶם; וַיִּקְצֹף, וַיִּשָּׁבַע לֵאמֹר.
And Jehovah heard the sound of your words, and he was angry and swore, saying:note
35
אִם-יִרְאֶה אִישׁ בָּאֲנָשִׁים הָאֵלֶּה, הַדּוֹר הָרָע הַזֶּה--אֵת, הָאָרֶץ הַטּוֹבָה, אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי, לָתֵת לַאֲבֹתֵיכֶם.
Not one of these men, this evil generation, shall see the good land that I swore to give to your fathers —note
36
זוּלָתִי כָּלֵב בֶּן-יְפֻנֶּה, הוּא יִרְאֶנָּה, וְלוֹ-אֶתֵּן אֶת-הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר דָּרַךְ-בָּהּ, וּלְבָנָיו--יַעַן, אֲשֶׁר מִלֵּא אַחֲרֵי יְהוָה.
except Caleb son of Jephunneh. He shall see it, and to him I will give the land he walked on, and to his sons, because he has fully followed Jehovah.note
37
גַּם-בִּי הִתְאַנַּף יְהוָה, בִּגְלַלְכֶם לֵאמֹר: גַּם-אַתָּה, לֹא-תָבֹא שָׁם.
With me also Jehovah was angry on your account, saying: You too shall not go in there.note
38
יְהוֹשֻׁעַ בִּן-נוּן הָעֹמֵד לְפָנֶיךָ, הוּא יָבֹא שָׁמָּה; אֹתוֹ חַזֵּק, כִּי-הוּא יַנְחִלֶנָּה אֶת-יִשְׂרָאֵל.
Joshua son of Nun, who stands before you — he shall go in there. Strengthen him, for he shall cause Israel to inherit it.note
39
וְטַפְּכֶם אֲשֶׁר אֲמַרְתֶּם לָבַז יִהְיֶה, וּבְנֵיכֶם אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יָדְעוּ הַיּוֹם טוֹב וָרָע--הֵמָּה, יָבֹאוּ שָׁמָּה; וְלָהֶם אֶתְּנֶנָּה, וְהֵם יִירָשׁוּהָ.
And your little ones, whom you said would become plunder, and your children, who today do not know good from evil — they shall go in there; and to them I will give it, and they shall take possession of it.note
40
וְאַתֶּם, פְּנוּ לָכֶם; וּסְעוּ הַמִּדְבָּרָה, דֶּרֶךְ יַם-סוּף.
But you — turn yourselves and set out for the wilderness by the way of the Sea of Reeds.note
41
וַתַּעֲנוּ וַתֹּאמְרוּ אֵלַי, חָטָאנוּ לַיהוָה--אֲנַחְנוּ נַעֲלֶה וְנִלְחַמְנוּ, כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר-צִוָּנוּ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵינוּ; וַתַּחְגְּרוּ, אִישׁ אֶת-כְּלֵי מִלְחַמְתּוֹ, וַתָּהִינוּ, לַעֲלֹת הָהָרָה.
And you answered and said to me, "We have sinned against Jehovah. We ourselves will go up and fight, according to all that Jehovah our God commanded us." And each of you strapped on his weapons of war, and you thought it a light thing to go up into the hill country.note
42
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֵלַי, אֱמֹר לָהֶם לֹא תַעֲלוּ וְלֹא-תִלָּחֲמוּ--כִּי אֵינֶנִּי, בְּקִרְבְּכֶם; וְלֹא, תִּנָּגְפוּ, לִפְנֵי, אֹיְבֵיכֶם.
And Jehovah said to me: Say to them, Do not go up and do not fight, for I am not among you — lest you be struck down before your enemies.note
43
וָאֲדַבֵּר אֲלֵיכֶם, וְלֹא שְׁמַעְתֶּם; וַתַּמְרוּ אֶת-פִּי יְהוָה, וַתָּזִדוּ וַתַּעֲלוּ הָהָרָה.
And I spoke to you, and you did not listen; and you rebelled against the mouth of Jehovah, and you acted presumptuously and went up into the hill country.note
44
וַיֵּצֵא הָאֱמֹרִי הַיֹּשֵׁב בָּהָר הַהוּא, לִקְרַאתְכֶם, וַיִּרְדְּפוּ אֶתְכֶם, כַּאֲשֶׁר תַּעֲשֶׂינָה הַדְּבֹרִים; וַיַּכְּתוּ אֶתְכֶם בְּשֵׂעִיר, עַד-חָרְמָה.
And the Amorite who dwelt in that hill country came out to meet you, and they chased you as bees do, and beat you down in Seir as far as Hormah.note
45
וַתָּשֻׁבוּ וַתִּבְכּוּ, לִפְנֵי יְהוָה; וְלֹא-שָׁמַע יְהוָה בְּקֹלְכֶם, וְלֹא הֶאֱזִין אֲלֵיכֶם.
And you returned and wept before Jehovah, but Jehovah did not listen to your voice and did not give ear to you.note
46
וַתֵּשְׁבוּ בְקָדֵשׁ, יָמִים רַבִּים, כַּיָּמִים, אֲשֶׁר יְשַׁבְתֶּם.
And you stayed in Kadesh many days — as many days as you stayed."note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–5 · eleven days, and forty years

"These are the words." Elleh ha-devarim — and that is the book's Hebrew name: Devarim, "Words." Every book of the Torah is named in Hebrew from its own opening line, and this one could not be better named: Deuteronomy is a book of speech. Moses does almost nothing in it but talk. ⚠️ The English title comes from a different route entirely — the Greek Deuteronomion, "second law," from the Septuagint's rendering of a phrase at 17:18 that in Hebrew means "a copy of this instruction." The Greek title has stuck for two thousand years, and it is not quite what the Hebrew says: this is not a second law but the first one preached.

⚠️ Verse 2 is one of the most devastating sentences in the Bible, and it looks like a travel note. "Eleven days from Horeb by the way of Mount Seir to Kadesh-barnea." Then verse 3: "in the fortieth year." The narrator sets the length of the journey beside the length of the trip and says nothing else. Eleven days of walking took a generation forty years, and the whole of chapter 1 is the explanation of the gap. Nothing in the verse editorialises; the arithmetic does it.

"Across the Jordan." Be-ever ha-Yarden — literally "in the crossing-place / the other side of the Jordan." ⚠️ It is worth being honest that the phrase describes the eastern side from the standpoint of someone standing in Canaan, on the west — which is where Israel is not yet. It may simply be the settled name for the region (as "the Transjordan" is for us, whichever side we stand on), or it may be the voice of a narrator writing later, from inside the land. This translation renders it plainly and leaves the observation in the note; it is one of the details that features in the authorship discussion on the book's introduction page.

"Undertook to make this instruction plain." Ho'il… be'er et ha-torah. Be'er is a rare and concrete verb — to dig out, to engrave, to make a thing unmistakably legible (Habakkuk 2:2 uses it of writing large on tablets so a runner can read it). And torah is not "law" in the narrow legislative sense but instruction, teaching, direction. So the verse is not "Moses restated the legislation" but something closer to: he set about making the teaching plain. KJV "began Moses to declare this law"; ASV the same; the ESV "undertook to explain this law." That is the project of the whole book in one verb.

The place-names of verse 1 are a puzzle. Suph, Tophel, Laban, Di-zahab — several appear nowhere else, and no one can now locate them with confidence. ⚠️ An old Jewish reading takes them not as a map but as an indictment, hearing in the names the wilderness failures (Di-zahab, "enough gold," heard as the golden calf). That is homiletic rather than geographic, and this translation prints them as the place-names they present themselves to be.

Verses 6–8 · "you have stayed long enough at this mountain"

The first words God speaks in the book are an eviction notice. Rav lakhem shevet ba-har ha-zeh — you have much of sitting at this mountain; enough. And the mountain in question is Horeb — Sinai, the place of the fire and the covenant and the tablets, the most important address in Israel's short history. They had been camped there roughly a year. The command is to leave it. ⚠️ This is a strange and easily-missed theological move: the point of the mountain was never the mountain. Israel is not a people who live at a holy site; they are a people who are going somewhere, and the sign that the encounter was real is that they pack up.

The land is described as already given. "See — I have set the land before you. Go in and take possession." The perfect tense is not a promise about the future but a statement about a completed act, with the taking left to them. And the boundaries in verse 7 are the largest in the Torah — hill country, lowland, Negev, coast, Lebanon, all the way to the Euphrates. ⚠️ Israel never in its history held that whole extent; the closest approach is the reign of Solomon. Readers have taken this as an ideal horizon, as a conditional grant never met, or as a description of a sphere of influence rather than settlement. The text states the extent and does not qualify it.

"Which Jehovah swore to your fathers, to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob." The oath is the same one Joseph quoted from his deathbed in the last verses of Genesis (50:24), naming the same three men in the same order. Deuteronomy 1 is standing at the border that sentence was pointing at.

Verses 9–18 · a man admits he cannot carry it

"I am not able to carry you by myself." The verb is nasa, to lift or bear — the same word used of carrying a load, and (in verse 31) of a father carrying a child. Moses uses it three times in four verses, and the admission is striking for a man in his position: the leader states plainly that the job is beyond one person. ⚠️ Note what he does not do. He does not treat the size of the people as a burden to complain about — verses 10-11 stop the argument to bless them, praying that God would multiply them a thousand times more. The problem is not that there are too many; it is that one man is too few.

⚠️ This is the third telling of the same event, and the tellings differ. In Exodus 18 the idea comes from Jethro, Moses' father-in-law, who watches him judging alone and proposes the system; in Numbers 11 God tells Moses to gather seventy elders and puts his spirit on them. Here Moses tells it with no Jethro and no seventy — he says it himself, the people approve, and the appointments follow. The three accounts are not easily merged, and the honest options are that they are different moments in one long process, or that a preacher retelling a story forty years on emphasises what serves his sermon. Deuteronomy is a retelling throughout, and this is the first place a reader can see it working.

"You shall not recognise faces in judgement." Lo takkiru panim — a Hebrew idiom for showing partiality (the New Testament's "respecter of persons" comes from the same picture). Then the two extraordinary clauses: hear the small as the great, and do not shrink before any man — the second addressing a judge's fear rather than a judge's greed, which is the rarer and harder corruption to legislate against. And the ground given is not fairness as a principle but ownership: "for the judgement is God's." A judge is handling something that is not his. KJV "ye shall not respect persons in judgment… for the judgment is God's"; NIV "Do not show partiality"; the NWT "you must not be partial in judgment."

Verses 19–25 · whose idea the spies were

⚠️ Here is the sharpest difference between this retelling and Numbers, and it should not be smoothed. In Numbers 13, God says to Moses, "Send men to spy out the land of Canaan" — the mission is divinely commanded. In Deuteronomy 1:22, Moses says: "all of you came near to me and said, Let us send men ahead of us" — the mission is the people's idea, and Moses only agrees to it ("the thing was good in my eyes"). These are genuinely different framings of the same event, and the difference does work in the sermon: Moses is building a case about a generation that would not simply go when told. The usual harmonisations are that the people proposed it and God then permitted and commanded it, or that Moses is assigning responsibility rhetorically rather than correcting the record. This translation reports both and adopts neither. It is exactly the kind of seam this project prefers to leave visible.

"Let them dig out the land." Ve-yachperu — literally to dig or search out, a different verb from the ordinary "spy" (ragal) used in verse 24. The people ask for a survey; what happens is reconnaissance.

And the report is good. Verse 25 is easy to skip past: they bring back fruit and say, "The land that Jehovah our God is giving us is good." In Moses' telling the spies' report contains no bad news at all. The fear in verse 28 is attributed not to the report but to the brothers who "melted our heart" — the discouragement is a second act, not the first. What went wrong was not information.

Verses 26–33 · "because Jehovah hated us"

The accusation is the low point of the chapter. "It is because Jehovah hated us that he brought us out of the land of Egypt." ⚠️ It is worth registering how complete an inversion this is. The exodus is the founding act of love in Israel's story, and here it is read as an act of hatred — a rescue reinterpreted as a trap, with a plan to hand them over to the Amorites. And they say it, the text notes precisely, in their tents: not to Moses' face, not in the assembly, but grumbling in private. Deuteronomy will later build its whole demand on love (6:5), and it opens by quoting the generation that accused God of the opposite.

"Cities fortified to the heavens." Deliberate hyperbole, and the book knows it — Moses will repeat the phrase at 9:1 and the reader is meant to hear the panic in it. The Anakim are the tall people of the hill country, later associated with Hebron and with Goliath's line.

"As a man carries his son." ⚠️ Verse 31 is the tenderest image in the book, and it is placed with real care: directly against the accusation of hatred. They said he brought them out to destroy them; Moses answers that he carried them, the way a father carries a boy too tired to walk. The verb is nasa again — the same word Moses used of the burden he could not carry alone (v. 9). What was too heavy for Moses was not too heavy for God. KJV "as a man doth bear his son"; ESV the same. And then the sentence lands hard in verse 32: "and in this thing you were not trusting Jehovah your God" — after the carrying, after the fire and the cloud.

Verses 34–40 · the sentence, and Moses' own exclusion

An "evil generation." The oath is that none of them will see the land — the forty years of verse 3, explained. Only Caleb is named as an exception here, "because he has fully followed Jehovah" (mille acharei, literally "he filled up after" — an idiom of wholehearted, unreserved following); Joshua is added in verse 38 in his own capacity, as the one who will lead. ⚠️ Both men are exceptions to a national judgement, which is itself worth noticing: the generation is condemned as a generation, and individuals within it are not.

⚠️ "With me also Jehovah was angry on your account." This is a real difficulty and the honest thing is to name it. In Numbers 20, Moses is barred from the land for what he himself did at the waters of Meribah — striking the rock and failing to treat God as holy before the people. Here, three times in Deuteronomy (1:37, 3:26, 4:21), Moses says he was excluded because of them. The readings offered: that both are true, since the people's provocation is what drove him to it; that Moses is speaking pastorally, folding his fate into theirs to make the lesson land; or that the sermon simply does not aim at the precision the narrative gives elsewhere. What should not be done is to pretend the two accounts say the same thing. They do not, and Deuteronomy is a sermon by a man who is about to die outside the land he spent forty years walking toward.

"Your children, who today do not know good from evil." The phrase for moral immaturity — the same pairing as the tree in Genesis 2 — and it is the hinge of the whole book. The people being addressed are those children, now grown. Moses is telling a second generation what happened to the first, and the entire sermon is aimed at making sure they do not repeat it.

Verses 41–46 · the repentance that came too late

They say the right words. "We have sinned against Jehovah. We ourselves will go up and fight." ⚠️ And it is refused — which makes this one of the hardest passages in the Torah, because a confession is answered with "do not go up, for I am not among you." The point is not that repentance is impossible but that obedience has a time. The command was to go up; they would not. Now the command is to turn back; they will not. Both refusals are the same refusal — doing what they have decided rather than what they have been told — and the second is dressed as repentance. The verb in verse 41 says it: va-tahinu, you thought it a light thing to go up.

"They chased you as bees do." A precise and humiliating image: not a rout by a superior army but a swarm — the smaller, angrier, more numerous thing driving them down the hill. KJV "chased you, as bees do."

"And you returned and wept before Jehovah, but Jehovah did not listen." The last line of the chapter is quieter and worse: they stayed in Kadesh "many days — as many days as you stayed." The Hebrew simply repeats itself, and translations differ over whether to smooth it into "a long time." Kept flat here, because the flatness is the point: nothing happened for a very long time. That is what the forty years were.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "make this instruction plain" for be'er et ha-torah — instruction, not "law," and be'er's sense of engraving a thing legibly; "carry" for nasa kept the same in verse 9 and verse 31, so the burden Moses cannot lift and the son God carries share one verb; "you shall not recognise faces" for the partiality idiom, with "the judgement is God's" left as ownership rather than principle; "rebelled against the mouth of Jehovah" kept literal; "thought it a light thing" for va-tahinu; and verse 46's flat repetition left unsmoothed.

Echoes paid. Joseph's dying oath — "the land he swore to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob" — quoted at the border it pointed to; the mountain of the covenant left behind on command; and knowing good and evil turned into a description of children too young to be guilty.

Echoes planted. Devarim — "words" — the name of the book and its method; the forty years whose cause this chapter supplies; Joshua, introduced here as the one to be strengthened; Caleb, who "fully followed"; and the generation "who do not know good from evil," who are the people Moses is actually talking to.

Next installment in this book: Deuteronomy 2 — the long way round. Israel is told to leave Edom, Moab and Ammon alone and not to take a foot of their land, because those countries were given to Esau's sons and to Lot's — and then the road runs into Sihon king of Heshbon.

Joshua 1 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיְהִי, אַחֲרֵי מוֹת מֹשֶׁה--עֶבֶד יְהוָה; וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-יְהוֹשֻׁעַ בִּן-נוּן, מְשָׁרֵת מֹשֶׁה לֵאמֹר.
And it happened, after the death of Moses the servant of Jehovah, that Jehovah said to Joshua son of Nun, Moses' attendant, saying:note
2
מֹשֶׁה עַבְדִּי, מֵת; וְעַתָּה קוּם עֲבֹר אֶת-הַיַּרְדֵּן הַזֶּה, אַתָּה וְכָל-הָעָם הַזֶּה, אֶל-הָאָרֶץ, אֲשֶׁר אָנֹכִי נֹתֵן לָהֶם לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל.
"Moses my servant is dead. And now, rise, cross this Jordan — you and all this people — into the land that I am giving to them, to the sons of Israel.note
3
כָּל-מָקוֹם, אֲשֶׁר תִּדְרֹךְ כַּף-רַגְלְכֶם בּוֹ--לָכֶם נְתַתִּיו: כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתִּי, אֶל-מֹשֶׁה.
Every place that the sole of your foot treads on, to you I have given it, just as I spoke to Moses.note
4
מֵהַמִּדְבָּר וְהַלְּבָנוֹן הַזֶּה וְעַד-הַנָּהָר הַגָּדוֹל נְהַר-פְּרָת, כֹּל אֶרֶץ הַחִתִּים, וְעַד-הַיָּם הַגָּדוֹל, מְבוֹא הַשָּׁמֶשׁ--יִהְיֶה, גְּבוּלְכֶם.
From the wilderness and this Lebanon as far as the great river, the river Euphrates — all the land of the Hittites — and to the Great Sea, the going-in of the sun, shall be your border.note
5
לֹא-יִתְיַצֵּב אִישׁ לְפָנֶיךָ, כֹּל יְמֵי חַיֶּיךָ: כַּאֲשֶׁר הָיִיתִי עִם-מֹשֶׁה אֶהְיֶה עִמָּךְ, לֹא אַרְפְּךָ וְלֹא אֶעֶזְבֶךָּ.
No man will stand before you all the days of your life. As I was with Moses, I will be with you; I will not let you go and I will not forsake you.note
6
חֲזַק, וֶאֱמָץ: כִּי אַתָּה, תַּנְחִיל אֶת-הָעָם הַזֶּה, אֶת-הָאָרֶץ, אֲשֶׁר-נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי לַאֲבוֹתָם לָתֵת לָהֶם.
Be strong and resolute, for you are the one who will give this people the land as an inheritance — the land I swore to their fathers to give them.note
7
רַק חֲזַק וֶאֱמַץ מְאֹד, לִשְׁמֹר לַעֲשׂוֹת כְּכָל-הַתּוֹרָה--אֲשֶׁר צִוְּךָ מֹשֶׁה עַבְדִּי, אַל-תָּסוּר מִמֶּנּוּ יָמִין וּשְׂמֹאול: לְמַעַן תַּשְׂכִּיל, בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר תֵּלֵךְ.
Only be strong and very resolute, to be careful to do according to all the instruction that Moses my servant commanded you. Do not turn aside from it, right or left, so that you may act wisely wherever you go.note
8
לֹא-יָמוּשׁ סֵפֶר הַתּוֹרָה הַזֶּה מִפִּיךָ, וְהָגִיתָ בּוֹ יוֹמָם וָלַיְלָה, לְמַעַן תִּשְׁמֹר לַעֲשׂוֹת, כְּכָל-הַכָּתוּב בּוֹ: כִּי-אָז תַּצְלִיחַ אֶת-דְּרָכֶךָ, וְאָז תַּשְׂכִּיל.
This book of the instruction shall not depart from your mouth; you shall murmur over it day and night, so that you may be careful to do according to all that is written in it — for then you will make your way prosper, and then you will act wisely.note
9
הֲלוֹא צִוִּיתִיךָ חֲזַק וֶאֱמָץ, אַל-תַּעֲרֹץ וְאַל-תֵּחָת: כִּי עִמְּךָ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ, בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר תֵּלֵךְ.{פ}
Have I not commanded you? Be strong and resolute. Do not be terrified and do not be dismayed, for Jehovah your God is with you wherever you go."note
10
וַיְצַו יְהוֹשֻׁעַ, אֶת-שֹׁטְרֵי הָעָם לֵאמֹר.
And Joshua commanded the officers of the people, saying:note
11
עִבְרוּ בְּקֶרֶב הַמַּחֲנֶה, וְצַוּוּ אֶת-הָעָם לֵאמֹר, הָכִינוּ לָכֶם, צֵידָה: כִּי בְּעוֹד שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים, אַתֶּם עֹבְרִים אֶת-הַיַּרְדֵּן הַזֶּה, לָבוֹא לָרֶשֶׁת אֶת-הָאָרֶץ, אֲשֶׁר יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם נֹתֵן לָכֶם לְרִשְׁתָּהּ.
"Pass through the midst of the camp and command the people, saying: Prepare provisions for yourselves, for in three more days you are crossing this Jordan to go in and take possession of the land that Jehovah your God is giving you to possess."note
12
וְלָראוּבֵנִי, וְלַגָּדִי, וְלַחֲצִי, שֵׁבֶט הַמְנַשֶּׁה--אָמַר יְהוֹשֻׁעַ, לֵאמֹר.
And to the Reubenites and to the Gadites and to the half-tribe of Manasseh, Joshua said, saying:note
13
זָכוֹר, אֶת-הַדָּבָר, אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֶתְכֶם מֹשֶׁה עֶבֶד-יְהוָה, לֵאמֹר: יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם מֵנִיחַ לָכֶם, וְנָתַן לָכֶם אֶת-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת.
"Remember the word that Moses the servant of Jehovah commanded you, saying: Jehovah your God is giving you rest and has given you this land.note
14
נְשֵׁיכֶם טַפְּכֶם, וּמִקְנֵיכֶם, יֵשְׁבוּ, בָּאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר נָתַן לָכֶם מֹשֶׁה בְּעֵבֶר הַיַּרְדֵּן; וְאַתֶּם תַּעַבְרוּ חֲמֻשִׁים לִפְנֵי אֲחֵיכֶם, כֹּל גִּבּוֹרֵי הַחַיִל, וַעֲזַרְתֶּם, אוֹתָם.
Your wives, your little ones, and your livestock will dwell in the land that Moses gave you across the Jordan; but you yourselves — all the mighty men of valour — shall cross over armed, ahead of your brothers, and help them,note
15
עַד אֲשֶׁר-יָנִיחַ יְהוָה לַאֲחֵיכֶם, כָּכֶם, וְיָרְשׁוּ גַם-הֵמָּה, אֶת-הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר-יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם נֹתֵן לָהֶם; וְשַׁבְתֶּם לְאֶרֶץ יְרֻשַּׁתְכֶם, וִירִשְׁתֶּם אוֹתָהּ, אֲשֶׁר נָתַן לָכֶם מֹשֶׁה עֶבֶד יְהוָה, בְּעֵבֶר הַיַּרְדֵּן מִזְרַח הַשָּׁמֶשׁ.
until Jehovah gives your brothers rest as he has you, and they too take possession of the land that Jehovah your God is giving them. Then you shall return to the land of your possession and possess it — the land that Moses the servant of Jehovah gave you across the Jordan, toward the rising of the sun."note
16
וַיַּעֲנוּ, אֶת-יְהוֹשֻׁעַ לֵאמֹר: כֹּל אֲשֶׁר-צִוִּיתָנוּ נַעֲשֶׂה, וְאֶל-כָּל-אֲשֶׁר תִּשְׁלָחֵנוּ נֵלֵךְ.
And they answered Joshua, saying: "All that you have commanded us we will do, and wherever you send us we will go.note
17
כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר-שָׁמַעְנוּ אֶל-מֹשֶׁה, כֵּן נִשְׁמַע אֵלֶיךָ: רַק יִהְיֶה יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ, עִמָּךְ, כַּאֲשֶׁר הָיָה, עִם-מֹשֶׁה.
Just as we listened to Moses in everything, so we will listen to you. Only may Jehovah your God be with you, as he was with Moses.note
18
כָּל-אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר-יַמְרֶה אֶת-פִּיךָ, וְלֹא-יִשְׁמַע אֶת-דְּבָרֶיךָ לְכֹל אֲשֶׁר-תְּצַוֶּנּוּ--יוּמָת: רַק, חֲזַק וֶאֱמָץ.{פ}
Any man who rebels against your mouth and does not listen to your words in all that you command him shall be put to death. Only be strong and resolute."note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–5 · "Moses my servant is dead"

The book of Joshua begins where the Torah stopped, and it does not soften the fact. The first words after "and it happened" are the last thing anyone wanted to hear: Moses is dead. ⚠️ This is the seam between the Five Books and everything that follows — in the Hebrew Bible, Joshua opens the section called the Former Prophets — and it is handled with a single flat clause and an immediate order to move. There is no eulogy here (Deuteronomy 34 gave that). The point of verse 2 is that the death of the greatest figure in Israel's history is not permitted to become a reason to stop: "Moses my servant is dead. And now, rise, cross."

The two titles are doing work. Moses is eved YHWH, "the servant of Jehovah" — the highest title in the Hebrew Bible, given to Moses again and again. Joshua, at the start, is only mesharet Moshe, "Moses' attendant," the word for a personal aide who waits on someone (the young Samuel is a mesharet in the temple). ⚠️ Watch the word across the book: the man who begins as Moses' assistant will himself be called "the servant of Jehovah" in the very last chapter, at his own death (24:29). Joshua 1 is the story of that promotion beginning, and it opens by naming the gap he has to cross.

"Every place the sole of your foot treads." The grant is enormous — wilderness to Lebanon to the Euphrates to the Great Sea (the Mediterranean, called here mevo ha-shemesh, "the going-in of the sun," i.e. the west). ⚠️ It is the same ideal extent promised in Deuteronomy 1:7, and the same one Israel never in fact held except briefly under Solomon. The land is given in the perfect tense — "to you I have given it" — while the treading is left to them; possession is declared as a settled fact and worked out as a task, which is the theological engine of the whole book.

"I will not let you go and I will not forsake you." Two verbs of holding-on, both negated. The promise is exactly what was made to the man who just died — as I was with Moses, I will be with you — and Hebrews 13:5 quotes this line as a promise to every believer. KJV "I will not fail thee, nor forsake thee"; the ASV the same.

Verses 6–9 · be strong and resolute — three times

The command is repeated, and it escalates. Chazak ve-ematz, "be strong and resolute," lands three times in four verses — verse 6, verse 7 (with "very"), and verse 9 — framing everything between. It is not a pep talk. KJV "Be strong and of a good courage"; the NIV "Be strong and courageous"; the ESV "Be strong and courageous." It is a military formula (David gives it to Solomon, and it recurs through the historical books), and here it brackets the one thing Joshua is actually told to be strong for: not the fighting, but the keeping of the instruction. ⚠️ Note where the "very" falls — verse 7, about obedience to the torah, not verse 6, about conquering. The book is quietly saying the harder courage is the second kind.

"You shall murmur over it day and night." The verb is hagah, and it is concrete and strange. It means to make a low sound — a lion growling over prey (Isaiah 31:4), a dove moaning, a person muttering. Applied to the scroll it is not silent study but reading aloud under the breath, the ancient practice of chewing a text over by voicing it continually. ⚠️ It is the very same word that opens the Psalter — the blessed man "murmurs" (hagah) in the torah of Jehovah day and night (Psalm 1:2) — and the overlap is not accidental: Joshua 1 and Psalm 1 are the two great "day and night in the torah" texts, one at the head of the Prophets and one at the head of the Writings. Most versions render it "meditate," which is not wrong but loses the sound. KJV "thou shalt meditate therein day and night"; NWT "you must read it in an undertone day and night," which keeps the voice.

"Then you will act wisely." Taskil — from sakal, the same root as the verb that gave the crossed hands their meaning in Genesis 48: to act with insight, to succeed because one acts with understanding. It brackets verse 8 with tatzliach, "prosper," so the two promises are success and skill — and both are attached, pointedly, to the scroll rather than the sword. ⚠️ This is the Bible's own definition of "success" for a general, and it is bookish: prosperity follows from doing what is written, not from strength of arms. The KJV's famous "then thou shalt have good success" (v. 8) reads taskil as prospering, which is close.

"Have I not commanded you?" The section closes by folding the courage-command and the presence-promise into one rhetorical question — the reason not to be terrified is not Joshua's competence but the sentence that has run through the whole passage: Jehovah your God is with you wherever you go.

Verses 10–11 · three days, and provisions

The first thing Joshua does with the command is delegate it. He has just been told, by God, to be strong; his first recorded act is to send the officers through the camp with an entirely practical order — pack food, we cross in three days. ⚠️ There is a small chronological puzzle here that the book does not stop to resolve: the "three days" of verse 11 and the spies of chapter 2 and the actual crossing of chapters 3-4 do not line up into a tidy single count, and readers have proposed various orderings. It is the ordinary looseness of a narrative that is telling a story rather than filing a report; noted, not smoothed. What matters here is the tone — the towering theophany of verses 1-9 comes down immediately to rations and a marching order.

Verses 12–15 · the two-and-a-half tribes, and a debt called in

Joshua's first move is to hold people to a promise. Reuben, Gad and half of Manasseh had asked Moses, back in Numbers 32, to settle on the east side of the Jordan — the good grazing land they had already conquered — rather than cross into Canaan proper. Moses agreed on one condition: that their fighting men cross over armed, ahead of their brothers, and not go home until the land was won. ⚠️ Joshua opens his command by cashing that promise. The families and herds stay east; the warriors go west. It is a deliberate picture of the nation as one body — the tribes who already have their inheritance do not sit it out while the others fight.

"Rest." Meniach lakhem, "giving you rest" — nuach, the word for settling, ceasing, coming to a stop. ⚠️ It is one of the book's key words and one of its promises: the point of the crossing is not conquest for its own sake but rest, a place to stop moving after forty years of not stopping. Hebrews 4 later reads this "rest" as unfinished — Joshua gave them a land, but "if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day" — which is why the Greek form of the name, Iēsous (the same name as Jesus), is doing quiet work in that argument.

Verses 16–18 · "as we listened to Moses, so we will listen to you"

The people accept the succession — with a condition attached. Their pledge is total ("all that you have commanded us we will do") and then, in the same breath, they pin it to the one thing that actually matters: only may Jehovah be with you, as he was with Moses. ⚠️ It is not a hedge; it is the right instinct. Their loyalty to Joshua is real but derivative — they will follow him precisely insofar as God is with him, which is exactly what the first nine verses promised. The people, without having heard God's private words to Joshua, name the very condition God had just guaranteed.

⚠️ "Shall be put to death." The sentence is harsh and worth being honest about: they undertake to execute anyone who rebels against Joshua's word. This is the language of a nation under military discipline crossing into a war, not a general ethic — the same generation is bound by a marching order, and desertion in a campaign was a capital matter across the ancient world. It is reported, not commended; the book states the terms the people set and moves on.

And they give the command back to him. The people's last word to Joshua is the very phrase God opened with: rak chazak ve-ematz, "only be strong and resolute." ⚠️ The charge God laid on him in verse 6 comes back out of the mouths of the people in verse 18 — the encouragement has gone all the way round the camp and returned to its source. A leader is told to be strong by God, and then told the same thing by the people he leads. It is the chapter's quiet last note, and a generous one.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "Moses' attendant" for mesharet, so the gap between the aide and the "servant of Jehovah" he becomes is visible; "be strong and resolute" for chazak ve-ematz, the military formula, kept identical across all three occurrences; "murmur over it" for hagah, keeping the sound rather than flattening to "meditate"; "act wisely" for taskil, holding the insight-and-success sense; "rest" for nuach; and "the going-in of the sun" for the west, left as the picture the Hebrew draws.

Echoes paid. Deuteronomy 1's ideal borders, repeated as the land is finally entered; the insight-verb of the crossed hands, now the promise made to Joshua; and Moses' charge at the end of Deuteronomy — "be strong and resolute" — handed to his successor and then handed back by the people.

Echoes planted. Joshua the attendant, on his way to "the servant of Jehovah"; "day and night in the torah," which Psalm 1 will make the mark of the blessed man; the rest that Hebrews 4 will call unfinished; and the two-and-a-half tribes' pledge, which the book will test at the very end when they build an altar by the Jordan and are nearly attacked for it (Joshua 22).

Next installment in this book: Joshua 2 — two spies cross into Jericho and lodge with Rahab, a prostitute, who hides them under the flax on her roof, lies to the king's men, and strikes a bargain for her family's lives with a scarlet cord in the window.

Judges 1 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיְהִי, אַחֲרֵי מוֹת יְהוֹשֻׁעַ, וַיִּשְׁאֲלוּ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, בַּיהוָה לֵאמֹר: מִי יַעֲלֶה-לָּנוּ אֶל-הַכְּנַעֲנִי בַּתְּחִלָּה, לְהִלָּחֶם בּוֹ.
And it happened, after the death of Joshua, that the sons of Israel inquired of Jehovah, saying, "Who will go up for us against the Canaanite first, to fight against him?"note
2
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה, יְהוּדָה יַעֲלֶה: הִנֵּה נָתַתִּי אֶת-הָאָרֶץ, בְּיָדוֹ.
And Jehovah said, "Judah will go up. Look — I have given the land into his hand."note
3
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה לְשִׁמְעוֹן אָחִיו עֲלֵה אִתִּי בְגֹרָלִי, וְנִלָּחֲמָה בַּכְּנַעֲנִי, וְהָלַכְתִּי גַם-אֲנִי אִתְּךָ, בְּגוֹרָלֶךָ; וַיֵּלֶךְ אִתּוֹ, שִׁמְעוֹן.
And Judah said to Simeon his brother, "Go up with me into my allotted portion, and let us fight against the Canaanite; and I too will go with you into yours." And Simeon went with him.note
4
וַיַּעַל יְהוּדָה, וַיִּתֵּן יְהוָה אֶת-הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְהַפְּרִזִּי בְּיָדָם; וַיַּכּוּם בְּבֶזֶק, עֲשֶׂרֶת אֲלָפִים אִישׁ.
And Judah went up, and Jehovah gave the Canaanite and the Perizzite into their hand; and they struck down ten thousand men at Bezek.note
5
וַיִּמְצְאוּ אֶת-אֲדֹנִי בֶזֶק, בְּבֶזֶק, וַיִּלָּחֲמוּ, בּוֹ; וַיַּכּוּ, אֶת-הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְאֶת-הַפְּרִזִּי.
And they found Adoni-bezek at Bezek and fought against him, and they struck down the Canaanite and the Perizzite.note
6
וַיָּנָס אֲדֹנִי בֶזֶק, וַיִּרְדְּפוּ אַחֲרָיו; וַיֹּאחֲזוּ אוֹתוֹ--וַיְקַצְּצוּ, אֶת-בְּהֹנוֹת יָדָיו וְרַגְלָיו.
And Adoni-bezek fled, and they pursued him and caught him and cut off his thumbs and his big toes.note
7
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲדֹנִי-בֶזֶק, שִׁבְעִים מְלָכִים בְּהֹנוֹת יְדֵיהֶם וְרַגְלֵיהֶם מְקֻצָּצִים הָיוּ מְלַקְּטִים תַּחַת שֻׁלְחָנִי--כַּאֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתִי, כֵּן שִׁלַּם-לִי אֱלֹהִים; וַיְבִיאֻהוּ יְרוּשָׁלִַם, וַיָּמָת שָׁם.{פ}
And Adoni-bezek said, "Seventy kings with their thumbs and big toes cut off used to gather under my table. As I have done, so God has repaid me." And they brought him to Jerusalem, and he died there.note
8
וַיִּלָּחֲמוּ בְנֵי-יְהוּדָה, בִּירוּשָׁלִַם, וַיִּלְכְּדוּ אוֹתָהּ, וַיַּכּוּהָ לְפִי-חָרֶב; וְאֶת-הָעִיר, שִׁלְּחוּ בָאֵשׁ.
And the sons of Judah fought against Jerusalem and captured it, and struck it with the edge of the sword, and sent the city up in fire.note
9
וְאַחַר, יָרְדוּ בְּנֵי יְהוּדָה, לְהִלָּחֵם, בַּכְּנַעֲנִי--יוֹשֵׁב הָהָר, וְהַנֶּגֶב וְהַשְּׁפֵלָה.
And afterward the sons of Judah went down to fight against the Canaanite who dwelt in the hill country and the Negev and the lowland.note
10
וַיֵּלֶךְ יְהוּדָה, אֶל-הַכְּנַעֲנִי הַיּוֹשֵׁב בְּחֶבְרוֹן, וְשֵׁם-חֶבְרוֹן לְפָנִים, קִרְיַת אַרְבַּע; וַיַּכּוּ אֶת-שֵׁשַׁי וְאֶת-אֲחִימַן, וְאֶת-תַּלְמָי.
And Judah went against the Canaanite who dwelt in Hebron — the name of Hebron formerly was Kiriath-arba — and they struck down Sheshai and Ahiman and Talmai.note
11
וַיֵּלֶךְ מִשָּׁם, אֶל-יוֹשְׁבֵי דְּבִיר; וְשֵׁם-דְּבִיר לְפָנִים, קִרְיַת-סֵפֶר.
And from there he went against the inhabitants of Debir — the name of Debir formerly was Kiriath-sepher.note
12
וַיֹּאמֶר כָּלֵב, אֲשֶׁר-יַכֶּה אֶת-קִרְיַת-סֵפֶר וּלְכָדָהּ--וְנָתַתִּי לוֹ אֶת-עַכְסָה בִתִּי, לְאִשָּׁה.
And Caleb said, "Whoever strikes Kiriath-sepher and captures it, to him I will give Achsah my daughter as a wife."note
13
וַיִּלְכְּדָהּ עָתְנִיאֵל בֶּן-קְנַז, אֲחִי כָלֵב הַקָּטֹן מִמֶּנּוּ; וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ אֶת-עַכְסָה בִתּוֹ, לְאִשָּׁה.
And Othniel son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother, captured it; and he gave him Achsah his daughter as a wife.note
14
וַיְהִי בְּבוֹאָהּ, וַתְּסִיתֵהוּ לִשְׁאֹל מֵאֵת-אָבִיהָ הַשָּׂדֶה, וַתִּצְנַח, מֵעַל הַחֲמוֹר; וַיֹּאמֶר-לָהּ כָּלֵב, מַה-לָּךְ.
And it happened, when she came to him, that she urged him to ask her father for a field. And she slid down from the donkey, and Caleb said to her, "What do you want?"note
15
וַתֹּאמֶר לוֹ הָבָה-לִּי בְרָכָה, כִּי אֶרֶץ הַנֶּגֶב נְתַתָּנִי, וְנָתַתָּה לִי, גֻּלֹּת מָיִם; וַיִּתֶּן-לָהּ כָּלֵב, אֵת גֻּלֹּת עִלִּית, וְאֵת, גֻּלֹּת תַּחְתִּית.{פ}
And she said to him, "Give me a blessing. Since you have given me the land of the Negev, give me also springs of water." And Caleb gave her the upper springs and the lower springs.note
16
וּבְנֵי קֵינִי חֹתֵן מֹשֶׁה עָלוּ מֵעִיר הַתְּמָרִים, אֶת-בְּנֵי יְהוּדָה, מִדְבַּר יְהוּדָה, אֲשֶׁר בְּנֶגֶב עֲרָד; וַיֵּלֶךְ, וַיֵּשֶׁב אֶת-הָעָם.
And the sons of the Kenite, Moses' father-in-law, went up from the City of Palms with the sons of Judah to the wilderness of Judah, which is in the Negev of Arad; and they went and dwelt with the people.note
17
וַיֵּלֶךְ יְהוּדָה, אֶת-שִׁמְעוֹן אָחִיו, וַיַּכּוּ, אֶת-הַכְּנַעֲנִי יוֹשֵׁב צְפַת; וַיַּחֲרִימוּ אוֹתָהּ, וַיִּקְרָא אֶת-שֵׁם-הָעִיר חָרְמָה.
And Judah went with Simeon his brother, and they struck down the Canaanite who dwelt in Zephath, and put it to the ban; and the name of the city was called Hormah.note
18
וַיִּלְכֹּד יְהוּדָה אֶת-עַזָּה וְאֶת-גְּבוּלָהּ, וְאֶת-אַשְׁקְלוֹן וְאֶת-גְּבוּלָהּ, וְאֶת-עֶקְרוֹן, וְאֶת-גְּבוּלָהּ.
And Judah captured Gaza and its territory, and Ashkelon and its territory, and Ekron and its territory.note
19
וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת-יְהוּדָה, וַיֹּרֶשׁ אֶת-הָהָר: כִּי לֹא לְהוֹרִישׁ אֶת-יֹשְׁבֵי הָעֵמֶק, כִּי-רֶכֶב בַּרְזֶל לָהֶם.
And Jehovah was with Judah, and he took possession of the hill country; but he could not drive out the inhabitants of the valley, because they had chariots of iron.note
20
וַיִּתְּנוּ לְכָלֵב אֶת-חֶבְרוֹן, כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר מֹשֶׁה; וַיּוֹרֶשׁ מִשָּׁם, אֶת-שְׁלֹשָׁה בְּנֵי הָעֲנָק.
And they gave Hebron to Caleb, as Moses had spoken; and he drove out from there the three sons of the Anak.note
21
וְאֶת-הַיְבוּסִי יֹשֵׁב יְרוּשָׁלִַם, לֹא הוֹרִישׁוּ בְּנֵי בִנְיָמִן; וַיֵּשֶׁב הַיְבוּסִי אֶת-בְּנֵי בִנְיָמִן, בִּירוּשָׁלִַם, עַד, הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה.{פ}
But the Jebusite who dwelt in Jerusalem the sons of Benjamin did not drive out; and the Jebusite has dwelt with the sons of Benjamin in Jerusalem to this day.note
22
וַיַּעֲלוּ בֵית-יוֹסֵף גַּם-הֵם, בֵּית-אֵל; וַיהוָה, עִמָּם.
And the house of Joseph, they too, went up against Bethel; and Jehovah was with them.note
23
וַיָּתִירוּ בֵית-יוֹסֵף, בְּבֵית-אֵל; וְשֵׁם-הָעִיר לְפָנִים, לוּז.
And the house of Joseph spied out Bethel — the name of the city formerly was Luz.note
24
וַיִּרְאוּ, הַשֹּׁמְרִים, אִישׁ, יוֹצֵא מִן-הָעִיר; וַיֹּאמְרוּ לוֹ, הַרְאֵנוּ נָא אֶת-מְבוֹא הָעִיר, וְעָשִׂינוּ עִמְּךָ, חָסֶד.
And the watchers saw a man coming out of the city, and they said to him, "Show us, please, the way into the city, and we will deal kindly with you."note
25
וַיַּרְאֵם אֶת-מְבוֹא הָעִיר, וַיַּכּוּ אֶת-הָעִיר לְפִי-חָרֶב; וְאֶת-הָאִישׁ וְאֶת-כָּל-מִשְׁפַּחְתּוֹ, שִׁלֵּחוּ.
And he showed them the way into the city, and they struck the city with the edge of the sword; but the man and all his family they let go.note
26
וַיֵּלֶךְ הָאִישׁ, אֶרֶץ הַחִתִּים; וַיִּבֶן עִיר, וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמָהּ לוּז--הוּא שְׁמָהּ, עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה.{פ}
And the man went to the land of the Hittites and built a city and called its name Luz — that is its name to this day.note
27
וְלֹא-הוֹרִישׁ מְנַשֶּׁה, אֶת-בֵּית-שְׁאָן וְאֶת-בְּנוֹתֶיהָ וְאֶת-תַּעְנַךְ וְאֶת-בְּנֹתֶיהָ, וְאֶת-יֹשְׁבֵי דוֹר וְאֶת-בְּנוֹתֶיהָ וְאֶת-יוֹשְׁבֵי יִבְלְעָם וְאֶת-בְּנֹתֶיהָ, וְאֶת-יוֹשְׁבֵי מְגִדּוֹ וְאֶת-בְּנוֹתֶיהָ; וַיּוֹאֶל, הַכְּנַעֲנִי, לָשֶׁבֶת, בָּאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת.
And Manasseh did not drive out Beth-shean and its daughter-towns, nor Taanach and its daughter-towns, nor the inhabitants of Dor and its daughter-towns, nor the inhabitants of Ibleam and its daughter-towns, nor the inhabitants of Megiddo and its daughter-towns; and the Canaanite was determined to dwell in that land.note
28
וַיְהִי כִּי-חָזַק יִשְׂרָאֵל, וַיָּשֶׂם אֶת-הַכְּנַעֲנִי לָמַס; וְהוֹרֵישׁ, לֹא הוֹרִישׁוֹ.{ס}
And it happened, when Israel grew strong, that they put the Canaanite to forced labour; but they did not utterly drive him out. note
29
וְאֶפְרַיִם לֹא הוֹרִישׁ, אֶת-הַכְּנַעֲנִי הַיּוֹשֵׁב בְּגָזֶר; וַיֵּשֶׁב הַכְּנַעֲנִי בְּקִרְבּוֹ, בְּגָזֶר.{ס}
And Ephraim did not drive out the Canaanite who dwelt in Gezer; and the Canaanite dwelt in his midst, in Gezer.note
30
זְבוּלֻן, לֹא הוֹרִישׁ אֶת-יוֹשְׁבֵי קִטְרוֹן, וְאֶת-יוֹשְׁבֵי, נַהֲלֹל; וַיֵּשֶׁב הַכְּנַעֲנִי בְּקִרְבּוֹ, וַיִּהְיוּ לָמַס.{ס}
Zebulun did not drive out the inhabitants of Kitron or the inhabitants of Nahalol; and the Canaanite dwelt in his midst and became forced labour.note
31
אָשֵׁר, לֹא הוֹרִישׁ אֶת-יֹשְׁבֵי עַכּוֹ, וְאֶת-יוֹשְׁבֵי, צִידוֹן; וְאֶת-אַחְלָב וְאֶת-אַכְזִיב וְאֶת-חֶלְבָּה, וְאֶת-אֲפִיק וְאֶת-רְחֹב.
Asher did not drive out the inhabitants of Acco or the inhabitants of Sidon, or Ahlab or Achzib or Helbah or Aphik or Rehob;note
32
וַיֵּשֶׁב, הָאָשֵׁרִי, בְּקֶרֶב הַכְּנַעֲנִי, יֹשְׁבֵי הָאָרֶץ: כִּי, לֹא הוֹרִישׁוֹ.{ס}
and the Asherite dwelt among the Canaanite inhabitants of the land, for he did not drive them out.note
33
נַפְתָּלִי, לֹא-הוֹרִישׁ אֶת-יֹשְׁבֵי בֵית-שֶׁמֶשׁ וְאֶת-יֹשְׁבֵי בֵית-עֲנָת, וַיֵּשֶׁב, בְּקֶרֶב הַכְּנַעֲנִי יֹשְׁבֵי הָאָרֶץ; וְיֹשְׁבֵי בֵית-שֶׁמֶשׁ וּבֵית עֲנָת, הָיוּ לָהֶם לָמַס.{ס}
Naphtali did not drive out the inhabitants of Beth-shemesh or the inhabitants of Beth-anath, and he dwelt among the Canaanite inhabitants of the land; but the inhabitants of Beth-shemesh and Beth-anath became forced labour for them.note
34
וַיִּלְחֲצוּ הָאֱמֹרִי אֶת-בְּנֵי-דָן, הָהָרָה: כִּי-לֹא נְתָנוֹ, לָרֶדֶת לָעֵמֶק.
And the Amorite pressed the sons of Dan back into the hill country, for they would not let him come down to the valley.note
35
וַיּוֹאֶל הָאֱמֹרִי לָשֶׁבֶת בְּהַר-חֶרֶס, בְּאַיָּלוֹן וּבְשַׁעַלְבִים; וַתִּכְבַּד יַד בֵּית-יוֹסֵף, וַיִּהְיוּ לָמַס.
And the Amorite was determined to dwell in Mount Heres, in Aijalon and in Shaalbim; but the hand of the house of Joseph grew heavy, and they became forced labour.note
36
וּגְבוּל, הָאֱמֹרִי, מִמַּעֲלֵה, עַקְרַבִּים--מֵהַסֶּלַע, וָמָעְלָה.{פ}
And the border of the Amorite ran from the Ascent of Scorpions, from the Rock and upward.note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–7 · after Joshua, and a king who did the same

The book begins the way Joshua did — "after the death of…" — and that is the point. Joshua opened "after the death of Moses"; Judges opens "after the death of Joshua." ⚠️ The pattern is deliberate and the book will make it terrible: Israel's story has become a sequence of leaders dying and a people left to see whether they will hold. This first chapter is the record of the generation right after Joshua trying to finish the conquest — and, tribe by tribe, failing to. It is the honest, unheroic underside of the book of Joshua, and the two books were almost certainly meant to be read against each other.

They ask before they act. "The sons of Israel inquired of Jehovah" — who goes up first? — and the answer is Judah, with the land declared already given ("I have given the land into his hand," the same settled-fact perfect as Joshua 1). The book opens well: they consult God, God answers, Judah wins. Watch how far it has fallen by the last verse of the chapter, and how much further by the last chapter of the book.

⚠️ Adoni-bezek, and a rough justice the text lets stand. A Canaanite king is caught and his thumbs and big toes are cut off — a real and horrible mutilation that disables a warrior (no grip, no balance) and humiliates a king. And then the man convicts himself: seventy kings with their thumbs and toes cut off used to gather scraps under my table; as I have done, so God has repaid me. KJV "as I have done, so God hath requited me"; the ASV the same. ⚠️ The narrator does not moralise; he lets a pagan king pronounce the sentence on his own cruelty and name the God of Israel as the one who balanced it. It is a grim opening note — Israel does to him exactly what he had done to others, and the victim calls it justice — and the book is signalling early that it will not clean up what its people do.

Verses 8–10 · Jerusalem taken — and not

⚠️ Verse 8 says Judah burned Jerusalem; verse 21 says the Jebusites still live there. Both stand. This is not a contradiction to hide but a seam to understand. Taking a city in ancient warfare — burning it, striking its fighters — is not the same as holding it and driving out its people; a raided city is reoccupied. Jerusalem is the perfect example: Judah strikes it here, Benjamin fails to dispossess it in verse 21, and it will not truly become Israelite until David takes the stronghold generations later (2 Samuel 5). The chapter is built on exactly this distinction — the difference between a victory and a possession — and Jerusalem is where it is starkest.

"Formerly Kiriath-arba." Hebron's older name means "the town of Arba," a giant, the ancestor of the Anakim — which is why the three names struck down here (Sheshai, Ahiman, Talmai) are the giants Caleb had marked out. The scene is a compressed retelling of material also in Joshua's conquest account; the two books overlap on purpose.

Verses 11–15 · Achsah, who asked for water

A whole marriage-and-inheritance story in five verses, and its hero is a woman. Caleb offers his daughter Achsah to whoever takes Kiriath-sepher; his younger kinsman Othniel does it (he becomes the first of the judges in chapter 3, so the book is quietly introducing its first deliverer as a footnote to a wedding). But the story is hers. ⚠️ She is the one who acts. She urges Othniel to ask her father for a field; then, when that is not enough, she goes to Caleb herself, slides down off her donkey — a gesture of respect, or of urgency — and asks outright: you have given me the dry Negev; give me springs of water too. And he does — the upper springs and the lower. In a chapter that is otherwise a catalogue of men failing to take land, a woman successfully negotiates for the one thing that makes land worth having, and gets both halves of what she asks.

"Give me a blessing." Havah li verakhah — berakhah, "blessing," used here concretely of a gift, a favour granted, and specifically of well-watered land. It is the same word that runs through the patriarchal stories; here it is a daughter claiming her portion, and she is right to, and her father agrees.

Verses 16–18 · the Kenites, Hormah, and the coast

The Kenites come in with Israel, not against it. The "sons of the Kenite, Moses' father-in-law" — the family of Jethro/Hobab — travel up from the City of Palms (Jericho) and settle peacefully among Judah. ⚠️ It is a small, important counter-note in a chapter about clearing out Canaanites: a non-Israelite people is absorbed by welcome rather than the sword, remembered as kin because of Moses' marriage. The Kenites matter later — it is a Kenite woman, Jael, who kills Sisera in chapter 4.

"Put it to the ban… called Hormah." Vayacharimu — from cherem, the devoted-to-destruction, the hardest concept in the conquest: a thing so given over to God that it is destroyed rather than taken as plunder. The town's new name, Hormah, is built from the same root — "Destruction" — so the naming freezes the act into the map. ⚠️ The cherem is the sharpest ethical difficulty in these books and this translation neither softens it nor explains it away; it renders it as what it is and leaves the weight of it in the note.

⚠️ Verse 18 says Judah took Gaza, Ashkelon and Ekron — three of the great Philistine cities — and it is a genuine tension. Everywhere else, including verse 19 immediately after, those coastal cities stay firmly out of Israel's hands (the Philistines are the great unconquered enemy of the whole book and of Samuel). The KJV and NWT follow the Masoretic "took Gaza… Ashkelon… Ekron"; the Septuagint resolves the tension by reading the opposite — "Judah did not take Gaza…" — and many take that as the original. This translation prints the Masoretic text as it stands and flags the variant; it may record a temporary raid that did not hold, which would fit the chapter's whole theme, or it may be a textual slip. The reading is genuinely unsettled.

Verses 19–21 · "but he could not, because they had iron"

⚠️ Here is the theological knot of the chapter, stated in one verse. "Jehovah was with Judah, and he took the hill country; but he could not drive out the inhabitants of the valley, because they had chariots of iron." KJV "but could not drive out the inhabitants of the valley, because they had chariots of iron"; the NIV the same. Read the two halves together: God was with them, and they could not win, because of military hardware. ⚠️ The book does not resolve which it means — that God's presence has limits against iron (which the rest of scripture flatly denies; God routs chariots elsewhere without trouble), or, far more likely given where the book is going, that the failure was Israel's, not God's, and "iron chariots" is the excuse Israel told itself. The very next chapters make the second reading unavoidable: Judges 2 says plainly that the tribes did not drive out the nations because they disobeyed, not because they were outgunned. The "could not" of verse 19 is the beginning of a self-deception the book is documenting.

"To this day." The Jebusites are still in Jerusalem "to this day" (v. 21) — a narrator's aside written from a time when it was still true, i.e. before David. It is one of the small phrases that dates the writing from the inside, and it is honest bookkeeping: the conquest is on the record as unfinished.

Verses 22–26 · Bethel, and a bargain at the gate

An unsettling little episode, and the text knows it. The house of Joseph takes Bethel not by assault but by turning an informer: they catch a man leaving the city and promise to "deal kindly" with him — chesed, covenant-loyalty — if he shows them the way in. He does; they destroy the city; they keep their word to him and let his family go. ⚠️ It is a deliberate echo of Rahab at Jericho, and also its shadow: there a Canaanite risked her life to help Israel out of faith and was spared; here a Canaanite betrays his own city to save his skin and is spared. The word chesed sits uncomfortably over a transaction that is really just a deal with a traitor. And then the sting: the man goes off and founds another Luz in Hittite territory — the old Canaanite culture is not destroyed, it relocates and rebuilds. Nothing is fully finished here either.

Verses 27–33 · "did not drive out" — seven times

This is the engine of the chapter, and it is a drumbeat. Read the refrain down the tribes: Manasseh did not drive out (v. 27), Ephraim did not drive out (v. 29), Zebulun did not drive out (v. 30), Asher did not drive out (v. 31), Naphtali did not drive out (v. 33). ⚠️ The verb horish, "dispossess," tolls again and again, each time with a negative, and the chapter has quietly inverted itself: it opened with Judah's victories and ends with a litany of failures marching north up the map. The list is geographical — the further from Judah, the worse it gets — and it is building toward a verdict the next chapter will deliver.

"Forced labour," not conquest. Where the tribes are strong enough, they do not expel the Canaanites but put them to mas — corvée, forced labour (KJV "put the Canaanites to tribute"; the ESV "put the Canaanites to forced labor"), the same word used of what Egypt did to Israel and of what Solomon later did to the survivors. ⚠️ It is presented as strength ("when Israel grew strong") but it is the opposite of the command: they were told to drive out, and instead they kept and exploited. Slavery is the compromise they reach with disobedience, and the irony is unmissable — a people redeemed from forced labour imposing it. The whole book of Judges is about to show what living beside the people you were told to remove actually does to you.

"The Canaanite was determined to dwell there." Vayo'el — he was willing, resolved, undeterred. The verb quietly credits the Canaanites with the resolve Israel lacked: they meant to stay, and did.

Verses 34–36 · Dan, pushed off its own land

The bottom of the slide. The chapter began with a tribe (Judah) driving Canaanites out of the hill country; it ends with a tribe (Dan) being driven by the Amorites into the hill country and not even allowed down into its own valley. ⚠️ The reversal is complete and deliberate: the conqueror has become the confined. Dan's failure here is the seed of one of the book's darkest later stories — in Judges 18 the tribe gives up on its assigned land altogether and migrates north to seize a quiet, unsuspecting town instead. The last named place in the chapter, the Ascent of Scorpions, is a fittingly bleak note to end on: a boundary line marking how far the enemy's territory reached, in a chapter about how much of the land Israel never took.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "did not drive out" for horish negated, kept identical down the tribal list so the drumbeat is audible; "put it to the ban" / "Hormah" for cherem and its name, unsoftened; "forced labour" for mas, the same word as Egypt's slavery; "deal kindly" for chesed, left as covenant-loyalty even where it covers a deal with a traitor; "was determined to dwell" for vayo'el; and Gaza/Ashkelon/Ekron printed per the Masoretic text with the Septuagint's negative in the note.

Echoes paid. Joshua's own opening, "after the death of…," repeated over Joshua himself; the conquest of Joshua retold from underneath, as the part that did not get finished; Judah given the first place in Jacob's blessing, made good here at the head of the fighting; and Rahab's rescue at Jericho, shadowed by the informer at Bethel.

Echoes planted. Othniel, the first judge, slipped in as a bridegroom; the Kenites, whose kinship will matter when Jael drives a tent-peg through Sisera; the Jebusites in Jerusalem, waiting for David; Dan pushed off its land, on the road to Judges 18; and the whole refrain of "did not drive out," which Judges 2 will turn from a report into an indictment.

Next installment in this book: Judges 2 — the angel of Jehovah comes up from Gilgal to Bochim and reads the verdict this chapter has been building toward, the people weep, Joshua's generation dies, and "another generation arose after them who did not know Jehovah" — and the long, terrible cycle of the judges begins.

Ruth 1 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיְהִי, בִּימֵי שְׁפֹט הַשֹּׁפְטִים, וַיְהִי רָעָב, בָּאָרֶץ; וַיֵּלֶךְ אִישׁ מִבֵּית לֶחֶם יְהוּדָה, לָגוּר בִּשְׂדֵי מוֹאָב--הוּא וְאִשְׁתּוֹ, וּשְׁנֵי בָנָיו.
And it happened, in the days when the judges judged, that there was a famine in the land. And a man went from Bethlehem in Judah to sojourn in the fields of Moab — he and his wife and his two sons.note
2
וְשֵׁם הָאִישׁ אֱלִימֶלֶךְ וְשֵׁם אִשְׁתּוֹ נָעֳמִי וְשֵׁם שְׁנֵי-בָנָיו מַחְלוֹן וְכִלְיוֹן, אֶפְרָתִים--מִבֵּית לֶחֶם, יְהוּדָה; וַיָּבֹאוּ שְׂדֵי-מוֹאָב, וַיִּהְיוּ-שָׁם.
And the name of the man was Elimelech, and the name of his wife Naomi, and the names of his two sons Mahlon and Chilion, Ephrathites from Bethlehem in Judah. And they came to the fields of Moab and remained there.note
3
וַיָּמָת אֱלִימֶלֶךְ, אִישׁ נָעֳמִי; וַתִּשָּׁאֵר הִיא, וּשְׁנֵי בָנֶיהָ.
And Elimelech, Naomi's husband, died; and she was left, she and her two sons.note
4
וַיִּשְׂאוּ לָהֶם, נָשִׁים מֹאֲבִיּוֹת--שֵׁם הָאַחַת עָרְפָּה, וְשֵׁם הַשֵּׁנִית רוּת; וַיֵּשְׁבוּ שָׁם, כְּעֶשֶׂר שָׁנִים.
And they took for themselves Moabite wives — the name of the one was Orpah, and the name of the second Ruth — and they lived there about ten years.note
5
וַיָּמֻתוּ גַם-שְׁנֵיהֶם, מַחְלוֹן וְכִלְיוֹן; וַתִּשָּׁאֵר, הָאִשָּׁה, מִשְּׁנֵי יְלָדֶיהָ, וּמֵאִישָׁהּ.
And the two of them also died, Mahlon and Chilion; and the woman was left, bereft of her two children and of her husband.note
6
וַתָּקָם הִיא וְכַלֹּתֶיהָ, וַתָּשָׁב מִשְּׂדֵי מוֹאָב: כִּי שָׁמְעָה, בִּשְׂדֵה מוֹאָב--כִּי-פָקַד יְהוָה אֶת-עַמּוֹ, לָתֵת לָהֶם לָחֶם.
And she arose, she and her daughters-in-law, and returned from the fields of Moab, for she had heard in the field of Moab that Jehovah had visited his people, to give them bread.note
7
וַתֵּצֵא, מִן-הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר הָיְתָה-שָּׁמָּה, וּשְׁתֵּי כַלּוֹתֶיהָ, עִמָּהּ; וַתֵּלַכְנָה בַדֶּרֶךְ, לָשׁוּב אֶל-אֶרֶץ יְהוּדָה.
And she went out from the place where she had been, and her two daughters-in-law with her; and they went on the road, to return to the land of Judah.note
8
וַתֹּאמֶר נָעֳמִי, לִשְׁתֵּי כַלֹּתֶיהָ, לֵכְנָה שֹּׁבְנָה, אִשָּׁה לְבֵית אִמָּהּ; יעשה יַעַשׂ יְהוָה עִמָּכֶם חֶסֶד, כַּאֲשֶׁר עֲשִׂיתֶם עִם-הַמֵּתִים וְעִמָּדִי.
And Naomi said to her two daughters-in-law, "Go, return each of you to her mother’s house. May Jehovah deal kindly with you, as you have dealt with the dead and with me.note
9
יִתֵּן יְהוָה, לָכֶם, וּמְצֶאןָ מְנוּחָה, אִשָּׁה בֵּית אִישָׁהּ; וַתִּשַּׁק לָהֶן, וַתִּשֶּׂאנָה קוֹלָן וַתִּבְכֶּינָה.
May Jehovah grant you that you find rest, each in the house of her husband." And she kissed them, and they lifted up their voice and wept.note
10
וַתֹּאמַרְנָה-לָּהּ: כִּי-אִתָּךְ נָשׁוּב, לְעַמֵּךְ.
And they said to her, "No — with you we will return to your people."note
11
וַתֹּאמֶר נָעֳמִי שֹׁבְנָה בְנֹתַי, לָמָּה תֵלַכְנָה עִמִּי: הַעוֹד-לִי בָנִים בְּמֵעַי, וְהָיוּ לָכֶם לַאֲנָשִׁים.
And Naomi said, "Turn back, my daughters. Why should you go with me? Have I yet sons in my womb, that they could become husbands for you?note
12
שֹׁבְנָה בְנֹתַי לֵכְןָ, כִּי זָקַנְתִּי מִהְיוֹת לְאִישׁ: כִּי אָמַרְתִּי, יֶשׁ-לִי תִקְוָה--גַּם הָיִיתִי הַלַּיְלָה לְאִישׁ, וְגַם יָלַדְתִּי בָנִים.
Turn back, my daughters, go — for I am too old to belong to a husband. Even if I said I had hope — even if I were to belong to a husband tonight, and even bore sons —note
13
הֲלָהֵן תְּשַׂבֵּרְנָה, עַד אֲשֶׁר יִגְדָּלוּ, הֲלָהֵן תֵּעָגֵנָה, לְבִלְתִּי הֱיוֹת לְאִישׁ; אַל בְּנֹתַי, כִּי-מַר-לִי מְאֹד מִכֶּם--כִּי-יָצְאָה בִי, יַד-יְהוָה.
would you wait for them until they were grown? Would you shut yourselves off for them and have no husband? No, my daughters. It is far more bitter for me than for you, for the hand of Jehovah has gone out against me."note
14
וַתִּשֶּׂנָה קוֹלָן, וַתִּבְכֶּינָה עוֹד; וַתִּשַּׁק עָרְפָּה לַחֲמוֹתָהּ, וְרוּת דָּבְקָה בָּהּ.
And they lifted up their voice and wept again. And Orpah kissed her mother-in-law, but Ruth clung to her.note
15
וַתֹּאמֶר, הִנֵּה שָׁבָה יְבִמְתֵּךְ, אֶל-עַמָּהּ, וְאֶל-אֱלֹהֶיהָ; שׁוּבִי, אַחֲרֵי יְבִמְתֵּךְ.
And she said, "Look, your sister-in-law has turned back to her people and to her gods. Turn back after your sister-in-law."note
16
וַתֹּאמֶר רוּת אַל-תִּפְגְּעִי-בִי, לְעָזְבֵךְ לָשׁוּב מֵאַחֲרָיִךְ: כִּי אֶל-אֲשֶׁר תֵּלְכִי אֵלֵךְ, וּבַאֲשֶׁר תָּלִינִי אָלִין--עַמֵּךְ עַמִּי, וֵאלֹהַיִךְ אֱלֹהָי.
But Ruth said, "Do not press me to leave you, to turn back from following you. For where you go I will go, and where you lodge I will lodge; your people are my people, and your God my God.note
17
בַּאֲשֶׁר תָּמוּתִי אָמוּת, וְשָׁם אֶקָּבֵר; כֹּה יַעֲשֶׂה יְהוָה לִי, וְכֹה יוֹסִיף--כִּי הַמָּוֶת, יַפְרִיד בֵּינִי וּבֵינֵךְ.
Where you die I will die, and there I will be buried. May Jehovah do so to me, and more also, if anything but death parts me from you."note
18
וַתֵּרֶא, כִּי-מִתְאַמֶּצֶת הִיא לָלֶכֶת אִתָּהּ; וַתֶּחְדַּל, לְדַבֵּר אֵלֶיהָ.
And when she saw that she was determined to go with her, she stopped speaking to her.note
19
וַתֵּלַכְנָה שְׁתֵּיהֶם, עַד-בּוֹאָנָה בֵּית לָחֶם; וַיְהִי, כְּבוֹאָנָה בֵּית לֶחֶם, וַתֵּהֹם כָּל-הָעִיר עֲלֵיהֶן, וַתֹּאמַרְנָה הֲזֹאת נָעֳמִי.
And the two of them went until they came to Bethlehem. And it happened, when they came to Bethlehem, that all the town was stirred over them, and the women said, "Is this Naomi?"note
20
וַתֹּאמֶר אֲלֵיהֶן, אַל-תִּקְרֶאנָה לִי נָעֳמִי: קְרֶאןָ לִי מָרָא, כִּי-הֵמַר שַׁדַּי לִי מְאֹד.
And she said to them, "Do not call me Naomi — call me Mara, for the Almighty has dealt very bitterly with me.note
21
אֲנִי מְלֵאָה הָלַכְתִּי, וְרֵיקָם הֱשִׁיבַנִי יְהוָה; לָמָּה תִקְרֶאנָה לִי, נָעֳמִי, וַיהוָה עָנָה בִי, וְשַׁדַּי הֵרַע לִי.
I went out full, and Jehovah has brought me back empty. Why call me Naomi, when Jehovah has testified against me, and the Almighty has brought evil upon me?"note
22
וַתָּשָׁב נָעֳמִי, וְרוּת הַמּוֹאֲבִיָּה כַלָּתָהּ עִמָּהּ, הַשָּׁבָה, מִשְּׂדֵי מוֹאָב; וְהֵמָּה, בָּאוּ בֵּית לֶחֶם, בִּתְחִלַּת, קְצִיר שְׂעֹרִים.
So Naomi returned, and Ruth the Moabite, her daughter-in-law, with her, who returned from the fields of Moab; and they came to Bethlehem at the beginning of the barley harvest.note

Translator’s Notes

Verses 1–5 · a story told against the Judges

«In the days when the judges judged.» The opening line pins the book to the era of the Judges — and Ruth is its deliberate counter-story. Where Judges spirals down into idolatry, civil war, and a Levite’s concubine cut into twelve pieces, its refrain “everyone did what was right in his own eyes,” Ruth tells one small, quiet story of chesed (loyal love) among ordinary people in that same dark time. It is the book that says the covenant survived not in the judges’ battles but in a widow, a foreigner, and a field. The Hebrew is literally “in the days of the judging of the judges,” a play on the root the English can only approximate. KJV and ASV “when the judges ruled.”

A famine in the House of Bread. The town’s name is Beth-lechem, “House of Bread” — and it has no bread. A man of the House of Bread leaves the land of promise to sojourn in Moab, the country east of the Dead Sea whose people descend from Lot’s daughters (Genesis 19:37) and who once hired Balaam to curse Israel (Numbers 22). The narrator states the move without a verdict; later readers hear a failure of faith in it (the leading family abandoning its starving town), but the text keeps its own counsel. It simply notes that the House of Bread emptied, and a family went hungry into old enemy ground.

⚠ Names as omens. Hebrew narrative loves a loaded name. Elimelech is “My God is King” (a heavy name for a man who dies in exile); Naomi is “my pleasant one, sweetness”; and the two sons — Mahlon and Chilion — carry roots that sound like “sickness / weakness” (chalah) and “wasting, coming to an end” (kalah). The etymologies are uncertain, likely folk-etymological, but the storyteller lets them ring: the sweet woman and her two failing sons. Within five verses all three men are dead and “the woman was left” — the verb of a bare survivor, sounded twice (vv3, 5), stripping her of husband and then of children until only she remains.

Verses 6–9 · the road home, and a prayer of chesed

«Jehovah had visited his people.» The turn comes on a rumour: paqad, the visitation verb — the same word used when Jehovah “visited” Sarah and gave her Isaac (Genesis 21:1), and would “visit” his people in Egypt (Exodus 3:16). He has visited to give bread; the House of Bread is filling again, and Naomi turns for home.

«May Jehovah deal kindly with you.» Here is the book’s key word, and it falls first from Naomi’s mouth over her two Gentile daughters-in-law: chesed — loyal, covenant love, kindness that keeps faith past all obligation. It runs the length of the book (1:8; 2:20; 3:10), Jehovah’s chesed and human chesed answering each other. Naomi has nothing left to give these women, so she gives them a blessing and lets them go, praying that the God of Israel will show them the loyalty they showed her dead sons. KJV and ESV “deal kindly”; the NWT “loyal love.”

«That you may find rest.» Menuchah — rest, a settled security; verse 9 spells out what she means, “in the house of her husband.” It is the one thing Naomi cannot give, and the very thing the whole book will circle back to place in Ruth’s hands at the end.

Verses 10–13 · Naomi’s argument, and the hand of the LORD

The levirate math. Naomi’s reply is built on the custom of levirate marriage — a dead man’s brother takes his widow to raise up an heir in his name (later codified in Deuteronomy 25:5–10, and the engine of the whole book’s ending). She presses it to the point of absurdity: even if she married tonight and bore sons, would these young women wait years for infants to grow up? She has no more sons to give, so no claim binds them to her. It is a self-emptying argument — a mother-in-law reasoning her own daughters-in-law free of every duty.

«The hand of Jehovah has gone out against me.» Naomi names God as the author of her grief without flinching — a note she will sound again at Mara. This is not the language of doubt but of an Israelite who assumes Jehovah’s hand in everything, the sweet and the bitter alike. KJV “the hand of the LORD is gone out against me.”

Verses 14–18 · Orpah’s kiss, Ruth’s clinging — and the great oath

Two verbs, two destinies. Orpah kisses her mother-in-law and turns back; Ruth clings to her. The clinging verb is dabaq — the very word of Genesis 2:24, where a man “clings” to his wife and the two become one flesh. Ruth’s loyalty to Naomi is written in marriage language: she cleaves to her as a spouse cleaves. ⚠ Orpah is not blamed — Naomi urged her to go, and she went home to her people and her gods, the reasonable choice. The book sets the reasonable choice beside the extravagant one and lets you feel the whole distance between them.

«Your people my people, your God my God.» The most famous words in the book, and one of the great confessions in the Bible — spoken by a Moabite. Ruth binds herself to Naomi’s road, lodging, people, God, death, and grave, and seals it with a self-imprecation using the covenant name: “May Jehovah do so to me, and more also” — the oath-formula of an Israelite, sworn by Jehovah by a woman of Moab. ⚠ This cuts hard against Deuteronomy 23:3, “no Moabite shall enter the assembly of Jehovah, even to the tenth generation.” The book’s whole quiet argument is here: covenant belonging is won by chesed and faith, not by blood — and this Moabite convert becomes the great-grandmother of David (4:17) and, in Matthew’s genealogy, an ancestress of Jesus (Matthew 1:5). Many read Ruth as written or kept as a gentle protest against the expulsion of foreign wives under Ezra and Nehemiah; the dating is debated, the argument unmistakable. KJV “whither thou goest, I will go… thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God.”

«She stopped speaking to her.» Naomi’s silence is defeat and consent at once. There is nothing to say to a loyalty that will not be argued down.

Verses 19–22 · Mara — full, and empty

«Is this Naomi?» The whole town is stirred (a strong verb, the ground fairly humming); the women barely know the one who left. And Naomi answers with a pun on her own name: do not call me Naomi (“sweet”) — call me Mara (“bitter”), for Shaddai, the Almighty, has dealt bitterly (hemar) with me. In her grief she reaches past the covenant name to the old patriarchal name of God, El Shaddai (Genesis 17:1), the God of Abraham’s hard promises. KJV and the NWT “the Almighty.”

«I went out full, and Jehovah has brought me back empty.» Her theology is honest and, the narrator gently shows, not quite true. She calls herself empty — but Ruth is standing right beside her, and the very next verse names her again: “and Ruth the Moabite, her daughter-in-law, with her.” Naomi cannot yet see that the emptiness she mourns is holding the redemption of the whole book.

«At the beginning of the barley harvest.» The last line turns the key. The famine that opened the book is over; the fields around the House of Bread stand white for harvest; and into a barley field, next chapter, will walk a man named Boaz. The emptying is finished; the filling is about to begin.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the exact / distinct words: “in the days when the judges judged” keeping the root-play that ties the book to Judges; “the woman was left” for the survivor’s verb sounded twice (vv3, 5); “deal kindly” / “loyal love” for chesed, the book’s theme word, kept the same wherever it recurs; “rest” for menuchah, the settled security of a home; “clung to her” for dabaq, the Genesis-2:24 marriage verb; “Mara” kept untranslated so the hemar pun on “bitter” can land; and “the Almighty” for Shaddai, the old patriarchal name Naomi reaches for.

Echoes paid. The famine that empties the House of Bread; paqad — Jehovah “visited” his people to give bread, the verb of Sarah (Genesis 21:1) and the Exodus (Exodus 3:16); Moab traced back to Lot’s daughters (Genesis 19:37) and Balaam (Numbers 22); the levirate custom (Deuteronomy 25:5–10) argued to absurdity by Naomi and fulfilled by Boaz four chapters on; and Deuteronomy 23:3’s ban on Moabites set against a Moabite who out-loves Israel.

Echoes planted. Chesed, sounded first here and again at 2:20 and 3:10; rest (menuchah), the thing Naomi cannot give and Boaz will; the barley harvest, the field Ruth walks into next chapter; the emptiness Naomi mourns while the redemption of the book stands beside her; and Ruth the Moabite herself, on the road to becoming the great-grandmother of David (4:17) and an ancestress of Jesus (Matthew 1:5).

Next in this book: Ruth 2 — Ruth goes out to glean and “happens” upon the field of Boaz, a man of standing and a kinsman of Elimelech; his kindness to a foreign widow, the first stirring of chesed answered, and Naomi’s first flicker of hope: “he is one of our redeemers.”

1 Samuel 1 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיְהִי אִישׁ אֶחָד מִן-הָרָמָתַיִם, צוֹפִים--מֵהַר אֶפְרָיִם; וּשְׁמוֹ אֶלְקָנָה בֶּן-יְרֹחָם בֶּן-אֱלִיהוּא, בֶּן-תֹּחוּ בֶן-צוּף--אֶפְרָתִי.
Now there was a certain man from Ramathaim-zophim, from the hill country of Ephraim, and his name was Elkanah son of Jeroham, son of Elihu, son of Tohu, son of Zuph, an Ephraimite.note
2
וְלוֹ, שְׁתֵּי נָשִׁים--שֵׁם אַחַת חַנָּה, וְשֵׁם הַשֵּׁנִית פְּנִנָּה; וַיְהִי לִפְנִנָּה יְלָדִים, וּלְחַנָּה אֵין יְלָדִים.
And he had two wives: the name of the one was Hannah, and the name of the other Peninnah. And Peninnah had children, but Hannah had no children.note
3
וְעָלָה הָאִישׁ הַהוּא מֵעִירוֹ מִיָּמִים יָמִימָה, לְהִשְׁתַּחֲוֺת וְלִזְבֹּחַ לַיהוָה צְבָאוֹת בְּשִׁלֹה; וְשָׁם שְׁנֵי בְנֵי-עֵלִי, חָפְנִי וּפִנְחָס, כֹּהֲנִים, לַיהוָה.
And this man went up from his city year by year to worship and to sacrifice to Jehovah of hosts at Shiloh. And there the two sons of Eli, Hophni and Phinehas, were priests to Jehovah.note
4
וַיְהִי הַיּוֹם, וַיִּזְבַּח אֶלְקָנָה; וְנָתַן לִפְנִנָּה אִשְׁתּוֹ, וּלְכָל-בָּנֶיהָ וּבְנוֹתֶיהָ--מָנוֹת.
And on the day that Elkanah sacrificed, he would give portions to Peninnah his wife and to all her sons and daughters;note
5
וּלְחַנָּה, יִתֵּן מָנָה אַחַת אַפָּיִם: כִּי אֶת-חַנָּה אָהֵב, וַיהוָה סָגַר רַחְמָהּ.
but to Hannah he would give a double portion, for he loved Hannah, though Jehovah had closed her womb.note
6
וְכִעֲסַתָּה צָרָתָהּ גַּם-כַּעַס, בַּעֲבוּר הַרְּעִמָהּ: כִּי-סָגַר יְהוָה, בְּעַד רַחְמָהּ.
And her rival provoked her grievously, to irritate her, because Jehovah had closed her womb.note
7
וְכֵן יַעֲשֶׂה שָׁנָה בְשָׁנָה, מִדֵּי עֲלֹתָהּ בְּבֵית יְהוָה--כֵּן, תַּכְעִסֶנָּה; וַתִּבְכֶּה, וְלֹא תֹאכַל.
So it went on year by year: as often as she went up to the house of Jehovah, so her rival would provoke her; and she wept and would not eat.note
8
וַיֹּאמֶר לָהּ אֶלְקָנָה אִישָׁהּ, חַנָּה לָמֶה תִבְכִּי וְלָמֶה לֹא תֹאכְלִי, וְלָמֶה, יֵרַע לְבָבֵךְ: הֲלוֹא אָנֹכִי טוֹב לָךְ, מֵעֲשָׂרָה בָּנִים.
And Elkanah her husband said to her, "Hannah, why do you weep? And why do you not eat? And why is your heart grieved? Am I not better to you than ten sons?"note
9
וַתָּקָם חַנָּה, אַחֲרֵי אָכְלָה בְשִׁלֹה וְאַחֲרֵי שָׁתֹה; וְעֵלִי הַכֹּהֵן, יֹשֵׁב עַל-הַכִּסֵּא, עַל-מְזוּזַת, הֵיכַל יְהוָה.
And Hannah rose after they had eaten in Shiloh, and after drinking; and Eli the priest was sitting on the seat by the doorpost of the temple of Jehovah.note
10
וְהִיא, מָרַת נָפֶשׁ; וַתִּתְפַּלֵּל עַל-יְהוָה, וּבָכֹה תִבְכֶּה.
And she was bitter in soul, and she prayed to Jehovah and wept bitterly.note
11
וַתִּדֹּר נֶדֶר וַתֹּאמַר, יְהוָה צְבָאוֹת אִם-רָאֹה תִרְאֶה בָּעֳנִי אֲמָתֶךָ וּזְכַרְתַּנִי וְלֹא-תִשְׁכַּח אֶת-אֲמָתֶךָ, וְנָתַתָּה לַאֲמָתְךָ, זֶרַע אֲנָשִׁים--וּנְתַתִּיו לַיהוָה כָּל-יְמֵי חַיָּיו, וּמוֹרָה לֹא-יַעֲלֶה עַל-רֹאשׁוֹ.
And she vowed a vow and said, "O Jehovah of hosts, if you will indeed look on the affliction of your servant and remember me, and not forget your servant, but give your servant a male child, then I will give him to Jehovah all the days of his life, and no razor shall touch his head."note
12
וְהָיָה כִּי הִרְבְּתָה, לְהִתְפַּלֵּל לִפְנֵי יְהוָה; וְעֵלִי, שֹׁמֵר אֶת-פִּיהָ.
And as she continued praying before Jehovah, Eli watched her mouth.note
13
וְחַנָּה, הִיא מְדַבֶּרֶת עַל-לִבָּהּ--רַק שְׂפָתֶיהָ נָּעוֹת, וְקוֹלָהּ לֹא יִשָּׁמֵעַ; וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ עֵלִי, לְשִׁכֹּרָה.
Now Hannah was speaking in her heart; only her lips moved, and her voice was not heard; so Eli thought she was drunk.note
14
וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלֶיהָ עֵלִי, עַד-מָתַי תִּשְׁתַּכָּרִין; הָסִירִי אֶת-יֵינֵךְ, מֵעָלָיִךְ.
And Eli said to her, "How long will you be drunk? Put away your wine from you."note
15
וַתַּעַן חַנָּה וַתֹּאמֶר, לֹא אֲדֹנִי, אִשָּׁה קְשַׁת-רוּחַ אָנֹכִי, וְיַיִן וְשֵׁכָר לֹא שָׁתִיתִי; וָאֶשְׁפֹּךְ אֶת-נַפְשִׁי, לִפְנֵי יְהוָה.
But Hannah answered and said, "No, my lord; I am a woman troubled in spirit. I have drunk no wine or strong drink, but I have poured out my soul before Jehovah.note
16
אַל-תִּתֵּן, אֶת-אֲמָתְךָ, לִפְנֵי, בַּת-בְּלִיָּעַל: כִּי-מֵרֹב שִׂיחִי וְכַעְסִי, דִּבַּרְתִּי עַד-הֵנָּה.
Do not regard your servant as a worthless woman, for out of my great anxiety and vexation I have spoken until now."note
17
וַיַּעַן עֵלִי וַיֹּאמֶר, לְכִי לְשָׁלוֹם; וֵאלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, יִתֵּן אֶת-שֵׁלָתֵךְ, אֲשֶׁר שָׁאַלְתְּ, מֵעִמּוֹ.
Then Eli answered and said, "Go in peace, and the God of Israel grant your petition that you have asked of him."note
18
וַתֹּאמֶר, תִּמְצָא שִׁפְחָתְךָ חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ; וַתֵּלֶךְ הָאִשָּׁה לְדַרְכָּהּ וַתֹּאכַל, וּפָנֶיהָ לֹא-הָיוּ-לָהּ עוֹד.
And she said, "Let your servant find favor in your eyes." So the woman went her way and ate, and her face was no longer sad.note
19
וַיַּשְׁכִּמוּ בַבֹּקֶר, וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ לִפְנֵי יְהוָה, וַיָּשֻׁבוּ וַיָּבֹאוּ אֶל-בֵּיתָם, הָרָמָתָה; וַיֵּדַע אֶלְקָנָה אֶת-חַנָּה אִשְׁתּוֹ, וַיִּזְכְּרֶהָ יְהוָה.
And they rose early in the morning and worshiped before Jehovah; then they returned and came to their house at Ramah. And Elkanah knew Hannah his wife, and Jehovah remembered her.note
20
וַיְהִי לִתְקֻפוֹת הַיָּמִים, וַתַּהַר חַנָּה וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן; וַתִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמוֹ שְׁמוּאֵל, כִּי מֵיְהוָה שְׁאִלְתִּיו.
And in due time Hannah conceived and bore a son, and she called his name Samuel: "because I have asked him of Jehovah."note
21
וַיַּעַל הָאִישׁ אֶלְקָנָה, וְכָל-בֵּיתוֹ, לִזְבֹּחַ לַיהוָה אֶת-זֶבַח הַיָּמִים, וְאֶת-נִדְרוֹ.
And the man Elkanah went up with all his household to offer to Jehovah the yearly sacrifice and his vow.note
22
וְחַנָּה, לֹא עָלָתָה: כִּי-אָמְרָה לְאִישָׁהּ, עַד יִגָּמֵל הַנַּעַר וַהֲבִאֹתִיו וְנִרְאָה אֶת-פְּנֵי יְהוָה, וְיָשַׁב שָׁם, עַד-עוֹלָם.
But Hannah did not go up, for she said to her husband, "Not until the child is weaned; then I will bring him, that he may appear before Jehovah and remain there forever."note
23
וַיֹּאמֶר לָהּ אֶלְקָנָה אִישָׁהּ עֲשִׂי הַטּוֹב בְּעֵינַיִךְ, שְׁבִי עַד-גָּמְלֵךְ אֹתוֹ--אַךְ יָקֵם יְהוָה, אֶת-דְּבָרוֹ; וַתֵּשֶׁב הָאִשָּׁה וַתֵּינֶק אֶת-בְּנָהּ, עַד-גָּמְלָהּ אֹתוֹ.
And Elkanah her husband said to her, "Do what seems good in your eyes; wait until you have weaned him; only may Jehovah establish his word." So the woman remained and nursed her son until she weaned him.note
24
וַתַּעֲלֵהוּ עִמָּהּ כַּאֲשֶׁר גְּמָלַתּוּ, בְּפָרִים שְׁלֹשָׁה וְאֵיפָה אַחַת קֶמַח וְנֵבֶל יַיִן, וַתְּבִאֵהוּ בֵית-יְהוָה, שִׁלוֹ; וְהַנַּעַר, נָעַר.
And when she had weaned him, she took him up with her, along with three bulls, one ephah of flour, and a skin of wine, and she brought him to the house of Jehovah at Shiloh; and the child was young.note
25
וַיִּשְׁחֲטוּ, אֶת-הַפָּר; וַיָּבִאוּ אֶת-הַנַּעַר, אֶל-עֵלִי.
And they slaughtered the bull, and they brought the child to Eli.note
26
