וַתֹּאמֶר בִּי אֲדֹנִי, חֵי נַפְשְׁךָ אֲדֹנִי; אֲנִי הָאִשָּׁה, הַנִּצֶּבֶת עִמְּכָה בָּזֶה, לְהִתְפַּלֵּל, אֶל-יְהוָה.
And she said, "Oh, my lord! As your soul lives, my lord, I am the woman who stood beside you here, praying to Jehovah.note
27
אֶל-הַנַּעַר הַזֶּה, הִתְפַּלָּלְתִּי; וַיִּתֵּן יְהוָה לִי אֶת-שְׁאֵלָתִי, אֲשֶׁר שָׁאַלְתִּי מֵעִמּוֹ.
For this child I prayed, and Jehovah has granted me my petition that I asked of him.note
28
וְגַם אָנֹכִי, הִשְׁאִלְתִּהוּ לַיהוָה, כָּל-הַיָּמִים אֲשֶׁר הָיָה, הוּא שָׁאוּל לַיהוָה; וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ שָׁם, לַיהוָה. {ס}
Therefore I have lent him to Jehovah; as long as he lives, he is lent to Jehovah." And he worshiped Jehovah there.note

Translator’s Notes

Verses 1–8 · a barren woman, and a rival’s cruelty

1 Samuel opens on a barren woman. The book that will give Israel its first kings begins, as the old stories so often do, at a closed womb. Hannah joins Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, and the mother of Samson — the wombs God shuts and then opens are the hinges of Israel’s history, and here the child will be the prophet who anoints the kings. “Jehovah had closed her womb” (v5) — the narrator says it twice (vv5, 6), plainly; it is God’s doing, and it is grief.

The pain is domestic and sharp. Elkanah loves Hannah and gives her a double portion, but love does not fill a cradle; and Peninnah, her rival (literally “her adversary,” tsarah), “provoked her grievously,” year after year, until Hannah wept and would not eat. Elkanah’s tender, clumsy question — “am I not better to you than ten sons?” — is kind, and misses the point entirely.

⚠ «Jehovah of hosts» (v3). This is the FIRST appearance in the Bible of the title YHWH Tzeva’ot — “Jehovah of armies/hosts” — the Name that will ring through the prophets and the Psalms. It enters, fittingly, at Shiloh, the sanctuary that housed the ark before Jerusalem, on the lips of a family come to worship. KJV “the LORD of hosts”; the ASV “Jehovah of hosts.”

Verses 9–11 · a vow poured out

Hannah rises “bitter in soul” (marat nefesh — the same bitterness Naomi named over herself, Ruth 1:20) and prays. Her vow (neder, v11) is a Nazirite dedication: if God gives her a son, she will give him back “all the days of his life, and no razor shall touch his head” — the lifelong vow of Samson, and now of Samuel (Numbers 6). She asks God to “look on the affliction of your servant and remember me” — the language of the covenant God who “remembered” Rachel and would “remember” his people in Egypt. KJV “if thou wilt indeed look on the affliction of thine handmaid.”

She prays at Shiloh, where Eli the priest sits “by the doorpost of the temple of Jehovah” — the Shiloh sanctuary that stood in Ephraim through the whole period of the Judges (Joshua 18:1), before it fell and the ark was captured (ch 4).

Verses 12–18 · mistaken for a drunk

One of the tenderest and most quietly damning scenes in the Old Testament. Hannah prays as few in the Bible do — silently, “in her heart; only her lips moved, and her voice was not heard” — a model of prayer that is between the soul and God alone. And Eli, the priest, the man whose whole office is to discern before God, looks at a praying woman and sees a drunk. It is the first hint of what the next chapters will make plain: the priesthood at Shiloh has grown blind.

Hannah’s dignified answer — “I have poured out my soul before Jehovah… do not regard your servant as a worthless woman” (bat-beliya’al, a ‘daughter of worthlessness’) — corrects the priest without insulting him; and Eli, to his credit, blesses her. She goes away and eats, and “her face was no longer sad” — she has left the grief at the altar before the child is even conceived. Faith, here, is a change of face before it is an answered prayer.

Verses 19–20 · Jehovah remembered her — and the name

«Jehovah remembered her» (v19) — the covenant verb zakar, the same word that turns the story at Rachel (Genesis 30:22) and at the Exodus (2:24); when God “remembers,” a rescue follows. Hannah conceives and bears a son, and names him Samuel (Shmuel), “because I have asked him of Jehovah.”

⚠ The explanation is a wordplay on sha’al, “to ask” — though the name “Samuel” more likely means “name of God” or “God has heard”; the narrator gives the folk-etymology that fits the story. And the play runs deeper than one name: sha’al (“asked, lent”) is the very root of SAUL (Sha’ul, “the asked-for one”) — so the woman who “asked” for Samuel names, in the same breath, the theme of the whole book: a people who will “ask” for a king. KJV “she called his name Samuel, saying, Because I have asked him of the LORD.”

Verses 21–28 · lent to Jehovah

Hannah keeps the boy until he is weaned (a few years, in that world), then makes the hardest journey of her life: she brings her only, longed-for son to Shiloh and gives him away. The chapter ends on the sha’al wordplay pressed to its limit: “I have LENT him (hishiltihu) to Jehovah; as long as he lives, he is LENT (sha’ul) to Jehovah” — the asked-for child is the given-back child. Her faith is not that she keeps what she prayed for, but that she returns it.

⚠ A textual note (v24): “three bulls” follows the Masoretic Hebrew; the Greek Septuagint and a Dead Sea Scroll read “a three-year-old bull” — one letter’s difference (the singular bull of v25 fits the latter). It is a small first taste of the fact that Samuel’s Hebrew text is one of the most damaged in the Old Testament, where the Scrolls and the Greek often preserve a better reading.

And the song comes next. The chapter that gives Israel a prophet ends with a mother’s empty arms — and the hymn Hannah sings over them (2:1-10), of the God who “raises the poor from the dust” and “brings low and lifts up,” becomes centuries later the template for Mary’s Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55). The barren woman’s answered prayer turns into the Bible’s great song of reversal.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the exact / distinct words: “Jehovah of hosts” for the Name’s first appearance as YHWH Tzeva’ot; “asked / lent” for sha’al, kept the same across the naming of Samuel and the giving of him back (and left to ring toward SAUL); “vowed a vow” for the doubled neder; “no razor shall touch his head” for the Nazirite dedication; “bitter in soul” for marat nefesh, held against Naomi’s Mara; and “remembered” for zakar, the covenant verb of rescue.

Echoes paid. The barren-womb line of Sarah, Rebekah, and Rachel; “Jehovah remembered her,” the verb that opened Rachel’s womb (Genesis 30:22); Naomi’s bitterness (Ruth 1:20); and the Nazirite vow of Samson (Numbers 6), now laid on Samuel from the womb.

Echoes planted. Eli and his sons Hophni and Phinehas, the failing priesthood whose fall fills the next chapters; Shiloh, the sanctuary that will be lost with the ark (ch 4); the sha’al-play that reaches forward to Saul, the asked-for king; and, above all, Hannah’s song in the next chapter — “he raises the poor from the dust” — the model on which Mary will sing her Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55).

1 Samuel is a new book on the site. Next in sequence: 1 Samuel 2 — Hannah’s song of the God who kills and makes alive, brings low and lifts up; and, against it, the wickedness of Eli’s sons and the boy Samuel “growing in favor with Jehovah and with men.”

Genesis 30 — The Mister Translation

1
וַתֵּרֶא רָחֵל, כִּי לֹא יָלְדָה לְיַעֲקֹב, וַתְּקַנֵּא רָחֵל, בַּאֲחֹתָהּ; וַתֹּאמֶר אֶל-יַעֲקֹב הָבָה-לִּי בָנִים, וְאִם-אַיִן מֵתָה אָנֹכִי.
When Rachel saw that she bore Jacob no children, Rachel envied her sister, and she said to Jacob, "Give me children, or else I die!"note
2
וַיִּחַר-אַף יַעֲקֹב, בְּרָחֵל; וַיֹּאמֶר, הֲתַחַת אֱלֹהִים אָנֹכִי, אֲשֶׁר-מָנַע מִמֵּךְ, פְּרִי-בָטֶן.
And Jacob's anger burned against Rachel, and he said, "Am I in God's place, who has withheld from you the fruit of the womb?"note
3
וַתֹּאמֶר, הִנֵּה אֲמָתִי בִלְהָה בֹּא אֵלֶיהָ; וְתֵלֵד, עַל-בִּרְכַּי, וְאִבָּנֶה גַם-אָנֹכִי, מִמֶּנָּה.
And she said, "Here is my maidservant Bilhah; go in to her, that she may bear on my knees, and I too may be built up through her."note
4
וַתִּתֶּן-לוֹ אֶת-בִּלְהָה שִׁפְחָתָהּ, לְאִשָּׁה; וַיָּבֹא אֵלֶיהָ, יַעֲקֹב.
And she gave him Bilhah her maidservant as a wife, and Jacob went in to her.note
5
וַתַּהַר בִּלְהָה, וַתֵּלֶד לְיַעֲקֹב בֵּן.
And Bilhah conceived and bore Jacob a son.note
6
וַתֹּאמֶר רָחֵל, דָּנַנִּי אֱלֹהִים, וְגַם שָׁמַע בְּקֹלִי, וַיִּתֶּן-לִי בֵּן; עַל-כֵּן קָרְאָה שְׁמוֹ, דָּן.
And Rachel said, "God has judged me, and has also heard my voice and given me a son"; therefore she named him Dan.note
7
וַתַּהַר עוֹד--וַתֵּלֶד, בִּלְהָה שִׁפְחַת רָחֵל: בֵּן שֵׁנִי, לְיַעֲקֹב.
And Bilhah, Rachel's maidservant, conceived again and bore Jacob a second son.note
8
וַתֹּאמֶר רָחֵל, נַפְתּוּלֵי אֱלֹהִים נִפְתַּלְתִּי עִם-אֲחֹתִי--גַּם-יָכֹלְתִּי; וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ, נַפְתָּלִי.
And Rachel said, "With wrestlings of God I have wrestled with my sister, and I have prevailed"; and she named him Naphtali.note
9
וַתֵּרֶא לֵאָה, כִּי עָמְדָה מִלֶּדֶת; וַתִּקַּח אֶת-זִלְפָּה שִׁפְחָתָהּ, וַתִּתֵּן אֹתָהּ לְיַעֲקֹב לְאִשָּׁה.
When Leah saw that she had stopped bearing, she took Zilpah her maidservant and gave her to Jacob as a wife.note
10
וַתֵּלֶד, זִלְפָּה שִׁפְחַת לֵאָה--לְיַעֲקֹב בֵּן.
And Zilpah, Leah's maidservant, bore Jacob a son.note
11
וַתֹּאמֶר לֵאָה, בָּא גָד; וַתִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמוֹ, גָּד.
And Leah said, "Fortune has come!" and she named him Gad.note
12
וַתֵּלֶד, זִלְפָּה שִׁפְחַת לֵאָה, בֵּן שֵׁנִי, לְיַעֲקֹב.
And Zilpah, Leah's maidservant, bore Jacob a second son.note
13
וַתֹּאמֶר לֵאָה--בְּאָשְׁרִי, כִּי אִשְּׁרוּנִי בָּנוֹת; וַתִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמוֹ, אָשֵׁר.
And Leah said, "Happy am I! for the daughters will call me happy"; and she named him Asher.note
14
וַיֵּלֶךְ רְאוּבֵן בִּימֵי קְצִיר-חִטִּים, וַיִּמְצָא דוּדָאִים בַּשָּׂדֶה, וַיָּבֵא אֹתָם, אֶל-לֵאָה אִמּוֹ; וַתֹּאמֶר רָחֵל, אֶל-לֵאָה, תְּנִי-נָא לִי, מִדּוּדָאֵי בְּנֵךְ.
And Reuben went in the days of the wheat harvest and found mandrakes in the field, and brought them to Leah his mother. And Rachel said to Leah, "Please give me some of your son's mandrakes."note
15
וַתֹּאמֶר לָהּ, הַמְעַט קַחְתֵּךְ אֶת-אִישִׁי, וְלָקַחַת, גַּם אֶת-דּוּדָאֵי בְּנִי; וַתֹּאמֶר רָחֵל, לָכֵן יִשְׁכַּב עִמָּךְ הַלַּיְלָה, תַּחַת, דּוּדָאֵי בְנֵךְ.
And she said to her, "Is it a small thing that you have taken my husband? Would you take my son's mandrakes too?" And Rachel said, "Then let him lie with you tonight, in return for your son's mandrakes."note
16
וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב מִן-הַשָּׂדֶה, בָּעֶרֶב, וַתֵּצֵא לֵאָה לִקְרָאתוֹ וַתֹּאמֶר אֵלַי תָּבוֹא, כִּי שָׂכֹר שְׂכַרְתִּיךָ בְּדוּדָאֵי בְּנִי; וַיִּשְׁכַּב עִמָּהּ, בַּלַּיְלָה הוּא.
And Jacob came from the field in the evening, and Leah went out to meet him and said, "You must come in to me, for I have surely hired you with my son's mandrakes." And he lay with her that night.note
17
וַיִּשְׁמַע אֱלֹהִים, אֶל-לֵאָה; וַתַּהַר וַתֵּלֶד לְיַעֲקֹב, בֵּן חֲמִישִׁי.
And God listened to Leah, and she conceived and bore Jacob a fifth son.note
18
וַתֹּאמֶר לֵאָה, נָתַן אֱלֹהִים שְׂכָרִי, אֲשֶׁר-נָתַתִּי שִׁפְחָתִי, לְאִישִׁי; וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ, יִשָּׂשכָר.
And Leah said, "God has given my hire, because I gave my maidservant to my husband"; and she named him Issachar.note
19
וַתַּהַר עוֹד לֵאָה, וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן-שִׁשִּׁי לְיַעֲקֹב.
And Leah conceived again and bore Jacob a sixth son.note
20
וַתֹּאמֶר לֵאָה, זְבָדַנִי אֱלֹהִים אֹתִי זֵבֶד טוֹב--הַפַּעַם יִזְבְּלֵנִי אִישִׁי, כִּי-יָלַדְתִּי לוֹ שִׁשָּׁה בָנִים; וַתִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמוֹ, זְבֻלוּן.
And Leah said, "God has endowed me with a good endowment; now my husband will honor me, because I have borne him six sons"; and she named him Zebulun.note
21
וְאַחַר, יָלְדָה בַּת; וַתִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמָהּ, דִּינָה.
And afterward she bore a daughter and named her Dinah.note
22
וַיִּזְכֹּר אֱלֹהִים, אֶת-רָחֵל; וַיִּשְׁמַע אֵלֶיהָ אֱלֹהִים, וַיִּפְתַּח אֶת-רַחְמָהּ.
And God remembered Rachel, and God listened to her and opened her womb.note
23
וַתַּהַר, וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן; וַתֹּאמֶר, אָסַף אֱלֹהִים אֶת-חֶרְפָּתִי.
And she conceived and bore a son, and said, "God has taken away my reproach."note
24
וַתִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמוֹ יוֹסֵף, לֵאמֹר: יֹסֵף יְהוָה לִי, בֵּן אַחֵר.
And she named him Joseph, saying, "May Jehovah add to me another son."note
25
וַיְהִי, כַּאֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה רָחֵל אֶת-יוֹסֵף; וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב, אֶל-לָבָן, שַׁלְּחֵנִי וְאֵלְכָה, אֶל-מְקוֹמִי וּלְאַרְצִי.
And when Rachel had borne Joseph, Jacob said to Laban, "Send me away, that I may go to my own place and my own land.note
26
תְּנָה אֶת-נָשַׁי וְאֶת-יְלָדַי, אֲשֶׁר עָבַדְתִּי אֹתְךָ בָּהֵן--וְאֵלֵכָה: כִּי אַתָּה יָדַעְתָּ, אֶת-עֲבֹדָתִי אֲשֶׁר עֲבַדְתִּיךָ.
Give me my wives and my children, for whom I have served you, and let me go; for you know the service I have served you."note
27
וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו לָבָן, אִם-נָא מָצָאתִי חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ; נִחַשְׁתִּי, וַיְבָרְכֵנִי יְהוָה בִּגְלָלֶךָ.
And Laban said to him, "If I have found favor in your eyes — I have divined that Jehovah has blessed me for your sake."note
28
וַיֹּאמַר: נָקְבָה שְׂכָרְךָ עָלַי, וְאֶתֵּנָה.
And he said, "Name your wages to me, and I will give it."note
29
וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו--אַתָּה יָדַעְתָּ, אֵת אֲשֶׁר עֲבַדְתִּיךָ; וְאֵת אֲשֶׁר-הָיָה מִקְנְךָ, אִתִּי.
And he said to him, "You know how I have served you, and how your livestock has fared with me.note
30
כִּי מְעַט אֲשֶׁר-הָיָה לְךָ לְפָנַי, וַיִּפְרֹץ לָרֹב, וַיְבָרֶךְ יְהוָה אֹתְךָ, לְרַגְלִי; וְעַתָּה, מָתַי אֶעֱשֶׂה גַם-אָנֹכִי--לְבֵיתִי.
For the little you had before me has increased abundantly, and Jehovah has blessed you at my every step; and now, when shall I provide for my own house too?"note
31
וַיֹּאמֶר, מָה אֶתֶּן-לָךְ; וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב, לֹא-תִתֶּן-לִי מְאוּמָה--אִם-תַּעֲשֶׂה-לִּי הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה, אָשׁוּבָה אֶרְעֶה צֹאנְךָ אֶשְׁמֹר.
And he said, "What shall I give you?" And Jacob said, "You shall give me nothing. If you will do this one thing for me, I will again pasture and keep your flock:note
32
אֶעֱבֹר בְּכָל-צֹאנְךָ הַיּוֹם, הָסֵר מִשָּׁם כָּל-שֶׂה נָקֹד וְטָלוּא וְכָל-שֶׂה-חוּם בַּכְּשָׂבִים, וְטָלוּא וְנָקֹד, בָּעִזִּים; וְהָיָה, שְׂכָרִי.
Let me pass through all your flock today, removing from it every speckled and spotted lamb, and every dark lamb among the sheep, and the spotted and speckled among the goats; and these shall be my wages.note
33
וְעָנְתָה-בִּי צִדְקָתִי בְּיוֹם מָחָר, כִּי-תָבוֹא עַל-שְׂכָרִי לְפָנֶיךָ: כֹּל אֲשֶׁר-אֵינֶנּוּ נָקֹד וְטָלוּא בָּעִזִּים, וְחוּם בַּכְּשָׂבִים--גָּנוּב הוּא, אִתִּי.
And my honesty will testify for me later, when you come to look over my wages before you: every one that is not speckled or spotted among the goats, or dark among the sheep, if found with me, shall be counted stolen."note
34
וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן, הֵן: לוּ, יְהִי כִדְבָרֶךָ.
And Laban said, "Good; let it be as you say."note
35
וַיָּסַר בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא אֶת-הַתְּיָשִׁים הָעֲקֻדִּים וְהַטְּלֻאִים, וְאֵת כָּל-הָעִזִּים הַנְּקֻדּוֹת וְהַטְּלֻאֹת, כֹּל אֲשֶׁר-לָבָן בּוֹ, וְכָל-חוּם בַּכְּשָׂבִים; וַיִּתֵּן, בְּיַד-בָּנָיו.
But that day he removed the streaked and spotted he-goats, and all the speckled and spotted she-goats, every one that had white on it, and every dark one among the sheep, and gave them into the hand of his sons.note
36
וַיָּשֶׂם, דֶּרֶךְ שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים, בֵּינוֹ, וּבֵין יַעֲקֹב; וְיַעֲקֹב, רֹעֶה אֶת-צֹאן לָבָן--הַנּוֹתָרֹת.
And he set a distance of three days' journey between himself and Jacob; and Jacob pastured the rest of Laban's flock.note
37
וַיִּקַּח-לוֹ יַעֲקֹב, מַקַּל לִבְנֶה לַח--וְלוּז וְעַרְמוֹן; וַיְפַצֵּל בָּהֵן, פְּצָלוֹת לְבָנוֹת--מַחְשֹׂף הַלָּבָן, אֲשֶׁר עַל-הַמַּקְלוֹת.
Then Jacob took fresh rods of poplar and almond and plane, and peeled white streaks in them, laying bare the white on the rods.note
38
וַיַּצֵּג, אֶת-הַמַּקְלוֹת אֲשֶׁר פִּצֵּל, בָּרְהָטִים, בְּשִׁקְתוֹת הַמָּיִם--אֲשֶׁר תָּבֹאןָ הַצֹּאן לִשְׁתּוֹת לְנֹכַח הַצֹּאן, וַיֵּחַמְנָה בְּבֹאָן לִשְׁתּוֹת.
And he set the rods he had peeled in the troughs, in the watering channels where the flocks came to drink, in front of the flocks; and they bred when they came to drink.note
39
וַיֶּחֱמוּ הַצֹּאן, אֶל-הַמַּקְלוֹת; וַתֵּלַדְןָ הַצֹּאן, עֲקֻדִּים נְקֻדִּים וּטְלֻאִים.
And the flocks bred before the rods, and the flocks brought forth streaked, speckled, and spotted young.note
40
וְהַכְּשָׂבִים, הִפְרִיד יַעֲקֹב, וַיִּתֵּן פְּנֵי הַצֹּאן אֶל-עָקֹד וְכָל-חוּם, בְּצֹאן לָבָן; וַיָּשֶׁת-לוֹ עֲדָרִים לְבַדּוֹ, וְלֹא שָׁתָם עַל-צֹאן לָבָן.
And Jacob separated the lambs, and set the faces of the flocks toward the streaked and all the dark in Laban's flock; and he made his own droves apart, and did not put them with Laban's flock.note
41
וְהָיָה, בְּכָל-יַחֵם הַצֹּאן הַמְקֻשָּׁרוֹת, וְשָׂם יַעֲקֹב אֶת-הַמַּקְלוֹת לְעֵינֵי הַצֹּאן, בָּרְהָטִים--לְיַחְמֵנָּה, בַּמַּקְלוֹת.
And whenever the stronger of the flock bred, Jacob would set the rods before the eyes of the flock in the troughs, that they might breed among the rods;note
42
וּבְהַעֲטִיף הַצֹּאן, לֹא יָשִׂים; וְהָיָה הָעֲטֻפִים לְלָבָן, וְהַקְּשֻׁרִים לְיַעֲקֹב.
but when the flocks were feeble he would not set them; so the feebler went to Laban, and the stronger to Jacob.note
43
וַיִּפְרֹץ הָאִישׁ, מְאֹד מְאֹד; וַיְהִי-לוֹ, צֹאן רַבּוֹת, וּשְׁפָחוֹת וַעֲבָדִים, וּגְמַלִּים וַחֲמֹרִים.
And the man increased exceedingly, and had large flocks, and maidservants and menservants, and camels and donkeys.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

The war of the wives, and the tribes are born — a chapter of desperate rivalry beneath which God quietly builds a nation. A birth-list, so the notes group by episode. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–13 · the war of the wives

"Give me children, or else I die." The chapter opens on Rachel's anguish — the beloved wife, barren, watching her unloved sister fill the tent with sons. Her cry to Jacob is raw, and his answer is sharp but true: "Am I in God's place, who has withheld from you the fruit of the womb?" Children are God's to give, not a husband's — the theology the whole chapter will bear out, against every scheme the sisters try. So begins a fertility contest fought by proxy: each wife hands her maidservant to Jacob to "bear on my knees" (a formal adoption gesture) — Rachel gives Bilhah, Leah gives Zilpah — exactly as Sarai once gave Hagar (16:2), and with the same word: "I too may be built up (ibbaneh) through her" — banah, "to build," the root of both ben ("son") and the hope of being built into a house.

Four more sons, four more name-sermons. Every birth is a claim in the sisters' quarrel, and each name is its Hebrew pun: Dan — "God has judged me" (dananni, from din); Naphtali — "with wrestlings of God I have wrestled (niphtalti) with my sister, and I have prevailed" (KJV "with great wrestlings"; the word niphtal, "to twist, wrestle," quietly foreshadows the night Jacob himself will wrestle God and be renamed Israel, 32:24–28); Gad — "Fortune has come!" (here the scroll writes one word, begad, and reads two, ba gad, a ketiv/qere — gad was also the name of a Canaanite luck-deity, though Leah means only "good fortune"); and Asher — "Happy am I! for the daughters will call me happy" (be-oshri, from osher, "happiness" — the very root of the ashrei, "Happy," that opens the Psalms and that Jesus takes up in the Beatitudes).

Verses 14–24 · דּוּדָאִים dudaim — the mandrakes, and God remembers Rachel

The love-apples. Little Reuben brings his mother mandrakes (dudaim; KJV "mandrakes") — a plant whose forked root and heady scent made it the ancient world's love-charm and fertility-drug, its very name echoing dod, "beloved." The scene drips with irony: Rachel, who has Jacob's love, has no children; Leah, who has children, has lost his nights — so they strike a bargain, and Rachel trades a night with her own husband for a handful of fertility-plants. And then the point lands like a verdict: it is Leah, not Rachel, who conceives from that night; the mandrakes do nothing. Leah bears a fifth son, Issachar — "God has given my hire" (sakhar, punning on the mandrakes she "hired" Jacob with); a sixth, Zebulun — "God has endowed me a good endowment; now my husband will dwell with me" (a double play on zeved and zaval); and a daughter, Dinah.

"And God remembered Rachel." Only after all the striving fails does the answer come — and it comes as sheer gift: "And God remembered (zakhar) Rachel… and opened her womb" (v. 22) — the same verb, and the same turn, as when "God remembered Noah" and the flood began to recede (8:1), and the same mercy that had opened Leah's womb (29:31). Her son's name carries a double meaning folded into one word: Joseph (Yosef) — "God has taken away (asaf) my reproach," and, looking ahead, "may Jehovah add (yosef) to me another son" — a prayer answered, at last, with Benjamin (35:18). The son the whole last quarter of Genesis will follow is born of the wife who waited longest.

Verses 25–43 · the speckled flocks — the deceiver out-deceived

The wages of a shrewd shepherd. With Joseph born, Jacob wants to go home, but Laban — who admits, "I have divined that Jehovah has blessed me for your sake" — will not let his lucky son-in-law leave. That admission is the Abrahamic promise at work in a pagan household: "in you all the families of the earth will be blessed" (12:3), the blessing spilling onto everyone near the chosen line (as it later will onto Potiphar and Pharaoh through Joseph). Jacob names his price: not a wage, but every off-colored animal — the speckled, spotted, streaked, and dark (KJV "ringstraked, speckled, and spotted") — the rare and undesirable ones in a flock of white sheep and dark goats. Laban agrees, then promptly cheats, culling every such animal that day and hustling it three days off with his sons.

The peeled rods. So Jacob answers guile with guile. He peels white streaks in fresh rods of poplar, almond, and plane, and sets them at the watering-troughs where the flocks breed, "in front of the flocks" — trusting the old belief that what a mother looks upon in the moment of conception marks her young — and the flocks bear streaked and speckled offspring in droves. Whatever the biology (the next chapter has Jacob tell it differently — a dream in which the angel of God, not the rods, made the flocks conceive, 31:10–12), the plot is unmistakable: the man who put on his brother's skin to steal a blessing, and was paid back a veiled bride by this same Laban, now out-maneuvers the master deceiver himself. He even breeds selectively, laying the rods only for the stronger animals, so the vigorous stock becomes his and the feeble stays Laban's. "And the man increased exceedingly" (me'od me'od, the "very, very" of the promise to Abraham, 17:2) — rich in flocks and servants and camels, and ready, now, to go home.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: the eight name-puns rendered so the Hebrew shows through — Dan/judged, Naphtali/wrestled, Gad/fortune, Asher/happy, Issachar/hire, Zebulun/endowed-and-dwell, and Joseph's double asaf/yasaf ("taken away" / "add"); "maidservant" kept for the surrogate-mothers' status (amah/shifchah); the flock-colors held distinct — speckled, spotted, streaked, and dark; and "increased exceedingly" for me'od me'od, the promise-word.

Echoes paid. "Built up through her" — Sarai's word over Hagar (16:2); "God remembered Rachel," as he remembered Noah (8:1) and opened Leah's womb (29:31); Laban blessed "for your sake," the 12:3 blessing on a pagan house; and "increased exceedingly" (17:2). Echoes planted: Naphtali's wrestling, waiting for the Jabbok (ch. 32); Joseph's "add another son," waiting for Benjamin (ch. 35); the mandrake-lender Leah's Issachar and the whole roll of twelve, waiting to be numbered as an army (Numbers 1); and Laban's cheated wages, waiting for the reckoning of chapter 31.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 31 — Jacob's flight from Haran, Rachel and the stolen household gods, Laban's pursuit, and the treaty-heap at Mizpah: "May Jehovah watch between you and me when we are out of each other's sight."

Genesis 31 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיִּשְׁמַע, אֶת-דִּבְרֵי בְנֵי-לָבָן לֵאמֹר, לָקַח יַעֲקֹב, אֵת כָּל-אֲשֶׁר לְאָבִינוּ; וּמֵאֲשֶׁר לְאָבִינוּ--עָשָׂה, אֵת כָּל-הַכָּבֹד הַזֶּה.
And Jacob heard the words of Laban's sons, saying, "Jacob has taken all that was our father's, and from what was our father's he has made all this wealth."note
2
וַיַּרְא יַעֲקֹב, אֶת-פְּנֵי לָבָן; וְהִנֵּה אֵינֶנּוּ עִמּוֹ, כִּתְמוֹל שִׁלְשׁוֹם.
And Jacob saw Laban's face, and behold, it was not toward him as before.note
3
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-יַעֲקֹב, שׁוּב אֶל-אֶרֶץ אֲבוֹתֶיךָ וּלְמוֹלַדְתֶּךָ; וְאֶהְיֶה, עִמָּךְ.
And Jehovah said to Jacob, "Return to the land of your fathers and to your kindred, and I will be with you."note
4
וַיִּשְׁלַח יַעֲקֹב, וַיִּקְרָא לְרָחֵל וּלְלֵאָה, הַשָּׂדֶה, אֶל-צֹאנוֹ.
And Jacob sent and called Rachel and Leah to the field, to his flock,note
5
וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶן, רֹאֶה אָנֹכִי אֶת-פְּנֵי אֲבִיכֶן, כִּי-אֵינֶנּוּ אֵלַי, כִּתְמֹל שִׁלְשֹׁם; וֵאלֹהֵי אָבִי, הָיָה עִמָּדִי.
and said to them, "I see your father's face, that it is not toward me as before; but the God of my father has been with me.note
6
וְאַתֵּנָה, יְדַעְתֶּן: כִּי, בְּכָל-כֹּחִי, עָבַדְתִּי, אֶת-אֲבִיכֶן.
And you yourselves know that I have served your father with all my strength.note
7
וַאֲבִיכֶן הֵתֶל בִּי, וְהֶחֱלִף אֶת-מַשְׂכֻּרְתִּי עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים; וְלֹא-נְתָנוֹ אֱלֹהִים, לְהָרַע עִמָּדִי.
Yet your father has cheated me and changed my wages ten times; but God did not let him harm me.note
8
אִם-כֹּה יֹאמַר, נְקֻדִּים יִהְיֶה שְׂכָרֶךָ--וְיָלְדוּ כָל-הַצֹּאן, נְקֻדִּים; וְאִם-כֹּה יֹאמַר, עֲקֻדִּים יִהְיֶה שְׂכָרֶךָ--וְיָלְדוּ כָל-הַצֹּאן, עֲקֻדִּים.
If he said, 'The speckled shall be your wages,' then all the flock bore speckled; and if he said, 'The streaked shall be your wages,' then all the flock bore streaked.note
9
וַיַּצֵּל אֱלֹהִים אֶת-מִקְנֵה אֲבִיכֶם, וַיִּתֶּן-לִי.
So God has taken away your father's livestock and given them to me.note
10
וַיְהִי, בְּעֵת יַחֵם הַצֹּאן, וָאֶשָּׂא עֵינַי וָאֵרֶא, בַּחֲלוֹם; וְהִנֵּה הָעַתֻּדִים הָעֹלִים עַל-הַצֹּאן, עֲקֻדִּים נְקֻדִּים וּבְרֻדִּים.
And at the time the flock bred, I lifted my eyes and saw in a dream, and behold, the he-goats mounting the flock were streaked, speckled, and mottled.note
11
וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלַי מַלְאַךְ הָאֱלֹהִים, בַּחֲלוֹם--יַעֲקֹב; וָאֹמַר, הִנֵּנִי.
And the angel of God said to me in the dream, 'Jacob'; and I said, 'Here I am.'note
12
וַיֹּאמֶר, שָׂא-נָא עֵינֶיךָ וּרְאֵה כָּל-הָעַתֻּדִים הָעֹלִים עַל-הַצֹּאן, עֲקֻדִּים נְקֻדִּים, וּבְרֻדִּים: כִּי רָאִיתִי, אֵת כָּל-אֲשֶׁר לָבָן עֹשֶׂה לָּךְ.
And he said, 'Lift up your eyes and see: all the he-goats mounting the flock are streaked, speckled, and mottled; for I have seen all that Laban is doing to you.note
13
אָנֹכִי הָאֵל, בֵּית-אֵל, אֲשֶׁר מָשַׁחְתָּ שָּׁם מַצֵּבָה, אֲשֶׁר נָדַרְתָּ לִּי שָׁם נֶדֶר; עַתָּה, קוּם צֵא מִן-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת, וְשׁוּב, אֶל-אֶרֶץ מוֹלַדְתֶּךָ.
I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed a pillar, where you vowed a vow to me. Now arise, go out from this land, and return to the land of your birth.'"note
14
וַתַּעַן רָחֵל וְלֵאָה, וַתֹּאמַרְנָה לוֹ: הַעוֹד לָנוּ חֵלֶק וְנַחֲלָה, בְּבֵית אָבִינוּ.
And Rachel and Leah answered him, "Is there still any portion or inheritance for us in our father's house?note
15
הֲלוֹא נָכְרִיּוֹת נֶחְשַׁבְנוּ לוֹ, כִּי מְכָרָנוּ; וַיֹּאכַל גַּם-אָכוֹל, אֶת-כַּסְפֵּנוּ.
Are we not reckoned by him as foreigners? For he has sold us, and he has utterly devoured our money.note
16
כִּי כָל-הָעֹשֶׁר, אֲשֶׁר הִצִּיל אֱלֹהִים מֵאָבִינוּ--לָנוּ הוּא, וּלְבָנֵינוּ; וְעַתָּה, כֹּל אֲשֶׁר אָמַר אֱלֹהִים אֵלֶיךָ--עֲשֵׂה.
For all the wealth that God has taken away from our father is ours and our children's; so now, do whatever God has said to you."note
17
וַיָּקָם, יַעֲקֹב; וַיִּשָּׂא אֶת-בָּנָיו וְאֶת-נָשָׁיו, עַל-הַגְּמַלִּים.
Then Jacob rose and set his sons and his wives on the camels,note
18
וַיִּנְהַג אֶת-כָּל-מִקְנֵהוּ, וְאֶת-כָּל-רְכֻשׁוֹ אֲשֶׁר רָכָשׁ--מִקְנֵה קִנְיָנוֹ, אֲשֶׁר רָכַשׁ בְּפַדַּן אֲרָם: לָבוֹא אֶל-יִצְחָק אָבִיו, אַרְצָה כְּנָעַן.
and drove off all his livestock and all the property he had gathered, the livestock of his acquiring that he had gathered in Paddan-aram, to go to Isaac his father in the land of Canaan.note
19
וְלָבָן הָלַךְ, לִגְזֹז אֶת-צֹאנוֹ; וַתִּגְנֹב רָחֵל, אֶת-הַתְּרָפִים אֲשֶׁר לְאָבִיהָ.
Now Laban had gone to shear his flock, and Rachel stole the teraphim that were her father's.note
20
וַיִּגְנֹב יַעֲקֹב, אֶת-לֵב לָבָן הָאֲרַמִּי--עַל-בְּלִי הִגִּיד לוֹ, כִּי בֹרֵחַ הוּא.
And Jacob stole the heart of Laban the Aramean, in that he did not tell him he was fleeing.note
21
וַיִּבְרַח הוּא וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ, וַיָּקָם וַיַּעֲבֹר אֶת-הַנָּהָר; וַיָּשֶׂם אֶת-פָּנָיו, הַר הַגִּלְעָד.
So he fled with all he had; he rose and crossed the River, and set his face toward the hill country of Gilead.note
22
וַיֻּגַּד לְלָבָן, בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי: כִּי בָרַח, יַעֲקֹב.
And on the third day Laban was told that Jacob had fled.note
23
וַיִּקַּח אֶת-אֶחָיו, עִמּוֹ, וַיִּרְדֹּף אַחֲרָיו, דֶּרֶךְ שִׁבְעַת יָמִים; וַיַּדְבֵּק אֹתוֹ, בְּהַר הַגִּלְעָד.
So he took his kinsmen with him and pursued after him a seven days' journey, and overtook him in the hill country of Gilead.note
24
וַיָּבֹא אֱלֹהִים אֶל-לָבָן הָאֲרַמִּי, בַּחֲלֹם הַלָּיְלָה; וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ, הִשָּׁמֶר לְךָ פֶּן-תְּדַבֵּר עִם-יַעֲקֹב--מִטּוֹב עַד-רָע.
But God came to Laban the Aramean in a dream of the night and said to him, "Take care that you speak to Jacob neither good nor bad."note
25
וַיַּשֵּׂג לָבָן, אֶת-יַעֲקֹב; וְיַעֲקֹב, תָּקַע אֶת-אָהֳלוֹ בָּהָר, וְלָבָן תָּקַע אֶת-אֶחָיו, בְּהַר הַגִּלְעָד.
And Laban caught up with Jacob. Now Jacob had pitched his tent in the hill country, and Laban with his kinsmen pitched in the hill country of Gilead.note
26
וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן, לְיַעֲקֹב, מֶה עָשִׂיתָ, וַתִּגְנֹב אֶת-לְבָבִי; וַתְּנַהֵג, אֶת-בְּנֹתַי, כִּשְׁבֻיוֹת, חָרֶב.
And Laban said to Jacob, "What have you done, that you have stolen my heart and driven off my daughters like captives of the sword?note
27
לָמָּה נַחְבֵּאתָ לִבְרֹחַ, וַתִּגְנֹב אֹתִי; וְלֹא-הִגַּדְתָּ לִּי, וָאֲשַׁלֵּחֲךָ בְּשִׂמְחָה וּבְשִׁרִים בְּתֹף וּבְכִנּוֹר.
Why did you flee secretly and steal from me, and not tell me, so that I might have sent you away with joy and songs, with tambourine and lyre?note
28
וְלֹא נְטַשְׁתַּנִי, לְנַשֵּׁק לְבָנַי וְלִבְנֹתָי; עַתָּה, הִסְכַּלְתָּ עֲשׂוֹ.
And you did not let me kiss my sons and my daughters! Now you have acted foolishly.note
29
יֶשׁ-לְאֵל יָדִי, לַעֲשׂוֹת עִמָּכֶם רָע; וֵאלֹהֵי אֲבִיכֶם אֶמֶשׁ אָמַר אֵלַי לֵאמֹר, הִשָּׁמֶר לְךָ מִדַּבֵּר עִם-יַעֲקֹב--מִטּוֹב עַד-רָע.
It is in the power of my hand to do you harm, but the God of your father spoke to me last night, saying, 'Take care that you speak to Jacob neither good nor bad.'note
30
וְעַתָּה הָלֹךְ הָלַכְתָּ, כִּי-נִכְסֹף נִכְסַפְתָּה לְבֵית אָבִיךָ; לָמָּה גָנַבְתָּ, אֶת-אֱלֹהָי.
And now you have surely gone, because you longed greatly for your father's house — but why did you steal my gods?"note
31
וַיַּעַן יַעֲקֹב, וַיֹּאמֶר לְלָבָן: כִּי יָרֵאתִי--כִּי אָמַרְתִּי, פֶּן-תִּגְזֹל אֶת-בְּנוֹתֶיךָ מֵעִמִּי.
And Jacob answered and said to Laban, "Because I was afraid; for I thought, lest you take your daughters from me by force.note
32
עִם אֲשֶׁר תִּמְצָא אֶת-אֱלֹהֶיךָ, לֹא יִחְיֶה--נֶגֶד אַחֵינוּ הַכֶּר-לְךָ מָה עִמָּדִי, וְקַח-לָךְ; וְלֹא-יָדַע יַעֲקֹב, כִּי רָחֵל גְּנָבָתַם.
With whomever you find your gods, he shall not live! Before our kinsmen, identify what is yours with me and take it." For Jacob did not know that Rachel had stolen them.note
33
וַיָּבֹא לָבָן בְּאֹהֶל יַעֲקֹב וּבְאֹהֶל לֵאָה, וּבְאֹהֶל שְׁתֵּי הָאֲמָהֹת--וְלֹא מָצָא; וַיֵּצֵא מֵאֹהֶל לֵאָה, וַיָּבֹא בְּאֹהֶל רָחֵל.
So Laban went into Jacob's tent, and into Leah's tent, and into the tent of the two maidservants, but found nothing; and he went out of Leah's tent and entered Rachel's tent.note
34
וְרָחֵל לָקְחָה אֶת-הַתְּרָפִים, וַתְּשִׂמֵם בְּכַר הַגָּמָל--וַתֵּשֶׁב עֲלֵיהֶם; וַיְמַשֵּׁשׁ לָבָן אֶת-כָּל-הָאֹהֶל, וְלֹא מָצָא.
Now Rachel had taken the teraphim and put them in the camel's saddle-cushion and sat on them. And Laban felt through all the tent but found nothing.note
35
וַתֹּאמֶר אֶל-אָבִיהָ, אַל-יִחַר בְּעֵינֵי אֲדֹנִי, כִּי לוֹא אוּכַל לָקוּם מִפָּנֶיךָ, כִּי-דֶרֶךְ נָשִׁים לִי; וַיְחַפֵּשׂ, וְלֹא מָצָא אֶת-הַתְּרָפִים.
And she said to her father, "Let my lord not be angry that I cannot rise before you, for the way of women is upon me." So he searched but did not find the teraphim.note
36
וַיִּחַר לְיַעֲקֹב, וַיָּרֶב בְּלָבָן; וַיַּעַן יַעֲקֹב, וַיֹּאמֶר לְלָבָן, מַה-פִּשְׁעִי מַה חַטָּאתִי, כִּי דָלַקְתָּ אַחֲרָי.
Then Jacob was angry and quarreled with Laban; and Jacob answered and said to Laban, "What is my offense? What is my sin, that you have hotly pursued me?note
37
כִּי-מִשַּׁשְׁתָּ אֶת-כָּל-כֵּלַי, מַה-מָּצָאתָ מִכֹּל כְּלֵי-בֵיתֶךָ--שִׂים כֹּה, נֶגֶד אַחַי וְאַחֶיךָ; וְיוֹכִיחוּ, בֵּין שְׁנֵינוּ.
Now that you have felt through all my goods, what have you found of all your household goods? Set it here before my kinsmen and yours, and let them decide between the two of us.note
38
זֶה עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה אָנֹכִי עִמָּךְ, רְחֵלֶיךָ וְעִזֶּיךָ לֹא שִׁכֵּלוּ; וְאֵילֵי צֹאנְךָ, לֹא אָכָלְתִּי.
These twenty years I have been with you; your ewes and your she-goats have not miscarried, and the rams of your flock I have not eaten.note
39
טְרֵפָה, לֹא-הֵבֵאתִי אֵלֶיךָ--אָנֹכִי אֲחַטֶּנָּה, מִיָּדִי תְּבַקְשֶׁנָּה; גְּנֻבְתִי יוֹם, וּגְנֻבְתִי לָיְלָה.
What was torn by beasts I did not bring to you; I bore its loss myself; from my hand you required it, whether stolen by day or stolen by night.note
40
הָיִיתִי בַיּוֹם אֲכָלַנִי חֹרֶב, וְקֶרַח בַּלָּיְלָה; וַתִּדַּד שְׁנָתִי, מֵעֵינָי.
By day the heat consumed me, and the frost by night, and my sleep fled from my eyes.note
41
זֶה-לִּי עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה, בְּבֵיתֶךָ, עֲבַדְתִּיךָ אַרְבַּע-עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה בִּשְׁתֵּי בְנֹתֶיךָ, וְשֵׁשׁ שָׁנִים בְּצֹאנֶךָ; וַתַּחֲלֵף אֶת-מַשְׂכֻּרְתִּי, עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים.
These twenty years I have been in your house: I served you fourteen years for your two daughters and six years for your flock, and you changed my wages ten times.note
42
לוּלֵי אֱלֹהֵי אָבִי אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם וּפַחַד יִצְחָק, הָיָה לִי--כִּי עַתָּה, רֵיקָם שִׁלַּחְתָּנִי; אֶת-עָנְיִי וְאֶת-יְגִיעַ כַּפַּי, רָאָה אֱלֹהִים--וַיּוֹכַח אָמֶשׁ.
Had not the God of my father, the God of Abraham and the Fear of Isaac, been with me, surely now you would have sent me away empty-handed. God has seen my affliction and the toil of my hands, and rendered judgment last night."note
43
וַיַּעַן לָבָן וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל-יַעֲקֹב, הַבָּנוֹת בְּנֹתַי וְהַבָּנִים בָּנַי וְהַצֹּאן צֹאנִי, וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה רֹאֶה, לִי-הוּא; וְלִבְנֹתַי מָה-אֶעֱשֶׂה לָאֵלֶּה, הַיּוֹם, אוֹ לִבְנֵיהֶן, אֲשֶׁר יָלָדוּ.
And Laban answered and said to Jacob, "The daughters are my daughters, and the children are my children, and the flocks are my flocks, and all that you see is mine; yet what can I do this day about these my daughters, or about their children whom they have borne?note
44
וְעַתָּה, לְכָה נִכְרְתָה בְרִית--אֲנִי וָאָתָּה; וְהָיָה לְעֵד, בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ.
So now, come, let us make a covenant, you and I, and let it be a witness between me and you."note
45
וַיִּקַּח יַעֲקֹב, אָבֶן; וַיְרִימֶהָ, מַצֵּבָה.
And Jacob took a stone and set it up as a pillar.note
46
וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב לְאֶחָיו לִקְטוּ אֲבָנִים, וַיִּקְחוּ אֲבָנִים וַיַּעֲשׂוּ-גָל; וַיֹּאכְלוּ שָׁם, עַל-הַגָּל.
And Jacob said to his kinsmen, "Gather stones"; and they took stones and made a heap, and they ate there by the heap.note
47
וַיִּקְרָא-לוֹ לָבָן, יְגַר שָׂהֲדוּתָא; וְיַעֲקֹב, קָרָא לוֹ גַּלְעֵד.
And Laban called it Jegar-sahadutha, but Jacob called it Galeed.note
48
וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן, הַגַּל הַזֶּה עֵד בֵּינִי וּבֵינְךָ הַיּוֹם; עַל-כֵּן קָרָא-שְׁמוֹ, גַּלְעֵד.
And Laban said, "This heap is a witness between me and you today." Therefore its name was called Galeed,note
49
וְהַמִּצְפָּה אֲשֶׁר אָמַר, יִצֶף יְהוָה בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ: כִּי נִסָּתֵר, אִישׁ מֵרֵעֵהוּ.
and Mizpah, for he said, "May Jehovah watch between me and you, when we are hidden from each other.note
50
אִם-תְּעַנֶּה אֶת-בְּנֹתַי, וְאִם-תִּקַּח נָשִׁים עַל-בְּנֹתַי--אֵין אִישׁ, עִמָּנוּ; רְאֵה, אֱלֹהִים עֵד בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ.
If you afflict my daughters, or if you take wives besides my daughters — though no one is with us — see, God is witness between me and you."note
51
וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן, לְיַעֲקֹב: הִנֵּה הַגַּל הַזֶּה, וְהִנֵּה הַמַּצֵּבָה, אֲשֶׁר יָרִיתִי, בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ.
And Laban said to Jacob, "Here is this heap, and here is the pillar that I have set between me and you.note
52
עֵד הַגַּל הַזֶּה, וְעֵדָה הַמַּצֵּבָה: אִם-אָנִי, לֹא-אֶעֱבֹר אֵלֶיךָ אֶת-הַגַּל הַזֶּה, וְאִם-אַתָּה לֹא-תַעֲבֹר אֵלַי אֶת-הַגַּל הַזֶּה וְאֶת-הַמַּצֵּבָה הַזֹּאת, לְרָעָה.
This heap is a witness, and the pillar is a witness, that I will not pass beyond this heap to you, and you shall not pass beyond this heap and this pillar to me, for harm.note
53
אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם וֵאלֹהֵי נָחוֹר, יִשְׁפְּטוּ בֵינֵינוּ--אֱלֹהֵי, אֲבִיהֶם; וַיִּשָּׁבַע יַעֲקֹב, בְּפַחַד אָבִיו יִצְחָק.
May the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor — the God of their father — judge between us." And Jacob swore by the Fear of his father Isaac.note
54
וַיִּזְבַּח יַעֲקֹב זֶבַח בָּהָר, וַיִּקְרָא לְאֶחָיו לֶאֱכָל-לָחֶם; וַיֹּאכְלוּ לֶחֶם, וַיָּלִינוּ בָּהָר.
And Jacob offered a sacrifice on the mountain and called his kinsmen to eat bread; and they ate bread and spent the night on the mountain.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Jacob flees Haran with his family and flocks, Rachel steals her father's gods, and the twenty-year contest of two schemers ends at a heap of stones. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–16 · "Return… and I will be with you"

The call home. The mood in Haran has soured — Laban's sons grumble that Jacob has swallowed their father's wealth, and Laban's face has turned — and it is exactly then that Jehovah speaks: "Return to the land of your fathers and to your kindred, and I will be with you" (v. 3). It is the promise of Bethel coming due: the God who said "I am with you and will keep you wherever you go" (28:15) now says the keeping is over — go home. Jacob calls Rachel and Leah out to the field, away from the house, and lays out his case: twenty years of hard service, a father-in-law who "changed my wages ten times," and a God who quietly turned every trick to Jacob's good.

The dream that reframes the rods. Jacob tells the sisters something the reader did not see in chapter 30: the speckled flocks were not really the work of his peeled sticks at all. "The angel of God" showed him in a dream that God was making the flocks bear streaked and speckled — "I have seen all that Laban is doing to you" — and named himself: "I am the God of Bethel, where you anointed a pillar and vowed a vow" (v. 13). The clever shepherd's trick was God's providence all along; the vow Jacob made as a frightened fugitive is now being called in. And the wives, for once, agree without a quarrel — with a startling verdict on their own father: "he has sold us, and utterly devoured our money" (v. 15). In this house, even the daughters feel like foreigners.

Verses 17–35 · גָּנַב ganav — a family of thieves, and the stolen gods

Everyone in this chapter steals. The word ganav, "to steal," rings through the flight like a bell. Rachel steals (ganav) her father's teraphim (v. 19; KJV "images"); Jacob "steals the heart" of Laban (v. 20) — ganav lev, the Hebrew idiom for hoodwinking someone (the same phrase Absalom will use to steal Israel's heart). Then Laban arrives and hurls the word back three times: "you have stolen my heart" (v. 26), "you stole from me" (v. 27), "why did you steal my gods?" (v. 30). The supplanter's household is a household of thieves — and God has to warn Laban in a dream not to lay a finger on them.

Why did Rachel take the gods? The teraphim were small household idols, and why Rachel wanted them the text leaves open — the interpretations run from the practical (in the customs of that world, holding the household gods could back a claim on the inheritance her father had denied her, v. 14) to the superstitious (portable luck, or divination) to the pious (rabbinic tradition: she took them to wean her father off idolatry). Whatever her reason, the scene is played for comedy at Laban's expense: he ransacks every tent while Rachel sits calmly on the idols, tucked in the camel's saddle, pleading "the way of women is upon me" — no man will ask a menstruating woman to stand. The gods of Laban end up sat upon. But there is a shadow over it, too: Jacob, not knowing, has sworn that whoever has the gods "shall not live" (v. 32) — an oath that hangs, unspoken, over the wife who will die young on the road home (35:16–19).

Verses 36–42 · פַּחַד יִצְחָק pachad Yitzchak — the Fear of Isaac

Twenty years, spoken at last. Now Jacob's own anger breaks, and out pours the one honest speech of his life so far — not a trick, just the truth of two decades of brutal shepherding: ewes that never miscarried, predators' kills he swallowed the cost of, "the heat by day and the frost by night, and my sleep fled from my eyes." He has been, for once, entirely faithful. And he names the God who carried him with a title found only here and a few verses on: the Fear of Isaac (pachad Yitzchak) — "the Awe-inspiring One whom Isaac reveres," the God of his father set beside "the God of Abraham." And the verb that seals it is Leah's: "God has seen my affliction" (oni, v. 42) — the very word she spoke over Reuben's birth (29:32); the God who sees the unloved sees the cheated servant too.

Verses 43–54 · יְגַר שָׂהֲדוּתָא · גַּלְעֵד — the heap of witness, in two tongues

The first Aramaic in the Bible. Two men who will never trust each other build a boundary and back away from it. They pile stones into a gal, a "heap," and name it — and here Genesis records a small, telling thing: Laban the Aramean names it in Aramaic, Jegar-sahadutha, and Jacob in Hebrew, Galeed — the two phrases mean exactly the same thing, "heap of witness," but in two languages. It is the earliest Aramaic in Scripture, and the moment the family's two branches are shown speaking two tongues; the name sticks to the whole region as Gilead.

The Mizpah "blessing" that is really a fence. The pillar is also called Mizpah, "the Watchpost," because Laban says, "May Jehovah watch (yitsef) between me and you, when we are hidden from each other" (v. 49; ASV "Jehovah watch between me and thee"). Cross-stitched onto keepsakes as a tender farewell, the line is nothing of the sort in context: it is two rivals invoking God to police a truce neither will keep on trust — "watch him, Lord, because I can't." Laban adds a clause protecting his daughters, and the two swear — but note by whom. Laban calls on "the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor," blurring the true God into his own family's gods; Jacob swears only, and precisely, "by the Fear of his father Isaac." A sacrifice, a shared meal on the mountain, a night's sleep — and in the morning Laban will kiss his grandchildren, bless them, and go home for good, out of the story. Twenty years in Haran are over; ahead lies the brother Jacob cheated, and a night by the Jabbok.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: the ganav ("steal") thread rendered "steal" throughout — Rachel steals the gods, Jacob "steals the heart" (deceives), Laban flings the word back — so the family of thieves is audible; "teraphim" kept as the specific term (KJV "images"); "the Fear of Isaac" for the divine epithet pachad Yitzchak; "affliction" for oni, Leah's word; and the bilingual heap kept in both tongues (Jegar-sahadutha / Galeed).

Echoes paid. "I will be with you" and "the God of Bethel, where you vowed" (28:15, 28:20) — the vow called in; "God has seen my affliction" (oni, 29:32); the speckled flocks of ch. 30 revealed as God's doing; and "the God of Nahor" (11:29), the family line Laban swears by. Echoes planted: Jacob's unwitting death-oath over the teraphim, waiting for Rachel's grave at Bethlehem (ch. 35); the crossing back toward Canaan, and the brother — and the wrestling angel — waiting at the Jabbok (ch. 32).

Next installment in this book: Genesis 32 — messengers to Esau, Jacob's terror and his prayer, the gift sent ahead, and the night by the Jabbok where a man wrestles him until dawn and renames him Israel, "for you have striven with God and with men, and have prevailed."

Genesis 32 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיַּשְׁכֵּם לָבָן בַּבֹּקֶר, וַיְנַשֵּׁק לְבָנָיו וְלִבְנוֹתָיו--וַיְבָרֶךְ אֶתְהֶם; וַיֵּלֶךְ וַיָּשָׁב לָבָן, לִמְקֹמוֹ.
And early in the morning Laban rose and kissed his sons and his daughters and blessed them; and Laban went and returned to his place.note
2
וְיַעֲקֹב, הָלַךְ לְדַרְכּוֹ; וַיִּפְגְּעוּ-בוֹ, מַלְאֲכֵי אֱלֹהִים.
And Jacob went on his way, and the angels of God met him.note
3
וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב כַּאֲשֶׁר רָאָם, מַחֲנֵה אֱלֹהִים זֶה; וַיִּקְרָא שֵׁם-הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא, מַחֲנָיִם. {פ}
And when Jacob saw them he said, "This is God's camp!" And he named that place Mahanaim.note
4
וַיִּשְׁלַח יַעֲקֹב מַלְאָכִים לְפָנָיו, אֶל-עֵשָׂו אָחִיו, אַרְצָה שֵׂעִיר, שְׂדֵה אֱדוֹם.
And Jacob sent messengers ahead of him to Esau his brother, to the land of Seir, the country of Edom.note
5
וַיְצַו אֹתָם, לֵאמֹר, כֹּה תֹאמְרוּן, לַאדֹנִי לְעֵשָׂו: כֹּה אָמַר, עַבְדְּךָ יַעֲקֹב, עִם-לָבָן גַּרְתִּי, וָאֵחַר עַד-עָתָּה.
And he commanded them, "Thus shall you say to my lord Esau: Thus says your servant Jacob — I have sojourned with Laban and stayed until now;note
6
וַיְהִי-לִי שׁוֹר וַחֲמוֹר, צֹאן וְעֶבֶד וְשִׁפְחָה; וָאֶשְׁלְחָה לְהַגִּיד לַאדֹנִי, לִמְצֹא-חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ.
and I have acquired oxen and donkeys, flocks and menservants and maidservants; and I have sent to tell my lord, that I may find favor in your eyes.'"note
7
וַיָּשֻׁבוּ, הַמַּלְאָכִים, אֶל-יַעֲקֹב, לֵאמֹר: בָּאנוּ אֶל-אָחִיךָ, אֶל-עֵשָׂו, וְגַם הֹלֵךְ לִקְרָאתְךָ, וְאַרְבַּע-מֵאוֹת אִישׁ עִמּוֹ.
And the messengers returned to Jacob, saying, "We came to your brother, to Esau, and he too is coming to meet you, and four hundred men with him."note
8
וַיִּירָא יַעֲקֹב מְאֹד, וַיֵּצֶר לוֹ; וַיַּחַץ אֶת-הָעָם אֲשֶׁר-אִתּוֹ, וְאֶת-הַצֹּאן וְאֶת-הַבָּקָר וְהַגְּמַלִּים--לִשְׁנֵי מַחֲנוֹת.
Then Jacob was greatly afraid and distressed; and he divided the people with him, and the flocks and herds and camels, into two camps.note
9
וַיֹּאמֶר, אִם-יָבוֹא עֵשָׂו אֶל-הַמַּחֲנֶה הָאַחַת וְהִכָּהוּ--וְהָיָה הַמַּחֲנֶה הַנִּשְׁאָר, לִפְלֵיטָה.
For he said, "If Esau comes to the one camp and strikes it, the camp that is left will escape."note
10
וַיֹּאמֶר, יַעֲקֹב, אֱלֹהֵי אָבִי אַבְרָהָם, וֵאלֹהֵי אָבִי יִצְחָק: יְהוָה הָאֹמֵר אֵלַי, שׁוּב לְאַרְצְךָ וּלְמוֹלַדְתְּךָ--וְאֵיטִיבָה עִמָּךְ.
And Jacob said, "O God of my father Abraham and God of my father Isaac, O Jehovah who said to me, 'Return to your land and to your kindred, and I will do you good' —note
11
קָטֹנְתִּי מִכֹּל הַחֲסָדִים, וּמִכָּל-הָאֱמֶת, אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתָ, אֶת-עַבְדֶּךָ: כִּי בְמַקְלִי, עָבַרְתִּי אֶת-הַיַּרְדֵּן הַזֶּה, וְעַתָּה הָיִיתִי, לִשְׁנֵי מַחֲנוֹת.
I am too small for all the kindness and all the faithfulness that you have shown your servant; for with my staff I crossed this Jordan, and now I have become two camps.note
12
הַצִּילֵנִי נָא מִיַּד אָחִי, מִיַּד עֵשָׂו: כִּי-יָרֵא אָנֹכִי, אֹתוֹ--פֶּן-יָבוֹא וְהִכַּנִי, אֵם עַל-בָּנִים.
Deliver me, I pray, from the hand of my brother, from the hand of Esau; for I am afraid of him, lest he come and strike me down, mother with children.note
13
וְאַתָּה אָמַרְתָּ, הֵיטֵב אֵיטִיב עִמָּךְ; וְשַׂמְתִּי אֶת-זַרְעֲךָ כְּחוֹל הַיָּם, אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יִסָּפֵר מֵרֹב.
But you said, 'I will surely do you good, and make your seed as the sand of the sea, which cannot be counted for multitude.'"note
14
וַיָּלֶן שָׁם, בַּלַּיְלָה הַהוּא; וַיִּקַּח מִן-הַבָּא בְיָדוֹ, מִנְחָה--לְעֵשָׂו אָחִיו.
And he lodged there that night; and he took from what he had at hand a present for Esau his brother:note
15
עִזִּים מָאתַיִם, וּתְיָשִׁים עֶשְׂרִים, רְחֵלִים מָאתַיִם, וְאֵילִים עֶשְׂרִים.
two hundred she-goats and twenty he-goats, two hundred ewes and twenty rams,note
16
גְּמַלִּים מֵינִיקוֹת וּבְנֵיהֶם, שְׁלֹשִׁים; פָּרוֹת אַרְבָּעִים, וּפָרִים עֲשָׂרָה, אֲתֹנֹת עֶשְׂרִים, וַעְיָרִם עֲשָׂרָה.
thirty milking camels with their young, forty cows and ten bulls, twenty she-donkeys and ten foals.note
17
וַיִּתֵּן, בְּיַד-עֲבָדָיו, עֵדֶר עֵדֶר, לְבַדּוֹ; וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל-עֲבָדָיו, עִבְרוּ לְפָנַי, וְרֶוַח תָּשִׂימוּ, בֵּין עֵדֶר וּבֵין עֵדֶר.
And he put them in the hand of his servants, each drove by itself, and said to his servants, "Pass on ahead of me, and put a space between drove and drove."note
18
וַיְצַו אֶת-הָרִאשׁוֹן, לֵאמֹר: כִּי יִפְגָשְׁךָ עֵשָׂו אָחִי, וּשְׁאֵלְךָ לֵאמֹר, לְמִי-אַתָּה וְאָנָה תֵלֵךְ, וּלְמִי אֵלֶּה לְפָנֶיךָ.
And he commanded the first, "When Esau my brother meets you and asks you, 'Whose are you, and where are you going, and whose are these ahead of you?'—note
19
וְאָמַרְתָּ, לְעַבְדְּךָ לְיַעֲקֹב--מִנְחָה הִוא שְׁלוּחָה, לַאדֹנִי לְעֵשָׂו; וְהִנֵּה גַם-הוּא, אַחֲרֵינוּ.
then you shall say, 'They are your servant Jacob's; it is a present sent to my lord Esau; and behold, he himself is behind us.'"note
20
וַיְצַו גַּם אֶת-הַשֵּׁנִי, גַּם אֶת-הַשְּׁלִישִׁי, גַּם אֶת-כָּל-הַהֹלְכִים, אַחֲרֵי הָעֲדָרִים לֵאמֹר: כַּדָּבָר הַזֶּה תְּדַבְּרוּן אֶל-עֵשָׂו, בְּמֹצַאֲכֶם אֹתוֹ.
And he commanded the second also, and the third, and all who followed the droves, "In this way you shall speak to Esau when you find him;note
21
וַאֲמַרְתֶּם--גַּם הִנֵּה עַבְדְּךָ יַעֲקֹב, אַחֲרֵינוּ: כִּי-אָמַר אֲכַפְּרָה פָנָיו, בַּמִּנְחָה הַהֹלֶכֶת לְפָנָי, וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן אֶרְאֶה פָנָיו, אוּלַי יִשָּׂא פָנָי.
and you shall say, 'Behold, your servant Jacob is also behind us.'" For he said, "I will appease his face with the present that goes ahead of me, and afterward I will see his face; perhaps he will lift up my face."note
22
וַתַּעֲבֹר הַמִּנְחָה, עַל-פָּנָיו; וְהוּא לָן בַּלַּיְלָה-הַהוּא, בַּמַּחֲנֶה.
So the present passed on ahead of him, while he himself lodged that night in the camp.note
23
וַיָּקָם בַּלַּיְלָה הוּא, וַיִּקַּח אֶת-שְׁתֵּי נָשָׁיו וְאֶת-שְׁתֵּי שִׁפְחֹתָיו, וְאֶת-אַחַד עָשָׂר, יְלָדָיו; וַיַּעֲבֹר, אֵת מַעֲבַר יַבֹּק.
And he rose that night and took his two wives, his two maidservants, and his eleven children, and crossed the ford of the Jabbok.note
24
וַיִּקָּחֵם--וַיַּעֲבִרֵם, אֶת-הַנָּחַל; וַיַּעֲבֵר, אֶת-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ.
He took them and sent them across the stream, and sent across what he had.note
25
וַיִּוָּתֵר יַעֲקֹב, לְבַדּוֹ; וַיֵּאָבֵק אִישׁ עִמּוֹ, עַד עֲלוֹת הַשָּׁחַר.
And Jacob was left alone; and a man wrestled with him until the dawn rose.note
26
וַיַּרְא, כִּי לֹא יָכֹל לוֹ, וַיִּגַּע, בְּכַף-יְרֵכוֹ; וַתֵּקַע כַּף-יֶרֶךְ יַעֲקֹב, בְּהֵאָבְקוֹ עִמּוֹ.
And when he saw that he could not prevail against him, he touched the socket of his thigh, and the socket of Jacob's thigh was wrenched as he wrestled with him.note
27
וַיֹּאמֶר שַׁלְּחֵנִי, כִּי עָלָה הַשָּׁחַר; וַיֹּאמֶר לֹא אֲשַׁלֵּחֲךָ, כִּי אִם-בֵּרַכְתָּנִי.
And he said, "Let me go, for the dawn has risen." But he said, "I will not let you go unless you bless me."note
28
וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו, מַה-שְּׁמֶךָ; וַיֹּאמֶר, יַעֲקֹב.
And he said to him, "What is your name?" And he said, "Jacob."note
29
וַיֹּאמֶר, לֹא יַעֲקֹב יֵאָמֵר עוֹד שִׁמְךָ--כִּי, אִם-יִשְׂרָאֵל: כִּי-שָׂרִיתָ עִם-אֱלֹהִים וְעִם-אֲנָשִׁים, וַתּוּכָל.
And he said, "Your name shall no longer be called Jacob, but Israel; for you have striven with God and with men, and have prevailed."note
30
וַיִּשְׁאַל יַעֲקֹב, וַיֹּאמֶר הַגִּידָה-נָּא שְׁמֶךָ, וַיֹּאמֶר, לָמָּה זֶּה תִּשְׁאַל לִשְׁמִי; וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתוֹ, שָׁם.
Then Jacob asked, "Please tell me your name." But he said, "Why is it that you ask my name?" And he blessed him there.note
31
וַיִּקְרָא יַעֲקֹב שֵׁם הַמָּקוֹם, פְּנִיאֵל: כִּי-רָאִיתִי אֱלֹהִים פָּנִים אֶל-פָּנִים, וַתִּנָּצֵל נַפְשִׁי.
And Jacob named the place Peniel; "for I have seen God face to face, and my life was delivered."note
32
וַיִּזְרַח-לוֹ הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ, כַּאֲשֶׁר עָבַר אֶת-פְּנוּאֵל; וְהוּא צֹלֵעַ, עַל-יְרֵכוֹ.
And the sun rose upon him as he passed Penuel, and he was limping on his thigh.note
33
עַל-כֵּן לֹא-יֹאכְלוּ בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת-גִּיד הַנָּשֶׁה, אֲשֶׁר עַל-כַּף הַיָּרֵךְ, עַד, הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה: כִּי נָגַע בְּכַף-יֶרֶךְ יַעֲקֹב, בְּגִיד הַנָּשֶׁה.
Therefore the children of Israel do not eat the sinew of the thigh that is on the socket of the hip, to this day; because he touched the socket of Jacob's thigh, at the sinew of the thigh.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Jacob comes home to face the brother he cheated — and first meets God in the dark, and is renamed. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–3 · Mahanaim — the angels who bracket the exile

A note on the numbering. This translation follows the HEBREW (Masoretic) verse-division, and here it parts from the common English one by a single verse. In the Hebrew, chapter 32 opens with Laban's farewell (verse 1) — most English Bibles print that verse as the last of chapter 31 (31:55) and begin chapter 32 with Jacob on the road. So through this chapter the Hebrew verse numbers run one ahead of the familiar English: our 32:2 is the English 32:1, and our 32:33 (the sinew of the thigh) is the English 32:32. Nothing is added or dropped — only the chapter line falls in a slightly different place, and this edition keeps it where the Hebrew scroll puts it.

Angels going, angels coming. Laban rides off, and Jacob "went on his way, and the angels of God met him." Twenty years earlier, fleeing this same road in the other direction, he had seen angels on a stairway at Bethel (28:12); now, coming home, angels meet him again — the messengers of God bracket his whole exile, at the border going out and at the border coming back. He names the place Mahanaim, "two camps" (God's camp and his own) — a word that will echo in a moment when, terrified, he splits his own company into two camps.

Verses 4–9 · "your servant Jacob" to "my lord Esau"

The supplanter, bowing. The blessing Jacob stole had made him lord: "be lord over your brothers, and let your mother's sons bow down to you" (27:29). Now, dreading the reckoning, he inverts it in every line of his message — "your servant Jacob… my lord Esau" — the thief of the birthright approaching the wronged brother with his face in the dust. And the word comes back like a blow: Esau is already on the road to meet him, "and four hundred men with him." Jacob, "greatly afraid and distressed," does the only thing his old self knows — he calculates, dividing everyone into two camps so that if one is slaughtered the other might escape. His hands are still scheming; but that night, for the first time, his voice will turn to prayer.

Verses 10–13 · קָטֹנְתִּי qatonti — "I am too small"

Jacob's first prayer. In all his life so far the schemer has never once been shown praying; here, cornered, he does — and it is a model of a prayer, laying hold of God's own words. He calls on "the God of my father Abraham and… Isaac, O Jehovah who said to me, 'Return… and I will do you good'" (v. 10) — reminding God of the very promise that sent him home. Then the line that turns the whole Jacob story on its hinge: qatonti, "I am too small" (v. 11; KJV "I am not worthy") — the man who has spent a lifetime grasping to be great, who has just become "two camps" of wealth, confesses he is small before "all the kindness and all the faithfulness" (chesed ve-emet) God has shown him. "With my staff I crossed this Jordan, and now I have become two camps": he remembers going out with nothing but a walking-stick. And he closes by holding God to his oath — "you said… I will make your seed as the sand of the sea" — the promise of the binding of Isaac (22:17). You cannot be killed tonight, Lord, if your own word is to stand.

Verses 14–22 · פָּנִים panim — a face to be covered, lifted, and seen

The gift, and the drumbeat of "face." Alongside the prayer, Jacob keeps working the problem the only other way he knows — with a lavish, staggered present (minchah): 550 animals sent ahead in wave after wave, each drove announced as "your servant Jacob's," so that Esau's anger is worn down by generosity before the brothers ever meet. And listen to the word that pulses through his plan — panim, "face": "I will cover his face (akhappera panav — the same verb, kaphar, that will mean 'make atonement') with the present that goes before my face… afterward I will see his face; perhaps he will lift up my face" (v. 21). Four faces in one verse — and it is all building, unknown to Jacob, toward the face he will see first: not Esau's, but God's, at a place he will call "the Face of God."

Verses 23–33 · יִשְׂרָאֵל Yisrael — the night he was renamed

Left alone. Jacob sends everyone he loves across the Jabbok — the stream whose name, Yabboq, chimes with both Ya'aqov ("Jacob") and the strange verb of this night, vayye'aveq, "and he wrestled." "And Jacob was left alone; and a man wrestled with him until the dawn rose." Who is the man? The text says only ish, "a man"; Jacob will say he saw Elohim, "God, face to face"; the prophet Hosea calls him "the angel" (Hosea 12:4). This translation lets the mystery stand, as it did at the well of Hagar — a man who is somehow God, met in the dark.

The name that is a verdict. Jacob cannot win, but he will not let go, even when his hip is wrenched from its socket: "I will not let you go unless you bless me" — the man who once stole a blessing now demands one, wounded, clinging in the dark. And the blessing is a new name: "no longer Jacob ('supplanter, heel-grabber'), but Israel — Yisrael — for you have striven (sarita) with God and with men, and have prevailed" (vatukhal). It is the very word Rachel had cried at Naphtali's birth, "I have prevailed" (30:8) — but where she prevailed over her sister by scheming, Jacob prevails over God by holding on. He asks the man's name and is refused ("Why do you ask my name?"), and named the place Peniel, "the Face of God" — "for I have seen God face to face, and my life was delivered" (KJV "for I have seen God face to face, and my life is preserved"). The sun rose on him as he limped away — and to this day the children of Israel do not eat "the sinew of the thigh" (gid ha-nasheh), remembering the night their father was crippled and crowned at once. The supplanter has become the God-wrestler; he goes to meet Esau at last, limping, blessed, and — for the first time — unafraid.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: the Hebrew versification kept (our 32:1 = Laban's farewell, the English 31:55), with the offset flagged; "I am too small" for qatonti (KJV "I am not worthy") — the grasping man made small; the panim ("face") drumbeat kept across the gift and the wrestling (covered, lifted, seen — up to Peni-el); "striven" for sarita in the naming of Israel; "prevailed" for vatukhal, matched to Rachel's word; and the man/God ambiguity of the wrestler left standing (ish / Elohim / the angel of Hosea 12).

Echoes paid. The angels of Bethel (28:12) now meet him coming home; "be lord over your brothers" (27:29) inverted into "your servant… my lord"; "deliver me from Esau," whose murder-grudge (27:41) drove the flight; the "sand of the sea" seed of the Aqedah (22:17); "I have prevailed" (30:8); and God seen "face to face," the seeing-God Hagar first named (16:13). Echoes planted: the name Israel, re-confirmed at Bethel (ch. 35) and worn by the nation for good; and the brothers' meeting, one river away (ch. 33).

Next installment in this book: Genesis 33 — the brothers meet at last; Esau runs to embrace the brother he swore to kill, and weeps on his neck, and Jacob says, "to see your face is like seeing the face of God."

Genesis 33 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב עֵינָיו, וַיַּרְא וְהִנֵּה עֵשָׂו בָּא, וְעִמּוֹ, אַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת אִישׁ; וַיַּחַץ אֶת-הַיְלָדִים, עַל-לֵאָה וְעַל-רָחֵל, וְעַל, שְׁתֵּי הַשְּׁפָחוֹת.
And Jacob lifted his eyes and looked, and behold, Esau was coming, and four hundred men with him. So he divided the children among Leah and Rachel and the two maidservants.note
2
וַיָּשֶׂם אֶת-הַשְּׁפָחוֹת וְאֶת-יַלְדֵיהֶן, רִאשֹׁנָה; וְאֶת-לֵאָה וִילָדֶיהָ אַחֲרֹנִים, וְאֶת-רָחֵל וְאֶת-יוֹסֵף אַחֲרֹנִים.
And he put the maidservants and their children first, Leah and her children after, and Rachel and Joseph last.note
3
וְהוּא, עָבַר לִפְנֵיהֶם; וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ אַרְצָה שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים, עַד-גִּשְׁתּוֹ עַד-אָחִיו.
And he himself passed on ahead of them and bowed to the ground seven times, until he came near to his brother.note
4
וַיָּרָץ עֵשָׂו לִקְרָאתוֹ וַיְחַבְּקֵהוּ, וַיִּפֹּל עַל-צַוָּארָו וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ; וַיִּבְכּוּ.
And Esau ran to meet him, and embraced him, and fell on his neck and kissed him; and they wept.note
5
וַיִּשָּׂא אֶת-עֵינָיו, וַיַּרְא אֶת-הַנָּשִׁים וְאֶת-הַיְלָדִים, וַיֹּאמֶר, מִי-אֵלֶּה לָּךְ; וַיֹּאמַר--הַיְלָדִים, אֲשֶׁר-חָנַן אֱלֹהִים אֶת-עַבְדֶּךָ.
And he lifted his eyes and saw the women and the children, and said, "Who are these with you?" And he said, "The children whom God has graciously given your servant."note
6
וַתִּגַּשְׁןָ הַשְּׁפָחוֹת הֵנָּה וְיַלְדֵיהֶן, וַתִּשְׁתַּחֲוֶיןָ.
Then the maidservants came near, they and their children, and bowed down.note
7
וַתִּגַּשׁ גַּם-לֵאָה וִילָדֶיהָ, וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ; וְאַחַר, נִגַּשׁ יוֹסֵף וְרָחֵל--וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ.
And Leah also came near with her children, and they bowed down; and afterward Joseph and Rachel came near, and they bowed down.note
8
וַיֹּאמֶר, מִי לְךָ כָּל-הַמַּחֲנֶה הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר פָּגָשְׁתִּי; וַיֹּאמֶר, לִמְצֹא-חֵן בְּעֵינֵי אֲדֹנִי.
And he said, "What is all this camp of yours that I met?" And he said, "To find favor in the eyes of my lord."note
9
וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו, יֶשׁ-לִי רָב; אָחִי, יְהִי לְךָ אֲשֶׁר-לָךְ.
And Esau said, "I have plenty, my brother; keep what is yours."note
10
וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב, אַל-נָא אִם-נָא מָצָאתִי חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ, וְלָקַחְתָּ מִנְחָתִי, מִיָּדִי: כִּי עַל-כֵּן רָאִיתִי פָנֶיךָ, כִּרְאֹת פְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים--וַתִּרְצֵנִי.
And Jacob said, "No, please; if I have found favor in your eyes, then take my present from my hand — for I have seen your face as one sees the face of God, and you have received me favorably.note
11
קַח-נָא אֶת-בִּרְכָתִי אֲשֶׁר הֻבָאת לָךְ, כִּי-חַנַּנִי אֱלֹהִים וְכִי יֶשׁ-לִי-כֹל; וַיִּפְצַר-בּוֹ, וַיִּקָּח.
Take, I pray, my blessing that is brought to you, for God has dealt graciously with me, and I have everything." And he pressed him, and he took it.note
12
וַיֹּאמֶר, נִסְעָה וְנֵלֵכָה; וְאֵלְכָה, לְנֶגְדֶּךָ.
And he said, "Let us set out and go, and I will go alongside you."note
13
וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו, אֲדֹנִי יֹדֵעַ כִּי-הַיְלָדִים רַכִּים, וְהַצֹּאן וְהַבָּקָר, עָלוֹת עָלָי; וּדְפָקוּם יוֹם אֶחָד, וָמֵתוּ כָּל-הַצֹּאן.
But he said to him, "My lord knows that the children are frail, and the flocks and herds are nursing in my care; if they are driven hard a single day, all the flock will die.note
14
יַעֲבָר-נָא אֲדֹנִי, לִפְנֵי עַבְדּוֹ; וַאֲנִי אֶתְנָהֲלָה לְאִטִּי, לְרֶגֶל הַמְּלָאכָה אֲשֶׁר-לְפָנַי וּלְרֶגֶל הַיְלָדִים, עַד אֲשֶׁר-אָבֹא אֶל-אֲדֹנִי, שֵׂעִירָה.
Let my lord pass on ahead of his servant, and I will make my way gently, at the pace of the livestock before me and the pace of the children, until I come to my lord in Seir."note
15
וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו--אַצִּיגָה-נָּא עִמְּךָ, מִן-הָעָם אֲשֶׁר אִתִּי; וַיֹּאמֶר לָמָּה זֶּה, אֶמְצָא-חֵן בְּעֵינֵי אֲדֹנִי.
And Esau said, "Let me leave with you some of the people who are with me." But he said, "Why so? Let me only find favor in the eyes of my lord."note
16
וַיָּשָׁב בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא עֵשָׂו לְדַרְכּוֹ, שֵׂעִירָה.
So Esau returned that day on his way to Seir.note
17
וְיַעֲקֹב נָסַע סֻכֹּתָה, וַיִּבֶן לוֹ בָּיִת; וּלְמִקְנֵהוּ עָשָׂה סֻכֹּת, עַל-כֵּן קָרָא שֵׁם-הַמָּקוֹם סֻכּוֹת. {ס}
And Jacob journeyed to Succoth, and built himself a house, and made booths for his livestock; therefore the place is named Succoth.note
18
וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב שָׁלֵם עִיר שְׁכֶם, אֲשֶׁר בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן, בְּבֹאוֹ, מִפַּדַּן אֲרָם; וַיִּחַן, אֶת-פְּנֵי הָעִיר.
And Jacob came in peace to the city of Shechem, which is in the land of Canaan, on his way from Paddan-aram, and camped before the city.note
19
וַיִּקֶן אֶת-חֶלְקַת הַשָּׂדֶה, אֲשֶׁר נָטָה-שָׁם אָהֳלוֹ, מִיַּד בְּנֵי-חֲמוֹר, אֲבִי שְׁכֶם--בְּמֵאָה, קְשִׂיטָה.
And he bought the parcel of the field where he had pitched his tent, from the hand of the sons of Hamor, Shechem's father, for a hundred kesitah.note
20
וַיַּצֶּב-שָׁם, מִזְבֵּחַ; וַיִּקְרָא-לוֹ--אֵל, אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל. {ס}
And there he set up an altar and called it El-Elohe-Israel.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

The dreaded reunion becomes an embrace — the brother who swore to kill Jacob runs to meet him and weeps. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–7 · the murderer who ran to embrace him

Still hedging, even now. Jacob has wrestled God and been renamed, but old habits die hard: seeing Esau and his four hundred men, he arranges his family by how much he can bear to lose — the maidservants and their children in front, Leah's children next, and Rachel and Joseph last, in the safest place. It is a father's love, and also the first open sign of the favoritism that will one day tear this family apart (ch. 37). Then Jacob goes out ahead of them all and bows to the ground seven times — the man whom the stolen blessing had made lord, "let your brothers bow down to you" (27:29), now on his face before the brother he cheated.

Grace outran fear. And the whole twenty years of dread dissolve in a single verse: "Esau ran to meet him, and embraced him, and fell on his neck and kissed him; and they wept." This is the same Esau who had said in his heart, "I will kill my brother Jacob" (27:41) — and here he runs, and falls on his neck, and weeps. The scene is so complete a picture of forgiveness that Jesus will reach for its very words to paint the father of the prodigal, who "ran and fell on his neck and kissed him" (Luke 15:20). Whatever Jacob deserved, what met him was mercy.

Verses 8–11 · בִּרְכָתִי birkhati — "my blessing," given back

The face of God, again. Esau waves off the enormous gift — "I have plenty (rav), my brother; keep what is yours" — but Jacob insists, and his reason reaches back to the night just past: "for I have seen your face as one sees the face of God, and you have received me favorably" (v. 10). The night before he had named a place Peniel, "I have seen God face to face" (32:31); now, forgiven by the brother he wronged, he says looking on Esau's merciful face is like that. Reconciliation with the brother mirrors the wrestling with God — he has survived both faces, and lived.

Restoring the blessing. And notice what Jacob calls the gift: not minchah, "present," as before, but birkhati, "my blessing" — "take my blessing that is brought to you." It is no accident. The one thing Jacob ever truly stole from Esau was a blessing (27:36, "he has taken my blessing!"), and here, in the plainest language, he tries to hand a blessing back — a quiet act of restitution. He adds a line that quietly out-measures his brother: Esau has "plenty" (rav), but "I have everything" (kol, v. 11) — abundance against contentment. (The KJV renders both men's words "I have enough," blurring the point that Esau has much and Jacob has all.)

Verses 12–16 · a reconciliation that keeps its distance

The peace that is real, and not a merger. Esau, warm and generous, wants to travel on together and even to leave an escort; Jacob gently declines twice over — the children are frail, the nursing flocks can't be driven hard — "let my lord go ahead, and I will come slowly… to Seir." But Jacob does not go to Seir. He turns the other way, to Succoth and Shechem, and the brothers will not meet again until they stand together at their father's grave (35:29). The old caution outlives the reconciliation: the embrace was true, the tears were true, and Jacob still keeps a careful distance from a brother he cannot quite trust. It is not entirely to his credit, and the text does not pretend otherwise — but it is, perhaps, its own kind of wisdom, two men who have made peace choosing to make it from a little way apart.

Verses 17–20 · שָׁלֵם shalem — home, whole, at an altar

Booths, and a safe arrival. Jacob stops first at Succoth, and the place-name is its own footnote: he built booths (sukkot) for his cattle, and "therefore the place is named Succoth" — the same word that will name Israel's festival of Sukkot, "Booths." Then he crosses at last into Canaan and "came in peace (shalem) to the city of Shechem." The word shalem — whole, safe, at peace, the cousin of shalom — is the perfect close to the crossing: he went out a fugitive with a stone for a pillow and comes home whole. (The KJV heard shalem as a place-name, "to Shalem, a city of Shechem"; the plain sense is that he arrived safe and sound.)

An altar, in his new name. And Jacob does two things his grandfather did here first. He buys land — the parcel where his tent stood, for a hundred kesitah (an old unit of money; KJV "pieces of money") — only the second patch of the Promised Land any patriarch has owned, after the grave at Machpelah; and he does it at Shechem, the very spot where Abraham built his first altar in Canaan (12:6-7). Then he raises an altar of his own and names it El-Elohe-Israel, "El, the God of Israel" — using the new name the angel gave him, and claiming the God of Abraham and Isaac, at last, as his own God. The vow he made fleeing this land — "then Jehovah shall be my God" (28:21) — has quietly come true.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "I have plenty" (Esau, rav) vs "I have everything" (Jacob, kol), kept apart where the KJV flattens both to "I have enough"; "my blessing" for birkhati (the gift Jacob calls a blessing, giving one back); "in peace" for shalem at Shechem (not the KJV's place-name "Shalem"); "kesitah" kept as the archaic coin; and the altar-name El-Elohe-Israel left in the Hebrew.

Echoes paid. "the face of God" — the Peniel night (32:31) reread in a brother's forgiveness; "my blessing" handed back (27:36); Esau's murder-vow (27:41) undone in an embrace; "bow down to you" (27:29) inverted as Jacob bows seven times; the second land bought in Canaan after Machpelah (23:19); the altar at Shechem where Abraham built his first (12:7); and the Bethel vow, "Jehovah shall be my God" (28:21), made good. Echoes planted: Rachel and Joseph set apart and loved best, waiting for the coat of many colors (ch. 37); and the tents pitched at Shechem, beside the daughter Dinah and the sons of Hamor (ch. 34).

Next installment in this book: Genesis 34 — the dark chapter at Shechem: the violation of Dinah, and the terrible revenge of Simeon and Levi on the city, that Jacob will never forgive them.

Genesis 34 — The Mister Translation

1
וַתֵּצֵא דִינָה בַּת-לֵאָה, אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה לְיַעֲקֹב, לִרְאוֹת, בִּבְנוֹת הָאָרֶץ.
And Dinah, the daughter of Leah, whom she had borne to Jacob, went out to see the daughters of the land.note
2
וַיַּרְא אֹתָהּ שְׁכֶם בֶּן-חֲמוֹר, הַחִוִּי--נְשִׂיא הָאָרֶץ; וַיִּקַּח אֹתָהּ וַיִּשְׁכַּב אֹתָהּ, וַיְעַנֶּהָ.
And Shechem son of Hamor the Hivite, the prince of the land, saw her, and took her, and lay with her, and violated her.note
3
וַתִּדְבַּק נַפְשׁוֹ, בְּדִינָה בַּת-יַעֲקֹב; וַיֶּאֱהַב, אֶת-הַנַּעֲרָ, וַיְדַבֵּר, עַל-לֵב הַנַּעֲרָ.
And his soul clung to Dinah the daughter of Jacob; and he loved the girl, and spoke to the girl's heart.note
4
וַיֹּאמֶר שְׁכֶם, אֶל-חֲמוֹר אָבִיו לֵאמֹר: קַח-לִי אֶת-הַיַּלְדָּה הַזֹּאת, לְאִשָּׁה.
And Shechem said to his father Hamor, saying, "Get me this girl for a wife."note
5
וְיַעֲקֹב שָׁמַע, כִּי טִמֵּא אֶת-דִּינָה בִתּוֹ, וּבָנָיו הָיוּ אֶת-מִקְנֵהוּ, בַּשָּׂדֶה; וְהֶחֱרִשׁ יַעֲקֹב, עַד-בֹּאָם.
Now Jacob heard that he had defiled his daughter Dinah, but his sons were with his livestock in the field; so Jacob held his peace until they came.note
6
וַיֵּצֵא חֲמוֹר אֲבִי-שְׁכֶם, אֶל-יַעֲקֹב, לְדַבֵּר, אִתּוֹ.
And Hamor, Shechem's father, went out to Jacob to speak with him.note
7
וּבְנֵי יַעֲקֹב בָּאוּ מִן-הַשָּׂדֶה, כְּשָׁמְעָם, וַיִּתְעַצְּבוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים, וַיִּחַר לָהֶם מְאֹד: כִּי-נְבָלָה עָשָׂה בְיִשְׂרָאֵל, לִשְׁכַּב אֶת-בַּת-יַעֲקֹב, וְכֵן, לֹא יֵעָשֶׂה.
And the sons of Jacob came in from the field when they heard; and the men were grieved, and they burned with anger, for he had done an outrage in Israel by lying with Jacob's daughter — a thing that should not be done.note
8
וַיְדַבֵּר חֲמוֹר, אִתָּם לֵאמֹר: שְׁכֶם בְּנִי, חָשְׁקָה נַפְשׁוֹ בְּבִתְּכֶם--תְּנוּ נָא אֹתָהּ לוֹ, לְאִשָּׁה.
And Hamor spoke with them, saying, "My son Shechem — his soul longs for your daughter; please give her to him for a wife.note
9
וְהִתְחַתְּנוּ, אֹתָנוּ: בְּנֹתֵיכֶם, תִּתְּנוּ-לָנוּ, וְאֶת-בְּנֹתֵינוּ, תִּקְחוּ לָכֶם.
And intermarry with us: give us your daughters, and take our daughters for yourselves.note
10
וְאִתָּנוּ, תֵּשֵׁבוּ; וְהָאָרֶץ, תִּהְיֶה לִפְנֵיכֶם--שְׁבוּ וּסְחָרוּהָ, וְהֵאָחֲזוּ בָּהּ.
And you shall dwell with us, and the land shall be before you: dwell, and trade in it, and take holdings in it."note
11
וַיֹּאמֶר שְׁכֶם אֶל-אָבִיהָ וְאֶל-אַחֶיהָ, אֶמְצָא-חֵן בְּעֵינֵיכֶם; וַאֲשֶׁר תֹּאמְרוּ אֵלַי, אֶתֵּן.
And Shechem said to her father and to her brothers, "Let me find favor in your eyes, and whatever you say to me I will give.note
12
הַרְבּוּ עָלַי מְאֹד, מֹהַר וּמַתָּן, וְאֶתְּנָה, כַּאֲשֶׁר תֹּאמְרוּ אֵלָי; וּתְנוּ-לִי אֶת-הַנַּעֲרָ, לְאִשָּׁה.
Pile on me a very great bride-price and gift, and I will give as you say to me; only give me the girl for a wife."note
13
וַיַּעֲנוּ בְנֵי-יַעֲקֹב אֶת-שְׁכֶם וְאֶת-חֲמוֹר אָבִיו, בְּמִרְמָה--וַיְדַבֵּרוּ: אֲשֶׁר טִמֵּא, אֵת דִּינָה אֲחֹתָם.
And the sons of Jacob answered Shechem and Hamor his father with deceit, and spoke — because he had defiled their sister Dinah —note
14
וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֲלֵיהֶם, לֹא נוּכַל לַעֲשׂוֹת הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה--לָתֵת אֶת-אֲחֹתֵנוּ, לְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ עָרְלָה: כִּי-חֶרְפָּה הִוא, לָנוּ.
and said to them, "We cannot do this thing, to give our sister to a man who is uncircumcised, for that is a disgrace to us.note
15
אַךְ-בְּזֹאת, נֵאוֹת לָכֶם: אִם תִּהְיוּ כָמֹנוּ, לְהִמֹּל לָכֶם כָּל-זָכָר.
Only on this will we consent to you: if you become like us, that every male among you be circumcised.note
16
וְנָתַנּוּ אֶת-בְּנֹתֵינוּ לָכֶם, וְאֶת-בְּנֹתֵיכֶם נִקַּח-לָנוּ; וְיָשַׁבְנוּ אִתְּכֶם, וְהָיִינוּ לְעַם אֶחָד.
Then we will give you our daughters, and take your daughters for ourselves; and we will dwell with you and become one people.note
17
וְאִם-לֹא תִשְׁמְעוּ אֵלֵינוּ, לְהִמּוֹל--וְלָקַחְנוּ אֶת-בִּתֵּנוּ, וְהָלָכְנוּ.
But if you will not listen to us, to be circumcised, then we will take our daughter and go."note
18
וַיִּיטְבוּ דִבְרֵיהֶם, בְּעֵינֵי חֲמוֹר, וּבְעֵינֵי, שְׁכֶם בֶּן-חֲמוֹר.
And their words were good in the eyes of Hamor and in the eyes of Shechem son of Hamor.note
19
וְלֹא-אֵחַר הַנַּעַר לַעֲשׂוֹת הַדָּבָר, כִּי חָפֵץ בְּבַת-יַעֲקֹב; וְהוּא נִכְבָּד, מִכֹּל בֵּית אָבִיו.
And the young man did not delay to do the thing, for he delighted in Jacob's daughter; and he was the most honored of all his father's house.note
20
וַיָּבֹא חֲמוֹר וּשְׁכֶם בְּנוֹ, אֶל-שַׁעַר עִירָם; וַיְדַבְּרוּ אֶל-אַנְשֵׁי עִירָם, לֵאמֹר.
And Hamor and Shechem his son came to the gate of their city, and spoke to the men of their city, saying,note
21
הָאֲנָשִׁים הָאֵלֶּה שְׁלֵמִים הֵם אִתָּנוּ, וְיֵשְׁבוּ בָאָרֶץ וְיִסְחֲרוּ אֹתָהּ, וְהָאָרֶץ הִנֵּה רַחֲבַת-יָדַיִם, לִפְנֵיהֶם; אֶת-בְּנֹתָם נִקַּח-לָנוּ לְנָשִׁים, וְאֶת-בְּנֹתֵינוּ נִתֵּן לָהֶם.
"These men are at peace with us; let them dwell in the land and trade in it, for the land — look — is wide enough on both hands for them. Their daughters we will take for ourselves as wives, and our daughters we will give to them.note
22
אַךְ-בְּזֹאת יֵאֹתוּ לָנוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים, לָשֶׁבֶת אִתָּנוּ--לִהְיוֹת, לְעַם אֶחָד: בְּהִמּוֹל לָנוּ כָּל-זָכָר, כַּאֲשֶׁר הֵם נִמֹּלִים.
Only on this will the men consent to dwell with us, to become one people: that every male among us be circumcised, as they are circumcised.note
23
מִקְנֵהֶם וְקִנְיָנָם וְכָל-בְּהֶמְתָּם, הֲלוֹא לָנוּ הֵם; אַךְ נֵאוֹתָה לָהֶם, וְיֵשְׁבוּ אִתָּנוּ.
Their livestock and their property and all their beasts — will they not be ours? Only let us consent to them, and they will dwell with us."note
24
וַיִּשְׁמְעוּ אֶל-חֲמוֹר וְאֶל-שְׁכֶם בְּנוֹ, כָּל-יֹצְאֵי שַׁעַר עִירוֹ; וַיִּמֹּלוּ, כָּל-זָכָר--כָּל-יֹצְאֵי, שַׁעַר עִירוֹ.
And all who went out of the gate of his city listened to Hamor and to Shechem his son; and every male was circumcised, all who went out of the gate of his city.note
25
וַיְהִי בַיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי בִּהְיוֹתָם כֹּאֲבִים, וַיִּקְחוּ שְׁנֵי-בְנֵי-יַעֲקֹב שִׁמְעוֹן וְלֵוִי אֲחֵי דִינָה אִישׁ חַרְבּוֹ, וַיָּבֹאוּ עַל-הָעִיר, בֶּטַח; וַיַּהַרְגוּ, כָּל-זָכָר.
And it happened on the third day, when they were in pain, that two of the sons of Jacob, Simeon and Levi, Dinah's brothers, each took his sword, and came upon the city unopposed, and killed every male.note
26
וְאֶת-חֲמוֹר וְאֶת-שְׁכֶם בְּנוֹ, הָרְגוּ לְפִי-חָרֶב; וַיִּקְחוּ אֶת-דִּינָה מִבֵּית שְׁכֶם, וַיֵּצֵאוּ.
And Hamor and Shechem his son they killed by the edge of the sword; and they took Dinah from Shechem's house, and went out.note
27
בְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב, בָּאוּ עַל-הַחֲלָלִים, וַיָּבֹזּוּ, הָעִיר--אֲשֶׁר טִמְּאוּ, אֲחוֹתָם.
The sons of Jacob came upon the slain and plundered the city, because they had defiled their sister.note
28
אֶת-צֹאנָם וְאֶת-בְּקָרָם, וְאֶת-חֲמֹרֵיהֶם, וְאֵת אֲשֶׁר-בָּעִיר וְאֶת-אֲשֶׁר בַּשָּׂדֶה, לָקָחוּ.
Their flocks and their herds and their donkeys, and whatever was in the city and whatever was in the field, they took.note
29
וְאֶת-כָּל-חֵילָם וְאֶת-כָּל-טַפָּם וְאֶת-נְשֵׁיהֶם, שָׁבוּ וַיָּבֹזּוּ; וְאֵת, כָּל-אֲשֶׁר בַּבָּיִת.
And all their wealth, and all their little ones and their wives, they captured and plundered — everything that was in the houses.note
30
וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֶל-שִׁמְעוֹן וְאֶל-לֵוִי, עֲכַרְתֶּם אֹתִי, לְהַבְאִישֵׁנִי בְּיֹשֵׁב הָאָרֶץ, בַּכְּנַעֲנִי וּבַפְּרִזִּי; וַאֲנִי, מְתֵי מִסְפָּר, וְנֶאֶסְפוּ עָלַי וְהִכּוּנִי, וְנִשְׁמַדְתִּי אֲנִי וּבֵיתִי.
And Jacob said to Simeon and to Levi, "You have brought trouble on me, making me a stench to the inhabitants of the land, the Canaanites and the Perizzites; and I am few in number — they will gather against me and strike me, and I will be destroyed, I and my house."note
31
וַיֹּאמְרוּ: הַכְזוֹנָה, יַעֲשֶׂה אֶת-אֲחוֹתֵנוּ. {פ}
But they said, "Should he treat our sister like a prostitute?"note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

The dark chapter at Shechem: the violation of Dinah, the brothers' deceit, and a massacre the text refuses to either bless or excuse. It is told plainly and unsparingly, and these notes try to do the same — naming the crime a crime, and the revenge a revenge. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–4 · וַיְעַנֶּהָ vay'anneha — "and he violated her"

The verb the text will not soften. Dinah "goes out" to see the women of the land, and the sentence turns brutal in three quick verbs: Shechem "saw her, and took her, and lay with her, and violated her."
