That last word is anah (the intensive innah) — to force, degrade, break down; the same verb the law uses for the rape of an unbetrothed woman (Deuteronomy 22:29) and that names what Amnon does to Tamar (2 Samuel 13). The shelf hears its weight unevenly: the NWT and NIV render it "violated," the Douay "by force," while the KJV and ASV settle for "humbled" — a genteel word for a crime, and one this translation declines. There is also a grammar of force in the plain Hebrew: it says he "lay her" (vayishkav otah, direct object), not the ordinary "lay with her" — the accusative itself reads like something done to her, not with her.

And then the whiplash. The very next verse says "his soul clung to Dinah… and he loved the girl, and spoke to her heart" — dibber al-lev, the tender idiom of comfort and courtship (Boaz will "speak to the heart" of Ruth). The text sets the violation and the "love" side by side without a word of comment, and the horror of that juxtaposition is the point: the man who has just wrecked her now wants to keep her, and sends his father to "get me this girl" as though she were a purchase. The narrator names her four times — Dinah, the girl, the girl again — and never once lets Shechem's tenderness stand in for her voice. She does not speak in this chapter. That silence is itself the wound.

Verses 5–12 · נְבָלָה nevalah — "an outrage in Israel"

Jacob's first silence. Word reaches the father — "Jacob heard that he had defiled (timme) his daughter" — and Jacob does nothing: "he held his peace (hecherish) until they came." It is the first of two silences that frame the chapter, and it will matter that the man renamed Israel, who wrestled God and prevailed, cannot find a word for his own daughter's ruin. The sons, out in the field, are the ones who feel it — "the men were grieved, and they burned with anger," for Shechem had done a nevalah in Israel.

The Bible's word for the unspeakable. Nevalah is not ordinary wrong; it is the fixed, heavy term for an atrocity that tears the fabric of a people — the same word for the gang-rape of the Levite's concubine that starts a civil war (Judges 19–20) and for what Amnon does to Tamar ("no such nevalah is done in Israel," 2 Samuel 13:12). The KJV flattens it to "folly," and the ASV follows — far too mild; the NIV "disgraceful thing" and the NWT "disgraceful folly" reach a little higher, but the force is closer to outrage, a thing that "should not be done." And note the phrase "in Israel": Jacob was renamed only two chapters ago, and here the new name has its first use as a people — a moral boundary — and it lands, of all places, on a crime. The nation is barely named before it must reckon with an atrocity inside its own tents.

Two offers. Hamor comes proposing a merger — intermarry, dwell, trade, "take holdings" — the language of assimilation that would quietly dissolve Abraham's set-apart line into the Canaanite towns. Shechem himself is more frantic and more honest: "let me find favor… pile on me a very great bride-price (mohar) and gift — only give me the girl." He will pay any price; the one thing neither he nor his father says is sorry.

Verses 13–24 · בְּמִרְמָה b'mirmah — "with deceit," the family trade

The word that runs in the blood. "The sons of Jacob answered… with deceit (b'mirmah)." Of all the words the narrator could have chosen, this is the one that indicts the whole family, because it is Jacob's own. His father Isaac said it of him: "your brother came with deceit (mirmah) and took your blessing" (27:35). Laban paid it back on him — the wrong sister in the dark — and Jacob's cry was the same word: "why have you deceived me?" (29:25). Now the deceiver's sons deceive in their turn, and one day they will deceive Jacob himself with a bloodied coat (ch. 37). The trait breeds true. The KJV renders it "deceitfully" (elsewhere "subtilty"), the NWT "with deception," the ASV "with guile" — all agreed on the ugly thing: this was a lie from the first word.

The covenant sign, made a weapon. And notice what they weaponize. Their "condition" is circumcision — the sign God gave Abraham as the everlasting mark of the covenant (ch. 17). The brothers dress a massacre in the language of faith ("we cannot give our sister to a man who is uncircumcised… a disgrace to us," cherpah), knowing full well that the third day after the surgery every man in town will be helpless. The holiest sign in their inheritance is turned into a surgeon's ambush. Whatever one thinks of the revenge, this detail the text does not let us admire.

Sold at the gate. Hamor and Shechem carry the deal to their townsmen, and here the mask slips: they do not sell it with love but with greed — "their livestock and their property and all their beasts — will they not be ours?" (v. 23). Each side is working the other; the beautiful phrase "become one people" is, on both lips, a swindle. And a quiet, bitter echo hangs over it: Jacob had just come "in peace" (shalem, 33:18) to this very city and bought his field "from the sons of Hamor" (33:19); the townsmen now call his people "peaceable" (shlemim, v. 21) — the same root — the last word anyone will be able to use of what happens next.

Verses 25–29 · the third day — Simeon and Levi

Two men, a whole town. "On the third day, when they were in pain," two of Jacob's sons — Simeon and Levi, "Dinah's brothers" — take each his sword and fall on the city "unopposed" and kill every male. The Hebrew betach carries the chill: it means "in security," and the versions split over whose — the KJV "came upon the city boldly" (the brothers' confidence), the ASV and NWT "unawares / while it felt secure" (the town's false safety). Both are true at once: a city that thought itself at peace, cut down by men who knew it could not fight back. They kill Hamor and Shechem "by the edge of the sword" (literally "the mouth of the sword," l'fi-charev), take Dinah out of the house, and go.

The narrator says nothing — and that is the verdict withheld. Then the rest of the sons come to plunder: flocks, herds, donkeys, wealth, and "all their little ones and their wives… they captured" — the women and children of the murdered men carried off, the "one people" now spoils. The report is flat, itemized, without a single adjective of horror or of praise. The scale is the argument the text lets stand on its own: one man committed the crime, and a whole city of men is slaughtered and its families enslaved for it. The narrator does not tell us what to feel. He does not have to.

Verses 30–31 · the rebuke that never names the wrong, and the question no one answers

Jacob's second silence. The father finally speaks — and every word is about himself. "You have brought trouble (achartem) on me, making me a stench to the inhabitants of the land… I am few in number; they will gather against me, and I will be destroyed, I and my house." Not one syllable for Dinah, for the dead, for right or wrong — only the danger to Jacob. The KJV "ye have troubled me to make me to stink," the NIV "made me obnoxious," the NWT "made me a stench" — the picture is a man worried about his reputation and his skin. His silence at the start was a father's paralysis; his silence about the morality, here at the end, is harder to forgive.

The last word is a question. "But they said, 'Should he treat our sister like a prostitute?'" — and the chapter simply stops. No answer from Jacob, none from the narrator. Both sides stand indicted and neither is acquitted: the rape was real and monstrous; the revenge was mass murder of the innocent and the enslavement of their families. The text holds the two in an unresolved grip — and leaves the reader in it too. This has always divided its readers: some hear the brothers as the only ones who acted, avengers of a sister nobody else defended; others hear cold, disproportionate slaughter cloaked in covenant language. The translation's job is to let both readings stand where the Hebrew leaves them — and to note that the Bible itself does not, in the end, stay neutral. The verdict only comes a lifetime later, at Jacob's deathbed, and it comes down against the brothers: "Simeon and Levi are brothers; weapons of violence are their swords… cursed be their anger, for it is fierce" (49:5-7). For that day's work the two tribes are scattered in Israel — Levi given no land of his own, Simeon absorbed into Judah. The question Dinah's brothers throw down here is answered, at last, by their father's dying mouth.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / unsoftened: "violated" for anah (not the KJV/ASV "humbled"); "an outrage in Israel" for nevalah (not the KJV's thin "folly"); "with deceit" for b'mirmah; "bride-price" for mohar; "at peace / peaceable" for shlemim (holding the echo of shalem); the covenant-sign named plainly; and Jacob's rebuke left as self-interested as the Hebrew makes it.

Echoes paid. Mirmah — the family's signature — passed from Jacob's theft of the blessing (27:35) through Laban's trick (29:25) to the sons here; Shechem, the town Jacob had just entered "in peace" and where he bought a field from Hamor's sons (33:18-19), destroyed by his own; shalem → shlemim, the "peace" of the arrival turned bitter at the gate; the covenant circumcision (ch. 17) made a weapon; and the new name Israel (ch. 32) getting its first collective use as the measure of an atrocity. Echoes planted: Jacob's silence and the brothers' swords, waiting for the deathbed curse of Simeon and Levi (49:5-7); Levi the avenger, whose descendants will become the priesthood; and the blood at Shechem that sends the family fleeing to Bethel next — where "the terror of God" falls on the cities around so that none pursue (35:5).

Next installment in this book: Genesis 35 — God calls Jacob back to Bethel to keep the vow he made fleeing; the foreign gods buried under the oak; the deaths of Deborah and, in childbirth, Rachel, naming Benjamin with her last breath; and old Isaac laid to rest by his two sons together.

Genesis 35 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים אֶל-יַעֲקֹב, קוּם עֲלֵה בֵית-אֵל וְשֶׁב-שָׁם; וַעֲשֵׂה-שָׁם מִזְבֵּחַ--לָאֵל הַנִּרְאֶה אֵלֶיךָ, בְּבָרְחֲךָ מִפְּנֵי עֵשָׂו אָחִיךָ.
And God said to Jacob, "Arise, go up to Bethel and dwell there; and make there an altar to the God who appeared to you when you fled from the face of Esau your brother."note
2
וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֶל-בֵּיתוֹ, וְאֶל כָּל-אֲשֶׁר עִמּוֹ: הָסִרוּ אֶת-אֱלֹהֵי הַנֵּכָר, אֲשֶׁר בְּתֹכְכֶם, וְהִטַּהֲרוּ, וְהַחֲלִיפוּ שִׂמְלֹתֵיכֶם.
And Jacob said to his household and to all who were with him, "Put away the foreign gods that are among you, and purify yourselves, and change your garments;note
3
וְנָקוּמָה וְנַעֲלֶה, בֵּית-אֵל; וְאֶעֱשֶׂה-שָּׁם מִזְבֵּחַ, לָאֵל הָעֹנֶה אֹתִי בְּיוֹם צָרָתִי, וַיְהִי עִמָּדִי, בַּדֶּרֶךְ אֲשֶׁר הָלָכְתִּי.
and let us arise and go up to Bethel, and I will make there an altar to the God who answered me in the day of my distress and was with me on the way that I went."note
4
וַיִּתְּנוּ אֶל-יַעֲקֹב, אֵת כָּל-אֱלֹהֵי הַנֵּכָר אֲשֶׁר בְּיָדָם, וְאֶת-הַנְּזָמִים, אֲשֶׁר בְּאָזְנֵיהֶם; וַיִּטְמֹן אֹתָם יַעֲקֹב, תַּחַת הָאֵלָה אֲשֶׁר עִם-שְׁכֶם.
And they gave to Jacob all the foreign gods that were in their hand, and the rings that were in their ears; and Jacob buried them under the terebinth that was by Shechem.note
5
וַיִּסָּעוּ; וַיְהִי חִתַּת אֱלֹהִים, עַל-הֶעָרִים אֲשֶׁר סְבִיבוֹתֵיהֶם, וְלֹא רָדְפוּ, אַחֲרֵי בְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב.
And they journeyed; and a terror of God was on the cities that were around them, and they did not pursue the sons of Jacob.note
6
וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב לוּזָה, אֲשֶׁר בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן--הִוא, בֵּית-אֵל: הוּא, וְכָל-הָעָם אֲשֶׁר-עִמּוֹ.
And Jacob came to Luz, which is in the land of Canaan — that is, Bethel — he and all the people who were with him.note
7
וַיִּבֶן שָׁם, מִזְבֵּחַ, וַיִּקְרָא לַמָּקוֹם, אֵל בֵּית-אֵל: כִּי שָׁם, נִגְלוּ אֵלָיו הָאֱלֹהִים, בְּבָרְחוֹ, מִפְּנֵי אָחִיו.
And he built there an altar, and called the place El-Bethel, because there God had revealed himself to him when he fled from the face of his brother.note
8
וַתָּמָת דְּבֹרָה מֵינֶקֶת רִבְקָה, וַתִּקָּבֵר מִתַּחַת לְבֵית-אֵל תַּחַת הָאַלּוֹן; וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ, אַלּוֹן בָּכוּת. {פ}
And Deborah, Rebekah's nurse, died, and she was buried below Bethel under the oak; and its name was called Allon-bacuth.note
9
וַיֵּרָא אֱלֹהִים אֶל-יַעֲקֹב עוֹד, בְּבֹאוֹ מִפַּדַּן אֲרָם; וַיְבָרֶךְ, אֹתוֹ.
And God appeared to Jacob again, when he came from Paddan-aram, and blessed him.note
10
וַיֹּאמֶר-לוֹ אֱלֹהִים, שִׁמְךָ יַעֲקֹב: לֹא-יִקָּרֵא שִׁמְךָ עוֹד יַעֲקֹב, כִּי אִם-יִשְׂרָאֵל יִהְיֶה שְׁמֶךָ, וַיִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמוֹ, יִשְׂרָאֵל.
And God said to him, "Your name is Jacob; your name shall no longer be called Jacob, but Israel shall be your name." And he called his name Israel.note
11
וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ אֱלֹהִים אֲנִי אֵל שַׁדַּי, פְּרֵה וּרְבֵה--גּוֹי וּקְהַל גּוֹיִם, יִהְיֶה מִמֶּךָּ; וּמְלָכִים, מֵחֲלָצֶיךָ יֵצֵאוּ.
And God said to him, "I am El Shaddai. Be fruitful and multiply; a nation and a company of nations shall come from you, and kings shall come out of your loins.note
12
וְאֶת-הָאָרֶץ, אֲשֶׁר נָתַתִּי לְאַבְרָהָם וּלְיִצְחָק--לְךָ אֶתְּנֶנָּה; וּלְזַרְעֲךָ אַחֲרֶיךָ, אֶתֵּן אֶת-הָאָרֶץ.
And the land that I gave to Abraham and to Isaac, to you I will give it; and to your seed after you I will give the land."note
13
וַיַּעַל מֵעָלָיו, אֱלֹהִים, בַּמָּקוֹם, אֲשֶׁר-דִּבֶּר אִתּוֹ.
And God went up from him, in the place where he had spoken with him.note
14
וַיַּצֵּב יַעֲקֹב מַצֵּבָה, בַּמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר-דִּבֶּר אִתּוֹ--מַצֶּבֶת אָבֶן; וַיַּסֵּךְ עָלֶיהָ נֶסֶךְ, וַיִּצֹק עָלֶיהָ שָׁמֶן.
And Jacob set up a pillar in the place where he had spoken with him, a pillar of stone; and he poured out a drink-offering on it, and poured oil on it.note
15
וַיִּקְרָא יַעֲקֹב אֶת-שֵׁם הַמָּקוֹם, אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר אִתּוֹ שָׁם אֱלֹהִים--בֵּית-אֵל.
And Jacob called the name of the place where God had spoken with him, Bethel.note
16
וַיִּסְעוּ מִבֵּית אֵל, וַיְהִי-עוֹד כִּבְרַת-הָאָרֶץ לָבוֹא אֶפְרָתָה; וַתֵּלֶד רָחֵל, וַתְּקַשׁ בְּלִדְתָּהּ.
And they journeyed from Bethel; and there was still some distance to come to Ephrath, when Rachel gave birth, and her labor was hard.note
17
וַיְהִי בְהַקְשֹׁתָהּ, בְּלִדְתָּהּ; וַתֹּאמֶר לָהּ הַמְיַלֶּדֶת אַל-תִּירְאִי, כִּי-גַם-זֶה לָךְ בֵּן.
And it happened, when her labor was hard, that the midwife said to her, "Do not fear, for this too is a son for you."note
18
וַיְהִי בְּצֵאת נַפְשָׁהּ, כִּי מֵתָה, וַתִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ, בֶּן-אוֹנִי; וְאָבִיו, קָרָא-לוֹ בִנְיָמִין.
And it happened, as her soul was departing — for she died — that she called his name Ben-oni; but his father called him Benjamin.note
19
וַתָּמָת, רָחֵל; וַתִּקָּבֵר בְּדֶרֶךְ אֶפְרָתָה, הִוא בֵּית לָחֶם.
And Rachel died, and was buried on the way to Ephrath — that is, Bethlehem.note
20
וַיַּצֵּב יַעֲקֹב מַצֵּבָה, עַל-קְבֻרָתָהּ--הִוא מַצֶּבֶת קְבֻרַת-רָחֵל, עַד-הַיּוֹם.
And Jacob set up a pillar on her grave; that is the pillar of Rachel's grave to this day.note
21
וַיִּסַּע, יִשְׂרָאֵל; וַיֵּט אָהֳלֹה, מֵהָלְאָה לְמִגְדַּל-עֵדֶר.
And Israel journeyed, and pitched his tent beyond Migdal-eder.note
22
וַיְהִי, בִּשְׁכֹּן יִשְׂרָאֵל בָּאָרֶץ הַהִוא, וַיֵּלֶךְ רְאוּבֵן וַיִּשְׁכַּב אֶת-בִּלְהָה פִּילֶגֶשׁ אָבִיו, וַיִּשְׁמַע יִשְׂרָאֵל; {פ} וַיִּהְיוּ בְנֵי-יַעֲקֹב, שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר.
And it happened, while Israel dwelt in that land, that Reuben went and lay with Bilhah his father's concubine; and Israel heard. Now the sons of Jacob were twelve:note
23
בְּנֵי לֵאָה, בְּכוֹר יַעֲקֹב רְאוּבֵן; וְשִׁמְעוֹן וְלֵוִי וִיהוּדָה, וְיִשָּׂשכָר וּזְבֻלוּן.
the sons of Leah: Reuben, Jacob's firstborn, and Simeon and Levi and Judah and Issachar and Zebulun;note
24
בְּנֵי רָחֵל, יוֹסֵף וּבִנְיָמִן.
the sons of Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin;note
25
וּבְנֵי בִלְהָה שִׁפְחַת רָחֵל, דָּן וְנַפְתָּלִי.
and the sons of Bilhah, Rachel's maidservant: Dan and Naphtali;note
26
וּבְנֵי זִלְפָּה שִׁפְחַת לֵאָה, גָּד וְאָשֵׁר; אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב, אֲשֶׁר יֻלַּד-לוֹ בְּפַדַּן אֲרָם.
and the sons of Zilpah, Leah's maidservant: Gad and Asher. These are the sons of Jacob who were born to him in Paddan-aram.note
27
וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב אֶל-יִצְחָק אָבִיו, מַמְרֵא קִרְיַת הָאַרְבַּע--הִוא חֶבְרוֹן, אֲשֶׁר-גָּר-שָׁם אַבְרָהָם וְיִצְחָק.
And Jacob came to Isaac his father at Mamre, at Kiriath-arba — that is, Hebron — where Abraham and Isaac had sojourned.note
28
וַיִּהְיוּ, יְמֵי יִצְחָק--מְאַת שָׁנָה, וּשְׁמֹנִים שָׁנָה.
And the days of Isaac were a hundred and eighty years.note
29
וַיִּגְוַע יִצְחָק וַיָּמָת וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל-עַמָּיו, זָקֵן וּשְׂבַע יָמִים; וַיִּקְבְּרוּ אֹתוֹ, עֵשָׂו וְיַעֲקֹב בָּנָיו. {פ}
And Isaac expired and died, and was gathered to his people, old and full of days; and Esau and Jacob his sons buried him.note

Translator's Notes — verse by verse

Jacob keeps the vow he made fleeing this land — back to Bethel, the foreign gods buried on the way — and God seals his new name a second time. Then the road exacts its price: Deborah's oak, Rachel's grave at Bethlehem, and old Isaac laid to rest by the two brothers together. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–8 · the vow comes due — the gods buried, and a terror of God

Twenty years, and the stone at Bethel is remembered. "Arise, go up to Bethel and dwell there" — God calls Jacob back to the exact place where, a fugitive with a stone for a pillow, he had dreamed the stairway and made his first vow: "if God will be with me… and I return in peace… then Jehovah shall be my God, and this stone shall be God's house, and of all you give me I will give a tenth" (28:20-22). He has returned, in peace and with a limp; now the vow is called due, and he goes to keep it. It is the whole arc of his exile closing on itself.

A house cleansed before it meets God. First he purges his own camp: "Put away the foreign gods (elohei nekhar)… purify yourselves, and change your garments" — the earliest form of the consecration Israel will make at Sinai ("wash your garments," Exodus 19:10-14). Among those household idols, surely, are the teraphim Rachel stole from Laban (31:19) and hid so cleverly; the text left their fate hanging, and here they are given up. Jacob buries them "under the terebinth by Shechem" — the very town his sons had just drenched in blood (ch. 34), now a grave for dead gods. The KJV "strange gods," the ASV and NWT "foreign gods."

Protected by fear, not by the sword. Then the family that expects reprisal for Shechem is met by something else: "a terror of God (chittat elohim) was on the cities around, and they did not pursue the sons of Jacob." The safety Simeon and Levi thought they had won with swords is given instead as a gift — a quiet verdict on their method. Jacob builds his altar and names it El-Bethel, "the God of Bethel" — not just the place but its God, now owned. And a tender footnote closes the scene: Deborah, Rebekah's old nurse (unnamed when she left home with the young bride, 24:59), dies and is buried under an oak called Allon-bacuth, "the oak of weeping." Rebekah herself gets no death-scene in Genesis; her nurse gets a named tree.

Verses 9–15 · Israel, again — אֵל שַׁדַּי El Shaddai and the pillar

The name sealed a second time. "Your name shall no longer be Jacob, but Israel" — but the reader has heard this before, wrestled out of the dark at Peniel (32:28). Here it is given again, in daylight, by God directly and in blessing at the altar. The two accounts sit side by side; source-critics read them as two traditions of one renaming (this the Priestly telling, the Jabbok the older), and this translation shows the doubling rather than smoothing it away — the new name is sealed twice, once striven for by night and once bestowed by day. What was won by struggle is now confirmed by grace.

El Shaddai, and the creation-blessing aimed at a nation. "I am El Shaddai" — the covenant-name God used with Abraham at the covenant of circumcision (17:1) — "be fruitful and multiply; a nation and a company of nations shall come from you, and kings shall come from your loins." The very words spoken over Adam and Noah (1:28; 9:1) are now narrowed onto one family and stretched forward: not just a nation but nations, and — for the first time — kings, the monarchy foreseen generations before there is one. The KJV renders the name "God Almighty" (following the Greek and Latin); the NWT "God Almighty" as well, footnoting the Hebrew. Jacob answers with worship: a pillar of stone (his second at Bethel, after his stone pillow, 28:18), and over it he pours a drink-offering (nesekh) — the Bible's first — and oil, and names the place Bethel once more.

Verses 16–22 · Rachel's grave — בֶּן-אוֹנִי Ben-oni and Benjamin

The added son, and the price. On the road to Ephrath Rachel goes into hard labor and dies bearing her second son. Long ago she had cried "give me children, or I die" (30:1), and named her first "may he add" (Joseph, 30:24) in hope of another; now the added son comes, and the old plea turns literal — she has her son and she dies. With her last breath she names him Ben-oni — "son of my sorrow" (from on/aven, trouble) — but "his father called him Benjamin," "son of the right hand" (yamin, the hand of strength and favor). He is the only son in Genesis whose name a parent overrides: Jacob will not let the last child of his beloved wife carry "my sorrow" for a lifetime, and turns a dying grief into a name of blessing. The KJV keeps both — "Benoni… Benjamin" — as this translation does; the meanings are the whole point.

Rachel at Bethlehem. She is buried "on the way to Ephrath — that is, Bethlehem," the town's first naming in the Bible, and Jacob sets a pillar that stands "to this day." Centuries on, Jeremiah will hear "Rachel weeping for her children… because they are no more" (Jeremiah 31:15), and Matthew will read that weeping over the children of Bethlehem killed by Herod (Matthew 2:18) — the mother buried by Bethlehem, weeping for Bethlehem's sons. Rachel dies at the door of the town where the Messiah will be born.

A firstborn's grab, and a father's silence. Then, in a single hard line: "Reuben went and lay with Bilhah his father's concubine; and Israel heard." It is more than lust — to take a father's concubine was to reach for a father's authority (as Absalom will later take David's, 2 Samuel 16:22). And Israel, as with Dinah, says nothing here; the scribes even drew a paragraph break in the middle of the verse, a veil across the shameful line. But the silence is not the end of it: at his deathbed Jacob strips Reuben the firstborn of his birthright — "unstable as water, you shall not have the excellency, because you went up to your father's bed" (49:3-4). The verdict only waits.

Verses 22–26 · the twelve, complete

The house assembled. "Now the sons of Jacob were twelve" — and the roster is read out by mother: Leah's six (Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun), Rachel's two (Joseph and Benjamin), Bilhah's two (Dan and Naphtali), Zilpah's two (Gad and Asher). These are the twelve tribes-to-be, named at last in one place. The Hebrew strings them with an unbroken "and… and… and" (the polysyndeton this translation keeps, where the NIV tidies them into a comma list) — a deliberate piling-up, the family counted like treasure. One small seam: the summary calls them all "born in Paddan-aram," though Benjamin was just born in Canaan a few verses back (v. 18) — a rounded-off formula for the household as a whole, not a strict claim about the last son. The full family stands complete on the page for the first time — just before the next chapter but one begins to tear it apart.

Verses 27–29 · Isaac laid to rest — the brothers at the grave

Home at last, and a father buried. Jacob reaches Isaac at Mamre / Hebron — the place of the family's one owned patch of ground, the cave of Machpelah — and Isaac dies at 180, "old and full of days," and is "gathered to his people," the death-formula that quietly reaches past the grave to the fathers. And the last line heals an old wound: "Esau and Jacob his sons buried him" — the twins who fought in the womb and over the birthright and the blessing stand together at the tomb, exactly as Isaac and Ishmael, the heir and the sent-away son, had stood together to bury Abraham (25:9). Genesis buries its patriarchs to the sound of estranged brothers making peace.

An obituary out of order. Isaac does not really die yet — he is 180 here, which means he outlives the sale of Joseph (ch. 37) by a dozen years and more. The narrator gives his death now, early, to close his generation cleanly before the book turns wholly to the next — the same thing it did with Abraham, whose death (25:8) is told before the Jacob-and-Esau story it actually precedes. It is a storyteller's mercy: clear the stage of the old man in peace, before the years of grief over Joseph begin.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "foreign gods" for elohei nekhar; "El-Bethel" left in the Hebrew; "El Shaddai" (dictionary shaddai) rather than the Greek-derived "Almighty"; Ben-oni and Benjamin kept apart so the mother's grief and the father's blessing both sound; "pillar" for matzevah and "drink-offering" for nesekh; and "gathered to his people," the death-formula, unflattened.

Echoes paid. The Bethel vow (28:20-22) kept — Jacob returns, buries the idols, builds the altar; the terror of God protecting the family, exactly as planted at Shechem (ch. 34); the name Israel (32:28) sealed a second time; the second pillar at Bethel (28:18); the buried foreign gods answering Rachel's stolen teraphim (31:19); Rachel's old plea for "another son" (30:24) answered and fatal; and Esau and Jacob at the grave mirroring Isaac and Ishmael burying Abraham (25:9). Echoes planted: Rachel's tomb and "Rachel weeping" (Jeremiah 31:15 → Matthew 2:18); Reuben's sin and the birthright he will lose (49:3-4); the twelve complete, waiting for the coat and Joseph (ch. 37); and Bethlehem, first named here, waiting for its king.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 36 — the book pauses to catalogue Esau: his Canaanite wives, his move to Seir, and the long roll of the clans, chiefs, and kings of Edom "before any king reigned over Israel" — tying off the brother's line before ch. 37 turns to Joseph and the coat that tears the family apart.

Genesis 36 — The Mister Translation

1
וְאֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת עֵשָׂו, הוּא אֱדוֹם.
And these are the generations of Esau — that is, Edom.note
2
עֵשָׂו לָקַח אֶת-נָשָׁיו, מִבְּנוֹת כְּנָעַן: אֶת-עָדָה, בַּת-אֵילוֹן הַחִתִּי, וְאֶת-אָהֳלִיבָמָה בַּת-עֲנָה, בַּת-צִבְעוֹן הַחִוִּי.
Esau took his wives from the daughters of Canaan: Adah the daughter of Elon the Hittite, and Oholibamah the daughter of Anah, the daughter of Zibeon the Hivite,note
3
וְאֶת-בָּשְׂמַת בַּת-יִשְׁמָעֵאל, אֲחוֹת נְבָיוֹת.
and Basemath, Ishmael's daughter, sister of Nebaioth.note
4
וַתֵּלֶד עָדָה לְעֵשָׂו, אֶת-אֱלִיפָז; וּבָשְׂמַת, יָלְדָה אֶת-רְעוּאֵל.
And Adah bore to Esau Eliphaz; and Basemath bore Reuel;note
5
וְאָהֳלִיבָמָה, יָלְדָה, אֶת-יְעוּשׁ וְאֶת-יַעְלָם, וְאֶת-קֹרַח; אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי עֵשָׂו, אֲשֶׁר יֻלְּדוּ-לוֹ בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן.
and Oholibamah bore Jeush and Jalam and Korah. These are the sons of Esau who were born to him in the land of Canaan.note
6
וַיִּקַּח עֵשָׂו אֶת-נָשָׁיו וְאֶת-בָּנָיו וְאֶת-בְּנֹתָיו, וְאֶת-כָּל-נַפְשׁוֹת בֵּיתוֹ, וְאֶת-מִקְנֵהוּ וְאֶת-כָּל-בְּהֶמְתּוֹ וְאֵת כָּל-קִנְיָנוֹ, אֲשֶׁר רָכַשׁ בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן; וַיֵּלֶךְ אֶל-אֶרֶץ, מִפְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב אָחִיו.
And Esau took his wives and his sons and his daughters, and all the persons of his household, and his livestock and all his beasts and all his possessions that he had gathered in the land of Canaan, and went to a land away from the face of his brother Jacob.note
7
כִּי-הָיָה רְכוּשָׁם רָב, מִשֶּׁבֶת יַחְדָּו; וְלֹא יָכְלָה אֶרֶץ מְגוּרֵיהֶם, לָשֵׂאת אֹתָם--מִפְּנֵי, מִקְנֵיהֶם.
For their possessions were too great for them to dwell together, and the land of their sojournings could not bear them because of their livestock.note
8
וַיֵּשֶׁב עֵשָׂו בְּהַר שֵׂעִיר, עֵשָׂו הוּא אֱדוֹם.
And Esau dwelt in the hill country of Seir — Esau is Edom.note
9
וְאֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת עֵשָׂו, אֲבִי אֱדוֹם, בְּהַר, שֵׂעִיר.
And these are the generations of Esau, father of Edom, in the hill country of Seir.note
10
אֵלֶּה, שְׁמוֹת בְּנֵי-עֵשָׂו: אֱלִיפַז, בֶּן-עָדָה אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו, רְעוּאֵל, בֶּן-בָּשְׂמַת אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו.
These are the names of Esau's sons: Eliphaz the son of Adah, Esau's wife; Reuel the son of Basemath, Esau's wife.note
11
וַיִּהְיוּ, בְּנֵי אֱלִיפָז--תֵּימָן אוֹמָר, צְפוֹ וְגַעְתָּם וּקְנַז.
And the sons of Eliphaz were Teman, Omar, Zepho, and Gatam, and Kenaz.note
12
וְתִמְנַע הָיְתָה פִילֶגֶשׁ, לֶאֱלִיפַז בֶּן-עֵשָׂו, וַתֵּלֶד לֶאֱלִיפַז, אֶת-עֲמָלֵק; אֵלֶּה, בְּנֵי עָדָה אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו.
And Timna was concubine to Eliphaz, Esau's son, and she bore to Eliphaz Amalek. These are the sons of Adah, Esau's wife.note
13
וְאֵלֶּה בְּנֵי רְעוּאֵל, נַחַת וָזֶרַח שַׁמָּה וּמִזָּה; אֵלֶּה הָיוּ, בְּנֵי בָשְׂמַת אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו.
And these are the sons of Reuel: Nahath and Zerah, Shammah and Mizzah. These were the sons of Basemath, Esau's wife.note
14
וְאֵלֶּה הָיוּ, בְּנֵי אָהֳלִיבָמָה בַת-עֲנָה בַּת-צִבְעוֹן--אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו; וַתֵּלֶד לְעֵשָׂו, אֶת-יְעוּשׁ וְאֶת-יַעְלָם וְאֶת-קֹרַח.
And these were the sons of Oholibamah the daughter of Anah, the daughter of Zibeon, Esau's wife: she bore to Esau Jeush and Jalam and Korah.note
15
אֵלֶּה, אַלּוּפֵי בְנֵי-עֵשָׂו: בְּנֵי אֱלִיפַז, בְּכוֹר עֵשָׂו--אַלּוּף תֵּימָן אַלּוּף אוֹמָר, אַלּוּף צְפוֹ אַלּוּף קְנַז.
These are the chiefs of the sons of Esau. The sons of Eliphaz, Esau's firstborn: the chief of Teman, the chief of Omar, the chief of Zepho, the chief of Kenaz,note
16
אַלּוּף-קֹרַח אַלּוּף גַּעְתָּם, אַלּוּף עֲמָלֵק; אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי אֱלִיפַז בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם, אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי עָדָה.
the chief of Korah, the chief of Gatam, the chief of Amalek. These are the chiefs of Eliphaz in the land of Edom; these are the sons of Adah.note
17
וְאֵלֶּה, בְּנֵי רְעוּאֵל בֶּן-עֵשָׂו--אַלּוּף נַחַת אַלּוּף זֶרַח, אַלּוּף שַׁמָּה אַלּוּף מִזָּה; אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי רְעוּאֵל, בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם--אֵלֶּה, בְּנֵי בָשְׂמַת אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו.
And these are the sons of Reuel, Esau's son: the chief of Nahath, the chief of Zerah, the chief of Shammah, the chief of Mizzah. These are the chiefs of Reuel in the land of Edom; these are the sons of Basemath, Esau's wife.note
18
וְאֵלֶּה, בְּנֵי אָהֳלִיבָמָה אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו--אַלּוּף יְעוּשׁ אַלּוּף יַעְלָם, אַלּוּף קֹרַח; אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי אָהֳלִיבָמָה, בַּת-עֲנָה--אֵשֶׁת עֵשָׂו.
And these are the sons of Oholibamah, Esau's wife: the chief of Jeush, the chief of Jalam, the chief of Korah. These are the chiefs of Oholibamah the daughter of Anah, Esau's wife.note
19
אֵלֶּה בְנֵי-עֵשָׂו וְאֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵיהֶם, הוּא אֱדוֹם. {ס}
These are the sons of Esau, and these are their chiefs — that is, Edom.note
20
אֵלֶּה בְנֵי-שֵׂעִיר הַחֹרִי, יֹשְׁבֵי הָאָרֶץ: לוֹטָן וְשׁוֹבָל, וְצִבְעוֹן וַעֲנָה.
These are the sons of Seir the Horite, the inhabitants of the land: Lotan and Shobal and Zibeon and Anah,note
21
וְדִשׁוֹן וְאֵצֶר, וְדִישָׁן; אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי הַחֹרִי בְּנֵי שֵׂעִיר, בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם.
and Dishon and Ezer and Dishan. These are the chiefs of the Horites, the sons of Seir, in the land of Edom.note
22
וַיִּהְיוּ בְנֵי-לוֹטָן, חֹרִי וְהֵימָם; וַאֲחוֹת לוֹטָן, תִּמְנָע.
And the sons of Lotan were Hori and Hemam; and Lotan's sister was Timna.note
23
וְאֵלֶּה בְּנֵי שׁוֹבָל, עַלְוָן וּמָנַחַת וְעֵיבָל, שְׁפוֹ, וְאוֹנָם.
And these are the sons of Shobal: Alvan and Manahath and Ebal, Shepho and Onam.note
24
וְאֵלֶּה בְנֵי-צִבְעוֹן, וְאַיָּה וַעֲנָה; הוּא עֲנָה, אֲשֶׁר מָצָא אֶת-הַיֵּמִם בַּמִּדְבָּר, בִּרְעֹתוֹ אֶת-הַחֲמֹרִים, לְצִבְעוֹן אָבִיו.
And these are the sons of Zibeon: Aiah and Anah — this is the Anah who found the hot springs in the wilderness as he pastured the donkeys of Zibeon his father.note
25
וְאֵלֶּה בְנֵי-עֲנָה, דִּשֹׁן; וְאָהֳלִיבָמָה, בַּת-עֲנָה.
And these are the children of Anah: Dishon, and Oholibamah the daughter of Anah.note
26
וְאֵלֶּה, בְּנֵי דִישָׁן--חֶמְדָּן וְאֶשְׁבָּן, וְיִתְרָן וּכְרָן.
And these are the sons of Dishon: Hemdan and Eshban and Ithran and Cheran.note
27
אֵלֶּה, בְּנֵי-אֵצֶר--בִּלְהָן וְזַעֲוָן, וַעֲקָן.
These are the sons of Ezer: Bilhan and Zaavan and Akan.note
28
אֵלֶּה בְנֵי-דִישָׁן, עוּץ וַאֲרָן.
These are the sons of Dishan: Uz and Aran.note
29
אֵלֶּה, אַלּוּפֵי הַחֹרִי: אַלּוּף לוֹטָן אַלּוּף שׁוֹבָל, אַלּוּף צִבְעוֹן אַלּוּף עֲנָה.
These are the chiefs of the Horites: the chief of Lotan, the chief of Shobal, the chief of Zibeon, the chief of Anah,note
30
אַלּוּף דִּשֹׁן אַלּוּף אֵצֶר, אַלּוּף דִּישָׁן; אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי הַחֹרִי לְאַלֻּפֵיהֶם, בְּאֶרֶץ שֵׂעִיר. {פ}
the chief of Dishon, the chief of Ezer, the chief of Dishan. These are the chiefs of the Horites, by their clans, in the land of Seir.note
31
וְאֵלֶּה, הַמְּלָכִים, אֲשֶׁר מָלְכוּ, בְּאֶרֶץ אֱדוֹם--לִפְנֵי מְלָךְ-מֶלֶךְ, לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל.
And these are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom before any king reigned over the sons of Israel.note
32
וַיִּמְלֹךְ בֶּאֱדוֹם, בֶּלַע בֶּן-בְּעוֹר; וְשֵׁם עִירוֹ, דִּנְהָבָה.
Bela the son of Beor reigned in Edom, and the name of his city was Dinhabah.note
33
וַיָּמָת, בָּלַע; וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו, יוֹבָב בֶּן-זֶרַח מִבָּצְרָה.
And Bela died, and Jobab the son of Zerah, of Bozrah, reigned in his place.note
34
וַיָּמָת, יוֹבָב; וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו, חֻשָׁם מֵאֶרֶץ הַתֵּימָנִי.
And Jobab died, and Husham of the land of the Temanites reigned in his place.note
35
וַיָּמָת, חֻשָׁם; וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו הֲדַד בֶּן-בְּדַד, הַמַּכֶּה אֶת-מִדְיָן בִּשְׂדֵה מוֹאָב, וְשֵׁם עִירוֹ, עֲוִית.
And Husham died, and Hadad the son of Bedad, who struck Midian in the field of Moab, reigned in his place; and the name of his city was Avith.note
36
וַיָּמָת, הֲדָד; וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו, שַׂמְלָה מִמַּשְׂרֵקָה.
And Hadad died, and Samlah of Masrekah reigned in his place.note
37
וַיָּמָת, שַׂמְלָה; וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו, שָׁאוּל מֵרְחֹבוֹת הַנָּהָר.
And Samlah died, and Shaul of Rehoboth-on-the-River reigned in his place.note
38
וַיָּמָת, שָׁאוּל; וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו, בַּעַל חָנָן בֶּן-עַכְבּוֹר.
And Shaul died, and Baal-hanan the son of Achbor reigned in his place.note
39
וַיָּמָת, בַּעַל חָנָן בֶּן-עַכְבּוֹר, וַיִּמְלֹךְ תַּחְתָּיו הֲדַר, וְשֵׁם עִירוֹ פָּעוּ; וְשֵׁם אִשְׁתּוֹ מְהֵיטַבְאֵל בַּת-מַטְרֵד, בַּת מֵי זָהָב.
And Baal-hanan the son of Achbor died, and Hadar reigned in his place; and the name of his city was Pau, and his wife's name was Mehetabel the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Me-zahab.note
40
וְאֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹת אַלּוּפֵי עֵשָׂו, לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָם, לִמְקֹמֹתָם, בִּשְׁמֹתָם: אַלּוּף תִּמְנָע אַלּוּף עַלְוָה, אַלּוּף יְתֵת.
And these are the names of the chiefs of Esau, by their clans and their places, by their names: the chief of Timna, the chief of Alvah, the chief of Jetheth,note
41
אַלּוּף אָהֳלִיבָמָה אַלּוּף אֵלָה, אַלּוּף פִּינֹן.
the chief of Oholibamah, the chief of Elah, the chief of Pinon,note
42
אַלּוּף קְנַז אַלּוּף תֵּימָן, אַלּוּף מִבְצָר.
the chief of Kenaz, the chief of Teman, the chief of Mibzar,note
43
אַלּוּף מַגְדִּיאֵל, אַלּוּף עִירָם; אֵלֶּה אַלּוּפֵי אֱדוֹם, לְמֹשְׁבֹתָם בְּאֶרֶץ אֲחֻזָּתָם--הוּא עֵשָׂו, אֲבִי אֱדוֹם. {פ}
the chief of Magdiel, the chief of Iram. These are the chiefs of Edom, by their settlements in the land of their possession. This is Esau, the father of Edom.note

Translator's Notes — grouped for a catalogue

A whole chapter of names: the family, chiefs, and kings of Edom. Genealogy chapters get fewer, wider notes — the shape and the placement are the point, not each name — so these zoom out. Quotations from copyrighted versions (NIV, TLB, NWT) are kept to short phrases for comparison; KJV, Geneva, Douay-Rheims and ASV are public domain.

Verses 1–8 · the other brother's book, and a peaceful parting

Genesis honors the son it did not choose. "These are the generations (toledot) of Esau" — the formula that frames the whole book (the heavens and earth, Adam, Noah, Shem, Isaac…) turns, before it will settle on Jacob and Joseph, to give Esau a full chapter of his own. It is the same courtesy the book paid Ishmael (25:12-18): the rejected son is named, counted, and blessed with a future before the story moves on. God keeps his word to Esau too — "kings shall come from you" was said to Jacob (35:11), but here the promise of nationhood the twins were both born under (25:23) comes true on Esau's side, with chiefs and kings of his own. The catalogue is an act of even-handedness.

Esau is Edom, in Seir. The chapter hammers the three names together — "Esau, that is Edom" (v. 1), "Esau dwelt in Seir, Esau is Edom" (v. 8), and again at the close (vv. 19, 43) — folding the man into the nation (Edom, adom, "red," from the red stew and the ruddy skin) and the land (Seir, sa'ir, "hairy"). And the brothers part in peace: "their possessions were too great for them to dwell together, and the land could not bear them" — word for word the reason Abraham and Lot separated (13:6), except that where their herdsmen quarreled, Esau simply gathers everything and goes "away from the face of his brother Jacob," leaving Canaan to the schemer and taking Seir for himself. The reconciliation of chapter 33 holds; the twins who fought in the womb end as neighbors making room. (The wives' names here differ from the lists at 26:34 and 28:9 — a long-noted crux the ancients explained by second names or variant spellings; the KJV keeps the differences plainly, as this translation does.)

Verses 9–19 · the chiefs — and Amalek, hidden in the roll

"Chiefs," not "dukes." The roll of Esau's sons becomes a roll of chiefs — alufim, from eleph, "a thousand / a clan": the head of a clan, a thousand-chief. The KJV famously renders it "duke" (from the Latin dux by way of the Vulgate) — a medieval European coronet comically out of place in Bronze-Age Seir; the ASV and NIV read "chief," the NWT "sheik," and this translation "chief." The word sketches Edom's shape: not one throne but a federation of clan-heads.

A fuse in the family tree. Buried mid-list is one of the Bible's quietest bombshells: "Timna was concubine to Eliphaz, Esau's son, and she bore Amalek" (v. 12). From Esau's grandson descends the nation that will ambush Israel's faint and straggling on the way out of Egypt (Exodus 17) — the one enemy given the oath "Jehovah will have war with Amalek from generation to generation." Agag the Amalekite king costs Saul his crown (1 Samuel 15), and Haman "the Agagite," who plots to wipe out every Jew in Persia (Esther), is read as the last shoot of this root. The chapter that honors Esau also, in a single verse, plants Israel's oldest enemy.

Verses 20–30 · the people who were there first

An honest record of who was displaced. The chapter turns aside to list "the sons of Seir the Horite, the inhabitants of the land" — the original population of Seir, the cave-people (from chor, "cave") whom Esau's descendants pushed out. Deuteronomy says it without flinching: "the Horites lived in Seir formerly, but the sons of Esau dispossessed them and dwelt in their place" (Deuteronomy 2:12). Genesis does not pretend Edom rose from empty ground — it names the people who were there and were displaced, the same honesty the Bible will later turn on Israel's own conquests.

The word no one can translate. One Horite, Anah, is remembered for a desert discovery: "this is the Anah who found the hot springs (yemim) in the wilderness as he pastured the donkeys" (v. 24). But yemim appears exactly once in the whole Bible, and the versions honestly disagree — the KJV guessed "mules" (from the donkey-herding context), the NIV and most moderns read "hot springs" (from an Aramaic cognate), others simply "water." No one knows; it is a clean little window on how a single rare word can leave every translator guessing. This translation renders "hot springs," with the doubt flagged.

Verses 31–43 · kings before there were kings

A verse that looks past its own moment. "These are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom before any king reigned over the sons of Israel" (v. 31). The line presupposes that Israel would have kings — which the promise had already guaranteed ("kings shall come from your loins," 35:11; 17:6) — and it became a famous test-case in the Middle Ages: the commentator Ibn Ezra hinted, carefully, that a sentence like this reads as though written once Israel had kings (a seed of what later became source-criticism), while traditional readers take it as Mosaic foresight, the promised monarchy spoken of as already sure. This translation lays out the reading and does not rule on the date — the Hebrew alone will not settle whether it is prophecy stated as fact or a later scribe's note.

Kings, but not a dynasty. And the list itself makes a quiet point: eight kings, each "died, and X reigned in his place" — but never "his son." Each comes from a different city (Dinhabah, Bozrah, Avith, Pau…); Edom's kingship was seized or elected, not inherited — the opposite of the enduring, hereditary throne God is promising Israel. Edom gets its kings first, and they do not hold. The chapter closes on the chiefs "by their settlements in the land of their possession," and seals the whole account: "This is Esau, the father of Edom" — the brother honored, catalogued, and closed, so the book is free to turn, in the next chapter, wholly to Joseph.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "chief" for alluf (not the KJV's quaint "duke"); "hot springs" for the one-off yemim, with the crux flagged; "generations" for toledot, the book's structural beam; and "Esau is Edom" left standing as the chapter's refrain.

Echoes paid. The peaceful separation echoing Abraham and Lot (13:6, word for word); the Canaanite wives that had "made life bitter" for Isaac and Rebekah (26:35); the promise of a nation (and now kings) to Esau's side of 25:23; and the reconciliation of chapter 33 quietly holding. Echoes planted: Amalek and the war "from generation to generation" (Exodus 17; 1 Samuel 15; Esther); the Horites dispossessed (Deuteronomy 2:12); and Edom as Israel's shadow-brother all through the prophets (Obadiah is one long oracle against it; "the pride of your heart has deceived you").

Next installment in this book: Genesis 37 — the story comes home to Jacob's house and to Joseph: the coat of many colors, the dreams that make his brothers hate him, the pit at Dothan, and the sale into Egypt — the beginning of the long road down that will save the family and enslave it both.

Genesis 37 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיֵּשֶׁב יַעֲקֹב, בְּאֶרֶץ מְגוּרֵי אָבִיו--בְּאֶרֶץ, כְּנָעַן.
And Jacob settled in the land of his father's sojournings, in the land of Canaan.note
2
אֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת יַעֲקֹב, יוֹסֵף בֶּן-שְׁבַע-עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה הָיָה רֹעֶה אֶת-אֶחָיו בַּצֹּאן, וְהוּא נַעַר אֶת-בְּנֵי בִלְהָה וְאֶת-בְּנֵי זִלְפָּה, נְשֵׁי אָבִיו; וַיָּבֵא יוֹסֵף אֶת-דִּבָּתָם רָעָה, אֶל-אֲבִיהֶם.
These are the generations of Jacob. Joseph, seventeen years old, was shepherding the flock with his brothers — he was a lad, with the sons of Bilhah and the sons of Zilpah, his father's wives — and Joseph brought a bad report of them to their father.note
3
וְיִשְׂרָאֵל, אָהַב אֶת-יוֹסֵף מִכָּל-בָּנָיו--כִּי-בֶן-זְקֻנִים הוּא, לוֹ; וְעָשָׂה לוֹ, כְּתֹנֶת פַּסִּים.
And Israel loved Joseph more than all his sons, for he was a son of his old age to him; and he made him a long ornamented tunic.note
4
וַיִּרְאוּ אֶחָיו, כִּי-אֹתוֹ אָהַב אֲבִיהֶם מִכָּל-אֶחָיו--וַיִּשְׂנְאוּ, אֹתוֹ; וְלֹא יָכְלוּ, דַּבְּרוֹ לְשָׁלֹם.
And his brothers saw that their father loved him more than all his brothers, and they hated him, and they could not speak to him in peace.note
5
וַיַּחֲלֹם יוֹסֵף חֲלוֹם, וַיַּגֵּד לְאֶחָיו; וַיּוֹסִפוּ עוֹד, שְׂנֹא אֹתוֹ.
And Joseph dreamed a dream and told it to his brothers; and they hated him still more.note
6
וַיֹּאמֶר, אֲלֵיהֶם: שִׁמְעוּ-נָא, הַחֲלוֹם הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר חָלָמְתִּי.
And he said to them, "Listen, please, to this dream that I have dreamed.note
7
וְהִנֵּה אֲנַחְנוּ מְאַלְּמִים אֲלֻמִּים, בְּתוֹךְ הַשָּׂדֶה, וְהִנֵּה קָמָה אֲלֻמָּתִי, וְגַם-נִצָּבָה; וְהִנֵּה תְסֻבֶּינָה אֲלֻמֹּתֵיכֶם, וַתִּשְׁתַּחֲוֶיןָ לַאֲלֻמָּתִי.
Look — we were binding sheaves in the middle of the field, and look, my sheaf rose and even stood upright; and look, your sheaves came around and bowed down to my sheaf."note
8
וַיֹּאמְרוּ לוֹ, אֶחָיו, הֲמָלֹךְ תִּמְלֹךְ עָלֵינוּ, אִם-מָשׁוֹל תִּמְשֹׁל בָּנוּ; וַיּוֹסִפוּ עוֹד שְׂנֹא אֹתוֹ, עַל-חֲלֹמֹתָיו וְעַל-דְּבָרָיו.
And his brothers said to him, "Will you really be king over us? Will you really rule over us?" And they hated him still more, for his dreams and for his words.note
9
וַיַּחֲלֹם עוֹד חֲלוֹם אַחֵר, וַיְסַפֵּר אֹתוֹ לְאֶחָיו; וַיֹּאמֶר, הִנֵּה חָלַמְתִּי חֲלוֹם עוֹד, וְהִנֵּה הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ וְהַיָּרֵחַ וְאַחַד עָשָׂר כּוֹכָבִים, מִשְׁתַּחֲוִים לִי.
And he dreamed yet another dream and recounted it to his brothers, and said, "Look, I have dreamed another dream: and look, the sun and the moon and eleven stars were bowing down to me."note
10
וַיְסַפֵּר אֶל-אָבִיו, וְאֶל-אֶחָיו, וַיִּגְעַר-בּוֹ אָבִיו, וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ מָה הַחֲלוֹם הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר חָלָמְתָּ: הֲבוֹא נָבוֹא, אֲנִי וְאִמְּךָ וְאַחֶיךָ, לְהִשְׁתַּחֲוֺת לְךָ, אָרְצָה.
And he recounted it to his father and to his brothers; and his father rebuked him and said to him, "What is this dream that you have dreamed? Shall we really come — I and your mother and your brothers — to bow down to you to the ground?"note
11
וַיְקַנְאוּ-בוֹ, אֶחָיו; וְאָבִיו, שָׁמַר אֶת-הַדָּבָר.
And his brothers were jealous of him; but his father kept the matter.note
12
וַיֵּלְכוּ, אֶחָיו, לִרְעוֹת אֶת-צֹאן אֲבִיהֶם, בִּשְׁכֶם.
And his brothers went to shepherd their father's flock at Shechem.note
13
וַיֹּאמֶר יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל-יוֹסֵף, הֲלוֹא אַחֶיךָ רֹעִים בִּשְׁכֶם--לְכָה, וְאֶשְׁלָחֲךָ אֲלֵיהֶם; וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ, הִנֵּנִי.
And Israel said to Joseph, "Are your brothers not shepherding at Shechem? Come, and I will send you to them." And he said to him, "Here I am."note
14
וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ, לֶךְ-נָא רְאֵה אֶת-שְׁלוֹם אַחֶיךָ וְאֶת-שְׁלוֹם הַצֹּאן, וַהֲשִׁבֵנִי, דָּבָר; וַיִּשְׁלָחֵהוּ מֵעֵמֶק חֶבְרוֹן, וַיָּבֹא שְׁכֶמָה.
And he said to him, "Go now, see whether your brothers are at peace, and whether the flock is, and bring me back word." So he sent him from the valley of Hebron, and he came to Shechem.note
15
וַיִּמְצָאֵהוּ אִישׁ, וְהִנֵּה תֹעֶה בַּשָּׂדֶה; וַיִּשְׁאָלֵהוּ הָאִישׁ לֵאמֹר, מַה-תְּבַקֵּשׁ.
And a man found him, and look — he was wandering in the field; and the man asked him, "What are you looking for?"note
16
וַיֹּאמֶר, אֶת-אַחַי אָנֹכִי מְבַקֵּשׁ; הַגִּידָה-נָּא לִי, אֵיפֹה הֵם רֹעִים.
And he said, "I am looking for my brothers. Tell me, please, where they are shepherding."note
17
וַיֹּאמֶר הָאִישׁ, נָסְעוּ מִזֶּה--כִּי שָׁמַעְתִּי אֹמְרִים, נֵלְכָה דֹּתָיְנָה; וַיֵּלֶךְ יוֹסֵף אַחַר אֶחָיו, וַיִּמְצָאֵם בְּדֹתָן.
And the man said, "They have moved on from here, for I heard them saying, 'Let us go to Dothan.'" And Joseph went after his brothers and found them at Dothan.note
18
וַיִּרְאוּ אֹתוֹ, מֵרָחֹק; וּבְטֶרֶם יִקְרַב אֲלֵיהֶם, וַיִּתְנַכְּלוּ אֹתוֹ לַהֲמִיתוֹ.
And they saw him from far off, and before he came near to them, they schemed against him to put him to death.note
19
וַיֹּאמְרוּ, אִישׁ אֶל-אָחִיו: הִנֵּה, בַּעַל הַחֲלֹמוֹת הַלָּזֶה--בָּא.
And they said, each to his brother, "Look — this master of dreams is coming!note
20
וְעַתָּה לְכוּ וְנַהַרְגֵהוּ, וְנַשְׁלִכֵהוּ בְּאַחַד הַבֹּרוֹת, וְאָמַרְנוּ, חַיָּה רָעָה אֲכָלָתְהוּ; וְנִרְאֶה, מַה-יִּהְיוּ חֲלֹמֹתָיו.
And now, come, let us kill him and throw him into one of the pits, and we will say, 'An evil beast has devoured him.' And we shall see what becomes of his dreams."note
21
וַיִּשְׁמַע רְאוּבֵן, וַיַּצִּלֵהוּ מִיָּדָם; וַיֹּאמֶר, לֹא נַכֶּנּוּ נָפֶשׁ.
And Reuben heard, and rescued him from their hand, and said, "Let us not strike down a life."note
22
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם רְאוּבֵן, אַל-תִּשְׁפְּכוּ-דָם--הַשְׁלִיכוּ אֹתוֹ אֶל-הַבּוֹר הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר בַּמִּדְבָּר, וְיָד אַל-תִּשְׁלְחוּ-בוֹ: לְמַעַן, הַצִּיל אֹתוֹ מִיָּדָם, לַהֲשִׁיבוֹ, אֶל-אָבִיו.
And Reuben said to them, "Do not shed blood! Throw him into this pit that is in the wilderness, but do not lay a hand on him" — so that he might rescue him from their hand, to bring him back to his father.note
23
וַיְהִי, כַּאֲשֶׁר-בָּא יוֹסֵף אֶל-אֶחָיו; וַיַּפְשִׁיטוּ אֶת-יוֹסֵף אֶת-כֻּתָּנְתּוֹ, אֶת-כְּתֹנֶת הַפַּסִּים אֲשֶׁר עָלָיו.
And so it was, when Joseph came to his brothers, that they stripped Joseph of his tunic — the long ornamented tunic that was on him —note
24
וַיִּקָּחֻהוּ--וַיַּשְׁלִכוּ אֹתוֹ, הַבֹּרָה; וְהַבּוֹר רֵק, אֵין בּוֹ מָיִם.
and they took him and threw him into the pit; and the pit was empty — there was no water in it.note
25
וַיֵּשְׁבוּ, לֶאֱכָל-לֶחֶם, וַיִּשְׂאוּ עֵינֵיהֶם וַיִּרְאוּ, וְהִנֵּה אֹרְחַת יִשְׁמְעֵאלִים בָּאָה מִגִּלְעָד; וּגְמַלֵּיהֶם נֹשְׂאִים, נְכֹאת וּצְרִי וָלֹט--הוֹלְכִים, לְהוֹרִיד מִצְרָיְמָה.
And they sat down to eat bread; and they lifted their eyes and looked, and look — a caravan of Ishmaelites coming from Gilead, their camels bearing gum and balm and ladanum, going to bring them down to Egypt.note
26
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה, אֶל-אֶחָיו: מַה-בֶּצַע, כִּי נַהֲרֹג אֶת-אָחִינוּ, וְכִסִּינוּ, אֶת-דָּמוֹ.
And Judah said to his brothers, "What gain is it if we kill our brother and cover his blood?note
27
לְכוּ וְנִמְכְּרֶנּוּ לַיִּשְׁמְעֵאלִים, וְיָדֵנוּ אַל-תְּהִי-בוֹ, כִּי-אָחִינוּ בְשָׂרֵנוּ, הוּא; וַיִּשְׁמְעוּ, אֶחָיו.
Come, let us sell him to the Ishmaelites, and let our hand not be on him — for he is our brother, our flesh." And his brothers listened.note
28
וַיַּעַבְרוּ אֲנָשִׁים מִדְיָנִים סֹחֲרִים, וַיִּמְשְׁכוּ וַיַּעֲלוּ אֶת-יוֹסֵף מִן-הַבּוֹר, וַיִּמְכְּרוּ אֶת-יוֹסֵף לַיִּשְׁמְעֵאלִים, בְּעֶשְׂרִים כָּסֶף; וַיָּבִיאוּ אֶת-יוֹסֵף, מִצְרָיְמָה.
And Midianite men, traders, passed by; and they drew Joseph up and lifted him out of the pit, and sold Joseph to the Ishmaelites for twenty pieces of silver. And they brought Joseph to Egypt.note
29
וַיָּשָׁב רְאוּבֵן אֶל-הַבּוֹר, וְהִנֵּה אֵין-יוֹסֵף בַּבּוֹר; וַיִּקְרַע, אֶת-בְּגָדָיו.
And Reuben returned to the pit, and look — Joseph was not in the pit! And he tore his clothes.note
30
וַיָּשָׁב אֶל-אֶחָיו, וַיֹּאמַר: הַיֶּלֶד אֵינֶנּוּ, וַאֲנִי אָנָה אֲנִי-בָא.
And he returned to his brothers and said, "The boy is not there! And I — where can I go?"note
31
וַיִּקְחוּ, אֶת-כְּתֹנֶת יוֹסֵף; וַיִּשְׁחֲטוּ שְׂעִיר עִזִּים, וַיִּטְבְּלוּ אֶת-הַכֻּתֹּנֶת בַּדָּם.
And they took Joseph's tunic, and slaughtered a male goat, and dipped the tunic in the blood.note
32
וַיְשַׁלְּחוּ אֶת-כְּתֹנֶת הַפַּסִּים, וַיָּבִיאוּ אֶל-אֲבִיהֶם, וַיֹּאמְרוּ, זֹאת מָצָאנוּ: הַכֶּר-נָא, הַכְּתֹנֶת בִּנְךָ הִוא--אִם-לֹא.
And they sent the long ornamented tunic and brought it to their father and said, "This we found. Recognize, please — is it your son's tunic, or not?"note
33
וַיַּכִּירָהּ וַיֹּאמֶר כְּתֹנֶת בְּנִי, חַיָּה רָעָה אֲכָלָתְהוּ; טָרֹף טֹרַף, יוֹסֵף.
And he recognized it and said, "My son's tunic! An evil beast has devoured him — Joseph is surely torn to pieces!"note
34
וַיִּקְרַע יַעֲקֹב שִׂמְלֹתָיו, וַיָּשֶׂם שַׂק בְּמָתְנָיו; וַיִּתְאַבֵּל עַל-בְּנוֹ, יָמִים רַבִּים.
And Jacob tore his garments and put sackcloth on his loins, and mourned for his son many days.note
35
וַיָּקֻמוּ כָל-בָּנָיו וְכָל-בְּנֹתָיו לְנַחֲמוֹ, וַיְמָאֵן לְהִתְנַחֵם, וַיֹּאמֶר, כִּי-אֵרֵד אֶל-בְּנִי אָבֵל שְׁאֹלָה; וַיֵּבְךְּ אֹתוֹ, אָבִיו.
And all his sons and all his daughters rose to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted, and said, "No — I will go down to my son, mourning, to Sheol." And his father wept for him.note
36
וְהַמְּדָנִים--מָכְרוּ אֹתוֹ, אֶל-מִצְרָיִם: לְפוֹטִיפַר סְרִיס פַּרְעֹה, שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים. {פ}
And the Medanites sold him into Egypt, to Potiphar, a court official of Pharaoh, chief of the executioners. {פ}

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–4 · the generations that are a story, and the tunic

A settler in a land of un-settledness. The chapter opens with a deliberate contrast to the one just closed. Esau "settled" (vayyeshev) in the hill country of Seir — land he owns (36:8). Jacob "settled" too, but in "the land of his father's sojournings" — megurim, the noun from ger, "resident alien." He settles in a land of not-settling. It is the same ache the book has been pressing since Abraham buried Sarah in the only plot he ever owned (23:4): the brother who took a different country has a home; the brother who has the promise has a lease.

A "generations" formula with no genealogy under it. "These are the toledot of Jacob" — and then, instead of the roll of names the formula has produced nine times before, comes a boy of seventeen and a grudge. This is the last of the book's structural headings and the one that breaks its own pattern: Jacob's "generations" are not a list, they are a plot. Genesis has been narrowing all along — clearing the side line before following the chosen one, Ishmael before Isaac, Esau before Jacob — and here the narrowing finishes and the book simply becomes a story, running unbroken now to its last verse.

The report, and a word with a future. Joseph brings his father dibbatam ra'ah — their bad report. Dibbah is not neutral news; it is the word for a whispering campaign, and the Bible uses it of the ten spies who bring back "an evil report of the land" that costs a generation the promise (Numbers 13:32), and of Jeremiah hearing "the whispering of many" from the men plotting his death (Jeremiah 20:10). The text does not say whether the report was true. It says what it did.

The most famous garment in the Bible, and nobody knows what it was. Ketonet passim is two words: a ketonet is a tunic (the same garment God makes for the man and his wife in 3:21), and passim is a puzzle. The KJV's beloved "coat of many colours" is inherited, not translated: the Septuagint guessed poikilos, "many-hued," the Vulgate followed with tunica polymita, "a many-threaded coat," and the Douay — translating that Latin — has "a coat of divers colours." But Hebrew pas elsewhere means the flat of the hand or foot (it is the word in Daniel's court for the palm of a hand that writes on a wall, Daniel 5:5), which yields a tunic reaching to the extremities — long-sleeved and ankle-length, the robe of someone who plainly is not going to be doing any work. The ASV keeps "many colours" in the line but concedes "a long garment with sleeves" in its margin; the NWT reads "a long striped garment"; the NIV has settled on "an ornate robe" and notes the meaning is uncertain; the TLB simply calls it "a brightly colored coat." The one hard clue is that the phrase occurs in only one other story in scripture: it is what Tamar, David's daughter, is wearing — "for so were the virgin daughters of the king robed" — when her brother rapes her and she tears it (2 Samuel 13:18-19). Both garments mark royal favor; both end torn in a story about a violated sibling. This translation reads Mister "a long ornamented tunic" — a garment of rank, with the doubt flagged rather than painted over. What matters to the plot is not its color but its visibility: you can see it coming from far off (v. 18).

The word they could not say. "They could not speak to him in peace" — le-shalom. Most versions smooth it to an adverb (KJV and ASV "speak peaceably unto him"), which is good English and loses the noun. Keep the noun, because the plot turns on it: in eleven verses their father will send Joseph out to inspect their shalom (v. 14). The errand is named with the very word they cannot bring themselves to speak.

Verses 5–11 · two dreams, and a father who says nothing

Told twice, because it is settled. Joseph dreams the same message in two registers — agricultural, then astronomical — and Genesis itself will later supply the rule for reading that: "the dream was doubled to Pharaoh because the thing is established by God, and God is hastening to do it" (41:32). The doubling is not adolescent repetition; it is the text's own signature for certainty. Everything the brothers do to stop these dreams becomes the machinery that fulfills them — they will bow to him, in Egypt, because they sold him there.

"Will you really be king over us?" The brothers answer with two Hebrew infinitive absolutes stacked for scorn — hamalokh timlokh… im-mashol timshol, "reigning will you reign… ruling will you rule?" And their second verb is a loaded one: mashal is the verb God used over the woman in the garden ("he will rule over you," 3:16) and over Cain's crouching sin ("you must rule over it," 4:7). It has been, from the beginning, a word about who is on top of whom — and the brothers spit it back as an accusation. They have understood the dream exactly. That is precisely why they hate it.

The mother who is not there. Jacob's rebuke assumes a cast of three: "I and your mother and your brothers." But Rachel died two chapters ago, on the road to Bethlehem (35:19). Readers have handled the wrinkle in three ways: Rashi took Jacob to be arguing from the impossible ("your mother is dead — so much for your dream"); others read Bilhah, the foster-mother who raised Joseph after Rachel died, as the "mother" meant; others take the sun, moon and eleven stars as a fixed astronomical figure — a complete household, schematic rather than a headcount. The text does not choose, and neither does this translation.

Two reactions, and only one of them is spoken. "His brothers were jealous of him; but his father kept the matter" — shamar et-hadavar, the verb for guarding, watching, keeping a charge. Jacob rebukes the boy out loud and files the dream away in silence. It is the first hint that the father half-believes it. Luke will use the identical gesture, in Greek, of another parent holding a son's inexplicable destiny without comment: "his mother kept all these sayings in her heart" (Luke 2:51). The KJV renders our line "observed the saying," the NIV "kept the matter in mind," the NWT "kept the saying" — all reaching for the same restraint.

Verses 12–17 · sent to Shechem — and a stranger in a field

Of all the places to pasture a flock. The brothers are grazing "at Shechem" — the town where Simeon and Levi killed every male and plundered the city three chapters ago (34:25-29), and after which Jacob said in plain terror, "you have made me a stench to the inhabitants of the land… I am few in number, and they will gather against me and strike me" (34:30). They have gone straight back to the scene. The text offers no comment at all — it simply puts the name there and lets the reader remember. And into that place the father sends his favorite, alone, unarmed.

"Here I am." Joseph's single word of reply is hineni — the answer of total availability that this book has used at exactly one other hinge: the Aqedah, where Abraham says it three times, and where a father takes the beloved son, the son he loves, up a mountain to kill him (22:1, 22:7, 22:11). Here a father sends a beloved son on a journey the reader already knows will end with his clothes soaked in blood and brought back to that father. The difference is the one that matters: at Moriah a voice from heaven stopped the knife. In this chapter heaven says nothing whatever. God is not named once in Genesis 37 — the only chapter of the Joseph story where the narrator lets him disappear entirely, exactly where you would most want him.

The errand. "See whether your brothers are at shalom" — the word from verse 4, now placed in the father's mouth as an innocent question. He is asking the one thing that has already been answered.

The man in the field. Joseph is to'eh — "wandering," straying, lost; it is the participle used of a sheep that has strayed, and of Hagar "wandering in the wilderness of Beersheba" (21:14). And then, for three verses, the story stops for an anonymous stranger. He has no name, no lineage, and no function except to redirect the plot: without his overheard scrap of conversation Joseph goes home, and there is no Egypt, no famine relief, no exodus. Jewish tradition, uneasy with an accident that large, identified him with the angel Gabriel (so Rashi, following the Midrash). The Hebrew says only ish, "a man." This translation leaves him a man, because the plainness is the point: the whole future of the family turns on a passerby who happened to be listening.

Dothan. A real place — Tell Dothan, about thirteen miles north of Shechem, excavated in the 1950s and 60s and occupied continuously through the Bronze and Iron Ages. It sits on the caravan road running from Gilead down toward the coast and Egypt, which is exactly why a spice caravan turns up in verse 25. It is also where Elisha's servant will one day wake to find the hills full of horses and chariots of fire (2 Kings 6:13-17) — the same town, and the other kind of morning.

Verses 18–24 · the plot, the dream-lord, and the empty pit

Scheming, in a family that has done this before. Vayyitnakkelu — "they schemed, dealt craftily" — is a rare and ugly verb. This household has a long record with cunning: Jacob took the blessing be-mirmah, "with deceit" (27:35); Laban paid him back with the same word at the wedding (29:25); Jacob's own sons answered Shechem "with deceit" (34:13). It has passed down like an inheritance, and now it is aimed at the family's own child.

"This master of dreams." Ba'al ha-chalomot ha-lazeh — literally "the owner of the dreams, this one here." Ba'al means possessor or proprietor; ha-lazeh is a demonstrative with a curl of contempt in it, roughly "this specimen." The KJV flattens it to "this dreamer," the NIV to "here comes that dreamer," the Geneva to "beholde, this dreamer commeth" — all readable, all a little too polite. The Hebrew is a sneer at a job title, and this translation keeps it: Mister "this master of dreams."

The lie they invent, and will hear again. "We will say, 'An evil beast has devoured him'" — and thirteen verses later the father says it back to them, word for word, as his own conclusion (v. 33). They do not even have to tell the lie; they only have to leave the coat where it will do the work, and the grieving man supplies the sentence himself. The text sets the two lines close enough that the reader hears the echo and the brothers, standing there, must hear it too.

Reuben's half-rescue. The firstborn intervenes — "let us not strike down a life" (nefesh, the same word as the "living creature" of the creation account) — but he intervenes by compromise: don't kill him, just throw him in a pit, and I will come back for him later. He never says "let him go." He is also, the reader remembers, the son who slept with his father's concubine (35:22) and has ground to make up; the narrator quietly gives us his private motive — "to bring him back to his father" — and then arranges for him to be absent at the one moment it mattered. The rescue attempt that talks instead of acting is one of the most recognizable failures in the book.

"And the pit was empty; there was no water in it." A famously redundant sentence — if it is empty, of course there is no water — and one the tradition has never been able to leave alone. The Talmud draws the grim practical inference: no water, but the reader should not assume nothing was in it (Shabbat 22a). The plainer reading is that the second clause is the narrator making sure we understand what kind of hole this is: a dry cistern, a bottle-shaped cut in the rock, smooth-sided, several men deep, impossible to climb out of. He is not in danger of drowning. He is in a stone jar. And the brothers, verse 25, sit down beside it to have lunch.

Verses 25–30 · what gain? — and the sale nobody can fully untangle

A caravan of cousins. The traders who come over the hill are Ishmaelites — descendants of Ishmael, Abraham's other son, the boy who was also put out into a wilderness by a family that preferred the younger brother (21:14). Their camels carry nekhot, tsori, va-lot — gum tragacanth, balm, and ladanum: resins, the luxury goods of the incense road. This is the freight of the Gilead-to-Egypt trade, and Jacob will one day send the very same three items back down that road as a gift to the Egyptian official he does not know is his son (43:11). The reader is watching a boy leave in a cargo he will later be sent presents in.

"What gain is it?" Judah's opening word is betsa — not neutral profit but the loaded noun for cut, take, unjust gain, the word the prophets use for graft. Jeremiah will aim it at a king of Judah whose "eyes and heart are on nothing but your unjust gain" (Jeremiah 22:17). Judah's proposal is often read as mercy — and it does save Joseph's life, and the brothers do stop short of murder — but the sentence he uses to sell it is an accountant's. He does not say "he is our brother, we cannot"; he says the killing has no margin in it. The KJV catches this exactly with "what profit is it," the NIV with "what will we gain"; the TLB's "what will we gain by killing him" is the same instinct in plainer clothes. Then he adds the pious clause — "for he is our brother, our flesh" — the language of kinship deployed as the closing argument for a sale.

And covering the blood. "…and cover his blood" — the phrase reaches back to the first murder in this book, where blood that was not covered cried out from the ground (4:10). Job says it as a prayer: "O earth, do not cover my blood" (Job 16:18). Judah's plan is precisely to make the ground keep quiet.

The seam this translation does not sand. Verse 28 is the most argued-over sentence in the chapter, and the difficulty is real: Midianite traders pass by; they draw Joseph out of the pit and sell him to the Ishmaelites. Grammatically the nearest subject for "drew" and "sold" is the Midianites, not the brothers — yet Joseph later tells his brothers flatly, "I am Joseph your brother, whom you sold into Egypt" (45:4). Then verse 36 has the Medanites — a third spelling — selling him to Potiphar, while 39:1 says Potiphar bought him "from the hand of the Ishmaelites." Two readings, both serious: (a) the standard source-critical account sees two tellings braided together — one in which Judah persuades his brothers to sell Joseph to Ishmaelites, another in which Reuben plans a rescue and passing Midianites steal the boy out of the pit before the brothers can act, which would make the brothers guilty of the intent and not the transaction. (b) The harmonizing reading treats the two names as overlapping terms for the same caravan traders — and it has one genuinely strong piece of evidence: Judges 8 tells of an army explicitly called Midianites, then explains their gold earrings with "because they were Ishmaelites" (Judges 8:24), using the words interchangeably in a single breath. On that reading the brothers pull Joseph up and sell him to a mixed caravan the text names loosely. The KJV and ASV render the ambiguity as it stands and leave the reader to it; the NIV quietly resolves it by supplying "his brothers pulled Joseph up." This translation keeps the Hebrew's unspecified "they," because the ambiguity is in the text and taking it out is a decision, not a translation.

Twenty pieces of silver. Not an arbitrary figure: Leviticus later fixes the valuation of a male between five and twenty years old at exactly twenty shekels (Leviticus 27:5). Joseph is seventeen. He is sold for the going institutional rate for a boy his age — the price is correct, and that is the horror of it. Readers have long heard the later rhyme in it, a descendant of Judah priced at thirty pieces (Zechariah 11:12; Matthew 26:15); Genesis makes nothing of it, and neither will we.

"And I — where can I go?" Reuben comes back to an empty hole and tears his clothes — the first of the chapter's two garment-tearings, and the reader should note that the second is his father's (v. 34), for the same boy, over a lie Reuben could have stopped. His cry is four Hebrew words and almost unpunctuatable: va-ani anah ani-va, "and I, where am I going?" It is not grief for Joseph. It is the eldest son calculating what this will cost him.

Verses 31–36 · "recognize, please" — the deceiver deceived, and Sheol

A goat, a garment, and a blind old man: it has happened before in this house. This is the chapter's masterstroke, and it is built entirely out of repeated props. To steal his brother's blessing, Jacob took his brother's garments and covered himself in the skins of goat kids, and went in to a father whose eyes were too dim to see (27:15-16). Now his sons take his favorite son's garment, slaughter a male goat, dip the one in the other's blood, and carry it to a father who cannot see what happened. The rabbis put it bluntly (Genesis Rabbah 84:19): with a kid he deceived his father, and with a kid he was deceived. Genesis never says it. It just hands him the goat.

The two most dangerous words in the family's vocabulary. "Haker-na — recognize, please." The verb is nakar, to recognize, identify, acknowledge — and it is the exact verb that failed at the earlier bedside: Isaac "did not recognize him, because his hands were hairy like his brother Esau's hands" (27:23). There the father failed to recognize a son who was present; here the father recognizes a son who is not. And the words come back one chapter later with terrible precision: when Judah — the brother who proposed the sale — is confronted by Tamar with the pledge he left her, she sends it to him with the identical phrase, "haker-na, recognize, please, whose these are" (38:25). The man who sent haker-na to his father receives haker-na from his daughter-in-law. That is why chapter 38, so often dismissed as an interruption of the Joseph story, sits exactly where it sits: it is the book's own comment on this scene.

The lie completes itself. The brothers ask a question and volunteer nothing — "this we found." They never say the word "beast." Jacob does, quoting their invention of verse 20 back at them unprompted, and then adds his own flourish: tarof toraf Yosef, "torn, torn is Joseph," another infinitive absolute — the same doubling-for-certainty the dreams used, now serving a falsehood. He is more certain than anyone in the chapter, and he is wrong.

Sackcloth, first worn here. Saq — coarse dark goat-hair cloth — makes its first appearance in the Bible in this verse, on Jacob, and will be the standard dress of grief and repentance for the whole rest of the canon, from Nineveh's king to the two witnesses of Revelation (Jonah 3:6; Revelation 11:3). The mourning garment enters scripture as a father's response to a lie.

The first Sheol. "I will go down to my son, mourning, to Sheol" — and this is the word's first occurrence in the Bible, which makes how we translate it a decision about a great deal more than one verse. Sheol is the Hebrew realm of the dead: down, dark, silent, and — crucially — where everyone goes, righteous and wicked alike; it is not a place of punishment, and the Hebrew Bible has no other word to set against it. The KJV renders it here "the grave," but renders the same word "hell" in thirty-one other places and "the pit" in three — so an English reader of the KJV cannot tell that Jacob, Job, David and Isaiah are all talking about one thing, and would reasonably conclude that Jacob expects to descend to his son in the grave while the wicked go somewhere else. That is not a small distortion; it is a whole doctrine assembled out of an inconsistent gloss. The Douay, following the Vulgate's infernum, has "into hell" right here — the strangest sentence on the shelf, since it has a patriarch expecting to join his beloved son there. The ASV and the NWT both do the honest thing and simply transliterate — "Sheol" — refusing to decide with a translation what the Hebrew leaves undecided, and this translation follows them on the merits. It is the same principle as "vault" and "side": where the familiar English word imports an idea the Hebrew does not have, keep the Hebrew and explain it.

He refused to be comforted. Vayema'en lehitnachem — from nacham, the verb this book has been turning over since Lamech named his son Noah, "this one will bring us comfort" (5:29), and since God himself was said to nacham, to be sorry that he had made humankind (6:6). Here it is refused. And the refusal has one more life in it: Jeremiah will see Rachel — Joseph's own mother, buried on the Bethlehem road two chapters ago (35:19) — "weeping for her children, refusing to be comforted, because they are not" (Jeremiah 31:15), and Matthew will hear that verse again over the children of Bethlehem (Matthew 2:18). The mother refusing comfort and the father refusing comfort are the same family, in the same words, over the same son's line.

A court official, and a title with a smell of blood. The last verse hands Joseph to Potiphar, seris Par'oh, sar ha-tabbachim. Saris is the court-official word — it can mean a literal eunuch, and often does, though it is also used broadly of any high functionary (Potiphar has a wife, which is why translators divide: the KJV reads "an officer," the NWT "a court official"). Sar ha-tabbachim is stranger: tabbach is a butcher or slaughterer, so the phrase is literally "chief of the slaughterers" — which the versions render "captain of the guard" on the reasonable ground that a royal bodyguard's business is executions. This translation reads Mister "chief of the executioners," because that is what the Aramaic equivalent plainly means when the same office appears in Daniel's Babylon — Arioch, "chief of the executioners," who comes to kill the wise men (Daniel 2:14). And with that title the two stories line up: a Hebrew boy, taken young into a world empire, handed over to the chief official of a foreign king, who will read dreams nobody else can read and rise to stand second in the kingdom. Genesis has just written the opening of Daniel.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "a long ornamented tunic" for ketonet passim, with the coat-of-many-colours pedigree given and the uncertainty flagged rather than painted over; "Sheol" left untranslated at its first appearance in the Bible (with the ASV and NWT, against the KJV's grave/hell/pit shuffle); "speak to him in peace" keeping shalom a noun so verse 14's errand can land on it; "bad report" for dibbah; "this master of dreams" for the brothers' sneer; "what gain" for Judah's betsa; "chief of the executioners" for sar ha-tabbachim; and verse 28's unspecified "they" left unspecified.

Echoes paid. Hineni — "Here I am" — from the Aqedah to another beloved son sent toward death, this time with no voice from heaven; toledot, the book's structural beam, used for the last time and broken open into a story; mashal, "rule," carried from 3:16 and 4:7 to the brothers' sneer; the goat and the garment of 27:15-16 turned back on Jacob; nakar, the verb that failed at Isaac's bedside, succeeding here on a bloody tunic; nacham, the comfort promised at Noah's naming, refused; Shechem, the killing-field of chapter 34, chosen as a pasture; and Judah's betsa answering Jeremiah's indictment of his royal line.

Echoes planted. Haker-na, "recognize, please" — returning in one chapter to trap Judah himself (38:25); the dreams that must be doubled "because the thing is established by God" (41:32); the gum, balm and ladanum going down to Egypt that will come back up as a gift (43:11); the brothers who will bow (42:6); Reuben's forfeited birthright and Judah's scepter (49:3-10); the sackcloth that will clothe every mourner from Nineveh to Revelation; Rachel refusing comfort (Jeremiah 31:15 → Matthew 2:18); and a Hebrew youth in a foreign court reading dreams — Daniel's whole book, sketched in this chapter's last line.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 38 — the story stops dead and turns to Judah: his Canaanite marriage, the deaths of Er and Onan, and Tamar at the roadside in a veil, who takes his seal, his cord and his staff, and sends them back with the two words he taught his father — "recognize, please."

Genesis 38 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיְהִי בָּעֵת הַהִוא, וַיֵּרֶד יְהוּדָה מֵאֵת אֶחָיו; וַיֵּט עַד-אִישׁ עֲדֻלָּמִי, וּשְׁמוֹ חִירָה.
And so it was at that time that Judah went down from his brothers and turned aside to a man of Adullam whose name was Hirah.note
2
וַיַּרְא-שָׁם יְהוּדָה בַּת-אִישׁ כְּנַעֲנִי, וּשְׁמוֹ שׁוּעַ; וַיִּקָּחֶהָ, וַיָּבֹא אֵלֶיהָ.
And Judah saw there the daughter of a Canaanite man whose name was Shua; and he took her and went in to her.note
3
וַתַּהַר, וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן; וַיִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמוֹ, עֵר.
And she conceived and bore a son, and he called his name Er.note
4
וַתַּהַר עוֹד, וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן; וַתִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמוֹ, אוֹנָן.
And she conceived again and bore a son, and she called his name Onan.note
5
וַתֹּסֶף עוֹד וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן, וַתִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמוֹ שֵׁלָה; וְהָיָה בִכְזִיב, בְּלִדְתָּהּ אֹתוֹ.
And she bore yet another son and called his name Shelah; and he was at Chezib when she bore him.note
6
וַיִּקַּח יְהוּדָה אִשָּׁה, לְעֵר בְּכוֹרוֹ; וּשְׁמָהּ, תָּמָר.
And Judah took a wife for Er his firstborn, and her name was Tamar.note
7
וַיְהִי, עֵר בְּכוֹר יְהוּדָה--רַע, בְּעֵינֵי יְהוָה; וַיְמִתֵהוּ, יְהוָה.
And Er, Judah's firstborn, was evil in the eyes of Jehovah, and Jehovah put him to death.note
8
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה לְאוֹנָן, בֹּא אֶל-אֵשֶׁת אָחִיךָ וְיַבֵּם אֹתָהּ; וְהָקֵם זֶרַע, לְאָחִיךָ.
And Judah said to Onan, "Go in to your brother's wife and do the duty of a brother-in-law to her, and raise up offspring for your brother."note
9
וַיֵּדַע אוֹנָן, כִּי לֹּא לוֹ יִהְיֶה הַזָּרַע; וְהָיָה אִם-בָּא אֶל-אֵשֶׁת אָחִיו, וְשִׁחֵת אַרְצָה, לְבִלְתִּי נְתָן-זֶרַע, לְאָחִיו.
And Onan knew that the offspring would not be his; so whenever he went in to his brother's wife, he ruined it on the ground, so as not to give offspring to his brother.note
10
וַיֵּרַע בְּעֵינֵי יְהוָה, אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה; וַיָּמֶת, גַּם-אֹתוֹ.
And what he did was evil in the eyes of Jehovah, and he put him to death also.note
11
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה לְתָמָר כַּלָּתוֹ שְׁבִי אַלְמָנָה בֵית-אָבִיךְ, עַד-יִגְדַּל שֵׁלָה בְנִי--כִּי אָמַר, פֶּן-יָמוּת גַּם-הוּא כְּאֶחָיו; וַתֵּלֶךְ תָּמָר, וַתֵּשֶׁב בֵּית אָבִיהָ.
And Judah said to Tamar his daughter-in-law, "Remain a widow in your father's house until Shelah my son grows up" — for he said, "Otherwise he too will die, like his brothers." And Tamar went and remained in her father's house.note
12
וַיִּרְבּוּ, הַיָּמִים, וַתָּמָת, בַּת-שׁוּעַ אֵשֶׁת-יְהוּדָה; וַיִּנָּחֶם יְהוּדָה, וַיַּעַל עַל-גֹּזְזֵי צֹאנוֹ הוּא וְחִירָה רֵעֵהוּ הָעֲדֻלָּמִי--תִּמְנָתָה.
And the days grew many, and Shua's daughter, Judah's wife, died. And Judah was comforted, and he went up to his sheepshearers — he and his friend Hirah the Adullamite — to Timnah.note
13
וַיֻּגַּד לְתָמָר, לֵאמֹר: הִנֵּה חָמִיךְ עֹלֶה תִמְנָתָה, לָגֹז צֹאנוֹ.
And it was told to Tamar, "Look — your father-in-law is going up to Timnah to shear his sheep."note
14
וַתָּסַר בִּגְדֵי אַלְמְנוּתָהּ מֵעָלֶיהָ, וַתְּכַס בַּצָּעִיף וַתִּתְעַלָּף, וַתֵּשֶׁב בְּפֶתַח עֵינַיִם, אֲשֶׁר עַל-דֶּרֶךְ תִּמְנָתָה: כִּי רָאֲתָה, כִּי-גָדַל שֵׁלָה, וְהִוא, לֹא-נִתְּנָה לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה.
And she took off the garments of her widowhood and covered herself with a veil and wrapped herself, and sat at the entrance of Enaim, which is on the way to Timnah — for she saw that Shelah had grown up, and she had not been given to him as a wife.note
15
וַיִּרְאֶהָ יְהוּדָה, וַיַּחְשְׁבֶהָ לְזוֹנָה: כִּי כִסְּתָה, פָּנֶיהָ.
And Judah saw her and thought her a prostitute, for she had covered her face.note
16
וַיֵּט אֵלֶיהָ אֶל-הַדֶּרֶךְ, וַיֹּאמֶר הָבָה-נָּא אָבוֹא אֵלַיִךְ, כִּי לֹא יָדַע, כִּי כַלָּתוֹ הִוא; וַתֹּאמֶר, מַה-תִּתֶּן-לִי, כִּי תָבוֹא, אֵלָי.
