And he turned aside to her by the way and said, "Come now, let me come in to you" — for he did not know that she was his daughter-in-law. And she said, "What will you give me, that you may come in to me?"note
17
וַיֹּאמֶר, אָנֹכִי אֲשַׁלַּח גְּדִי-עִזִּים מִן-הַצֹּאן; וַתֹּאמֶר, אִם-תִּתֵּן עֵרָבוֹן עַד שָׁלְחֶךָ.
And he said, "I will send a kid of the goats from the flock." And she said, "Only if you give a pledge until you send it."note
18
וַיֹּאמֶר, מָה הָעֵרָבוֹן אֲשֶׁר אֶתֶּן-לָךְ, וַתֹּאמֶר חֹתָמְךָ וּפְתִילֶךָ, וּמַטְּךָ אֲשֶׁר בְּיָדֶךָ; וַיִּתֶּן-לָהּ וַיָּבֹא אֵלֶיהָ, וַתַּהַר לוֹ.
And he said, "What is the pledge that I should give you?" And she said, "Your seal and your cord, and your staff that is in your hand." And he gave them to her and went in to her, and she conceived by him.note
19
וַתָּקָם וַתֵּלֶךְ, וַתָּסַר צְעִיפָהּ מֵעָלֶיהָ; וַתִּלְבַּשׁ, בִּגְדֵי אַלְמְנוּתָהּ.
And she rose and went, and took off her veil, and put on the garments of her widowhood.note
20
וַיִּשְׁלַח יְהוּדָה אֶת-גְּדִי הָעִזִּים, בְּיַד רֵעֵהוּ הָעֲדֻלָּמִי, לָקַחַת הָעֵרָבוֹן, מִיַּד הָאִשָּׁה; וְלֹא, מְצָאָהּ.
And Judah sent the kid of the goats by the hand of his friend the Adullamite, to take back the pledge from the woman's hand; but he did not find her.note
21
וַיִּשְׁאַל אֶת-אַנְשֵׁי מְקֹמָהּ, לֵאמֹר, אַיֵּה הַקְּדֵשָׁה הִוא בָעֵינַיִם, עַל-הַדָּרֶךְ; וַיֹּאמְרוּ, לֹא-הָיְתָה בָזֶה קְדֵשָׁה.
And he asked the men of her place, "Where is the consecrated woman — the one at Enaim, by the way?" And they said, "There has been no consecrated woman here."note
22
וַיָּשָׁב, אֶל-יְהוּדָה, וַיֹּאמֶר, לֹא מְצָאתִיהָ; וְגַם אַנְשֵׁי הַמָּקוֹם אָמְרוּ, לֹא-הָיְתָה בָזֶה קְדֵשָׁה.
And he returned to Judah and said, "I did not find her; and the men of the place also said, 'There has been no consecrated woman here.'"note
23
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה תִּקַּח-לָהּ, פֶּן נִהְיֶה לָבוּז; הִנֵּה שָׁלַחְתִּי הַגְּדִי הַזֶּה, וְאַתָּה לֹא מְצָאתָהּ.
And Judah said, "Let her take them for herself, or we will become a laughingstock. Look — I sent this kid, and you did not find her."note
24
וַיְהִי כְּמִשְׁלֹשׁ חֳדָשִׁים, וַיֻּגַּד לִיהוּדָה לֵאמֹר זָנְתָה תָּמָר כַּלָּתֶךָ, וְגַם הִנֵּה הָרָה, לִזְנוּנִים; וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה, הוֹצִיאוּהָ וְתִשָּׂרֵף.
And it happened about three months later that it was told to Judah, "Tamar your daughter-in-law has played the prostitute — and look, she is even pregnant by prostitution." And Judah said, "Bring her out, and let her be burned."note
25
הִוא מוּצֵאת, וְהִיא שָׁלְחָה אֶל-חָמִיהָ לֵאמֹר, לְאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר-אֵלֶּה לּוֹ, אָנֹכִי הָרָה; וַתֹּאמֶר, הַכֶּר-נָא--לְמִי הַחֹתֶמֶת וְהַפְּתִילִים וְהַמַּטֶּה, הָאֵלֶּה.
She was being brought out, and she sent to her father-in-law, saying, "By the man to whom these belong I am pregnant." And she said, "Recognize, please — whose are these: the seal and the cords and the staff?"note
26
וַיַּכֵּר יְהוּדָה, וַיֹּאמֶר צָדְקָה מִמֶּנִּי, כִּי-עַל-כֵּן לֹא-נְתַתִּיהָ, לְשֵׁלָה בְנִי; וְלֹא-יָסַף עוֹד, לְדַעְתָּהּ.
And Judah recognized them and said, "She is more righteous than I — because I did not give her to Shelah my son." And he did not know her again.note
27
וַיְהִי, בְּעֵת לִדְתָּהּ; וְהִנֵּה תְאוֹמִים, בְּבִטְנָהּ.
And it happened at the time of her giving birth that — look — twins were in her womb.note
28
וַיְהִי בְלִדְתָּהּ, וַיִּתֶּן-יָד; וַתִּקַּח הַמְיַלֶּדֶת, וַתִּקְשֹׁר עַל-יָדוֹ שָׁנִי לֵאמֹר, זֶה, יָצָא רִאשֹׁנָה.
And it happened as she gave birth that one put out a hand; and the midwife took a scarlet thread and tied it on his hand, saying, "This one came out first."note
29
וַיְהִי כְּמֵשִׁיב יָדוֹ, וְהִנֵּה יָצָא אָחִיו, וַתֹּאמֶר, מַה-פָּרַצְתָּ עָלֶיךָ פָּרֶץ; וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ, פָּרֶץ.
And it happened, as he drew back his hand, that — look — his brother came out. And she said, "How you have broken out — a breach upon you!" And he called his name Perez.note
30
וְאַחַר יָצָא אָחִיו, אֲשֶׁר עַל-יָדוֹ הַשָּׁנִי; וַיִּקְרָא שְׁמוֹ, זָרַח. {ס}
And afterward his brother came out, the one with the scarlet thread on his hand; and he called his name Zerah. {ס}note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–11 · the brother who walked away — and two deaths

"At that time" — and the story stops dead. Joseph has just been sold and is on the road to Egypt, and Genesis abruptly drops him to follow Judah for thirty verses. Readers have called this an interruption since antiquity; it is the opposite. Watch what the chapter does to the man who said "what gain is it?" — and note that the very words he used to break his father will be used, here, to break him. Chapter 38 is the book's own comment on chapter 37, and it is placed exactly where the comment belongs.

"Judah went DOWN from his brothers." Vayyered — the same verb, in the same breath of narrative, that is carrying Joseph down to Egypt. Two brothers go down in these two chapters: one is dragged, one walks. The Hebrew lets you hear that Judah's departure is a descent in more than direction — he separates from the family, marries outside it, and buries two sons before the chapter is half over.

A Canaanite marriage, reported without comment. Abraham had made a servant swear an oath to prevent exactly this (24:3), and Isaac and Rebekah were made bitter by Esau's Canaanite wives (26:34-35). Judah simply "saw… and took." The narrator declines to editorialize — this book prefers to let a reader who has been paying attention supply the judgment.

The levirate duty, two centuries before the law. "Do the duty of a brother-in-law" is a single Hebrew verb, yabbem, built from the noun for a husband's brother — the custom later written into law at Deuteronomy 25:5-10: when a man dies childless, his brother marries the widow, and the first son counts as the dead man's, so the name is not blotted out. It is a provision for the widow and for the dead, and everything in this chapter turns on Judah's household refusing it. The KJV's "perform the duty of an husband's brother unto her" spells the idea out; the NWT reads "perform brother-in-law marriage with her," which is exactly the institution.

What Onan actually did, and what the text actually says about it. The verb is worth keeping: Onan ruined it on the ground — shichet, the same root this translation renders "ruin" all through the flood, where the earth was ruined and God resolved to ruin it in turn (6:11-13). And the narrator gives the motive without being asked: "Onan knew that the offspring would not be his… so as not to give offspring to his brother." That is a property calculation — as the firstborn's death had made him heir, a son credited to Er would displace him. The chapter's stated charge is the withholding: he took the marriage and refused the duty that was its whole point, using his brother's widow while cheating his brother's name. The word "onanism" is a much later coinage that reads a different sin back into the verse; the older Jewish and Christian commentaries argued the point at length, and a reader is entitled to know that the argument is theirs and not the text's. Genesis says what Onan withheld, and from whom.

Judah's own withholding. Then the pattern repeats one rung up. Judah tells Tamar to sit as a widow in her father's house until Shelah grows up — and the narrator lets us hear the private reason he does not say aloud: "Otherwise he too will die, like his brothers." He suspects the woman. So he sends her home indefinitely, neither married nor free to remarry, in exactly the limbo Onan's refusal created. Onan withheld a son; Judah withholds a son too. She waits. He forgets.

Verses 12–19 · the veil, the Opening of the Eyes, and a pledge

"And Judah was comforted." Vayyinnachem — and this is the cruelest quiet sentence in the Joseph story. One chapter earlier Jacob, told his son was dead, refused to be comforted — vayema'en lehitnachem, from this same root nacham. Judah, who engineered the sale that caused it, buries a wife and is comforted, and goes off to a sheepshearing — which in this culture is a festival. The father cannot be consoled; the son gets over it and goes to the party. The narrator sets the two verbs eleven verses apart and says nothing.

The veil, in a family that knows what veils are for. Tamar's disguise is one garment, and this house has form: Rebekah covered herself with a veil when she first saw Isaac (24:65), and Laban's wedding-night substitution of Leah for Rachel (29:23-25) only works because a bride's face is hidden. Jacob deceived a father who could not see; Leah deceived a husband who could not see; now a daughter-in-law deceives a father-in-law who does not look. Note carefully what verse 15 says: he thought her a prostitute because she had covered her face — the covering is what marks her as available, not the reverse of what a modern reader might assume.

The place is a joke at his expense. She sits at petach einayim — "the entrance of Enaim," a real town on the Timnah road. But einayim is also, transparently, the ordinary Hebrew word for EYES, so the phrase reads at the same time as "the Opening of the Eyes." She takes her seat at the place called Eyes-Open, and the next verse is "he did not know that she was his daughter-in-law." The KJV misses both the town and the pun, reading "in an open place"; the ASV has "in the gate of Enaim" and the NIV "at the entrance to Enaim," which recover the place-name. This translation keeps the name and lets the note carry the wordplay — it is untranslatable, but it is not decoration: the whole chapter is about a man who cannot recognize what is in front of him.

He offers a goat. Of course he does. Judah's price is a kid of the goats — gedi izzim. Two chapters ago his brothers slaughtered a male goat to soak Joseph's tunic in its blood (37:31), and before that Jacob wore goatskins to rob his blind father (27:16). The goat is this family's instrument of deception, passed down three generations, and here Judah cheerfully offers one as the fee — walking into a deception on the very animal he has used to run one.

The pledge is his identity. She does not ask for money. She asks for his seal, his cord, and his staff — a man's cylinder seal on its neck-cord (the thing he signs with, the ancient equivalent of a signature) and the carved staff that marks his standing. It is the most extraordinary demand in the chapter, and he hands it over without a blink: everything that proves who he is, surrendered to a stranger he does not intend to see again. The KJV's "thy signet, and thy bracelets, and thy staff" reads petil as "bracelets"; it is a cord or thread — the ASV, the NIV and the NWT all read "cord," and the cord is what the seal hangs on.

Verses 20–23 · a friend's careful vocabulary

The word changes on the way to town. This is a small thing the versions mostly bury, and it is very funny. In verse 15 the narrator says Judah took her for a zonah — the plain word for a common prostitute. But when Hirah has to go and ask directions from the locals, he does not use it. He asks for the qedeshah — literally a consecrated woman, from the root qadosh, "holy," the same root as the sanctuary. Whether the two words describe genuinely different roles (a shrine functionary versus a roadside prostitute) is still argued, and the evidence outside the Bible is thinner than confident commentaries suggest. What is not in doubt is the social register: Hirah reaches for the dignified term when he has to say it out loud to strangers, on his friend's behalf. The KJV flattens both words to "harlot" and the distinction vanishes entirely; the NIV reads "shrine prostitute" and the NWT "temple prostitute," both committing to the cultic reading; the ASV keeps a difference in the line and flags the Hebrew in its margin. This translation renders "consecrated woman" — close to what the word literally says, without deciding an institution the evidence will not settle.

"Or we will become a laughingstock." Judah gives up. He has lost his seal, his cord and his staff to a woman nobody can find, and the only thing that worries him is being laughed at — buz, contempt. He would rather write off the instruments of his own identity than be seen looking for them. Three months later that decision arrives back with a witness.

Verses 24–26 · "recognize, please" — the sentence comes home

The double standard, stated without a flicker. Judah has just bought a roadside prostitute and is untroubled. Told that his daughter-in-law is pregnant, his entire deliberation is two words in Hebrew: hotsi'uha ve-tissaref — "bring her out and let her be burned." Not a hearing, not a question. Burning is a punishment the later law reserves for a very few cases; whatever legal standing Judah imagines he has here as head of the family, the narrator reports the speed of it, and the speed is the point. The KJV's "Bring her forth, and let her be burnt" has never needed improving.

And then the two words. "Haker-na — recognize, please — whose are these." It is the same verb and the same particle of polite entreaty that Judah and his brothers used when they sent their father a bloodied coat and asked him to identify it: "haker-na, is it your son's tunic, or not?" (37:32). He is answered in his own sentence. And the props rhyme as exactly as the words: he sent a garment with a goat's blood on it to make a father believe a lie; he is sent his own seal and staff to make him admit a truth. Note too that the verb has now failed and landed three times in three generations of this family — Isaac "did not recognize" the son in front of him (27:23), Jacob did recognize a coat that was lying to him, and Judah recognizes the one thing he cannot argue with, because it is his signature.

"She is more righteous than I." Tsadqah mimmenni — the first honest sentence Judah speaks in the Bible, and the hinge of his life. The root is tsadaq, the righteousness that was "counted" to Abram when he believed (15:6); here it is counted to a Canaanite widow who tricked a patriarch, by the patriarch, out loud, in public. The Hebrew is comparative and slightly awkward — "she is in the right, more than I" — and every version reaches for it a little differently: the KJV "She hath been more righteous than I," the NWT "She is more righteous than I am," the NIV "She is more righteous than I," the Geneva "She is more righteous then I." He does not excuse himself and he does not blame her; he names the withheld son as the cause, which is the one fact he had kept quiet for years. Everything Judah becomes later — the brother who offers his own body as surety for Benjamin, the one who gets the kingship in Jacob's blessing — starts in this verse. The chapter that looked like a detour was the road.

Verses 27–30 · twins again, and the line to David

The family's oldest struggle, one more time. Twins wrestling at birth over who comes out first is not new here: Jacob himself was born gripping Esau's heel, having lost that race (25:26), and the whole book has been prying the birthright away from firstborns ever since. The midwife ties a scarlet thread — shani — on the hand that appears, an early and entirely practical piece of record-keeping; and then the hand goes back in and the other child is out first anyway. Even the marker cannot make the order hold.

Perez — "breach." "How you have broken out — a breach upon you!" The midwife's cry is a pun on the name: parats, to burst through, and perets, a gap torn in a wall. It sounds like an exclamation of dismay and is preserved as a name. The KJV spells him Pharez, the Douay Phares (through the Greek), the ASV and moderns Perez — the same man under three spellings, which is worth knowing when you meet him in a genealogy.

Which is where this chapter has been going all along. Ruth ends by tracing its own hero's line — "these are the generations of Perez" — ten names down to David (Ruth 4:18-22), and the Gospel of Matthew opens the New Testament by running the same line to Jesus, naming in its first paragraph "Perez and Zerah by Tamar" (Matthew 1:3). She is one of only four women in that genealogy, and Matthew keeps her. So the messianic line does not descend through the son Judah protected, or through the marriage he arranged, or through any part of his own plan. It descends through the widow he wronged and the twins conceived at a roadside — and Genesis, which never once calls Tamar righteous in its own voice, lets Judah say it for the record and leaves the sentence standing.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "ruined it on the ground" for shichet — the flood's own "ruin" verb, kept audible; "do the duty of a brother-in-law" for the single verb yabbem; "prostitute" for zonah but "consecrated woman" for qedeshah, keeping Hirah's careful upgrade visible where the KJV flattens both to "harlot"; "seal and cord and staff" (not the KJV's "bracelets"); "Enaim" kept as the town it is, with the Eyes-Open pun left to the note; and "She is more righteous than I" plain, in Judah's own mouth.

Echoes paid. Haker-na, "recognize, please" — sent to a father in 37:32, returned to its sender here, with a garment-and-goat answered by a seal-and-staff; nakar completing its third turn in three generations after Isaac's bedside; nacham — Jacob refusing comfort while Judah "was comforted" eleven verses later; the goat of 27:16 and 37:31 offered as a fee; the veil of 24:65 and the wedding-night substitution of 29:25; struggling twins after 25:26; and tsedaqah, "righteousness," counted at 15:6 and now spoken over Tamar.

Echoes planted. Judah, who has just learned to say "I was wrong," becoming the brother who offers himself as surety for Benjamin (43:9; 44:33) — the change starts here; the scepter that "shall not depart from Judah" (49:10); Perez's line running through Ruth to David (Ruth 4:18-22) and into Matthew's first paragraph, where Tamar is named (Matthew 1:3); and the levirate duty, unwritten here, becoming law at Deuteronomy 25:5-10 — and the engine of the book of Ruth.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 39 — back to Joseph, and down in Egypt: Potiphar's house, where "Jehovah was with Joseph" for the first time in his story, the master's wife, the garment left in her hand — a second garment used to tell a lie about him — and the prison that is the making of him.

Genesis 39 — The Mister Translation

1
וְיוֹסֵף, הוּרַד מִצְרָיְמָה; וַיִּקְנֵהוּ פּוֹטִיפַר סְרִיס פַּרְעֹה שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים, אִישׁ מִצְרִי, מִיַּד הַיִּשְׁמְעֵאלִים, אֲשֶׁר הוֹרִדֻהוּ שָׁמָּה.
And Joseph was brought down to Egypt; and Potiphar, a court official of Pharaoh, chief of the executioners, an Egyptian man, bought him from the hand of the Ishmaelites who had brought him down there.note
2
וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת-יוֹסֵף, וַיְהִי אִישׁ מַצְלִיחַ; וַיְהִי, בְּבֵית אֲדֹנָיו הַמִּצְרִי.
And Jehovah was with Joseph, and he became a man who prospered; and he was in the house of his master the Egyptian.note
3
וַיַּרְא אֲדֹנָיו, כִּי יְהוָה אִתּוֹ; וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר-הוּא עֹשֶׂה, יְהוָה מַצְלִיחַ בְּיָדוֹ.
And his master saw that Jehovah was with him, and that Jehovah made everything he did prosper in his hand.note
4
וַיִּמְצָא יוֹסֵף חֵן בְּעֵינָיו, וַיְשָׁרֶת אֹתוֹ; וַיַּפְקִדֵהוּ, עַל-בֵּיתוֹ, וְכָל-יֶשׁ-לוֹ, נָתַן בְּיָדוֹ.
And Joseph found favor in his eyes and attended him; and he appointed him over his house, and all that he had he gave into his hand.note
5
וַיְהִי מֵאָז הִפְקִיד אֹתוֹ בְּבֵיתוֹ, וְעַל כָּל-אֲשֶׁר יֶשׁ-לוֹ, וַיְבָרֶךְ יְהוָה אֶת-בֵּית הַמִּצְרִי, בִּגְלַל יוֹסֵף; וַיְהִי בִּרְכַּת יְהוָה, בְּכָל-אֲשֶׁר יֶשׁ-לוֹ--בַּבַּיִת, וּבַשָּׂדֶה.
And so it was, from the time he appointed him over his house and over all that he had, that Jehovah blessed the Egyptian's house for Joseph's sake; and the blessing of Jehovah was on all that he had, in the house and in the field.note
6
וַיַּעֲזֹב כָּל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ, בְּיַד-יוֹסֵף, וְלֹא-יָדַע אִתּוֹ מְאוּמָה, כִּי אִם-הַלֶּחֶם אֲשֶׁר-הוּא אוֹכֵל; וַיְהִי יוֹסֵף, יְפֵה-תֹאַר וִיפֵה מַרְאֶה.
And he left all that he had in Joseph's hand, and with him there he knew nothing except the bread he ate. And Joseph was beautiful in form and lovely to look at.note
7
וַיְהִי, אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה, וַתִּשָּׂא אֵשֶׁת-אֲדֹנָיו אֶת-עֵינֶיהָ, אֶל-יוֹסֵף; וַתֹּאמֶר, שִׁכְבָה עִמִּי.
And so it was, after these things, that his master's wife lifted her eyes to Joseph, and she said, "Lie with me."note
8
וַיְמָאֵן--וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל-אֵשֶׁת אֲדֹנָיו, הֵן אֲדֹנִי לֹא-יָדַע אִתִּי מַה-בַּבָּיִת; וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר-יֶשׁ-לוֹ, נָתַן בְּיָדִי.
But he refused, and said to his master's wife, "Look — my master, with me here, does not know what is in the house, and all that he has he has given into my hand.note
9
אֵינֶנּוּ גָדוֹל בַּבַּיִת הַזֶּה, מִמֶּנִּי, וְלֹא-חָשַׂךְ מִמֶּנִּי מְאוּמָה, כִּי אִם-אוֹתָךְ בַּאֲשֶׁר אַתְּ-אִשְׁתּוֹ; וְאֵיךְ אֶעֱשֶׂה הָרָעָה הַגְּדֹלָה, הַזֹּאת, וְחָטָאתִי, לֵאלֹהִים.
He is not greater in this house than I am, and he has withheld nothing from me except you, since you are his wife. How then could I do this great evil, and sin against God?"note
10
וַיְהִי, כְּדַבְּרָהּ אֶל-יוֹסֵף יוֹם יוֹם; וְלֹא-שָׁמַע אֵלֶיהָ לִשְׁכַּב אֶצְלָהּ, לִהְיוֹת עִמָּהּ.
And so it was, as she spoke to Joseph day after day, that he did not listen to her, to lie beside her, to be with her.note
11
וַיְהִי כְּהַיּוֹם הַזֶּה, וַיָּבֹא הַבַּיְתָה לַעֲשׂוֹת מְלַאכְתּוֹ; וְאֵין אִישׁ מֵאַנְשֵׁי הַבַּיִת, שָׁם--בַּבָּיִת.
And so it was on a day like this, that he came into the house to do his work — and none of the men of the house were there in the house.note
12
וַתִּתְפְּשֵׂהוּ בְּבִגְדוֹ לֵאמֹר, שִׁכְבָה עִמִּי; וַיַּעֲזֹב בִּגְדוֹ בְּיָדָהּ, וַיָּנָס וַיֵּצֵא הַחוּצָה.
And she seized him by his garment, saying, "Lie with me." And he left his garment in her hand and fled and went outside.note
13
וַיְהִי, כִּרְאוֹתָהּ, כִּי-עָזַב בִּגְדוֹ, בְּיָדָהּ; וַיָּנָס, הַחוּצָה.
And so it was, when she saw that he had left his garment in her hand and had fled outside,note
14
וַתִּקְרָא לְאַנְשֵׁי בֵיתָהּ, וַתֹּאמֶר לָהֶם לֵאמֹר, רְאוּ הֵבִיא לָנוּ אִישׁ עִבְרִי, לְצַחֶק בָּנוּ: בָּא אֵלַי לִשְׁכַּב עִמִּי, וָאֶקְרָא בְּקוֹל גָּדוֹל.
that she called to the men of her house and said to them, "See — he has brought us in a Hebrew man to laugh at us! He came to me to lie with me, and I called out in a loud voice.note
15
וַיְהִי כְשָׁמְעוֹ, כִּי-הֲרִימֹתִי קוֹלִי וָאֶקְרָא; וַיַּעֲזֹב בִּגְדוֹ אֶצְלִי, וַיָּנָס וַיֵּצֵא הַחוּצָה.
And so it was, when he heard me lift my voice and call out, that he left his garment beside me and fled and went outside."note
16
וַתַּנַּח בִּגְדוֹ, אֶצְלָהּ, עַד-בּוֹא אֲדֹנָיו, אֶל-בֵּיתוֹ.
And she laid his garment beside her until his master came to his house.note
17
וַתְּדַבֵּר אֵלָיו, כַּדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה לֵאמֹר: בָּא-אֵלַי הָעֶבֶד הָעִבְרִי, אֲשֶׁר-הֵבֵאתָ לָּנוּ--לְצַחֶק בִּי.
And she spoke to him these same words, saying, "The Hebrew slave whom you brought to us came to me to laugh at me.note
18
וַיְהִי, כַּהֲרִימִי קוֹלִי וָאֶקְרָא; וַיַּעֲזֹב בִּגְדוֹ אֶצְלִי, וַיָּנָס הַחוּצָה.
And so it was, as I lifted my voice and called out, that he left his garment beside me and fled outside."note
19
וַיְהִי כִשְׁמֹעַ אֲדֹנָיו אֶת-דִּבְרֵי אִשְׁתּוֹ, אֲשֶׁר דִּבְּרָה אֵלָיו לֵאמֹר, כַּדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה, עָשָׂה לִי עַבְדֶּךָ; וַיִּחַר, אַפּוֹ.
And so it was, when his master heard the words of his wife that she spoke to him — "These are the things your slave did to me" — that his anger burned.note
20
וַיִּקַּח אֲדֹנֵי יוֹסֵף אֹתוֹ, וַיִּתְּנֵהוּ אֶל-בֵּית הַסֹּהַר--מְקוֹם, אֲשֶׁר-אסורי אֲסִירֵי הַמֶּלֶךְ אֲסוּרִים; וַיְהִי-שָׁם, בְּבֵית הַסֹּהַר.
And Joseph's master took him and put him into the prison-house, the place where the king's prisoners were bound; and he was there in the prison-house.note
21
וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת-יוֹסֵף, וַיֵּט אֵלָיו חָסֶד; וַיִּתֵּן חִנּוֹ, בְּעֵינֵי שַׂר בֵּית-הַסֹּהַר.
But Jehovah was with Joseph and extended kindness to him, and gave him favor in the eyes of the keeper of the prison-house.note
22
וַיִּתֵּן שַׂר בֵּית-הַסֹּהַר, בְּיַד-יוֹסֵף, אֵת כָּל-הָאֲסִירִם, אֲשֶׁר בְּבֵית הַסֹּהַר; וְאֵת כָּל-אֲשֶׁר עֹשִׂים שָׁם, הוּא הָיָה עֹשֶׂה.
And the keeper of the prison-house gave into Joseph's hand all the prisoners who were in the prison-house; and everything they did there, he was the one who did it.note
23
אֵין שַׂר בֵּית-הַסֹּהַר, רֹאֶה אֶת-כָּל-מְאוּמָה בְּיָדוֹ, בַּאֲשֶׁר יְהוָה, אִתּוֹ; וַאֲשֶׁר-הוּא עֹשֶׂה, יְהוָה מַצְלִיחַ. {פ}
The keeper of the prison-house did not look at anything in his hand, because Jehovah was with him; and whatever he did, Jehovah made prosper. {פ}note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–6 · the sentence chapter 37 refused to say

"And Jehovah was with Joseph." Read the previous two chapters again and notice what is missing from them: in chapter 37 — the coat, the pit, the sale, a father destroyed by a lie — God is never named once. Not a word from heaven, not a line of comment, nothing. Then the boy is sold into a foreign house as property, and the narrator, who has been silent through everything that could be called a tragedy, finally speaks: vayehi YHVH et-Yosef. And having said it, he will not stop saying it — four times in twenty-three verses (vv. 2, 3, 21, 23), twice on either side of the disaster in the middle. The chapter is built as a frame, and the frame is the sentence. Where a reader most wants to hear that God is present, Genesis says nothing; when everything has already gone wrong, it says it four times.

"A man who prospered" — in slavery. The word is matsliach, from tsalach, to succeed, to be made to go well — and the chapter uses it of a man who has been robbed of his family, his freedom, and his name. It is not a promise that things are pleasant; Joseph is still a slave in verse 6 and will be a prisoner by verse 20. Whatever "prospering" means here, it plainly does not mean "getting what he wanted." The KJV has "a prosperous man," the ASV the same, the NIV "he prospered," the NWT "a successful man" — all fine; the discomfort is in the Hebrew, not the English.

An Egyptian's house, blessed for a slave's sake. Verse 5 is quietly enormous: the blessing runs through Joseph into a foreign, idol-worshipping household — "in the house and in the field," which is to say the whole estate. This is the Abrahamic promise operating in miniature and out of sight: "in you all the families of the earth will be blessed" (12:3). The first person we ever see it happen to is a slave-owner in Egypt, and he has no idea why his accounts have improved.

"He knew nothing except the bread he ate." An odd little clause, and the versions divide on whether it is bookkeeping or delicacy. Read plainly, Potiphar handed over everything and stopped paying attention to anything but his own dinner. But some readers hear a euphemism (his wife, discreetly), and others a cultural note — Egyptians would not eat with Hebrews, a rule Genesis itself states outright later (43:32), so the master's food was the one thing a Hebrew steward could not touch. The KJV's "he knew not aught he had, save the bread which he did eat" keeps the ambiguity; the NIV resolves it toward bookkeeping ("he did not concern himself with anything except the food he ate"). This translation keeps it as it stands.

His mother's face. The chapter ends its first movement with a description that ought to stop a careful reader: Joseph was beautiful in form and lovely to look at. That is not a general compliment — it is, word for word, the Hebrew used of exactly one other person in the Bible, Rachel (yefat to'ar vifat mar'eh, 29:17), Joseph's own mother, whose beauty set the whole Jacob-Laban story in motion. This translation renders both with the same English, because the Hebrew is the same and the echo is the point; the KJV splits them ("beautiful and well favoured" for Rachel, "a goodly person, and well favoured" for Joseph), so an English reader cannot see that the son has his mother's face. And the sentence is placed here, immediately before verse 7, for a reason: it is not admiration, it is the hinge of the plot.

Verses 7–10 · two words, and a long answer

She speaks in two words; he answers in fifty. The Hebrew of her proposition is shikhvah immi — an imperative and a pronoun, blunt as a command from an owner to a piece of property, which is exactly the power relation in the room. Joseph's reply runs three full verses and is the longest speech he has made in his life. He argues the trust ("he has given all he has into my hand… he has withheld nothing from me except you"), and then he stops arguing about Potiphar altogether. "How then could I do this great evil, and sin against God?" The wrong he names last is not the wrong to his master or to himself — it is a wrong against someone who is not in the room and whose name has just been said four times over his life. This is the boy who told his brothers his dreams; he has learned to speak.

"Day after day." Verse 10 quietly refuses the version of this story where a man is ambushed once by temptation and heroically resists. Yom yom — it went on. The text says he did not listen, and then adds the smaller and harder detail: not "to lie beside her" and not even "to be with her." He would not be in the room. The narrative is describing a siege, not a moment.

A story the Egyptians were also telling. Honesty requires the comparison: a strikingly similar tale survives in Egyptian, the Tale of Two Brothers (Papyrus d'Orbiney, written down around 1200 BC), in which a man's wife propositions his younger brother, is refused, and accuses him to her husband. Folklorists file the shape under its own type-name — the spurned-wife accusation — and it turns up in Greek myth too (Phaedra and Hippolytus). What that means is argued: borrowing, a shared stock of story-shapes, or simply that this is a thing that happens to powerless men in powerful houses and always has. Genesis is not embarrassed by the resemblance, and neither is this translation; it is worth knowing that the ancient world had a name for what happened to Joseph.

Verses 11–18 · the second garment

It happens to him twice. This is the detail the chapter is built to make you feel. Joseph's brothers stripped him of his ornamented tunic, soaked it in goat's blood, and carried it to their father as evidence of a death that never happened. Now a second garment is taken off him, kept back deliberately (v. 16 — she laid it beside her and waited for her husband to come home), and produced as evidence of a crime that never happened. Twice Joseph is stripped; twice the cloth is left in someone else's hand; twice it is used to tell a lie about him to the man who owns his future. He does not get to speak in his own defence either time — in fact the chapter never records Joseph saying a single word after verse 9.

The word repeated until you notice. "He left his garment… and fled and went outside" is said four times in seven verses (vv. 12, 13, 15, 18), twice by the narrator and twice by her. Hebrew narrative repeats deliberately, and trimming it is editing — but watch what she changes. The narrator says he left it in her hand (be-yadah) — because she was gripping it. In her retellings it becomes beside me (etsli), which removes her hand from the story entirely. One preposition, and the seizing disappears.

"A Hebrew man" — and who she says it to. She does not begin with her husband. She calls the household staff first and builds a coalition, and her opening word to them is ethnic: "he has brought us in a Hebrew man to laugh at us." Ivri is the outsider-word, used in the Bible mostly by foreigners or to foreigners — first of Abram the Hebrew, and later all through the Egyptian scenes. To her husband, one verse later, the phrase upgrades to "the Hebrew slave whom you brought to us" — the blame quietly relocated onto the man she is complaining to. It is a small masterpiece of accusation, and Genesis reports it without a syllable of comment.

"To laugh at us." The verb she uses is letsacheq — the laughter root that has run through this book since Abraham and Sarah laughed at the promise, that named Isaac, and that turned nasty at 21:9, where Ishmael was seen metsacheq and Sarah demanded he be thrown out. It can mean play, mockery, or something coarser depending on where you stand — and she is exploiting exactly that range. The KJV renders it "to mock us," the ASV the same; the NIV reads "to make sport of us" and the NWT "to make us a laughingstock." The family's own laughter-word, aimed at Rachel's son by a stranger.

Verses 19–20 · what Potiphar does not do

The punishment is the loudest evidence in the chapter. A slave accused of assaulting his owner's wife, by an Egyptian officer whose actual job title is chief of the executioners, does not go to prison. He is killed, that day, and nobody writes it down. Potiphar's anger burns — and then he files him into the royal detention house and, as far as the text lets us see, never speaks of it again. Genesis does not explain this. But it does place the sentence next to the man's job description, and it does have his wife say "the Hebrew slave whom you brought to us," which is a complaint aimed at her husband; a reader is left to wonder how much of this Potiphar believed, and whether the cell was a punishment or the cheapest way to keep a valuable steward alive while saving a marriage. The chapter's silence is not an oversight — it is the same restraint that let the brothers stand in front of a father saying "an evil beast has devoured him."

The prison-house. Bet ha-sohar — "the house of the sohar," and nobody is quite sure what sohar means. It occurs only in the Joseph story, never elsewhere in the Bible; guesses run from a root meaning "round" (a fortress-tower or round-house) to an Egyptian loanword. This translation keeps "prison-house" so that bet, "house," stays audible — because that is the chapter's own bitter joke. Joseph is put in charge of a house (v. 4), loses it, and is handed a second house to run (v. 22). The KJV and ASV read simply "the prison"; the NWT "the prison house," keeping the same seam. ⚠️ Verse 20 also carries a ketiv/qere — Mechon-Mamre prints the unpointed written form beside the pointed one it is read as — kept here as received.

Verses 21–23 · the frame closes

"But Jehovah was with Joseph." The same four words as verse 2, now on the far side of the false accusation, and the chapter's architecture is complete: God with him in the house of the man who bought him, God with him in the jail of the man who framed him. Nothing external has improved — he has gone from slave to prisoner, which is downward — and the sentence does not change. It is the plainest statement Genesis makes about what "God is with you" does and does not promise.

Kindness, and favour. Two words worth keeping distinct. Chesed is covenant loyalty — the faithful, committed kindness this book has used of Lot's rescue (19:19) and of the servant's prayer at the well (24:12) — and it is God's, "extended" to Joseph like a hand reaching down into a cell. Chen is favour, the charm that makes someone want to help you, and it is what the keeper feels. The one produces the other. The versions reach variously: the KJV "shewed him mercy," the ASV "showed kindness unto him," the NWT "showed him loyal love" — and the NWT's compound is arguably the most exact of the three, since chesed's whole freight is loyalty rather than mere niceness. This translation reads "kindness" for continuity with 19:19 and 24:12, with the loyalty in the dictionary where it can be explained.

The same man, in a smaller room. Verses 22-23 are verses 4-6 again, deliberately: everything given into his hand, the keeper looking at nothing, Jehovah making it prosper. Joseph does in the jail exactly what he did in the house — he runs it. Whatever else this chapter is about, it is about a man whose competence and character are not situational, and the narrator makes the point by repeating himself rather than praising him. Note what has been quietly established by the end: Joseph is now the trusted manager of a prison full of the king's prisoners. The next two men through that door will be Pharaoh's cupbearer and Pharaoh's baker.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "beautiful in form and lovely to look at" for yefeh to'ar vifeh mar'eh — rendered identically to Rachel's description because the Hebrew is identical and the KJV's split hides it; "prospered" for tsalach, uncomfortable as it is of a slave; "prison-house" for bet ha-sohar, keeping bet audible against the two other houses Joseph is given to run; "kindness" for chesed (continuous with 19:19 and 24:12) kept distinct from "favor" for chen; "to laugh at us" keeping the Isaac laughter-root audible in her accusation; and the fourfold "he left his garment… and fled and went outside" left repeated, as Hebrew narrative intends.

Echoes paid. The silence of chapter 37 — where God is never named — answered here four times over; the stripped tunic of 37:23 and the second garment, both taken off him and both used as false evidence; Rachel's beauty (29:17) reappearing on her son's face and endangering him; tsachaq, the laughter that named Isaac and turned cruel at 21:9, now weaponised by a stranger; Ivri, "Hebrew," last heard of Abram; the promise that "all the families of the earth will be blessed" (12:3) quietly enriching an Egyptian who never learns why; and chesed extended into a prison.

Echoes planted. A prison full of "the king's prisoners" and a Hebrew who runs it — the cupbearer and the baker are one chapter away (40); dreams, which Joseph has neither had nor interpreted since his brothers threw him in a pit, about to become the thing that lifts him out (40-41); "Jehovah was with him" becoming the formula the whole Bible uses for its unlikely deliverers; and Joseph's own verdict on all of it, still twelve chapters off — "you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good" (50:20).

Next installment in this book: Genesis 40 — two royal servants dream on the same night in Joseph's prison, and the man whose own dreams put him there discovers he can read other people's: three branches and three baskets, one restored and one hanged — and "do not interpretations belong to God?" Then the cupbearer forgets him for two full years.

Genesis 40 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיְהִי, אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה, חָטְאוּ מַשְׁקֵה מֶלֶךְ-מִצְרַיִם, וְהָאֹפֶה--לַאֲדֹנֵיהֶם, לְמֶלֶךְ מִצְרָיִם.
And so it was, after these things, that the cupbearer of the king of Egypt and the baker sinned against their lord, the king of Egypt.note
2
וַיִּקְצֹף פַּרְעֹה, עַל שְׁנֵי סָרִיסָיו--עַל שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים, וְעַל שַׂר הָאוֹפִים.
And Pharaoh was furious with his two court officials — with the chief of the cupbearers and with the chief of the bakers.note
3
וַיִּתֵּן אֹתָם בְּמִשְׁמַר, בֵּית שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים--אֶל-בֵּית הַסֹּהַר: מְקוֹם, אֲשֶׁר יוֹסֵף אָסוּר שָׁם.
And he put them in custody in the house of the chief of the executioners, into the prison-house — the place where Joseph was bound.note
4
וַיִּפְקֹד שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים אֶת-יוֹסֵף, אִתָּם--וַיְשָׁרֶת אֹתָם; וַיִּהְיוּ יָמִים, בְּמִשְׁמָר.
And the chief of the executioners appointed Joseph to be with them, and he attended them; and they were in custody for some days.note
5
וַיַּחַלְמוּ חֲלוֹם שְׁנֵיהֶם אִישׁ חֲלֹמוֹ, בְּלַיְלָה אֶחָד--אִישׁ, כְּפִתְרוֹן חֲלֹמוֹ: הַמַּשְׁקֶה וְהָאֹפֶה, אֲשֶׁר לְמֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם, אֲשֶׁר אֲסוּרִים, בְּבֵית הַסֹּהַר.
And the two of them dreamed a dream, each man his own dream in one night, each man according to the interpretation of his dream — the cupbearer and the baker of the king of Egypt, who were bound in the prison-house.note
6
וַיָּבֹא אֲלֵיהֶם יוֹסֵף, בַּבֹּקֶר; וַיַּרְא אֹתָם, וְהִנָּם זֹעֲפִים.
And Joseph came in to them in the morning and saw them, and look — they were downcast.note
7
וַיִּשְׁאַל אֶת-סְרִיסֵי פַרְעֹה, אֲשֶׁר אִתּוֹ בְמִשְׁמַר בֵּית אֲדֹנָיו--לֵאמֹר: מַדּוּעַ פְּנֵיכֶם רָעִים, הַיּוֹם.
And he asked Pharaoh's court officials who were with him in custody in his master's house, saying, "Why are your faces bad today?"note
8
וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלָיו--חֲלוֹם חָלַמְנוּ, וּפֹתֵר אֵין אֹתוֹ; וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יוֹסֵף, הֲלוֹא לֵאלֹהִים פִּתְרֹנִים--סַפְּרוּ-נָא, לִי.
And they said to him, "We have dreamed a dream, and there is no one to interpret it." And Joseph said to them, "Do not interpretations belong to God? Tell it to me, please."note
9
וַיְסַפֵּר שַׂר-הַמַּשְׁקִים אֶת-חֲלֹמוֹ, לְיוֹסֵף; וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ--בַּחֲלוֹמִי, וְהִנֵּה-גֶפֶן לְפָנָי.
And the chief of the cupbearers recounted his dream to Joseph and said to him, "In my dream — look, a vine was before me;note
10
וּבַגֶּפֶן, שְׁלֹשָׁה שָׂרִיגִם; וְהִוא כְפֹרַחַת עָלְתָה נִצָּהּ, הִבְשִׁילוּ אַשְׁכְּלֹתֶיהָ עֲנָבִים.
and on the vine were three branches; and it was as though it budded, its blossom came up, its clusters ripened into grapes.note
11
וְכוֹס פַּרְעֹה, בְּיָדִי; וָאֶקַּח אֶת-הָעֲנָבִים, וָאֶשְׂחַט אֹתָם אֶל-כּוֹס פַּרְעֹה, וָאֶתֵּן אֶת-הַכּוֹס, עַל-כַּף פַּרְעֹה.
And Pharaoh's cup was in my hand; and I took the grapes and pressed them into Pharaoh's cup, and I placed the cup on Pharaoh's palm."note
12
וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ יוֹסֵף, זֶה פִּתְרֹנוֹ: שְׁלֹשֶׁת, הַשָּׂרִגִים--שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים, הֵם.
And Joseph said to him, "This is its interpretation: the three branches are three days.note
13
בְּעוֹד שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים, יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת-רֹאשֶׁךָ, וַהֲשִׁיבְךָ, עַל-כַּנֶּךָ; וְנָתַתָּ כוֹס-פַּרְעֹה, בְּיָדוֹ, כַּמִּשְׁפָּט הָרִאשׁוֹן, אֲשֶׁר הָיִיתָ מַשְׁקֵהוּ.
In three days more Pharaoh will lift up your head and restore you to your post, and you will place Pharaoh's cup in his hand as before, when you were his cupbearer.note
14
כִּי אִם-זְכַרְתַּנִי אִתְּךָ, כַּאֲשֶׁר יִיטַב לָךְ, וְעָשִׂיתָ-נָּא עִמָּדִי, חָסֶד; וְהִזְכַּרְתַּנִי, אֶל-פַּרְעֹה, וְהוֹצֵאתַנִי, מִן-הַבַּיִת הַזֶּה.
Only remember me when it goes well with you, and do kindness with me, please: mention me to Pharaoh, and bring me out of this house.note
15
כִּי-גֻנֹּב גֻּנַּבְתִּי, מֵאֶרֶץ הָעִבְרִים; וְגַם-פֹּה לֹא-עָשִׂיתִי מְאוּמָה, כִּי-שָׂמוּ אֹתִי בַּבּוֹר.
For I was stolen — stolen away — from the land of the Hebrews; and here too I have done nothing that they should put me in the pit."note
16
וַיַּרְא שַׂר-הָאֹפִים, כִּי טוֹב פָּתָר; וַיֹּאמֶר, אֶל-יוֹסֵף, אַף-אֲנִי בַּחֲלוֹמִי, וְהִנֵּה שְׁלֹשָׁה סַלֵּי חֹרִי עַל-רֹאשִׁי.
And the chief of the bakers saw that he had interpreted well, and he said to Joseph, "I too was in my dream — and look, three baskets of white bread were on my head;note
17
וּבַסַּל הָעֶלְיוֹן, מִכֹּל מַאֲכַל פַּרְעֹה--מַעֲשֵׂה אֹפֶה; וְהָעוֹף, אֹכֵל אֹתָם מִן-הַסַּל--מֵעַל רֹאשִׁי.
and in the topmost basket was every kind of food for Pharaoh, the work of a baker — and the birds were eating them out of the basket, from off my head."note
18
וַיַּעַן יוֹסֵף וַיֹּאמֶר, זֶה פִּתְרֹנוֹ: שְׁלֹשֶׁת, הַסַּלִּים--שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים, הֵם.
And Joseph answered and said, "This is its interpretation: the three baskets are three days.note
19
בְּעוֹד שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים, יִשָּׂא פַרְעֹה אֶת-רֹאשְׁךָ מֵעָלֶיךָ, וְתָלָה אוֹתְךָ, עַל-עֵץ; וְאָכַל הָעוֹף אֶת-בְּשָׂרְךָ, מֵעָלֶיךָ.
In three days more Pharaoh will lift up your head — from off you — and hang you on a tree; and the birds will eat your flesh from off you."note
20
וַיְהִי בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי, יוֹם הֻלֶּדֶת אֶת-פַּרְעֹה, וַיַּעַשׂ מִשְׁתֶּה, לְכָל-עֲבָדָיו; וַיִּשָּׂא אֶת-רֹאשׁ שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים, וְאֶת-רֹאשׁ שַׂר הָאֹפִים--בְּתוֹךְ עֲבָדָיו.
And so it was on the third day — Pharaoh's birthday — that he made a feast for all his servants; and he lifted up the head of the chief of the cupbearers and the head of the chief of the bakers among his servants.note
21
וַיָּשֶׁב אֶת-שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים, עַל-מַשְׁקֵהוּ; וַיִּתֵּן הַכּוֹס, עַל-כַּף פַּרְעֹה.
And he restored the chief of the cupbearers to his cupbearing, and he placed the cup on Pharaoh's palm;note
22
וְאֵת שַׂר הָאֹפִים, תָּלָה: כַּאֲשֶׁר פָּתַר לָהֶם, יוֹסֵף.
but the chief of the bakers he hanged — just as Joseph had interpreted to them.note
23
וְלֹא-זָכַר שַׂר-הַמַּשְׁקִים אֶת-יוֹסֵף, וַיִּשְׁכָּחֵהוּ. {פ}
And the chief of the cupbearers did not remember Joseph — and he forgot him. {פ}note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–8 · two officials, one night — and who owns an interpretation

Butlers and bakers, and how big these jobs were. The KJV's "butler" has shrunk in four hundred years into a comic figure in a tailcoat; the Hebrew mashqeh is simply "one who gives drink," and the office was enormous. A king's cupbearer tasted what the king swallowed, which means he was chosen for loyalty above almost anyone at court and stood closer to the royal ear than most ministers; centuries later Nehemiah is cupbearer to the king of Persia and can get imperial policy changed over a single sad face (Nehemiah 1-2). Both men here are sarisim — the same court-official word used of Potiphar — and their offence is left completely unstated. Genesis does not care what they did. It cares that they dreamed.

The wheel turns quietly. Notice who owns the prison: they are put "in the house of the chief of the executioners" — Potiphar's house, the same title from 37:36 and 39:1. Joseph is still, technically, inside his master's establishment; and it is Potiphar himself who appoints Joseph to attend them. Whatever the man believed about his wife's accusation, he is still handing Joseph responsibility. And the verb in verse 4 is the same one used when he first put Joseph over his house (39:4). Slave, steward, prisoner, steward again.

"Why are your faces bad today?" A small line that does a lot of work. The literal Hebrew — ra'im, "bad," the plain word for evil — is blunt, and every version softens it ("sad," KJV and ASV; "dejected," NIV; "gloomy," NWT). This translation keeps "bad" and lets the strangeness stand. But the point is that he asked. A slave in a foreign jail, framed and forgotten, notices that two other men are having a worse morning than usual and stops to ask why. The entire remainder of his life turns on that question.

"Do not interpretations belong to God?" The chapter's thesis, and it is aimed at a very specific audience. Egypt had a serious, professional dream-interpretation industry — an actual reference manual survives, the Dream Book of Papyrus Chester Beatty III, which lists dreams in columns with standard readings ("if a man sees himself looking into a deep well: bad; it means being put in prison"). The two officials complain that "there is no one to interpret" — meaning no specialist, because they are in jail and cut off from the professionals. Joseph's reply sidesteps the whole profession: pitronim are God's, not a technician's. It is also, quietly, the sharpest thing he could have said about himself. This is the boy whose own dreams got him thrown in a pit and who has not mentioned a dream since. He does not claim the gift; he assigns it upward and then asks to hear.

Verses 9–15 · the vine, and the favour he asks for

A dream in fast-forward. The vine buds, blossoms and ripens in a single sentence — budding, blossom, clusters — which is agriculturally impossible and exactly how dreams work. The cupbearer's hands are doing his old job throughout: he takes, he presses, he places the cup on Pharaoh's palm. The dream is a man rehearsing his own restoration, and the detail that gives Joseph the reading is the number three.

"Pharaoh will lift up your head." Here is the phrase the whole chapter is built on — nasa rosh, "to lift the head." It is a real court idiom meaning to single a man out of the crowd for notice: to review him, to reckon him, to raise him up. Applied to the cupbearer it means pardon and promotion. Hold on to it; it comes back twice, and it does not mean the same thing either time. The KJV and ASV both keep it literally ("shall Pharaoh lift up thine head"), which is the right call and preserves the pun; the NIV resolves it to "will lift up your head and restore you," and the TLB paraphrases the idiom away entirely — losing the machinery of verse 19.

The one thing he asks for. Joseph interprets for free and then makes a request, and the word he uses for it is chesed — "do kindness with me." It is the covenant word, the loyal, faithful sort of kindness — and the last time it appeared, two verses from the end of the previous chapter, it was God extending it to Joseph in this same jail (39:21). Joseph now asks a man for what God has been giving him. The chapter's final verse is the answer.

"I was stolen — stolen away." Gunnov gunnavti, an infinitive absolute doubled for emphasis, and a startling choice of verb. Joseph does not say "my brothers sold me." He says he was stolen — ganav, the theft word, which when the object is a human being names a capital crime in the law that is coming ("whoever steals a man… shall surely be put to death," Exodus 21:16). He states the crime precisely and omits the criminals entirely. Whether that is discretion, family loyalty, or simply what it honestly felt like from inside a caravan, Genesis does not say — but it is the only time Joseph ever refers to the sale before he reveals himself, and he does it without naming a single brother.

"They should put me in the pit." And here the versions bury something. The word is bor — the identical Hebrew noun used of the dry cistern his brothers threw him into at Dothan (37:24, "the pit was empty; there was no water in it"). Joseph is describing an Egyptian jail with the same word he would use for that hole in the ground, which is either the plainest possible statement of how his life looks from the inside, or the sharpest. But the KJV, ASV and NIV all read "dungeon" here while reading "pit" back in chapter 37 — so the English reader cannot hear that Joseph has been in this hole before. This translation renders both "pit."

Verses 16–19 · the same words, inside out

He only speaks because the first answer was good. "And the chief of the bakers saw that he had interpreted well" — the narrator tells us the man's motive before he opens his mouth. He is not seeking truth; he heard a favourable verdict and would like one too. It is a very ordinary human thing and Genesis records it without comment, one verse before the worst news in the chapter.

Three baskets of something. Salei chori — the second word is uncertain. Traditionally it is derived from a root for "white," giving the KJV's "three white baskets" (and, following the same instinct, "white bread"); others connect it to holes, giving wicker or openwork baskets; the NIV settles on "three baskets of bread." Nobody is certain. This translation reads "white bread" with the doubt noted here — the detail is not load-bearing, and the number is.

Now watch the idiom turn. Joseph gives the baker the identical opening — "in three days more Pharaoh will lift up your head" — and then adds two words: from off you. The court phrase for being singled out for honour becomes, with a preposition, a sentence of decapitation, and the second half of the verse confirms it: hanged on a tree, the birds eating his flesh. It is one of the cruellest and most brilliant turns in the Hebrew Bible, and it only works because the two interpretations are worded the same way up to that point. The birds the baker saw eating from the basket on his head are the same birds, doing the same thing, in the reading.

Hanged on a tree. Ancient practice here is almost certainly impalement or the public display of a corpse after execution rather than hanging in the modern sense. The phrase acquires a long afterlife: Deuteronomy legislates that a man "hanged on a tree" must be taken down before nightfall, "for a hanged man is a curse of God" (21:22-23) — a text Paul later quotes in an argument about the crucifixion (Galatians 3:13). Genesis itself is doing none of that. It is reporting an Egyptian execution, three days out, correctly predicted.

Verses 20–22 · one phrase, two men, two fates

The birthday. The first birthday celebration mentioned in the Bible belongs to a pharaoh, and it is the occasion for a prisoner amnesty and an execution on the same afternoon — which is roughly how royal birthdays worked. (The only other birthday party the Bible records is Herod's, and a man loses his head at that one too: Matthew 14:6-10. The pattern was not lost on later readers.) The KJV says plainly "Pharaoh's birthday," as do all the versions; the Hebrew is a slightly odd construction, literally "the day of giving birth to Pharaoh."

And then the sentence lands on both of them at once. Verse 20 is the payoff, and in Hebrew it is a single verb governing two objects: Pharaoh lifted up the head of the chief cupbearer and the head of the chief baker. One phrase. Two men. One is lifted back to his cup; the other is lifted off his shoulders. The narrator does not explain the wordplay or apologize for it — he simply uses the idiom in the way that includes both fates, and lets the next two verses sort out which man got which. Every English version that renders the idiom literally (the KJV and ASV do) keeps this; every version that smooths it into "summoned" or "sent for" loses the entire architecture of the chapter.

"Just as Joseph had interpreted to them." The narrator's only editorial comment, and it is about accuracy. Both readings were right, including the one nobody wanted. Joseph told a man he would die in three days, to his face, having been asked; there is no record of him softening it.

Verse 23 · the verb that fails

"Did not remember… and forgot him." The chapter ends on a doubled negative that is not redundant but insistent: he did not remember, and he forgot. And zakhar is not a neutral word in this book. It is the covenant verb — "God remembered Noah" and the waters went down (8:1); "God remembered Abraham" and Lot was pulled out (19:29); "God remembered Rachel" and Joseph himself was born (30:22). In Hebrew it never means merely "recalled"; it means acted on behalf of. Every time God has remembered someone in Genesis, a rescue followed. Here the verb is put in a man's hands, with a favour attached, and it fails. That is the chapter's real ending: not cruelty, just ordinary human forgetfulness by a man who got his job back and had a lot on.

Two years. Genesis does not say how long the silence lasts until the very next verse of the next chapter — "at the end of two full years" (41:1). The reader is not told that at the time. Verse 23 simply closes, and the effect is deliberate: the man who can read other men's futures is left with no reading of his own. It is the third time Joseph has been in a hole and the third time someone has walked away holding something of his — a coat, a garment, and now a promise. When the cupbearer finally does speak, the first words out of his mouth are "I remember my faults today" (41:9), and by then Joseph is thirty years old.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "lift up your head" kept literally in all three places (vv. 13, 19, 20), because the chapter's whole architecture is one court idiom used for a pardon, an execution, and both at once; "pit" for bor in v. 15 — the same English as 37:24, where the versions switch to "dungeon" and hide the fact that Joseph has been in this hole before; "cupbearer" for mashqeh rather than the KJV's now-comic "butler"; "stolen — stolen away" for the doubled gunnov gunnavti; "your faces are bad" left blunt; and "kindness" for chesed, continuous with 39:21.

Echoes paid. Chesed — extended by God to Joseph in the same jail one chapter ago, now requested from a man, and refused by forgetfulness; bor, the pit of 37:24, reused by Joseph for his cell; dreams, absent since his brothers threw him in that pit for having them (37:5-11), returning as someone else's and as the thing that will lift him out; the land of the Hebrews, naming Canaan by the outsider-word last heard of Abram; and the chief of the executioners still, quietly, putting Joseph in charge of things.

Echoes planted. A cupbearer who will remember two years too late — "I remember my faults today" (41:9); Pharaoh's own pair of dreams, and an Egyptian court that again has no interpreter (41:8); "it is not in me — God will answer" (41:16), Joseph saying verse 8 again in front of a king; and zakhar, the remembering that always precedes a rescue in this book, waiting to be done properly.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 41 — two years later Pharaoh dreams of seven fat cows and seven gaunt ones, and no one in Egypt can read it; the cupbearer finally remembers; and a thirty-year-old Hebrew prisoner is shaved, dressed, and brought from the pit to the throne room to say "it is not in me."

Genesis 41 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיְהִי, מִקֵּץ שְׁנָתַיִם יָמִים; וּפַרְעֹה חֹלֵם, וְהִנֵּה עֹמֵד עַל-הַיְאֹר.
And so it was, at the end of two full years, that Pharaoh was dreaming — and look, he was standing by the Nile.note
2
וְהִנֵּה מִן-הַיְאֹר, עֹלֹת שֶׁבַע פָּרוֹת, יְפוֹת מַרְאֶה, וּבְרִיאֹת בָּשָׂר; וַתִּרְעֶינָה, בָּאָחוּ.
And look — out of the Nile came up seven cows, lovely to look at and fat of flesh, and they grazed in the reed-grass.note
3
וְהִנֵּה שֶׁבַע פָּרוֹת אֲחֵרוֹת, עֹלוֹת אַחֲרֵיהֶן מִן-הַיְאֹר, רָעוֹת מַרְאֶה, וְדַקּוֹת בָּשָׂר; וַתַּעֲמֹדְנָה אֵצֶל הַפָּרוֹת, עַל-שְׂפַת הַיְאֹר.
And look — seven other cows came up after them out of the Nile, bad to look at and thin of flesh, and they stood beside the cows on the bank of the Nile.note
4
וַתֹּאכַלְנָה הַפָּרוֹת, רָעוֹת הַמַּרְאֶה וְדַקֹּת הַבָּשָׂר, אֵת שֶׁבַע הַפָּרוֹת, יְפֹת הַמַּרְאֶה וְהַבְּרִיאֹת; וַיִּיקַץ, פַּרְעֹה.
And the cows that were bad to look at and thin of flesh ate up the seven cows that were lovely to look at and fat. And Pharaoh woke.note
5
וַיִּישָׁן, וַיַּחֲלֹם שֵׁנִית; וְהִנֵּה שֶׁבַע שִׁבֳּלִים, עֹלוֹת בְּקָנֶה אֶחָד--בְּרִיאוֹת וְטֹבוֹת.
And he slept and dreamed a second time — and look, seven ears of grain were coming up on one stalk, fat and good.note
6
וְהִנֵּה שֶׁבַע שִׁבֳּלִים, דַּקּוֹת וּשְׁדוּפֹת קָדִים--צֹמְחוֹת, אַחֲרֵיהֶן.
And look — seven ears, thin and scorched by the east wind, were sprouting after them.note
7
וַתִּבְלַעְנָה, הַשִּׁבֳּלִים הַדַּקּוֹת, אֵת שֶׁבַע הַשִּׁבֳּלִים, הַבְּרִיאוֹת וְהַמְּלֵאוֹת; וַיִּיקַץ פַּרְעֹה, וְהִנֵּה חֲלוֹם.
And the thin ears swallowed the seven fat and full ears. And Pharaoh woke — and look, it was a dream.note
8
וַיְהִי בַבֹּקֶר, וַתִּפָּעֶם רוּחוֹ, וַיִּשְׁלַח וַיִּקְרָא אֶת-כָּל-חַרְטֻמֵּי מִצְרַיִם, וְאֶת-כָּל-חֲכָמֶיהָ; וַיְסַפֵּר פַּרְעֹה לָהֶם אֶת-חֲלֹמוֹ, וְאֵין-פּוֹתֵר אוֹתָם לְפַרְעֹה.
And so it was in the morning that his spirit was disturbed; and he sent and called all the diviner-priests of Egypt and all its wise men, and Pharaoh recounted his dream to them — and there was no one to interpret them for Pharaoh.note
9
וַיְדַבֵּר שַׂר הַמַּשְׁקִים, אֶת-פַּרְעֹה לֵאמֹר: אֶת-חֲטָאַי, אֲנִי מַזְכִּיר הַיּוֹם.
And the chief of the cupbearers spoke to Pharaoh, saying, "I bring my faults to remembrance today.note
10
פַּרְעֹה, קָצַף עַל-עֲבָדָיו; וַיִּתֵּן אֹתִי בְּמִשְׁמַר, בֵּית שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים--אֹתִי, וְאֵת שַׂר הָאֹפִים.
Pharaoh was furious with his servants, and he put me in custody in the house of the chief of the executioners — me and the chief of the bakers.note
11
וַנַּחַלְמָה חֲלוֹם בְּלַיְלָה אֶחָד, אֲנִי וָהוּא: אִישׁ כְּפִתְרוֹן חֲלֹמוֹ, חָלָמְנוּ.
And we dreamed a dream in one night, he and I; each of us dreamed according to the interpretation of his dream.note
12
וְשָׁם אִתָּנוּ נַעַר עִבְרִי, עֶבֶד לְשַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים, וַנְּסַפֶּר-לוֹ, וַיִּפְתָּר-לָנוּ אֶת-חֲלֹמֹתֵינוּ: אִישׁ כַּחֲלֹמוֹ, פָּתָר.
And there with us was a Hebrew lad, a slave of the chief of the executioners; and we recounted them to him, and he interpreted our dreams for us — to each man according to his dream he interpreted.note
13
וַיְהִי כַּאֲשֶׁר פָּתַר-לָנוּ, כֵּן הָיָה: אֹתִי הֵשִׁיב עַל-כַּנִּי, וְאֹתוֹ תָלָה.
And so it was: as he interpreted for us, so it happened — me he restored to my post, and him he hanged."note
14
וַיִּשְׁלַח פַּרְעֹה וַיִּקְרָא אֶת-יוֹסֵף, וַיְרִיצֻהוּ מִן-הַבּוֹר; וַיְגַלַּח וַיְחַלֵּף שִׂמְלֹתָיו, וַיָּבֹא אֶל-פַּרְעֹה.
And Pharaoh sent and called Joseph, and they hurried him out of the pit; and he shaved and changed his garments, and came in to Pharaoh.note
15
וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה, אֶל-יוֹסֵף, חֲלוֹם חָלַמְתִּי, וּפֹתֵר אֵין אֹתוֹ; וַאֲנִי, שָׁמַעְתִּי עָלֶיךָ לֵאמֹר, תִּשְׁמַע חֲלוֹם, לִפְתֹּר אֹתוֹ.
And Pharaoh said to Joseph, "I have dreamed a dream, and there is no one to interpret it; and I have heard it said of you that you hear a dream, to interpret it."note
16
וַיַּעַן יוֹסֵף אֶת-פַּרְעֹה לֵאמֹר, בִּלְעָדָי: אֱלֹהִים, יַעֲנֶה אֶת-שְׁלוֹם פַּרְעֹה.
And Joseph answered Pharaoh, saying, "Not I — God will answer for Pharaoh's peace."note
17
וַיְדַבֵּר פַּרְעֹה, אֶל-יוֹסֵף: בַּחֲלֹמִי, הִנְנִי עֹמֵד עַל-שְׂפַת הַיְאֹר.
And Pharaoh spoke to Joseph: "In my dream — there I was, standing on the bank of the Nile.note
18
וְהִנֵּה מִן-הַיְאֹר, עֹלֹת שֶׁבַע פָּרוֹת, בְּרִיאוֹת בָּשָׂר, וִיפֹת תֹּאַר; וַתִּרְעֶינָה, בָּאָחוּ.
And look — out of the Nile came up seven cows, fat of flesh and beautiful in form, and they grazed in the reed-grass.note
19
וְהִנֵּה שֶׁבַע-פָּרוֹת אֲחֵרוֹת, עֹלוֹת אַחֲרֵיהֶן, דַּלּוֹת וְרָעוֹת תֹּאַר מְאֹד, וְרַקּוֹת בָּשָׂר: לֹא-רָאִיתִי כָהֵנָּה בְּכָל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, לָרֹעַ.
And look — seven other cows came up after them, poor and very bad in form and lean of flesh; I have never seen their like in all the land of Egypt for badness.note
20
וַתֹּאכַלְנָה, הַפָּרוֹת, הָרַקּוֹת, וְהָרָעוֹת--אֵת שֶׁבַע הַפָּרוֹת הָרִאשֹׁנוֹת, הַבְּרִיאֹת.
And the lean and bad cows ate up the first seven fat cows.note
21
וַתָּבֹאנָה אֶל-קִרְבֶּנָה, וְלֹא נוֹדַע כִּי-בָאוּ אֶל-קִרְבֶּנָה, וּמַרְאֵיהֶן רַע, כַּאֲשֶׁר בַּתְּחִלָּה; וָאִיקָץ.
And they went inside them, and it could not be known that they had gone inside them — their appearance was as bad as at the start. And I woke.note
22
וָאֵרֶא, בַּחֲלֹמִי; וְהִנֵּה שֶׁבַע שִׁבֳּלִים, עֹלֹת בְּקָנֶה אֶחָד--מְלֵאֹת וְטֹבוֹת.
And I saw in my dream — and look, seven ears were coming up on one stalk, full and good.note
23
וְהִנֵּה שֶׁבַע שִׁבֳּלִים, צְנֻמוֹת דַּקּוֹת שְׁדֻפוֹת קָדִים--צֹמְחוֹת, אַחֲרֵיהֶם.
And look — seven ears, shrivelled, thin, scorched by the east wind, were sprouting after them.note
24
וַתִּבְלַעְןָ הַשִּׁבֳּלִים הַדַּקֹּת, אֵת שֶׁבַע הַשִּׁבֳּלִים הַטֹּבוֹת; וָאֹמַר, אֶל-הַחַרְטֻמִּים, וְאֵין מַגִּיד, לִי.
And the thin ears swallowed the seven good ears. And I told it to the diviner-priests, and there was no one to declare it to me."note
25
וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל-פַּרְעֹה, חֲלוֹם פַּרְעֹה אֶחָד הוּא: אֵת אֲשֶׁר הָאֱלֹהִים עֹשֶׂה, הִגִּיד לְפַרְעֹה.
And Joseph said to Pharaoh, "Pharaoh's dream is one. What God is about to do he has declared to Pharaoh.note
26
שֶׁבַע פָּרֹת הַטֹּבֹת, שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים הֵנָּה, וְשֶׁבַע הַשִּׁבֳּלִים הַטֹּבֹת, שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים הֵנָּה: חֲלוֹם, אֶחָד הוּא.
The seven good cows are seven years, and the seven good ears are seven years — the dream is one.note
27
וְשֶׁבַע הַפָּרוֹת הָרַקּוֹת וְהָרָעֹת הָעֹלֹת אַחֲרֵיהֶן, שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים הֵנָּה, וְשֶׁבַע הַשִּׁבֳּלִים הָרֵקוֹת, שְׁדֻפוֹת הַקָּדִים--יִהְיוּ, שֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי רָעָב.
And the seven lean and bad cows that came up after them are seven years, and the seven empty ears scorched by the east wind — they will be seven years of famine.note
28
הוּא הַדָּבָר, אֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתִּי אֶל-פַּרְעֹה: אֲשֶׁר הָאֱלֹהִים עֹשֶׂה, הֶרְאָה אֶת-פַּרְעֹה.
This is the thing I have spoken to Pharaoh: what God is about to do he has shown Pharaoh.note
29
הִנֵּה שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים, בָּאוֹת--שָׂבָע גָּדוֹל, בְּכָל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם.
Look — seven years are coming of great plenty in all the land of Egypt.note
30
וְקָמוּ שֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי רָעָב, אַחֲרֵיהֶן, וְנִשְׁכַּח כָּל-הַשָּׂבָע, בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם; וְכִלָּה הָרָעָב, אֶת-הָאָרֶץ.
And seven years of famine will rise after them, and all the plenty will be forgotten in the land of Egypt, and the famine will finish off the land.note
31
וְלֹא-יִוָּדַע הַשָּׂבָע בָּאָרֶץ, מִפְּנֵי הָרָעָב הַהוּא אַחֲרֵי-כֵן: כִּי-כָבֵד הוּא, מְאֹד.
And the plenty will not be known in the land because of that famine afterward, for it will be very heavy.note
32
וְעַל הִשָּׁנוֹת הַחֲלוֹם אֶל-פַּרְעֹה, פַּעֲמָיִם--כִּי-נָכוֹן הַדָּבָר מֵעִם הָאֱלֹהִים, וּמְמַהֵר הָאֱלֹהִים לַעֲשֹׂתוֹ.
And as for the dream being doubled to Pharaoh twice — it is because the thing is established from God, and God is hurrying to do it.note
33
וְעַתָּה יֵרֶא פַרְעֹה, אִישׁ נָבוֹן וְחָכָם; וִישִׁיתֵהוּ, עַל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם.
And now let Pharaoh look out a man of discernment and wisdom, and set him over the land of Egypt.note
34
יַעֲשֶׂה פַרְעֹה, וְיַפְקֵד פְּקִדִים עַל-הָאָרֶץ; וְחִמֵּשׁ אֶת-אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, בְּשֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי הַשָּׂבָע.
Let Pharaoh act, and appoint overseers over the land, and take a fifth of the land of Egypt during the seven years of plenty.note
35
וְיִקְבְּצוּ, אֶת-כָּל-אֹכֶל הַשָּׁנִים הַטֹּבוֹת, הַבָּאֹת, הָאֵלֶּה; וְיִצְבְּרוּ-בָר תַּחַת יַד-פַּרְעֹה, אֹכֶל בֶּעָרִים--וְשָׁמָרוּ.
And let them gather all the food of these good years that are coming, and pile up grain under Pharaoh's hand — food in the cities — and let them keep it.note
36
וְהָיָה הָאֹכֶל לְפִקָּדוֹן, לָאָרֶץ, לְשֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי הָרָעָב, אֲשֶׁר תִּהְיֶיןָ בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם; וְלֹא-תִכָּרֵת הָאָרֶץ, בָּרָעָב.
And the food will be a reserve for the land for the seven years of famine that will be in the land of Egypt, and the land will not be cut off by the famine."note
37
וַיִּיטַב הַדָּבָר, בְּעֵינֵי פַרְעֹה, וּבְעֵינֵי, כָּל-עֲבָדָיו.
And the thing was good in the eyes of Pharaoh and in the eyes of all his servants.note
38
וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה, אֶל-עֲבָדָיו: הֲנִמְצָא כָזֶה--אִישׁ, אֲשֶׁר רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים בּוֹ.
And Pharaoh said to his servants, "Can we find one like this — a man in whom is the spirit of God?"note
39
וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אֶל-יוֹסֵף, אַחֲרֵי הוֹדִיעַ אֱלֹהִים אוֹתְךָ אֶת-כָּל-זֹאת, אֵין-נָבוֹן וְחָכָם, כָּמוֹךָ.
And Pharaoh said to Joseph, "Since God has made all this known to you, there is no one as discerning and wise as you.note
40
אַתָּה תִּהְיֶה עַל-בֵּיתִי, וְעַל-פִּיךָ יִשַּׁק כָּל-עַמִּי; רַק הַכִּסֵּא, אֶגְדַּל מִמֶּךָּ.
You will be over my house, and at your mouth all my people will order themselves; only as to the throne will I be greater than you."note
41
וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה, אֶל-יוֹסֵף: רְאֵה נָתַתִּי אֹתְךָ, עַל כָּל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם.
And Pharaoh said to Joseph, "See — I have set you over all the land of Egypt."note
42
וַיָּסַר פַּרְעֹה אֶת-טַבַּעְתּוֹ מֵעַל יָדוֹ, וַיִּתֵּן אֹתָהּ עַל-יַד יוֹסֵף; וַיַּלְבֵּשׁ אֹתוֹ בִּגְדֵי-שֵׁשׁ, וַיָּשֶׂם רְבִד הַזָּהָב עַל-צַוָּארוֹ.
And Pharaoh took off his signet ring from his hand and put it on Joseph's hand, and clothed him in garments of fine linen, and set the gold collar on his neck.note
43
וַיַּרְכֵּב אֹתוֹ, בְּמִרְכֶּבֶת הַמִּשְׁנֶה אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ, וַיִּקְרְאוּ לְפָנָיו, אַבְרֵךְ; וְנָתוֹן אֹתוֹ, עַל כָּל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם.
And he had him ride in the second chariot that was his, and they called out before him, "Abrekh!" — and he set him over all the land of Egypt.note
44
וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אֶל-יוֹסֵף, אֲנִי פַרְעֹה; וּבִלְעָדֶיךָ, לֹא-יָרִים אִישׁ אֶת-יָדוֹ וְאֶת-רַגְלוֹ--בְּכָל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם.
And Pharaoh said to Joseph, "I am Pharaoh — and without you no man will lift his hand or his foot in all the land of Egypt."note
45
וַיִּקְרָא פַרְעֹה שֵׁם-יוֹסֵף, צָפְנַת פַּעְנֵחַ, וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ אֶת-אָסְנַת בַּת-פּוֹטִי פֶרַע כֹּהֵן אֹן, לְאִשָּׁה; וַיֵּצֵא יוֹסֵף, עַל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם.
And Pharaoh called Joseph's name Zaphenath-paneah, and gave him Asenath the daughter of Potiphera priest of On as a wife. And Joseph went out over the land of Egypt.note
46
וְיוֹסֵף, בֶּן-שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה, בְּעָמְדוֹ, לִפְנֵי פַּרְעֹה מֶלֶךְ-מִצְרָיִם; וַיֵּצֵא יוֹסֵף מִלִּפְנֵי פַרְעֹה, וַיַּעֲבֹר בְּכָל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם.
And Joseph was thirty years old when he stood before Pharaoh king of Egypt. And Joseph went out from before Pharaoh and passed through all the land of Egypt.note
47
וַתַּעַשׂ הָאָרֶץ, בְּשֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי הַשָּׂבָע--לִקְמָצִים.
And in the seven years of plenty the land produced by handfuls.note
48
וַיִּקְבֹּץ אֶת-כָּל-אֹכֶל שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים, אֲשֶׁר הָיוּ בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, וַיִּתֶּן-אֹכֶל, בֶּעָרִים: אֹכֶל שְׂדֵה-הָעִיר אֲשֶׁר סְבִיבֹתֶיהָ, נָתַן בְּתוֹכָהּ.
And he gathered all the food of the seven years that were in the land of Egypt and put food in the cities; the food of the field around each city he put inside it.note
49
וַיִּצְבֹּר יוֹסֵף בָּר כְּחוֹל הַיָּם, הַרְבֵּה מְאֹד--עַד כִּי-חָדַל לִסְפֹּר, כִּי-אֵין מִסְפָּר.
And Joseph piled up grain like the sand of the sea, very much — until he stopped counting, for it was beyond number.note
50
וּלְיוֹסֵף יֻלַּד שְׁנֵי בָנִים, בְּטֶרֶם תָּבוֹא שְׁנַת הָרָעָב, אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה-לּוֹ אָסְנַת, בַּת-פּוֹטִי פֶרַע כֹּהֵן אוֹן.
And to Joseph were born two sons before the year of famine came, whom Asenath the daughter of Potiphera priest of On bore to him.note
51
וַיִּקְרָא יוֹסֵף אֶת-שֵׁם הַבְּכוֹר, מְנַשֶּׁה: כִּי-נַשַּׁנִי אֱלֹהִים אֶת-כָּל-עֲמָלִי, וְאֵת כָּל-בֵּית אָבִי.
And Joseph called the name of the firstborn Manasseh — "for God has made me forget all my toil, and all my father's house."note
52
וְאֵת שֵׁם הַשֵּׁנִי, קָרָא אֶפְרָיִם: כִּי-הִפְרַנִי אֱלֹהִים, בְּאֶרֶץ עָנְיִי.
And the name of the second he called Ephraim — "for God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction."note
53
וַתִּכְלֶינָה, שֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי הַשָּׂבָע, אֲשֶׁר הָיָה, בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם.
And the seven years of plenty that were in the land of Egypt came to an end,note
54
וַתְּחִלֶּינָה שֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי הָרָעָב, לָבוֹא, כַּאֲשֶׁר, אָמַר יוֹסֵף; וַיְהִי רָעָב בְּכָל-הָאֲרָצוֹת, וּבְכָל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם הָיָה לָחֶם.
and the seven years of famine began to come, just as Joseph had said. And there was famine in all the lands, but in all the land of Egypt there was bread.note
55
וַתִּרְעַב כָּל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, וַיִּצְעַק הָעָם אֶל-פַּרְעֹה לַלָּחֶם; וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה לְכָל-מִצְרַיִם לְכוּ אֶל-יוֹסֵף, אֲשֶׁר-יֹאמַר לָכֶם תַּעֲשׂוּ.
And all the land of Egypt was famished, and the people cried out to Pharaoh for bread; and Pharaoh said to all Egypt, "Go to Joseph; whatever he says to you, do."note
56
וְהָרָעָב הָיָה, עַל כָּל-פְּנֵי הָאָרֶץ; וַיִּפְתַּח יוֹסֵף אֶת-כָּל-אֲשֶׁר בָּהֶם, וַיִּשְׁבֹּר לְמִצְרַיִם, וַיֶּחֱזַק הָרָעָב, בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם.
And the famine was over all the face of the earth; and Joseph opened all that was in them and sold grain to Egypt, and the famine grew strong in the land of Egypt.note
57
וְכָל-הָאָרֶץ בָּאוּ מִצְרַיְמָה, לִשְׁבֹּר אֶל-יוֹסֵף: כִּי-חָזַק הָרָעָב, בְּכָל-הָאָרֶץ.
And all the earth came to Egypt to buy grain from Joseph, for the famine was strong in all the earth.note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–7 · two years later, a king dreams

"At the end of two full years." The previous chapter ended with a man forgetting; this one opens by telling you how long the forgetting lasted. Genesis does not describe those two years and does not need to — the reader has been holding the cupbearer's promise since 40:23, and the delay is the point. Nothing happens because someone remembers a favour. Something happens because a king has a bad night.

Cows out of the Nile. The imagery is precisely, verifiably Egyptian. The ye'or — a Hebrew word borrowed from Egyptian itrw, used almost exclusively of the Nile — is the source of everything in that country: its annual flood decides whether there is food, and a low Nile means famine. Cattle standing in the shallows and grazing the achu (reed-grass, another Egyptian loanword) is an ordinary Delta scene. Seven emaciated cows walking up out of the river that feeds Egypt is a nightmare about the river failing, dressed in the country's own furniture. The famine is announced in Egyptian imagery two chapters before anyone says the word.

"Scorched by the east wind." The qadim is the khamsin — the desert wind off the Sahara that arrives in spring and can burn a standing crop in hours. Again the dream is speaking local weather. And note the pairing that runs the whole chapter: the bad devours the good and is no better for it (v. 21, "it could not be known that they had gone inside them") — which is a fair description of what famine does to a granary.

Verses 8–13 · the experts fail, and the debt is remembered

The professionals. Pharaoh summons the chartummim — not "magicians" in the conjuring sense, but the lector-priests of the Egyptian temple establishment, the literate specialists who kept the ritual books and, among much else, read dreams. This is the trade Joseph sidestepped in 40:8, and it is a real profession with real manuals (the Dream Book of Papyrus Chester Beatty III survives). They fail. That is not a slur on Egyptian learning; the narrator is making a narrower claim — that this particular dream is not in the reference books, because it did not come from where dreams usually come from. The word will turn up again in another empire's court, where Babylon's experts fail in exactly the same way in front of exactly the same kind of Hebrew (Daniel 1:20; 2:2). The KJV reads "magicians," the NIV the same, the NWT "magic-practicing priests" — which is clumsy English but closer to the institution.

"I bring my faults to remembrance today." And there it is. The verb is zakhar in its causative form — the cupbearer causes to be remembered — and it is the word the last chapter ended on when he did not remember Joseph and forgot him (40:23). Two years late, prompted entirely by his own exposure in a room where the king is upset and nobody has an answer, he remembers. Read his speech carefully and notice how he tells it: "a Hebrew lad, a slave of the chief of the executioners." Both words are true and both are diminishing, and he never once uses Joseph's name. The man who saved his life is introduced to the throne as an anonymous foreign servant.

Verses 14–24 · out of the pit, and "not I"

"They hurried him out of the pit." Bor — the third and last time this word is used of Joseph, and the only time he comes up out of one. His brothers threw him into a bor at Dothan; he called his Egyptian cell a bor when he asked the cupbearer for help; now they run him out of the bor to stand in front of a king. Every English version that reads "dungeon" in these last two verses (the KJV, ASV and NIV all do) breaks a three-link chain that the Hebrew forges deliberately. This translation reads "pit" all three times.

He shaved. Two words of costume detail, and they are exactly right. Egyptians were a clean-shaven people — priests shaved the whole body, and courtiers wore false beards only as formal regalia — while Semites wore beards; Egyptian tomb painting is unambiguous about the difference. A man cannot be brought before Pharaoh looking like a foreigner out of a cell, so he is shaved and re-dressed. It is also the third time in this story that Joseph's clothes are changed by someone else's decision: the coat stripped by his brothers, the garment seized by his master's wife, and now prison rags exchanged for something fit for a throne room.

"Not I." Pharaoh says he has heard that Joseph hears a dream to interpret it, and Joseph's first word to the most powerful man on earth is biladai — "apart from me," "not I." It is the same thing he said to two prisoners in 40:8, said now where it costs something: the one moment when overstating his gift would have served him, and he refuses the credit before he has been offered anything. The KJV renders it "It is not in me: God shall give Pharaoh an answer of peace," the ASV nearly identically; the NWT "Not I! God will speak concerning Pharaoh's welfare." And that last word is shalom — the word the brothers could not speak to him (37:4) and the errand his father sent him on (37:14). Joseph promises a king the thing his own family withheld.

Pharaoh's retelling is not identical. Compare verses 17-24 with 1-7 and small things have moved: the lean cows are now "poor and very bad in form… I have never seen their like in all the land of Egypt for badness," and there is a whole new detail — they ate the fat cows and you could not tell (v. 21). Hebrew narrative repeats with variation on purpose; a dreamer retelling a nightmare adds what frightened him most. The version Joseph actually interprets is the version with the horror in it.

Verses 25–32 · one dream, and why it came twice

"Pharaoh's dream is one." The first thing Joseph does is reduce two dreams to a single message — cows and grain are the same seven years said twice — and the second thing he does is assign the whole business to God, twice, before he says a word about famine ("what God is about to do he has declared to Pharaoh… has shown Pharaoh"). Only then does he give the reading. The order matters: he is not selling a service.

And now the sentence this whole book has been waiting to say. "As for the dream being doubled to Pharaoh twice — it is because the thing is established from God, and God is hurrying to do it." That is the interpretive key to every doubled dream in Genesis, supplied at last by the man whose own two dreams — sheaves and stars — were the doubled pair his brothers hated him for. He is explaining, to a foreign king, the rule that governs his own life; and by this rule the boy's dreams were never adolescent boasting but a settled decree, and his brothers will bow. He does not appear to notice. Two chapters from now they will walk in and do it.

Seven-year famines are not a literary invention. An Egyptian text known as the Famine Stele, carved on Sehel Island near Aswan, tells of a seven-year famine under the Third Dynasty king Djoser and a low Nile that would not rise. ⚠️ The inscription itself is Ptolemaic — roughly two thousand years later than the reign it describes — so it is evidence for the seven-year famine as an established Egyptian tradition, not independent confirmation of this chapter. Egypt's food supply hung on the flood, records of Nile levels were kept for millennia, and successive lean years were a standing national fear. The dream is speaking to a real anxiety.

Verses 33–36 · the interpreter writes a policy

Nobody asked him for this. Pharaoh requested an interpretation; Joseph supplies one and then keeps talking — appoint overseers, take a fifth in the good years, store it in the cities under royal control, hold it as a reserve. It is a complete grain policy, offered unbidden by a prisoner who has been in the room for about four minutes. And it is the reason he gets the job: verse 37 says "the thing was good in the eyes of Pharaoh," and "the thing" is the plan, not the prophecy.

"A man of discernment and wisdom." Navon ve-chakham — the pairing of binah, the discernment that tells things apart, with chokhmah, the practical master-skill. It is the vocabulary of the wisdom books, and Pharaoh will hand it straight back in verse 39. ⚠️ Interpreters differ on whether Joseph is angling: he describes an office and does not name himself, which is either admirable restraint or the oldest move in the book. Genesis, characteristically, does not say.

A fifth. A 20% levy in the fat years — high, but not confiscatory, and Egypt is later described as living under a permanent one-fifth tax that dates from this administration (47:26). ⚠️ Worth reading on to the end of that story before calling this a straightforward rescue: by chapter 47 the same policy has taken the Egyptians' money, then their livestock, then their land, then their persons, and Genesis reports it flatly, without praise or apology. The plan that saves lives also builds a state monopoly, and both halves of that are in the text.

Verses 37–44 · slave to vizier in an afternoon

"A man in whom is the spirit of God." Said by an Egyptian king, about a Hebrew convict, in front of his court — and it is the same diagnosis Potiphar made silently in 39:3 ("his master saw that Jehovah was with him") and the prison keeper made after him. Three foreign employers in a row reach the same conclusion about the same man.
