Pharaoh names Elohim, the general word for God, not the divine name; the narrator lets the pagan king's theology stand as he said it.

The regalia is documented Egyptian practice. Every item in verses 42-43 is attested: the transfer of the royal signet ring as delegation of authority to seal documents in the king's name; fine linen (shesh — the Hebrew word is itself Egyptian) as the dress of rank; the gold collar, the shebyu-necklace, a specific decoration shown in tomb paintings being hung on officials at their investiture; and the second chariot, riding in the vehicle immediately behind the king's. The chapter knows what an Egyptian promotion looked like. And there is a quiet rhyme in the ring: the last man in this book to hand over a seal was Judah, surrendering his identity to a stranger by a roadside; here a king gives his away to make a slave into himself.

"Abrekh!" — and nobody knows what it means. The word is a complete mystery: a Hebrew guess from barakh "to kneel" gives the KJV's and ASV's "Bow the knee"; an Egyptian derivation (ib-r-k, "attention!" or a title like "chief steward") gives the NIV's "Make way!"; an Akkadian loanword abarakku, a high household officer, is a third serious proposal. The NWT simply transliterates — "Abrekh!" — and this translation does the same, because a herald's shout in a foreign court that no one has successfully translated in two thousand years should look like what it is. It is one of the small honest pleasures of the Hebrew Bible that a word can survive perfectly intact and completely opaque.

Verses 45–49 · an Egyptian name, an Egyptian wife, thirteen years

Zaphenath-paneah. Pharaoh renames him, as conquerors and courts do — the same move Babylon makes on four Hebrew youths in Daniel 1, and the inverse of God renaming Abram. The meaning is disputed: the most widely accepted Egyptian reconstruction is something like ḏd-pꜣ-nṯr-iw.f-ꜥnḫ, "the god speaks and he lives"; older Jewish tradition read it through Hebrew as "revealer of secrets." No one is certain. What is certain is that the name is genuinely Egyptian in shape, as are Asenath, Potiphera and On — this chapter's proper nouns behave like real Egyptian, whatever century they come from.

The marriage Genesis does not fuss about. Joseph is given the daughter of a priest of the sun-cult at On — Heliopolis — which makes the mother of two Israelite tribes an Egyptian priest's daughter. Abraham sent a servant a thousand miles to avoid a Canaanite bride for Isaac; Judah's Canaanite marriage in 38:2 was reported without comment and went badly. Here the narrator says nothing at all, and it goes fine. Later readers were less comfortable — a Hellenistic novella, Joseph and Aseneth, exists largely to convert her first.

Thirty years old. The one hard number in the chapter, and it does arithmetic on the whole story: he was seventeen when his brothers sold him. Thirteen years — as a slave, as a steward, as a convict, as a forgotten man — compressed into the white space between two verses. Genesis never once tells us what he felt during them.

Verses 50–57 · two names, and the whole world at the door

Manasseh: "God has made me forget." The name puns on nashani, and the sentence he attaches to it is one of the strangest in Genesis: "God has made me forget all my toil, and all my father's house." He is not only naming a son; he is announcing that the ache has stopped — including the ache of home. Given what the reader knows is coming up the road with money in its hands, the line is nearly unbearable. And it will be answered: at the end, Joseph will weep over the very house he claims to have forgotten.

Ephraim: "God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction." Hiphrani, punning on the name, with oni — affliction, misery — for the country where he is now the second most powerful man alive. Both names credit God twice, and both are honest in a way that a purely triumphant man's names would not be: the second son's name concedes, in a single word, that Egypt is still where he was hurt. KJV "the land of my affliction," and no version has improved on it.

"Go to Joseph." Pharaoh's instruction to a starving nation (v. 55) is the sentence the whole chapter has been building toward, and it makes the trap: everyone who is hungry, anywhere, must come and stand in front of this man. The chapter closes with "all the earth came to Egypt to buy grain from Joseph," which is a statement about the scale of the famine and, at the same time, the machinery clicking into place. Somewhere in Canaan there is a hungry old man with eleven sons left. Two of the dreams that started all this involved bowing.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "pit" for bor a third time (v. 14), completing the chain from 37:24 and 40:15 that every major version breaks by reading "dungeon"; "Abrekh!" left untranslated because two thousand years of guessing have not settled it (with the NWT, against the KJV's committed "Bow the knee"); "diviner-priests" for chartummim rather than the conjuring-word "magicians"; "Not I" for the blunt biladai; "peace" keeping shalom visible in Joseph's promise to Pharaoh; and "the land of my affliction" left as Joseph said it.

Echoes paid. Zakhar — "I bring my faults to remembrance today" (v. 9), two years after the cupbearer forgot; the pit, entered at Dothan and finally exited here; "the doubled dream" (v. 32) supplying at last the rule for reading Joseph's own two dreams; "Do not interpretations belong to God?" repeated in front of a king as "Not I"; the diviner-priests who cannot read a dream, exactly as Babylon's experts later cannot; shalom, unspeakable by his brothers, promised to Pharaoh; and the signet — pledged away by Judah, handed over by a king.

Echoes planted. "Go to Joseph" and "all the earth came to Egypt" — the brothers are one verse of the next chapter away, and they will bow (42:6), which is the dream of 37:7 coming due; Manasseh's "God has made me forget… all my father's house," waiting to be undone by the sight of Benjamin; the one-fifth policy, which by 47:26 will have bought Egypt itself; Jacob crossing his hands over these two boys to put the younger first (48:14); and Joseph's final verdict on the whole thirteen years, still nine chapters off — "you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good" (50:20).

Next installment in this book: Genesis 42 — ten brothers come down to Egypt to buy grain and bow to a governor they do not recognize; he knows them at once, speaks harshly through an interpreter, accuses them of spying, and keeps Simeon in chains — and the brothers say to each other, not knowing he understands every word, "we are guilty concerning our brother."

Genesis 42 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיַּרְא יַעֲקֹב, כִּי יֶשׁ-שֶׁבֶר בְּמִצְרָיִם; וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב לְבָנָיו, לָמָּה תִּתְרָאוּ.
And Jacob saw that there was grain in Egypt, and Jacob said to his sons, "Why do you keep looking at one another?"note
2
וַיֹּאמֶר--הִנֵּה שָׁמַעְתִּי, כִּי יֶשׁ-שֶׁבֶר בְּמִצְרָיִם; רְדוּ-שָׁמָּה וְשִׁבְרוּ-לָנוּ מִשָּׁם, וְנִחְיֶה וְלֹא נָמוּת.
And he said, "Look — I have heard that there is grain in Egypt. Go down there and buy grain for us from there, that we may live and not die."note
3
וַיֵּרְדוּ אֲחֵי-יוֹסֵף, עֲשָׂרָה, לִשְׁבֹּר בָּר, מִמִּצְרָיִם.
And Joseph's brothers went down, ten of them, to buy grain from Egypt.note
4
וְאֶת-בִּנְיָמִין אֲחִי יוֹסֵף, לֹא-שָׁלַח יַעֲקֹב אֶת-אֶחָיו: כִּי אָמַר, פֶּן-יִקְרָאֶנּוּ אָסוֹן.
But Benjamin, Joseph's brother, Jacob did not send with his brothers, for he said, "Otherwise harm may befall him."note
5
וַיָּבֹאוּ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, לִשְׁבֹּר בְּתוֹךְ הַבָּאִים: כִּי-הָיָה הָרָעָב, בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן.
And the sons of Israel came to buy grain among those who came, for the famine was in the land of Canaan.note
6
וְיוֹסֵף, הוּא הַשַּׁלִּיט עַל-הָאָרֶץ--הוּא הַמַּשְׁבִּיר, לְכָל-עַם הָאָרֶץ; וַיָּבֹאוּ אֲחֵי יוֹסֵף, וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ-לוֹ אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה.
And Joseph — he was the ruler over the land; he was the one who sold grain to all the people of the land. And Joseph's brothers came and bowed down to him, faces to the ground.note
7
וַיַּרְא יוֹסֵף אֶת-אֶחָיו, וַיַּכִּרֵם; וַיִּתְנַכֵּר אֲלֵיהֶם וַיְדַבֵּר אִתָּם קָשׁוֹת, וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם מֵאַיִן בָּאתֶם, וַיֹּאמְרוּ, מֵאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן לִשְׁבָּר-אֹכֶל.
And Joseph saw his brothers and recognized them — and made himself unrecognizable to them, and spoke harshly with them. And he said to them, "Where have you come from?" And they said, "From the land of Canaan, to buy food."note
8
וַיַּכֵּר יוֹסֵף, אֶת-אֶחָיו; וְהֵם, לֹא הִכִּרֻהוּ.
And Joseph recognized his brothers, but they did not recognize him.note
9
וַיִּזְכֹּר יוֹסֵף--אֵת הַחֲלֹמוֹת, אֲשֶׁר חָלַם לָהֶם; וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם מְרַגְּלִים אַתֶּם, לִרְאוֹת אֶת-עֶרְוַת הָאָרֶץ בָּאתֶם.
And Joseph remembered the dreams that he had dreamed about them, and he said to them, "You are spies! You have come to see the nakedness of the land."note
10
וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלָיו, לֹא אֲדֹנִי; וַעֲבָדֶיךָ בָּאוּ, לִשְׁבָּר-אֹכֶל.
And they said to him, "No, my lord — your servants have come to buy food.note
11
כֻּלָּנוּ, בְּנֵי אִישׁ-אֶחָד נָחְנוּ; כֵּנִים אֲנַחְנוּ, לֹא-הָיוּ עֲבָדֶיךָ מְרַגְּלִים.
All of us are the sons of one man; we are honest men. Your servants have never been spies."note
12
וַיֹּאמֶר, אֲלֵהֶם: לֹא, כִּי-עֶרְוַת הָאָרֶץ בָּאתֶם לִרְאוֹת.
And he said to them, "No — you have come to see the nakedness of the land."note
13
וַיֹּאמְרוּ, שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר עֲבָדֶיךָ אַחִים אֲנַחְנוּ בְּנֵי אִישׁ-אֶחָד--בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן; וְהִנֵּה הַקָּטֹן אֶת-אָבִינוּ הַיּוֹם, וְהָאֶחָד אֵינֶנּוּ.
And they said, "Your servants are twelve brothers, the sons of one man in the land of Canaan; and look — the youngest is with our father today, and the one is not."note
14
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם, יוֹסֵף: הוּא, אֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתִּי אֲלֵכֶם לֵאמֹר--מְרַגְּלִים אַתֶּם.
And Joseph said to them, "That is what I said to you: you are spies.note
15
בְּזֹאת, תִּבָּחֵנוּ: חֵי פַרְעֹה אִם-תֵּצְאוּ מִזֶּה, כִּי אִם-בְּבוֹא אֲחִיכֶם הַקָּטֹן הֵנָּה.
By this you will be tested: as Pharaoh lives, you will not go out from here unless your youngest brother comes here.note
16
שִׁלְחוּ מִכֶּם אֶחָד, וְיִקַּח אֶת-אֲחִיכֶם, וְאַתֶּם הֵאָסְרוּ, וְיִבָּחֲנוּ דִּבְרֵיכֶם הַאֱמֶת אִתְּכֶם; וְאִם-לֹא--חֵי פַרְעֹה, כִּי מְרַגְּלִים אַתֶּם.
Send one of you, and let him fetch your brother, and you will be bound, and your words will be tested — whether the truth is with you. And if not, as Pharaoh lives, you are spies."note
17
וַיֶּאֱסֹף אֹתָם אֶל-מִשְׁמָר, שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים.
And he gathered them into custody for three days.note
18
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יוֹסֵף בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי, זֹאת עֲשׂוּ וִחְיוּ; אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים, אֲנִי יָרֵא.
And on the third day Joseph said to them, "Do this and live — I fear God.note
19
אִם-כֵּנִים אַתֶּם--אֲחִיכֶם אֶחָד, יֵאָסֵר בְּבֵית מִשְׁמַרְכֶם; וְאַתֶּם לְכוּ הָבִיאוּ, שֶׁבֶר רַעֲבוֹן בָּתֵּיכֶם.
If you are honest men, let one of your brothers be bound in your place of custody, and you go and carry grain for the hunger of your households.note
20
וְאֶת-אֲחִיכֶם הַקָּטֹן תָּבִיאוּ אֵלַי, וְיֵאָמְנוּ דִבְרֵיכֶם וְלֹא תָמוּתוּ; וַיַּעֲשׂוּ-כֵן.
And bring your youngest brother to me, and your words will be confirmed and you will not die." And they did so.note
21
וַיֹּאמְרוּ אִישׁ אֶל-אָחִיו, אֲבָל אֲשֵׁמִים אֲנַחְנוּ עַל-אָחִינוּ, אֲשֶׁר רָאִינוּ צָרַת נַפְשׁוֹ בְּהִתְחַנְנוֹ אֵלֵינוּ, וְלֹא שָׁמָעְנוּ; עַל-כֵּן בָּאָה אֵלֵינוּ, הַצָּרָה הַזֹּאת.
And they said, each to his brother, "Truly we are guilty concerning our brother — that we saw the distress of his soul when he pleaded with us, and we did not listen. That is why this distress has come upon us."note
22
וַיַּעַן רְאוּבֵן אֹתָם לֵאמֹר, הֲלוֹא אָמַרְתִּי אֲלֵיכֶם לֵאמֹר אַל-תֶּחֶטְאוּ בַיֶּלֶד--וְלֹא שְׁמַעְתֶּם; וְגַם-דָּמוֹ, הִנֵּה נִדְרָשׁ.
And Reuben answered them, saying, "Did I not say to you, 'Do not sin against the boy'? And you did not listen. And now — look — his blood is being required."note
23
וְהֵם לֹא יָדְעוּ, כִּי שֹׁמֵעַ יוֹסֵף: כִּי הַמֵּלִיץ, בֵּינֹתָם.
And they did not know that Joseph was listening, for the interpreter was between them.note
24
וַיִּסֹּב מֵעֲלֵיהֶם, וַיֵּבְךְּ; וַיָּשָׁב אֲלֵהֶם, וַיְדַבֵּר אֲלֵהֶם, וַיִּקַּח מֵאִתָּם אֶת-שִׁמְעוֹן, וַיֶּאֱסֹר אֹתוֹ לְעֵינֵיהֶם.
And he turned away from them and wept; and he returned to them and spoke to them, and took Simeon from among them and bound him before their eyes.note
25
וַיְצַו יוֹסֵף, וַיְמַלְאוּ אֶת-כְּלֵיהֶם בָּר, וּלְהָשִׁיב כַּסְפֵּיהֶם אִישׁ אֶל-שַׂקּוֹ, וְלָתֵת לָהֶם צֵדָה לַדָּרֶךְ; וַיַּעַשׂ לָהֶם, כֵּן.
And Joseph gave orders, and they filled their vessels with grain, and returned each man's silver to his sack, and gave them provisions for the road; and so it was done for them.note
26
וַיִּשְׂאוּ אֶת-שִׁבְרָם, עַל-חֲמֹרֵיהֶם; וַיֵּלְכוּ, מִשָּׁם.
And they loaded their grain on their donkeys and went from there.note
27
וַיִּפְתַּח הָאֶחָד אֶת-שַׂקּוֹ, לָתֵת מִסְפּוֹא לַחֲמֹרוֹ--בַּמָּלוֹן; וַיַּרְא, אֶת-כַּסְפּוֹ, וְהִנֵּה-הוּא, בְּפִי אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ.
And one of them opened his sack to give fodder to his donkey at the lodging place, and he saw his silver — and look, it was in the mouth of his bag.note
28
וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל-אֶחָיו הוּשַׁב כַּסְפִּי, וְגַם הִנֵּה בְאַמְתַּחְתִּי; וַיֵּצֵא לִבָּם, וַיֶּחֶרְדוּ אִישׁ אֶל-אָחִיו לֵאמֹר, מַה-זֹּאת עָשָׂה אֱלֹהִים, לָנוּ.
And he said to his brothers, "My silver has been returned — and look, it is even in my bag!" And their heart went out of them, and they turned trembling each to his brother, saying, "What is this God has done to us?"note
29
וַיָּבֹאוּ אֶל-יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם, אַרְצָה כְּנָעַן; וַיַּגִּידוּ לוֹ, אֵת כָּל-הַקֹּרֹת אֹתָם לֵאמֹר.
And they came to Jacob their father in the land of Canaan and told him all that had befallen them, saying,note
30
דִּבֶּר הָאִישׁ אֲדֹנֵי הָאָרֶץ, אִתָּנוּ--קָשׁוֹת; וַיִּתֵּן אֹתָנוּ, כִּמְרַגְּלִים אֶת-הָאָרֶץ.
"The man, the lord of the land, spoke harshly with us and treated us as spies of the land.note
31
וַנֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו, כֵּנִים אֲנָחְנוּ: לֹא הָיִינוּ, מְרַגְּלִים.
And we said to him, 'We are honest men; we have never been spies.note
32
שְׁנֵים-עָשָׂר אֲנַחְנוּ אַחִים, בְּנֵי אָבִינוּ; הָאֶחָד אֵינֶנּוּ, וְהַקָּטֹן הַיּוֹם אֶת-אָבִינוּ בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן.
We are twelve brothers, sons of our father; the one is not, and the youngest is with our father today in the land of Canaan.'note
33
וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלֵינוּ, הָאִישׁ אֲדֹנֵי הָאָרֶץ, בְּזֹאת אֵדַע, כִּי כֵנִים אַתֶּם: אֲחִיכֶם הָאֶחָד הַנִּיחוּ אִתִּי, וְאֶת-רַעֲבוֹן בָּתֵּיכֶם קְחוּ וָלֵכוּ.
And the man, the lord of the land, said to us, 'By this I will know that you are honest men: leave one of your brothers with me, and take grain for the hunger of your households and go.note
34
וְהָבִיאוּ אֶת-אֲחִיכֶם הַקָּטֹן, אֵלַי, וְאֵדְעָה כִּי לֹא מְרַגְּלִים אַתֶּם, כִּי כֵנִים אַתֶּם; אֶת-אֲחִיכֶם אֶתֵּן לָכֶם, וְאֶת-הָאָרֶץ תִּסְחָרוּ.
And bring your youngest brother to me, and I will know that you are not spies but honest men. I will give you your brother, and you may trade in the land.'"note
35
וַיְהִי, הֵם מְרִיקִים שַׂקֵּיהֶם, וְהִנֵּה-אִישׁ צְרוֹר-כַּסְפּוֹ, בְּשַׂקּוֹ; וַיִּרְאוּ אֶת-צְרֹרוֹת כַּסְפֵּיהֶם, הֵמָּה וַאֲבִיהֶם--וַיִּירָאוּ.
And so it was, as they were emptying their sacks, that look — each man's bundle of silver was in his sack; and they saw their bundles of silver, they and their father, and they were afraid.note
36
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם, אֹתִי שִׁכַּלְתֶּם: יוֹסֵף אֵינֶנּוּ, וְשִׁמְעוֹן אֵינֶנּוּ, וְאֶת-בִּנְיָמִן תִּקָּחוּ, עָלַי הָיוּ כֻלָּנָה.
And Jacob their father said to them, "You have bereaved me! Joseph is not, and Simeon is not, and you would take Benjamin. All of it has come upon me!"note
37
וַיֹּאמֶר רְאוּבֵן, אֶל-אָבִיו לֵאמֹר, אֶת-שְׁנֵי בָנַי תָּמִית, אִם-לֹא אֲבִיאֶנּוּ אֵלֶיךָ; תְּנָה אֹתוֹ עַל-יָדִי, וַאֲנִי אֲשִׁיבֶנּוּ אֵלֶיךָ.
And Reuben spoke to his father, saying, "You may put my two sons to death if I do not bring him back to you. Put him in my hand, and I will return him to you."note
38
וַיֹּאמֶר, לֹא-יֵרֵד בְּנִי עִמָּכֶם: כִּי-אָחִיו מֵת וְהוּא לְבַדּוֹ נִשְׁאָר, וּקְרָאָהוּ אָסוֹן בַּדֶּרֶךְ אֲשֶׁר תֵּלְכוּ-בָהּ, וְהוֹרַדְתֶּם אֶת-שֵׂיבָתִי בְּיָגוֹן, שְׁאוֹלָה.
And he said, "My son will not go down with you, for his brother is dead and he alone is left; and if harm should befall him on the road you are going, you would bring down my grey head in sorrow to Sheol."note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–5 · "why do you keep looking at one another?"

A famine, and a family paralysed. Jacob's first line is impatience: lammah titra'u — literally "why are you looking at each other?" It is the sound of ten grown men standing in a yard avoiding a decision, and their father snapping at them to move. The KJV's "why do ye look one upon another?" keeps it exactly; the NIV softens to "why do you just keep looking at each other?" — the same picture. But there may be more in it. The one place these men are being told to go is Egypt, and the last thing that went to Egypt from this family was their brother. They know something their father does not, and the narrator has just put them in a room where nobody will meet anyone's eye.

"Go down." Redu — down to Egypt, the same verb that carried Joseph down (39:1) and that Judah used when he "went down from his brothers" (38:1). Egypt is always down in this book, geographically and otherwise.

Benjamin held back. "Joseph's brother" — the narrator's phrase, not Jacob's, and it tells you why the boy stays home: he is the other son of Rachel, and Jacob has already lost one on a journey. The reason given is ason, "harm, calamity" — a rare and heavy word that Jacob will use again at the chapter's end and once more in ch. 44. Note what it implies about the ten: their father does not trust them with this one. He does not say so. He just does not send him.

Verses 6–13 · the dream comes true, and a verb turns inside out

"And they bowed down to him, faces to the ground." Twenty-two years and five chapters after a seventeen-year-old told his brothers that their sheaves came around and bowed down to his sheaf — and they hated him for it and sold him to stop it — they do it. The narrator does not comment. He does not need to: he has just written the sentence the whole book has been aimed at, and put it in a single clause in the middle of a verse about grain administration. The dream did not come true despite the pit; it came true through it.

The chapter's masterstroke, in one word. Verse 7: Joseph saw them and recognized them — vayyakkirem — and made himself unrecognizable to them — vayyitnakker. Same root, nakar, twice in four words, in opposite directions: to know a face, and to make your own face unknown. English cannot do it in one word, and most versions do not try — the KJV reads "he knew them, but made himself strange unto them," which is actually the best of them and preserves the reversal; the NIV's "he pretended to be a stranger" reads smoothly and loses the pun entirely. This translation uses "recognized / made himself unrecognizable" to keep the seam visible. And note where this verb has been: Isaac failed to recognize the son in front of him; Jacob recognized a coat that was lying to him; Judah recognized his own seal and confessed. Now the fourth turn: a man recognizes his family perfectly and chooses to be unrecognized.

"And Joseph remembered the dreams." Vayyizkor — the book's rescue-verb, and here it is doing something more complicated. He remembers, and immediately accuses them of espionage. Readers have split on this for two thousand years: is this vengeance, a man with absolute power toying with the brothers who ruined him? Or is it the beginning of a deliberate test — a controlled reconstruction of the original crime (a favoured younger son, a chance to abandon him) to find out whether they are the same men? The text withholds a motive here and gives us one only in ch. 45. This translation lets the ambiguity stand, because it is genuinely there, and a reader who feels uneasy in this chapter is reading it correctly.

"The nakedness of the land." Ervah — the word for shameful exposure, used of a body. Applied to a country it means the undefended places, the weak points, and it is a plausible charge: Canaanites arriving in a group during a famine, at Egypt's vulnerable eastern approach, is exactly what a border official would look at twice. The accusation is not absurd. It is simply not true.

"And the one is not." Ve-ha-echad einennu. They tell the truth without telling it — three words, spoken to the man they are talking about, and it is the same phrase Reuben used at the empty pit, "the boy is not." They have been saying it for twenty-two years and it has never once been true.

Verses 14–20 · three days, and "I fear God"

Three days in custody. He puts them where he was — mishmar, the same word for the ward he was held in (40:3) — and for three days. Whatever else is happening, the man is giving his brothers a very short taste of a thing he had thirteen years of.

He changes the terms. His first demand (v. 16) was that nine stay bound and one go; on the third day he inverts it — one stays, nine go home with food. That is a softening, and it is the first evidence in the chapter that this is not simple cruelty: a man taking revenge does not reduce the sentence after sleeping on it.

"I fear God." Said to ten frightened foreigners by an Egyptian official with total power over them, and it is the most reassuring sentence available in the ancient world — it means there is a limit on what I will do to you. It is also, for the reader, the tell: this is the man who told Potiphar's wife he would not "sin against God," speaking now in an Egyptian accent through an interpreter, saying the one thing that would mean nothing to an Egyptian and everything to a son of Jacob — if only they could hear it.

Verses 21–24 · what they heard him do, and never told

The detail Genesis kept back for five chapters. "We saw the distress of his soul when he pleaded with us, and we did not listen." Go back to 37:24 and read it again: the brothers strip Joseph, throw him in the pit, and sit down to eat bread. The narrator never said Joseph begged. He never said a word about the boy in the hole at all. It is only now, twenty-two years later, in a foreign prison, that his brothers supply the sound-track — and the withholding was deliberate. Genesis let us watch the scene in silence so that we would hear the pleading for the first time exactly when they finally admit it. It is one of the great strokes of narrative in the Hebrew Bible, and it costs the reader something.

"We are guilty." Ashemim anachnu — the language of real culpability, not regret. And notice the theology in the second half: "that is why this distress has come upon us." They read their trouble as a moral ledger settling. They happen to be wrong about the mechanism — this is not heaven's justice, it is their brother running a test — but they are not wrong about the debt. The KJV's "We are verily guilty concerning our brother" is exactly right.

Reuben, being Reuben. The eldest's contribution is "I told you so" — accurate (he did try, 37:22) and useless, and delivered while his brother sits in a cell. He is the man who always says the right thing slightly too late to matter, and the narrative has been consistent about him since the pit.

"For the interpreter was between them." The single most quietly devastating line in the chapter. They speak freely in Hebrew in front of the Egyptian official because a melits stands between them — and he understands every syllable. Note also what this tells you about the disguise: Joseph has been speaking Egyptian this whole time, through a translator, to his own family. Then he turns away and weeps — the first of the times he will do this, each one closer to breaking — comes back, and binds Simeon in front of them. ⚠️ Why Simeon? The text does not say. He is the second-oldest, so taking him spares Reuben (who tried to save Joseph); he is also, with Levi, one of the two who massacred Shechem, so the reader has grounds to think of him as the family's dangerous man. Jewish tradition adds that Simeon was the one who threw Joseph into the pit. The text is silent; the silence is the kind this book keeps on purpose.

Verses 25–28 · the money in the sack

He will not take their money. Joseph fills the sacks, returns every man's silver, and adds provisions for the road. It is generosity delivered as a threat: ten men who have just been accused of espionage discover they are carrying, unexplained, the payment they thought they had made. Nobody in Egypt will believe they did not steal it. Whatever he intended, the effect is that they cannot come back and cannot stay away.

"Their heart went out of them." A physical idiom for panic — vayyetse libbam, the heart leaving the body. And their question is the chapter's second theological reading of events: "What is this God has done to us?" They are frightened by a kindness. Guilty men read gifts as traps, which is its own kind of sentence. And they are half right again: God is in this — just not in the way they think, and not without their brother's hand on the sack.

⚠️ A seam worth noting rather than smoothing: in verse 27 one brother finds his silver at the lodging place, but in verse 35 they all find it when they get home and are surprised again. Source critics read the two discoveries as two versions of the scene braided together; harmonizers note that only one sack was opened on the road (for fodder) and the rest were not unpacked until Canaan. Both readings work; the doubling is in the text and this translation leaves it there.

Verses 29–35 · the report, edited

What they leave out. Compare their account to their father with what actually happened. They tell him the man spoke harshly, called them spies, demanded Benjamin, and kept a brother — all true. They omit the three days in custody, they omit that Simeon was bound before their eyes, and they add a sweetener the man never said: "and you may trade in the land" (v. 34). They also do not mention the sentence they said to each other in that cell. This family manages its father with edited information, and has since a bloodied coat was carried home with the question "recognize, please."

Verses 36–38 · an old man counting his losses

"Joseph is not, and Simeon is not." Jacob uses the brothers' own word — einennu, the euphemism they have hidden behind for two decades (vv. 13, 32) — and applies it to two sons, one of whom is alive and governing Egypt and the other of whom is alive in Egyptian custody. Every man he names is fine. He is not wrong to grieve; he is simply, and completely, wrong.

"All of it has come upon me." Alay hayu khullanah — the KJV's famous "all these things are against me." Note the pronoun: me. There is real pain in the verse and there is also a man who has made a career of grief, and Genesis does not tidy either away. He is also, on the plain facts, accusing his sons of killing Joseph and Simeon — which for nine of them is nearly true, and they say nothing.

Reuben's offer, which is monstrous. "You may put my two sons to death if I do not bring him back." He is proposing that his father kill his own grandsons as security. It is meant as an extravagant pledge and it lands as evidence that Reuben still does not understand what a lost son costs — the same failure of imagination that made him suggest a pit instead of an escape. ⚠️ Contrast this, when you get there, with Judah in 43:9: "I will be surety for him; from my hand you shall require him." Judah offers himself. Jacob says yes to that one. It is the chapter's quietest measurement of how far Judah has come since he said "she is more righteous than I."

"Down to Sheol." The old man ends where he ended in 37:35 — she'olah, the same word, the same grey head, the same refusal of comfort, twenty-two years on. He has not moved. And the versions repeat their old inconsistency exactly: the KJV reads "bring down my gray hairs with sorrow to the grave" here, as it did there, while the ASV and NWT both read "Sheol." Keeping the Hebrew word lets the reader hear that this is not a fresh lament but the same sentence, unchanged, from the day the coat came back.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "recognized… and made himself unrecognizable" for the nakar pun of v. 7, which the smooth versions dissolve; "the one is not" (einennu) left as the euphemism the brothers actually use, so it can land again in Jacob's mouth at v. 36; "Sheol" for the second time (v. 38), matching 37:35 where the KJV reads "grave" both times; "the nakedness of the land" for ervah; "we are guilty" for ashemim, not softened to regret; and "the interpreter was between them" left as flatly as the Hebrew says it.

Echoes paid. The sheaves that bowed — the dream of 37:7 fulfilled in a single clause, and fulfilled through the pit that was meant to stop it; nakar's fourth turn after Isaac, Jacob and Judah; zakhar — Joseph "remembered the dreams," the verb that has been failing and firing all through this story; einennu, Reuben's word at the empty pit; Jacob's grey head and Sheol, word for word, twenty-two years later; and the pleading in the pit that 37:24 deliberately did not report.

Echoes planted. Judah's surety — "I will be surety for him" (43:9) and "let your servant remain instead of the boy" (44:33) — the answer to Reuben's grotesque offer here, and the moment Joseph's test is finally passed; the silver in the sacks, which will be repeated on purpose with a divining cup (44:2); Benjamin, who must come; and Joseph's own verdict on all of it, still eight chapters off — "you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good" (50:20).

Next installment in this book: Genesis 43 — the grain runs out, and Jacob has to let Benjamin go. Judah puts himself up as surety in the words Reuben could not find; the brothers go down with double silver and a gift of balm and honey; and the man who accused them of spying seats them in his own house in birth order, stares at his mother's other son, and has to leave the room.

Genesis 43 — The Mister Translation

1
וְהָרָעָב, כָּבֵד בָּאָרֶץ.
And the famine was heavy in the land.note
2
וַיְהִי, כַּאֲשֶׁר כִּלּוּ לֶאֱכֹל אֶת-הַשֶּׁבֶר, אֲשֶׁר הֵבִיאוּ, מִמִּצְרָיִם; וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵיהֶם אֲבִיהֶם, שֻׁבוּ שִׁבְרוּ-לָנוּ מְעַט-אֹכֶל.
And so it was, when they had finished eating the grain they had brought from Egypt, that their father said to them, "Go back, buy us a little food."note
3
וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו יְהוּדָה, לֵאמֹר: הָעֵד הֵעִד בָּנוּ הָאִישׁ לֵאמֹר לֹא-תִרְאוּ פָנַי, בִּלְתִּי אֲחִיכֶם אִתְּכֶם.
And Judah said to him, "The man solemnly warned us, saying, 'You will not see my face unless your brother is with you.'note
4
אִם-יֶשְׁךָ מְשַׁלֵּחַ אֶת-אָחִינוּ, אִתָּנוּ--נֵרְדָה, וְנִשְׁבְּרָה לְךָ אֹכֶל.
If you will send our brother with us, we will go down and buy you food.note
5
וְאִם-אֵינְךָ מְשַׁלֵּחַ, לֹא נֵרֵד: כִּי-הָאִישׁ אָמַר אֵלֵינוּ, לֹא-תִרְאוּ פָנַי, בִּלְתִּי, אֲחִיכֶם אִתְּכֶם.
But if you will not send him, we will not go down — for the man said to us, 'You will not see my face unless your brother is with you.'"note
6
וַיֹּאמֶר, יִשְׂרָאֵל, לָמָה הֲרֵעֹתֶם, לִי--לְהַגִּיד לָאִישׁ, הַעוֹד לָכֶם אָח.
And Israel said, "Why did you deal badly with me, to tell the man you had another brother?"note
7
וַיֹּאמְרוּ שָׁאוֹל שָׁאַל-הָאִישׁ לָנוּ וּלְמוֹלַדְתֵּנוּ לֵאמֹר, הַעוֹד אֲבִיכֶם חַי הֲיֵשׁ לָכֶם אָח, וַנַּגֶּד-לוֹ, עַל-פִּי הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה; הֲיָדוֹעַ נֵדַע--כִּי יֹאמַר, הוֹרִידוּ אֶת-אֲחִיכֶם.
And they said, "The man asked and asked about us and about our kindred, saying, 'Is your father still alive? Do you have a brother?' And we told him according to these words. Could we possibly know that he would say, 'Bring your brother down'?"note
8
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה אֶל-יִשְׂרָאֵל אָבִיו, שִׁלְחָה הַנַּעַר אִתִּי--וְנָקוּמָה וְנֵלֵכָה; וְנִחְיֶה וְלֹא נָמוּת, גַּם-אֲנַחְנוּ גַם-אַתָּה גַּם-טַפֵּנוּ.
And Judah said to Israel his father, "Send the boy with me, and we will rise and go — that we may live and not die, we and you and our little ones.note
9
אָנֹכִי, אֶעֶרְבֶנּוּ--מִיָּדִי, תְּבַקְשֶׁנּוּ: אִם-לֹא הֲבִיאֹתִיו אֵלֶיךָ וְהִצַּגְתִּיו לְפָנֶיךָ, וְחָטָאתִי לְךָ כָּל-הַיָּמִים.
I myself will be surety for him — from my hand you shall require him. If I do not bring him to you and set him before you, then I will have sinned against you all my days.note
10
כִּי, לוּלֵא הִתְמַהְמָהְנוּ--כִּי-עַתָּה שַׁבְנוּ, זֶה פַעֲמָיִם.
For if we had not delayed, by now we would have returned twice over."note
11
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יִשְׂרָאֵל אֲבִיהֶם, אִם-כֵּן אֵפוֹא זֹאת עֲשׂוּ--קְחוּ מִזִּמְרַת הָאָרֶץ בִּכְלֵיכֶם, וְהוֹרִידוּ לָאִישׁ מִנְחָה: מְעַט צֳרִי, וּמְעַט דְּבַשׁ, נְכֹאת וָלֹט, בָּטְנִים וּשְׁקֵדִים.
And Israel their father said to them, "If it must be so, then do this: take some of the land's best in your vessels and carry down a gift to the man — a little balm and a little honey, gum and ladanum, pistachios and almonds.note
12
וְכֶסֶף מִשְׁנֶה, קְחוּ בְיֶדְכֶם; וְאֶת-הַכֶּסֶף הַמּוּשָׁב בְּפִי אַמְתְּחֹתֵיכֶם, תָּשִׁיבוּ בְיֶדְכֶם--אוּלַי מִשְׁגֶּה, הוּא.
And take double silver in your hand; and the silver that was returned in the mouth of your bags, take back in your hand — perhaps it was a mistake.note
13
וְאֶת-אֲחִיכֶם, קָחוּ; וְקוּמוּ, שׁוּבוּ אֶל-הָאִישׁ.
And take your brother, and rise, go back to the man.note
14
וְאֵל שַׁדַּי, יִתֵּן לָכֶם רַחֲמִים לִפְנֵי הָאִישׁ, וְשִׁלַּח לָכֶם אֶת-אֲחִיכֶם אַחֵר, וְאֶת-בִּנְיָמִין; וַאֲנִי, כַּאֲשֶׁר שָׁכֹלְתִּי שָׁכָלְתִּי.
And may El Shaddai give you compassion before the man, that he may send back to you your other brother, and Benjamin. And as for me — if I am bereaved, I am bereaved."note
15
וַיִּקְחוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים אֶת-הַמִּנְחָה הַזֹּאת, וּמִשְׁנֶה-כֶּסֶף לָקְחוּ בְיָדָם וְאֶת-בִּנְיָמִן; וַיָּקֻמוּ וַיֵּרְדוּ מִצְרַיִם, וַיַּעַמְדוּ לִפְנֵי יוֹסֵף.
And the men took this gift, and they took double silver in their hand, and Benjamin; and they rose and went down to Egypt and stood before Joseph.note
16
וַיַּרְא יוֹסֵף אִתָּם, אֶת-בִּנְיָמִין, וַיֹּאמֶר לַאֲשֶׁר עַל-בֵּיתוֹ, הָבֵא אֶת-הָאֲנָשִׁים הַבָּיְתָה; וּטְבֹחַ טֶבַח וְהָכֵן, כִּי אִתִּי יֹאכְלוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים בַּצָּהֳרָיִם.
And Joseph saw Benjamin with them, and he said to the man over his house, "Bring the men into the house, and slaughter an animal and prepare it, for the men will eat with me at noon."note
17
וַיַּעַשׂ הָאִישׁ, כַּאֲשֶׁר אָמַר יוֹסֵף; וַיָּבֵא הָאִישׁ אֶת-הָאֲנָשִׁים, בֵּיתָה יוֹסֵף.
And the man did as Joseph had said, and the man brought the men into Joseph's house.note
18
וַיִּירְאוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים, כִּי הוּבְאוּ בֵּית יוֹסֵף, וַיֹּאמְרוּ עַל-דְּבַר הַכֶּסֶף הַשָּׁב בְּאַמְתְּחֹתֵינוּ בַּתְּחִלָּה, אֲנַחְנוּ מוּבָאִים--לְהִתְגֹּלֵל עָלֵינוּ וּלְהִתְנַפֵּל עָלֵינוּ, וְלָקַחַת אֹתָנוּ לַעֲבָדִים וְאֶת-חֲמֹרֵינוּ.
And the men were afraid because they were brought into Joseph's house, and they said, "It is because of the silver that was returned in our bags the first time that we are being brought in — to roll himself upon us and fall upon us, and take us as slaves, and our donkeys."note
19
וַיִּגְּשׁוּ, אֶל-הָאִישׁ, אֲשֶׁר, עַל-בֵּית יוֹסֵף; וַיְדַבְּרוּ אֵלָיו, פֶּתַח הַבָּיִת.
And they came near to the man who was over Joseph's house and spoke to him at the entrance of the house,note
20
וַיֹּאמְרוּ, בִּי אֲדֹנִי; יָרֹד יָרַדְנוּ בַּתְּחִלָּה, לִשְׁבָּר-אֹכֶל.
and they said, "Please, my lord — we came down, indeed came down, the first time to buy food.note
21
וַיְהִי כִּי-בָאנוּ אֶל-הַמָּלוֹן, וַנִּפְתְּחָה אֶת-אַמְתְּחֹתֵינוּ, וְהִנֵּה כֶסֶף-אִישׁ בְּפִי אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ, כַּסְפֵּנוּ בְּמִשְׁקָלוֹ; וַנָּשֶׁב אֹתוֹ, בְּיָדֵנוּ.
And so it was, when we came to the lodging place and opened our bags — look, each man's silver was in the mouth of his bag, our silver in its full weight; and we have brought it back in our hand.note
22
וְכֶסֶף אַחֵר הוֹרַדְנוּ בְיָדֵנוּ, לִשְׁבָּר-אֹכֶל; לֹא יָדַעְנוּ, מִי-שָׂם כַּסְפֵּנוּ בְּאַמְתְּחֹתֵינוּ.
And we have brought down other silver in our hand to buy food. We do not know who put our silver in our bags."note
23
וַיֹּאמֶר שָׁלוֹם לָכֶם אַל-תִּירָאוּ, אֱלֹהֵיכֶם וֵאלֹהֵי אֲבִיכֶם נָתַן לָכֶם מַטְמוֹן בְּאַמְתְּחֹתֵיכֶם--כַּסְפְּכֶם, בָּא אֵלָי; וַיּוֹצֵא אֲלֵהֶם, אֶת-שִׁמְעוֹן.
And he said, "Peace to you — do not be afraid. Your God and the God of your father gave you treasure in your bags; your silver came to me." And he brought Simeon out to them.note
24
וַיָּבֵא הָאִישׁ אֶת-הָאֲנָשִׁים, בֵּיתָה יוֹסֵף; וַיִּתֶּן-מַיִם וַיִּרְחֲצוּ רַגְלֵיהֶם, וַיִּתֵּן מִסְפּוֹא לַחֲמֹרֵיהֶם.
And the man brought the men into Joseph's house and gave them water, and they washed their feet; and he gave fodder to their donkeys.note
25
וַיָּכִינוּ, אֶת-הַמִּנְחָה, עַד-בּוֹא יוֹסֵף, בַּצָּהֳרָיִם: כִּי שָׁמְעוּ, כִּי-שָׁם יֹאכְלוּ לָחֶם.
And they prepared the gift for Joseph's coming at noon, for they had heard that they would eat bread there.note
26
וַיָּבֹא יוֹסֵף הַבַּיְתָה, וַיָּבִיאּוּ לוֹ אֶת-הַמִּנְחָה אֲשֶׁר-בְּיָדָם הַבָּיְתָה; וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ-לוֹ, אָרְצָה.
And Joseph came into the house, and they brought him the gift that was in their hand, into the house, and bowed down to him to the ground.note
27
וַיִּשְׁאַל לָהֶם, לְשָׁלוֹם, וַיֹּאמֶר הֲשָׁלוֹם אֲבִיכֶם הַזָּקֵן, אֲשֶׁר אֲמַרְתֶּם--הַעוֹדֶנּוּ, חָי.
And he asked after their peace, and said, "Is your father well — the old man of whom you spoke? Is he still alive?"note
28
וַיֹּאמְרוּ, שָׁלוֹם לְעַבְדְּךָ לְאָבִינוּ--עוֹדֶנּוּ חָי; וַיִּקְּדוּ, וישתחו וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ.
And they said, "Your servant our father is well; he is still alive." And they knelt and bowed down.note
29
וַיִּשָּׂא עֵינָיו, וַיַּרְא אֶת-בִּנְיָמִין אָחִיו בֶּן-אִמּוֹ, וַיֹּאמֶר הֲזֶה אֲחִיכֶם הַקָּטֹן, אֲשֶׁר אֲמַרְתֶּם אֵלָי; וַיֹּאמַר, אֱלֹהִים יָחְנְךָ בְּנִי.
And he lifted his eyes and saw Benjamin his brother, his mother's son, and said, "Is this your youngest brother, of whom you spoke to me?" And he said, "God be gracious to you, my son."note
30
וַיְמַהֵר יוֹסֵף, כִּי-נִכְמְרוּ רַחֲמָיו אֶל-אָחִיו, וַיְבַקֵּשׁ, לִבְכּוֹת; וַיָּבֹא הַחַדְרָה, וַיֵּבְךְּ שָׁמָּה.
And Joseph hurried out, for his compassion grew warm toward his brother, and he sought to weep; and he went into the inner room and wept there.note
31
וַיִּרְחַץ פָּנָיו, וַיֵּצֵא; וַיִּתְאַפַּק--וַיֹּאמֶר, שִׂימוּ לָחֶם.
And he washed his face and came out, and held himself in, and said, "Serve the bread."note
32
וַיָּשִׂימוּ לוֹ לְבַדּוֹ, וְלָהֶם לְבַדָּם; וְלַמִּצְרִים הָאֹכְלִים אִתּוֹ, לְבַדָּם--כִּי לֹא יוּכְלוּן הַמִּצְרִים לֶאֱכֹל אֶת-הָעִבְרִים לֶחֶם, כִּי-תוֹעֵבָה הִוא לְמִצְרָיִם.
And they served him by himself, and them by themselves, and the Egyptians who ate with him by themselves — because the Egyptians cannot eat bread with the Hebrews, for that is an abomination to Egypt.note
33
וַיֵּשְׁבוּ לְפָנָיו--הַבְּכֹר כִּבְכֹרָתוֹ, וְהַצָּעִיר כִּצְעִרָתוֹ; וַיִּתְמְהוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים, אִישׁ אֶל-רֵעֵהוּ.
And they sat before him, the firstborn according to his birthright and the youngest according to his youth; and the men looked at one another in astonishment.note
34
וַיִּשָּׂא מַשְׂאֹת מֵאֵת פָּנָיו, אֲלֵהֶם, וַתֵּרֶב מַשְׂאַת בִּנְיָמִן מִמַּשְׂאֹת כֻּלָּם, חָמֵשׁ יָדוֹת; וַיִּשְׁתּוּ וַיִּשְׁכְּרוּ, עִמּוֹ.
And portions were carried to them from before him, and Benjamin's portion was five times more than all of theirs. And they drank and grew merry with him.note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–10 · Judah offers himself

The famine outlasts the food. Nothing has been resolved since ch. 42 — Simeon is still in Egypt, Jacob has still refused, and the family simply eats until the sacks are empty. The father's opening line is a masterpiece of avoidance: "Go back, buy us a little food," as though this were a small errand and not the thing he has forbidden. Judah has to say the quiet part out loud.

"Why did you deal badly with me?" Jacob's instinct under pressure is to find whose fault it is. The brothers' answer — "the man asked and asked about us and about our kindred" — is defensive and, as far as ch. 42 records, not quite true: the man asked whether they had a father and a brother only after they volunteered "we are twelve brothers… the one is not" (42:13). They are shading the account to their father again, exactly as they did on the first trip.

The sentence Reuben could not find. Here is the chapter's hinge, and it should be read against 42:37. Reuben's offer was "you may put my two sons to death if I do not bring him back" — a man proposing his own children as collateral, which is monstrous and also costs him nothing he has to feel. Judah says: "I myself will be surety for him — from my hand you shall require him." Arav is the commercial word for standing as guarantor of a debt: if the thing is lost, the surety pays it. He is putting himself up. And Jacob, who said an immediate and absolute no to Reuben, says yes to this. The KJV's "I will be surety for him; of my hand shalt thou require him" is exact and has never been bettered.

This is the same man from chapter 38. Judah proposed selling Joseph for a profit (37:26); Judah withheld Shelah from Tamar and would have burned her for it; and Judah is the one who said, out loud and in public, "she is more righteous than I." Genesis has been building this character quietly for five chapters, and the reader who wondered why chapter 38 interrupted the Joseph story gets the answer here: this is what the interruption was for. The man who learned to say "I was wrong" is the only one who can now say "hold me responsible."

Verses 11–14 · a gift that has been down this road before

Look at what is in the bags. Jacob sends "a little balm and a little honey, gum and ladanum, pistachios and almonds." The shelf wanders on the spice names — the KJV reads "spices, and myrrh, nuts, and almonds," the ASV "spicery and myrrh," and the NIV "spices and myrrh, some pistachio nuts and almonds." Three of those are not a random hamper: nekhot, tsori and lot — gum, balm and ladanum — are precisely, word for word, the cargo the Ishmaelite caravan was carrying down to Egypt on the day his brothers sold Joseph into it. The old man is loading the same three commodities onto donkeys and sending them down the same road, as a present, to the son he thinks is dead. He has no idea. Genesis does not say a word about it; it simply lists the items twice, six chapters apart, and trusts you.

"Perhaps it was a mistake." Jacob's instruction about the returned silver is the most ordinary sentence in the chapter and quietly reveals him: take it back, and let us assume an accounting error. It is the one explanation that requires nobody to have intended anything.

El Shaddai, and a word from the womb. He prays by the covenant name last heard when God renamed Abram (17:1) — the name for the God who gives children to people who have none — and he asks for rachamim. Keep hold of that word: it is built on rechem, the womb, and means the ache of a parent for a child, mother-love used as the standard word for mercy. Jacob asks God to put womb-feeling in a hard Egyptian official. Sixteen verses later the text will say, of that official, that his rachamim grew warm and he had to leave the room. The prayer is answered before it finishes travelling, in a body Jacob does not know is his son's.

"If I am bereaved, I am bereaved." Ka'asher shakholti shakhalti — a resigned, fatalistic doubling, and the same construction Esther will use walking in to the king unsummoned ("if I perish, I perish," Esther 4:16). The KJV's "if I be bereaved of my children, I am bereaved" catches the tone: not faith, exactly. A man out of options.

Verses 15–25 · terror at the door, and a steward who says the word

Kindness read as ambush. Invited to the governor's own house — an enormous honour — the brothers panic, and the reason is the silver. Their reasoning is grimly practical: they will be "taken as slaves." These are men who once sold a brother into slavery for silver, now convinced that unexplained silver is about to make slaves of them. Genesis lets the symmetry sit there without a word of comment.

"Peace to you — do not be afraid." And here the chapter does something remarkable. The steward's first word is shalom — the word this family has been unable to say since 37:4, when the brothers "could not speak to him in peace," and the word their father put in the errand that got Joseph sold: "go, see whether your brothers are at peace" (37:14). It has been withheld for six chapters. It comes back, in Egypt, from a servant, to the men who could not pronounce it. The KJV's "Peace be to you, fear not" keeps the noun where a modern ear might expect "it's all right"; the NIV's "It's all right… don't be afraid" is exactly that smoothing, and the thread disappears with it. Then he tells them their God gave them treasure — a household servant handing ten frightened Hebrews a theological explanation of something his master did — and brings out Simeon.

"Your silver came to me." Which is true, and hides everything. The steward is in on it.

Verses 26–31 · the dream again, and the second weeping

They bow twice more. Verse 26 and verse 28, and by now the reader has stopped being surprised — which is itself the point. What was a boy's insufferable dream in 37:7 has become the ordinary daily posture of ten adult men doing business. Prophecy fulfilled does not announce itself; it just becomes the way things are.

He asks after their peace. Three times in two verses the root shalom: he asked after their shalom, "is your father shalom," "your servant our father is shalom." The man who was sent to inspect his brothers' shalom and was thrown into a pit for it is now the one asking — and getting an answer. English cannot keep the repetition without sounding odd ("is your father at peace?"), so this translation uses "asked after their peace" for the frame and natural English inside it, with the thread flagged here.

"His mother's son." The narrator adds three words that no one at the table could have said: Benjamin is not just his youngest brother, he is ben immo — Rachel's other son, the only full brother he has. Joseph asks a formal question, receives no answer, and blesses the boy anyway ("God be gracious to you, my son" — yechonkha, from chanan, the root of Hannah, John, and "grace"). Then he stops being able to do this.

"His compassion grew warm." Nikhmeru rachamav — the verb means to grow hot, to be kindled, and the noun is Jacob's word from verse 14. Hebrew locates feeling in the body, and this is the body reporting: the womb-love heated up and the man had to leave. The KJV's "his bowels did yearn upon his brother" is a genuinely faithful rendering of the physicality that has simply aged into comedy for modern ears; the NIV reads "deeply moved," which is decorous and loses the heat. This is Joseph's second weeping (after 42:24) and he still hides it — washes his face, comes out, and held himself in (vayyit'appaq, to restrain, force oneself). Count them as they come: the restraint is going to fail.

Verses 32–34 · three tables, and a portion five times over

Three separate tables, and a note that pays an old debt. Joseph alone; the brothers alone; the Egyptians alone — "because the Egyptians cannot eat bread with the Hebrews, for that is an abomination to Egypt." Genesis states the custom flatly, without mockery — the KJV "for that is an abomination unto the Egyptians," the ASV the same, the NWT "a detestable thing" — and it lands on something left open four chapters ago: Potiphar "knew nothing of what he had except the bread he ate" — a clause the note there said might be bookkeeping, delicacy, or a dietary rule. Here is the dietary rule, stated outright by the narrator. And notice where it leaves Joseph: not at the Egyptians' table and not at the Hebrews'. He eats alone, between two peoples, which is a fair description of his whole life.

The seating. They are placed in exact birth order, "the firstborn according to his birthright and the youngest according to his youth" — ten men who cannot imagine how an Egyptian could know it, staring at each other. It is a small controlled miracle designed to unsettle them, and it does.

Five portions — and the last test being set up. Benjamin gets five times what anyone else does — the KJV famously calls it a "mess" ("Benjamin's mess was five times so much as any of theirs"), an archaism for a served portion that has drifted into meaning a muddle — which is not generosity so much as an experiment. Joseph is recreating the original conditions with clinical precision: a father's favourite, one brother conspicuously preferred in front of the others, at a table where they can all see it. The last time these men watched a father lavish a preference on Rachel's son, they stripped him and sold him. The narrator's final word on the meal is the answer to the experiment, and it is not what happened in 37:4: they drank and grew merry with him. Nobody resents it. The trap for that, with a silver cup, is one chapter away.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "I myself will be surety for him" for arav, the guarantor's commercial word, so Judah's offer reads as the legal undertaking it is; "compassion" for rachamim in both Jacob's prayer (v. 14) and Joseph's body (v. 30), because they must be the same word for the answer to be visible; "peace" keeping shalom audible in vv. 23, 27 and 28; "abomination" for to'evah; and the gift's gum, balm and ladanum rendered exactly as at 37:25 so the cargo can be recognised.

Echoes paid. Reuben's grotesque offer answered by Judah putting up himself — and the character built in chapter 38 finally doing the work it was built for; the caravan's gum, balm and ladanum loaded by Jacob as a present and sent back down the road his son left on; shalom, unspeakable at 37:4 and the subject of the errand at 37:14, spoken over them in Egypt; rachamim, asked of God in v. 14 and reported in a man's body in v. 30; the bread Potiphar kept to himself explained by the Egyptian table rule; and the bowing, twice more, now unremarkable.

Echoes planted. Benjamin's five portions and a favoured youngest son at a table — the conditions of the original crime rebuilt on purpose, with the silver cup about to be planted in his sack (44:2); Judah's surety, which he will have to make good on in the longest speech in Genesis ("let your servant remain instead of the boy," 44:33) — and that is the moment Joseph stops being able to hold himself in; and the restraint of v. 31, counted twice now and failing.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 44 — the silver cup is hidden in Benjamin's sack and the brothers are overtaken on the road; the test is complete, the favoured youngest is condemned, and the others are free to walk away exactly as they once did. Then Judah steps forward and speaks for eighteen verses.

Genesis 44 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיְצַו אֶת-אֲשֶׁר עַל-בֵּיתוֹ, לֵאמֹר, מַלֵּא אֶת-אַמְתְּחֹת הָאֲנָשִׁים אֹכֶל, כַּאֲשֶׁר יוּכְלוּן שְׂאֵת; וְשִׂים כֶּסֶף-אִישׁ, בְּפִי אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ.
And he commanded the man over his house, saying, "Fill the men's bags with food, as much as they can carry, and put each man's silver in the mouth of his bag.note
2
וְאֶת-גְּבִיעִי גְּבִיעַ הַכֶּסֶף, תָּשִׂים בְּפִי אַמְתַּחַת הַקָּטֹן, וְאֵת, כֶּסֶף שִׁבְרוֹ; וַיַּעַשׂ, כִּדְבַר יוֹסֵף אֲשֶׁר דִּבֵּר.
And my cup, the silver cup, put in the mouth of the youngest one's bag, along with his grain-silver." And he did according to the word Joseph had spoken.note
3
הַבֹּקֶר, אוֹר; וְהָאֲנָשִׁים שֻׁלְּחוּ, הֵמָּה וַחֲמֹרֵיהֶם.
The morning was light, and the men were sent off, they and their donkeys.note
4
הֵם יָצְאוּ אֶת-הָעִיר, לֹא הִרְחִיקוּ, וְיוֹסֵף אָמַר לַאֲשֶׁר עַל-בֵּיתוֹ, קוּם רְדֹף אַחֲרֵי הָאֲנָשִׁים; וְהִשַּׂגְתָּם וְאָמַרְתָּ אֲלֵהֶם, לָמָּה שִׁלַּמְתֶּם רָעָה תַּחַת טוֹבָה.
They had gone out of the city — they had not gone far — when Joseph said to the man over his house, "Get up, pursue the men; and when you overtake them, say to them, 'Why have you repaid evil for good?note
5
הֲלוֹא זֶה, אֲשֶׁר יִשְׁתֶּה אֲדֹנִי בּוֹ, וְהוּא, נַחֵשׁ יְנַחֵשׁ בּוֹ; הֲרֵעֹתֶם, אֲשֶׁר עֲשִׂיתֶם.
Is this not the one my lord drinks from — and by which he indeed practises divination? You have done evil in what you have done.'"note
6
וַיַּשִּׂגֵם; וַיְדַבֵּר אֲלֵהֶם, אֶת-הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה.
And he overtook them and spoke these words to them.note
7
וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֵלָיו--לָמָּה יְדַבֵּר אֲדֹנִי, כַּדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה; חָלִילָה, לַעֲבָדֶיךָ, מֵעֲשׂוֹת, כַּדָּבָר הַזֶּה.
And they said to him, "Why does my lord speak such words? Far be it from your servants to do such a thing!note
8
הֵן כֶּסֶף, אֲשֶׁר מָצָאנוּ בְּפִי אַמְתְּחֹתֵינוּ--הֱשִׁיבֹנוּ אֵלֶיךָ, מֵאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן; וְאֵיךְ, נִגְנֹב מִבֵּית אֲדֹנֶיךָ, כֶּסֶף, אוֹ זָהָב.
Look — the silver we found in the mouth of our bags we brought back to you from the land of Canaan. How then should we steal silver or gold from your lord's house?note
9
אֲשֶׁר יִמָּצֵא אִתּוֹ מֵעֲבָדֶיךָ, וָמֵת; וְגַם-אֲנַחְנוּ, נִהְיֶה לַאדֹנִי לַעֲבָדִים.
Whichever of your servants it is found with — let him die; and we also will be slaves to my lord."note
10
וַיֹּאמֶר, גַּם-עַתָּה כְדִבְרֵיכֶם כֶּן-הוּא: אֲשֶׁר יִמָּצֵא אִתּוֹ יִהְיֶה-לִּי עָבֶד, וְאַתֶּם תִּהְיוּ נְקִיִּם.
And he said, "Now also, let it be according to your words: the one it is found with will be my slave, and you will be innocent."note
11
וַיְמַהֲרוּ, וַיּוֹרִדוּ אִישׁ אֶת-אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ--אָרְצָה; וַיִּפְתְּחוּ, אִישׁ אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ.
And they hurried and each man lowered his bag to the ground, and each man opened his bag.note
12
וַיְחַפֵּשׂ--בַּגָּדוֹל הֵחֵל, וּבַקָּטֹן כִּלָּה; וַיִּמָּצֵא, הַגָּבִיעַ, בְּאַמְתַּחַת, בִּנְיָמִן.
And he searched — he began with the eldest and finished with the youngest — and the cup was found in Benjamin's bag.note
13
וַיִּקְרְעוּ, שִׂמְלֹתָם; וַיַּעֲמֹס אִישׁ עַל-חֲמֹרוֹ, וַיָּשֻׁבוּ הָעִירָה.
And they tore their garments; and each man loaded his donkey, and they returned to the city.note
14
וַיָּבֹא יְהוּדָה וְאֶחָיו בֵּיתָה יוֹסֵף, וְהוּא עוֹדֶנּוּ שָׁם; וַיִּפְּלוּ לְפָנָיו, אָרְצָה.
And Judah and his brothers came to Joseph's house — and he was still there — and they fell before him to the ground.note
15
וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם יוֹסֵף, מָה-הַמַּעֲשֶׂה הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר עֲשִׂיתֶם; הֲלוֹא יְדַעְתֶּם, כִּי-נַחֵשׁ יְנַחֵשׁ אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר כָּמֹנִי.
And Joseph said to them, "What deed is this that you have done? Did you not know that a man like me indeed practises divination?"note
16
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה, מַה-נֹּאמַר לַאדֹנִי, מַה-נְּדַבֵּר, וּמַה-נִּצְטַדָּק; הָאֱלֹהִים, מָצָא אֶת-עֲוֺן עֲבָדֶיךָ--הִנֶּנּוּ עֲבָדִים לַאדֹנִי, גַּם-אֲנַחְנוּ גַּם אֲשֶׁר-נִמְצָא הַגָּבִיעַ בְּיָדוֹ.
And Judah said, "What shall we say to my lord? What shall we speak? And how shall we clear ourselves? God has found out the guilt of your servants. Here we are — slaves to my lord, both we and the one in whose hand the cup was found."note
17
וַיֹּאמֶר--חָלִילָה לִּי, מֵעֲשׂוֹת זֹאת; הָאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר נִמְצָא הַגָּבִיעַ בְּיָדוֹ, הוּא יִהְיֶה-לִּי עָבֶד, וְאַתֶּם, עֲלוּ לְשָׁלוֹם אֶל-אֲבִיכֶם. {ס}
And he said, "Far be it from me to do this. The man in whose hand the cup was found — he will be my slave; and you, go up in peace to your father." {ס}note
18
וַיִּגַּשׁ אֵלָיו יְהוּדָה, וַיֹּאמֶר בִּי אֲדֹנִי, יְדַבֶּר-נָא עַבְדְּךָ דָבָר בְּאָזְנֵי אֲדֹנִי, וְאַל-יִחַר אַפְּךָ בְּעַבְדֶּךָ: כִּי כָמוֹךָ, כְּפַרְעֹה.
And Judah came near to him and said, "Please, my lord — let your servant speak a word in my lord's ears, and do not let your anger burn against your servant, for you are like Pharaoh himself.note
19
אֲדֹנִי שָׁאַל, אֶת-עֲבָדָיו לֵאמֹר: הֲיֵשׁ-לָכֶם אָב, אוֹ-אָח.
My lord asked his servants, saying, 'Have you a father, or a brother?'note
20
וַנֹּאמֶר, אֶל-אֲדֹנִי, יֶשׁ-לָנוּ אָב זָקֵן, וְיֶלֶד זְקֻנִים קָטָן; וְאָחִיו מֵת, וַיִּוָּתֵר הוּא לְבַדּוֹ לְאִמּוֹ וְאָבִיו אֲהֵבוֹ.
And we said to my lord, 'We have an old father, and a young child of his old age; and his brother is dead, and he alone is left of his mother, and his father loves him.'note
21
וַתֹּאמֶר, אֶל-עֲבָדֶיךָ, הוֹרִדֻהוּ, אֵלָי; וְאָשִׂימָה עֵינִי, עָלָיו.
And you said to your servants, 'Bring him down to me, that I may set my eyes on him.'note
22
וַנֹּאמֶר, אֶל-אֲדֹנִי, לֹא-יוּכַל הַנַּעַר, לַעֲזֹב אֶת-אָבִיו: וְעָזַב אֶת-אָבִיו, וָמֵת.
And we said to my lord, 'The boy cannot leave his father; if he left his father, his father would die.'note
23
וַתֹּאמֶר, אֶל-עֲבָדֶיךָ, אִם-לֹא יֵרֵד אֲחִיכֶם הַקָּטֹן, אִתְּכֶם--לֹא תֹסִפוּן, לִרְאוֹת פָּנָי.
And you said to your servants, 'Unless your youngest brother comes down with you, you will not see my face again.'note
24
וַיְהִי כִּי עָלִינוּ, אֶל-עַבְדְּךָ אָבִי; וַנַּגֶּד-לוֹ--אֵת, דִּבְרֵי אֲדֹנִי.
And so it was, when we went up to your servant my father, that we told him the words of my lord.note
25
וַיֹּאמֶר, אָבִינוּ: שֻׁבוּ, שִׁבְרוּ-לָנוּ מְעַט-אֹכֶל.
And our father said, 'Go back, buy us a little food.'note
26
וַנֹּאמֶר, לֹא נוּכַל לָרֶדֶת: אִם-יֵשׁ אָחִינוּ הַקָּטֹן אִתָּנוּ, וְיָרַדְנוּ--כִּי-לֹא נוּכַל לִרְאוֹת פְּנֵי הָאִישׁ, וְאָחִינוּ הַקָּטֹן אֵינֶנּוּ אִתָּנוּ.
And we said, 'We cannot go down. If our youngest brother is with us, then we will go down — for we cannot see the man's face unless our youngest brother is with us.'note
27
וַיֹּאמֶר עַבְדְּךָ אָבִי, אֵלֵינוּ: אַתֶּם יְדַעְתֶּם, כִּי שְׁנַיִם יָלְדָה-לִּי אִשְׁתִּי.
And your servant my father said to us, 'You know that my wife bore me two.note
28
וַיֵּצֵא הָאֶחָד, מֵאִתִּי, וָאֹמַר, אַךְ טָרֹף טֹרָף; וְלֹא רְאִיתִיו, עַד-הֵנָּה.
And the one went out from me, and I said, "Surely he is torn to pieces" — and I have not seen him since.note
29
וּלְקַחְתֶּם גַּם-אֶת-זֶה מֵעִם פָּנַי, וְקָרָהוּ אָסוֹן--וְהוֹרַדְתֶּם אֶת-שֵׂיבָתִי בְּרָעָה, שְׁאֹלָה.
And if you take this one also from my face and harm befalls him, you will bring down my grey head in calamity to Sheol.'note
30
וְעַתָּה, כְּבֹאִי אֶל-עַבְדְּךָ אָבִי, וְהַנַּעַר, אֵינֶנּוּ אִתָּנוּ; וְנַפְשׁוֹ, קְשׁוּרָה בְנַפְשׁוֹ.
And now, when I come to your servant my father and the boy is not with us — and his own soul is bound up with the boy's soul —note
31
וְהָיָה, כִּרְאוֹתוֹ כִּי-אֵין הַנַּעַר--וָמֵת; וְהוֹרִידוּ עֲבָדֶיךָ אֶת-שֵׂיבַת עַבְדְּךָ אָבִינוּ, בְּיָגוֹן--שְׁאֹלָה.
then, when he sees that the boy is not there, he will die; and your servants will bring down the grey head of your servant our father in sorrow to Sheol.note
32
כִּי עַבְדְּךָ עָרַב אֶת-הַנַּעַר, מֵעִם אָבִי לֵאמֹר: אִם-לֹא אֲבִיאֶנּוּ אֵלֶיךָ, וְחָטָאתִי לְאָבִי כָּל-הַיָּמִים.
For your servant became surety for the boy to my father, saying, 'If I do not bring him back to you, then I will have sinned against my father all my days.'note
33
וְעַתָּה, יֵשֶׁב-נָא עַבְדְּךָ תַּחַת הַנַּעַר--עֶבֶד, לַאדֹנִי; וְהַנַּעַר, יַעַל עִם-אֶחָיו.
And now, please, let your servant remain instead of the boy as a slave to my lord — and let the boy go up with his brothers.note
34
כִּי-אֵיךְ אֶעֱלֶה אֶל-אָבִי, וְהַנַּעַר אֵינֶנּוּ אִתִּי: פֶּן אֶרְאֶה בָרָע, אֲשֶׁר יִמְצָא אֶת-אָבִי.
For how can I go up to my father, and the boy not with me? — lest I see the evil that would find my father."note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–13 · the trap, built to the original specification

What the test actually is. Strip the theatre away and look at the engineering. Joseph arranges for the favoured youngest son of Rachel — the only one their father cannot bear to lose — to be caught in a capital offence, alone, with the other ten free to go home unharmed. That is not a random cruelty; it is the situation of chapter 37 rebuilt to specification, with the roles recast. Once, given the chance to abandon Rachel's son and walk away with their own skins intact, these men took it and sat down to eat. The whole point of the cup is to offer them that chance again.

The silver, put back a second time. Notice verse 1: he returns their money again. Nothing turns on it in the plot — the cup is what gets found — but it means every man is carrying unexplained silver at the moment the guard catches up. Joseph is not leaving them a plausible defence.

The divining cup. Nachesh yenachesh — the infinitive doubled for emphasis: he indeed divines by it. The practice is real and Egyptian: lecanomancy, reading the patterns made by oil dropped into water in a bowl, is attested across the ancient Near East. ⚠️ It is also a practice the Torah will flatly prohibit (Leviticus 19:26; Deuteronomy 18:10), which leaves the honest reader with a question the text does not answer: is Joseph describing what he does, or maintaining an Egyptian persona that includes a magician's reputation? He is a man who has told a foreign king that "not I — God will answer," so the second is at least as likely as the first. Genesis reports the claim and never comments on it. The KJV reads "whereby indeed he divineth," the ASV the same, the NIV "uses for divination"; none of them soften it, and neither does this.

They swear to their own innocence, and set the penalty. "Let him die; and we also will be slaves" — an oath of the confident. It is the second time this family has volunteered a death sentence for a crime it did not understand: Jacob said the same about Laban's stolen household gods (31:32) without knowing Rachel had them under her. The steward, who knows exactly where the cup is, immediately negotiates the terms down — nobody dies, only the guilty man is enslaved, the rest go free. He is not being merciful. He is setting the trap's release catch so the other nine can leave.

"He began with the eldest and finished with the youngest." One clause, and it is pure cruelty of pacing — a search that runs down the whole line in birth order while ten men watch, each one's relief lasting exactly as long as it takes to open the next bag. The steward knows. The reader knows. It takes as long as it takes.

And then the sentence that answers everything. "And they tore their garments" — the gesture their father made over the bloodied coat (37:34) and Reuben made at the empty pit (37:29) — and then every one of them loads his donkey and goes back to the city. They were free. The steward had explicitly released them. Not one of them takes it. That, and not the speech that follows, is the moment the test is passed; Judah's words are the explanation of a decision the whole group has already made with its feet.

Verses 14–17 · a confession about the wrong crime

"God has found out the guilt of your servants." Read Judah's answer carefully, because he does not defend Benjamin and he does not deny the theft — he cannot, and he does not try. What he says is stranger: God has found out the guilt of your servants — avon, the weightiest of the words for iniquity, the crookedness a person carries. They are innocent of the cup and Judah knows it. He is confessing something else, and the reader knows what, and the man in front of him knows what. It is the same reading of events the brothers made in 42:21: a ledger settling at last. They are wrong about the mechanism again — this is not heaven catching them, it is their brother — and right about the debt. The KJV's "God hath found out the iniquity of thy servants" is exact; the NIV's "God has uncovered your servants' guilt" is the same. Judah then offers all eleven into slavery together.

And Joseph refuses the group offer. "Far be it from me" — chalilah, the same protest Abraham used when he argued with God over Sodom ("far be it from you to do such a thing, to kill the righteous with the wicked," 18:25) and the word the brothers themselves used three verses earlier. Joseph is not being merciful either. A collective sentence would ruin the experiment. He insists on precisely the outcome that gives the ten what they once wanted: "the man in whose hand the cup was found — he will be my slave; and you, go up in peace to your father." Rachel's son enslaved; the rest of you, walk away clean. He even says shalom — go up in peace. It is the cruellest offer in Genesis, and it is made by a man who is testing whether it will be taken.

Verses 18–29 · the longest speech in Genesis

Seventeen verses, and it is almost all quotation. Judah's speech is the longest single utterance in the book, and its technique is remarkable: he barely argues. He recounts. He simply retells the conversation — you asked, we said, you said, we said, our father said — and lets the accumulated weight of an old man's words do the work. There is no appeal to justice (he has no case), no protest of innocence (he has already conceded), no offer of money. It is a speech made entirely out of somebody else's grief.

"You are like Pharaoh himself." The opening is exact court protocol from a man begging not to be cut off mid-sentence — and, as the reader alone knows, an understatement rather than flattery.

What Judah says to Joseph's face, without knowing. "His brother is dead, and he alone is left of his mother, and his father loves him." Every clause is a small detonation. He tells the man that the man is dead; he calls Benjamin the only surviving son of his mother, which quietly concedes the thing the whole family could never say out loud — that Jacob had one wife who counted and two sons who counted; and he states as bare fact the favouritism that started all of this: his father loves him. Judah is describing, without a flicker of self-pity or excuse, the exact structure that made him sell the man he is talking to. And notice what has changed: he is no longer complaining about it. He accepts it as the shape of his father's heart, and he has come to protect the beneficiary of it.

"My wife bore me two." Judah reports Jacob's words verbatim, and they are outrageous — the old man, in front of the sons of Leah, Bilhah and Zilpah, says "my wife" as though he had only ever had one, and counts his children as two. Judah repeats it to a stranger without editorialising. He has stopped keeping score.

"Surely he is torn to pieces." Tarof toraf — the exact words Jacob cried over the bloodied tunic in 37:33, delivered now, by one of the men who staged that scene, to the man it was staged about. And the reason the old man believes it is that Judah helped make him believe it. The room contains one person who does not know this is a lie, and it is not Judah, and it is not Joseph.

"To Sheol." Twice in five verses (vv. 29, 31), quoting his father: the grey head brought down in calamity, in sorrow, to Sheol. It is the third and fourth time this book has used the word, and every time it is the same man's grief — 37:35, 42:38, and now twice in Judah's mouth. The KJV reads "grave" in all four places; the ASV and NWT read "Sheol," and keeping it lets the reader hear one unbroken lament running twenty-two years and four chapters.

Verses 30–34 · the substitution

"His soul is bound up with the boy's soul." Nafsho qeshurah be-nafsho — nefesh twice, tied together with a verb used of literal knotting. Judah is telling a stranger that his father and his youngest brother are one life in two bodies, and therefore that a sentence on the boy is a sentence on the old man. He is arguing, with complete accuracy, about the very love that displaced him.

The surety comes due. "For your servant became surety for the boy" — the word from 43:9, and this is the moment it stops being a form of words. A guarantor pays when the thing is lost. The thing is lost. He pays.

"Let your servant remain instead of the boy." Tachat ha-na'ar — in his place, in exchange for him. The man who once said "what profit is there if we kill our brother? — come, let us sell him" (37:26-27) now offers to become the slave so that Rachel's son can go home. He proposes, precisely and voluntarily, to take the fate he once sold his brother into. Genesis does not tell you this is the point. It just puts the two sentences in the same book and lets them face each other.

And the last line is not about himself. "For how can I go up to my father, and the boy not with me? — lest I see the evil that would find my father." He does not end by pleading his own case, or his family's, or even Benjamin's. He ends on the thing he cannot bear to watch: an old man's face when he is told, a second time, that a son of Rachel is gone. It is the exact grief Judah once inflicted and then lived beside for twenty-two years, and he will do anything not to be in the room for it again. This is where the test ends. The first words of the next chapter are "Joseph could no longer hold himself in."

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "indeed practises divination" for the doubled nachesh yenachesh, unsoftened, with the tension against Deuteronomy 18:10 left in the note rather than smoothed away; "the guilt" for avon, the heaviest of the iniquity words, because Judah is confessing something other than the cup; "Far be it" for chalilah in both mouths (vv. 7, 17), matching Abraham at Sodom; "Sheol" twice more, matching 37:35 and 42:38 where the KJV reads "grave" throughout; "his soul is bound up with the boy's soul" keeping nefesh twice; and "instead of the boy" for tachat, the language of substitution.

Echoes paid. Chapter 37 rebuilt to specification — Rachel's favoured son condemned alone, the others free to walk away — and this time nobody walks; the torn garments of Reuben and Jacob, now torn by all ten; arav, Judah's surety from 43:9, called in; tarof toraf, "surely he is torn to pieces," quoted from 37:33 back to the man it was invented about; chalilah from Abraham's argument at Sodom; and Judah's own "what profit is there" answered by an offer to be sold in his brother's place.

Echoes planted. "Joseph could no longer hold himself in" — the restraint counted at 43:31 failing in the next verse of the next chapter (45:1); "I am Joseph — is my father still alive?"; and the verdict that reframes the whole thirteen years, six chapters off: "you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good" (50:20). Judah's substitution also becomes his blessing: the brother who offered to take a slave's place is the one who receives the sceptre (49:8-10).

Next installment in this book: Genesis 45 — Joseph clears the room and breaks, and the Egyptians hear him weeping from the next building. "I am Joseph. Is my father still alive?" — and his brothers cannot answer him, because they are terrified. Then the sentence that has been twenty-two years arriving: "it was not you who sent me here, but God."

Genesis 45 — The Mister Translation

1
וְלֹא-יָכֹל יוֹסֵף לְהִתְאַפֵּק, לְכֹל הַנִּצָּבִים עָלָיו, וַיִּקְרָא, הוֹצִיאוּ כָל-אִישׁ מֵעָלָי; וְלֹא-עָמַד אִישׁ אִתּוֹ, בְּהִתְוַדַּע יוֹסֵף אֶל-אֶחָיו.
And Joseph could no longer hold himself in before all who stood by him, and he called out, "Send every man out from me!" And no man stood with him when Joseph made himself known to his brothers.note
2
וַיִּתֵּן אֶת-קֹלוֹ, בִּבְכִי; וַיִּשְׁמְעוּ מִצְרַיִם, וַיִּשְׁמַע בֵּית פַּרְעֹה.
And he gave his voice in weeping, and Egypt heard, and the house of Pharaoh heard.note
3
וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל-אֶחָיו אֲנִי יוֹסֵף, הַעוֹד אָבִי חָי; וְלֹא-יָכְלוּ אֶחָיו לַעֲנוֹת אֹתוֹ, כִּי נִבְהֲלוּ מִפָּנָיו.
And Joseph said to his brothers, "I am Joseph. Is my father still alive?" And his brothers could not answer him, for they were terrified before him.note
4
וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל-אֶחָיו גְּשׁוּ-נָא אֵלַי, וַיִּגָּשׁוּ; וַיֹּאמֶר, אֲנִי יוֹסֵף אֲחִיכֶם, אֲשֶׁר-מְכַרְתֶּם אֹתִי, מִצְרָיְמָה.
And Joseph said to his brothers, "Come near to me, please." And they came near. And he said, "I am Joseph your brother, whom you sold into Egypt.note
5
וְעַתָּה אַל-תֵּעָצְבוּ, וְאַל-יִחַר בְּעֵינֵיכֶם, כִּי-מְכַרְתֶּם אֹתִי, הֵנָּה: כִּי לְמִחְיָה, שְׁלָחַנִי אֱלֹהִים לִפְנֵיכֶם.
And now, do not be pained, and let it not burn in your own eyes that you sold me here — for God sent me before you to preserve life.note
6
כִּי-זֶה שְׁנָתַיִם הָרָעָב, בְּקֶרֶב הָאָרֶץ; וְעוֹד חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים, אֲשֶׁר אֵין-חָרִישׁ וְקָצִיר.
For these two years the famine has been in the midst of the land, and there are yet five years in which there is no plowing and no harvest.note
7
וַיִּשְׁלָחֵנִי אֱלֹהִים לִפְנֵיכֶם, לָשׂוּם לָכֶם שְׁאֵרִית בָּאָרֶץ, וּלְהַחֲיוֹת לָכֶם, לִפְלֵיטָה גְּדֹלָה.
And God sent me before you to set for you a remnant in the land, and to keep you alive for a great deliverance.note
8
וְעַתָּה, לֹא-אַתֶּם שְׁלַחְתֶּם אֹתִי הֵנָּה, כִּי, הָאֱלֹהִים; וַיְשִׂימֵנִי לְאָב לְפַרְעֹה, וּלְאָדוֹן לְכָל-בֵּיתוֹ, וּמֹשֵׁל, בְּכָל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם.
And now — it was not you who sent me here, but God; and he has made me a father to Pharaoh, and lord of all his house, and ruler in all the land of Egypt.note
9
מַהֲרוּ, וַעֲלוּ אֶל-אָבִי, וַאֲמַרְתֶּם אֵלָיו כֹּה אָמַר בִּנְךָ יוֹסֵף, שָׂמַנִי אֱלֹהִים לְאָדוֹן לְכָל-מִצְרָיִם; רְדָה אֵלַי, אַל-תַּעֲמֹד.
Hurry and go up to my father, and say to him, 'So says your son Joseph: God has made me lord of all Egypt. Come down to me — do not stand still.note
10
וְיָשַׁבְתָּ בְאֶרֶץ-גֹּשֶׁן, וְהָיִיתָ קָרוֹב אֵלַי--אַתָּה, וּבָנֶיךָ וּבְנֵי בָנֶיךָ; וְצֹאנְךָ וּבְקָרְךָ, וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר-לָךְ.
And you will dwell in the land of Goshen, and you will be near to me — you and your sons and your sons' sons, and your flocks and your herds and all that is yours.note
11
וְכִלְכַּלְתִּי אֹתְךָ שָׁם, כִּי-עוֹד חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים רָעָב: פֶּן-תִּוָּרֵשׁ אַתָּה וּבֵיתְךָ, וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר-לָךְ.
And I will provide for you there, for there are yet five years of famine — lest you come to poverty, you and your house and all that is yours.'note
12
וְהִנֵּה עֵינֵיכֶם רֹאוֹת, וְעֵינֵי אָחִי בִנְיָמִין: כִּי-פִי, הַמְדַבֵּר אֲלֵיכֶם.
And look — your own eyes see, and the eyes of my brother Benjamin, that it is my mouth speaking to you.note
13
וְהִגַּדְתֶּם לְאָבִי, אֶת-כָּל-כְּבוֹדִי בְּמִצְרַיִם, וְאֵת, כָּל-אֲשֶׁר רְאִיתֶם; וּמִהַרְתֶּם וְהוֹרַדְתֶּם אֶת-אָבִי, הֵנָּה.
And you shall tell my father of all my honour in Egypt, and of all that you have seen; and you shall hurry and bring my father down here."note
14
וַיִּפֹּל עַל-צַוְּארֵי בִנְיָמִן-אָחִיו, וַיֵּבְךְּ; וּבִנְיָמִן--בָּכָה, עַל-צַוָּארָיו.
And he fell on the neck of Benjamin his brother and wept, and Benjamin wept on his neck.note
15
וַיְנַשֵּׁק לְכָל-אֶחָיו, וַיֵּבְךְּ עֲלֵהֶם; וְאַחֲרֵי כֵן, דִּבְּרוּ אֶחָיו אִתּוֹ.
And he kissed all his brothers and wept over them; and after that his brothers spoke with him.note
16
וְהַקֹּל נִשְׁמַע, בֵּית פַּרְעֹה לֵאמֹר, בָּאוּ, אֲחֵי יוֹסֵף; וַיִּיטַב בְּעֵינֵי פַרְעֹה, וּבְעֵינֵי עֲבָדָיו.
And the sound was heard in the house of Pharaoh, saying, "Joseph's brothers have come!" And it was good in the eyes of Pharaoh and in the eyes of his servants.note
17
וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אֶל-יוֹסֵף, אֱמֹר אֶל-אַחֶיךָ זֹאת עֲשׂוּ: טַעֲנוּ, אֶת-בְּעִירְכֶם, וּלְכוּ-בֹאוּ, אַרְצָה כְּנָעַן.
And Pharaoh said to Joseph, "Say to your brothers: Do this — load your beasts and go, come to the land of Canaan,note
18
וּקְחוּ אֶת-אֲבִיכֶם וְאֶת-בָּתֵּיכֶם, וּבֹאוּ אֵלָי; וְאֶתְּנָה לָכֶם, אֶת-טוּב אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, וְאִכְלוּ, אֶת-חֵלֶב הָאָרֶץ.
and take your father and your households and come to me; and I will give you the good of the land of Egypt, and you will eat the fat of the land.note
19
וְאַתָּה צֻוֵּיתָה, זֹאת עֲשׂוּ: קְחוּ-לָכֶם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם עֲגָלוֹת, לְטַפְּכֶם וְלִנְשֵׁיכֶם, וּנְשָׂאתֶם אֶת-אֲבִיכֶם, וּבָאתֶם.
And you are commanded: Do this — take for yourselves wagons from the land of Egypt for your little ones and for your wives, and carry your father, and come.note
20
וְעֵינְכֶם, אַל-תָּחֹס עַל-כְּלֵיכֶם: כִּי-טוּב כָּל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, לָכֶם הוּא.
And let not your eye look with regret on your goods, for the good of all the land of Egypt is yours."note
21
וַיַּעֲשׂוּ-כֵן בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, וַיִּתֵּן לָהֶם יוֹסֵף עֲגָלוֹת עַל-פִּי פַרְעֹה; וַיִּתֵּן לָהֶם צֵדָה, לַדָּרֶךְ.
And the sons of Israel did so. And Joseph gave them wagons at Pharaoh's mouth, and he gave them provisions for the way.note
22
לְכֻלָּם נָתַן לָאִישׁ, חֲלִפוֹת שְׂמָלֹת; וּלְבִנְיָמִן נָתַן שְׁלֹשׁ מֵאוֹת כֶּסֶף, וְחָמֵשׁ חֲלִפֹת שְׂמָלֹת.
To all of them, to each man, he gave changes of garments; and to Benjamin he gave three hundred of silver and five changes of garments.note
23
וּלְאָבִיו שָׁלַח כְּזֹאת, עֲשָׂרָה חֲמֹרִים, נֹשְׂאִים, מִטּוּב מִצְרָיִם; וְעֶשֶׂר אֲתֹנֹת נֹשְׂאֹת בָּר וָלֶחֶם וּמָזוֹן, לְאָבִיו--לַדָּרֶךְ.
And to his father he sent this: ten donkeys carrying of the good of Egypt, and ten she-donkeys carrying grain and bread and food for his father for the way.note
24
וַיְשַׁלַּח אֶת-אֶחָיו, וַיֵּלֵכוּ; וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם, אַל-תִּרְגְּזוּ בַּדָּרֶךְ.
And he sent his brothers off, and they went; and he said to them, "Do not be agitated on the way."note
25
וַיַּעֲלוּ, מִמִּצְרָיִם; וַיָּבֹאוּ אֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן, אֶל-יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם.
And they went up from Egypt and came to the land of Canaan, to Jacob their father.note
26
וַיַּגִּדוּ לוֹ לֵאמֹר, עוֹד יוֹסֵף חַי, וְכִי-הוּא מֹשֵׁל, בְּכָל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם; וַיָּפָג לִבּוֹ, כִּי לֹא-הֶאֱמִין לָהֶם.
And they told him, saying, "Joseph is still alive! And he is ruler in all the land of Egypt!" And his heart went numb, for he did not believe them.note
27
וַיְדַבְּרוּ אֵלָיו, אֵת כָּל-דִּבְרֵי יוֹסֵף אֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר אֲלֵהֶם, וַיַּרְא אֶת-הָעֲגָלוֹת, אֲשֶׁר-שָׁלַח יוֹסֵף לָשֵׂאת אֹתוֹ; וַתְּחִי, רוּחַ יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם.
And they spoke to him all the words of Joseph that he had spoken to them, and he saw the wagons that Joseph had sent to carry him — and the spirit of Jacob their father revived.note
28
וַיֹּאמֶר, יִשְׂרָאֵל, רַב עוֹד-יוֹסֵף בְּנִי, חָי; אֵלְכָה וְאֶרְאֶנּוּ, בְּטֶרֶם אָמוּת.{פ}
And Israel said, "Enough! Joseph my son is still alive. Let me go and see him before I die."note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–4 · the restraint fails, and the room is cleared

"Could no longer hold himself in." Lo yakhol lehit'appeq — and this is a debt being paid, not a fresh phrase. The same verb was counted twice already: at 43:31 Joseph washed his face and held himself in to get through a meal, and the whole of chapter 44 is a man holding himself in while his brother speaks. Here the verb goes negative. Everything he has built for thirteen years — the Egyptian name, the interpreter, the persona — comes apart in one clause. The KJV has "could not refrain himself," the ASV the same; the NWT "was no longer able to control himself," which is the sense exactly.

He clears the room first. This is the last administrative act of the vizier, and it is done to protect them. What he is about to say — you sold me — is a confession of a capital crime, spoken in the palace of a foreign king, in front of witnesses who work for that king. So there are no witnesses. The Egyptians go out. What follows is heard by no one who could use it, which is precisely why verse 2 matters: he cannot keep the sound in even when he has kept the room clear. They hear him through the walls; they do not hear what he says.

"I am Joseph. Is my father still alive?" Three words in Hebrew, then four. And note that the question is not a request for information — they have just told him, at length, that their father is alive and grieving (44:30-31). He knows. It is the question that has been sitting underneath every interview, every accusation, every planted cup: not is he alive but is he still my father. ⚠️ Some read it as a genuine gasp — the man has been maintaining a fiction so long that the truth arrives as a shock even to him. Others read it as the first sentence of the real conversation, the one he has been unable to have. The Hebrew supports either and prefers neither.

"They were terrified before him." Nivhalu — struck dumb, thrown into panic. Not joy. Not relief. The most powerful man they have ever met has just told them he is the boy they threw in a pit, and the arithmetic is instant and terrible. Genesis does not give them a line.
