It gives them silence, and it is the correct silence.

"Come near to me, please." Geshu-na — and then he says it again, adding the two things that make it survivable and the one thing that makes it true: your brother, and whom you sold into Egypt. He does not euphemise. When he told the cupbearer his story he said only "I was stolen away," leaving the criminals out of the sentence. Now, to their faces, with the door shut, he names the act and the actors in four words — and then spends the next four verses making it bearable. Both halves are the same speech. Nobody in this chapter pretends it did not happen.

Verses 5–13 · "it was not you who sent me here, but God"

The two sentences that have to be held together. In verse 4 he says you sold me. In verse 8 he says it was not you who sent me here. These are not a contradiction and the second is not a retraction — if it were, verse 4 would not be in the chapter. Genesis puts them nine words apart on purpose. The brothers did it, freely, and it was evil; and God was working through it toward something they did not intend. The book never resolves the tension by deleting one side. Five chapters on it will state both in a single sentence — "you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good" (50:20) — and this is the first half of that verdict, spoken while it is still a decision rather than a summary.

What he is actually doing pastorally. "Do not be pained, and let it not burn in your own eyes." He is not telling them their guilt was imaginary. He is telling them not to be destroyed by it — because a group of men paralysed by shame in a famine will not go home and fetch their father. He needs them functional. The theology in verse 8 is doing work in the room, not just in the commentary.

The salvation vocabulary arrives here. Three words in three verses, and all three will carry enormous freight later in the Hebrew Bible: michyah, "to preserve life" (v. 5); she'erit, "a remnant" (v. 7) — the word the prophets will use for the surviving fragment of Israel; and peletah, "a great deliverance" or escape (v. 7). This translation keeps all three distinct rather than flattening them into "to save your lives." The KJV has "to preserve you a posterity in the earth, and to save your lives by a great deliverance"; ASV "to preserve you a remnant"; the ESV "to keep alive for you many survivors." Joseph is describing a family of seventy people, and he is using the language of national survival.

"A father to Pharaoh." Av le-Phar'oh — not a metaphor invented by the narrator but a genuine Egyptian court title (it-netjer, "father of the god," borne by senior officials and royal tutors). ⚠️ The correspondence is real but should not be pushed too hard: the title is attested across long stretches of Egyptian history and cannot by itself date the story. It is one of a cluster of details in these chapters that read as at home in Egypt rather than invented from outside it. See the encyclopedia entry on Joseph's pharaoh for how the dating question actually stands.

"It is my mouth speaking to you." Verse 12, and it is the quietest payoff in the chapter. At 42:23 the narrator paused to tell us there was an interpreter between them, because Joseph was pretending not to understand Hebrew — and then let us watch him overhear their confession and turn away to weep. The interpreter is now gone. He is speaking his own language, in his own mouth, and he tells them to notice. Benjamin's eyes are named separately because Benjamin is the only one who never knew him.

Goshen, named for the first time. The eastern Nile Delta, good pasture, close to Canaan and slightly outside Egypt proper — which is exactly why a family of shepherds can live there without being absorbed. It is also, four hundred years later, where Israel will be enslaved and from which they will leave. The address of the redemption is given here, in an invitation.

Verses 14–24 · weeping, wagons, and five changes of garments

Benjamin first, and no words. Joseph weeps four times in this story and the trajectory is worth tracking: he turned away to weep (42:24), he went into a room to weep (43:30), the Egyptians heard him through a wall (45:2), and now he simply falls on his brother's neck in front of everyone. Benjamin gets no line in the entire Joseph story — not one — but he weeps back, and that is his whole part.

"And after that his brothers spoke with him." Verse 15, and it is the reunion's actual hinge. The kissing and weeping come first; speech takes longer. They had not been able to answer him in verse 3. It takes an embrace and a kiss and a lot of crying before ten men can say anything at all to the brother they sold — and Genesis notes the delay rather than skipping it.

Pharaoh is glad, and that is not a small thing. A famine, a foreign vizier, and the arrival of a large foreign family with livestock: a king could reasonably be cool about this. Instead he doubles Joseph's offer unprompted — the good of the land, the fat of the land, wagons at state expense — and adds the line in verse 20 that is almost a joke: don't waste a thought on your furniture. The KJV's "regard not your stuff" is famous and, for once, exactly right in tone.

The wagons. Agalot — Egyptian wheeled carts, not something a Canaanite herdsman owned or could easily fake. Remember them for verse 27: they are what finally convinces Jacob, when words could not.

⚠️ Five changes of garments. Read verse 22 slowly. Every brother gets a change of clothes; Benjamin gets five, plus three hundred pieces of silver, in front of the other ten. This is the man who was nearly murdered over a coat his father gave him (37:3-4), handing Rachel's other son a conspicuous multiple of what everyone else receives. Readings differ and the text does not adjudicate: some take it as a final quiet test — will they resent it now? — though the testing is plainly over; some as generosity toward the brother he has just got back; and some, most uncomfortably, as evidence that the family's oldest pattern survives the reconciliation intact, reasserting itself in the very hour of it. Genesis reports the number and moves on. It is not in the habit of protecting its heroes.

"Do not be agitated on the way." Al tirgezu ba-derekh, and the verb ragaz covers a range: to tremble, to be stirred up, to quarrel. So the versions split. The KJV's "see that ye fall not out by the way" takes it as don't argue — and the old Jewish reading behind it is pointed: don't spend the journey assigning blame for who suggested the pit. The NWT's "do not get upset" and the NIV's "don't quarrel" split the same difference; the ESV has "do not quarrel." This translation keeps agitated, which is where the Hebrew actually sits and leaves both readings open — though given what he has just handed Benjamin in front of them, the quarrelling sense is hard to dismiss.

Verses 25–28 · an old man's heart goes numb

"His heart went numb." Vayyafag libbo — the root pug means to grow faint, sluggish, cold; it is used of a limb that has lost feeling. Not joy, and not quite shock: a system refusing to process an input. The KJV and ASV have "Jacob's heart fainted," which is close; the NWT "his heart grew numb," which is closer to the physical metaphor. And the reason is given without decoration: he did not believe them. Twenty-two years ago these same men handed him a bloodied coat and let him conclude the rest. He has no reason to believe anything they bring him.

It is the wagons that do it, not the words. Verse 27 is careful about the order: they told him everything Joseph had said — and he saw the wagons. Egyptian carts, sent to carry him. Testimony did not move him; evidence did. Genesis lets the detail stand without a word of comment, and it is one of the most human sentences in the book.

"The spirit of Jacob their father revived." Vatechi ruach — literally lived, from the same root as the "keep you alive" of verse 7. The man who said he would go down to Sheol mourning (37:35, 42:38, 44:31 — four times in all) comes back to life in a clause.

And the name changes in the last verse. Watch it: verse 25 Jacob their father, verse 27 the spirit of Jacob — and then verse 28, "and Israel said." The narrator has been switching between the two names all through this story, and the switch here lands on the decision. The grieving father is Jacob; the man who says "Enough — let me go and see him" is Israel. Rav, "enough," is blunt to the point of rudeness: stop talking, it is sufficient, I have heard what I needed. He does not ask about the ruling of Egypt or the wagons or the honour. One thing is still alive and he wants to see it before he dies.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "could no longer hold himself in," the same verb as 43:31, so the failure of the restraint is visible as a failure; "to preserve life" / "a remnant" / "a great deliverance" kept as three distinct words (michyah, she'erit, peletah) rather than merged into one idea; "a father to Pharaoh" left as the court title it is; "agitated" for ragaz, holding the quarrel/tremble ambiguity open; "his heart went numb" for the physical sense of pug; and "Enough!" for rav, kept as blunt as the Hebrew.

Echoes paid. The restraint counted at 43:31, failing in the first clause; "there was an interpreter between them" answered by "it is my mouth speaking to you"; "I was stolen away" — the sentence with the criminals left out — replaced by "whom you sold"; turning away to weep and weeping in a locked room, now audible through the palace wall; and the coat that started it, reappearing as five changes of garments handed to Rachel's other son in front of the same men.

Echoes planted. Goshen, the address of the descent, which becomes the address of the slavery and then of the Exodus; the wagons, which persuade Jacob where testimony cannot; "it was not you who sent me here, but God," half of a verdict completed at 50:20; and the name Israel arriving on the decision to go down to Egypt — a journey God will shortly have to promise him is safe (46:3-4).

Next installment in this book: Genesis 46 — Israel packs up everything he owns and goes as far as Beersheba, where he stops and offers sacrifices, and God speaks to him in the visions of the night: "Do not be afraid to go down to Egypt." Then the seventy names, listed one by one.

Genesis 46 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיִּסַּע יִשְׂרָאֵל וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ, וַיָּבֹא בְּאֵרָה שָּׁבַע; וַיִּזְבַּח זְבָחִים, לֵאלֹהֵי אָבִיו יִצְחָק.
And Israel journeyed, and all that was his, and came to Beersheba; and he offered sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac.note
2
וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים לְיִשְׂרָאֵל בְּמַרְאֹת הַלַּיְלָה, וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב יַעֲקֹב; וַיֹּאמֶר, הִנֵּנִי.
And God said to Israel in the visions of the night, and he said, "Jacob, Jacob." And he said, "Here I am."note
3
וַיֹּאמֶר, אָנֹכִי הָאֵל אֱלֹהֵי אָבִיךָ; אַל-תִּירָא מֵרְדָה מִצְרַיְמָה, כִּי-לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל אֲשִׂימְךָ שָׁם.
And he said, "I am the God, the God of your father. Do not fear to go down to Egypt, for I will make you into a great nation there.note
4
אָנֹכִי, אֵרֵד עִמְּךָ מִצְרַיְמָה, וְאָנֹכִי, אַעַלְךָ גַם-עָלֹה; וְיוֹסֵף, יָשִׁית יָדוֹ עַל-עֵינֶיךָ.
I myself will go down with you to Egypt, and I myself will surely bring you up again; and Joseph will lay his hand on your eyes."note
5
וַיָּקָם יַעֲקֹב, מִבְּאֵר שָׁבַע; וַיִּשְׂאוּ בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת-יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם, וְאֶת-טַפָּם וְאֶת-נְשֵׁיהֶם, בָּעֲגָלוֹת, אֲשֶׁר-שָׁלַח פַּרְעֹה לָשֵׂאת אֹתוֹ.
And Jacob rose up from Beersheba; and the sons of Israel carried Jacob their father, and their little ones and their wives, in the wagons that Pharaoh had sent to carry him.note
6
וַיִּקְחוּ אֶת-מִקְנֵיהֶם, וְאֶת-רְכוּשָׁם אֲשֶׁר רָכְשׁוּ בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן, וַיָּבֹאוּ, מִצְרָיְמָה: יַעֲקֹב, וְכָל-זַרְעוֹ אִתּוֹ.
And they took their livestock and their goods that they had gained in the land of Canaan, and came to Egypt — Jacob, and all his offspring with him:note
7
בָּנָיו וּבְנֵי בָנָיו, אִתּוֹ, בְּנֹתָיו וּבְנוֹת בָּנָיו, וְכָל-זַרְעוֹ--הֵבִיא אִתּוֹ, מִצְרָיְמָה.{ס}
his sons and his sons' sons with him, his daughters and his sons' daughters, and all his offspring he brought with him to Egypt.note
8
וְאֵלֶּה שְׁמוֹת בְּנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל הַבָּאִים מִצְרַיְמָה, יַעֲקֹב וּבָנָיו: בְּכֹר יַעֲקֹב, רְאוּבֵן.
And these are the names of the sons of Israel who came to Egypt, Jacob and his sons: Jacob's firstborn, Reuben.note
9
וּבְנֵי, רְאוּבֵן--חֲנוֹךְ וּפַלּוּא, וְחֶצְרֹן וְכַרְמִי.
And the sons of Reuben: Hanoch and Pallu and Hezron and Carmi.note
10
וּבְנֵי שִׁמְעוֹן, יְמוּאֵל וְיָמִין וְאֹהַד--וְיָכִין וְצֹחַר; וְשָׁאוּל, בֶּן-הַכְּנַעֲנִית.
And the sons of Simeon: Jemuel and Jamin and Ohad and Jachin and Zohar, and Shaul the son of the Canaanite woman.note
11
וּבְנֵי, לֵוִי--גֵּרְשׁוֹן, קְהָת וּמְרָרִי.
And the sons of Levi: Gershon, Kohath and Merari.note
12
וּבְנֵי יְהוּדָה, עֵר וְאוֹנָן וְשֵׁלָה--וָפֶרֶץ וָזָרַח; וַיָּמָת עֵר וְאוֹנָן בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן, וַיִּהְיוּ בְנֵי-פֶרֶץ חֶצְרֹן וְחָמוּל.
And the sons of Judah: Er and Onan and Shelah and Perez and Zerah — and Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan. And the sons of Perez were Hezron and Hamul.note
13
וּבְנֵי, יִשָּׂשכָר--תּוֹלָע וּפֻוָה, וְיוֹב וְשִׁמְרֹן.
And the sons of Issachar: Tola and Puvah and Iob and Shimron.note
14
וּבְנֵי, זְבֻלוּן--סֶרֶד וְאֵלוֹן, וְיַחְלְאֵל.
And the sons of Zebulun: Sered and Elon and Jahleel.note
15
אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי לֵאָה, אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה לְיַעֲקֹב בְּפַדַּן אֲרָם, וְאֵת, דִּינָה בִתּוֹ: כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ בָּנָיו וּבְנוֹתָיו, שְׁלֹשִׁים וְשָׁלֹשׁ.
These are the sons of Leah, whom she bore to Jacob in Paddan-aram, and Dinah his daughter: all the souls of his sons and his daughters, thirty-three.note
16
וּבְנֵי גָד, צִפְיוֹן וְחַגִּי שׁוּנִי וְאֶצְבֹּן, עֵרִי וַאֲרוֹדִי, וְאַרְאֵלִי.
And the sons of Gad: Ziphion and Haggi, Shuni and Ezbon, Eri and Arodi and Areli.note
17
וּבְנֵי אָשֵׁר, יִמְנָה וְיִשְׁוָה וְיִשְׁוִי וּבְרִיעָה--וְשֶׂרַח אֲחֹתָם; וּבְנֵי בְרִיעָה, חֶבֶר וּמַלְכִּיאֵל.
And the sons of Asher: Imnah and Ishvah and Ishvi and Beriah, and Serah their sister. And the sons of Beriah: Heber and Malchiel.note
18
אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי זִלְפָּה, אֲשֶׁר-נָתַן לָבָן לְלֵאָה בִתּוֹ; וַתֵּלֶד אֶת-אֵלֶּה לְיַעֲקֹב, שֵׁשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה נָפֶשׁ.
These are the sons of Zilpah, whom Laban gave to Leah his daughter; and she bore these to Jacob — sixteen souls.note
19
בְּנֵי רָחֵל אֵשֶׁת יַעֲקֹב, יוֹסֵף וּבִנְיָמִן.
The sons of Rachel, Jacob's wife: Joseph and Benjamin.note
20
וַיִּוָּלֵד לְיוֹסֵף, בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה-לּוֹ אָסְנַת, בַּת-פּוֹטִי פֶרַע כֹּהֵן אֹן--אֶת-מְנַשֶּׁה, וְאֶת-אֶפְרָיִם.
And to Joseph, in the land of Egypt, were born Manasseh and Ephraim, whom Asenath the daughter of Poti-phera priest of On bore to him.note
21
וּבְנֵי בִנְיָמִן, בֶּלַע וָבֶכֶר וְאַשְׁבֵּל, גֵּרָא וְנַעֲמָן, אֵחִי וָרֹאשׁ; מֻפִּים וְחֻפִּים, וָאָרְדְּ.
And the sons of Benjamin: Bela and Becher and Ashbel, Gera and Naaman, Ehi and Rosh, Muppim and Huppim and Ard.note
22
אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי רָחֵל, אֲשֶׁר יֻלַּד לְיַעֲקֹב--כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ, אַרְבָּעָה עָשָׂר.
These are the sons of Rachel who were born to Jacob — all the souls, fourteen.note
23
וּבְנֵי-דָן, חֻשִׁים.
And the sons of Dan: Hushim.note
24
וּבְנֵי, נַפְתָּלִי--יַחְצְאֵל וְגוּנִי, וְיֵצֶר וְשִׁלֵּם.
And the sons of Naphtali: Jahzeel and Guni and Jezer and Shillem.note
25
אֵלֶּה בְּנֵי בִלְהָה, אֲשֶׁר-נָתַן לָבָן לְרָחֵל בִּתּוֹ; וַתֵּלֶד אֶת-אֵלֶּה לְיַעֲקֹב, כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ שִׁבְעָה.
These are the sons of Bilhah, whom Laban gave to Rachel his daughter; and she bore these to Jacob — all the souls, seven.note
26
כָּל-הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַבָּאָה לְיַעֲקֹב מִצְרַיְמָה, יֹצְאֵי יְרֵכוֹ, מִלְּבַד, נְשֵׁי בְנֵי-יַעֲקֹב--כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ, שִׁשִּׁים וָשֵׁשׁ.
All the souls belonging to Jacob who came to Egypt, who came out of his loins, apart from the wives of Jacob's sons — all the souls were sixty-six.note
27
וּבְנֵי יוֹסֵף אֲשֶׁר-יֻלַּד-לוֹ בְמִצְרַיִם, נֶפֶשׁ שְׁנָיִם: כָּל-הַנֶּפֶשׁ לְבֵית-יַעֲקֹב הַבָּאָה מִצְרַיְמָה, שִׁבְעִים.{ס}
And the sons of Joseph who were born to him in Egypt, two souls. All the souls of the house of Jacob who came to Egypt: seventy.note
28
וְאֶת-יְהוּדָה שָׁלַח לְפָנָיו, אֶל-יוֹסֵף, לְהוֹרֹת לְפָנָיו, גֹּשְׁנָה; וַיָּבֹאוּ, אַרְצָה גֹּשֶׁן.
And he sent Judah ahead of him to Joseph, to point the way before him to Goshen; and they came to the land of Goshen.note
29
וַיֶּאְסֹר יוֹסֵף מֶרְכַּבְתּוֹ, וַיַּעַל לִקְרַאת-יִשְׂרָאֵל אָבִיו גֹּשְׁנָה; וַיֵּרָא אֵלָיו, וַיִּפֹּל עַל-צַוָּארָיו, וַיֵּבְךְּ עַל-צַוָּארָיו, עוֹד.
And Joseph harnessed his chariot and went up to meet Israel his father, to Goshen; and he appeared to him, and fell on his neck, and wept on his neck a long while.note
30
וַיֹּאמֶר יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל-יוֹסֵף, אָמוּתָה הַפָּעַם, אַחֲרֵי רְאוֹתִי אֶת-פָּנֶיךָ, כִּי עוֹדְךָ חָי.
And Israel said to Joseph, "Let me die this time, after my seeing your face, that you are still alive."note
31
וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל-אֶחָיו וְאֶל-בֵּית אָבִיו, אֶעֱלֶה וְאַגִּידָה לְפַרְעֹה; וְאֹמְרָה אֵלָיו, אַחַי וּבֵית-אָבִי אֲשֶׁר בְּאֶרֶץ-כְּנַעַן בָּאוּ אֵלָי.
And Joseph said to his brothers and to his father's house, "I will go up and tell Pharaoh, and say to him, 'My brothers and my father's house, who were in the land of Canaan, have come to me.note
32
וְהָאֲנָשִׁים רֹעֵי צֹאן, כִּי-אַנְשֵׁי מִקְנֶה הָיוּ; וְצֹאנָם וּבְקָרָם וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר לָהֶם, הֵבִיאוּ.
And the men are shepherds of flocks, for they have been men of livestock; and their flocks and their herds and all that they have, they have brought.'note
33
וְהָיָה, כִּי-יִקְרָא לָכֶם פַּרְעֹה; וְאָמַר, מַה-מַּעֲשֵׂיכֶם.
And so it will be, when Pharaoh calls you and says, 'What is your occupation?' —note
34
וַאֲמַרְתֶּם, אַנְשֵׁי מִקְנֶה הָיוּ עֲבָדֶיךָ מִנְּעוּרֵינוּ וְעַד-עַתָּה--גַּם-אֲנַחְנוּ, גַּם-אֲבֹתֵינוּ: בַּעֲבוּר, תֵּשְׁבוּ בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן, כִּי-תוֹעֲבַת מִצְרַיִם, כָּל-רֹעֵה צֹאן.
you shall say, 'Your servants have been men of livestock from our youth until now, both we and our fathers' — so that you may dwell in the land of Goshen; for every shepherd of flocks is an abomination to Egypt."note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–7 · the last stop, and permission to leave

Why he stops at Beersheba. An old man given wagons and told to hurry does not pause to sacrifice unless something is troubling him — and Beersheba is not a random halt. It is the southern edge of the land, the last place in Canaan before the road becomes the road to Egypt; it is where Abraham planted a tamarisk and called on the everlasting God (21:33); and it is where Isaac received his own night visit and built his own altar (26:24-25). Jacob sacrifices there specifically "to the God of his father Isaac" — not "of Abraham and Isaac," the usual pairing. There is a reason for the narrowing, and it is in the next paragraph.

The thing he is afraid of. ⚠️ Every previous descent into Egypt in this book has gone badly or been forbidden outright. Abraham went down because of a famine and immediately lied about his wife (12:10-20). And when a famine came in Isaac's day and Isaac moved to go down, God stopped him flat: "Do not go down to Egypt" (26:2). That is the prohibition Jacob is carrying, spoken to the very father whose God he is now sacrificing to. He is at the border asking whether the family is permitted to cross it. The answer, in verse 3, uses the same words and reverses them: al tira me-redah mitzrayemah — "do not fear to go down to Egypt." This is not a generic reassurance. It is a specific licence, and Genesis has spent thirty-four chapters making it necessary.

"Jacob, Jacob." The doubled name is rare and it is always the same kind of moment: "Abraham, Abraham" at the knife (22:11), and later "Moses, Moses" at the bush and "Samuel, Samuel" in the dark. The answer is always the one word — hineni, "here I am" — which Abraham said on the mountain and which Joseph said when his father sent him to find his brothers (37:13), the errand that ended in the pit. The old man now says it himself, at the border, in the dark.

The two anokhis. Verse 4 is emphatic twice over — "I myself will go down with you… and I myself will surely bring you up." The promise is not that the family will be spared the descent but that God is going into it with them; and a'alkha gam aloh, the doubled "bring up," is the verb the whole book of Exodus will be named by. Israel goes down to Egypt with the return already promised in the same breath. ⚠️ It is fulfilled twice over and neither reading cancels the other: personally, when Jacob's body is carried back to the cave at Machpelah (50:13), and nationally, four hundred years later.

"Joseph will lay his hand on your eyes." The office of closing a dead man's eyes belonged to his nearest son. It is the gentlest sentence God speaks in Genesis, and it answers the fear Jacob has actually been living with since chapter 37 — not that he will die in Egypt, but that he would die without that son in the room. KJV "and Joseph shall put his hand upon thine eyes."

Verses 8–27 · seventy souls, and what a list is for

Why the list is here at all. A modern reader skims it; ancient readers did not. This is the roster of the nation at its smallest — the moment before the multiplication — and it exists so that Exodus 1 can open by repeating its first words ("these are the names of the sons of Israel who came to Egypt") and then say that they filled the land. The list is the "before" photograph. It is also, quietly, the ledger of everything that has happened in this book: the deaths, the irregular marriages, the one daughter anyone bothered to name.

The things it refuses to leave out. Verse 12 records Judah's sons and then adds, flatly, "and Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan" — chapter 38 acknowledged in a subordinate clause, in a document meant to honour the family. Verse 10 names "Shaul the son of the Canaanite woman," marking one of Simeon's sons as born of exactly the marriage the patriarchs kept sending servants a thousand miles to avoid. Verse 15 counts Dinah among Leah's children, so that chapter 34 is not quietly dropped. And verse 17 names Serah, the sister — a granddaughter listed by name for no stated reason, one of the very few women in the roster, which is precisely why later Jewish tradition grew a whole legend around her. Genesis does not explain her. It just declines to lose her.

⚠️ The count does not add up cleanly, and pretending otherwise would be dishonest. Verse 15 says Leah's line is thirty-three, but the names given total thirty-two (thirty-three only if you count Jacob himself, or Dinah, or read Er and Onan differently). Verses 26 and 27 give sixty-six who came, then seventy for the whole house — which works if you add Joseph, his two sons, and Jacob (66 + 3 + 1 = 70), but the arithmetic depends on which people the "sixty-six" is already excluding. Ancient readers noticed too: the Septuagint reads seventy-five, adding grandsons of Joseph, and that is the number Stephen quotes in Acts 7:14 — so the New Testament and the Hebrew text disagree on the total because they are quoting different editions. A Dead Sea Scrolls fragment of Exodus supports the seventy-five reading. This translation follows the Masoretic seventy, as it does throughout, and notes the variant rather than harmonising it. Seventy is in any case doing more than counting: it is the number of the nations in chapter 10, and Israel goes down into Egypt as one small world.

"Souls." Nefesh, kept literal here as it is everywhere in this translation. The count is not of "persons" in the abstract but of living breathing throats — the same word used of the animals on the fifth day and of Judah's father whose nefesh is bound to the boy's. KJV and ASV both keep "souls"; the NIV and ESV render "persons," which is accurate to the sense and loses the thread.

Verses 28–30 · Judah goes first, and an old man is finished

Judah is sent ahead. Of eleven available sons, this is the one entrusted to go find Joseph and point the road. Nothing is said about it; the narrative simply hands him the job. It is the same man who proposed the sale (37:26), stood surety for Benjamin (43:9), and offered to stay a slave in his place (44:33). Genesis promotes Judah by giving him errands, not speeches — and it will end by giving him the sceptre.

Joseph harnesses his own chariot. The second man in Egypt, who has people for this, does it himself and drives out to meet an old shepherd. And then a striking asymmetry: "he fell on his neck and wept on his neck a long while." The weeping is all Joseph's — Jacob is not said to weep at all, here or anywhere in the reunion. ⚠️ A famous rabbinic reading fills the silence by saying Jacob was reciting the Shema; that is commentary, not text. What the text gives is one man sobbing on another man's neck and the other man saying nothing until verse 30. The KJV has "wept on his neck a good while," which catches the duration; the Hebrew's final word is simply od, "still, yet, more" — he kept on.

"Let me die this time." Amutah ha-pa'am — awkward in English and worth keeping awkward. Not "now I can die happy"; something closer to this time I can die, as against all the times he said he would go down to Sheol mourning (37:35, 42:38, 44:31). He is not asking to die. He is saying the thing that made death unbearable has been removed. KJV "Now let me die, since I have seen thy face"; the NWT "Now I am ready to die," which is the sense, at the cost of the strangeness.

Verses 31–34 · the coaching, and the usefulness of being despised

Joseph writes their script. He tells them exactly what to say and exactly which word to lead with: not "shepherds" but "men of livestock" — anshei mikneh, a phrase with more standing to it, the language of owners rather than hired hands — while he himself uses the blunter "shepherds of flocks" when briefing them. He is a court operator managing an audience, and Genesis lets you watch him do it.

The strategy is the opposite of assimilation. Read the logic of verse 34 slowly: say you are herdsmen in order that you may live in Goshen, because every shepherd is an abomination to Egypt. He is deliberately leading with the fact that will disgust his hosts, so that they will be housed at arm's length on the frontier instead of absorbed into the centre of Egyptian life. The thing that makes his family despised is the thing that will keep them a family. ⚠️ Whether that separation is a mercy or the seedbed of Exodus 1's "there arose a new king who did not know Joseph" is a question this chapter opens and does not answer.

Was Egypt really revolted by shepherds? ⚠️ Honest answer: not in any blanket way we can document. Egyptians kept enormous herds themselves, and tomb art shows herdsmen everywhere. Proposals for what lies behind the line include class contempt for a low-status trade, an ethnic distaste for Asiatic pastoralists drifting in from the east, and — most often repeated, least supported — a memory of the Hyksos. No Egyptian text says it. The narrator states it as a plain fact about the country his readers are being told about, and it is doing narrative work regardless of what stands behind it. KJV "for every shepherd is an abomination unto the Egyptians"; the NWT "detestable thing," which is what to'evah weighs.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "Do not fear to go down to Egypt" kept in the same words as the prohibition to Isaac at 26:2, so the licence is visibly a reversal; "I myself… and I myself" for the doubled anokhi; "surely bring you up" for the doubled aloh, the Exodus verb; "souls" for nefesh throughout the census; "Let me die this time" for amutah ha-pa'am, left as strange as it is; and "abomination" for to'evah, unsoftened.

Echoes paid. Hineni — Abraham's word at the binding and Joseph's at 37:13 — now Jacob's; the prohibition to Isaac lifted at the same border; the wagons that persuaded him, now carrying him; Er and Onan and Dinah both refusing to disappear from the family record; and "let me go and see him before I die" answered in verse 30.

Echoes planted. "These are the names of the sons of Israel who came to Egypt" — the sentence Exodus opens by repeating, with the nation grown; "I will surely bring you up," the promise the whole exodus discharges; Goshen chosen precisely because it is the far edge; and the separation strategy of verse 34, which keeps them a people and eventually marks them as a target.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 47 — five brothers stand in front of the most powerful man on earth and say they are shepherds, and Jacob, asked his age, answers that his years have been "few and evil." Then the famine grinds on, and Joseph buys Egypt — its silver, its livestock, its land, and finally its people — for Pharaoh.

Genesis 47 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיָּבֹא יוֹסֵף, וַיַּגֵּד לְפַרְעֹה, וַיֹּאמֶר אָבִי וְאַחַי וְצֹאנָם וּבְקָרָם וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר לָהֶם, בָּאוּ מֵאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן; וְהִנָּם, בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן.
And Joseph came and told Pharaoh, and said, "My father and my brothers, and their flocks and their herds and all that is theirs, have come from the land of Canaan; and look — they are in the land of Goshen."note
2
וּמִקְצֵה אֶחָיו, לָקַח חֲמִשָּׁה אֲנָשִׁים; וַיַּצִּגֵם, לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה.
And from among his brothers he took five men and presented them before Pharaoh.note
3
וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה אֶל-אֶחָיו, מַה-מַּעֲשֵׂיכֶם; וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֶל-פַּרְעֹה, רֹעֵה צֹאן עֲבָדֶיךָ--גַּם-אֲנַחְנוּ, גַּם-אֲבוֹתֵינוּ.
And Pharaoh said to his brothers, "What is your occupation?" And they said to Pharaoh, "Your servants are shepherds of flocks, both we and our fathers."note
4
וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֶל-פַּרְעֹה, לָגוּר בָּאָרֶץ בָּאנוּ, כִּי-אֵין מִרְעֶה לַצֹּאן אֲשֶׁר לַעֲבָדֶיךָ, כִּי-כָבֵד הָרָעָב בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן; וְעַתָּה יֵשְׁבוּ-נָא עֲבָדֶיךָ, בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן.
And they said to Pharaoh, "We have come to sojourn in the land, for there is no pasture for the flocks that belong to your servants, for the famine is heavy in the land of Canaan; and now, please let your servants dwell in the land of Goshen."note
5
וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה, אֶל-יוֹסֵף לֵאמֹר: אָבִיךָ וְאַחֶיךָ, בָּאוּ אֵלֶיךָ.
And Pharaoh said to Joseph, saying, "Your father and your brothers have come to you.note
6
אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, לְפָנֶיךָ הִוא--בְּמֵיטַב הָאָרֶץ, הוֹשֵׁב אֶת-אָבִיךָ וְאֶת-אַחֶיךָ: יֵשְׁבוּ, בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן--וְאִם-יָדַעְתָּ וְיֶשׁ-בָּם אַנְשֵׁי-חַיִל, וְשַׂמְתָּם שָׂרֵי מִקְנֶה עַל-אֲשֶׁר-לִי.
The land of Egypt is before you; in the best of the land settle your father and your brothers — let them dwell in the land of Goshen. And if you know that there are capable men among them, then set them as chiefs of livestock over what is mine."note
7
וַיָּבֵא יוֹסֵף אֶת-יַעֲקֹב אָבִיו, וַיַּעֲמִדֵהוּ לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה; וַיְבָרֶךְ יַעֲקֹב, אֶת-פַּרְעֹה.
And Joseph brought in Jacob his father and stood him before Pharaoh; and Jacob blessed Pharaoh.note
8
וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה, אֶל-יַעֲקֹב: כַּמָּה, יְמֵי שְׁנֵי חַיֶּיךָ.
And Pharaoh said to Jacob, "How many are the days of the years of your life?"note
9
וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב, אֶל-פַּרְעֹה, יְמֵי שְׁנֵי מְגוּרַי, שְׁלֹשִׁים וּמְאַת שָׁנָה: מְעַט וְרָעִים, הָיוּ יְמֵי שְׁנֵי חַיַּי, וְלֹא הִשִּׂיגוּ אֶת-יְמֵי שְׁנֵי חַיֵּי אֲבֹתַי, בִּימֵי מְגוּרֵיהֶם.
And Jacob said to Pharaoh, "The days of the years of my sojournings are a hundred and thirty years. Few and evil have been the days of the years of my life, and they have not reached the days of the years of the lives of my fathers in the days of their sojournings."note
10
וַיְבָרֶךְ יַעֲקֹב, אֶת-פַּרְעֹה; וַיֵּצֵא, מִלִּפְנֵי פַרְעֹה.
And Jacob blessed Pharaoh, and went out from before Pharaoh.note
11
וַיּוֹשֵׁב יוֹסֵף, אֶת-אָבִיו וְאֶת-אֶחָיו, וַיִּתֵּן לָהֶם אֲחֻזָּה בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, בְּמֵיטַב הָאָרֶץ בְּאֶרֶץ רַעְמְסֵס--כַּאֲשֶׁר, צִוָּה פַרְעֹה.
And Joseph settled his father and his brothers, and gave them a holding in the land of Egypt, in the best of the land, in the land of Rameses, as Pharaoh had commanded.note
12
וַיְכַלְכֵּל יוֹסֵף אֶת-אָבִיו וְאֶת-אֶחָיו, וְאֵת כָּל-בֵּית אָבִיו--לֶחֶם, לְפִי הַטָּף.
And Joseph provided his father and his brothers and all his father's house with bread, according to the little ones.note
13
וְלֶחֶם אֵין בְּכָל-הָאָרֶץ, כִּי-כָבֵד הָרָעָב מְאֹד; וַתֵּלַהּ אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, וְאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן, מִפְּנֵי, הָרָעָב.
And there was no bread in all the land, for the famine was very heavy; and the land of Egypt and the land of Canaan languished because of the famine.note
14
וַיְלַקֵּט יוֹסֵף, אֶת-כָּל-הַכֶּסֶף הַנִּמְצָא בְאֶרֶץ-מִצְרַיִם וּבְאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן, בַּשֶּׁבֶר, אֲשֶׁר-הֵם שֹׁבְרִים; וַיָּבֵא יוֹסֵף אֶת-הַכֶּסֶף, בֵּיתָה פַרְעֹה.
And Joseph gathered all the silver that was found in the land of Egypt and in the land of Canaan, for the grain they were buying; and Joseph brought the silver into Pharaoh's house.note
15
וַיִּתֹּם הַכֶּסֶף, מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם וּמֵאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן, וַיָּבֹאוּ כָל-מִצְרַיִם אֶל-יוֹסֵף לֵאמֹר הָבָה-לָּנוּ לֶחֶם, וְלָמָּה נָמוּת נֶגְדֶּךָ: כִּי אָפֵס, כָּסֶף.
And the silver from the land of Egypt and from the land of Canaan came to an end, and all Egypt came to Joseph, saying, "Give us bread! And why should we die in front of you? — for the silver is gone."note
16
וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף הָבוּ מִקְנֵיכֶם, וְאֶתְּנָה לָכֶם בְּמִקְנֵיכֶם--אִם-אָפֵס, כָּסֶף.
And Joseph said, "Give your livestock, and I will give you for your livestock, if the silver is gone."note
17
וַיָּבִיאוּ אֶת-מִקְנֵיהֶם, אֶל-יוֹסֵף, וַיִּתֵּן לָהֶם יוֹסֵף לֶחֶם בַּסּוּסִים וּבְמִקְנֵה הַצֹּאן וּבְמִקְנֵה הַבָּקָר, וּבַחֲמֹרִים; וַיְנַהֲלֵם בַּלֶּחֶם בְּכָל-מִקְנֵהֶם, בַּשָּׁנָה הַהִוא.
And they brought their livestock to Joseph, and Joseph gave them bread for the horses and for the livestock of the flocks and for the livestock of the herds and for the donkeys; and he led them along with bread that year, for all their livestock.note
18
וַתִּתֹּם, הַשָּׁנָה הַהִוא, וַיָּבֹאוּ אֵלָיו בַּשָּׁנָה הַשֵּׁנִית וַיֹּאמְרוּ לוֹ לֹא-נְכַחֵד מֵאֲדֹנִי, כִּי אִם-תַּם הַכֶּסֶף וּמִקְנֵה הַבְּהֵמָה אֶל-אֲדֹנִי: לֹא נִשְׁאַר לִפְנֵי אֲדֹנִי, בִּלְתִּי אִם-גְּוִיָּתֵנוּ וְאַדְמָתֵנוּ.
And that year came to an end, and they came to him in the second year and said to him, "We will not hide from my lord that the silver is spent, and the herds of cattle are my lord's; there is nothing left before my lord but our bodies and our ground.note
19
לָמָּה נָמוּת לְעֵינֶיךָ, גַּם-אֲנַחְנוּ גַּם אַדְמָתֵנוּ--קְנֵה-אֹתָנוּ וְאֶת-אַדְמָתֵנוּ, בַּלָּחֶם; וְנִהְיֶה אֲנַחְנוּ וְאַדְמָתֵנוּ, עֲבָדִים לְפַרְעֹה, וְתֶן-זֶרַע וְנִחְיֶה וְלֹא נָמוּת, וְהָאֲדָמָה לֹא תֵשָׁם.
Why should we die before your eyes, both we and our ground? Buy us and our ground for bread, and we and our ground will be servants to Pharaoh; and give seed, that we may live and not die, and that the ground not become desolate."note
20
וַיִּקֶן יוֹסֵף אֶת-כָּל-אַדְמַת מִצְרַיִם, לְפַרְעֹה, כִּי-מָכְרוּ מִצְרַיִם אִישׁ שָׂדֵהוּ, כִּי-חָזַק עֲלֵהֶם הָרָעָב; וַתְּהִי הָאָרֶץ, לְפַרְעֹה.
And Joseph bought all the ground of Egypt for Pharaoh, for every Egyptian sold his field, because the famine was strong upon them; and the land became Pharaoh's.note
21
וְאֶת-הָעָם--הֶעֱבִיר אֹתוֹ, לֶעָרִים: מִקְצֵה גְבוּל-מִצְרַיִם, וְעַד-קָצֵהוּ.
And the people — he moved them into the cities, from one end of the border of Egypt to its other end.note
22
רַק אַדְמַת הַכֹּהֲנִים, לֹא קָנָה: כִּי חֹק לַכֹּהֲנִים מֵאֵת פַּרְעֹה, וְאָכְלוּ אֶת-חֻקָּם אֲשֶׁר נָתַן לָהֶם פַּרְעֹה--עַל-כֵּן, לֹא מָכְרוּ אֶת-אַדְמָתָם.
Only the ground of the priests he did not buy, for the priests had a fixed allowance from Pharaoh, and they ate their allowance that Pharaoh gave them; therefore they did not sell their ground.note
23
וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל-הָעָם, הֵן קָנִיתִי אֶתְכֶם הַיּוֹם וְאֶת-אַדְמַתְכֶם לְפַרְעֹה; הֵא-לָכֶם זֶרַע, וּזְרַעְתֶּם אֶת-הָאֲדָמָה.
And Joseph said to the people, "Look — I have bought you today, and your ground, for Pharaoh. Here is seed for you, and you shall sow the ground.note
24
וְהָיָה, בַּתְּבוּאֹת, וּנְתַתֶּם חֲמִישִׁית, לְפַרְעֹה; וְאַרְבַּע הַיָּדֹת יִהְיֶה לָכֶם לְזֶרַע הַשָּׂדֶה וּלְאָכְלְכֶם, וְלַאֲשֶׁר בְּבָתֵּיכֶם--וְלֶאֱכֹל לְטַפְּכֶם.
And it shall be at the harvests that you shall give a fifth to Pharaoh, and four of the parts shall be yours, for seed of the field and for your food, and for those in your houses, and for your little ones to eat."note
25
וַיֹּאמְרוּ, הֶחֱיִתָנוּ; נִמְצָא-חֵן בְּעֵינֵי אֲדֹנִי, וְהָיִינוּ עֲבָדִים לְפַרְעֹה.
And they said, "You have kept us alive! Let us find favour in the eyes of my lord, and we will be servants to Pharaoh."note
26
וַיָּשֶׂם אֹתָהּ יוֹסֵף לְחֹק עַד-הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה עַל-אַדְמַת מִצְרַיִם, לְפַרְעֹה--לַחֹמֶשׁ: רַק אַדְמַת הַכֹּהֲנִים, לְבַדָּם--לֹא הָיְתָה, לְפַרְעֹה.
And Joseph made it a statute to this day over the ground of Egypt — the fifth for Pharaoh. Only the ground of the priests alone did not become Pharaoh's.note
27
וַיֵּשֶׁב יִשְׂרָאֵל בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן; וַיֵּאָחֲזוּ בָהּ, וַיִּפְרוּ וַיִּרְבּוּ מְאֹד.
And Israel dwelt in the land of Egypt, in the land of Goshen; and they took holdings in it, and were fruitful and multiplied greatly.note
28
וַיְחִי יַעֲקֹב בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, שְׁבַע עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה; וַיְהִי יְמֵי-יַעֲקֹב, שְׁנֵי חַיָּיו--שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים, וְאַרְבָּעִים וּמְאַת שָׁנָה.
And Jacob lived in the land of Egypt seventeen years; and the days of Jacob, the years of his life, were a hundred and forty-seven years.note
29
וַיִּקְרְבוּ יְמֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל, לָמוּת, וַיִּקְרָא לִבְנוֹ לְיוֹסֵף וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ אִם-נָא מָצָאתִי חֵן בְּעֵינֶיךָ, שִׂים-נָא יָדְךָ תַּחַת יְרֵכִי; וְעָשִׂיתָ עִמָּדִי חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת, אַל-נָא תִקְבְּרֵנִי בְּמִצְרָיִם.
And the days of Israel drew near to die, and he called for his son Joseph and said to him, "If now I have found favour in your eyes, put your hand, please, under my thigh, and deal with me in kindness and truth — do not, please, bury me in Egypt.note
30
וְשָׁכַבְתִּי, עִם-אֲבֹתַי, וּנְשָׂאתַנִי מִמִּצְרַיִם, וּקְבַרְתַּנִי בִּקְבֻרָתָם; וַיֹּאמַר, אָנֹכִי אֶעֱשֶׂה כִדְבָרֶךָ.
And I will lie down with my fathers, and you shall carry me out of Egypt and bury me in their burial place." And he said, "I myself will do according to your word."note
31
וַיֹּאמֶר, הִשָּׁבְעָה לִי--וַיִּשָּׁבַע, לוֹ; וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ יִשְׂרָאֵל, עַל-רֹאשׁ הַמִּטָּה.{פ}
And he said, "Swear to me." And he swore to him. And Israel bowed himself at the head of the bed.note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–12 · an old shepherd blesses the king of Egypt

The script works, and Pharaoh improves on it. They say exactly what Joseph told them to say (46:34) — shepherds, both we and our fathers — and it lands: Goshen is granted. Then Pharaoh goes further than asked and offers to put the capable ones over the royal herds, which is the opposite of the arm's-length settlement Joseph engineered. Genesis does not say whether any of them took the job.

Jacob blesses Pharaoh. Twice. This is the strangest thing in the chapter and it is easy to skate over. A landless herdsman, brought in off the frontier, walks into the presence of a man his own culture regarded as a living god — and blesses him, on the way in and on the way out, framing the entire audience. He does not petition. He does not flatter. The only thing he is recorded as giving Pharaoh is a blessing, which is the one currency the throne cannot issue for itself. The letter to the Hebrews later builds an argument on exactly this asymmetry ("the lesser is blessed by the greater," 7:7); Genesis simply reports it twice and lets the shape speak.

"Few and evil." ⚠️ He is a hundred and thirty years old and he calls it few — measured against Abraham's hundred and seventy-five and Isaac's hundred and eighty, which he says outright he has not reached. And ra'im, "evil, hard, bad," is a startling word to hand a king who politely asked your age. It is also, on the evidence, accurate: a stolen blessing, twenty years under Laban, a night of wrestling, Dinah, Rachel dead on the road to Bethlehem, and twenty-two years believing Joseph was torn to pieces. KJV "few and evil have the days of the years of my life been"; ASV the same; the NIV softens to "few and difficult," which is kinder than the Hebrew is.

"Sojournings," not "life." Pharaoh asks about the days of his life; Jacob answers about the days of his megurim — his residing-as-an-alien, the word for a resident foreigner with no title to the ground under him. He applies it to his fathers too. Standing in the richest country on earth, having just been handed the best of its land, he describes three generations as people who never owned anywhere. It is the theological centre of the patriarchal story said in one word to a man who owns everything he can see.

⚠️ "The land of Rameses." Here is a genuine difficulty and it should not be smoothed over. The Egyptian city Pi-Ramesses takes its name from Ramesses II, who reigned in the thirteenth century BC — centuries after any date proposed for Joseph. Two honest options. (1) It is an editorial updating of a place name, so readers could find it on their own map: the same book calls a town "Dan" (14:14) long before the tribe of Dan existed to give it that name, so the practice is demonstrably at work inside Genesis itself. (2) The name is original and our Egyptology is incomplete — much the weaker case, since the royal name is well attested and late. ⚠️ It should also be said plainly that this verse, together with the store-city "Raamses" of Exodus 1:11, is one of the strongest arguments for dating the exodus to the thirteenth century — which is not the chronology this project follows elsewhere (see the note on Joseph's pharaoh). The updated-place-name reading is what reconciles them, and it is a reading, not a proof.

Verses 13–26 · how Joseph bought Egypt

Read the sequence for what it is. In four stages, a famine transfers an entire country to the crown. First the silver — all of it, from two countries, into Pharaoh's house. Then the livestock, when the silver runs out. Then, in the second year, the people themselves propose the last stage, because there is nothing else left: "buy us and our ground." Joseph does. He acquires the land, relocates the population, and imposes a permanent twenty-percent levy on every harvest. ⚠️ It is worth being clear-eyed about the mechanism: the grain he is selling back to them is the grain his own administration took from them in the seven good years (41:34). They are buying back their own surplus, and paying for it with everything they own.

Genesis does not tell you what to think about this. There is no word of praise and no word of blame — the same flat reportorial voice used for the sale of Joseph himself. Readers have gone both ways for two thousand years. It is a competent famine administration that keeps a nation alive when the alternative is mass starvation; it is also the invention of a serf state, carried out by a foreigner, in a book whose next act is Egypt enslaving his descendants. Both readings survive contact with the text. Neither is imposed by it.

"You have kept us alive." Hecheyitanu — the same root as the michyah, "to preserve life," that Joseph used to explain his whole career to his brothers (45:5). Now the sentence is in the mouths of people who have just sold themselves into servitude, and they say it gratefully. Preservation and subjugation come out of the same policy and are named by the same word. Genesis places the two uses two chapters apart and offers no comment.

⚠️ Verse 21 has a real textual variant, and it changes the verse. The Masoretic Hebrew reads he'evir otam le-arim, "he moved them to the cities" — a population transfer, perhaps to put people near the granaries. The Septuagint and the Samaritan Pentateuch read he'evid otam la-avadim, "he made them servants" — which differs by a single letter (resh ר and dalet ד are all but identical in Hebrew script) and turns the line into an explicit statement of enslavement. This translation follows the Masoretic Text, as throughout, and flags the alternative rather than choosing the more dramatic reading. Modern versions divide: KJV and ASV keep "removed them to cities"; the ESV and NIV follow the Greek and Samaritan with "made servants of them" / "reduced the people to servitude." Note that verses 19, 23 and 25 already say plainly that they became Pharaoh's servants, so the variant sharpens the point rather than creating it.

The priests keep their land. Verse 22 is not incidental. The one class exempted from the whole operation is the Egyptian priesthood, who hold their fields because the crown pays them a fixed allowance. ⚠️ Joseph is married to the daughter of a priest of On (41:45). Genesis records the exemption and the marriage and never connects them. Whether the reader should is left entirely open.

The twenty percent is real economic history, or close to it. A crown that owns the land and takes a fixed share of the harvest, with a tax-exempt temple estate alongside it, is a recognisable description of later Egyptian agriculture. "To this day" tells you the narrator is explaining to his readers why the country they know is organised the way it is — the same kind of aside that explains a place name or a custom elsewhere in Genesis.

Verses 27–28 · one sentence that changes key

Watch the sentence immediately after Egypt is enslaved. "And Israel dwelt in the land of Egypt… and they took holdings in it, and were fruitful and multiplied greatly." Vayifru vayirbu — the creation blessing (1:28), and word for word the opening of Exodus 1:7, where the same verbs describe a people so numerous the land is filled with them and a new king becomes afraid. In a single verse the seventy who came down begin to become a nation, on the very ground where everyone else has just lost theirs. ⚠️ And vayye'achazu, "they took holdings," is from the root behind achuzzah — a permanent possession, the word used of the family's one purchased grave in Canaan (23:9). They are putting down roots in the wrong country. The next two verses are an old man making very sure his own body does not stay there.

Seventeen years. Jacob raised Joseph for seventeen years before losing him (37:2), and now Joseph keeps his father for seventeen years. The book does not point at the symmetry. It just gives the same number twice.

Verses 29–31 · the oath, and a famous ambiguity

"Put your hand under my thigh." The same oath Abraham required of his servant before sending him for a wife (24:2), and both times it concerns the future of the line rather than an ordinary promise. The gesture is generally understood as an oath sworn by the organs of generation — by the covenant sign and the descendants who carry it — which is why it appears exactly twice in the Bible and both times for the survival of the family's identity. This translation keeps it literal and lets the reader meet it as ancient readers did.

"Kindness and truth." Chesed ve-emet — steadfast loyalty joined to reliability, the pair used of covenant faithfulness itself. Jacob is not asking a favour; he is invoking the standard by which the family is supposed to treat its own. And the thing he wants is startlingly specific: not to be buried in the richest country on earth, in the province he has just been given, but carried out and laid in a cave in a land his family owns almost none of.

Why it matters that he insists. He has spent seventeen comfortable years in Goshen, and his last recorded act is to make his most powerful son swear an oath that the family's future is not here. It is the practical form of the promise he was given at Beersheba — "I will surely bring you up again." Joseph will make the same request of his own brothers at the end of the book, and the bones will wait four hundred years.

⚠️ "At the head of the bed" — or "on the top of his staff." One of the best-known variants in Genesis, and it comes down to vowels. Written without them, המטה can be read ha-mittah, "the bed," or ha-matteh, "the staff." The Masoretes pointed it as bed, which this translation follows; the Septuagint read staff, and that is the reading quoted in Hebrews 11:21 — "leaning on the top of his staff." Neither is a mistake; they are the same consonants read two ways, and the New Testament is quoting the Greek Bible its readers used. KJV "bowed himself upon the bed's head"; NWT "bowed down over the head of his bed." ⚠️ A further open question: to whom does he bow? The text does not say. Toward God in thanks is the traditional reading; toward Joseph is grammatically available and would quietly complete the dream of 37:10, the one Jacob rebuked his son for — "shall I and your mother indeed come to bow down to you?" Genesis leaves the object of the verb out, and this translation leaves it out too.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "sojournings" for megurim, so Jacob's answer visibly does not answer Pharaoh's question; "few and evil" for me'at ve-ra'im, unsoftened; "the fifth" for chomesh; "bodies" for geviyah in verse 18, which is what the people call themselves once the livestock is gone; "You have kept us alive" sharing the root of michyah at 45:5; "kindness and truth" for chesed ve-emet; and "he moved them into the cities" following the Masoretic consonants, with the Greek and Samaritan "made them servants" in the note rather than in the line.

Echoes paid. The coached script delivered and accepted; the fifth taken in the good years now sold back and made permanent; "God sent me… to preserve life" answered by a nation saying "you have kept us alive" as it sells itself; Abraham's oath under the thigh; and "I will surely bring you up again" turned into a sworn instruction.

Echoes planted. "Fruitful and multiplied greatly," the exact verbs Exodus 1:7 opens with, and the fear that follows them; the bones that must leave Egypt, which Joseph will ask for himself at the end of the book and which wait four centuries; a country where the crown owns the land and everyone works it for a fifth, which is the state the Israelites will eventually be building store cities for; and the slow inversion Genesis never comments on — the Hebrew who made Egypt Pharaoh's servants, and the Egypt that makes his descendants slaves.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 48 — Jacob adopts Joseph's two Egyptian sons as his own, and then crosses his hands. Joseph tries to correct him and is refused: "I know, my son, I know." The younger brother takes the blessing again, for the fourth generation running.

Genesis 48 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיְהִי, אַחֲרֵי הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה, וַיֹּאמֶר לְיוֹסֵף, הִנֵּה אָבִיךָ חֹלֶה; וַיִּקַּח אֶת-שְׁנֵי בָנָיו, עִמּוֹ--אֶת-מְנַשֶּׁה, וְאֶת-אֶפְרָיִם.
And it happened after these things that someone said to Joseph, "Look — your father is ill." And he took his two sons with him, Manasseh and Ephraim.note
2
וַיַּגֵּד לְיַעֲקֹב--וַיֹּאמֶר, הִנֵּה בִּנְךָ יוֹסֵף בָּא אֵלֶיךָ; וַיִּתְחַזֵּק, יִשְׂרָאֵל, וַיֵּשֶׁב, עַל-הַמִּטָּה.
And it was told to Jacob, "Look — your son Joseph has come to you." And Israel gathered his strength and sat up on the bed.note
3
וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֶל-יוֹסֵף, אֵל שַׁדַּי נִרְאָה-אֵלַי בְּלוּז בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן; וַיְבָרֶךְ, אֹתִי.
And Jacob said to Joseph, "El Shaddai appeared to me at Luz in the land of Canaan, and blessed me,note
4
וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלַי, הִנְנִי מַפְרְךָ וְהִרְבִּיתִךָ, וּנְתַתִּיךָ, לִקְהַל עַמִּים; וְנָתַתִּי אֶת-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת, לְזַרְעֲךָ אַחֲרֶיךָ--אֲחֻזַּת עוֹלָם.
and said to me, 'Look — I am making you fruitful and multiplying you, and I will make you into an assembly of peoples; and I will give this land to your offspring after you as an everlasting holding.'note
5
וְעַתָּה שְׁנֵי-בָנֶיךָ הַנּוֹלָדִים לְךָ בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם, עַד-בֹּאִי אֵלֶיךָ מִצְרַיְמָה--לִי-הֵם: אֶפְרַיִם, וּמְנַשֶּׁה--כִּרְאוּבֵן וְשִׁמְעוֹן, יִהְיוּ-לִי.
And now, your two sons who were born to you in the land of Egypt before I came to you in Egypt — they are mine. Ephraim and Manasseh, like Reuben and Simeon, shall be mine.note
6
וּמוֹלַדְתְּךָ אֲשֶׁר-הוֹלַדְתָּ אַחֲרֵיהֶם, לְךָ יִהְיוּ; עַל שֵׁם אֲחֵיהֶם יִקָּרְאוּ, בְּנַחֲלָתָם.
And your offspring whom you father after them shall be yours; they shall be called by the name of their brothers in their inheritance.note
7
וַאֲנִי בְּבֹאִי מִפַּדָּן, מֵתָה עָלַי רָחֵל בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן בַּדֶּרֶךְ, בְּעוֹד כִּבְרַת-אֶרֶץ, לָבֹא אֶפְרָתָה; וָאֶקְבְּרֶהָ שָּׁם בְּדֶרֶךְ אֶפְרָת, הִוא בֵּית לָחֶם.
And as for me — when I came from Paddan, Rachel died on me in the land of Canaan, on the way, when there was still some distance to go to Ephrath; and I buried her there on the road to Ephrath, that is, Bethlehem."note
8
וַיַּרְא יִשְׂרָאֵל, אֶת-בְּנֵי יוֹסֵף; וַיֹּאמֶר, מִי-אֵלֶּה.
And Israel saw Joseph's sons, and said, "Who are these?"note
9
וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף, אֶל-אָבִיו, בָּנַי הֵם, אֲשֶׁר-נָתַן-לִי אֱלֹהִים בָּזֶה; וַיֹּאמַר, קָחֶם-נָא אֵלַי וַאֲבָרְכֵם.
And Joseph said to his father, "They are my sons, whom God has given me here." And he said, "Take them, please, to me, and I will bless them."note
10
וְעֵינֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל כָּבְדוּ מִזֹּקֶן, לֹא יוּכַל לִרְאוֹת; וַיַּגֵּשׁ אֹתָם אֵלָיו, וַיִּשַּׁק לָהֶם וַיְחַבֵּק לָהֶם.
Now Israel's eyes were heavy from age; he could not see. And he brought them near to him, and he kissed them and embraced them.note
11
וַיֹּאמֶר יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל-יוֹסֵף, רְאֹה פָנֶיךָ לֹא פִלָּלְתִּי; וְהִנֵּה הֶרְאָה אֹתִי אֱלֹהִים, גַּם אֶת-זַרְעֶךָ.
And Israel said to Joseph, "I had not expected to see your face — and look, God has shown me your offspring as well."note
12
וַיּוֹצֵא יוֹסֵף אֹתָם, מֵעִם בִּרְכָּיו; וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ לְאַפָּיו, אָרְצָה.
And Joseph brought them out from between his knees, and bowed with his face to the ground.note
13
וַיִּקַּח יוֹסֵף, אֶת-שְׁנֵיהֶם--אֶת-אֶפְרַיִם בִּימִינוֹ מִשְּׂמֹאל יִשְׂרָאֵל, וְאֶת-מְנַשֶּׁה בִשְׂמֹאלוֹ מִימִין יִשְׂרָאֵל; וַיַּגֵּשׁ, אֵלָיו.
And Joseph took the two of them — Ephraim in his right hand, toward Israel's left, and Manasseh in his left hand, toward Israel's right — and brought them near to him.note
14
וַיִּשְׁלַח יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת-יְמִינוֹ וַיָּשֶׁת עַל-רֹאשׁ אֶפְרַיִם, וְהוּא הַצָּעִיר, וְאֶת-שְׂמֹאלוֹ, עַל-רֹאשׁ מְנַשֶּׁה: שִׂכֵּל, אֶת-יָדָיו, כִּי מְנַשֶּׁה, הַבְּכוֹר.
And Israel stretched out his right hand and laid it on Ephraim's head — and he was the younger — and his left hand on Manasseh's head: he crossed his hands knowingly, for Manasseh was the firstborn.note
15
וַיְבָרֶךְ אֶת-יוֹסֵף, וַיֹּאמַר: הָאֱלֹהִים אֲשֶׁר הִתְהַלְּכוּ אֲבֹתַי לְפָנָיו, אַבְרָהָם וְיִצְחָק--הָאֱלֹהִים הָרֹעֶה אֹתִי, מֵעוֹדִי עַד-הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה.
And he blessed Joseph and said: "The God before whom my fathers walked, Abraham and Isaac — the God who has shepherded me all my life until this day,note
16
הַמַּלְאָךְ הַגֹּאֵל אֹתִי מִכָּל-רָע, יְבָרֵךְ אֶת-הַנְּעָרִים, וְיִקָּרֵא בָהֶם שְׁמִי, וְשֵׁם אֲבֹתַי אַבְרָהָם וְיִצְחָק; וְיִדְגּוּ לָרֹב, בְּקֶרֶב הָאָרֶץ.
the angel who has redeemed me from all evil — may he bless the boys; and let my name be called on them, and the name of my fathers Abraham and Isaac; and let them teem into a multitude in the midst of the earth."note
17
וַיַּרְא יוֹסֵף, כִּי-יָשִׁית אָבִיו יַד-יְמִינוֹ עַל-רֹאשׁ אֶפְרַיִם--וַיֵּרַע בְּעֵינָיו; וַיִּתְמֹךְ יַד-אָבִיו, לְהָסִיר אֹתָהּ מֵעַל רֹאשׁ-אֶפְרַיִם--עַל-רֹאשׁ מְנַשֶּׁה.
And Joseph saw that his father was laying his right hand on Ephraim's head, and it was evil in his eyes; and he took hold of his father's hand to move it from Ephraim's head to Manasseh's head.note
18
וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל-אָבִיו, לֹא-כֵן אָבִי: כִּי-זֶה הַבְּכֹר, שִׂים יְמִינְךָ עַל-רֹאשׁוֹ.
And Joseph said to his father, "Not so, my father — for this one is the firstborn. Put your right hand on his head."note
19
וַיְמָאֵן אָבִיו, וַיֹּאמֶר יָדַעְתִּי בְנִי יָדַעְתִּי--גַּם-הוּא יִהְיֶה-לְּעָם, וְגַם-הוּא יִגְדָּל; וְאוּלָם, אָחִיו הַקָּטֹן יִגְדַּל מִמֶּנּוּ, וְזַרְעוֹ, יִהְיֶה מְלֹא-הַגּוֹיִם.
And his father refused, and said, "I know, my son, I know. He also will become a people, and he also will be great — but truly his younger brother will be greater than he, and his offspring will become the fullness of the nations."note
20
וַיְבָרְכֵם בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא, לֵאמוֹר, בְּךָ יְבָרֵךְ יִשְׂרָאֵל לֵאמֹר, יְשִׂמְךָ אֱלֹהִים כְּאֶפְרַיִם וְכִמְנַשֶּׁה; וַיָּשֶׂם אֶת-אֶפְרַיִם, לִפְנֵי מְנַשֶּׁה.
And he blessed them that day, saying, "By you Israel will bless, saying, 'God make you like Ephraim and like Manasseh.'" And he set Ephraim before Manasseh.note
21
וַיֹּאמֶר יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל-יוֹסֵף, הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי מֵת; וְהָיָה אֱלֹהִים, עִמָּכֶם, וְהֵשִׁיב אֶתְכֶם, אֶל-אֶרֶץ אֲבֹתֵיכֶם.
And Israel said to Joseph, "Look — I am dying; but God will be with you, and will bring you back to the land of your fathers.note
22
וַאֲנִי נָתַתִּי לְךָ, שְׁכֶם אַחַד--עַל-אַחֶיךָ: אֲשֶׁר לָקַחְתִּי מִיַּד הָאֱמֹרִי, בְּחַרְבִּי וּבְקַשְׁתִּי.{פ}
And I have given you one shoulder above your brothers, which I took from the hand of the Amorite with my sword and with my bow."note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–7 · a legal act, and an interruption about a grave

What verse 5 actually does. This is not sentiment; it is adoption, and it is the most consequential legal sentence in the last quarter of Genesis. "Ephraim and Manasseh, like Reuben and Simeon, shall be mine" — named against the first and second sons, which is the point. Two grandsons, born in Egypt to an Egyptian mother, are made Jacob's own sons, and therefore heads of tribes in their own right. Everything downstream follows from this: there is no tribe of Joseph in the land, there are the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh; Joseph receives, through them, the double portion that belongs to a firstborn — forfeited by Reuben at 35:22 and reassigned here, as 1 Chronicles 5:1-2 later spells out. Verse 6 closes the loop: any later sons stay Joseph's and inherit inside their brothers' allotments. It is drafted like a will because it is one.

He starts by quoting God. Before he adopts anyone he recites the promise made to him at Luz — Bethel — naming El Shaddai, "an assembly of peoples," and the land as an everlasting holding. The word is achuzzat olam, the same root as the "holdings" the family had just started taking in Goshen (47:27). He is quietly correcting the address. The permanent property is somewhere else.

⚠️ And then, with no transition, Rachel. "And as for me — when I came from Paddan, Rachel died on me…" It reads like an old man's mind slipping its track, and in a sense it is; but the association is not random. He has just taken Rachel's grandsons as his own sons, which is the nearest thing available to giving Rachel the children she died without having. And metah alai — "died on me" — is a construction of loss laid against the speaker, not the neutral "died." He is also, three verses after asking to be carried out of Egypt, remembering the one person he did not manage to bury at Machpelah, "when there was still some distance to go." Sixty years on he still measures the distance. Genesis gives no comment and no connective; it simply lets the sentence arrive where grief actually puts it.

Verses 8–16 · a blind man asks who is in the room

"Who are these?" ⚠️ The chapter has been careful to tell you, one verse later, that Israel's eyes were heavy with age and he could not see. If that arrangement feels familiar it should: the last time this book put a dying, blind father in a room to bless two brothers of unequal birth order, the father was Isaac, the question was "who are you?", and the man supplying the answer was Jacob, lying (27:18-24). Genesis has staged the scene again, with Jacob now in Isaac's chair. The difference is the whole point of the chapter, and it lands in verse 19.

"I had not expected to see your face." Lo pillalti — from a root about judging or reckoning; not "I hoped and it happened" but closer to I had not reckoned on it. He had written it off. And then the excess: not only the son but the son's children. Twice in three chapters the old man is handed more than he asked for.

"From between his knees." The boys are placed at their grandfather's knees and taken out from them, which is a recognised gesture of acknowledgement in the ancient world — a child received on or between the knees is a child claimed. It is the physical enactment of the legal sentence in verse 5.

"The angel who has redeemed me from all evil." ⚠️ This is the first appearance of GA'AL in the Bible, and it arrives in a family blessing. The go'el is not a religious abstraction: he is the nearest kinsman with the standing and the obligation to buy back what a relative has lost — land, freedom, a life. Jacob applies it to the messenger who saw him through everything from Esau to the famine, and puts him in apposition with "the God who has shepherded me." Whether "the angel" is being identified with God or distinguished from him is grammatically open, and readers have gone both ways for two thousand years; this translation keeps the apposition as loose as the Hebrew keeps it. The word goes on to carry Ruth's Boaz, the "kinsman-redeemer" laws of Leviticus 25, and a great deal of Isaiah. KJV "the Angel which redeemed me from all evil"; ASV the same; NWT "the angel who has been recovering me from all calamity."

"Let them teem." Ve-yidgu — a verb built on dag, "fish," and used nowhere else in the Bible of people. Multiply like fish: shoals of them, uncountable. The KJV's "let them grow into a multitude" is smooth and drops the image entirely; this keeps the swarming.

Verses 17–20 · the crossed hands, and "I know, my son, I know"

Why the right hand matters at all. The blessing is not merely words; it is words delivered through a hand, and which hand lands where is the content. The right hand carries the greater portion. Joseph, who is a competent administrator and has thought about the logistics, positions the boys so that the firstborn will meet his father's right hand automatically — Manasseh to Israel's right, Ephraim to his left. He has set the room up so that nothing can go wrong. Then his father crosses his arms.

The verb is the sentence. Sikkel et yadav. Every translation renders it "he crossed his hands," and that is certainly what happens — but the verb is sakal, whose whole family of meanings is about insight, prudence, acting with understanding. It is not the ordinary word for crossing anything. Hebrew has chosen, at the exact moment the hands go over each other, a word that means to do a thing advisedly. So the crossing and the knowing are the same act, in one word, and the narrator has told you the answer before Joseph asks the question. The KJV caught this and has never been bettered on it: "guiding his hands wittingly." ASV "guiding his hands wittingly"; the ESV "crossing his hands"; the NIV "crossing his arms" — accurate, and the deliberateness evaporates. This translation keeps both halves: he crossed his hands knowingly.

What Joseph does next is the most human thing in the chapter. "It was evil in his eyes" — the same phrase used of genuine wrong elsewhere — and he physically takes hold of his father's wrist to move it. He is not being pious about custom; he is watching his firstborn get demoted and reaching in to stop it. He is also, without knowing it, playing Esau's part in a scene his father wrote.

"I know, my son, I know." Yada'ti veni yada'ti — three words, doubled, refusing the correction without raising the voice. ⚠️ This is the hinge of the whole chapter, and it is a moral event, not a genealogical one. Four times now this book has put the younger ahead of the elder: Isaac over Ishmael, Jacob over Esau, Judah and Joseph over Reuben, and now Ephraim over Manasseh. The first three arrived through expulsion, a bought birthright, a stolen blessing, a rape, an incest. This one happens in daylight, with the father's eyes shut, announced in advance, over the loud objection of the most powerful man in Egypt — and nobody is deceived. The old man who put on his brother's clothes and lied to a blind father is now the blind father, and he refuses to be lied to or to lie. The pattern that has run through Genesis by fraud is finally performed openly, by the one man in the book who knows exactly what it costs.

Was he right? Historically, yes, and conspicuously. Ephraim became the dominant northern tribe — so dominant that the prophets simply call the whole northern kingdom "Ephraim." Manasseh was large and split by the Jordan, but it never led. Melo ha-goyim, "the fullness of the nations," is an odd and expansive phrase for a tribal allotment; KJV "a multitude of nations."

The blessing that outlived everyone in the room. Verse 20 is not a prediction but a formula: this is what Israelites will say when they bless a child. And it is still said — over sons, on Friday nights, in Jewish homes, in these words. ⚠️ It is worth noticing what it means that the standing blessing for a Jewish boy names these two: brothers born in Egypt to a foreign mother, raised entirely outside the land, who were adopted in and who — uniquely among the brothers in this book — never fought each other. Nothing in the text says that is why. It is simply the pair the tradition kept.

Verses 21–22 · "one shoulder above your brothers"

"God will be with you" — plural. The Hebrew switches from addressing Joseph alone to addressing all of them: God will be with you (pl.) and bring you (pl.) back. The dying man is not promising his favourite son a private future; he is handing on, in the last words he speaks to him, the same promise he was given at Beersheba (46:4) and then made Joseph swear an oath about (47:29-31). Egypt is an address, not a destination.

⚠️ "One shoulder above your brothers." Shekhem achad, and it is genuinely ambiguous in three directions at once. Shekhem means a shoulder, and by extension a ridge or slope of land — so "one portion" or "one mountain-slope." It is also, unavoidably, the name of the city Shechem, which sits in exactly the territory Ephraim and Manasseh will inherit and where Joseph's bones are eventually buried (Joshua 24:32). Translations split: KJV "one portion above thy brethren"; ASV the same with a margin note; the ESV "one mountain slope"; the NIV makes the pun explicit with "the ridge of land," footnoting Shechem. Keeping "one shoulder" holds all three open, which is where the Hebrew sits.

⚠️ "With my sword and with my bow" is a real problem, and it should be said out loud. Jacob never took anything from the Amorites by force. The only violence at Shechem in this book is chapter 34 — Simeon and Levi's massacre — which Jacob condemned at the time and will curse from his deathbed one chapter from now (49:5-7). And the only ground he is recorded as acquiring there, he bought (33:19). The explanations offered are: that he is claiming his sons' conquest as the family's by right if not by approval; that a lost tradition of a battle stands behind it; that "sword and bow" is figurative for what God secured for him; or that he means the purchase held only because the family could defend it. Genesis supplies none of them, and this translation supplies none either. It is one of the places where the book declines to reconcile itself.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "he crossed his hands knowingly" for sikkel, holding both the gesture and the deliberateness the verb's own root carries (the KJV's "wittingly" is the model); "redeemed" for ga'al, its first occurrence in the Bible, left as the kinsman-word it is; "let them teem" for ve-yidgu, keeping the fish; "died on me" for metah alai; "it was evil in his eyes" for Joseph's reaction, unsoftened; and "one shoulder" for shekhem achad, holding portion, slope and city together.

Echoes paid. Isaac's blindness restaged with Jacob in the chair — and this time nobody is deceived; the birthright Reuben forfeited reassigned as a double portion; Rachel's grave on the road to Ephrath remembered sixty years on, in the middle of a legal document; "I will surely bring you up again" handed on in the plural; and Joseph's own dreams of precedence answered by a father who overrules his choice of heirs to his face.

Echoes planted. Ephraim, whose name the prophets will use for the entire northern kingdom; the two tribes that replace Joseph on every later map; the go'el, who becomes Boaz, and the redemption laws of Leviticus 25, and much of Isaiah; Shechem, where Joseph's bones are finally buried (Joshua 24:32); and the blessing of verse 20, still spoken over children in these words.

Next installment in this book: Genesis 49 — Jacob calls all twelve sons to his bed and tells them "what will happen to you in the latter days." It is the longest poem in Genesis, and it is not gentle: Reuben is stripped of his rank in three lines, Simeon and Levi are cursed by name for what they did at Shechem, and Judah is handed the sceptre.

Genesis 49 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיִּקְרָא יַעֲקֹב, אֶל-בָּנָיו; וַיֹּאמֶר, הֵאָסְפוּ וְאַגִּידָה לָכֶם, אֵת אֲשֶׁר-יִקְרָא אֶתְכֶם, בְּאַחֲרִית הַיָּמִים.
And Jacob called to his sons and said, "Gather, and let me tell you what will happen to you in the latter days.note
2
הִקָּבְצוּ וְשִׁמְעוּ, בְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב; וְשִׁמְעוּ, אֶל-יִשְׂרָאֵל אֲבִיכֶם.
Assemble and hear, sons of Jacob;
and listen to Israel your father.note
3
רְאוּבֵן בְּכֹרִי אַתָּה, כֹּחִי וְרֵאשִׁית אוֹנִי--יֶתֶר שְׂאֵת, וְיֶתֶר עָז.
Reuben, you are my firstborn,
my might and the first of my strength,
excess of dignity and excess of power.note
4
פַּחַז כַּמַּיִם אַל-תּוֹתַר, כִּי עָלִיתָ מִשְׁכְּבֵי אָבִיךָ; אָז חִלַּלְתָּ, יְצוּעִי עָלָה.{פ}
Reckless as water — you shall not excel,
for you went up to your father's bed;
then you defiled it — he went up to my couch.note
5
שִׁמְעוֹן וְלֵוִי, אַחִים--כְּלֵי חָמָס, מְכֵרֹתֵיהֶם.
Simeon and Levi are brothers;
weapons of violence are their swords.note
6
בְּסֹדָם אַל-תָּבֹא נַפְשִׁי, בִּקְהָלָם אַל-תֵּחַד כְּבֹדִי: כִּי בְאַפָּם הָרְגוּ אִישׁ, וּבִרְצֹנָם עִקְּרוּ-שׁוֹר.
Into their council let my soul not come,
with their assembly let my honour not be joined;
for in their anger they killed a man,
and at their pleasure they hamstrung an ox.note
7
אָרוּר אַפָּם כִּי עָז, וְעֶבְרָתָם כִּי קָשָׁתָה; אֲחַלְּקֵם בְּיַעֲקֹב, וַאֲפִיצֵם בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל.{פ}
Cursed be their anger, for it is fierce,
and their fury, for it is cruel.
I will divide them in Jacob
and scatter them in Israel.note
8
יְהוּדָה, אַתָּה יוֹדוּךָ אַחֶיךָ--יָדְךָ, בְּעֹרֶף אֹיְבֶיךָ; יִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לְךָ, בְּנֵי אָבִיךָ.
Judah — you, your brothers shall praise you;
your hand on the neck of your enemies;
your father's sons shall bow down to you.note
9
גּוּר אַרְיֵה יְהוּדָה, מִטֶּרֶף בְּנִי עָלִיתָ; כָּרַע רָבַץ כְּאַרְיֵה וּכְלָבִיא, מִי יְקִימֶנּוּ.
A lion's whelp is Judah;
from the prey, my son, you have gone up.
He crouched, he lay down like a lion,
and like a lioness — who will rouse him?note
10
לֹא-יָסוּר שֵׁבֶט מִיהוּדָה, וּמְחֹקֵק מִבֵּין רַגְלָיו, עַד כִּי-יָבֹא שִׁילֹה, וְלוֹ יִקְּהַת עַמִּים.
The sceptre shall not depart from Judah,
nor the ruler's staff from between his feet,
until Shiloh comes
— and to him the obedience of peoples.note
11
אֹסְרִי לַגֶּפֶן עִירֹה, וְלַשֹּׂרֵקָה בְּנִי אֲתֹנוֹ; כִּבֵּס בַּיַּיִן לְבֻשׁוֹ, וּבְדַם-עֲנָבִים סוּתֹה.
Binding his foal to the vine,
and his donkey's colt to the choice vine,
he washed his clothing in wine,
and his garment in the blood of grapes.note
12
חַכְלִילִי עֵינַיִם, מִיָּיִן; וּלְבֶן-שִׁנַּיִם, מֵחָלָב.{פ}
Darker of eyes than wine,
and whiter of teeth than milk.note
13
זְבוּלֻן, לְחוֹף יַמִּים יִשְׁכֹּן; וְהוּא לְחוֹף אֳנִיֹּת, וְיַרְכָתוֹ עַל-צִידֹן.{פ}
Zebulun shall dwell at the shore of seas;
and he shall be a shore for ships,
and his flank toward Sidon.note
14
יִשָּׂשכָר, חֲמֹר גָּרֶם--רֹבֵץ, בֵּין הַמִּשְׁפְּתָיִם.
Issachar is a bony donkey,
crouching between the saddlebags.note
15
וַיַּרְא מְנֻחָה כִּי טוֹב, וְאֶת-הָאָרֶץ כִּי נָעֵמָה; וַיֵּט שִׁכְמוֹ לִסְבֹּל, וַיְהִי לְמַס-עֹבֵד.{ס}
And he saw that rest was good,
and that the land was pleasant;
and he bent his shoulder to bear,
and became a slave at forced labour.note
16
דָּן, יָדִין עַמּוֹ--כְּאַחַד, שִׁבְטֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל.
Dan shall judge his people,
as one of the tribes of Israel.note
17
יְהִי-דָן נָחָשׁ עֲלֵי-דֶרֶךְ, שְׁפִיפֹן עֲלֵי-אֹרַח--הַנֹּשֵׁךְ, עִקְּבֵי-סוּס, וַיִּפֹּל רֹכְבוֹ, אָחוֹר.
Let Dan be a serpent on the way,
a viper on the path,
biting the horse's heels
so that its rider falls backward.note
18
לִישׁוּעָתְךָ, קִוִּיתִי יְהוָה.{ס}
For your salvation I have waited, Jehovah.note
19
גָּד, גְּדוּד יְגוּדֶנּוּ; וְהוּא, יָגֻד עָקֵב.{ס}
Gad — a raiding band shall raid him,
but he shall raid at their heel.note
20
מֵאָשֵׁר, שְׁמֵנָה לַחְמוֹ; וְהוּא יִתֵּן, מַעֲדַנֵּי-מֶלֶךְ.{ס}
From Asher — his bread shall be rich,
and he shall give royal delicacies.note
21
נַפְתָּלִי, אַיָּלָה שְׁלֻחָה--הַנֹּתֵן, אִמְרֵי-שָׁפֶר.{ס}
Naphtali is a doe let loose,
giving beautiful words.note
22
בֵּן פֹּרָת יוֹסֵף, בֵּן פֹּרָת עֲלֵי-עָיִן; בָּנוֹת, צָעֲדָה עֲלֵי-שׁוּר.
A fruitful son is Joseph,
a fruitful son by a spring;
daughters — each strode over a wall.note
23
וַיְמָרְרֻהוּ, וָרֹבּוּ; וַיִּשְׂטְמֻהוּ, בַּעֲלֵי חִצִּים.
And they embittered him and shot at him,
and the masters of arrows harassed him.note
24
וַתֵּשֶׁב בְּאֵיתָן קַשְׁתּוֹ, וַיָּפֹזּוּ זְרֹעֵי יָדָיו; מִידֵי אֲבִיר יַעֲקֹב, מִשָּׁם רֹעֶה אֶבֶן יִשְׂרָאֵל.
But his bow stayed firm,
and the arms of his hands were made supple
by the hands of the Mighty One of Jacob
— from there, the Shepherd, the Stone of Israel —note
25
מֵאֵל אָבִיךָ וְיַעְזְרֶךָּ, וְאֵת שַׁדַּי וִיבָרְכֶךָּ, בִּרְכֹת שָׁמַיִם מֵעָל, בִּרְכֹת תְּהוֹם רֹבֶצֶת תָּחַת; בִּרְכֹת שָׁדַיִם, וָרָחַם.
from the God of your father — and may he help you —
and with Shaddai — and may he bless you:
blessings of heaven above,
blessings of the deep crouching below,
blessings of breasts and womb.note
26
בִּרְכֹת אָבִיךָ, גָּבְרוּ עַל-בִּרְכֹת הוֹרַי, עַד-תַּאֲוַת, גִּבְעֹת עוֹלָם; תִּהְיֶיןָ לְרֹאשׁ יוֹסֵף, וּלְקָדְקֹד נְזִיר אֶחָיו.{פ}
The blessings of your father have prevailed
over the blessings of my forebears,
to the bound of the everlasting hills.
Let them be on the head of Joseph,
and on the crown of the one set apart from his brothers.note
27
בִּנְיָמִין זְאֵב יִטְרָף, בַּבֹּקֶר יֹאכַל עַד; וְלָעֶרֶב, יְחַלֵּק שָׁלָל.
Benjamin is a wolf that tears;
in the morning he eats prey,
and at evening he divides spoil."note
28
כָּל-אֵלֶּה שִׁבְטֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר; וְזֹאת אֲשֶׁר-דִּבֶּר לָהֶם אֲבִיהֶם, וַיְבָרֶךְ אוֹתָם--אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר כְּבִרְכָתוֹ, בֵּרַךְ אֹתָם.
All these are the tribes of Israel, twelve; and this is what their father spoke to them, and he blessed them — each man according to his blessing he blessed them.note
29
וַיְצַו אוֹתָם, וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם אֲנִי נֶאֱסָף אֶל-עַמִּי--קִבְרוּ אֹתִי, אֶל-אֲבֹתָי: אֶל-הַמְּעָרָה--אֲשֶׁר בִּשְׂדֵה, עֶפְרוֹן הַחִתִּי.
And he charged them and said to them, "I am being gathered to my people. Bury me with my fathers, in the cave that is in the field of Ephron the Hittite,note
30
בַּמְּעָרָה אֲשֶׁר בִּשְׂדֵה הַמַּכְפֵּלָה, אֲשֶׁר עַל-פְּנֵי-מַמְרֵא--בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן: אֲשֶׁר קָנָה אַבְרָהָם אֶת-הַשָּׂדֶה, מֵאֵת עֶפְרֹן הַחִתִּי--לַאֲחֻזַּת-קָבֶר.
in the cave that is in the field of Machpelah, which faces Mamre in the land of Canaan, which Abraham bought with the field from Ephron the Hittite as a holding for a burial place.note
31
שָׁמָּה קָבְרוּ אֶת-אַבְרָהָם, וְאֵת שָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ, שָׁמָּה קָבְרוּ אֶת-יִצְחָק, וְאֵת רִבְקָה אִשְׁתּוֹ; וְשָׁמָּה קָבַרְתִּי, אֶת-לֵאָה.
There they buried Abraham and Sarah his wife; there they buried Isaac and Rebekah his wife; and there I buried Leah —note
32
מִקְנֵה הַשָּׂדֶה וְהַמְּעָרָה אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ, מֵאֵת בְּנֵי-חֵת.
the purchase of the field and the cave that is in it, from the sons of Heth."note
33
וַיְכַל יַעֲקֹב לְצַוֺּת אֶת-בָּנָיו, וַיֶּאֱסֹף רַגְלָיו אֶל-הַמִּטָּה; וַיִּגְוַע, וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל-עַמָּיו.
And Jacob finished charging his sons, and he gathered his feet into the bed, and expired, and was gathered to his people.note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–7 · the summons, and the three who lose everything

What kind of document this is. The longest poem in Genesis, and the last thing Jacob says. It is a tribal oracle as much as a family one — most of the sayings describe what the tribe will be like, not what the man was, and several are transparently about geography and history (Zebulun by the sea, Issachar under forced labour, Dan ambushing from the roadside). Be-acharit ha-yamim, "in the latter days," means the far end of things — the horizon of what is coming, without in itself specifying how far. ⚠️ Later readers took it as a technical term for the end of history and read this chapter accordingly; the phrase does not carry that weight on its own here, and this translation renders it plainly and leaves the reach of it open.

Reuben: three lines of grandeur, then the sentence. Verse 3 is genuinely magnificent — firstborn, might, first of my strength, excess of dignity and excess of power — and it exists to make the fall further. Then pachaz ka-mayim: reckless, unbridled, boiling over like water. ⚠️ Watch the grammar break at the end of verse 4. He has been speaking to Reuben — "you went up to your father's bed" — and then, mid-sentence, cannot: "he went up to my couch." The second person collapses into the third. Whether it is a stylistic flourish or an old man unable to keep looking at his son while saying it, the seam is in the Hebrew, and this translation keeps it rather than tidying the pronoun. The event is 35:22, reported there in one flat verse with the note that "Israel heard." He heard, and said nothing, for something like forty years.

Simeon and Levi: he curses the anger, not the men. ⚠️ This is precise and it matters. "Cursed be their anger" — not cursed be they. A father on his deathbed condemns a disposition and its consequences while stopping short of cursing his own sons; the distinction is deliberate and it is the only mercy in the verse. The crime is chapter 34, the massacre at Shechem, which he condemned at the time on prudential grounds ("you have brought trouble on me") and now condemns morally, thirty years too late to matter. "They hamstrung an ox" is troubling and not fully explained by chapter 34; it may refer to wanton destruction of livestock in the raid, and some read shor, "ox," as a poetic figure for a man.

"I will divide them and scatter them" — and both landed. Simeon's allotment was swallowed inside Judah's (Joshua 19:1-9) and the tribe effectively disappears. Levi received no territory at all, but was distributed through the country in forty-eight cities as the priestly tribe. ⚠️ The same sentence, on the same two brothers, becomes extinction for one and ordination for the other. Genesis says nothing about that asymmetry; it is worth noticing that a curse of scattering is exactly what a priesthood requires.

Verses 8–12 · Judah, and "until Shiloh comes"

The name is the blessing. Yehudah… yodukha — "Judah… they shall praise you." Leah named him from the verb yadah, "to give thanks / praise," at 29:35, and the poem simply hands the name back to him as a prediction. Then the hand on the enemy's neck, and the brothers bowing — which is what Joseph dreamed, awarded here to somebody else.

Why Judah gets this at all. He is the fourth son, and the three ahead of him have just been disqualified in seven verses. But the case for him was built over chapters, not conferred: he proposed the sale (37:26), was told he was more righteous than he was by a woman he had wronged (38:26), stood surety for Benjamin, and offered to be enslaved in his brother's place (44:33). The sceptre goes to the one who volunteered to be a slave.

⚠️ Verse 10 is the most disputed line in Genesis, and this translation will not pretend otherwise. The Hebrew is ad ki yavo shiloh, and there are four serious readings of that one word.
