(1) A name — "until Shiloh comes." This is what the Masoretic pointing gives and what this translation prints; it is also the least explanatory, since it leaves the referent undefined. (2) "Until he comes to Shiloh" — the town in Ephraim where the tabernacle stood, which would make it a historical marker rather than a person. (3) Reading the consonants as shello — "until that which is his comes," or "until he comes to whom it belongs." This is the ancient understanding behind the Septuagint and the Syriac, and Ezekiel 21:27 appears to allude to it ("until he comes whose right it is"). (4) Shai lo — "until tribute comes to him." ⚠️ It should be said plainly that this verse has been read messianically in both traditions for a very long time — the Jewish Targums render it "until Messiah comes," and Christian readers have applied it to Jesus — and equally plainly that the messianic reading rests on option (1) or (3) rather than on anything the consonants settle by themselves. This project's rule is to give readings with their pedigrees and not to vote. KJV "until Shiloh come"; ASV the same with a margin note offering "till he come to Shiloh"; the ESV "until tribute comes to him," footnoting the alternatives; the NWT "until Shiloh comes." The versions genuinely disagree, and that is the honest state of the question.

"From between his feet." A mechoqeq is a commander's staff, held or planted between the feet of a seated ruler; the phrase is about a dynasty seated in place. It has also been read as a euphemism for descendants, which would make the line about an unbroken line rather than an unbroken reign — the two readings largely converge.

The vineyard lines. Verses 11-12 are extravagance, not agriculture: a man so rich in vines that he tethers a donkey to one — which would strip it — and does his laundry in wine. "The blood of grapes" and the darkened eyes have drawn heavy later interpretation; the plain sense is abundance so great it is wasteful. KJV's "his eyes shall be red with wine" reads chakhlili as redness; "dark, dull, glowing" is the likelier range, and this keeps it comparative as the Hebrew does — darker than wine.

Verses 13–21 · six short sayings, and a prayer that interrupts

The puns are the point. Three of these sayings are built on the sound of the name and the translation keeps the play where English can carry it: Dan yadin — Dan will judge; Gad, gedud yegudennu… yagud — a raiding band will raid him, he will raid at their heel, three forms of the same root in six words. These are not decorations; in a tribal oracle the name is treated as containing the destiny.

Issachar is not a compliment. "A bony donkey crouching between the saddlebags" — strong, and settling down under a load. He saw that rest was good and that the land was pleasant, and traded his freedom for both: mas oved, forced labour, is the phrase used of state corvée. It is a portrait of a tribe that took the comfortable valley and paid tribute to keep it. ⚠️ Several of these sayings are like this — Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Issachar, and arguably Benjamin are not flattering — which is what makes verse 28's summary line ("he blessed them") worth stopping at.

Verse 18 is the strangest line in the chapter. "For your salvation I have waited, Jehovah." A dying man, mid-oracle, halfway through a list of his sons, breaks off and prays. It is addressed to God, not to the sons; it is in the first person; and then he simply resumes with Gad. ⚠️ Nothing in the text explains it. Readings offered: that the vision of Dan's serpent-ambush frightened him; that the poem's litany of struggle drew the prayer out; that it is an old liturgical line embedded in the poem. It also contains the noun yeshu'ah, "salvation" — the word behind the name Joshua and later Jesus — and this translation leaves the interruption exactly where it is, unsmoothed, because it is one of the most human moments in the book: a man reciting the future of a nation stops for a second and asks for help.

Verses 22–27 · Joseph, and the God with four names

⚠️ Verse 22 is genuinely obscure. Ben porat can be read as "a fruitful son" (from parah, to bear fruit — hence the traditional "fruitful bough by a well, whose branches run over the wall") or as "a wild colt," taking porat as a young animal and banot tsa'adah alei shur as wild asses or a colt striding — which fits the surrounding animal imagery (lion, donkey, serpent, doe, wolf) rather better. The Hebrew of the second half is broken enough that no translation is confident: KJV "a fruitful bough… whose branches run over the wall"; the ESV keeps the bough with a footnote; the NIV has "a fruitful vine" and footnotes "a wild colt." This keeps the fruitful reading, flags the other, and does not paper over the fact that "daughters — each strode over a wall" is close to untranslatable.

The archers. Verses 23-24 are the only place in the poem where Jacob describes what was done to a son. He never says who the archers were. Given that he is speaking to the ten men who threw this son into a pit, and that he apparently never learned the truth (nothing in Genesis says he was told), the line sits in the room with a weight the speaker may not know it has.

Four divine titles in two verses, three of them new. "The Mighty One of Jacob" (avir Ya'akov), "the Shepherd," "the Stone of Israel," and "Shaddai." Avir and even Yisrael appear here for the first time; "the Stone of Israel" occurs nowhere else in the Bible in that form. ⚠️ The Hebrew of verse 24's second half is compressed to the point of ambiguity — "from there, the Shepherd, the Stone of Israel" — and translations insert connectives freely; this keeps it bare.

The wordplay in verse 25 is the one Genesis planted long ago. "Shaddai" beside "blessings of shadayim — breasts — and womb." Whether the divine name is genuinely related to that noun is uncertain (see the dictionary entry), but the poet clearly hears them together, and the blessing is stacked in three tiers: heaven above, the deep below, and the human body between them.

"The one set apart from his brothers." Nezir echav — from the root behind nazir, one consecrated and separated by vow. It can mean "prince" by extension, and most versions render it that way (KJV "him that was separate from his brethren"; NIV "the prince among his brothers"). Kept as separation here, because that is what actually happened to him.

Benjamin the wolf. Two lines, and they are not affectionate. ⚠️ The saying is hard to reconcile with the boy the story has just spent five chapters protecting — but it is a tribal oracle, and the tribe of Benjamin went on to produce Ehud, the archers of Judges 20, and Saul. The poem is not describing the child in the room.

Verses 28–33 · "he blessed them" — and Leah

⚠️ Read verse 28 against what just happened. Reuben was demoted, Simeon and Levi's anger was cursed, Issachar was called a pack animal who sold himself for a comfortable valley, and Benjamin was called a wolf — and the narrator's summary is "and he blessed them, each man according to his blessing." The word covers the lot. It is worth taking seriously rather than explaining away: in this book a blessing is a true word about a future, spoken with authority, not a compliment. Telling a man the truth about what his descendants will be is inside the category. The qualifier does the work — "each according to his blessing," which is to say, his own, and not another's.

The last instruction is a property deed. He does not close with a prophecy but with a title search: the cave, the field, Ephron the Hittite, Machpelah, facing Mamre, bought, from the sons of Heth. He recites the transaction because the transaction is the point — it is the only ground the family owns in the land it has been promised, and he is being carried four hundred miles to lie in it. Compare what he told Pharaoh two chapters ago: the days of my sojournings.

⚠️ "And there I buried Leah." Four words, at the end of the list, and Genesis has never mentioned her death. We are told here, in passing, in a burial instruction. And the man who worked fourteen years for Rachel, who buried Rachel on a roadside because there was still some distance to go, and who was still measuring that distance one chapter ago (48:7), is asking to be laid beside Leah — in the family grave, with Abraham and Sarah and Isaac and Rebekah. The text offers no reflection whatsoever on this. It is simply the last arrangement he makes, and the wife he did not choose is the one he chooses to lie next to for good.

"He gathered his feet into the bed." He had gathered his strength and sat up when Joseph came in (48:2); now, having finished, he draws his feet back up and lies down. Then three verbs and it is over: he expired, and was gathered to his people — the same verb, asaf, as "gather" in the poem's opening line (v. 1) and as his feet in this one. The chapter begins with a man calling his sons to gather and ends with the man himself gathered.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: the pronoun break in verse 4 kept as the Hebrew has it ("you went up… he went up"), not tidied; "cursed be their anger," not cursed be they; the name-puns kept audible — Judah/praise, Dan/judge, Gad/raid; "until Shiloh comes" following the Masoretic pointing with all four readings in the note and no vote; "the one set apart from his brothers" for nezir rather than "prince"; "darker of eyes than wine" kept comparative; and Shaddai left beside shadayim so the wordplay survives.

Echoes paid. Reuben and Bilhah, forty years of silence ended in one line; Shechem condemned at last on the right grounds; Leah's naming of Judah — "this time I will praise" — handed back as prophecy; Joseph's dream of brothers bowing, awarded in this poem to Judah; Judah's offer to be enslaved answered with the sceptre; and Abraham's purchase at Machpelah recited as a deed by the last man to be buried in it.

Echoes planted. The sceptre of Judah, and everything the later books build on it; the Lion of Judah; Levi scattered — a curse that becomes a priesthood; Simeon absorbed into Judah and effectively gone; Shiloh, where the tabernacle will stand; and the bones of Jacob starting a journey his descendants will repeat.

Next installment in this book — and the last of Genesis: chapter 50. Joseph weeps on his father's face, and Egypt mourns him seventy days. Then the brothers, with the old man dead, invent a message from him and beg for their lives — and get the sentence the whole book has been building toward: "You meant evil against me, but God meant it for good."

Genesis 50 — The Mister Translation

1
וַיִּפֹּל יוֹסֵף, עַל-פְּנֵי אָבִיו; וַיֵּבְךְּ עָלָיו, וַיִּשַּׁק-לוֹ.
And Joseph fell on his father's face, and wept over him, and kissed him.note
2
וַיְצַו יוֹסֵף אֶת-עֲבָדָיו אֶת-הָרֹפְאִים, לַחֲנֹט אֶת-אָבִיו; וַיַּחַנְטוּ הָרֹפְאִים, אֶת-יִשְׂרָאֵל.
And Joseph commanded his servants the physicians to embalm his father; and the physicians embalmed Israel.note
3
וַיִּמְלְאוּ-לוֹ אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם, כִּי כֵּן יִמְלְאוּ יְמֵי הַחֲנֻטִים; וַיִּבְכּוּ אֹתוֹ מִצְרַיִם, שִׁבְעִים יוֹם.
And forty days were fulfilled for him, for so are fulfilled the days of the embalmed; and Egypt wept for him seventy days.note
4
וַיַּעַבְרוּ, יְמֵי בְכִיתוֹ, וַיְדַבֵּר יוֹסֵף, אֶל-בֵּית פַּרְעֹה לֵאמֹר: אִם-נָא מָצָאתִי חֵן, בְּעֵינֵיכֶם--דַּבְּרוּ-נָא, בְּאָזְנֵי פַרְעֹה לֵאמֹר.
And the days of weeping for him passed, and Joseph spoke to the house of Pharaoh, saying, "If now I have found favour in your eyes, speak, please, in the ears of Pharaoh, saying:note
5
אָבִי הִשְׁבִּיעַנִי לֵאמֹר, הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי מֵת--בְּקִבְרִי אֲשֶׁר כָּרִיתִי לִי בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן, שָׁמָּה תִּקְבְּרֵנִי; וְעַתָּה, אֶעֱלֶה-נָּא וְאֶקְבְּרָה אֶת-אָבִי--וְאָשׁוּבָה.
'My father made me swear, saying, "Look — I am dying. In my grave, which I dug for myself in the land of Canaan, there you shall bury me." And now let me go up, please, and bury my father — and I will return.'"note
6
וַיֹּאמֶר, פַּרְעֹה: עֲלֵה וּקְבֹר אֶת-אָבִיךָ, כַּאֲשֶׁר הִשְׁבִּיעֶךָ.
And Pharaoh said, "Go up and bury your father, as he made you swear."note
7
וַיַּעַל יוֹסֵף, לִקְבֹּר אֶת-אָבִיו; וַיַּעֲלוּ אִתּוֹ כָּל-עַבְדֵי פַרְעֹה, זִקְנֵי בֵיתוֹ, וְכֹל, זִקְנֵי אֶרֶץ-מִצְרָיִם.
And Joseph went up to bury his father; and all Pharaoh's servants went up with him, the elders of his house and all the elders of the land of Egypt,note
8
וְכֹל בֵּית יוֹסֵף, וְאֶחָיו וּבֵית אָבִיו: רַק, טַפָּם וְצֹאנָם וּבְקָרָם--עָזְבוּ, בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן.
and all Joseph's house, and his brothers, and his father's house. Only their little ones and their flocks and their herds they left in the land of Goshen.note
9
וַיַּעַל עִמּוֹ, גַּם-רֶכֶב גַּם-פָּרָשִׁים; וַיְהִי הַמַּחֲנֶה, כָּבֵד מְאֹד.
And chariots also went up with him, and horsemen; and the company was very heavy.note
10
וַיָּבֹאוּ עַד-גֹּרֶן הָאָטָד, אֲשֶׁר בְּעֵבֶר הַיַּרְדֵּן, וַיִּסְפְּדוּ-שָׁם, מִסְפֵּד גָּדוֹל וְכָבֵד מְאֹד; וַיַּעַשׂ לְאָבִיו אֵבֶל, שִׁבְעַת יָמִים.
And they came to the threshing floor of Atad, which is beyond the Jordan, and there they lamented with a great and very heavy lamentation; and he made for his father a mourning of seven days.note
11
וַיַּרְא יוֹשֵׁב הָאָרֶץ הַכְּנַעֲנִי אֶת-הָאֵבֶל, בְּגֹרֶן הָאָטָד, וַיֹּאמְרוּ, אֵבֶל-כָּבֵד זֶה לְמִצְרָיִם; עַל-כֵּן קָרָא שְׁמָהּ, אָבֵל מִצְרַיִם, אֲשֶׁר, בְּעֵבֶר הַיַּרְדֵּן.
And the inhabitant of the land, the Canaanite, saw the mourning at the threshing floor of Atad, and they said, "This is a heavy mourning for Egypt." Therefore its name was called Abel-Mizraim, which is beyond the Jordan.note
12
וַיַּעֲשׂוּ בָנָיו, לוֹ--כֵּן, כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּם.
And his sons did for him just as he had commanded them.note
13
וַיִּשְׂאוּ אֹתוֹ בָנָיו, אַרְצָה כְּנַעַן, וַיִּקְבְּרוּ אֹתוֹ, בִּמְעָרַת שְׂדֵה הַמַּכְפֵּלָה: אֲשֶׁר קָנָה אַבְרָהָם אֶת-הַשָּׂדֶה לַאֲחֻזַּת-קֶבֶר, מֵאֵת עֶפְרֹן הַחִתִּי--עַל-פְּנֵי מַמְרֵא.
And his sons carried him to the land of Canaan and buried him in the cave of the field of Machpelah, which Abraham bought with the field as a holding for a burial place from Ephron the Hittite, facing Mamre.note
14
וַיָּשָׁב יוֹסֵף מִצְרַיְמָה הוּא וְאֶחָיו, וְכָל-הָעֹלִים אִתּוֹ לִקְבֹּר אֶת-אָבִיו, אַחֲרֵי, קָבְרוֹ אֶת-אָבִיו.
And Joseph returned to Egypt, he and his brothers and all who had gone up with him to bury his father, after he had buried his father.note
15
וַיִּרְאוּ אֲחֵי-יוֹסֵף, כִּי-מֵת אֲבִיהֶם, וַיֹּאמְרוּ, לוּ יִשְׂטְמֵנוּ יוֹסֵף; וְהָשֵׁב יָשִׁיב, לָנוּ, אֵת כָּל-הָרָעָה, אֲשֶׁר גָּמַלְנוּ אֹתוֹ.
And Joseph's brothers saw that their father was dead, and they said, "What if Joseph hates us, and pays us back in full for all the evil that we dealt him?"note
16
וַיְצַוּוּ, אֶל-יוֹסֵף לֵאמֹר: אָבִיךָ צִוָּה, לִפְנֵי מוֹתוֹ לֵאמֹר.
And they sent word to Joseph, saying, "Your father commanded before his death, saying,note
17
כֹּה-תֹאמְרוּ לְיוֹסֵף, אָנָּא שָׂא נָא פֶּשַׁע אַחֶיךָ וְחַטָּאתָם כִּי-רָעָה גְמָלוּךָ, וְעַתָּה שָׂא נָא, לְפֶשַׁע עַבְדֵי אֱלֹהֵי אָבִיךָ; וַיֵּבְךְּ יוֹסֵף, בְּדַבְּרָם אֵלָיו.
'So you shall say to Joseph: Please — lift away, please, the transgression of your brothers and their sin, for they dealt you evil.' And now, please lift away the transgression of the servants of the God of your father." And Joseph wept when they spoke to him.note
18
וַיֵּלְכוּ, גַּם-אֶחָיו, וַיִּפְּלוּ, לְפָנָיו; וַיֹּאמְרוּ, הִנֶּנּוּ לְךָ לַעֲבָדִים.
And his brothers also went and fell before him, and said, "Look — we are your servants."note
19
וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יוֹסֵף, אַל-תִּירָאוּ: כִּי הֲתַחַת אֱלֹהִים, אָנִי.
And Joseph said to them, "Do not be afraid — for am I in the place of God?note
20
וְאַתֶּם, חֲשַׁבְתֶּם עָלַי רָעָה; אֱלֹהִים, חֲשָׁבָהּ לְטֹבָה, לְמַעַן עֲשֹׂה כַּיּוֹם הַזֶּה, לְהַחֲיֹת עַם-רָב.
And you — you devised evil against me; God devised it for good, in order to do as it is this day: to keep a numerous people alive.note
21
וְעַתָּה, אַל-תִּירָאוּ--אָנֹכִי אֲכַלְכֵּל אֶתְכֶם, וְאֶת-טַפְּכֶם; וַיְנַחֵם אוֹתָם, וַיְדַבֵּר עַל-לִבָּם.
And now, do not be afraid. I myself will provide for you and for your little ones." And he comforted them, and spoke to their heart.note
22
וַיֵּשֶׁב יוֹסֵף בְּמִצְרַיִם, הוּא וּבֵית אָבִיו; וַיְחִי יוֹסֵף, מֵאָה וָעֶשֶׂר שָׁנִים.
And Joseph dwelt in Egypt, he and his father's house; and Joseph lived a hundred and ten years.note
23
וַיַּרְא יוֹסֵף לְאֶפְרַיִם, בְּנֵי שִׁלֵּשִׁים; גַּם, בְּנֵי מָכִיר בֶּן-מְנַשֶּׁה--יֻלְּדוּ, עַל-בִּרְכֵּי יוֹסֵף.
And Joseph saw Ephraim's children of the third generation; the sons of Machir son of Manasseh were also born on Joseph's knees.note
24
וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף אֶל-אֶחָיו, אָנֹכִי מֵת; וֵאלֹהִים פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד אֶתְכֶם, וְהֶעֱלָה אֶתְכֶם מִן-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת, אֶל-הָאָרֶץ, אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּע לְאַבְרָהָם לְיִצְחָק וּלְיַעֲקֹב.
And Joseph said to his brothers, "I am dying; but God will surely visit you, and will bring you up from this land to the land that he swore to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob."note
25
וַיַּשְׁבַּע יוֹסֵף, אֶת-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֵאמֹר: פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד אֱלֹהִים אֶתְכֶם, וְהַעֲלִתֶם אֶת-עַצְמֹתַי מִזֶּה.
And Joseph made the sons of Israel swear, saying, "God will surely visit you, and you shall carry up my bones from here."note
26
וַיָּמָת יוֹסֵף, בֶּן-מֵאָה וָעֶשֶׂר שָׁנִים; וַיַּחַנְטוּ אֹתוֹ, וַיִּישֶׂם בָּאָרוֹן בְּמִצְרָיִם.{ש}
And Joseph died, a hundred and ten years old; and they embalmed him, and he was put in a coffin in Egypt.note

Translator's Notes

Verses 1–6 · a state funeral, in Egyptian

Before anything else, a son on his father's face. Three verbs — fell, wept, kissed — and then the machinery of an empire starts up. It is the last time Joseph weeps in this book, and the fifth: turning away (42:24), locked in a room (43:30), heard through the palace wall (45:2), on his father's neck at Goshen (46:29), and now on his father's face.

⚠️ The embalming is genuinely Egyptian, and it is one of only two in the Bible. Jacob here and Joseph in verse 26 — both in Egypt, both at the end of the book, and nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible does anyone get mummified. The numbers fit what is known of the practice: Herodotus reports a seventy-day process, and Egyptian sources describe a natron-drying stage of roughly forty days inside a longer ritual period. Genesis gives forty for the embalming and seventy for the national mourning, which is the same arithmetic seen from the outside. The narrator is not explaining Egypt to Egyptians; he is reporting a foreign custom accurately to readers who do not practise it. KJV "and the Egyptians mourned for him threescore and ten days"; ASV the same.

Why Joseph does not simply ask Pharaoh himself. ⚠️ Verse 4 is odd on its face: the second most powerful man in Egypt asks courtiers to pass a message to the king he sees regularly. The likeliest explanation is ritual — a mourner in Egypt went unshaven and unkempt, and appearing before the king in that state was not done (compare 41:14, where Joseph is shaved before being brought out of the pit-prison to Pharaoh). The man who once had to be groomed to enter the throne room now cannot enter it at all.

"The grave which I dug for myself." Karithi — dug, hewed out. ⚠️ Nothing in Genesis records Jacob digging a grave; the cave at Machpelah was bought by Abraham (23:16) and Jacob's own instruction two chapters ago recited that purchase carefully. The verb can carry the sense of acquiring or preparing a burial place rather than excavating one, and some read it of a grave dug within the existing cave. It may also be Joseph's diplomatic compression for a foreign court — "my own grave" is simply easier to explain to Pharaoh's household than a title deed from Ephron the Hittite. This translation keeps the literal verb and flags the tension.

Verses 7–14 · Egypt buries a shepherd

Look at who goes. All Pharaoh's servants, the elders of his household, all the elders of the land of Egypt, chariots and horsemen. This is a state cortège for a foreign herdsman — the same trade the Egyptians are said to find detestable (46:34) — travelling several hundred miles to bury him in another country. The honour is really being paid to Joseph, and Genesis lets the size of it speak. Kaved me'od, "very heavy," is the same word used of the famine's weight and of the mourning in verse 10; the procession is heavy in the way grief is.

⚠️ And look at what stays behind. "Only their little ones and their flocks and their herds they left in the land of Goshen." The children are hostages in all but name. Egypt is lending an army's worth of chariots to a funeral in Canaan and keeping the family's next generation where it can be found. Nothing in the text says so; the sentence simply sits there, and it is the first faint chill of Exodus 1.

Abel-Mizraim. The Canaanites, watching, name the place from what they see: evel mitzrayim, "mourning of Egypt" — heard as avel mitzrayim, "meadow of Egypt," the two words being identical in the consonants. The joke of it is that they are burying an Israelite and the observers cannot tell; from the roadside it is Egypt that has come to grieve. ⚠️ The location "beyond the Jordan" is a real puzzle, since the natural route from Egypt to Hebron does not cross the Jordan at all. It may reflect the perspective of a writer west of the river, or a longer detour, or a site whose location is simply lost.

Verses 15–21 · the message their father never sent

Seventeen years, and they still think he might kill them. That is the situation of verse 15, and it is worth sitting with. They have lived in Goshen under his protection since chapter 47. He has fed them through five years of famine. And the moment the old man dies they conclude, immediately and unanimously, that the only thing holding him back was their father. The Hebrew is conditional and frightened — lu yistemenu, "what if he hates us" — and the verb for repayment is doubled: pay us back in full.

⚠️ And the message is almost certainly invented. Genesis records no such instruction from Jacob. Two chapters of deathbed speech are given in detail — the adoption, the poem, the burial charge — and none of it contains a word to Joseph about forgiving his brothers. More than that: nothing in the book ever says Jacob learned what they did. It is possible he knew and it went unrecorded; it is possible they are telling the truth about a private conversation. But the plain reading is that ten frightened men have put words in their dead father's mouth, because they do not believe their own case will hold without him. Note also that they send word rather than come — they negotiate at a distance first, and only appear in person in verse 18, after they hear how he answered. Genesis reports the manoeuvre without a syllable of comment, exactly as it reported the pit.

"Lift away, please." Sa na — the ordinary verb for carrying, used of forgiveness: take this weight off us. KJV "forgive the trespass"; ESV "please forgive the transgression." Keeping the physical sense matters here, because what they are asking him to carry is real and the next verse prices it.

"And Joseph wept when they spoke to him." Not at the memory of the crime. At the message. After everything — the reveal, the embrace, the seventeen years — they are still managing him, still bargaining, still calling themselves "the servants of the God of your father" rather than his brothers. The weeping is the whole answer to their fear, and it arrives before he says a word.

"Am I in the place of God?" ⚠️ The same phrase Jacob threw at Rachel in anger when she demanded children (30:2) — am I in the place of God? — meaning there it was a refusal of power he did not have. Here it is a refusal of a role he does have. He is the second man in Egypt; he could do exactly what they fear, that afternoon, lawfully. He declines on the grounds that judgement is not his office. It is also, incidentally, the correct answer to what they have just tried: they wanted a verdict of acquittal, and he tells them he is not the judge.

Verse 20 is the sentence the book has been walking toward — and the grammar matters. "You devised evil against me; God devised it for good." The same verb, chashav, twice — to plan, to reckon, to intend. Notice what the sentence does not say. It does not say the evil was secretly good. It does not say they did not do it — the object of the first verb is evil, flatly, and verse 17 has just used their own words: "the transgression of your brothers and their sin." And it does not say God made them do it. Two agents, one event, opposite intentions, and both intentions real. This is the fully developed form of what Joseph said five chapters earlier in the heat of the reveal — "it was not you who sent me here, but God" — and it is stated here more carefully, with the guilt left in. KJV "ye thought evil against me; but God meant it unto good"; ASV the same; the ESV "you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good"; the NWT "you had evil in mind against me. God had good in mind." Every version keeps both agents; the point is unmissable in the Hebrew and none of them has tried to soften it.

"To keep a numerous people alive." Lehachayot — the same root as the michyah of 45:5 and the "you have kept us alive" of the Egyptians selling themselves at 47:25. Three times now, and each time it means survival bought at a price somebody paid.

"And spoke to their heart." Vaydabber al libbam — an idiom of tenderness, used elsewhere of reassuring the frightened and of courtship. Most versions render the sense rather than the words (KJV "spake kindly unto them"; NIV "reassured them"); this keeps the heart in it. It is the last thing he does for them, and it answers a fear he could simply have dismissed.

Verses 22–26 · a hundred and ten years, and a coffin in Egypt

⚠️ A hundred and ten is not a round number by accident. It is the Egyptian ideal lifespan — the figure that appears repeatedly in Egyptian wisdom literature and funerary inscriptions as the perfect span a well-lived life should reach. Hebrew ideals run differently (Abraham 175, Isaac 180, Jacob 147; Psalm 90 sets the human limit at seventy or eighty). Genesis gives Joseph the Egyptian number. He is buried in the Egyptian manner, he holds an Egyptian title, his sons have an Egyptian mother — and the book closes his life in the vocabulary of the country he served. Whether that is honour or irony is not stated.

Great-grandchildren on his knees. The same gesture of acknowledgement Jacob used on Ephraim and Manasseh (48:12). Joseph, the son who was taken from his father, ends surrounded by four generations — and the last recorded thing he does with his hands is claim children.

"God will surely visit you." Paqod yifqod — the infinitive doubled for certainty, and he says it twice, in verses 24 and 25, in the last words he speaks. Paqad means to attend to, to take notice of, to call to account; it is what a commander does at a muster and what a shepherd does at a count. KJV "God will surely visit you"; ASV the same; the ESV "God will visit you," dropping the doubled infinitive that makes it a certainty. ⚠️ These exact words are picked up at the burning bush and again on the night of the exodus — Moses is told to tell the elders "I have surely visited you" (Exodus 3:16), and when Israel finally leaves, the narrator stops the departure to say that Moses took the bones of Joseph with him, "for he had solemnly sworn the sons of Israel, saying: God will surely visit you" (Exodus 13:19). The oath is honoured four hundred years later, and the bones are finally buried at Shechem, in ground Jacob bought (Joshua 24:32). Joseph does not ask to be taken home now. He asks to be carried out when — and stakes his body on a promise he will not see kept.

The last word of Genesis. Ba-aron be-mitzrayim — "in a coffin, in Egypt." Aron is simply a chest or box; it is the same word that will later name the ark of the covenant, the chest carried through the wilderness. KJV "he was put in a coffin in Egypt"; NWT "he was placed in a coffin in Egypt" — every version keeps the box. So the book that opens "in the beginning God created the heavens and the earth" ends with an embalmed man in a box in a foreign country. ⚠️ And that is deliberately not a resolution. Every promise in the book is outstanding: the land is not held, the nation is seventy people in a border province, and the last image is a container waiting to be picked up. The final Hebrew word is the name of the wrong country. Genesis ends the way it does so that Exodus has to begin.

Patterns worth carrying forward

Where this translation keeps the words exact / distinct: "devised… devised" for the doubled chashav in verse 20, so the two agents share one verb; "lift away" for nasa in the plea for forgiveness, which is what the word does — carry a burden off someone; "am I in the place of God?" kept identical to Jacob's phrase at 30:2; "spoke to their heart" left as the idiom; "surely visit" for paqod yifqod, the doubled infinitive Exodus quotes back; and "coffin" for aron, with the note that it is the ark's own word.

Echoes paid. The pit answered at last, by the man who was in it; "it was not you who sent me here, but God" restated with the guilt left in; the oath about the burial discharged with an Egyptian army as escort; Abraham's purchase at Machpelah used for the fourth and last time; the knees that claimed Ephraim and Manasseh claiming their grandchildren; and the dream of brothers bowing completed in verse 18 — where Joseph, who had it, refuses to enjoy it.

Echoes planted. "God will surely visit you", quoted verbatim at the bush and on the night Israel leaves; Joseph's bones, carried out by Moses and buried at Shechem four hundred years later; the children left behind in Goshen; and a family of seventy that Exodus will open by calling too numerous to be safe.

📖 That is the end of Genesis. Fifty chapters — the first complete book in this translation. It began with a formless earth and ends with a box in Egypt, and almost nothing promised in it has arrived: the land is a grave-plot, the nation is a household, and the blessing that was supposed to reach all the families of the earth has so far reached one Egyptian province during a famine. What the book does deliver is a method — a God who works through people who lie, cheat, favour the wrong child and sell their brother, without at any point pretending they did not. The last thing anyone says in it is a request to be carried home later.

Next in this translation: the story picks up in Exodus, which opens by repeating Genesis 46's list of names and then says they filled the land — and a new king arises who did not know Joseph. Exodus 1–3 are already translated here; the work continues from chapter 4.
